docs.title: Karna: The Tragic Hero of the Mahabharata
docs.text:
The Mahabharata is a gallery of heroes, and Karna is perhaps the most heroic, yet most tragic, of them all. Fate denied him his rightful dues, but he fought and achieved all that a man could aspire to have.

His story began before the great war, when King Kuntibhoja adopted his young niece, Pritha, and renamed her Kunti.

One day, the fiery and easily-angered sage Durvasa visited the king's palace. "The venerable sage will be our guest. Look after him well," the king instructed Kunti. For a full year, Kunti served the sage carefully, patiently, and with absolute devotion.

Durvasa was deeply pleased. "I will give you a divine mantra," the sage told her. "It will enable you to call upon any god to come and bless you with a son who is in every way his equal."

As soon as Durvasa left, the young and curious Kunti looked up at the sun. "I wonder if it really works. Oh! Lord Surya smiles at me. I will call him down," she thought, reciting the mantra.

Instantly, Surya, the Sun God, appeared in his blinding glory.

Terrified, Kunti stammered, "But I am not married. I was only curious to see if you would come. Please, please go back."

"You shall have the son you desire," Surya gently replied. "I cannot go, as the spell of the mantra holds me."

Thus, Karna was born. From his divine father, the radiant infant inherited an impenetrable golden armor (kavacha) and brilliant earrings (kundalas) attached to his skin.

But Kunti, an unwed princess, was terrified of the world's judgment. Weeping bitterly, she placed the baby in a wicker basket and set it afloat on the river. "Ah! Dear son, forgive me," she cried. "If I ever see you again, will I have the strength to acknowledge you then?"

Further down the river, a childless charioteer named Adhiratha spotted the basket. "Ah! What a beautiful child, abandoned by some heartless mother, no doubt." He took the baby home to his overjoyed wife, Radha.

"Let us call him Vasushena," Radha beamed, cradling the golden child.

"He shall be Radheya, dear wife," Adhiratha said proudly. "Radheya, the son of Radha."

Karna’s early childhood was a happy one. Meanwhile, Kunti married King Pandu of Hastinapura. Because of a curse, Pandu could not father children, so he retreated to the Himalayas. Using the mantra Durvasa had given her, Kunti and Pandu's second wife, Madri, called upon the gods to bless them with five sons—the Pandava princes. When Pandu died, Kunti brought the five fatherless princes to Hastinapura, where the elders Bheeshma and Vidura welcomed them to grow up alongside their cousins, the Kauravas.

From the beginning, a bitter rivalry sprang up. The Kauravas grew resentful of the skilled Pandavas. "Bheema torments me on the playground and haunts my dreams. Unfair!" complained Duryodhana, the eldest Kaurava.

The great guru Dronacharya taught all the princes the arts of war. Karna, drawn to the martial arts, also became his pupil. But he noticed a bitter truth. "He is partial to Arjuna," Karna thought, watching Drona dote on the young Pandava. "He will never let me surpass him."

Hoping to equal his rival, Karna approached his teacher. "Guru, please teach me the Brahmastra."

Drona scoffed. "The Brahmastra is only meant for Brahmanas, and high-born Kshatriyas. I cannot teach it to you—a charioteer's son."

Stung by the humiliation, Karna made a bold decision. He traveled to the ashram of the legendary warrior Parashurama. "Parashurama, they say, hates Kshatriyas. I will go to him in the guise of a Brahman. He is sure to accept me," Karna plotted.

"I am a Brahman lad. Please accept me as your pupil," Karna bowed before the sage.

"Come, my son. I am willing to teach any worthy youth, who is not a Kshatriya," Parashurama welcomed him.

Under Parashurama, Karna mastered the highest weapons. But one afternoon, as the sage was resting his head on Karna's lap, a large, vicious insect burrowed into Karna's thigh. Blood flowed, and the pain was excruciating. Ah! How the little insect stings, Karna winced internally. But I will not disturb your rest, beloved master. Sleep on.

When Parashurama woke and saw the pool of blood, his eyes narrowed in fury. "You have deceived me. Only a Kshatriya can bear such pain to protect another. Tell me the truth!"

"I am the son of Adhiratha, the charioteer, a sutaputra. Forgive me, sir," Karna confessed.

"For deceiving your guru," the enraged sage cursed, "your knowledge will fail you when you need it most!"

Dejected, Karna wandered blindly into a nearby forest. Startled by rustling bushes, he shot an arrow on instinct, only to realize he had accidentally slain a Brahman's cow.

"Sinner, you have killed my poor innocent cow!" the Brahman screamed, adding a second, fatal curse to the young warrior's burden: "You too will be killed when you are helpless to defend yourself!"

Karna returned home to his mother, Radha. "Mother, I hear that an open tournament is to be held at Hastinapura... A valiant warrior will find his place among the valiant," he told her, determined to prove himself.

At the tournament, Arjuna dazzled the crowd with his archery. But suddenly, the gates opened, and Karna strode in, his golden armor gleaming. To the astonishment of everyone, he effortlessly matched and replicated every single feat Arjuna had performed.

"Bravo! Well done!" the crowd roared.

Duryodhana, thrilled to see someone challenge the arrogant Pandavas, rushed forward. "Welcome, O valiant one! It is our good fortune that you are here today. What shall be your reward?"

"Good king, all I ask is your love and the opportunity to meet Drona's favourite pupil Arjuna in single combat!" Karna declared.

But before the duel could begin, Kripa, a royal teacher, stepped forward. "Who are you? Reveal your parentage. Our prince may fight only with his equal in birth and status."

Karna lowered his head in shame, while high up in the stands, Queen Kunti recognized the golden armor and fainted in grief.

"I crown Karna prince of Anga!" Duryodhana declared loudly, throwing a crown onto Karna's head to give him royal status.

Just then, the old charioteer Adhiratha hobbled onto the stage. "My son! My blessings!" he wept, embracing Karna.

Bhima roared with laughter. "Go sutaputra! Go drive your chariot! You are not worthy of death at Arjuna's hands."

Duryodhana, seething with anger at the insult, took Karna by the arm. "Come Karna! It takes a hero to recognise a hero!" he said, leading him away. From that moment, a bond of absolute loyalty was forged. "Duryodhana, you have not befriended me in vain," Karna vowed silently. "I will kill Arjuna in single combat one day! I will!"

As the years passed, the rivalry erupted into a war for the throne of Hastinapura. In the heavens, Lord Indra watched nervously. If Karna is deprived of his kavacha and kundalas, my son Arjuna need fear no mortal, Indra plotted.

Surya, the Sun God, appeared in Karna's dream to warn him. "Lord Indra will try to take advantage of your generosity. Do not part with your kavacha and kundalas."

But the next morning, Indra approached Karna disguised as a poor Brahman. "I beg for alms. Karna, it is said, refuses nothing to those who ask. I want your kavacha and kundalas."

Despite his father's warning, Karna smiled and sliced the golden armor from his flesh. "My lord, I know who you are and why you are here," Karna said, handing over his invincibility. "Yet, I give to you my very life. Never let it be said that Karna refused anyone anything."

Touched by this absolute selflessness, Indra offered a boon. "In return I grant you my master weapon Shakti, but you may use it only once."

As war loomed, Lord Krishna went to Karna to try to avert the disaster by revealing the truth of his birth. "Come with me. I will make you a great emperor. You will have five powerful brothers and a mother who longs for you."

Karna shook his head. "Duryodhana's love is what I value most in my life. So if you really have my interest at heart, promise me that you will keep the secret of my birth till I die."

Desperate, Kunti herself visited Karna as he finished his prayers. Weeping, she confessed her sin and pleaded with him not to fight his brothers.

"Mother, what you ask me to do is against my dharma," Karna replied gently but firmly. "I will fight against your sons, my brothers, but I will not kill any except Arjuna. You shall still have five sons alive, and I, my honour."

The horrific battle of Kurukshetra began. On the tenth day, the venerable commander Bheeshma fell on a bed of arrows. Karna, who had been kept out of the battle by Bheeshma's refusal to fight alongside a sutaputra, rushed to the dying patriarch.

"My lord! I have never been fortunate enough to be liked by you. Yet I have come to pay my respects..." Karna bowed.

"Dear Karna! I knew who you were," Bheeshma smiled weakly. "Your firm loyalty to Duryodhana is your chosen dharma. May you lead his forces to victory! Go fight your fated enemies."

Karna entered the fray. On the fourteenth day, he cornered Bhima but lowered his bow. Forgive me, Duryodhana. I have Bhima at my mercy but I cannot kill him. I have promised my mother.

That night, Bhima's monstrous half-demon son, Ghatotkacha, wreaked havoc on the Kaurava army. "Kill that fellow immediately, Karna, or else our whole army will be vanquished," Duryodhana pleaded.

With no other choice to save his friend's army, Karna unleashed the infallible Shakti weapon, killing the demon. Alas! I have killed Ghatotkacha, but I have lost the mightiest weapon Shakti which I had meant to use on Arjuna.

On the sixteenth day, Drona fell, and Karna was made commander. The final confrontation drew near.

The next day, Karna faced Arjuna. As their arrows collided with earth-shattering force, the wheel of Karna's chariot sank deep into the muddy ground.

"My memory fails me. I cannot recall the mantras of the great Parashurama!" Karna gasped as Parashurama's curse took effect. He jumped down to drag the heavy wheel out of the mud.

Seeing Karna unarmed and struggling, Arjuna hesitated. But Lord Krishna, knowing Karna was too powerful to defeat in a fair fight, commanded Arjuna to strike.

As Karna strained against the wheel, Arjuna drew his bow and shot the fatal arrow, severing the head of the great hero. The curse of the Brahman had been fulfilled—Karna died helpless to defend himself.

After the war, the victorious Pandavas gathered to perform the death ceremonies for their fallen kin.

"Wait! There is one more left," Kunti said through her tears.

"But why should I do it for Karna?" Yudhishthira asked. "Karna, our arch enemy, Karna the charioteer's son!"

Kunti broke down and told them the tragic tale of her firstborn.

The sun set slowly over the battlefield as the truth settled heavily upon the brothers. Each of the Pandavas remembered his own encounters with the fierce, noble warrior who had spared their lives. Thus Karna, who had longed for love, recognition, and a good name while he lived, secured them—like many great men before him—only in death.

docs.title: The Queen's Necklace
docs.text:
One sunny day, the queen and her attendants were enjoying a bath in the royal pool. A young maid was tasked with sitting on the banks to watch over the queen's beautiful clothes and precious jewellery. As the afternoon wore on, the warm sun made the girl drowsy, and she soon drifted off to sleep.

High up in a nearby tree, a female monkey had been watching the sparkling jewels with great interest. "That pearl necklace would look wonderful on me," she thought mischievously. "How can I get it without being seen? Oh, she's dozing. This is my chance!"

The monkey quietly climbed down, snatched the gleaming pearl necklace, and scrambled back up the tree. She hid the stolen treasure in a hollow branch and sat over it, waiting to see what would happen.

Soon, the maid awoke. She gasped in horror. "The queen's necklace is gone! Thief! Thief! A thief has stolen the queen's necklace!"

The palace guards came rushing to the scene. "Did you see which way he went?" the captain demanded.

"No!" the terrified girl cried.

"He must have jumped over the wall. Let's look outside!" The guards poured out of the palace gates. The very first person they spotted was a poor, innocent woodcutter carrying a bundle of sticks.

"There he is! Catch him!" they shouted.

Seeing the angry, armed guards charging at him, the woodcutter panicked. They say these soldiers are cruel, heartless fellows, he thought. I wonder why they want me, but I'd better not stop to ask! He dropped his wood and ran for his life, but he tripped and fell with a heavy thump.

"Where's the necklace?" the captain roared, hauling the woodcutter to his feet. "Don't pretend to be innocent! I'll bang your head against a tree!"

"Oh, my God! These ruffians are just looking for an excuse to beat me," the woodcutter thought frantically. "No, no, don't hurt me! I'll tell you everything! I gave the necklace to... to a priest!"

The guards immediately hauled the woodcutter into town and dragged a bewildered priest out of his home. "Where's the necklace?" they demanded.

The priest, seeing the angry guards, thought the exact same thing: This is no doubt happening to me because of some sin I have committed in my past life. "Please don't touch me," he begged. "I'll tell you everything. I gave it to that musician."

The guards dragged the musician from his house. Terrified, the musician pointed at a passing palanquin. "I gave the necklace to... to that dancer!"

The dancer was stopped and pulled from her carriage. "Where's the necklace?" they demanded.

"I've never seen him before in my life!" the dancer protested indignantly, glaring at the musician. "Why are you looking at me like that? Stay away from me! Stay away from me, you big bully!"

Confused and angry, the guards arrested all four of them—the woodcutter, the priest, the musician, and the dancer—and dragged them before the King and his wise Dewan.

The King interrogated them. The woodcutter swore he gave it to the priest, the priest swore he gave it to the musician, and the musician swore he gave it to the dancer, who vehemently denied it all.

"Lock them all up!" the King commanded in frustration. "He's lying!"

But the clever Dewan stroked his chin thoughtfully. "Yes, your majesty," he agreed quietly. But later, he went to the King privately. "The palace is well-guarded," the Dewan reasoned. "The woodcutter could not have come in and gone out without being seen. He's lying to save himself from a beating. The other two must be lying for the exact same reason. But if they are innocent... who is the guilty one?"

The Dewan took a walk through the palace gardens. He noticed the monkeys playing in the trees above the pool. "Who could have... Oh! I think I know. Only a monkey could have taken that necklace without being seen."

The Dewan quickly hatched a plan. "Catch some of those monkeys," he ordered the guards. "Put cheap bead necklaces around their necks and let them go."

The guards did as they were told. The monkeys, thrilled with their new shiny beads, swung through the trees, proudly showing them off to everyone.

Deep in her hollow, the female monkey who had stolen the real necklace saw her friends showing off. "They are so happy with their glass beads," she thought smugly. "Let them see my pearls. Their eyes will pop out!"

She proudly pulled the queen's pearl necklace from the hollow and draped it around her neck.

"There's a monkey wearing the queen's necklace! Catch it!" the guards yelled.

Using the bead necklaces, they tricked the monkey into coming closer and swapped the beads for the pearls. The Dewan triumphantly returned the necklace to the King. The King, realizing his mistake, immediately released the four innocent prisoners.

"We must apologise to the dancer," the Dewan smiled. "As for the others, let them go with a warning to be more truthful in future!"

docs.title: The Greedy Fisherman
docs.text:
One bright morning, a fisherman and his young son went down to the riverbank to fish. The fisherman threw his line into the water and waited patiently. After some time, he felt a massive tug on the line.

"Wow! I've got one!" he cheered, wrestling with his fishing rod. But the fish was incredibly strong. "I can't pull him in. He must be as big as a mountain! I'll wade into the water and pull this fish out."

Turning to his son, the fisherman grinned greedily. "Son, go tell your mother I have caught a big one! And... tell her to pick a quarrel with the neighbours."

The boy looked confused. "Quarrel with the neighbours? Why?"

"Because if I don't quarrel with them, they'll come running in when your father brings the fish, and then we'll have to share it with them," he explained.

The boy ran all the way home and delivered the strange message to his mother. "Mother, father wants you to quarrel with the neighbours. He has caught a big fish."

"I would never dream of quarrelling with my dear neighbours..." the woman began, before pausing. "But you know how greedy our neighbours are. They'll want the best parts. Now run along."

Determined to hoard the entire fish for her family, the woman hatched a bizarre plan to make her neighbors hate her. She rubbed dark soot all over her face, stuck a large green leaf behind her ear, and stepped out of the house.

"Good neighbours are more precious than all the jewels in the world," an elderly neighbor woman was just saying to her friend. "And all the..."

She stopped, staring in shock at the fisherman's wife. "Good Lord, what have you done to yourself? Has she gone mad?"

"Who are you calling 'mad', you nitwit?" the fisherman's wife snapped loudly, picking the fight exactly as planned.

"Who's calling whom a nitwit?" the elderly woman shot back, enraged.

"Don't you dare stare at me with those big eyes of yours!" the fisherman's wife yelled.

In no time at all, a massive, screaming quarrel flared up between the women. They made such a terrible noise that the town Kotwal (police chief) arrived with his guards to break it up. He immediately dragged them both away to the magistrate's court.

The magistrate patiently heard both sides of the ridiculous argument. Finally, he slammed his gavel and pointed at the fisherman's wife. "You are the one who started the quarrel. You will pay a fine of eight coins!"

Meanwhile, down by the river, things had not gone well for the greedy fisherman. In his struggle to catch the massive fish, he had been pulled into the mud and lost his catch entirely. Soaked, bruised, and empty-handed, he limped back into town, only to hear the town children laughing and jeering.

He looked up to see his wife being escorted home by the Kotwal, looking utterly dejected.

"What happened?" the fisherman gasped. "Where are your clothes? What's wrong with your eyes?"

"You and your brilliant ideas," his wife sobbed, wiping the soot from her face. "I was fined eight coins for starting that quarrel!"

The fisherman clutched his head in despair. He had lost the fish, and now he had to pay a massive fine. "I could have bought four large fish with eight coins! I hope the fish my husband has caught is worth all this trouble and expense," she cried.

The fisherman groaned. "All this has happened because of our greed. We wanted to keep the whole fish to ourselves. Now we have nothing!"

docs.title: The Greedy Hawker
docs.text:
Long ago, two hawkers met near a ferry crossing, preparing to sell their wares in a nearby town. One hawker was an honest, kind-hearted man, but the other was incredibly greedy and cunning.

"How's business?" the honest hawker asked.

"Bad," the greedy hawker grumbled, eyeing the town across the river.

"Let's hope we have better luck on the other side," the honest man smiled. "Today, you take the road to the left and I'll take the one to the right."

"I don't think he has been to this town before," the greedy hawker thought to himself, grinning maliciously. "There are plenty of houses on the right, but practically none on the left. As you say," he agreed out loud, quickly grabbing the better route for himself.

In the town, the greedy hawker walked the streets shouting, "I buy anything... I sell everything. Your favourite pedlar is here!"

Inside a run-down house, a young girl pulled at her grandmother's sari. "Grandmother, why don't you sell that old bowl we have in the storeroom?"

The grandmother, hoping to buy her granddaughter a small treat, brought the heavy, soot-covered bowl to the doorway. "Pedlar, come here! What have you to offer me, old woman? What will you give my poor child in exchange for this bowl?" she asked, handing it over.

The greedy hawker took the bowl. "It's heavy," he thought, turning it over. Using a sharp needle, he stealthily scratched the bottom. A brilliant gold color shone through the grime. Let me scratch it and see... Yes, it is gold! And this foolish woman doesn't know it, he realized, his heart racing with greed.

He decided to cheat the poor family entirely. He threw the priceless golden bowl onto the ground with a disgusted sneer. "This bowl is worthless! I cannot give you anything for it. Can't you give me anything at all? Nothing! Throw that old piece of junk away." He stormed off, pretending to be angry, but secretly plotting. I'll come back tomorrow and offer her two silver coins. She'll be so desperate she'll take it!

The next day, the honest hawker happened to walk down that same street. "Pots and pans... to sell and exchange!" he called.

"Grandmother, here comes another pedlar," the little girl said hopefully.

The grandmother sighed, picking up the dirty bowl again. "Pedlar! Pedlar! Yes, mother? What can I do for you?" the honest hawker asked.

"I want to sell this bowl."

The honest hawker took the bowl and noticed the scratch at the bottom. His eyes went wide. "Mother, this is a golden bowl! Good God, I remember now! That bowl used to belong to your grandfather."

"Golden!" the grandmother gasped in shock.

"We were rich then! Oh, those were the days!" she wept happily.

"It's worth two thousand coins at least," the honest hawker said gently. "I don't have that much money. I have five hundred coins in cash and my goods, which are worth another five hundred. That's a thousand in total."

"You are an honest man," the grandmother smiled warmly. "Give us whatever you can. That's more than enough."

The deal was made. The honest hawker gave the woman five hundred coins and all his goods, leaving him with almost nothing, but happy. "Goodbye, mother. Goodbye, my little sister," he smiled, waving as he left the town. "Grandma, we are rich now," the little girl cheered.

Hours later, the greedy hawker returned, confidently striding up to the old woman's door. "Now tell me what you... pots and pans! Pots and pans!" he shouted, expecting her to beg him for a sale. "Old woman, fetch that bowl! Though it is worthless, I am in a good mood today and I'll give you two coins for it!"

"Yes, two whole coins!" he grinned, holding out the silver pieces.

The grandmother glared at him with fiery eyes. "Go away, you greedy man! I've already sold that bowl for a thousand coins. And to a very honest man."

"Y-you've sold that bowl?" the greedy hawker stammered, his face turning pale. "Oh, this is terrible."

As he staggered away from the house, the weight of his own greed crushed him. "That bowl is worth two thousand coins and that man got it for just a thousand! I could have bought it for ten yesterday. What a heavy price I've paid for my greed," he wept, tearing his hair out.

Cursing himself and his own foolishness, the greedy hawker left the town entirely empty-handed.

docs.title: Adi Shankara: The Great Philosopher
docs.text:
Over a thousand years ago, in the peaceful village of Kaladi in Kerala, there lived a pious couple named Shivaguru and Aryamba. Though deeply devoted, they carried a heavy sorrow in their hearts: they had no children.

Hoping for a miracle, they set out on a long pilgrimage to the temple of Chandramoulishwara in Trichur. After a long day of worship, they went to bed exhausted but content. That night, both of them shared the exact same vivid dream. Lord Shiva, disguised as a radiant sage, appeared before them.

"You shall be blessed with either many ordinary sons, or one extraordinary son who will not live long. You may choose," the divine vision offered.

Shivaguru and Aryamba spoke with one voice, "Revered sir, give us the one son."

Full of joy, they returned home. Months later, a child was born, radiant and perfect in all respects. On the eleventh day, the proud parents named him Shankara.

When Shankara was five years old, he was invested with the sacred thread and sent to live and learn in the house of a learned teacher, as was the custom. As part of their discipline, the young students had to go out daily to collect alms.

One day, Shankara stopped at a dilapidated hut. A poor woman stepped out, tears streaming down her face. "My son, I am ashamed... I have only an amlaka fruit to offer," she wept, handing him the small, sour fruit.

Shankara was deeply moved by her pure devotion despite her crushing poverty. "Do not grieve, mother," he said softly, closing his eyes in prayer. "May the blessings of the Divine Mother be yours."

Suddenly, the skies shimmered, and a shower of solid golden amlaka fruit rained down upon the woman's courtyard.

The brilliant Shankara quickly mastered all the scriptures and returned home, only to find that his father had passed away. Aryamba, overwhelmed with grief, fainted into his arms.

"O Shankara," she wept later, "will I ever be able to bathe in the river again?"

"Yes, mother," Shankara promised. Gathering his friends, he built a massive sand bund that forced the mighty river to change its course, bringing the water right past their home. "I am indeed blessed to have Shankara for my son," Aryamba smiled.

But while at home, Shankara was always deep in thought. He saw a fractured land. Kings at war and intolerant priests are destroying my country. This land needs unity. Spiritual unity comes first. National unity will follow, he reasoned. As a sanyasi, I shall achieve this.

Word of his immense scholarship reached King Rajashekhara of Kerala. "Come and live in my palace as my court poet," the King offered.

"I am honoured, noble king; but more pressing tasks await me," Shankara declined respectfully.

He went to his mother. "Mother, I wish to leave home and become a sanyasi. I seek your permission."

Aryamba recoiled in horror. "How can you bear to leave your mother? Besides, you must marry. No! I cannot let you go."

Shankara bowed his head. "I will not go without my mother's permission. One day she will relent. I'll wait."

He did not have to wait long. One evening, while bathing in the river, a massive crocodile clamped its jaws onto his leg and dragged him under.

"Mother! I am about to die!" Shankara shouted as he struggled against the beast. "Give me your permission. Let me die a sanyasi!"

Seeing her son in the jaws of death, Aryamba wept. "O son! What is the use of my permission now? But fulfil your wish if you must. I give you permission to become a sanyasi!"

At that exact moment, the crocodile miraculously let go and disappeared into the depths. Shankara waded to the shore. "Mother, I am safe!"

"You had to live," Aryamba realized, hugging him. "You have great tasks ahead of you."

Three days later, Shankara left home in search of a guru. "Mother, I promise, I will come back when you need me," he vowed.

He travelled northwards until he reached the banks of the Narmada river, where the great sage Govind Bhagavadpada lived in a cave.

"Come, my son," the sage welcomed him. "What are your beliefs and what conclusions have you arrived at?"

Shankara answered in ten brilliant verses. The sage was stunned. "Your mind is full of the light of knowledge," he said, ordaining the young boy as a Paramahamsa Sanyasi, the highest order.

One day, while the master was deep in a yogic trance, the Narmada river swelled into a violent flood, submerging the village and rushing toward the hermitage. Shankara calmly stepped outside, placed his wooden kamandalu (water pot) in the path of the raging waters, and commanded, "Peace! Be still, O Narmada, and recede!" Instantly, the water subsided, and the river returned to its normal course.

Recognizing his immense power, the guru eventually told him, "My son, go to Kashi and spread our philosophy of the scriptures."

At Kashi, Shankara's profound teachings drew disciples from all over. A South Indian scholar named Vishnu Sharman became his closest disciple, renamed Sanandana. A famous poet, Kalananda, was so moved by Shankara that he begged, "Pray be my refuge, Master Shankara," and was initiated as Thotakacharya.

One day, as Shankara and his disciples were returning from a bath in the Ganga, an outcaste walked toward them on the narrow path. "Move out of the way! Move! Move!" the disciples shouted.

The outcaste stopped and looked directly at Shankara. "What shall I move? My body of common clay or my soul of all-pervading consciousness?"

Shankara was struck by a divine vision. He has seen the one reality in all. He is indeed my guru, regardless of his low birth, Shankara realized, bowing to the man.

On another occasion, Shankara heard an aged teacher fiercely drilling his students on complex grammar rules: Dukrunkarana, Dukrunkarana...

Shankara stopped and gently told the teacher, "The soul is not awakened by perfecting mere book-learning. Worship the Lord, and let not the soul become numb. Bhaja Govindam, Bhaja Govindam."

Throughout his travels, Shankara fiercely debated and redefined Hinduism. "True happiness does not lie in practising mere rituals," he taught. "It lies in understanding the presence of the one reality in all."

He travelled to Haridwar and Rishikesh, where he recovered a sacred idol hidden from hill-tribes and reinstalled it. He fearlessly marched into the camps of savage tribes. "Stop! Cease your evil ways and try to seek true happiness!" he commanded. Moved by his presence, many reformed and followed him.

He climbed the harsh Himalayas to Badrinath, Kedarnath, and Gangotri. At Badrinath, he called out to his disciple Sanandana, who was on the opposite bank of a turbulent river. "Sanandana!"

"I'm coming, Gurudeva!" Sanandana yelled, and without thinking, stepped onto the raging water. Miraculously, giant lotus flowers sprang up beneath his feet, supporting his weight as he walked across the river.

"A wonder! Miraculous!" the disciples gasped.

"Lotus-footed! He is Padmapada!" Shankara smiled, renaming him for his absolute faith.

Shankara then travelled to Mahishmati to discourse with Mandana Mishra, a fierce defender of rigid rituals. Mandana's brilliant wife, Saraswati, agreed to act as the judge.

"You may begin the debate now, wise ascetic," Saraswati declared, placing a fresh garland of flowers around each scholar's neck. "As the flowers in either garland fade, I will know whose faith has faded."

For days, the intellectual battle raged. "I hold that worship and rituals make for happiness here and hereafter," Mandana argued passionately.

"Rituals do not bring the highest happiness," Shankara countered. "Complete knowledge through the Vedas is the only answer."

Gradually, the flowers around Mandana's neck wilted and died. Mandana bowed his head, accepting defeat, and was ordained as Sureshwaracharya.

Continuing his monumental journey, Shankara travelled across the breadth of India, establishing great Maths (spiritual centers) at Sringeri in the south, Dwarka in the west, Puri in the east, and Jyotirmath in the north to unify the country through his philosophy of Advaita (non-dualism).

One day, while in Sringeri, Shankara had a sudden premonition. "My mother needs me. I must hasten to her side."

He rushed back to Kaladi, arriving just in time to comfort his dying mother and impart to her the knowledge of the highest truth. She passed away peacefully.

When Shankara tried to perform her funeral rites, the orthodox Nambudiri community turned their backs on him. "What! A sanyasi to perform the rites of a householder? Never!" they sneered, refusing to help.

Undaunted, Shankara carried his mother's body to a corner of their own backyard, built a pyre of plantain stems, and cremated her himself. His immense filial love shamed the orthodox community into repentance.

At the young age of 32, having unified a fractured land and established a philosophical legacy that would endure for millennia, Adi Shankara ended his stupendous earthly mission. His teachings of the brotherhood of all humanity and the oneness of truth live on to this day.

docs.title: Harischandra: The King of Truth
docs.text:
In the era of Treta Yuga, King Harischandra ruled the grand kingdom of Ayodhya. A righteous and deeply moral king, he was loved and respected by all his subjects. His name was entirely synonymous with truth.

One day, while hunting in the dense forest, Harischandra heard a desperate cry. "Help! Help!"

Thinking it was a woman in distress, the king rushed toward the sound, breaking through the thicket. He burst into a clearing, only to find the fearsome Sage Vishwamitra sitting in deep meditation.

"What have I done?" Harischandra thought, his heart sinking. "This is Sage Vishwamitra! What can I do now?"

He bowed deeply. "I am sorry I disturbed you, sir! I heard someone crying for help!"

Vishwamitra’s eyes snapped open, blazing with anger. "It was the spirit of the sciences I am trying to master! I was about to succeed! You have spoiled everything by coming here! My curse will destroy your kingdom!"

Terrified of the sage's wrath, Harischandra pleaded, "Ask for anything and I shall give it to you!"

"Anything?" Vishwamitra challenged, raising an eyebrow.

"Yes, anything."

"All right! I shall remember your promise!" the sage declared.

A few days later, Harischandra was seated on his throne when a loud voice broke the silence of the royal court. "Harischandra!"

It was Vishwamitra. He marched down the aisle, pointing a stern finger at the king. "I have come for what you promised me! I want your kingdom!"

"My kingdom?" Harischandra gasped.

"Yes! If you fail to keep your promise, my curse will come upon Ayodhya!"

The hushed courtiers watched in absolute horror as Harischandra poured sacred water into the sage's hands. "I hand over my kingdom to you," the king said softly.

"Your majesty, how can you leave like this?" his ministers wept.

"If I break my promise, my subjects will suffer the sage's curse," Harischandra replied resolutely. Stripped of their royal robes, King Harischandra, Queen Saivya, and the young Prince Rohitaswa walked out of the city gates. The whole city came to the road to watch them go, weeping for their beloved royal family.

But as they walked away, Vishwamitra called out, "Wait!"

"Haven't you forgotten something?" the sage demanded. "You have made a gift of the kingdom, but where is the dakshina (honorarium)? With every gift given, one has to give a dakshina!"

Harischandra looked at him helplessly. "I have given away everything! I have nothing left."

"You must fulfil your promise! Your dakshina is due!" Vishwamitra insisted.

"Please give me a month!" Harischandra begged. The sage agreed.

The king, the queen, and the young prince roamed from land to land, journeying through harsh forests and scorching deserts. "One month is nearly over!" Harischandra thought in despair. "I have not been able to get any work. How shall I pay the sage?"

At last, they reached the holy temple city of Varanasi on the banks of the river Ganga. The tired family approached a grand temple. "Let us go and meet the high priest!" Harischandra suggested. "I hope I shall get some work here!"

The high priest looked at them closely. "Who are you? Your face shows marks of nobility!"

"We are weary travellers! Can you give me some work?" Harischandra asked.

"What work can a Kshatriya do in a temple? No, there is no work! But take whatever food you need," the priest offered kindly.

On the final day of the month, as the trio walked through a crowded street, Harischandra heard a familiar, terrifying voice. "Harischandra!"

It was Vishwamitra. "One month is over today!" the sage declared.

"I know, but... the day is not yet over! Please wait till nightfall," Harischandra pleaded. As Vishwamitra walked away, Harischandra's mind raced. In a few hours the sun will set! How shall I pay the dakshina?

Back at their small shelter, young Prince Rohitaswa cried, "Mother! I am very hungry! Please give me some food!"

Saivya fed him some rice, turning to her husband. "Do we have to beg for our food now?"

Harischandra looked at his family with tears in his eyes. "There is only one way to fulfil my promise! I can sell myself!"

"Oh, no! No!" Saivya wept.

But Harischandra went to the marketplace, standing on the auction block. "Who will buy me? I can do any work!" he shouted to the crowds. Man after man looked at his frail, starved body and shook their heads. "No, you look weak! Look at that frail man! What work can he do?"

I must sell myself! I must sell myself! those words beat constantly in his head.

"Nobody will buy you!" a merchant laughed. "But someone may buy your wife for house-work!"

Hearing this, Queen Saivya approached her husband. "No one will buy you! But you have something else for sale. I have given you a son! My life is fulfilled! Sell me and pay your debt!"

"What do you mean?" Harischandra choked. "Do you know what you are saying? Sell my own wife?"

"Truth is more important than human beings! You must uphold truth!" she insisted firmly. "Do you remember what the sage said?"

With a heavy heart, Harischandra led his wife to the auction platform. "Who will buy a woman for household work? My wife is for sale!" he yelled, his voice cracking.

"What kind of a man are you to sell your wife?" a disgusted onlooker spat.

"I am no man but a heartless monster! But I must do what I must do!" Harischandra replied, tears streaming down his face.

An old, wealthy Brahman stepped forward. "I will take her! She will be my wife's servant. What is her price?"

"How can a man fix the price of his wife, the mother of his son?" Harischandra whispered.

The Brahman, delighted that he could offer whatever he liked, tossed a purse. "Then take these five hundred gold coins and give up all claim on her!"

As Harischandra tied the purse to his waist, young Rohitaswa realized his mother was being taken away. "Mother! Mother!" he screamed, running after her.

"Please let me take him with me!" Saivya begged the Brahman. "He can't live without me!"

The Brahman shrugged. "Here's another two-hundred and fifty! I shall buy the boy as well!"

Harischandra stood completely alone, watching helplessly as his wife and son were dragged away. "I've nothing else to live for now except to pay my debt!" he sobbed.

Vishwamitra appeared right on cue. "Have you got the money? The day is almost over!"

Harischandra handed over the purse of seven-hundred and fifty coins. Vishwamitra counted it and frowned. "What is this? This is not enough! You have to pay me two-hundred and fifty more!"

"But I've nothing else left!"

"Get it somehow. I shall come at nightfall!" the sage demanded, walking away.

Driven by desperation, Harischandra returned to the market. "Who will buy a man as his slave?" he yelled.

The people recognized him and laughed cruelly. "You sold your wife! Is your need for money so great? You will be a burden to the one that buys you!"

Just then, a dark, terrifying man with a skull in one hand and a staff in the other stepped through the crowd. He was a Chandala, a keeper of the cremation grounds—the lowest, most impure caste in the kingdom.

"I am strong!" Harischandra pleaded to the man. "It's only that I've not eaten for a few days! Who will buy me?"

"I shall buy you, if you wish to be bought!" the Chandala grunted. "You'll have to pay me two hundred and fifty gold coins!" Harischandra blurted out.

"All right! Follow me!" the Chandala agreed.

Harischandra paid off Vishwamitra and followed his new master to the dark, smoky, and terrifying cremation grounds. "What work do I have to do?" Harischandra asked.

"Whatever work a Chandala does!" his master sneered. "Your duty is to wait on cremators and demand the cloth, rice and money. A sixth part goes to the king, three to me and the rest will be yours!"

Harischandra’s days passed in a living nightmare, collecting money from grieving families and watching corpses burn. The heat and smoke shrivelled his skin, turning him into a skeletal, unrecognizable figure.

Meanwhile, Queen Saivya was forced to work day and night in the cruel Brahman's household. One evening, her son came to her weeping. "The Brahman beat me! I was playing in his room!"

"Go to the garden! You can play there!" she consoled him.

But when the queen went to fetch him after her chores, she couldn't find him. Holding a lamp, she searched the dark garden until she stumbled upon a horrific sight. Young Rohitaswa lay cold and lifeless under a tree. "He is cold! What are these marks? Oh, my son! A serpent has bitten him!" she screamed.

Her eyes filled with tears, the Queen carried the dead child to the Brahman. "My son is dead. Please give me some money for his funeral!"

"Money? Isn't it enough that we feed you?" the Brahman yelled, slamming the door.

Carrying her son's lifeless body, Saivya walked alone through the deserted streets to the cremation ground. At the gates, a bearded, shrivelled Chandala blocked her way.

"What do you want? Have you brought the rice and cash?" he demanded gruffly.

"No!" Saivya wept, her face hidden by a veil.

The Chandala looked down at the dead boy. Suddenly, his heart began to pound. The child's face bears the mark of royalty! Who is he? Why is my heart beating so fast?

"He is indeed a royal child!" the Chandala gasped. "His father was Harischandra!"

Saivya looked up in shock. "What did you say? My son! My son!" She peered closely at the Chandala's dirty, ruined face. "Why did you cry out! Let me look at your face... My husband!"

Harischandra fell to his knees. "Yes! I'm that wretched man! But... you will have to bring the cloth and rice!"

Saivya stared at him in disbelief. "My husband who was a king is now a Chandala? You are asking the payment for your son's funeral?"

"I have no choice!" Harischandra wept. "I cannot deprive my master of his dues!"

"But how can I pay you?" she sobbed.

Harischandra choked back his tears, firmly adhering to his duty. "Give me half of your upper garment!"

"Yes! It is my duty to my master!" Saivya agreed, reaching to tear her only piece of clothing.

Suddenly, a heavy hand grabbed Harischandra's arm, stopping him. Harischandra turned around. It was his master, the Chandala.

"Harischandra! Your test is over!" the Chandala smiled.

Before Harischandra could speak, the dark sky erupted with blinding light. The Devas (heavenly beings) descended from the clouds, led by Lord Indra.

"Harischandra! We were testing your devotion to truth! You have passed the supreme test," Indra declared.

"But, my lord, did you have to test me by taking away my only child?" Harischandra cried.

Indra simply smiled and pointed. Harischandra turned to see young Rohitaswa standing there, rubbing his eyes and smiling, completely alive and well.

Sage Vishwamitra stepped out from the crowd of Devas. "Harischandra! My friend, forgive me for testing you so harshly!"

"But how can I go back? I have sold myself to the Chandala," Harischandra said, ever mindful of his promise.

The Chandala stepped forward, his dark form shimmering until he transformed into Yama, the God of Virtue and Death. "No Harischandra! You are a free man! The Chandala was Yama himself. You have proved your virtue! You have earned your place in heaven!"

"I cannot go to heaven without my subjects!" Harischandra insisted.

"Your subjects will get what they deserve!" Yama argued.

"If so, then let me share my blessings with them on the earth!" Harischandra requested humbly.

"You are truly a great king! Your request will be granted!" Indra proclaimed.

Word reached Ayodhya that their beloved king was returning. The streets were lined with weeping, joyous crowds. Riding a magnificent chariot alongside his queen, with young Rohitaswa leading the procession on horseback, King Harischandra returned to his throne, his name forever etched in history as the ultimate symbol of unwavering truth.

docs.title: Chhatrasal: The Founder of Bundelkhand
docs.text:
In a dense, darkening jungle, a young boy sat alone under a tree, sad and forlorn.

An old man passing by stopped in surprise. "Who are you, son? What are you doing here in this jungle?"

The boy looked up with tear-filled eyes. "I am Chhatrasal, the younger son of Champatrai, the Bundela warrior. My parents are dead."

"I am Mahabali, the oil man," the kind stranger said. "I was once their servant. I am sorry to hear that. What happened? How did they die?"

"There was a surprise attack by the Mughal troops. They were completely surrounded," Chhatrasal explained, his voice hardening. "They preferred death to the humiliation of being captured, and stabbed each other. I escaped. I will avenge the fate of my parents."

Mahabali took the boy in. "Come home with me. Spend the day at my place. Rest for a while before you proceed to your uncle's village."

After a brief rest, Chhatrasal rode out. On the way, he spotted two Mughal soldiers mercilessly harassing innocent devotees outside a temple. Blind with rage, young Chhatrasal charged his horse forward, his sword flashing in the sun, and killed both soldiers instantly.

The crowd cheered. "Who are you?" they asked.

"I am Chhatrasal, a son of the Bundela warrior Champatrai!"

"Long live Chhatrasal! Glory be to Chhatrasal!" the people roared.

But in the nearby village, his uncle Sujanrai was pacing worriedly. "No word from Chhatrasal. His whereabouts... could the Mughals have captured him?"

Suddenly, a guard announced, "Sir, look! Someone is coming."

Chhatrasal rode in, safe and sound. "You—Chhatrasal! Thank God you are safe," his uncle gasped in relief. "Why did you take so long?"

"I had to pass through Mughal territory," Chhatrasal replied grimly. "My parents must be avenged. I must found a Bundela kingdom."

"A worthy ambition," Sujanrai nodded. "But it is not easy to fight the Mughal armies. You must first learn the art of warfare."

Sujanrai arranged for Chhatrasal to be trained by Amar Singh, the commander of their forces. For four years, Chhatrasal trained rigorously in swordsmanship, archery, and strategy.

"We are now ready, equipped to fight the Mughals," Chhatrasal told his commander. "But my uncle does not seem to be very enthusiastic." Hearing that his elder brother Angadrai was gathering forces elsewhere, Chhatrasal left without telling his uncle.

"I am bent on avenging our parents' death and establishing a Bundela kingdom," Chhatrasal told his brother. "I do not want to delay anymore."

Angadrai was hesitant. It would take time to prepare our forces, but Chhatrasal is getting impatient, he thought. Ah! That's it. I'll get him married. It will divert him.

Soon enough, Chhatrasal was married to Devkunwar, the daughter of a feudal lord. But the fire in Chhatrasal's heart had not died down.

"We have already wasted quite some time. We must now get busy," Chhatrasal insisted to his brother. "Before going to war, we have to collect enough funds and forces, and also study the war strategy of the Mughals."

"How is it possible for us to study their strategy?" Angadrai asked.

"Let us go to Raja Jaisingh. He is the chief of the Mughal forces," Chhatrasal suggested. "But remember, no one should guess our real intentions."

They travelled to the Mughal camp and presented themselves. "What? You, the sons of Champatrai, here to enlist in the Mughal army? Couldn't you get any other job?" the officers mocked.

"We are warriors. You are a warrior, too. We came in the hope that you would appropriately honour us," Angadrai replied smoothly.

"Champatrai was no doubt a brave warrior, but he was not a friend of the Mughals," the officer warned.

"We are here to make amends. Give us a chance," Chhatrasal said.

They were accepted into the army under the command of Bahadur Khan. Soon, Kurmamal, the ruler of Deogarh, rose in rebellion against the empire. The Mughal army, with the Bundela brothers in its ranks, marched to suppress it.

"The fort is strong indeed. A deep moat on three sides... and just one gate, closed and well-guarded," Bahadur Khan observed. "But there must be some entrance at the back."

That night, Chhatrasal scouted the fort walls. Spotting a faint light bleeding through a crack across the moat, he realized it was a secret entrance. The next morning, he led a swift, stealthy strike through the moat, breaching the secret entrance and throwing the gates open for the main army.

The Mughal army easily conquered Deogarh. Bahadur Khan was thrilled. "The emperor has sent me his congratulations. He has called us to Delhi. You too will come."

"Why should I? Deogarh was won by you," Chhatrasal deferred.

"No Chhatrasal, it was won by you. I will not take any undue credit," the honest commander insisted. "I will convince the emperor that such brave men should be honoured and befriended—not antagonised."

In the grand court of Delhi, Emperor Aurangzeb sat on his throne. "Bahadur Khan, I congratulate you on your victory over Deogarh."

"If it may please your majesty, congratulate Champatrai's son, Chhatrasal," Bahadur Khan corrected him respectfully. "It was he who won Deogarh."

Aurangzeb's eyes narrowed maliciously. "O! The son of Champatrai should be happy that I have not ordered him beheaded. Arrest him!"

Before the guards could move, Chhatrasal drew his sword and slashed his way out of the court. "The one to arrest Chhatrasal is yet to be born!" he yelled, escaping on his horse.

Realizing the Mughals could never be trusted, Chhatrasal headed south to meet the legendary Maratha leader, Chhatrapati Shivaji.

"I want to establish an independent kingdom. How shall I go about it?" Chhatrasal asked the great king.

"The Mughal army is strong in numbers but it always fails to withstand surprise attacks," Shivaji advised wisely. He pointed to a portrait of his own guru, Swami Ramdas. "He is my patron saint. I draw my strength and inspiration from him."

Shivaji presented Chhatrasal with a magnificent sword. "I am sure you will never bring dishonour to this gift of mine."

Inspired, Chhatrasal returned to Bundelkhand. The local leaders rallied behind him. "Maharaj Champatrai was our leader. We are indebted to him. We will do whatever you command us to do," they swore.

"If we rise in rebellion we may have to pay for it—probably with our lives," Chhatrasal warned.

"We will! We will!" the men roared.

Despite the support, Chhatrasal was overwhelmed. His army was tiny and poor. Wandering the forest one night, he prayed, "I need guidance. I wish I had a guru."

As if in answer, he spotted a light in the uninhabited forest and found a sage sitting by a fire. "I've been waiting for you, Chhatrasal," the sage, Pran Nath, smiled.

"Amazing! I've never met him, yet he knows me," Chhatrasal thought, falling to his knees. "I am desperate for a guru. Sir, I need guidance... I wish to fight the Mughals, but to raise an army, I need plenty of money. What shall I do?"

"That is my concern, not yours," Pran Nath declared. "Tonight, on your horse, you will cover as wide an area as you can. And all that region will produce diamonds. Go with my blessings."

Empowered by the sage's incredible blessing, Chhatrasal soon possessed immense wealth. Men enlisted in his army by the hundreds, and they began liberating areas of Bundelkhand.

Furious, Aurangzeb dispatched a massive force under Syed Bahadur. The Mughals surrounded Chhatrasal's camp in the jungle. But Chhatrasal was prepared. His trusted lieutenant, Nathoo Singh, slipped through the enemy cordon at night.

"Welcome back, Nathoo Singh. Our men are ready. They await your signal," Chhatrasal whispered the next morning.

As the unsuspecting Mughals closed in on the camp, Nathoo Singh's forces screamed, "Har Har Mahadev!" and attacked brutally from the rear. Caught in a deadly trap, the Mughal army was completely routed.

Aurangzeb sent another massive force under Ruhalla Khan. "Don't lose courage," Chhatrasal told his generals. "This will be a battle of wits."

Chhatrasal feigned a retreat, hiding his troops in the hills. "Tomorrow morning we will attack," the Mughal commander laughed in his tent, watching dancers perform.

Suddenly, a sentry rushed in. "Run... run... Chhatrasal has attacked! That's a big battalion carrying torches!"

The Mughals scrambled out in a panic, only to realize the "torches" were tied to the horns of a herd of cattle storming down the hill. While the Mughals were distracted by the stampede, Chhatrasal's real forces struck from behind, claiming another stunning victory.

Chhatrasal continued his run of victories, officially establishing his capital in Panna. But years later, an enormous Mughal army under Mohammad Khan Bangas descended upon them.

"Bangas is getting the better of us," a general reported. "He has lost only 4,000 men to our 12,000."

Realizing his kingdom was on the brink of collapse, the aging Chhatrasal wrote a desperate letter to the Maratha Peshwa, Baji Rao. "As the elephant was overpowered by the crocodile, so are the Bundelas losing out today. Save us, Baji Rao."

Baji Rao's massive army arrived just in time. Together, the Marathas and Bundelas besieged Bangas in the fort of Jaitpur. With their supply lines cut, the Mughals began starving. "Kill the camels and horses and eat them... if that's not enough, eat one another!" Bangas yelled in despair, before finally surrendering unconditionally.

To commemorate the ultimate victory, Chhatrasal ordered grand celebrations. He ruled Bundelkhand until a ripe old age, bringing an era of deep peace, justice, and prosperity to Central India. The grateful people of Bundelkhand cherish his memory and his magnificent sword to this day.

docs.title: The Monkey King's Sacrifice
docs.text:
Deep in the heart of a lush forest, a large tribe of monkeys lived happily on a massive, sprawling mango tree that grew right on the bank of a great river. The tree bore the sweetest, most delicious mangoes in the world, and the monkeys feasted on them every day.

But their wise leader, the Monkey King, gathered them one day with a worried frown. "Friends! We have been living happily on this mango tree for years. But I anticipate trouble soon."

"Why, master?" one of the monkeys asked.

"Men have come to live near our forest," the King explained. "They have never tasted the mango fruit. Once they do, it will be dangerous for us. Make sure that not a single fruit falls into the river! Pluck each and every bud from the branches that spread out over the water."

The monkeys did their best to carefully pluck the buds. But despite all their care, one juicy, ripe mango slipped through the leaves. With a soft splat, it fell into the river and was carried away by the current.

Miles downstream, a fisherman was pulling in his net when he saw the bright golden fruit. "Look! See what I have found among the fish. A strange fruit. It looks delicious!" he showed his wife.

"I have never seen a fruit like that before," she marveled. "I think we ought to take it to the King. He will be overjoyed."

They presented the strange fruit to the King. The King immediately sent for his chief forester. "He will tell us what fruit it is."

The forester examined it closely. "That's the rare mango, sir."

"Is it poisonous?" the King asked cautiously.

"Not at all, sir. In fact, it is very tasty."

The King took a small bite, and his eyes lit up. "Hm-m-m! Delicious! It smells good, too! Tell me, forester, where does this mango fruit grow?"

"There is a mango grove, just a little upstream, sir."

The greedy King immediately turned to his minister. "Make the necessary arrangements. We are leaving for that grove. And I hope you have brought some archers along."

The royal party sailed upstream until they found the magnificent mango tree. The King stepped off his boat and smiled. "Tell everyone to eat as much as they can. We will stay here for a day or two."

As night fell, the monkeys, who had been hiding in terror, began moving about the branches to find food. Hearing the rustling, the King pointed up. "Minister, what was that?"

"Just monkeys, sir, scampering among the branches."

The King licked his lips. "When it's morning, tell the archers to shoot every single monkey. Today, along with the mangoes, we shall eat monkey's flesh!"

At the very top of the tree, the terrified monkeys huddled around their leader. "Oh master, we're trapped. What are we to do now?"

"Don't panic. I'll find a way out," the Monkey King reassured them. "Now, all of you do as I tell you."

He grabbed a thick, long creeper vine, tying one end securely to a sturdy branch and the other end around his own waist. "Now I will swing over the river to that fig tree on the opposite bank. Once I am secure, one by one, you can climb across my back to the other side."

He leaped across the rushing river. But as he grasped for the fig tree, he realized his mistake. Oh no! The vine is too short!

Unable to reach the trunk, he desperately grabbed the very end of a leafy branch, stretching his body taut over the rushing water to bridge the gap.

"What are you waiting for? Cross over my back," the King strained, holding on with all his might.

The monkeys quickly hurried across his stretched body. "You are very kind, master," they wept as they reached safety.

The King of men woke up and watched the spectacle in utter astonishment. "Look at that monkey king. How he must love his subjects."

But the very last monkey in the tribe was a wicked, bitter monkey who had always hated his leader. Seeing the King exhausted and stretched to his limit, the wicked monkey smiled maliciously. Here is my chance to take revenge. I shall jump across and push him down.

"Come on. Please hurry," the King groaned. "I am tired. I can barely hold on any longer."

"Don't worry," the wicked monkey sneered, climbing onto the King's back. "I shall soon give you everlasting peace!" With a vicious shriek, he leaped into the air and stomped down on the King's back with all his might.

SNAP!

The vine broke under the sudden force. The Monkey King plummeted toward the river, crashing violently onto a rock below with a sickening THUD, breaking his jaw and back.

On the opposite bank, the King of men was horrified. "Come on. Let us help the poor fellow!" he ordered his guards.

They gently lifted the dying Monkey King onto the riverbank. The human King knelt beside him in awe. "You saved them at the cost of your own life!"

The Monkey King smiled faintly, his breathing shallow. "I don't mind. I have done my duty. I am happy that my subjects are safe."

With those final words, the noble King passed away, leaving the human King forever changed by the profound lesson of true, selfless leadership.

docs.title: The Stupid Crocodile and the Monkey
docs.text:
In a dense jungle, near a rushing river, lived a clever little monkey. Right in the middle of the river was a small, lush island where plenty of delicious fruit grew. Whenever the monkey felt hungry, he would go straight to the island.

To cross the rushing water, the monkey had a perfect system. He would jump from the riverbank onto a large, smooth rock jutting out of the water, and from there, leap safely onto the island.

"Breakfast, here I come!" he would cheer every morning.

Not far away, beneath the murky waters, lived a crocodile and his wife. The crocodile's wife had been watching the monkey jump back and forth every day.

"You know dear, I've got it!" she told her husband one afternoon. "I know how we can catch that little monkey for our dinner."

The crocodile groaned. "Oh please! Not again. We've tried it too many times before. Let us be happy with the fish I catch."

"This time my plan won't fail. I promise," she insisted, swimming toward the monkey's favorite rock. "You see that rock there? The monkey uses it to reach the island. When he's on the island, busy eating his breakfast, you lie on that rock. When he has to return, he will think you're the rock. As soon as he jumps on you, kill him!"

The crocodile smiled. "Hmm! The plan sounds good."

The next day, as usual, the monkey jumped onto the rock and over to the island. While he was busy stuffing his cheeks with fruit, the massive crocodile quietly climbed onto the rock and waited.

When the monkey had eaten his fill, he walked to the edge of the island to jump back. He stopped. "Hey! That's strange! That rock looks bigger today," he observed closely. "The river has not gone dry for it to look bigger. I'm sure it is that stupid crocodile, up to his tricks again. I won't jump till I'm sure."

The clever monkey cupped his hands around his mouth. "Hello rock! How are you today, my friend?"

There was only silence.

The monkey sighed loudly. "Why aren't you speaking to me today, rock? Have I annoyed you? It looks as if this rock used to talk to him!"

Under the water, the stupid crocodile panicked. I'd better reply, he thought. Rising slightly, he croaked, "What is it, monkey?"

"You look rather big today, rock," the monkey giggled.

"I'm not a rock," the crocodile growled, realizing his cover was blown. "I'm a crocodile and I'm going to kill you for my dinner."

I was right. It is him. Now I'm in real trouble, the monkey thought, his mind racing. Must think fast. Wait... I've got it. I've seen him eating many times before. Whenever he opens his mouth wide, his eyes always close.

"I'll tell you what, crocodile," the monkey yelled out. "You open your mouth wide, and I'll jump right into it from here!"

The stupid crocodile was thrilled. "Good! This is going to save us both a lot of trouble."

"Okay, get ready now," the monkey said. "Open wide!"

The crocodile opened his massive, terrifying jaws as wide as he could. Just as the monkey had observed, the crocodile's eyes squeezed tightly shut.

"Take that!" the monkey yelled. He leaped through the air, but instead of landing in the jaws, he stomped his feet down hard on the top of the crocodile's snout with a heavy THUD. Using the crocodile's head as a springboard, the monkey vaulted safely onto the riverbank.

The crocodile opened his eyes, dizzy from the blow. "What was that? What are you doing over there?" he sputtered. "You promised you would jump into my mouth!"

The monkey laughed from the safety of the trees. "Did you really believe I'd do a stupid thing like that?"

"Grr! Wait till I get my teeth into you," the crocodile thrashed angrily.

"You never will, crocodile," the monkey grinned. "You may be big and strong... but you are stupid!"

docs.title: The Demon Outwitted
docs.text:
Long ago, a large band of monkeys came and settled on the outskirts of a dense, mysterious forest. Their wise leader, who knew the dangers of the place well, called a meeting.

"Attention, all of you! I have something important to say," the leader warned. "There are certain poisonous trees with very tempting fruit, and one of the lakes is haunted by a dangerous demon. You will have to be careful about two things in this forest: You must not drink water or eat any fruit without asking me first."

The monkeys nodded, promising to obey.

However, one hot day, while searching for firewood, the monkeys wandered deep into the forest. Exhausted and parched, a young monkey cried, "Mother, I'm thirsty."

"Ah! There is a lake close by," the mother noticed.

The thirsty monkeys started running toward the sparkling water. Suddenly, an older monkey stood in their way. "Wait! Don't go near the water," he shouted. "Don't you remember our leader's warning?"

"Oh dear! I'd completely forgotten," the mother gasped, pulling her child back. "Thank you for reminding me. All right! Let us wait till our leader comes."

A little later, the leader arrived to find his troop sitting patiently by the shore. "What's the matter? You seem to have a problem," he asked.

"Master, we are very thirsty," they explained. "We are waiting for you to let us know if this lake is safe."

"That was very wise of you," the leader praised them. "Now let me examine the lake."

The wise leader walked carefully around the edge of the water, studying the muddy banks. "Hmm! All the footsteps lead into the lake," he muttered to himself, tracing the prints. "But not a single one leads out of it."

He returned to the group, his face pale. "No! This lake is haunted!"

"Oh dear! What shall we do? I'm very thirsty. I must have some water," the monkeys panicked.

"Let me think in peace," the leader said, crossing his arms.

Suddenly, the water bubbled and churned. With a terrifying WHA-SPLASH, a massive, hideous demon burst from the surface, roaring loudly. "What are you doing near my lake?"

"Are you the demon of this lake?" the monkey leader asked bravely.

"Yes! And I eat all those who dare enter my lake!" the demon roared, his fangs dripping.

"In that case, we will not enter the lake," the leader replied calmly.

The demon laughed a booming, cruel laugh. "And let your herd die of thirst?"

"Oh no! We won't die. We will drink the water from your lake," the monkey leader smiled confidently.

"What??" the demon scoffed.

"Yes we will. Without entering it."

"And how, may I ask?" the demon mocked.

"Wait and see," the leader said. He turned to his troop. "Come on, boys. Follow me. Collect all the bamboo reeds you can."

The monkeys rushed into the nearby grove, gathering armfuls of long, sturdy bamboo.

"Hm.. M.. M... This one's fine," the leader inspected one. "Absolutely hollow."

Working quickly, the leader joined a number of the hollow reeds together, creating one incredibly long, continuous pipe.

"Now I'll slide that end into the water," the leader instructed, dropping one end into the lake. "Now what?" a monkey asked.

"I'll suck the water from this end," the leader said.

With all his might, the leader took a deep breath and sucked on the giant reed. SKURRRRR! The water shot up the pipe and gushed out in a thick, glorious stream over the riverbank.

"Water! Delicious water! And all for us!" the monkeys cheered, splashing and drinking happily, completely safe on dry land.

Full of rage, the outwitted demon stomped back into the depths of the lake, defeated by a simple bamboo reed. The monkeys, their thirst well quenched, laughed and returned home.

docs.title: The Monkey's Heart
docs.text:
There once lived a plump, happy monkey in the trees along the bank of a river. What the monkey didn't know was that a little downstream, a crocodile and his wife were watching him closely.

"Have you seen that plump monkey upstream?" the crocodile's wife drooled over her cooking pot. "I'm longing to eat his heart."

"I would love to get it for you," her husband sighed. "But he lives up in the trees. How can I catch him?"

"You are a crocodile, aren't you? Use your cunning!" his wife scolded.

The crocodile rubbed his chin. "Humm! Let's see. My idea might just work."

The next morning, the crocodile swam upstream to the monkey's favorite tree. "Hello monkey! Aren't you sick of eating the fruit of the same tree every day?"

The monkey chewed on a banana thoughtfully. "Hmm! I guess so."

"Why don't you go across to the opposite bank?" the crocodile suggested sweetly. "There are some delicious fruits there."

"But how shall I get across?" the monkey asked. "I can't swim."

"Oh, that's simple," the crocodile smiled, exposing rows of sharp teeth. "You can ride on my back."

"You are a friend indeed. Let's go," the trusting monkey cheered, leaping down onto the crocodile's scaly back. "This is an excellent idea. Why didn't we think of it before?"

But as soon as they were completely surrounded by deep water in the middle of the river, the crocodile suddenly dove downward, plunging the monkey into the freezing current.

"Hey! Are you trying to drown me?" the monkey coughed, scrambling to stay above water.

"Not at all," the crocodile sneered. "For I'm going to kill you."

"But why? What have I done?" the terrified monkey cried.

"Nothing. But my wife wants to eat your heart. So you die."

The monkey clung to the crocodile's back, his mind racing. I'd better think fast if I love my life.

Suddenly, the monkey burst into a fit of uproarious laughter. "Ha! Ha! Ha! Ho! Ho! Hee! Heee!"

"Stop that," the crocodile snapped. "What's so funny about dying?"

"The joke's on you, crocodile," the monkey snickered. "You may kill me. But you will never get my heart."

"What do you mean?" the confused crocodile asked.

"We monkeys hide our hearts in safe places before we venture out!" the monkey lied smoothly.

"Really?" the crocodile gasped. "How was I to know?"

"Never mind," the monkey sighed playfully. "Turn back. Since you were so kind to me, I'll show you where my heart is. Can you see that tall, big tree on the bank where I live?"

"Of course I can," the crocodile said, turning around.

"I hide it in the topmost branch of that tree," the monkey said. "Take me back and I'll give it to you."

As soon as the crocodile bumped against the muddy riverbank, the monkey leaped off his back like lightning, scrambling up the trunk of his tree until he was safe in the highest branches.

"Now I'd better act fast," the monkey panted, safely out of reach.

He looked down at the waiting crocodile and burst into laughter once again. "You poor fool. If my heart were removed from my body I'd be dead! It's safe inside me, and you'll never lay your hands on it!"

The defeated crocodile slumped back into the water, cursing his own foolishness, while the clever monkey enjoyed his bananas in peace.

docs.title: The Monkeys and the Gardener
docs.text:
The King of Varanasi had a beautiful, sprawling garden, which was meticulously looked after by a deeply loyal gardener.

One night, a friend came to visit the gardener at his small hut. "Hello friend. The great festival in town begins tomorrow."

"I know!" the gardener sighed, rubbing his tired eyes.

"What! Aren't you coming? There's going to be plenty of fun," his friend asked in surprise.

"How I wish I could come!" the gardener said sadly. "But if I do, who will water the garden? It will die without me."

The next day, as the gardener was sullenly digging around a rosebush, a friendly monkey swung down from the trees. "Why do you look so sad, gardener?"

"There's a festival on in town. I want to go to it. But who will water my garden?" the gardener explained miserably.

"Pooh! That's no problem," the monkey laughed. "My friends and I will water the garden for you."

The gardener looked up, amazed. "You will? Thank you, dear monkey. I'll return in a few days, as soon as possible. Have a fine time, friend!"

Thrilled, the monkey gathered the gardener's watering cans. "Now to round up all my friends!"

Soon, a large troop of monkeys arrived. "Come on, my monkeys," their leader cheered. "We have work to do. Very important work! The gardener wants us to water his garden while he is away. Right away! Go get the watering cans and buckets."

The monkeys eagerly grabbed the tools. But as they stood before the massive garden, one monkey scratched his head. "Some trees are small and some are big! How much water should we give to each tree?"

"That's no problem at all," the confident leader announced. "All you have to do is pull up each plant and have a look at the size of its roots!"

"And then?"

"You pour less water for the smaller roots, and more water for the bigger roots!"

The troop gasped in admiration. "You are really clever! What a wonderful idea. Oh, it was quite simple really," the leader boasted.

With great enthusiasm, the monkeys spread out across the garden. Grunting and straining, they began ruthlessly uprooting every single beautiful plant, flower, and bush, checking the roots, pouring a splash of water into the empty holes, and tossing the ruined plants aside.

Meanwhile, in town, the gardener was enjoying the festival music. I wonder how my monkey friends are getting along with their task? he thought happily.

Two days later, the gardener returned home. He stopped dead in his tracks, dropping his turban in sheer horror. The magnificent royal garden was completely destroyed. Thousands of uprooted, dead plants littered the muddy ground.

"What have you done?" he screamed at the smiling monkeys.

"We watered each plant according to the needs of its roots! That's right. We did," the monkeys nodded proudly, holding up the dead bushes.

The gardener collapsed onto a bucket, burying his face in his hands. "Whatever shall I do now? What shall I tell the King? That's what comes of depending on a fool. He may mean well... but will end up doing more harm than anything else."

docs.title: Draupadi: The Queen of the Pandavas
docs.text:
King Drupada of Panchala performed a grand yagna (fire sacrifice) to obtain a mighty son who could defeat his enemies. As the flames of the sacrificial fire leapt high, an excellent, handsome, and armor-clad youth sprang from the fire. "Let the boy be called Dhrishtadyumna," the Brahmans declared.

But then, to the amazement of all, there arose from the same fire a breathtakingly beautiful, dark-skinned maiden, wafting the fragrance of a blue lotus in bloom.

"This girl is an exceptional woman," a celestial voice echoed. "She will bring about the destruction of the Kauravas."

She was named Krishnaa, but the world would come to know her as Draupadi, the daughter of Drupada.

From the moment she was born, one thought obsessed King Drupada: her marriage. Arjuna, the valiant Pandava, would have been an ideal husband for my daughter, the King thought sadly. If only he, his mother, and his brothers had survived that fateful fire at Varanavata.

Hoping to find a peerless archer, Drupada had a heavy, massive bow crafted. "I shall have a heavy bow made which only Arjuna or his peer can wield," he declared. "Then a target to challenge an ace archer like him. He who hits the mark shall wed my sister," his son Dhrishtadyumna announced.

Unbeknownst to Drupada, the Pandavas and their mother Kunti had survived the fire and were living in hiding at Ekachakrapura, disguised as humble Brahmans. Hearing of the grand swayamvara (marriage contest), the brothers were eager to attend.

On their way to Panchala, the Pandavas met the great Sage Vyasa. He told them a secret of Draupadi's past. "In an earlier birth, she was the daughter of a rishi. Fated to remain unmarried, she performed severe penances to Lord Shiva. When Shiva appeared, she was so overwhelmed with eagerness that she repeated her request five times: 'An accomplished husband... an accomplished husband...'"

Vyasa smiled. "Shiva granted her wish: 'So be it. You shall have five husbands from among the Bharata princes.' When the girl was confounded, Shiva explained that his words could not be revoked, but the event would be postponed to her next life. You, the five Pandavas, are the five husbands Draupadi is destined to have."

At the swayamvara, princes and kings from all over India tried and failed to string the massive bow. Even the great warrior Karna stepped forward. It's Karna! We may consider the mark as already hit, the Pandavas thought in dismay. But as Karna strung the bow and took aim, Draupadi stood up gracefully.

"I will never marry one of the suta (charioteer) caste!" she announced firmly. Insulted, Karna threw down the bow and walked away.

Finally, Arjuna, dressed as a humble Brahman, stepped forward. With noble confidence, he effortlessly strung the bow and hit the target. Full of joy, Draupadi stepped forward and garlanded the handsome Brahman.

The brothers returned to the potter's house where they were staying. "Look mother! See what we have brought today!" they called out from the door.

Kunti, busy with her prayers and thinking they had brought alms, replied without looking. "Whatever it is, share it equally and enjoy it."

When she came out and saw Draupadi, she gasped. "Alas! Yudhishthira, what have I said? Arjuna won her. It is proper that he marry her."

"No, mother. And commit the sin of ignoring my mother's orders?" Yudhishthira insisted. Knowing it was destined by the gods, he declared, "Then Draupadi shall be a wife to all of us."

When the news reached Hastinapura that the Pandavas were alive and allied with the powerful King Drupada, the Kuru elders advised King Dhritarashtra to be just. He invited the Pandavas back and granted them half the kingdom. They built a glorious new capital at Khandavaprastha (Indraprastha) and ruled virtuously.

But Duryodhana, burning with envy, plotted with his wicked uncle, Shakuni. "Yudhishthira is an addict to the game of dice, and I am a master at cheating," Shakuni smiled. "We will invite him, and we will win."

Bound by royal honor, Yudhishthira accepted the challenge. As Shakuni rolled his loaded dice, Yudhishthira lost his wealth, his kingdom, his brothers, and finally himself.

"O King, there is still one dear stake you hold on to," Shakuni sneered. "Draupadi. Stake her and win yourself back."

In a tragic lapse of judgment, Yudhishthira agreed, and lost again. A jubilant Duryodhana sent a messenger to fetch Draupadi. When she refused to come, realizing the illegality of a slave staking his wife, Duryodhana ordered his brutal brother Duhshasana to drag her in.

Duhshasana broke into her chamber, grabbed her by her long, beautiful hair, and dragged the weeping Queen into the royal assembly.

"The Kuru elders have lost their reason!" Draupadi challenged the silent kings. "Why else do they look silently on this great crime?" She looked desperately at her husbands, but Yudhishthira cast his eyes down in shame.

"The very clothes she is wearing belong to us! She is our slave," Duhshasana laughed, moving to strip her of her sari.

"O Krishna, save me!" Draupadi prayed, closing her eyes.

A miracle occurred. As Duhshasana pulled at her sari, endless yards of exquisite silk materialized, constantly keeping her covered. Frustrated and exhausted by the miles of cloth, Duhshasana finally collapsed.

Quivering with rage, Bheema swore a terrifying oath. "I shall, in the battlefield, tear open the breast of this villain... and drink his life blood!"

Frightened by evil omens that suddenly echoed through the hall, Dhritarashtra intervened. He granted Draupadi boons, allowing her to free her husbands from slavery. But the cruel game of dice resumed. The Pandavas lost again, and were sentenced to twelve years of exile in the forest, followed by a thirteenth year in disguise.

Before leaving, an enraged Draupadi vowed, "Thirteen years hence, the wives of those villains will languish in desolate widowhood! I will know no peace till his hands are ripped off."

Life in the forest was harsh. To aid Draupadi in feeding the many sages who visited them, the Sun God Surya gave her the Akshaya Patra—a magical vessel that provided inexhaustible food until Draupadi herself had eaten.

Duryodhana, seeking to humiliate them further, intentionally sent the incredibly ill-tempered Sage Durvasa and his ten thousand disciples to visit the Pandavas after Draupadi had finished her meal. Knowing she had no food left, Draupadi prayed to Lord Krishna.
