Krishna suddenly appeared. "Draupadi, I am hungry. Fetch the vessel." He found a single grain of rice and a scrap of vegetable stuck to the side, and ate it. "May the soul of the universe be satiated with it."

Instantly, down at the river, Sage Durvasa and his ten thousand disciples suddenly felt painfully full, as if they had eaten a massive feast. Embarrassed that they could not eat the meal they requested, they quietly fled the forest, and Draupadi was saved from the sage's curse.

For their thirteenth year in disguise, the Pandavas and Draupadi served in the court of King Virata. Draupadi worked as a maid, Sairandhri, to Queen Sudheshna.

Ten months passed peacefully until Keechaka, the Queen's powerful and arrogant brother, became smitten with Draupadi. Despite her warnings that she was protected by invisible Gandharvas, he continually harassed her. When the Queen sent her to his room for wine, he chased her and kicked her brutally in the royal court.

That night, a weeping Draupadi went to Bheema. "I will kill Keechaka this very night," Bheema promised, his eyes blazing. "Agree to meet him tonight in the dark, empty dance-hall."

When the lustful Keechaka entered the dark hall expecting Draupadi, he was met by the crushing grip of Bheema, who silently and brutally ended his life.

The fourteen years of exile and disguise finally ended. When Duryodhana refused to return even five villages to the Pandavas, the catastrophic Mahabharata war was declared.

For eighteen bloody days, the battle raged. Bheema fulfilled his terrible oath, cornering Duhshasana, breaking his limbs for dragging Draupadi by the hair, and drinking his blood.

On the final day, Bheema faced Duryodhana in a mace duel. As the fight dragged on, Krishna subtly slapped his own thigh. Bheema caught the cue. Remembering the day Duryodhana had insultingly slapped his thigh and invited Draupadi to sit on it, Bheema swung his mace low, shattering Duryodhana's thighs and winning the war.

Their oaths fulfilled and their honor restored, the victorious Pandavas blew their conches. Yudhishthira was crowned Emperor, and Draupadi, her flowing hair finally tied, reigned by his side as the cherished and powerful Queen.

docs.title: Dasharatha: The Story of Rama's Father
docs.text:
Long ago, young Dasharatha, the beloved crown prince of Ayodhya, was renowned for his incredible skill with a bow. He was so masterful that he could hit a target in the dead of night, guided only by the sound of its movement.

One dark, rainy night, Dasharatha was hunting in the forest when he heard a deep gurgling noise nearby. Glug... glug... glug...

What's that? A thirsty elephant's trunk, out for its fill! he thought. Relying on his hearing, he aimed at the sound and let his deadly arrow fly.

But when the arrow found its mark, it was not an elephant that cried out, but a young man. "Ah! Murder! But I have harmed none," the voice wept in agony. "Ah! My poor, old, blind parents! I leave you, helpless."

Dasharatha ran to the spot, his heart pounding with dread. He had mistaken the gurgling of a hermit's water pitcher for an elephant drinking. As he reached the riverbank, he found a young ascetic dying in the mud, Dasharatha's arrow piercing his chest.

"What have I done!" Dasharatha cried in horror. "I who am a virtuous prince, the protector of the weak!"

The dying youth looked up weakly. "O prince, my thirsty parents are waiting at the hermitage nearby. Go and tell them about me. But first, remove this arrow. Let death relieve me of this pain."

Dasharatha did as he was told. He carefully removed the arrow, and the youth breathed his last. Filled with unbearable guilt, Dasharatha filled the pitcher with water and slowly made his way to the hermitage.

Inside the small hut, an old, blind couple sat waiting. "Our son has been away a long while. Could something have happened to him?" the father asked nervously.

Hearing Dasharatha's footsteps, the old man smiled. "Ah! There you are at last. Quick, give me the water. I cannot bear the thirst any longer."

Tears flowed down Dasharatha's face. He stood there, speechless.

"Why are you silent, my son?" the blind father asked.

"O venerable one, I am not your son," Dasharatha wept. "My name is Dasharatha. I have committed a heinous sin and stand before you, sad and repentant." He explained how he had unknowingly shot their son in the dark.

The old couple sat stunned. Then, past his choking grief, the bereft father cursed the prince. "It was my son. Our only son. You killed him. Your son, too, shall be parted from you. And you shall die grieving for him."

A thoroughly shaken Dasharatha returned to his palace. But as the years passed, the tragic incident gradually faded from his memory. After his father, King Aja, retired to the forest, Dasharatha became the King of Ayodhya. He married Kausalya, a princess of Kosala.

Dasharatha was a noble, virtuous king. He was so renowned for his valour that even Indra, the King of the Devas, sought his help in wars against the Asuras. But despite his greatness, Dasharatha was unhappy. Kausalya had not borne him any sons.

Hoping for an heir, he sought the hand of the lovely Kaikeyi. In order to win her, Dasharatha made a solemn promise to her father: "The son born to Kaikeyi shall become the crown prince."

But years passed, and Kaikeyi too failed to bear a son. Dasharatha then wed the gentle Princess Sumitra, but she also remained barren.

One day, Indra asked for Dasharatha's help in a great battle against the Asura Shambara. Kaikeyi, a bold and fiercely loyal queen, insisted on accompanying her husband to the battlefield. During a terrible clash, Dasharatha was severely wounded and lay senseless on the field.

Kaikeyi bravely rode her chariot through the carnage, rescued the King, and nursed him day and night in her tent until he regained consciousness.

"Kaikeyi, my dearest Queen, you have brought me back to life," Dasharatha smiled. "Ask any two boons of me."

"At this moment, with you alive, I have all I need, my Lord," Kaikeyi smiled back. "I'll ask for the boons when I need them."

From then on, Kaikeyi became Dasharatha's favorite queen. Desperate for an heir, Dasharatha eventually performed a grand sacrifice. From the blazing fire, a divine being emerged holding a bowl of payasa (a sweet gruel). "O King, give this to your worthy queens and they shall bear you sons."

The just King first took the payasa to Kausalya and gave her half. He then went to Sumitra and gave her a fourth. But realizing he needed to favor Kaikeyi, he changed his mind, giving Kaikeyi an eighth, and the remaining eighth back to gentle Sumitra.

Within a year, all three queens gave birth. Kausalya gave birth to Rama; Kaikeyi to Bharata; and Sumitra to the twins Lakshmana and Shatrughna. Though Dasharatha loved all four princes, Rama, the eldest, was his favorite.

Years later, when the princes had grown into handsome youths, Dasharatha decided it was time to step down. "I shall see Rama crowned Yuvraja (heir-apparent), without any delay," he announced to his council.

The kingdom rejoiced at the news. But in Kaikeyi's chambers, her wicked, hunch-backed nurse, Manthara, was furious.

"Wake up, Kaikeyi! Your good days are about to come to a rude end," Manthara hissed. "The crafty King has sent your son Bharata away, only to install his Rama as the heir-apparent. Don't you understand? Kausalya will humiliate you!"

At first, Kaikeyi dismissed her, but Manthara’s poisonous words slowly twisted her mind. "If Rama becomes king, he will banish Bharata! You will be a slave in the royal household!"

"Ah, Kaikeyi! Listen to me," Manthara urged. "Remember the two boons Dasharatha had offered you many years ago? Ask for them now. Ask for the installation of Bharata and the exile of Rama for fourteen years."

Kaikeyi, her mind fully poisoned by jealousy and fear, cast off her royal finery and threw herself on the floor of the 'chamber of protest'.

When Dasharatha found her there weeping, he was horrified. "O Kaikeyi, why are you in tears? Tell me what you want, and I swear by Rama that I will do whatever you want."

"Name the boons and I shall see that they are yours," he promised.

Kaikeyi slowly looked up. "Then let my son Bharata be proclaimed heir-apparent. And let Rama be banished to the forest for fourteen years."

Dasharatha was stunned. "No-o-o-o!" he screamed, clutching his chest. "I may banish Kausalya or Sumitra, but never Rama! My eldest son, whose love sustains my life!"

But Kaikeyi was immovable. "Nothing stains that fair name as a broken promise," she mocked coldly.

Realizing he was trapped by his own vow, Dasharatha collapsed at her feet in despair. By morning, he was a broken man. "Let Rama go to the forest," he whispered weakly. "And let the preparations for tomorrow be for my funeral."

When Rama was called, he accepted the cruel decree without a moment of hesitation. "I would willingly surrender not only the kingdom but also my very life for your order alone, mother," Rama told Kaikeyi calmly.

Despite the weeping of the entire kingdom, Rama, his wife Sita, and his loyal brother Lakshmana departed for the forest.

The moment Rama left, Dasharatha collapsed. He refused to let Kaikeyi near him. "Keep away, O vile Kaikeyi! Your touch will pollute me," he gasped, demanding to be taken to Kausalya's palace.

On the sixth night after Rama's departure, a haunting memory from Dasharatha's youth resurfaced in his feverish mind. The image of the blind, old hermit he had orphaned so many years ago flashed before his eyes.

"His father's curse has come true," Dasharatha wept to Kausalya. "I die yearning for my son... O my prince, my son, where are you? O Kausalya! Ah, gentle Sumitra! I..."

With those final words, King Dasharatha breathed his last, his heart broken by the very curse he had earned in his youth.

Yet Kaikeyi's wicked plot yielded her nothing but ash. When Bharata returned and learned what his mother had done, he furiously refused the throne. Placing Rama's wooden slippers on the royal seat, Bharata ruled Ayodhya only as a humble caretaker until the true King, Rama, returned from his exile.

docs.title: Chandra Shekhar Azad: Great Indian Revolutionary
docs.text:
Chandra Shekhar was born in 1906, in Bhanwra, a small village in the Central Provinces of British India. From a young age, he was a robust, intelligent, and fiercely curious boy. Once, his mother caught him lighting an entire box of matches at once.

"Experimenting, mother," he explained innocently. "A single match gives a good deal of light. I wanted to see how much light a boxful of matches could give."

"Haven't I told you that fire is dangerous?" she scolded, pulling him away.

"But it is also useful, mother," he smiled. "It chases away darkness."

As he grew older, Chandra Shekhar learned to shoot a bow and arrow with incredible accuracy from his local Bhil friends. Eager to explore the world, he caught a ride to Bombay with a traveling pearl trader. He worked as a painter's assistant in the docks for a few months, but quickly realized he wanted something more meaningful. "In what way will this sort of work benefit me?" he thought. "I'll go to Varanasi and become a Sanskrit pandit."

In 1921, the Non-Cooperation Movement against British rule was in full swing. The streets of Varanasi echoed with patriotic fervor. Fired by the zeal of his countrymen, young Chandra Shekhar joined a massive procession of revolutionaries, holding a banner high. "Inquilab Zindabad! Bharat Mata ki Jai!" he shouted.

The British police descended on the crowd. "Arrest the lot, insolent brats!" the officer yelled. Chandra Shekhar was among the very first to be arrested.

When produced before the British Magistrate, the boy stood tall and fearless.

"What's your name?" the Magistrate asked.

"Azad," he replied clearly. The word meant 'Free'.

"What is your father's name? Where do you live?"

"Freedom is my father's name and jail, my home."

Furious, the Magistrate yelled, "Impudent brat! But you are still a child. So I'll let you off with a good caning. Take him away."

The police dragged him to the courtyard, tied him to a wooden frame, and whipped him mercilessly. But Azad did not squirm or cry out even once. Instead, after every agonizing stroke of the cane, he shouted, "Inquilab Zindabad! Bharat Mata ki Jai!"

When news of his incredible bravery spread, the revolutionaries of Varanasi gave him a hero's welcome. "Friends! I dedicate my life to the service of my country," Azad addressed the cheering crowd. "The police will never again take me alive! I will always remain free. I am Azad."

From that day on, the name 'Azad' stuck forever.

Two years later, in 1923, the Hindustan Republican Association established a center in Varanasi. Azad quickly became a staunch supporter alongside leaders like Ramprasad Bismil and Ashfaqullah.

"We need funds for our work. How are we to raise it?" Bismil asked during a secret meeting.

"There is only one way—let's rob the rich," a member suggested.

"Robbing the rich will only make us unpopular," another argued. "Let us loot the government. Then alone will the people support our movement."

On the 9th of August, 1925, Azad and his comrades executed a daring plan. They secretly boarded the 8-Down Lucknow passenger train at Kakori. Once the train reached a secluded spot, they pulled the chain, overpowered the guards, broke open the state treasury safe with sledgehammers, and vanished with the cash. The daring heist became known as the Kakori Conspiracy.

A massive manhunt followed, and sadly, many of the conspirators were caught and sentenced to death. Heartbroken but undeterred, Azad eluded the British police for years, constantly changing disguises. He lived as a wandering Hindu ascetic in a Hanuman temple near Jhansi, terrifying local police officers who dared question him. Later, he lived as a humble mechanic named 'Hari Shankar', quietly learning how to drive and repair cars while secretly organizing the party from the shadows.

By 1928, the movement had gathered massive momentum. Azad traveled to Kanpur and joined forces with the fiery revolutionary from Punjab, Bhagat Singh. Together, they formed the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association. Azad commanded the military division, while Bhagat Singh led the organizers.

When the British sent the Simon Commission to India, millions protested. During a massive protest in Lahore, the police ordered a brutal lathi-charge. The beloved independence leader, Lala Lajpat Rai, was severely beaten and died from his injuries.

Azad and Bhagat Singh were furious. "I cannot bear it any longer. We must take revenge," Azad declared.

They planned to assassinate Scott, the British police official responsible. But in the chaos of the ambush, they mistakenly shot and killed another officer, Saunders. "Never mind. Saunders too was among those who caused Lalaji's death," they realized.

A massive dragnet was thrown over Lahore. "Close all roads leading out of the town," the police chief ordered. But Azad was a master of evasion. While Bhagat Singh escaped disguised as a British gentleman, Azad dressed as a singing sadhu and slipped right past the armed guards onto a train to Mathura.

Azad and Bhagat Singh set up secret bomb factories in Agra and Kanpur. In 1929, to awaken the masses, Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt threw a non-lethal bomb into the Central Legislative Assembly, intentionally getting themselves arrested to use their trial as a platform for independence.

"I must be arrested and sentenced to death," Bhagat Singh insisted to a worried Azad. "It will inspire others to dedicate their lives to the cause."

With Bhagat Singh in prison, Azad took total command of the movement. He organized daring raids, smuggled weapons, and even planted a bomb under Lord Irwin's train (though the Viceroy narrowly escaped). But tragedy struck again when the British government sentenced Bhagat Singh to death by hanging. Despite Azad's tireless efforts to save him, Bhagat Singh was martyred on March 23, 1931.

A devastated Azad shifted his headquarters to Allahabad. The ranks of the movement had dwindled, and traitors lurked in the shadows.

On February 27, 1931, Azad and his comrade Sukhdevraj were walking through Alfred Park in Allahabad. A traitor spotted them and immediately informed the police. Within minutes, eighty armed policemen in four vans surrounded the park.

Azad was caught completely unawares. Before he could even draw his famous Mauser pistol, a police bullet struck him deeply in the thigh.

"Panditji!" his comrade screamed.

"Get away from here, Raj. I will hold them," Azad ordered, bleeding heavily. Sukhdevraj made a desperate run for safety while Azad turned and opened fire on the overwhelming forces.

Azad was quickly shot in the right arm, rendering it useless. Unfazed, he grabbed his Mauser with his left hand and continued the ferocious gun battle. For thirty-two minutes, the wounded lion held eighty heavily armed policemen at bay from behind a single tree, returning fire with deadly accuracy.

Finally, Azad checked his pistol. He had only one bullet left.

"No! I won't be taken alive!" Azad whispered fiercely. True to the vow he had made as a child in Varanasi, he raised the Mauser to his own temple and pressed the trigger.

Even as his lifeless body fell to the ground, the British police were so terrified of him that the officer ordered his men to shoot his corpse again before daring to approach.

Chandra Shekhar Azad was only twenty-five years old when he became a martyr for the cause of Indian independence. But true to his name, he lived and died a free man.

docs.title: How Friends Are Parted
docs.text:
There was once a rich merchant who was travelling alone through a deep forest in a cart loaded with goods for sale. "If all goes well and I sell these goods, I will make a lot of money," he thought happily.

But all did not go well. His heavy cart hit a rut, and one of his two bulls stumbled and fell with a painful cry.

"Oh, no!" the merchant groaned. He examined the poor animal. "His foreleg is broken. I'll have to leave him here." Unable to wait, the merchant abandoned the injured bull in the middle of the dense jungle.

I am all alone in this forest, the bull thought sadly. Well, if I'm fated to live, I will; if not, I'll die.

Fortunately, the forest was full of tender, lush grass. As the days passed, the bull fed on the grass, his leg healed, and he grew incredibly strong and healthy. Ah! I am happier here than I ever was with my master, he realized, roaming freely.

In the same forest lived two cunning jackals named Damanaka and Karataka. They had recently fallen from the favor of King Lion and were hungry.

"We have fallen from King Lion's favour. No more feasts for us!" Karataka complained.

"Mark my words," Damanaka schemed, watching the Lion pass by. "By our efforts, we will win back the confidence of our King."

Nearby, the Lion approached a pool of water for a grand feast. Just as he bent down to drink, a terrifying, thunderous sound echoed through the trees. MO-O-W-M-O-O!

It was the bull bellowing with all his might, though the Lion did not know what it was. Terrified that a terrible beast had entered his forest, the Lion ran back to his cave to hide.

Damanaka sprang to his feet. "Here is our chance!"

He confidently entered the Lion's den. "A servant should serve his master. That is why I have come," Damanaka said smoothly.

"Hush! So you saw me," the Lion admitted, embarrassed. "Don't tell anyone yet. But we may have to leave this forest. Didn't you hear the thundering roar of a mighty beast?"

"My brother and I can find a way to help you," Damanaka promised. The Lion happily fed the jackals a royal feast in return for their help.

Damanaka and Karataka went to find the source of the noise. "I do know what it was," Damanaka laughed to his brother. "It was the bellowing of a bull! Ha! Ha! A bull, my brother, whom even you and I could kill!"

"Then why didn't you tell him so and forgo that fine feast?" Karataka asked.

"Because now we can tell him, and earn another feast!" Damanaka sneered.

They found the bull grazing peacefully. "O bull, I am the keeper of this forest," Damanaka lied. "Don't you know you can't come here without permission? Ask Karataka, the King's general, who commands you to appear before him."

The innocent bull, terrified by the thought of a King Lion, fell at Karataka's feet. "O mighty general, what would you have me do?"

"You must go and fall at the imperial feet of King Lion," Karataka ordered.

Damanaka ran ahead to the Lion's den. "Your majesty! I have seen him. He is as dangerous as your majesty feared. But after much persuasion, he is willing to be your friend."

When the bull arrived, the Lion graciously welcomed him. "Come, come, my good friend! We are equals," the Lion smiled. The bull was pleasantly surprised and lived in the forest as the honored guest of his majesty.

One day, the Lion went out to hunt, leaving the bull in charge. When he returned with a fresh kill, the jackals approached for their share.

"No, Damanaka," the Lion said. "You will have to receive your provisions from the bull. He is my treasurer now."

Days rolled by. The Lion and the bull became inseparable friends, constantly seen together.

"The bull has become too powerful," Karataka growled.

"So what? There is nothing that cunning cannot accomplish," Damanaka replied maliciously. "We must turn the King and the bull against each other."

Damanaka went to the Lion alone. "Your majesty, I have something very unpleasant to tell you. My lord, the bull is a traitor. He plans to seize your kingdom."

"But why should he? He enjoys more power than any other minister," the Lion said in disbelief.

"That is why! He now wants to be more powerful than even you," Damanaka lied smoothly. "You will have proof soon. He plans to come here with his horns lowered, as bulls do when they attack. Will you believe me then?"

Immediately, Damanaka ran to the bull, putting on a tragic face. "Ah! There's nothing but misery for those who serve wicked masters. I should not betray my King... but I had promised you protection. His majesty's heart is turned against you. He has made secret plans to kill you and let us feast on your flesh."

"But why? What have I done to displease him?" the bull cried.

"Don't waste time. Think of what you can do to save your life," Damanaka urged. "Go now to him. When he glares at you with his tail stiff, his mouth agape, and his paw raised, you'll see for yourself."

Damanaka ran ahead to the Lion and waited. Soon, the bull arrived near the den. Remembering Damanaka's warning, the Lion lowered himself, glaring fiercely with a raised paw, ready to defend himself.

Seeing the Lion's terrifying, aggressive stance, the bull believed Damanaka's lie. To defend his own life, he lowered his horns and charged.

"He lowered his horns and charged!" the Lion roared, leaping forward.

A brutal fight ensued, but the mighty Lion quickly overpowered the bull and killed him. As the Lion stood staring at the carcass of his innocent friend, a weird sound caught his ears.

It was the gleeful howling of Damanaka and Karataka. "What's that I hear?" the Lion realized in horror. "The gleeful howl of the jackals! Ah, I have let the cunning jackals outwit me and part me from a true friend!"

docs.title: The Washerman's Jackass
docs.text:
Long ago, there lived an ass and a dog who both belonged to a washerman in Varanasi.

One evening, the washerman locked his doors and went to bed early. Outside in the yard, the tired ass sighed. "All is quiet. It's been a hard day. Tonight I shall sleep like a log."

But the dog was pacing angrily back and forth. "There! He has once again forgotten to give me my dinner."

A few hours later, in the dead of night, a thief quietly crept into the yard. The washerman was fast asleep inside.

"Good! He's fast asleep. I'm lucky. I can begin filling my sack," the thief grinned, quietly packing the washerman's goods.

The ass woke up and noticed the intruder. He turned to the dog, who was watching silently. "Look! A thief is carrying off our master's goods," the ass whispered urgently. "Why don't you bark and wake him up?"

The dog scoffed. "Why should I? Our master neglects me. A little fright will remind him that he needs me and must look after me better."

The ass was furious. "How can you let our master down, you unfaithful, ungrateful cur? Should I be grateful for being neglected? No. I will not let the thief go. I'll wake him up."

"Then I will," the ass declared bravely. "Before the thief goes, I'd better be quick!"

The ass threw his head back and began to bray as loudly as he possibly could. HE-HAW! HE-HAW!

The incredible noise startled the thief, who immediately dropped his sack and ran off into the night.

Inside the house, the washerman woke up with a massive start. "The silly ass! He'll wake up the whole neighbourhood," he grumbled, rushing to the window. But by the time he looked outside, the thief had already gotten away unseen.

"That stupid ass has ruined my sleep," the washerman fumed. "I'll teach him to bray at odd hours!"

Grabbing a heavy wooden cudgel, the washerman rushed out into the yard. Because he didn't know why the ass had brayed so loudly, he beat the poor animal mercilessly. WHAM! WHAM!

The dog shook his head in pity, watching the ass suffer. Poor ass, the dog thought. If only you had stuck to your duties instead of trying to perform mine, would you have suffered this fate?

docs.title: The Cat Who Served a Lion
docs.text:
Once upon a time, there lived a Lion. He was a mighty beast before whom all trembled... all except a tiny mouse.

Day after day, the mouse nibbled at the Lion's magnificent mane while he slept in his den.

"Ha! Ha! Your fine mane will make a soft bed for my little ones when they come," the mouse squeaked, chewing off a tuft of hair.

Each day when the Lion woke up, he noticed more hair missing. "Again! More of my magnificent mane has gone!" he roared in frustration. He tried waiting up to catch the culprit. "Ah! There you are. Wait till I catch you," he growled, swiping his paw.

But the little mouse quickly scuttled into her tiny hole in the cave wall.

"She's gone! Well, mice are not my prey," the Lion sighed. "I'm no cat. Oh! Why didn't I think of it before? I'll get myself a cat right away."

He soon came across a stray cat in the forest. "Will you come and live in my den, friend cat?" he asked.

"Why do you want me?" the cat asked cautiously.

"Just to frighten away a little mouse," the Lion explained. "A mouse! I'll come, friend Lion."

At the den, the Lion treated his new bodyguard like royalty. "Here, dear cat. The freshest of fish—all for you. Only keep that mouse away from my mane while I sleep."

"All for me! Mm-m-m," the cat purred happily.

While the cat ate, she schemed to herself. Oho! Our mighty friend has kept a cat as a bodyguard! I must be careful. Aha! The little pest is restless. Thanks to the cat, I'm not. That reminds me. I must give him his treat of fish for today. Wait, that was the Lion thinking about the cat! Ah, the cat is doing a good job, the Lion thought, bringing more fish.

The cat happily gorged on the daily fish. "Hm-m-m! Delicious, my friend. You really spoil me," she told the Lion. If I kill the mouse, the cat thought, the Lion will stop bringing me fish. I must just keep her frightened.

That afternoon, as the Lion napped and the lazy cat dozed nearby, the starving mouse crept timidly out of her hole.

"It's my lucky day," the mouse sniffed. "The cat has dropped some food on the ground. And I starve! It's more than a week since I've left my hole. I must find something to eat."

But she spoke too soon. Suddenly, the cat pounced on her. The mouse tried to run, but the cat was too fast. With a swift swipe, the cat caught the mouse and killed her.

"The richest of cream or the freshest of fish—these are nothing compared to a delicious mouse," the cat purred, licking her lips.

As the days passed, the Lion no longer heard the little pest moving about. "I no longer hear the little pest move about," the Lion thought happily. "She must have gone somewhere else."

Because he no longer needed the cat's services, the Lion did not bother to feed him.

"Friend Lion, I'm hungry," the cat complained.

The Lion yawned lazily. "Then go and catch yourself some mice. I'd like to sleep now. Don't disturb me."

The cat left the den, a wiser animal. "I've been a fool! I should not have killed that mouse," she muttered, her stomach rumbling.

docs.title: The Terrible Bell
docs.text:
One afternoon, when all the men of Brahmapura were away at work in the fields, a thief sneaked into the village temple.

"Oh! Bad luck! The inner doors are closed," the thief cursed. "The only thing I can take away is the bell."

The thief unhooked the large, heavy brass bell from the ceiling and ran towards the hills. "I'll cross the hilltop and sell this in another village. It should fetch a good price," he plotted.

But that was not to be. For as soon as he entered the dense forest in the hills, a massive tiger leaped from the bushes. "A tiger! Help! Save me! E-e-ah!" the thief screamed.

The tiger killed him instantly, dropping the bell in the grass as he dragged the thief's body away.

The next morning, a troop of curious monkeys came swinging by. "What's this?" one monkey asked, poking the shiny brass object.

"I've seen one like it before," another monkey said, picking it up. "It makes a funny sound if you shake it."

The monkey shook it hard. TING! TING! CLANG! CLANG!

"I bet I can make a louder sound!" the first monkey challenged, snatching it back. He shook it violently. CLANG! CLANG! CLANG!

The entire hilly region echoed with the loud, ringing of the bell.

Sometime later, two travellers were on their way to Brahmapura. "Brother, do you see that?" one pointed to the bushes.

"Yes, the bones of a man," the other gulped.

Suddenly, a terrifying CLANG, CLANG erupted from the trees above them.

"Run, brother, run! That must be a demon!" they screamed, dropping their bags and breaking into a dead sprint.

When they reached Brahmapura, they were completely out of breath. "Why have you been running?" the villagers asked.

"There is a demon on the hill. He makes a terrifying noise," the travellers gasped. "And we saw the bones of a man killed by him."

Just then, a faint CLANG, CLANG echoed down from the distant hills.

"There! Can you hear it? Yes, it must be the demon!" the villagers panicked.

Wild rumors quickly spread about the cruel demon. "His ears ring like bells as he swings them about!" one man claimed. "He is the demon Ghantakarna! He is a killer!"

But one of the villagers, a wise old woman, listened carefully. I refuse to believe there's a demon there, unless I see him with my own eyes, she thought. The noise sounds exactly like the clanging of a bell. I'm sure some monkeys have somehow got hold of a bell, and...

Meanwhile, the terrified villagers went to their Headman. "Sir, there is a demon on the hill. We are afraid he'll come here and kill us. We want to leave the village."

As the Headman wondered what to do, the wise woman spoke up. "Sir, pay me some money and I will chase the demon away."

The Headman could not believe his ears. "You will chase him away?"

"Yes."

He turned to his assistant. "Give her all the money she wants."

The woman took the money, went out, and bought a large sack of delicious, ripe mangoes. She set out for the hill alone. In the forest, she scattered the mangoes all over the ground and quickly hid behind a thick tree.

"If my guess is right, the 'demon' will come out!" she smiled.

The monkeys up in the trees smelt the sweet mangoes and came scampering down. In their mad dash to grab the fruit, the monkey holding the bell completely forgot about it and dropped it on the grass.

While the monkeys were busy stuffing their faces with mangoes, the woman crept out from her hiding place, grabbed the bell, and ran all the way back to the village.

She walked straight to the Headman's house and placed the bell on the floor. "Here is your demon! The terrible sound came from this harmless temple bell."

The Headman laughed in pure relief. "It was clever of you to have guessed the truth. You will be rewarded."

The Headman rewarded her handsomely, and from that day on, she became the most respected person in all of Brahmapura.

docs.title: Nachiketa
docs.text:
Thousands of years ago, there lived a sage named Vajashrava. His young son, Nachiketa, was deeply curious about the world. Nachiketa would watch his father perform sacred yajnas (fire sacrifices) with wide eyes.

"Father, when will you let me worship the sacred fire?" little Nachiketa asked.

"Soon after your initiation, son," his father smiled.

Nachiketa was sent to a gurukula, a forest school, where he learned to recite sacred hymns and tend to his guru's cattle. But as he grew older, the boy began to notice the suffering in the world. He watched old, weak cows struggle to graze, and he wept when a beautiful deer he had befriended suddenly dropped dead.

"Why is he so lazy today?" Nachiketa asked his friend.

"He is dead, Nachiketa. There is no life left in him," his friend replied.

"But death is not the end. There is life after death," Nachiketa insisted.

"That's a lie," his friend argued. "Death is the end."

Troubled by this debate, Nachiketa asked his guru. "It is a difficult question, my son," the wise guru said. "Each one must discover the truth for himself."

Soon after, Vajashrava summoned his son home to witness the great Vishvajit sacrifice. "It is one of the greatest sacrifices," a fellow student explained to Nachiketa. "For the one who performs it gives away absolutely everything he possesses."

When Nachiketa arrived, he was shocked by what he saw. His father was giving away sick, old, and dying cows as charity to the visiting Brahmans. What a poor offering this is! Nachiketa thought. By giving away these pathetic cows, father will surely go to the dark world of the joyless.

Desperate to save his father's soul, Nachiketa stepped forward. "But what can my poor father do? This is all he possesses!" he realized. But I, too, belong to my father. I am hale and hearty. By offering myself, I can save my father.

"To whom will you give me, father?" Nachiketa asked aloud.

Vajashrava ignored him, irritated by the interruption. Nachiketa asked again. And again.

"He is trying my patience!" Vajashrava exploded in anger. "I will give you to Yama, the God of Death!"

The sage immediately regretted his angry words, but Nachiketa calmly accepted his fate. "Father, do not grieve. Men of old have passed away, and those of days to come will also pass away. Let me go to the abode of death with your blessing."

Nachiketa travelled to the dark realm of Yama. But the God of Death was not home. For three days and three nights, the young boy waited patiently at the gates without a single drop of water or morsel of food.

When Yama finally returned, he was horrified to find a Brahman boy starved at his doorstep. "Hope, expectations, sacrifices, and good deeds are all snatched away from him who does not offer hospitality to a Brahmana," Yama panicked. "Since you have come as a guest and received no hospitality for three days, you may choose three boons to atone for my sin."

"For my first boon," Nachiketa said, "Grant my father peace of mind and make him cheerful. Let him greet me happily when I return."

"He shall have it," Yama agreed.

"For my second boon, teach me the sacred fire sacrifice by which no hunger or thirst is felt in heaven."

"I will do so gladly," Yama smiled, teaching him the complex ritual. "Henceforth, such a sacrifice will be known as the Nachiketa Sacrifice."

"For my third boon," Nachiketa said firmly, "Some people believe there is life after death; others say there isn't. Tell me who is right. Instruct me in the mysteries of life after death."

Yama became deeply thoughtful. "Even the gods are not certain about this. It is not easily understood. Ask for something else! Ask for cattle, elephants, gold, an empire, long life... look at these lovely celestial maidens! I will give you anything, but do not ask me about life after death."

"I am not interested in things that bring momentary happiness," Nachiketa replied with steadfast faith. "I want that which will bring everlasting bliss. I do not choose any other boon."

Impressed by the boy's immunity to temptation, Yama finally relented. "Two paths are open to men. The wise take the good path and realise the Self. The foolish choose the path of pleasure and go through the never-ending cycle of birth and death."

Yama began to teach Nachiketa the great secret of the universe. "When a man is dead, it is the body that perishes, not the Self. The Self is never born, nor does it die."

"But aren't the body and the Self the same?" Nachiketa asked.

"No, they are not. The Self resides in the body as a master does in his chariot," Yama explained, pointing to a grand chariot. "The intellect is the driver. The mind is the reins. And the sense organs—the eyes, ears, nose, tongue, and skin—are like the horses that pull the chariot."

"Men are pulled in different directions by their sense organs," Yama continued. "Just as horses are held in check by the reins, so also the sense organs are held in check by the mind. The man who cannot control his senses won't reach his goal. But when all desires have been given up, and the mind is controlled, man becomes immortal, and realizes Brahman—the ultimate truth."

Armed with the supreme knowledge of the universe, Nachiketa thanked Yama, returned to the land of the living, and was joyously reunited with his father. As he grew older, his renown spread, and many seekers of truth came to his hermitage for guidance.

docs.title: Satyakama
docs.text:
Gautama was one of the foremost sages in ancient India. Many seekers of truth found their way to his peaceful forest school.

One day, a boy of eight years approached the great sage with folded hands. "Salutations to you, O master. Accept me, Satyakama, as your disciple."

Gautama looked kindly at the boy. "Tell me, child, to which family do you belong?"

Satyakama lowered his head. "I don't know to which family I belong, nor does my mother. I have no father, sir. My mother, Jabala, has asked me to call myself 'Satyakama Jabala'. Please take me as your disciple."

A senior disciple scoffed. "Sir, only a Brahmana is entitled to sacred knowledge. Satyakama may not be one."

But Gautama shook his head. "He who speaks the truth without fear or hesitation is a Brahmana. Satyakama, I accept you as my disciple. What goal have you set for yourself?"

"Sir, I want to experience Brahman—the supreme reality," Satyakama declared.

The master became thoughtful. "This child is truthful and sincere," he thought. "I will help him in achieving his goal."

Gautama took Satyakama to the cattle pen. "There are four hundred weak and sickly cows and bulls here," the sage instructed. "Take them to a distant forest. Come back only when they are fat and healthy and number over a thousand."

Satyakama obeyed at once. Driving the cattle into the deep, isolated forest, he built a small hut and settled in. At first, he was confused. I told the guru that I want to experience Brahman. I wonder why he sent me to the forest? Is Brahman to be found in the forest?

But soon, Satyakama stopped worrying. "How peaceful it is here! And everywhere I look, I see the beauty of nature! The sun, the sky, the river, the trees, the flowers..."

For years, Satyakama took excellent care of the cattle. At dusk, while the animals rested, he meditated deeply. Where should I look for Brahman? Where is he?

Under his loving care, the cows improved in health, and their numbers multiplied until they surpassed a thousand.

"Now that the cattle number over a thousand, I have fulfilled the task given to me by my guru," Satyakama realized.

As he was preparing to return, he noticed a tiny calf was missing. He searched frantically in the north, south, east, and west until he finally found it. He hugged the calf tightly.

That evening, as he gazed at an aged bull, a strange peace washed over him. Suddenly, it seemed as if the bull was speaking to him. "Brahman is in the North. He is in the South, the East, and the West."

"That's true!" Satyakama gasped, his eyes wide. "Why search for Him? He is present everywhere."

The next day, Satyakama began the long journey back to his guru's ashram, driving the massive herd before him. At dusk, he built a campfire and sat beside it. As he watched the flames dance, he felt as if the fire was speaking to him.

"Brahman pervades the earth, the sky, the atmosphere, and the ocean," the fire seemed to crackle.

"Yes, Brahman is endless like the sky," Satyakama realized.

The next morning, as the sun rose over the river, a beautiful swan flew past. "Brahman is in the sun, in the moon, in the lightning, and in the fire," the swan's graceful flight seemed to say.

"Of course! It is Brahman who makes the sun shine!" Satyakama smiled.

Satyakama stood completely still as a flood of new thoughts rushed into his mind. "Then who makes me breathe? Who makes me see, hear, and think?"

A small bird fluttered past his face, chirping sweetly. "It is Brahman, it is Brahman, it is Brahman," the bird sang.

When Satyakama finally reached the forest school, he radiated a profound, inner peace. Sage Gautama looked at him in awe.

"Satyakama, you glow like one who has attained knowledge of Brahman. How did this happen?" the sage asked.

Satyakama described his profound experiences in the forest—how the bull, the fire, the swan, and the bird had revealed the truth to him. "Nature taught me," Satyakama smiled. "But knowledge imparted by the teacher helps the most in attaining one's goal. Please instruct me, sir."

Gautama smiled warmly and imparted the final, sacred knowledge to his deserving disciple, who later became a great and revered sage himself.

docs.title: Prajapati
docs.text:
Long ago, there were three kinds of beings in the universe: the Devas (celestial gods), the Manavas (men on earth), and the Asuras (demons).

In heaven, the Devas had all the comforts they could possibly aspire for and led a life of endless pleasure. They drank divine nectar, watched beautiful dancers, and lived in luxury. Yet, one day, they grew restless.

"Here in heaven we are free from hunger, thirst, old age, and death," one Deva noted. "Yet, why aren't we happy?"

"It's true," another agreed. "We seem to have everything, yet we feel the lack of something."

"Let us go to Prajapati, our father and the creator of all, and seek his guidance," their leader suggested.

Unlike their celestial brothers, the Manavas on earth were leading a hard life. They plowed the fields, carried heavy pots of water, and chased away birds who tried to steal their grain.

"A good harvest! Our future is secure. Go away, you wicked bird! Stealing my grain! On no account will we part with what we have gained," a wealthy farmer shouted, throwing a stone at a bird.

Yet, despite all the riches they had earned and stored away, the Manavas, too, were far from happy.

"We have no contentment," a wealthy merchant sighed.

"What we lack is peace of mind," another agreed.

"Let us approach Prajapati, our father, for guidance," they decided.

Deep in the underworld, the Asuras inspired fear among both the Manavas and the Devas. They were brutal and merciless warriors.

"Spare my life. Show mercy," a terrified victim begged an Asura warlord.

"Mercy? What does that mean? Prepare to die!" the Asura laughed, swinging his massive sword.

But the Asuras, too, were overcome by a feeling of deep dissatisfaction. "Although we are powerful and feared by all, we are not happy," an Asura admitted as they sat around a fire.

"Let us go to Prajapati, our father, and seek his guidance," the warlord suggested.

The Devas, the Manavas, and the Asuras all travelled to the divine abode of Lord Prajapati. Thousands of beings gathered before the four-faced creator of the universe.

"O father, tell us what we should do to achieve contentment and peace," they prayed in unison.

Prajapati looked at his children thoughtfully. Then, he uttered just one single syllable.

"DA...."

Everyone was completely perplexed. They looked at each other in confusion. "DA? What could that mean?"

"I think it is the first syllable of a secret word which will tell us what to do," one of the men guessed.

"I think HE wants us to discover the word for ourselves," a Deva realized.

Accepting the puzzle, the three groups left for their respective homes to ponder over the meaning of "DA."

On returning to their celestial world, the Devas were just about to resume their life of endless pleasure when their leader suddenly raised his hand.

"I've got it! I know what 'DA' means!" he shouted joyously.

"Tell us what it means," the others asked.

"'DA' means 'Damyata' (Restrain yourselves). Yes, the father has asked us to restrain ourselves from endless indulgence."

All the Devas put aside their cups of wine and stepped away from the dancers. They began to practise self-control, and for the first time, they experienced a profound satisfaction they had never known before.

Meanwhile, down on earth, the Manavas had gone back to their fields. The wealthy farmer was just about to fling a heavy stone at a flock of birds when he froze.

"I've got it! 'DA' means 'Datta' (Give)!" he cried out. "The father wants us to give away, not to hoard!"

"Only now do I realise that there is joy in giving," another man smiled.

"How right you are! Let the birds share our grain," the farmer agreed happily, tossing seeds to the birds. The men began to practise charity, and their hearts were filled with true peace.

Down in the underworld, the Asuras were about to resume their violent raids. An Asura warlord had his sword raised high to execute a prisoner when he suddenly froze.

"Wait! 'DA' means 'Dayadhvam' (Be compassionate)," the warlord gasped. "Yes, the father wants us to show compassion."

He lowered his sword. "Release him. Go without fear, friend. No harm will come to you."

"You are kind! Thank you," the prisoner wept in relief.

As the Asuras began to practise compassion and mercy, their leader smiled warmly. "My heart is filled with a kind of joy I've never experienced before," he admitted.

And so, the Devas learned Damyata (self-control), the Manavas learned Datta (charity), and the Asuras learned Dayadhvam (compassion).

To this day, to remind all people of the three cardinal virtues needed for a happy and peaceful life, the heavens thunder now and again: DA... DA... DA...

docs.title: The Learned Pandit
docs.text:
One day, several men were being ferried across a wide, turbulent river. Among them was an extremely learned Pandit, who looked around at his fellow passengers with mild disdain.

I'll talk to one of these fellows, the Pandit thought to himself. Not that it will benefit me in any way, but it will at least help me pass the time.

He cleared his throat and looked at a simple man sitting near him. "Who are you? Where do you come from?"

"I am a farmer, good sir, from a village on the other side of the river," the man replied politely.

The Pandit puffed out his chest. "I am a scholar. I have made a deep study of the Upanishads. Have you read the Upanishads?"

The farmer shook his head. "No, sir, I haven't."

The Pandit gasped in exaggerated horror. "You haven't? You poor fellow! A quarter of your life has been completely wasted! Well, you must have read the Shastras then?"

"No, sir. I can't say that I have."

"Then half your life is gone!" the Pandit declared dramatically. "What about the six systems of philosophy?"

"I am afraid I haven't even heard of them, sir," the farmer said humbly.

"Not heard of them!" the Pandit cried. "Then, my friend, three-fourths of your life is..."

Suddenly, a massive gust of wind rocked the small boat.

"Hey! The boat! It's..." the boatman yelled in panic. Dark clouds had rolled in, and the river was churning violently. "It's a storm! I can't control the boat. It's going to turn over! Jump overboard and swim to the shore!"

As the boat began to capsize, the farmer turned to the terrified scholar. "Good sir, I hope you know how to swim."

The Pandit’s eyes went wide with sheer terror. "N-n-no! I've never learnt swimming in my life!"

The farmer shook his head in pity as he prepared to dive into the water. "Never learnt swimming? Then your whole life is gone, Panditji! Knowledge derived from books is not enough. One must acquire practical learning too."

docs.title: The Fisherman Who Pretended to be a Holy Man
docs.text:
One dark, moonless night, a sneaky fisherman stole into the private, walled garden of a wealthy estate.

"The house is dark and quiet. Everyone seems to be asleep! Good!" the fisherman grinned. He crept up to the large, private pond in the center of the garden. "I can cast my net right away... and catch enough fish for the week."

He threw his heavy net into the pond with a loud SPLASH!

Inside the mansion, the wealthy owner shot up in bed. "What was that sound? Did you hear it?" he asked his wife. "I did. It sounded like a net falling into the water."

"A net? In the water? The pond! My fish! Somebody's stealing my fish! Thief! Thief!" the owner yelled, jumping out of bed.

He quickly woke up his servants. "Quick! It's a thief! Catch him!" they shouted, grabbing lanterns and running out into the garden.

Outside, the fisherman panicked. "They're awake! They're coming this way. I'm trapped. What shall I do?"

He looked desperately around the garden. A smouldering bonfire sat nearby, leftover from the gardener's work. Fate couldn't have been kinder! he thought.

Thinking fast, he pulled off his turban, grabbed a handful of white ash from the fire, and hurriedly smeared it all over his arms, chest, and forehead. He sat down beneath a tree, closed his eyes, and pretended to be deep in meditation.

Moments later, the servants burst through the bushes with their lanterns. "Look! Can you see a man there?"

They shined their lights on the ash-covered figure. "Oh! Oh! It's a sanyasi!" they gasped, dropping to their knees in reverence. "Forgive us, sir. We did not mean to disturb your meditation."

The servants quietly backed away and ran back to their master. "Well, what happened? Have you found him?" the owner demanded.

"No luck, sir. He escaped. But we saw a holy man in the garden," they reported.

"A holy man? In my garden? Take me to him."

The owner tiptoed into the garden and saw the ash-covered figure sitting peacefully under the tree. "Hush!" the owner whispered to his servants. "He must be a great saint. We mustn't disturb him. He is meditating. Let's go away."

Left alone in the dark, the fisherman chuckled to himself. I've fooled even the master of the house. Now aren't I clever!

At dawn the next day, the fisherman prepared to sneak away. "It's time I left," he thought, dusting himself off. But as he stood up, he froze. "Hey! Who are these people?"

A massive crowd of villagers, led by the wealthy owner and his family, had gathered in the garden. "O holy saint, we heard of you and have come to seek your blessings," the owner bowed respectfully.

"Please bless our child," a mother pleaded, laying her baby before him.

"Good God! More and more of them," the fisherman thought in shock as people brought forward silver bowls filled with expensive fruits, sweets, and fresh flowers. "Are they mad?"

The fisherman sat back down, overwhelmed by the outpouring of love and reverence. "And to crown it all, the look of utter devotion in their eyes!" he thought, deeply moved.

As an old man touched his feet with trembling hands, the fisherman had a profound realization. How strange! I am not a real sadhu. And yet, these people show so much respect for me. How much greater would be the respect they'd show if I were a real sadhu!

Tears welled up in the fisherman's eyes. Yes, I shall become a true man of God! I shall give up thieving for good.

And so, the thief who began by merely pretending to be holy, truly became a great saint.

docs.title: The Traveller and the Wish-Tree
docs.text:
On a scorching hot summer's day, a weary traveller was walking through a barren landscape. Spotting a large, shady tree in the distance, he rushed toward it and collapsed beneath its branches.

"Phew! The heat is unbearable!" he gasped, wiping sweat from his brow. "I'll rest here for a while."

He leaned back against the trunk. "Ah! This is good!" he sighed contentedly. "But how much better it would be, if I had a soft bed to sleep on."

Little did the traveller realise that the tree he was resting under was a magical Wish-Tree.

Instantly, the air shimmered, and a magnificent, intricately carved bed with plush pillows appeared right beside him.

"And lo! A bed! Appearing out of nowhere! I can't believe it!" the traveller gasped, quickly climbing onto it.

"This is perfect!" he smiled, bouncing on the mattress. "The sheets are white and soft as a swan's down. But... now all I need is a maiden to keep me company in this lonely place."

POOF!

A stunningly beautiful maiden adorned in gold and jewels materialized before him. She smiled warmly and began fanning him with a peacock feather.

"Today is truly my lucky day," the traveller grinned. "I couldn't have had a lovelier companion! But... my stomach is growling. I am hungry. If only I had some good food to eat!"

Instantly, a massive feast fit for a king appeared on silver platters before him.

"Why, it's a feast fit for a king!" he cheered.

The traveller ate the delicious food and drank sweet wine until he was completely content. Stuffed and happy, he lay down on the soft bed again.

"This is the absolute peak of happiness," he sighed, closing his eyes. But as he lay there, his mind began to wander. "What if all this should suddenly disappear? And what if a ferocious tiger should suddenly attack me..."

Before he even finished the thought, a massive, terrifying tiger sprang from the bushes with a deafening roar. "EE-A-AH!" the traveller screamed in sheer panic.

He leaped off the bed and scrambled up the trunk of the tree as fast as he could, narrowly avoiding the tiger's snapping jaws.

As he clung to the high branches, trembling in fear while the tiger growled below, the traveller realized his fatal mistake.

Had I been content with just the shade of the tree, I would not be at the mercy of this ferocious tiger! he thought sadly.

docs.title: The Ascetic and His Loin-Cloth
docs.text:
In a quiet forest near a small village, there lived a wise Guru and his young disciple. They spent all their time in deep prayer and meditation, living a life of absolute simplicity.

Then, one day, the Guru stood up. "My son, I have to leave you for a while. I am going on a pilgrimage," he announced. "Remember all that I have taught you. And above all, lead a simple life."

"I will, Gurudev," the disciple promised humbly.

The disciple continued his simple life. One morning, as usual, the ascetic washed his only spare loin-cloth in the river and hung it out on a tree branch to dry.

"I will now go to the village to beg for alms," he thought, walking away.

But when the ascetic returned to his hut later that afternoon, he was shocked. "Hey! That looks like a piece of my loin-cloth!"

He examined the shredded fabric hanging from the tree. "It is! The rats have torn it to shreds! I will have to beg for another."

The next day at the village, he approached a kind woman. "Mother, I need a loin-cloth. Can you spare me one?"

"A loin-cloth? I have a new one. I'll give it to you, holy sage," she smiled, handing it over.

The ascetic thanked her, washed it, and hung it high up on the roof of his hut. "Today, I'll hang my cloth high up here on the roof," he thought smartly.

But that afternoon, when he returned, it was destroyed again. "Oh no, the rats have torn up this one too! I'll have to get myself a cat."

The ascetic found a stray cat and brought it home. And soon enough, the rats stopped troubling him.

"I must keep my cat well fed," he realized a few days later as the cat meowed hungrily. "I know what. I'll get myself a cow."

And so he got himself a cow. "My cat has as much milk as it needs and more," he smiled happily. But then he scratched his head. "But I need straw to feed my cow. How shall I go about getting that? Ah! I could cultivate the land around my hut. And when the grain is harvested, I'll have all the straw my cow would need."

Accordingly, the ascetic began to till the land around his hut. Soon, he had a massive, golden harvest.

"What a fine crop I have harvested!" he cheered. "The straw my cow will have, but what shall I do with the grain? I'll need a barn to store it in."

So the ascetic hired labourers to build large brick barns for him. "Make it taller. There's plenty of grain to be stored," he ordered the masons.

With so much grain and property, the ascetic had now become a wealthy landlord.

"I need a wife to help me manage all this. I think I will marry," he decided.

And so, he married a beautiful woman, had children, and lived a life of absolute luxury, smoking a hookah in his grand mansion.

One fine day, the Guru returned from his long pilgrimage. Walking down the forest path, he was completely bewildered by the sprawling estate before him.

"Am I on the right road?" the Guru muttered, scratching his head. "Where is my disciple's little hut? Has some rich man driven my poor disciple away and built his estate here?"

When a servant came out of the grand house, the Guru went up to him. "My good man, an ascetic used to live here once. Do you know where he is now?"

"Er... I... he..." the servant stammered, pointing behind him. "My lord, you!"

The master of the house stepped out. It was the disciple!

He rushed forward and fell at his Guru's feet in shame.

"My son, what is all this?" the Guru gasped. "I left you leading the simple life of an ascetic. I return to find you surrounded by wealth and possessions. How did this happen?"

The disciple wept, burying his face in his hands. "Oh Gurudev, it all began with a single loin-cloth. To protect it from the rats I needed a cat, and then there was no end to my needs. Forgive me, Gurudev."

docs.title: To Each His Own
docs.text:
One evening, after a long, exhausting day at the market, a fishwife finally sold her very last fish.

On her way home, she stopped at her friend's house and knocked on the door. Her friend, who was a flower-girl by trade, answered the door with a bright smile.

"Why, it's you! Come in, my friend," the flower-girl greeted her warmly. "You must have dinner with me and spend the night here. We can have a long chat."

"All right, my friend," the fishwife agreed happily.

After a lovely dinner and catching up on all the town gossip, it was time to go to bed.

"We'll sleep in the room where I keep my flowers," the flower-girl said, leading her into a room filled to the brim with thousands of fresh, blooming roses and jasmine. "You'll love it."

"Oh! It's a beautiful room," the fishwife smiled.

The two women lay down to sleep. But hours later, the fishwife was tossing and turning, unable to close her eyes.

"Why are you so restless? What's the matter?" the flower-girl asked, waking up.

"I just can't sleep," the fishwife groaned miserably. "I think it's the smell of these flowers. It's too sweet. I can't bear it."

The flower-girl looked at her in surprise.

"I know what!" the fishwife suddenly suggested. "May I have my fish-basket?"

"Certainly! I'll bring it in for you," her friend agreed.

The flower-girl went out, fetched the empty, foul-smelling fish-basket, and brought it back into the room.

The fishwife excitedly grabbed the basket and sprinkled it with some water to freshen it up.

"Ugh! What a stench!" the flower-girl gagged, covering her nose.

But the fishwife took a deep, happy breath, hugging the stinking basket close to her pillow. "Ah! This is much better," she sighed contentedly. "Now I shall sleep like a log."

And, right enough, within a few minutes, she was fast asleep and snoring loudly.

The flower-girl watched her friend in complete disbelief. "Surprising! She prefers the stench of fish to the fragrance of flowers! Well, I suppose one feels most at home with what one is used to."

docs.title: The Hermit and the Nawab
docs.text:
Once, a poor, wandering hermit went to the grand palace of a Nawab to beg for alms. He approached the armed guards at the main gates.

"Good sirs, I have some business with the Nawab," the hermit said humbly.

The guards recognized him as a holy man and lowered their spears. "Walk straight in, holy one. You'll find him in the prayer-room," they directed him.

The hermit walked deep into the luxurious palace and up to the prayer-room. Peeking through the curtains, he saw the immensely wealthy Nawab kneeling on a rich carpet, his hands raised in deep prayer.

"I'll wait for him to finish his prayers," the hermit thought, standing quietly by the door.

The Nawab ended his prayers with his usual, daily request for prosperity.

"O Lord, I salute you with devotion," the Nawab prayed aloud, his eyes closed. "Give me money, give me riches, give me fame and fortune..."

Hearing this, the hermit shook his head. "Oh! Oh! I've come to the wrong place!" he muttered to himself, turning around and walking away.

As the Nawab stood up to leave, he spotted the hermit walking down the hall.

"Wait, my good man!" the Nawab called out, hurrying after him. "You came to see me. Why are you going away without even speaking to me?"

The hermit stopped and turned around. "I came to ask you for money," he explained simply. "But when I heard you begging for money and riches from God, I changed my mind."

The Nawab looked at him in confusion.

"Why should I beg from one who is himself a beggar?" the hermit smiled wisely. "If I must beg, let me beg from God."

docs.title: The Brahmana and the Cow
docs.text:
There was once an old Brahmana who owned a beautiful, sprawling garden filled with rare flowers and fruit trees. He loved it dearly and spent an immense amount of time tending to it with his own hands.

But he was incredibly selfish. He did not want to share his beautiful garden with anyone. Not even the birds!

"Parrots in my garden! Sitting on my sunflowers! How dare they!" he would shout, waving his arms wildly. "Get away, you wretched birds!"

The mischievous boys of the village liked to tease the grumpy old man. They sat on his garden wall and laughed at him. "Be a sport, sir. Parrots love to eat sunflower seeds. Why did you shoo them away?"

"Besides, you are a Brahmana, sir. You must not be rude to your guests," another boy teased.

"Guests! Humph! I don't want any pesky birds... or brats in or near my garden," the old man yelled, chasing them off with a stick. "Run off! Off with you!"

And so the days went by. Then, one day, the Brahmana walked out to check on his new saplings. "Let me go and see how tall my mango saplings have grown," he smiled.

But when he reached the flowerbeds, his jaw dropped. "A stray cow in my garden! And she's eating my saplings!"

In a blind fit of rage, the Brahmana picked up a heavy, wooden stick and began to beat the cow mercilessly. "You wicked beast!" he screamed, striking her again and again.

The cow was already old, thin, and very hungry. The vicious beating gave her such a terrible fright that she collapsed onto the ground and dropped down, dead.

The Brahmana dropped his stick in shock. "O God! What have I done? I've killed a cow," he realized in horror.

Word quickly spread, and the Brahmana had to face the angry villagers.

"You have committed the greatest of sins!" the village headman scolded him. "You have put your garden above everything else, even the life of a sacred cow. The cow gives us her sweet and nourishing milk, and in return you kill her?"

"What kind of hand is yours that it can kill a cow? We're going now, but we'll see you later," the villagers threatened, leaving the disgraced old man alone.

"They will throw me out of the village. I'm sure they will. What shall I do?" the Brahmana panicked, pacing nervously.

Suddenly, an idea struck him. "Ah! I've got it. It was my hand that killed the cow. And Lord Indra is supposed to be the presiding deity of the hand. If it is Indra who guides the hand, it is not I who killed the cow, but he! He is to blame! Yes, I'll tell the villagers that."

So the next day, at the bustling village market, he loudly proclaimed his innocence. "It was not I! It was Indra. It was he who made my hand kill the cow."

This news spread like wildfire, until it reached the heavens and the ears of Lord Indra himself.

"I'd better assume a disguise and go and see this Brahmana," Indra sighed, stepping down from his celestial throne.

Taking the form of an old man, Indra walked casually into the Brahmana's garden.

"Sir, I am a stranger to this town," Indra greeted the Brahmana. "I was just passing this way and couldn't help noticing your garden. It is very beautiful."

The Brahmana was highly flattered. "It is kind of you to say so, sir. I have tended it as if it were my own child."

"I can see that. How well this path has been laid! Did you lay it?"

"Yes, sir. I laid it myself," the Brahmana boasted.

"What a charming fountain! Did you set it up yourself?"

"Yes, I set it up with my own hands."

"How tall these fruit trees have grown! Did you plant them yourself?" Indra continued.

"Yes, sir," the Brahmana puffed his chest out proudly. "From seed to fruit, it was all my work."

Suddenly, the old man's disguise vanished in a flash of divine light, and the mighty Lord Indra stood before the trembling Brahmana in all his glory.

"O Brahmana," Indra thundered. "If you take all the credit for setting up your beautiful garden with your own hands, then you should take the blame for killing the cow with those same hands too! Why blame poor Indra for your own wicked temper?"

docs.title: Manduka: The Lucky Astrologer
docs.text:
One day, a poor, down-on-his-luck Brahmana was passing by the house of a wealthy merchant when he heard the sounds of joyous celebration.

"Hey! There's a wedding going on there!" he realized, feeling a pang of sadness. "No one even bothered to tell me about it. Who tells me anything? Even when I was a child, everybody ignored me... except father, of course, who never failed to tell me one thing every so often."

He remembered his father's harsh words: "You are as dull as a Manduka (frog) in a well."

"That's what I've become, a Manduka! And I'm tired of it," he sighed. "I wish I could do something... anything... which would make everyone sit up and take notice of me."

Suddenly, as he walked past a beautifully decorated white horse tied to a post near the wedding, an idea struck him.

"Hey, I've got it!" he grinned maliciously.

He waited until no one was looking, snuck up to the post, and quietly led away the horse on which the bridegroom had arrived. He hid the horse deep in the forest near a stream.

The next morning, the merchant's house was in an absolute uproar.

"Master, the bridegroom's horse has disappeared!" a servant cried.

"Disappeared? How could it disappear? Go and look for it!" the merchant yelled.

"We already have, master. It has vanished without a trace."

The merchant paced nervously. "Oh, this is terrible! What am I to do now?"

"Master," another servant interrupted. "There's a woman outside who says we should seek the help of her husband. She says he's an astrologer."

"Go fetch him at once!" the merchant ordered desperately.

Sometime later, the Brahmana, dressed formally with charts under his arm, arrived at the merchant's house. How well my plan is working! he thought gleefully. Already I'm being treated as an honoured guest.

"Your servants tell me you have lost a horse," the Brahmana said sagely.

"That's right," the merchant pleaded. "I shall reward you handsomely if you can help us find him."

"Give me a few moments and I shall tell you exactly where he is," the Brahmana promised, unrolling his astrological charts. He pretended to study them intently, muttering to himself. "I'll have to study these charts and... and... Ah! You'll find the horse near the stream in the forest. But make haste, he is in danger of being stolen."

"Rush to the stream!" the merchant ordered his men.

Sure enough, the servants returned leading the white horse. The merchant was overjoyed to see the horse again. "I can't believe it! So the astrologer was right. You have helped me save my face. I can never thank you enough," the merchant smiled, handing the Brahmana a heavy bag of gold coins.

"It was nothing... all in a day's work!" the Brahmana shrugged modestly.

The merchant's servants quickly spread the word about what the incredible astrologer had done. "And he told us the exact spot at which we could find the horse!"

"You don't say! I had always thought he was a half-wit."

"You should never go by appearances. What a great astrologer he is!"

Soon, people everywhere began to show him deep respect. The Brahmana's fame spread far and wide. Everyone bows to me now, he smiled to himself. At last the Manduka has jumped out of the well!

Then, one day, royal guards arrived at his doorstep. "Are you Pandit Bhojraj? Your presence is requested at the palace. Please come with me."

At the palace, the King looked incredibly worried. "The Queen's necklace has been lost... or stolen," the King explained. "I have heard that you once told a merchant where he could find his lost horse. I am sure you can tell us where the necklace is."

The Brahmana swallowed hard, cold sweat breaking out on his forehead. "I... er... I need time."

"Well? You can take as long as you want," the King said firmly. "But you must not leave the palace till the necklace is found."

Guards escorted the Brahmana to a private chamber. This is the end of the road for me, he panicked. I wonder what the King will do to me when he discovers that I am not a real astrologer.

The Brahmana shut himself up in his chamber and prepared for the worst. "Why did I claim to be what I am not? Now I will just have to pay for it," he cried, pulling his hair. "It's my tongue which has got me into all this trouble!"

Just then, outside the heavy wooden door, the Queen's maid was listening nervously. He is a great astrologer, the maid panicked. He will soon know that it is I who stole the necklace. Why, oh, why did I do such a foolish thing! Let me see what he is doing.

She pressed her eye to the keyhole.

Inside, the Brahmana, unaware that the maid was peeping, began to angrily scold his own tongue (which in Sanskrit is called Jihvah).

"What have you done, Jihvah? Why did you do it?" the Brahmana yelled at himself.

Outside, the maid gasped in pure terror. He knows!

She burst into the room and threw herself at the Brahmana's feet. "O holy sir, have mercy on me! I am doomed!" she sobbed.

The Brahmana jumped back in shock. "Did what? Who are you?"

"I am Jihvah, the maid. I heard you," she cried, pressing her hands together. "I did it in a moment of weakness. I don't know why I stole the necklace. Please don't tell the King!"

The Brahmana's eyes widened in realization. She thought I was talking to her! he realized, quickly composing himself.

"Er... well... you have committed a great sin," he said sternly. "But I shall forgive you this once. Now, where is the necklace?"

Later that day, as the King was strolling in the garden, the Brahmana approached him confidently. "I know where the necklace is, Maharaj. Please follow me."

He led the King to a specific tree and pointed. "Here."

A gardener quickly dug up the earth and found the glittering necklace.

"You are without doubt the greatest astrologer I have ever met!" the King gasped. "Now tell me who hid the necklace here?"

The Brahmana smiled mysteriously. "That must remain a secret, Maharaj. There are some things which even I may not know."

But the King's Prime Minister was suspicious. "There is some mystery here, Maharaj," he whispered. "If he is such a great astrologer, why can't he tell us who the thief is? He must have found the necklace in the palace and hidden it here himself. Let us test him once more."

The King agreed. After some time, the Brahmana was summoned to the King's chamber.

"I am amazed by your power, O Pandit, and you shall be well rewarded for your services," the King said. He pointed to a small, sealed clay jar on a pedestal. "Can you tell us what is in that jar?"

The Brahmana stared at the jar, his heart sinking. How neatly he has trapped me! he thought miserably. Now I'll have to tell him that I am not really an astrologer... and that I fooled him and everybody else. He'll surely put me to death!

In absolute despair, he struck his forehead and cried out his childhood nickname. "O Manduka, you were better off in the well!"

The King's eyes widened in sheer shock. "Well done! I should never have doubted your powers," the King gasped.

The Minister lifted the lid off the jar, and a small green frog—a manduka—hopped out onto the floor!

"There is indeed a manduka in the jar," the Minister admitted, thoroughly amazed.

The King rewarded the incredibly lucky Brahmana with costly gifts and a permanent position at court, and he returned home to a hero's welcome, never to be called a dull frog again.

docs.title: The Hidden Meaning
docs.text:
There was once a farmer named Buddhuram. He was hard-working but not too bright, and he was a contented soul with absolutely no ambitions in life.

His wife, however, was exactly the opposite.

"Our neighbour's son-in-law has bought two buffaloes!" she yelled, slamming a pot of dough onto the floor. "Everyone is buying buffaloes or land... except us. You must make money somehow. Did you hear me?"

"Yes, yes! Now please let me eat in peace!" Buddhuram groaned.

Sometimes I feel like breaking a pot over his thick skull! his wife fumed.

Matters came to a head when one day, their neighbour excitedly burst into the house. "You won't believe it! My husband has been honoured by the King!"

"What! Are you all right? It's the heat!" Buddhuram's wife gasped, fanning her friend.

"Yes, yes! Don't worry about me. Oh, what marvellous news... er, for what did the King honour him?"

"For his poetry!" the neighbour beamed. "Yes. He recited one of his poems to the King. The King was so pleased with it that he presented my husband with two gold coins!"

"How marvellous!" Buddhuram's wife forced a smile. As soon as the neighbour left, her smile vanished. From now on she'll become even more insufferable. And it's all because of that dim-witted husband of mine! If only he would do something spectacular!

When Buddhuram returned from the fields, she was waiting for him with a broom.

"Imagine! Two gold coins!" she screamed, hitting him with the broom. "People around us have become rich and famous... and all you can think of is food! Well, I have had enough! You are going to sit down and write a poem too!"

"A poem! But I don't know how to read and write," Buddhuram protested.

"I'll teach you! If her husband can write poetry, so can you! I'll teach you the alphabet first. We'll begin right now!"

"What about food?"

"You'll get your food as soon as you have learnt the first letter of the alphabet!"

Forced into learning, Buddhuram sat in the dirt as his wife drew symbols. "The first letter of the Sanskrit alphabet is 'A'. It's written like this! Now go to the riverside and practise writing it in the sand!"

Every day, his wife would teach him one letter, and then send him to the riverside to practise writing it. But poor Buddhuram had a terrible memory.

"Now what did she say the third letter was? Eeee? Beee? Ehh?" he muttered, staring blankly at the sand.

Two travellers walking by noticed him struggling. "What are you doing my good man?"

"I am... er, learning the alphabet so that I can write poetry," Buddhuram explained.

The traveller laughed. "You want to write poetry, do you? And do you think everyone who knows the alphabet can write poetry? You need inspiration to write poetry, my friend! Inspiration!"

"Who's that?"

"Not who, what. Inspiration is a feeling! When you get inspired a thrill runs through your body and... oh! Never mind!" the traveller gave up and walked away.

When Buddhuram returned home, he had completely forgotten the letters. "Then you don't get any food!" his wife yelled, slamming the door in his face.

Life became utterly miserable for Buddhuram. "Why doesn't she leave me alone? I was happy just being a farmer," he wept. Hungry, tired, and desperate, he walked back to the river. "I can't go on like this! I'll end my life in the river!"

He bravely dove into the deep water. But the river was icy cold, and Buddhuram immediately began to shiver violently.

"What's happening? Why's my whole body trembling?" he chattered, his teeth clacking together. Then, his eyes widened. "Perhaps this is what the pandit meant by inspiration! What a stroke of luck! But now that I have got inspiration I must write a poem! But about what? About whom?"

Just then, he noticed a crow sitting on a rock near the bank, sharpening its beak on the stone.

"Ah, that crow... he is sharpening his beak in expectation of a feast," Buddhuram observed. He watched the crow dip its beak in the water, rub it on the rock, and repeat the process.

"You dip it in water and sharpen it... again and again. O Kalia, I can read your mind," Buddhuram said aloud.

He gasped. "There! I have composed a poem!"

He scrambled out of the icy water and ran all the way home, dripping wet. "Wife! Wife! I have composed a poem! Yes! I entered the river and suddenly I had... INSPIRATION! Seizing the opportunity, I immediately composed a poem. Listen..."

He struck a dramatic pose. "You dip it in water and sharpen it again and again. O Kalia, I can read your mind."

His wife stared at him. "I... er... is that a poem?" she asked, thoroughly confused. Perhaps it is a poem, she thought to herself. Who can tell? Perhaps the goddess had pity on my poor husband and on me and inspired him the way she inspired Kalidasa.

"Well, now that you have a poem, you'd better go to the palace and recite it before the King," she declared proudly.

So Buddhuram set off for the palace. When he got there, he boldly took his place among the kingdom's greatest poets. He waited impatiently as famed scholars held the audience spellbound with brilliant epics and sonnets.

"Is there anybody left?" the King finally asked.

"Yes!!" Buddhuram shouted, jumping up.

"You may begin, then."

Buddhuram cleared his throat, threw out his chest, and began to recite his composition in a loud, booming voice.

"You dip it in water and sharpen it again and again. O Kalia, I can read your mind."

There was a moment of stunned silence. Then, the entire audience burst out laughing. HAHAHA! HOHOHO! KHI KHI!

But the King maintained a discreet, serious silence. No one would dare to come here and recite such nonsense unless there was some meaning in the words, the King thought cleverly. And the man appears so confident. He's surely not a fool.

The King turned to his Prime Minister. "Well, what did you think of the poem?"

The Minister, noticing the King wasn't laughing, quickly adjusted his expression. "I... well... He didn't laugh with the rest of us. Perhaps the words have some significance which the rest of us missed. It is obvious that the poem is... er... pregnant with meaning, Maharaj."
