"Just as I thought," the King nodded sagely. He turned to the crowd. "Can anyone interpret the poem?"

The laughing poets fell silent, looking at each other in confusion.

"If anyone can interpret the poem, he shall be rewarded with ten gold coins!" the King announced. He ordered the poem to be written on a huge board and set up in the royal courtyard.

"What about me, Maharaj?" Buddhuram asked.

"You too will get your reward... but in due course," the King promised.

For days, poets and scholars puzzled over the rhyme on the board. But no one could see any special meaning in the strange words about a crow.

Then one morning, as the King was being prepared for a shave by his royal barber, Kalia.

"What's the matter, Kalia? You seem rather nervous today?" the King noticed.

"I... I'm all right, Maharaj," the barber stammered, his hands shaking.

The barber picked up his razor, dipped it into a cup of water, and began to sharpen it on a whetstone.

Outside the window, a guard was reading the board aloud to himself. "You dip it in water and sharpen it... again and again. O Kalia, I can read your mind."

Inside the room, the barber froze in absolute terror. The poem! It knew his name, and exactly what he was doing!

"Forgive me, Maharaj!" Kalia screamed, dropping the razor and falling to his knees. "It was not my idea at all! They asked me to do it!"

The King frowned in confusion. "Do what?"

"Cut your throat!" the barber confessed, sobbing uncontrollably.

The King's eyes widened in realization. "Oh, I see it all now," he gasped. He immediately called for his guards. "Arrest this man and take down his statement! And summon the poet here! He is a true poet. He sees all!"

So it was that Buddhuram the simple farmer inadvertently uncovered an assassination plot and became a great hero. The King rewarded him with immense wealth, and he returned home to his astounded wife, rich and famous forever.

docs.title: Subhas Chandra Bose
docs.text:
Subhas Chandra Bose was born in Cuttack, Orissa, on January 23, 1897. He was a brilliant, inquisitive boy, the son of Prabhavati and Janaki Nath Bose, a famous lawyer.

When Subhas was five, he was admitted to the Protestant European School at Cuttack. He was an incredibly bright student and quickly became a favorite among the teachers. However, as he grew older, he began to notice the stark differences between the Indian students and their British teachers.

"Have you heard? Bengali has been made compulsory for Bengalees at the matric level," a classmate told him one day. "But Bengali is not taught at our school."

Realizing the importance of his roots, a twelve-year-old Subhas transferred to the Ravenshaw Collegiate School. "So you have never learnt Bengali," the headmaster noted.

"No. But I soon will, sir," Subhas promised.

The headmaster, Beni Madhav Das, was a true Indian nationalist. "Never forget, we are Indians and India is a great country," he would tell his students. "While you learn your lessons, learn to love your country too."

Inspired by his teacher, Subhas began to organize his fellow students. On the eve of the first anniversary of the martyrdom of Kshudiram Bose (a young Indian revolutionary hanged by the British), Subhas proposed a secret fast. "I propose that we fast tomorrow. What do you say?" The students enthusiastically agreed.

The British government, threatened by the rising nationalism in the school, quickly transferred Headmaster Beni Madhav Das away to Bengal. But the seeds of patriotism had already been sown in Subhas's heart.

At fifteen, Subhas discovered the writings of Swami Vivekananda. "How noble and inspiring," he thought, spending hours in deep meditation and yoga. But his soul was still restless. "I need a guru to guide me to my destiny."

After passing his matriculation exams and standing second in the entire university, Subhas traveled across India visiting famous pilgrim centers in search of a true spiritual guide. But his search proved futile. "These so-called saints are interested more in worldly matters than in spiritualism," he realized in disappointment.

He returned to Calcutta and joined the Presidency College. However, the racist behavior of the British professors deeply angered him. Professor Oten, in particular, was notoriously abusive.

"You natives have nothing in your brains but sawdust!" Oten yelled at them in class. "You are barbarians! It is we English who have civilized you."

The Indian students were appalled. When Oten physically assaulted a student, Subhas took charge. He organized a massive strike, rallying over a thousand students to boycott classes. It was the first organized student strike in modern India.

Though the strike forced Professor Oten to apologize, the British Principal retaliated by rusticating Subhas from the college. Undeterred, Subhas returned home and spent his time serving the poor and sick, eventually gaining readmission to the Scottish Church College with the help of supportive Indian leaders.

He graduated from Calcutta University at the age of 22 and traveled to England to appear for the prestigious Indian Civil Service (ICS) examination. He passed with flying colors, ranking fourth in the entire British Empire.

But to everyone's utter shock, Subhas refused the highly coveted position.

"I plan to resign from the service," he announced. "I appeared for my I.C.S. examination to educate myself—not to become a part of the bureaucratic machine of the British Empire."

His resignation created a massive sensation. Returning to India, he met with Mahatma Gandhi, who advised him to work with Deshabandhu Chittaranjan Das, the "uncrowned king of Bengal." Subhas quickly became Das's right-hand man, dedicating his life to the freedom struggle.

The British government, fearing Subhas's immense popularity and brilliant organizing skills, constantly harassed and arrested him. He was thrown into horrific jails, from Calcutta to the infamous Mandalay Jail in Burma, where the inhuman conditions caused him to contract tuberculosis.

When the British offered to release him on the condition that he leave for Europe, Subhas fiercely refused. "I refuse conditional freedom. I would rather die here." Forced by massive public protests, the government finally released him unconditionally.

As the years passed, Subhas's vision for India's freedom began to clash with Gandhi's path of strict non-violence. Subhas believed that the British would never leave India peacefully and that a massive armed struggle was necessary.

"The country is ready to fight," Subhas declared.

"My differences with the Mahatma will split the party. I must resign," he eventually decided, stepping down as Congress President and forming his own radical faction, the Forward Bloc. "We demand Indian independence, here and now."

The British, terrified of a violent uprising during World War II, placed Subhas under strict house arrest in Calcutta. But Subhas was a master of disguise and evasion.

On the midnight of January 7, 1941, a bearded Muslim man walked out of Subhas's house and got into a chauffeured car. He then boarded a train as an insurance agent named Moulvi Jiauddin. He traveled to Peshawar, posing as a deaf and dumb Pathan, crossed the rugged mountains into Afghanistan, and finally made his way to Berlin, Germany.

The man in disguise was none other than Subhas Chandra Bose.

In Germany, he met with Adolf Hitler to seek military help to liberate India. Realizing Germany was too far away to mount a direct attack on India, Subhas made a perilous journey. He traveled halfway across the world in a German U-boat (submarine), secretly transferring to a Japanese submarine in the middle of the ocean, and finally arrived in Southeast Asia.

There, he took command of the Indian National Army (INA), raising a massive fighting force from Indian prisoners of war and civilian volunteers. He formed the Azad Hind Government and established the Rani of Jhansi regiment, an all-women combat unit.

His fiery speeches electrified Indians across Asia. "Give me blood, I promise you freedom!" he roared to massive crowds. "Chalo Delhi! (Onward to Delhi!)"

The people responded by pouring out their wealth, donating their entire life savings and jewelry to fund the INA's war effort. They gave him the title "Netaji" (Respected Leader).

The INA marched bravely alongside the Japanese army, fighting their way through the dense jungles of Burma and finally entering Indian soil at Kohima and Imphal. They raised the Indian tricolor flag, fighting ferociously against the British.

However, the terrible monsoon rains and the eventual retreat of the Japanese forces cut off the INA's vital supply lines. Heavily outgunned and starving, the INA was forced into a tragic retreat.

When Japan officially surrendered in 1945, the INA was forced to lay down its arms. With the British and American forces hunting him, Netaji's life was in grave danger.

On August 17, 1945, Netaji boarded a Japanese plane for an unknown destination. What became of Netaji on that fateful trip remains a profound mystery. But his colossal sacrifice, unyielding courage, and his immortal cry of "Jai Hind!" awakened a fire in the Indian soul that ultimately drove the British out of India forever.

docs.title: Abhimanyu: Star-Crossed Prince
docs.text:
Abhimanyu was the handsome and much-loved son of the great Pandava warrior, Arjuna, and Subhadra, the sister of Lord Krishna. His father could not help loving him more than his other sons.

"Abhimanyu shall grow up to be the foremost among the Pandavas as well as the Yadavas!" Lord Krishna declared, holding the infant prince.

"Hush! One ought not to praise one's own child thus," Subhadra smiled modestly.

But misfortune soon befell the Pandavas. Their jealous cousins, the Kauravas, led by the wicked Prince Duryodhana, plotted against them.

"The Pandavas are becoming too powerful," Duryodhana fumed. "I have it! Yudhishthira has a weakness for gambling. Let us invite him to a game of dice and make the dice obey us."

The Kauravas carried out their wicked plot with the help of their cunning uncle, Shakuni. They cheated Yudhishthira of his wealth, his kingdom, and forced the Pandavas into a humiliating thirteen-year exile in the forest.

Abhimanyu was a mere child when these tragic events took place. But he was well looked after by his mother, Subhadra, in the kingdom of Dwaraka.

"Your valour shall one day avenge this humiliation," she would tell him.

He grew up to be a valiant youth and Krishna's favourite nephew. "I shall fight the Kauravas and win back our kingdom," the young prince vowed.

He was loved and admired by all who knew him. He had inherited Yudhishthira's patience, Krishna's conduct, Bhima's strength, the gentleness of Nakula and Sahadeva, and his father Arjuna's stunning looks and prowess with a bow.

Meanwhile, the thirteen years of exile passed. The Pandavas had spent their final year in disguise at the court of King Virata. When Arjuna helped Virata's son triumph over a Kaurava attack, the grateful King offered his daughter, Princess Uttara, in marriage.

Arjuna respectfully declined for himself, as he had been her teacher, but accepted her for his brave son, Abhimanyu. The wedding was solemnised amidst great feasting, attended by a host of friendly kings.

After the wedding, the Pandavas held a council. Yudhishthira wanted to win back their kingdom without bloodshed, but King Duryodhana was obstinate.

"I refuse them even an inch of land," Duryodhana sneered. "Let them fight and take it if they can."

And so, the terrible war of the Mahabharata began on the battlefield of Kurukshetra.

On the very first day, the Kaurava grand-sire, Bheeshma, created absolute havoc among the Pandava troops. Hearing of this, young Abhimanyu fearlessly charged at the invincible elder.

"A mere stripling of a boy, but what a fine warrior!" Bheeshma admired, as Abhimanyu's arrows seemingly flew from everywhere at once. The young prince distinguished himself brilliantly on his first day.

The battle raged on for days. On the tenth day, Bheeshma finally fell, and the great teacher Dronacharya was made the supreme commander of the Kaurava armies.

Duryodhana demanded a singular boon from Drona: "Capture Yudhishthira alive!"

Drona tried fiercely, but failed. "We can never take Yudhishthira as long as Arjuna is by his side," Drona admitted.

Duryodhana hatched a cunning plan. He had his allies draw Arjuna away to a distant part of the battlefield. With Arjuna gone, Drona immediately formed his troops into the impregnable, deadly circular formation known as the Chakravyuha and mercilessly attacked the vulnerable Pandavas.

Desperate, Yudhishthira approached young Abhimanyu. "Only you, Arjuna, Krishna, and Pradyumna know how to break through Drona's circular formation. None of them is here, but you. Son, will you lead us?"

"I will gladly try to break through the formation," Abhimanyu agreed bravely. "But... I do not know how to come out of it, if I am overpowered and in danger."

"Break the formation and make way for us to enter," Yudhishthira promised. "We will follow close upon your heels and protect you."

"I will break through it and slaughter the Kauravas!" the valiant prince declared.

He commanded his charioteer to drive straight at Drona's troops. "The Pandavas have placed a heavy burden on you today," his charioteer warned. "Think well before you proceed. Drona is an adept in the art of warfare."

"I am Arjuna's son and Krishna's nephew," Abhimanyu replied fearlessly. "Drive on."

Like a proud young lion falling upon a herd of elephants, Abhimanyu rushed at the Chakravyuha. He fought with blinding speed, smashing through the outer rings and plunging deep into the heart of the enemy formation, right under the nose of Dronacharya himself.

"He is indeed the greatest among archers!" Drona marvelled. "He is capable of destroying an entire army if he chooses!"

But alas for the young hero, before Yudhishthira and the Pandava warriors could follow him through the breach, the powerful Kaurava ally, King Jayadratha, swiftly moved in and completely sealed the gap.

Abhimanyu was trapped alone inside the deadliest formation in the world.

Unfazed, the young lion fought with the fury of a hurricane. He felled countless Kaurava warriors, covering the earth with broken chariots and mutilated bodies. Terrified by his unstoppable onslaught, Kaurava troops tried to flee.

Seeing his army being routed by a single boy, Duryodhana grew furious and rushed at him. Drona, alarmed for the King's safety, sent a massive host of warriors to rescue Duryodhana. Enraged that his prize target was snatched away, Abhimanyu vented his fiery wrath on everyone in his path.

In a shameful violation of the ancient codes of war, the greatest Kaurava generals—Drona, Karna, Ashwatthama, and others—surrounded the lone boy and attacked him all at once.

But even then, Abhimanyu held them off, his bow a blur of motion. He killed the warrior Asmaka, pierced the mighty Karna's armour, wounded King Shalya so badly he could not move, and shattered the chariot of Shalya's brother with a single arrow.

"He stands unequalled as a bowman," Drona admitted in awe. "If he wishes he can wipe out this whole host, single-handed."

Duryodhana overheard and screamed at his generals. "Listen to how partial Dronacharya is towards Arjuna's son! We must kill the boy soon!"

Duryodhana's wicked brother, Duhshasana, charged in, but quickly fell senseless under Abhimanyu's arrows. Next came Karna, but Abhimanyu intercepted all his arrows and forced him to retreat. Even Duryodhana's own brave son, Lakshmana, rushed at the boy, but a sharp arrow from Abhimanyu pierced him, killing the Kaurava prince instantly.

"KILL! KILL THE WICKED ABHIMANYU!" Duryodhana screamed in blind grief.

The entire might of the Kaurava army descended on the boy. "Tell us how to kill him before he slays us all!" Karna asked Drona desperately.

"You will never be able to pierce his armour," Drona advised shamefully. "Aim at the reins of his horses and cut them off. He will be disabled. Then attack from behind."

Karna cowardly shot an arrow from behind, slicing Abhimanyu's bowstring. The others quickly killed his horses and his charioteer.

Stranded on the ground, the unarmed prince grabbed his heavy sword and shield, whirling them to hold his ground against the massive army. Soon, an arrow from Drona broke his sword, and an arrow from Karna shattered his shield.

Totally disabled but undaunted, the fierce teenager picked up the heavy wooden wheel of his broken chariot. He whirled it above his head, roaring like a wounded tiger at his unjust, cowardly enemies.

But arrows rained down from all directions, shattering the wheel to splinters.

Bleeding from a hundred wounds, Abhimanyu picked up a fallen mace. Duhshasana's powerful son closed in with his own mace. The two warriors clashed, striking each other so hard that both fell to the ground, stunned.

But Duhshasana's son was the first to recover. As Abhimanyu struggled to his hands and knees, the Kaurava prince brought his heavy mace crashing down on the young hero's skull.

The star-crossed prince fell dead, his blood staining the earth of Kurukshetra.

Like savages, the Kaurava warriors danced around his dead body, celebrating the murder of a lone, unarmed child. But their victory was incredibly short-lived. Not a single one of the men who cowardly murdered Abhimanyu outlived the war. Arjuna, Bhima, and the mighty Pandavas brutally avenged his death in the terrible battles that followed, utterly annihilating the Kaurava army.

And the breathtaking bravery of the young lion, Abhimanyu, is remembered and sung about till this very day.

docs.title: The Adventures of Agad Dutta
docs.text:
One day, Dridha Prahari, one of ancient Kaushambi's most renowned teachers, presented his brilliant young disciple, Agad Datta, at the royal court. The gifted youth performed spectacular feats of archery before the King, shooting moving targets blindfolded.

"He certainly is a skilful archer!" the King applauded. "Give him a hundred gold coins for his trouble and send him away."

But Agad Datta refused the gold. "Maharaj, I came here to win your appreciation, not your gold," the young man said seriously.

Before the King could respond, the Chief of the Guards burst into the courtroom. "Maharaj, the dacoits have struck again!" he cried. "We've lost everything but our lives. We've complained to the chief of the guards, but the situation hasn't improved."

The King turned to Agad Datta. "Now tell me, of what use are your feats of archery when dacoits harass my citizens with absolute impunity? Maharaj, I have tried my best, but the thugs are elusive," the King sighed.

"Give me a chance, Maharaj!" Agad Datta offered bravely. "I'll try to capture the leader of the gang."

"If you do that, you will win my deep appreciation and my eternal gratitude," the King promised.

As Agad Datta left the palace, he planned his next move. The best way to avoid attracting attention would be to change into some old clothes. I'll do so and then go to a quiet place to think.

He dressed in rags and wandered to a ruined temple in a forest on the outskirts of the city. Thieves usually choose gambling-dens, liquor shops, old gardens and temple ruins as hide-outs. I must first visit these.

Just then, a bearded Sadhu holding a heavy iron trident came towards him.

This man looks worried, and poor. He'd make a good disciple! the Sadhu thought. He approached Agad Datta. "Son, who are you? Why do you look so worried?"

"I'm from Ujjayini. I've lost all my wealth here in Kaushambi," Agad Datta lied perfectly. "What shall I tell him? Not the truth in any case."

"Don't worry," the Sadhu said kindly. "I'll show you how to get it back. And if you are clever enough, you'll get back more than you've lost. Come. Let's go to the city. We have a lot to do before dawn."

As they walked towards the city, a sudden roar of a wild animal echoed through the trees. To Agad Datta's complete amazement, the Sadhu grabbed his trident, twisted the stem, and drew a long, gleaming sword hidden perfectly inside the hollow iron staff!

What! A sadhu armed with a secret weapon! Strange! Very strange! Agad Datta realized.

"Ha! That was a false alarm! Let's go on," the Sadhu laughed, sliding the sword back into the trident.

The Sadhu led Agad Datta into the dark city and stopped in the backyard of a massive mansion. "I'll have to get into the house," the Sadhu whispered.

"Shall I knock at the door, Gurudev?" Agad Datta asked innocently.

"No! That would unnecessarily disturb everyone. You'll soon see."

The Sadhu pulled out tools and expertly began breaking through the thick brick wall of the mansion.

He's breaking into the house! He... he's a thug! Agad Datta realized, his heart pounding. There you are! That's exactly what I propose to do, my friend. I'm certain I won't have to go much further in search of my man!

After some time, the fake Sadhu came crawling back out of the hole with a heavy bundle of stolen gold. "Here. Take this and follow me."

The Sadhu led him deep into the forest to a secret hide-out. Several other thugs were already there, counting their own loot.

"Ah, my friends! I see that you have made a better haul than I!" the Sadhu laughed. "But I have found a new disciple!"

As dawn broke, the tired thugs lay down to sleep under the trees. Agad Datta pretended to sleep but kept his eyes slightly open.

So he is the leader of the thugs! Agad Datta thought. I wonder what he's up to now!

He watched as the Sadhu quietly stood up, pulled out a small pouch, and began sprinkling a strange powder on the faces of his sleeping men.

What's that? Why is he sprinkling powder on their faces? Agad Datta realized the horrible truth. He plans to kill his men and keep all the loot for himself! The scoundrel!

As the Sadhu drew his hidden sword to slaughter the sleeping men, Agad Datta sprang up like lightning. "No you don't, you rascal!"

Agad Datta quickly snatched the Sadhu's own sword from his hands and struck him down with a single, fatal blow.

Hearing the commotion, the other thugs woke up. Realizing that their leader had just tried to kill them, they immediately surrendered to Agad Datta. One of the thugs pointed to the dead Sadhu.

"My son... you shouldn't have done that," the dying thug groaned. "My sister will appear... give her my sword. She will marry you... and you will become... the master of... all my wealth..." With those words, the leader died.

Following the dead man's instructions, Agad Datta walked to the corner of a nearby crematorium and called out. A beautiful maiden emerged from an underground passage. Agad Datta handed her the sword.

"Yes, I recognize it. It's my brother's sword," she smiled. "You are welcome, stranger!"

The villain! He's slain my brother, she thought fiercely to herself. But I must not betray my emotions. I must remain calm.

She led Agad Datta through the underground passage to a luxurious cellar. "You seem to be tired. Why don't you rest on this bed while I warm up some food for you?" she offered sweetly.

Perhaps, this is a death-trap. I must not trust her, Agad Datta realized.

As soon as she left the room, he quickly rolled off the bed and hid in the corner. Just in time! With a loud CRASH!, a massive, heavy stone block plummeted from the ceiling, completely crushing the bed where he was supposed to be sleeping.

"Oh, God!" the maiden gasped, running back into the room.

Agad Datta stepped out of the shadows. "No, you haven't, wicked one! I'm still alive."

Realizing she was defeated, she fell to her knees. "No human being could have survived that! Lord, I beg you, please forgive me."

Agad Datta took her and all the recovered wealth directly to the King.

"Maharaj! I found the leader of the thugs but I couldn't capture him alive," Agad Datta reported, presenting the vast treasure. He pointed to the maiden. "But I request you to pardon her."

"How can I refuse you, Agad Datta?" the King smiled in immense relief. "She may stay in the palace and be my Queen's maid."

The King rewarded Agad Datta handsomely. "Agad Datta, my guards need a Chief of your calibre. Why don't you make Kaushambi your home?"

"Maharaj, my father was Charioteer to the King of Ujjayini," Agad Datta bowed respectfully. "It was my mother's wish that I should learn the art of warfare here, and later acquire the office once held by my father. Now that I have completed my training, I would like to return home."

Before leaving Kaushambi, Agad Datta married a beautiful girl named Shyama. With his new wife by his side, he rode out of Kaushambi in a grand chariot.

On the road, he met a group of wealthy travellers. "We welcome you, Agad Datta," they greeted him. "We have also heard of your skill in wielding weapons and of your presence of mind. If we could accompany you, we'd have nothing to fear from wild animals and dacoits."

Agad Datta happily agreed. A while later, another Sadhu walking on the road asked to join them. "Could I come with you to Ujjayini? I have fifty gold coins given to me by a sadhu, and I fear for my safety."

"You are welcome," Agad Datta smiled.

But Agad Datta noticed something deeply suspicious about the new Sadhu. Who is that young man? the Sadhu thought maliciously, staring at Agad Datta. Don't you know? He is the famous Agad Datta of Ujjayini! The villain who killed my friend! The friend who gave me the fifty gold coins! My search has ended.

Towards evening, they camped in a dense jungle. The Sadhu cheerfully offered to cook for everyone. "My friends have been generous. There's enough for all of us," the Sadhu smiled, bringing out a huge vessel of delicious, sweet kheer.

As soon as the kheer was portioned out, Agad Datta stopped his wife. "Shyama! Wait! It's not wise to eat food cooked by strangers. Don't eat it!"

"I'm hungry. But I won't eat it, if you say so," Shyama agreed.

The other hungry travellers, however, eagerly swallowed their portions to the very last gulp. Within minutes, they all fell into a deep, unbreakable slumber.

"The journey must have made them tired and sleepy," Shyama yawned.

"Or was it the kheer?" Agad Datta whispered, drawing his sword. "I'm sure the kheer was drugged."

When it became pitch-dark, the Sadhu silently stood up and pulled a sharp sword from the hollow stem of his trident!

"Shyama, pretend to be asleep, but remain alert!" Agad Datta whispered. "I don't trust that sadhu."

As the Sadhu raised his sword to execute the sleeping travellers and steal their wealth, Agad Datta leaped out of the shadows. "You scoundrel!"

A fierce, brutal sword fight followed in the moonlight. Agad Datta's superior skills quickly overwhelmed the murderous thug, and the villain was struck down.

When the sun rose and the drugged travellers finally woke from their stupor, they gasped in shock at the dead body. "Why, this is the dreaded dacoit Dhanpujak! He must have been out to avenge the death of his friend!"

Agad Datta had saved them all. When he finally reached Ujjayini, his mother gave him a tearful, joyous welcome.

At the royal court, the King of Ujjayini welcomed him with open arms. "Agad Datta, Ujjayini is proud of you. The coveted post of Royal Charioteer is now yours!"

"I am honoured, Maharaj," Agad Datta smiled, taking his rightful place in the kingdom.

docs.title: The Royal Priest
docs.text:
The Raja of Simhapur loved to hear of the grand adventures of merchants who sailed to distant, exotic lands.

One day, an adventurous merchant named Bhadramitra of Padminikhet found himself at the Raja's court, regaling the King with tales of his voyages.

"I do envy you!" the Raja smiled. "That was an exciting adventure!"

"And this has been an interesting game. I must thank you for your hospitality, Maharaj," Bhadramitra bowed. "When will you be setting sail again?"

"Tomorrow," Bhadramitra replied. "That reminds me—I need your help. I have some gold with me which I want to keep with someone trustworthy while I am at sea."

"You can safely leave it with my royal priest, Shribhuti," the Raja suggested warmly. "A more honest man would be hard to find!"

So Bhadramitra confidently left a heavy casket containing all his gold with Shribhuti, the royal priest, and set sail in search of trade in other lands.

Over the years, Bhadramitra bought and sold a vast variety of exotic goods, amassing a great deal of wealth. Finally, he was on his homeward voyage back to Simhapur.

"The good King of Simhapur and his honest priest will be pleased with the gifts I have for them," Bhadramitra mused happily, watching the horizon.

But as he mused, the skies suddenly darkened, and a violent storm broke out. The ship was mercilessly tossed about by mountainous waves, and, in spite of all the crew's desperate efforts to save it, the ship sank.

Bhadramitra barely managed to grab a floating plank of wood. Clinging to the plank for dear life, he floated on the rough seas for several agonizing hours. Miraculously, he was finally washed ashore, exhausted and half-dead, not far from the city of Simhapur.

Hours later, late at night, the royal priest Shribhuti heard a feeble knock at his door.

"Who could it be at this hour?" the priest muttered, opening the door.

A soaking wet, shivering man in rags stood there. "It's me, Bhadramitra. I have... come for my... casket."

Shribhuti stared at the beggar. Knowing the merchant had lost everything, a wave of wicked greed washed over the priest.

"Away with you!" Shribhuti yelled, violently shoving the exhausted merchant back into the street. "I don't know any Bhadramitra. You've come to the wrong place."

"No! No! You can't do this to me!" Bhadramitra cried in sheer despair. "I've lost... everything. I'm... hungry... thirsty. I need my gold. Please give me my casket..."

Ignoring his pitiful cries, Shribhuti slammed the heavy door shut and locked it.

The next morning, Bhadramitra squatted outside the royal palace. "Then I'll squat here and demand justice till I get it," he vowed.

When Shribhuti arrived for his daily duties, Bhadramitra raised his voice and continued to loudly accuse him. "The royal priest has swindled me of my wealth! I want justice!"

The cries became louder and louder until even the Raja inside the palace was able to hear them. "What's all this? What is he shouting about?" the Raja asked the priest.

"Ignore him, Maharaj!" Shribhuti lied smoothly. "He is a madman. You know that whatever I earn each day, I give away in charity. Would I swindle anyone?"

But Bhadramitra persisted in crying for justice day and night, refusing to leave the palace steps. Finally, the Raja could no longer ignore him and turned to his clever Prime Minister for help.

The next morning, the Minister walked out to Bhadramitra. "Justice... I want justice. The royal priest has..." Bhadramitra croaked, his throat completely hoarse.

"Why don't you come home with me, my good man?" the Minister offered kindly. "You could bathe, change your clothes—and then tell me your story."

At the Minister's house, Bhadramitra gratefully ate a hot meal and narrated the entire tragedy.

"So that's it," the Minister frowned. "Do you have any proof? Any document or receipt to support your story?"

"I'm afraid I don't, good sir. I didn't think it necessary when the man was recommended by the Raja himself."

The Minister took the matter back to the King. "... Shribhuti flatly denies that he took any gold from the merchant. And we have absolutely no proof!"

"Then what can we do?" the Raja sighed.

The Minister smiled slyly. "I have an idea. Why don't you invite Shribhuti to a game of dice tomorrow, and..." The Minister laid out a brilliant plot to trap the greedy priest.

"Excellent! I'll do as you say," the Raja laughed.

The next day, Shribhuti arrived at the palace, completely unaware of the trap. "Ah, Shribhuti! There you are! Come, let's begin right away," the Raja greeted him cheerfully.

They sat down on the floor cushions to play dice. After a while, the Raja wiped his brow. "It's rather warm, isn't it? How clammy my palms are! I'd better remove my signet ring." The Raja took off his heavy royal ring and placed it on the floor.

"It is warm. I'll remove mine too," Shribhuti agreed, slipping his own distinct gold ring off his finger and placing it next to the Raja's.

As they continued to play, the Raja purposely bumped his hand against the rings, scattering them across the board. "Oh! Oh! How clumsy of me! I'll..."

"Don't worry, Maharaj, I'll pick them up," Shribhuti offered eagerly.

Just then, right on cue, the Prime Minister walked into the room. "Maharaj, pardon me for interrupting your game, but I have an urgent matter to discuss with you in private."

"You will excuse me, Shribhuti. After all, royal duties must come first," the Raja said, getting up and leaving the room with the Minister.

Once out of sight, the Minister pulled something from his pocket. "Here's the ring! I did manage to secretly switch them while he was distracted. I leave the rest to you."

The Raja smiled and went back to finish the game.

Meanwhile, a few minutes later, a royal attendant knocked on the door of Shribhuti's house across the city. Shribhuti's wife answered the door.

"The priest has asked for the casket of gold coins to be brought to the palace," the attendant demanded officially.

"But... but..." the wife hesitated, knowing the dark secret of the stolen casket.

"Why do you hesitate?" the attendant asked sternly. "If you don't trust me, take a look at this. He asked me to show it to you if you were hesitant."

The attendant held up Shribhuti's personal gold ring!

"His ring!" the wife gasped. "He wouldn't want anyone to see it! Of course, he wouldn't!"

Panicking, she rushed inside, grabbed the heavy casket, and handed it to the attendant. "Please deliver it safely. And... and cover it with your cloth. He wouldn't want anyone to see it."

Back at the palace, the attendant carried the covered casket straight into the game room and stood before the Raja and Shribhuti.

"Do you recognise that casket, Shribhuti?" the Raja asked coldly as the cloth was pulled away.

Shribhuti's jaw dropped in absolute horror. Before he could stammer a lie, the Minister walked into the room, leading the merchant Bhadramitra.

"Perhaps you do recognise Bhadramitra, the merchant of Padminikhet," the Minister smiled sharply. "Is your memory refreshed, or does it need another shock?"

The Raja glared at his disgraced priest. "Come, take your casket, Bhadramitra," the Raja said kindly. "While I am truly sorry for the misery we have caused you, I am deeply grateful to you for exposing this total hypocrite."

The Raja turned his fury on Shribhuti. "Shribhuti, I don't know who is more embarrassed. You, the royal priest who could act like a common cheat... or I, the Raja who recommended you for your honesty! Go away! And never set foot in my kingdom again."

docs.title: Agastya
docs.text:
Agastya is perhaps the most famous of the Rishis of India, revered in both the North and the South. Though almost a dwarf in size, he possessed incredible spiritual strength and wisdom.

One day, Sage Agastya came upon a strange and disturbing sight in a forest. Several men were hanging upside down from the branches of a tree over a deep, dark pit.

"Who could they be?" Agastya wondered.

"We are your ancestors," the men cried out pitifully.

"Why are you hanging here like this?" Agastya asked in horror.

"Because you are not yet married!" they explained. "Our souls will have no peace till we are assured of the continuance of our line. A son born of you can redeem us. And you too will escape this hell."

"O revered ones, I shall act for your redemption," Agastya promised solemnly.

As Agastya walked away, he pondered his vow. "The mother of my son shall be the most worthy of women."

He searched long and hard in all the three worlds but could not find the ideal woman. "I shall have to create her, taking in all that is most beautiful," he decided. Using his immense spiritual powers, Agastya created a beautiful baby girl.

At that time, the King of Vidarbha was performing severe penances to have a child. "The child I have created shall take birth as the daughter of this king," Agastya decreed.

When the Queen of Vidarbha gave birth, the King was overjoyed. The Brahmans were deeply impressed by the extraordinary beauty of the child. "She shall be called Lopamudra."

Lopamudra grew up into a stunningly beautiful and virtuous young woman. The King began searching for a worthy husband for her. Meanwhile, Agastya realized it was time. "Lopamudra must now be ready for marriage. I shall go to her father and ask for her hand."

When Agastya approached the King of Vidarbha, the King was deeply troubled. I wish to marry and beget a son. Will you give me your daughter? Agastya had asked. How can I give my beautiful daughter to an ascetic living in the forest? the King worried. But I dare not displease Agastya.

Just then, Lopamudra herself entered the room. "Father, why do you hesitate? I am willing to marry the great sage."

Relieved, the King agreed, and Agastya married Lopamudra. After the wedding, Agastya looked at her rich clothing. "Lopamudra, your royal robes do not befit a sage's wife. Discard them."

Without a moment's hesitation, Lopamudra cast off her costly silks and jewels. "Henceforth I shall wear only bark and skin and rags, my lord."

They left Vidarbha and travelled to Agastya's remote hermitage at Gangotri in the Himalayas. The days passed, and Lopamudra served her husband excellently through his severe penances.

"Dear wife, you have pleased me well. You will soon become my equal in spiritual merit," Agastya praised her one day.

"My lord, I too wish to be pleased," Lopamudra replied gently. "We are householders. It is certainly not wrong for us to possess wealth. I would like to live as I did in my father's house."

"Then I will go out in search of wealth. Wait here for me," Agastya agreed.

Agastya first went to King Srutarva, who was renowned for his riches. "O great one, what can I do for you?" the King bowed.

"I have come to you for wealth. Give me what you can spare," Agastya said.

Srutarva was generous but honest. "I have no wealth to spare, O Sage. However, you may take as much as you want from what I have."

Agastya, using his spiritual insight, saw that the King's accounts were perfectly balanced. "If I take anything from this King, I will be depriving others," Agastya realized. He turned to the King. "Let us go and see if King Brihadastha can help me."

But King Brihadastha, too, had no wealth to spare without harming his subjects. "Perhaps King Trasadasyu would be able to help," Brihadastha suggested. "Let us all go to him."

However, King Trasadasyu was also in the same position. "My revenue and expenditure are equal," the King explained. "But I shall come to your aid if you need it."

"No. I do not want to acquire wealth by depriving others," Agastya refused.

The three Kings conferred with each other. "There is an Asura called Ilvala who has a great deal of wealth. Let us go to him," they suggested.

Ilvala was a wealthy but wicked Asura. He and his brother Vatapi deeply hated Brahmans and had vowed to kill as many as they could using a dark, magical trick.

"Vatapi, I hear that the great Brahman sage, Agastya, is coming here today," Ilvala laughed wickedly. "Get ready to change your form! I'll become a goat as usual. You will kill and cook me... and feed you to the Brahman. Then when I call you... I'll tear his stomach and come rushing out... and another hated Brahman, the greatest of them all, will be destroyed!"

When Agastya and the three Kings arrived, Ilvala pretended to be a gracious host. "Welcome, O Sage, and you, O Kings. Come, I have cooked a special meal in your honour."

The three Kings, who knew of Ilvala's deadly trick, were terrified. "Alas! I never thought he would dare do it to the great sage," they whispered. "We must warn Agastya."

But Agastya calmly stopped them. "Do not worry. I will be safe."

Agastya sat down and heartily ate the massive meal of cooked meat. "Hm..m.m! The tastiest meal I have ever eaten!" he smiled, finishing the very last morsel.

And the last, you hated Brahman, Ilvala thought gleefully. He stepped forward and shouted the magical command. "Vatapi, O Vatapi, come out!"

Agastya patted his stomach and let out a massive, thunderous burp. BURRRRRP.

Ilvala waited for his brother to burst out of the sage's stomach. Nothing happened. He panicked. "Vatapi! O Vatapi! Come out. I, Ilvala, am calling you!"

Agastya burst into laughter. "Ha! Ha! How can he come out? I have already digested him."

Realizing his brother was gone forever and his magic had failed against the great sage, Ilvala accepted utter defeat. Trembling, he fell to his knees. "Why have you come here? What can I do for you?"

"We know that you are wealthy. These Kings and I need wealth," Agastya commanded. "Give us what you can without depriving any other."

Ilvala was quiet for a moment, silently calculating. "I shall give the Kings 10,000 cows each and as many gold coins. To Agastya - 20,000 cows and as many gold coins. I will also give him my golden chariot and horses," he thought to himself.

He looked up at Agastya. "If you can guess what I intend to give you, that will be yours."

Agastya, with his divine spiritual insight, smiled. "You intend to give the Kings 10,000 cows each and as many gold coins. And to me, 20,000 cows, as many coins, and your golden chariot and horses."

Ilvala bowed his head. "You have won them all. Take them."

Agastya and the Kings loaded the vast treasure onto the magnificent golden chariot and rode away. "I have heard that Ilvala's horses are the most fleet-footed in the land," one King remarked. In no time at all, they reached Agastya's hermitage at Gangotri. The Kings took their share and returned to their cities.

Agastya went to his wife. "I have brought what you wanted, Lopamudra. We will now live as you wish."

A few years later, Lopamudra gave birth to a radiant son. "At last the promise I made to my ancestors is fulfilled," Agastya smiled, his lineage secure.

One day, while Agastya, Lopamudra, and their son were living peacefully at Gangotri, a massive crisis threatened the world. The towering Vindhya Mountain, located in central India, became deeply jealous of Mount Meru.

"O glorious sun, you go around Mount Meru every day and honour him," the Vindhya Mountain complained to the Sun God. "I too would like to be honoured in the same manner."

"My path has been assigned to me since creation," the Sun replied patiently. "I do no special honour to Meru."

The Sun's dismissal infuriated the arrogant mountain. "Then I shall grow taller and block your path!"

The Vindhya Mountain began to grow rapidly, stretching higher and higher into the sky. The Devas (gods) in heaven were extremely alarmed. "This unchecked growth of Vindhya will soon plunge the earth in absolute darkness! It will upset the balance of creation."

They pleaded with the mountain, but Vindhya was stubborn. "That is not my concern," it scoffed, continuing to grow.

"Let us hasten to Agastya," the desperate Devas finally decided. "He is the only one who can help us."

The Devas rushed to Agastya's hermitage and begged for his intervention. "Do not worry. I will do all I can," Agastya promised. He turned to his wife. "Come, dear one. Let us go and see if we can stop him. It is our duty."

Agastya and his family travelled to the foot of the massive, looming Vindhya Mountain. When the mountain saw the great, revered Sage approaching, it respectfully bowed its peak down low to allow him to pass.

"O great mountain, I wish to cross over you to the southern country," Agastya said softly. "Wait till I return."

"O great Sage, I will stop increasing my size till you return from the south," the mountain promised, remaining bowed.

Agastya, Lopamudra, and their son crossed over the bowed mountain and stepped into the beautiful, lush lands of South India.

"Lopamudra, we will not return to the north," Agastya smiled. "Then Vindhya will never be able to grow any taller."

And so, Agastya and his family settled in the country south of the Vindhya Mountain, bringing his immense wisdom and grace to the southern lands forever.

Some time later, a ferocious group of underwater Asuras known as the Kalkeyas, led by the terrible demon Vritra, began terrorizing the three worlds. The Devas, desperately needing help, sought the advice of Lord Brahma.

"Go to the great sage Dadhichi and ask him for his bones," Brahma instructed Indra, the King of the Devas. "Take the bones to the divine architect Twashtri and ask him to make a weapon for you with them."

Sage Dadhichi willingly sacrificed his life, and Twashtri forged his indestructible bones into the supreme weapon, the Vajra (Thunderbolt). Armed with the Vajra, Indra and the Devas attacked the Asuras. A terrifying battle ensued, and Indra finally hurled the mighty Vajra at Vritra.

BOOM!

The Vajra struck Vritra perfectly, and the great Asura leader fell dead.

Panic-stricken without their leader, the surviving Kalkeyas turned and fled. "We are doomed! Run! Run into the deep ocean. They will not find us here!"

The Kalkeyas hid deep in the dark, impenetrable trenches of the ocean. "We must kill all good men on earth," they plotted underwater. "Their death will destroy the whole universe. We must do our work by night, so we will not be found out."

Under the cover of darkness, the Kalkeyas began a terrifying campaign of slaughter, emerging from the sea at night to murder sages and good men, and vanishing back into the depths before dawn.

"The good men on earth are being destroyed," the Devas wept to Lord Vishnu. "We do not know who the culprits are."

"The Kalkeyas have taken shelter under the sea," Vishnu revealed. "They are the culprits. They cannot be killed as long as they hide in the ocean bed."

"What should we do?" Indra despaired.

"You must find out some means to dry up the entire ocean," Vishnu advised.

"But who can accomplish such an impossible task?" Indra gasped.

Then, the Devas realized there was only one being in all the three worlds capable of such a feat. "Who else, but Agastya!"

The hopeful Devas travelled south to Agastya's hermitage. "Great one, you consumed the evil Vatapi. You prevented Vindhya from plunging the world in eternal darkness. So we have come to you for help."

"What do you want me to do?" Agastya asked calmly.

"We want you to drink the ocean! Then we shall be able to kill our enemies, the evil Kalkeyas."

"I shall do what you desire as it will benefit the world," Agastya agreed at once. "Array yourselves for war while I drink the ocean."

Agastya walked to the edge of the roaring sea, scooped water into his hands, and simply began to drink. In a matter of moments, the waters receded, and the entire ocean bed became completely dry and visible.

The Kalkeyas, suddenly stripped of their watery shield, gasped in sheer terror. "Wh... what! Where is all the water going? RUN!"

But the Devas were well prepared, waiting on the newly exposed sea floor. "Attack! Do not spare a single one of them!" Indra commanded.

A terrible battle ensued, and without the cover of the ocean, the Kalkeyas were quickly and utterly vanquished.

Agastya watched the victory of the Devas with deep happiness. "My task is accomplished," the great sage smiled. "I may now go back to my penances. Peace be with you."

Thus, once more, the just and wise Sage Agastya had suppressed evil and protected the good of the universe.

docs.title: Shiva and Arjuna
docs.text:
Deep in the quiet, peaceful Himalayan retreat of Indrakeela, an ascetic arrived and began observing severe penances. The other young ascetics living in the forest watched him closely from a distance.

"Who could this stranger be?" they wondered. "Look at his bow! Could he be Arjuna, the Pandava?"

It was indeed the great Pandava warrior, Arjuna. He had retreated to the mountains to propitiate Lord Shiva, hoping to earn divine grace to win back his kingdom from the Kauravas.

"Om Namo Shivaya," Arjuna chanted, day and night. For days, he persisted in deep meditation. As the months passed, his penance became so intense and severe that a terrible, burning heat began to radiate from his body, spreading far and wide and choking the whole forest with smoke.

Unable to bear the heat, the other ascetics of Indrakeela travelled all the way to Mount Kailasa, the icy abode of Lord Shiva.

"Lord, grant Arjuna his wish, and relieve us of this suffering," they pleaded.

"So be it," Lord Shiva nodded.

When the ascetics departed, Goddess Parvati turned to Shiva. "What does Arjuna want, my Lord?"

"He wants celestial weapons," Shiva explained.

"Can he wield them, my Lord?"

"I will find out by testing him," Shiva smiled. "I'll appear before him as a Kirata (a tribal hunter) and engage him in a duel."

"May I accompany you?" Parvati asked excitedly.

"You may, in the guise of a Kirata woman."

Hearing of this great combat, the fierce, skeletal hordes of Shiva also begged to join. Accompanied by his cheering hordes disguised as Kirata women, Lord Shiva and Parvati approached the forests of Indrakeela.

As they neared Arjuna's hermitage, a massive wild boar suddenly ran past them. Unbeknownst to anyone, the boar was actually the deadly demon Mookasura in disguise, sent to assassinate Arjuna.

"Hah! A fit target for my arrow!" Shiva announced, raising his bow.

But the wily, swift boar outdistanced the Kirata and charged straight into the quiet hermitage, destroying everything in its path and driving the ascetics helter-skelter.

"It is Mookasura again! Now in the form of a boar!" the ascetics panicked.

Disturbed by the massive din, Arjuna opened his eyes, grabbed his mighty bow, and took aim at the charging beast.

Just as he was about to shoot, Shiva burst through the trees. "Stop! It's mine," the Kirata yelled. "Leave it to me. I was stalking it."

"Hunters do not beg," Arjuna replied coldly.

Both warriors released their arrows at the exact same instant. The boar was caught perfectly between the two unerring arrows and fell dead, instantly revealing its true, terrifying demonic form.

"Victory to our Lord! Your arrow killed it," the Kirata women cheered, dancing around Shiva.

The wild exultation amused Arjuna. "O Kirata, does not this thick forest terrify your women folk? And you their only escort? Perhaps you are terrified. You do appear soft!"

"Soft? Me? Didn't you see the force of my arrow piercing the boar?" Shiva laughed.

"It was our chief's arrow that killed the boar," the Kirata women insisted.

Arjuna was deeply enraged by the insult. "All right, Kirata. We will settle once and for all who is the better shot."

A ferocious duel began. Arrows whizzed past as the two great archers matched their incredible skills. But to Arjuna's utter shock, no matter how many arrows he fired, he could not land a single hit on the tribal hunter.

After a while, Arjuna's quiver was completely empty. "My quiver is empty and not a scratch yet on the Kirata," Arjuna thought in disbelief.

"O mighty archer, shall I lend you a few arrows?" the Kirata mocked.

Furious, Arjuna lunged forward and, in a deft move, caught the Kirata in his bowstring. But the next moment, the Kirata effortlessly wrested the heavy bow from Arjuna's hands and tossed it away like a twig.

"The ascetic is beaten!" the Kirata women danced for joy.

Undaunted, Arjuna drew his heavy sword and rushed at the Kirata. He brought the sword crashing down on the hunter's head, but with a loud CLANG, the supposedly invincible sword shattered into pieces against the Kirata's skull!

Shorn of his arms, Arjuna continued the fight, uprooting massive trees and hurling them at the hunter. When that failed, he charged at the Kirata with his bare hands. But the Kirata remained completely unscathed.

Finally, with a simple flick of his wrist, the Kirata lifted the muscular Pandava warrior over his head and flung him down onto the hard earth.

Bruised, beaten, and completely humbled, Arjuna sat in the dirt. No ordinary mortal could ever defeat me so easily, Arjuna realized. He closed his eyes and thought of Lord Shiva and his divine grace.

Right on the spot, he fashioned a simple Shiva Linga out of clay and began to worship it. As he offered a handful of forest flowers to the Linga, a new, divine power surged through his exhausted limbs.

A rejuvenated Arjuna sprang to his feet and challenged his rival again. "Kirata, your time is up!"

But as he raised his fists, Arjuna stopped, absolutely transfixed.

The fresh flowers he had just offered to the clay Linga were now resting peacefully on the head of the Kirata!

"The flowers, I offered to my Lord Shiva, on your head! You are none other than Him!" Arjuna gasped in awe. He immediately fell to his knees, pressing his forehead to the Kirata's feet. "O Lord, pardon me and my vanity."

Lord Shiva smiled warmly and revealed himself in his true, magnificent form, and so did Goddess Parvati.

"I am pleased with your devotion and unparalleled courage, Arjuna," Lord Shiva blessed him. "I shall impart to you the secret of the invincible Pashupata missile, which will help you in the hour of need."

Shiva's word came true. Years later in the great Mahabharata war, it was only with the divine Pashupata that Arjuna could finally defeat his arch-rival, Karna.

docs.title: Shiva the Fisherman
docs.text:
Once in Mount Kailasa, Lord Shiva started expounding the deep, complex mysteries of the Vedas to his wife, Goddess Parvati, who was listening attentively.

Years passed by without a single break. Shiva continued his cosmic lecture, but gradually, in spite of her very best efforts, Parvati's attention began to flag, and her eyes wandered.

Noticing her distraction, Shiva was deeply annoyed.

"The Vedas are not for you!" Shiva scolded her angrily. "Since you are no better than a common fisherwoman... may you be born as one!"

Instantly, before Shiva could take back his harsh words, Parvati vanished from Kailasa.

The very next moment, Shiva clutched his head in absolute grief. "What have I done!" he wept. "In thoughtless haste have I cast away one whose love for me was peerless."

Shiva's depressed state of mind did not escape Nandi, his trusted, bull-headed servant. Now my master will know no peace until Mother Parvati returns, Nandi realized.

Meanwhile, down on Earth, Parvati had already taken the form of an adorable human infant. She lay crying softly under a Punnai tree near the coast, where she was soon found by the chief of the Paravars, a local clan of fishermen.

"What a lovely child! No doubt it is God's gift to me," the chief smiled, picking her up gently. "I'll call her Parvati."

Little Parvati grew up happily by the sea. She used to go with her foster father whenever he went fishing, and as she grew older, she even learnt to perfectly row the heavy wooden boats.

"You are a real help to me, Parvati," her proud father smiled.

She grew into a stunningly beautiful young woman, beloved by all the fishermen. "A pretty girl! Strong and clever too! Lucky is the man who weds her!" they would whisper as she walked by the docks.

Meanwhile, back at Kailasa, Nandi approached his brooding master. "My Lord, why don't you go down to earth and claim her?"

"How can I, Nandi?" Shiva sighed miserably. "She is a fisherwoman now. She will now marry a fisherman."

Nandi smiled to himself. Then I will see to it that you are forced to become a fisherman!

Assuming the form of a massive, terrifying shark, Nandi plunged into the ocean and headed straight for the coast where the Paravars lived.

"A shark!" the fishermen screamed as the giant beast breached the surface. "Quick! We must get it!"

But before the fishermen could even cast their nets, the shark attacked, violently smashing their small wooden boats to splinters and throwing the men into the churning water.

As the days went by, the massive shark became a regular menace, destroying boats and tearing expensive fishing nets to shreds.

"Our nets are torn, and our boats in pieces! What shall we do?" the terrified fishermen wept.

At last, the desperate chief of the Paravars came up with an award. He stood before his tribe. "I will offer the hand of my beautiful daughter, Parvati, to the one who captures the shark!" he announced.

Many a brave young man took to the sea with heavy nets, hoping to win the chief's daughter... but the shark effortlessly destroyed their boats, and they all failed.

Having lost all hope, the desperate Paravars at last sought divine help. The chief fell to his knees before a Shiva Linga at the village temple. "O compassionate one, save us from the shark," he prayed.

The chief's daughter, Parvati, also knelt and prayed with all her heart. "Lord, come to our rescue. Don't fail us in the hour of need."

Up in Kailasa, Lord Shiva heard her sweet voice. He immediately transformed himself, appearing before the chief of the Paravars as a handsome, muscular young fisherman.

"I have come to catch the shark," Shiva announced.

"Our tribe will be indebted to you forever, if you succeed," the chief said, grasping his hands in relief.

Net in hand, Shiva waded confidently down into the roaring sea. When he cast his net, the terrifying shark, who was none other than his loyal servant Nandi, immediately surrendered himself, swimming peacefully into the ropes.

As Shiva effortlessly dragged the massive, subdued shark ashore, the entire village erupted in joyous cheers.

"You are our saviour!" the chief cried.

Parvati looked at the handsome young fisherman, her heart fluttering. How fortunate for me that this brave man has caught the shark! she thought, blushing deeply.

The chief embraced Shiva. "Welcome to our tribe, young man. By catching the great fish you have become a great fisherman. I am indeed glad to hear that. Accept my daughter as your wife."

And so, Lord Shiva, disguised as a fisherman, married his beloved Parvati, the fisherwoman. As the celebrations began, Nandi happily assumed his true, divine form and joyously carried the reunited couple back to their eternal home in Kailasa.

docs.title: Shiva and Markandeya
docs.text:
Sage Mrikandu and his wife lived a pious, devout life, but they were deeply saddened because they had no children. Hoping to earn a divine blessing, the sage observed severe, painful penances in the forest to propitiate Lord Shiva.

Pleased by his devotion, Lord Shiva finally appeared before him. "Lord, favour me with a son," the sage begged, falling to his knees.

Shiva raised his hand in blessing, but offered a difficult choice. "Do you want a son who will live long though lacking in virtues... or a son who will be incredibly wise and virtuous, but will live for only sixteen years?"

Sage Mrikandu did not hesitate. "I will have the virtuous son, my Lord."

Granting the wish of the sage, Lord Shiva vanished.

In due course, Mrikandu's wife gave birth to a beautiful, radiant baby boy. "The boy shall be named Markandeya," the proud father announced.

Markandeya grew up to be a brilliant and deeply devoted child. While barely sixteen, he had already mastered the complex wisdom of the Vedas.

One day, after a group of visiting sages had left their home, Mrikandu and his wife sat alone, tears streaming down their faces. "Mrikandu, you are fortunate to have such a brilliant son," a sage had remarked.

"I cannot forget that all this brilliance will soon be put out by cruel death," the father wept.

Just then, Markandeya came home with a basket of fresh flowers for his daily worship. "Mother! Why are you weeping? What can I do to make you smile?" he asked gently.

When his mother couldn't speak through her sobs, he turned to his father. "Why? What is wrong with being sixteen?"

"I will explain. Listen!" Mrikandu sighed heavily, and told him the tragic truth about the events leading to his birth, and the terrifying condition of Lord Shiva's boon. "...I will be sixteen years old tomorrow!" Markandeya realized.

But instead of panicking, the young boy smiled peacefully. "Don't cry, mother. I will not die," he promised. "I will seek immortality through the grace of Shiva, who is the conqueror of death."

His parents hugged him tightly. "May you succeed by His grace!"

"Shiva, I leave my son to your mercy. Protect him," Mrikandu prayed as Markandeya walked out of the house.

Early the next morning, on his sixteenth birthday, Markandeya walked down to the peaceful sea-shore. With his own hands, he moulded a beautiful Shiva Linga out of the wet sand, adorned it with fresh, fragrant flowers, and sat down to pray.

Towards nightfall, filled with pure devotion, he began to sing and dance joyously before the Lord.

Suddenly, a dark, terrifying shadow fell across the sand. It was Yama, the terrifying God of Death, holding his deadly noose.

"Get ready to leave this world," Yama boomed.

Markandeya looked up, unfazed. "O Lord of Death, please wait. I have not yet finished my worship."

"Foolish boy, don't you know that death waits for none?" Yama sneered.

Markandeya hugged the sand Linga tightly. "Please do not obstruct me in my worship of Lord Shiva," he pleaded.

"Fool! Do you hope to escape from me by clinging to Shiva? The grip of death is fatal as you shall now know!" Yama roared.

With a swift, violent motion, Yama threw his heavy rope, catching Markandeya's neck in the deadly noose, and brutally dragged him away from the Linga.

"Lord, protect me!" Markandeya screamed.

The very next moment, the sand Linga erupted in a blinding flash of divine light. Lord Shiva himself sprang from the Linga, his eyes blazing with fury, and viciously kicked Yama squarely in the chest.

Yama crashed to the ground, dropping the noose. Trembling in sheer terror before the Supreme Destroyer, Yama fell to his knees. "Lord! Pardon me," the God of Death begged.

"O Lord, the source of life, I salute you," Markandeya wept in joy, bowing at Shiva's feet.

Shiva smiled warmly at the devoted boy. "Markandeya, you will forever be free from death. You will be immortal."

With that ultimate blessing, Shiva vanished.

And Markandeya returned home, forever a sixteen-year-old boy, running into the arms of his overjoyed parents. "Mother! The Lord did save me!"

docs.title: The Value of Friends
docs.text:
Long ago, on the peaceful shore of a large lake, there lived a solitary hawk.

One day, a she-hawk came to live on the opposite shore. When the hawk saw her, he immediately flew over to her. "Will you be my wife?" he asked hopefully. "Together we could raise a fine family."

"All right," she smiled. "But tell me, do you have any friends?"

The hawk shook his head. "No. I don't need friends."

"Then you must make some friends," the she-hawk insisted gently. "In times of need, it is friends who help."

"But I'll do as you say," the hawk agreed. "Who shall we start with?"

"Our neighbours, of course! Go and call on the lion, the osprey, and the tortoise!"

The hawk agreed and flew to the tiny island in the middle of the lake where the tortoise dwelt. "O tortoise, accept me as your friend," the hawk requested.

"With pleasure!" the tortoise smiled.

He then flew to the osprey. "Any time you need me, send for me," the osprey promised.

Finally, the hawk called on the lion in his den. "Now that you are my friend, no one will harm you," the mighty lion assured him.

The hawk then returned to the she-hawk. "I have three friends now."

"Ah! Now we can decide where to make our home," she smiled.

"What about the Kadamba tree on the island where the tortoise lives? That's a good idea."

So the two flew over to the island and built a sturdy nest in the branches of the Kadamba tree. Soon, two little fledglings were born to them. "This is the safest place we could find—far from the haunts of men," the proud parents thought.

But the place was not as safe as the hawks imagined. One day, two hunters arrived at the lake. "It's been a bad day. We've caught nothing, not even a rabbit! And it's almost evening!"

"What shall we do?" one hunter sighed.

"Let's swim across to that island, and spend the night there," the other suggested. "At dawn we'll try our luck again. We might yet catch a fish or a young tortoise, perhaps."

When they reached the island, night fell, and the thick swarms of mosquitoes became unbearable. "These mosquitoes are impossible! Let's light a fire. That should drive them away," the hunters decided.

Up in the Kadamba tree, the smoke woke the fledglings, who began to cry. CHEEP CHEEP!

"Did you hear that?" a hunter gasped, looking up. "I did! Fledglings! On this tree! They are planning to eat our children!"

The she-hawk panicked. "Friend tortoise is asleep. Quick, my dear, fly to the osprey and seek his help."

The hawk immediately flew to the osprey. "What brings you here at this hour?" the osprey asked in surprise.

When the hawk told him the terrifying story, the osprey took flight at once. "Have they climbed the tree yet?"

"Not yet. They're busy lighting a fire," the hawk explained.

"Then fly back to your wife and comfort her. I'll take care of the hunters," the brave osprey promised.

Under the cover of night, the osprey flew to the Kadamba tree and waited. "There! The fire is blazing merrily. Let's get the birds," the hunters cheered.

Suddenly, the osprey dived straight into the lake, came out dripping wet, flew over the fire, and violently shook himself. The spray of water hissed and completely put out the fire!

"What's that? Is it the fire? Perhaps the wood was wet!" the hunters groaned, starting all over again.

But as soon as the second fire was blazing, the osprey dived into the lake and put out the fire once again. "Come down," the hunters sighed in frustration. "It's no use catching the fledglings till the fire is ablaze again."

"Ah! My little ones, you are safe!" the she-hawk wept in relief. "Now do you see the value of having friends?"

This went on till midnight. The she-hawk felt incredibly sorry for the exhausted osprey. "He'll lose his life trying to save our young ones. Go to friend tortoise and see if he can help."

When the hawk flew to the tortoise and told him the whole story, the tortoise nodded. "Don't worry. I'll come there as quickly as I can."

As the massive tortoise emerged from the water, the hunters spotted him. "Hey, look! A huge tortoise! Let's forget the birds and catch him. We'll have enough food for days."

The hunters quickly tore their waistbands into thick strips of cloth and tightly bound the tortoise to their own bodies. "Now, pull away!"

"I will!" the hunters grunted, digging their heels into the mud.

But the tortoise was immensely strong. Suddenly, he pulled forward, dragging both hunters screaming straight into the deep water! Coughing and spluttering, the hunters desperately loosened the strips of cloth to save themselves from drowning.

"The fellow is dangerous!" they gasped, scrambling ashore. "Let's leave him here and make another fire for our fledglings."

They began to frantically collect twigs. Up in the tree, the she-hawk panicked again. "They're back! Go to the lion and seek his help."

The hawk flew to the lion's den. A few minutes later, the fire was finally ready. "Ah! The fire is ready! Let's go and catch the..."

Suddenly, a terrifying roar echoed through the dark island. GR-R-R!

"EE-A-AH! A LION!" the hunters screamed in absolute terror. They abandoned everything, ran for their lives, and jumped straight into the freezing lake, swimming away as fast as they could.

The hawks hugged their babies close. Because they had valued friendship, their little family was finally safe.

docs.title: Greed Does Not Pay
docs.text:
There once was a poor Brahmana who had a loving wife and three young daughters. He loved them dearly and took excellent care of them. But suddenly, one day, he tragically died.

"How could he leave us and go? Who will care for us now?" his widow wept bitterly.

Kind neighbours rushed in to comfort the family. "We will. We won't let you starve. Now please wipe your tears."

But their life became one of deep poverty, living entirely on the charity of others.

Meanwhile, because of his good deeds, the Brahmana was reborn as a magnificent, magical golden swan. One day, swimming in a beautiful lake, he remembered his past life.

"I must go and see how my wife and daughters are getting along," he thought.

He flew to his old village and peeked into the small hut. "They live on charity! How degrading! I must do something to help them."

He landed in the courtyard. "Mother! Look! A golden bird! Let's catch it!" his youngest daughter cheered.

"Wait!" the swan spoke. "I am your husband, reborn as a bird. I can't bear to see your plight. I'll give you one of my golden feathers. Sell it and use the money to buy food and clothing."

He plucked out one of his pure golden feathers, gave it to her, and flew away.

Week after week, the magical swan returned to give his family yet another golden feather. The widow sold them all, and very soon, the family became incredibly rich.

But, alas, like most people who suddenly acquire wealth, the widow became fiercely greedy.

"Suppose he stops coming?" she plotted one day. "A mere bird is not to be trusted. The next time he comes, I'll pluck out all his feathers."

"No, mother! You can't do that!" her daughters gasped in horror. "You can't mean it, mother!"

But the widow had made up her mind. The next time the kind bird came to help his family, she lunged forward, caught him tightly by the neck, and despite all his desperate struggling, violently plucked him clean!

"There! Now we'll never be poor again!" she laughed, holding a massive bundle of golden feathers.

But as she looked down at the pile in her hands, her jaw dropped in absolute shock. The feathers were no longer gold. They were plain, ordinary white.

"Alas! What have you done!" the naked swan wept.

"I've been cheated!" the widow screamed. "They're just ordinary white feathers! H-how did it happen?"

"I'll tell you how," the shivering bird explained. "My golden feathers turned white because you... you plucked them against my will! I forgot to warn you never to do that."

"You forgot? How could you be so careless! We are ruined!" the cruel widow shrieked. "That's where a useless bird belongs... the dustbin! One of you give him something to eat."

She violently threw the poor swan into a dirty basket in the corner.

Within months, the swan's feathers grew back. But they were all perfectly white.

"What's the use of staying on here? I'd better go away," the swan sighed, flying out the window forever.

As for the greedy wife, without the golden feathers, she soon spent all her hoarded money and fell right back into terrible poverty, a fate she well and truly deserved.

docs.title: The Loyal General
docs.text:
Long ago in Varanasi, there lived a majestic King Crow who had a mate he loved dearly.

One day, as they flew past the royal palace of the human King of Varanasi, the Queen Crow saw a sight through the palace window which made her mouth water. The royal cook was serving the King a massive, steaming platter of delicious fish.

"Ah! How I wish I could taste just a tiny bit of that fish!" the Queen Crow sighed longingly.

The next day, her craving had grown so intense that she refused to leave her nest. "Come, my love. Let's go out and look for some food," the King Crow coaxed.

"I don't want the kind of food we find!" she stubbornly refused. "I want the food I saw in the palace on the King's table. If I can't have it, I'll die."

As the King Crow sat on a branch, wondering what on earth he should do to save his beloved mate, his most trusted General came up to him.

"Your Majesty, what's the matter?" the General asked.

When the King Crow told him the impossible problem, the General puffed out his chest confidently. "Oh! Is that all? Don't worry. I'll see that our queen has the food she craves for."

Taking eight of the best, most agile crows with him, the General flew directly towards the heavily guarded human palace. "Let's perch on the kitchen roof," he ordered his strike team.

"Now listen carefully," the General laid out the daring plan. "While the food is being taken to the King, I'll dive bomb the cook and make him drop the dishes. Four of you must then immediately fill your beaks with rice, and four with fish, and fly straight to our Queen."

The crows watched the courtyard below like hawks. "Ah! Here comes the cook!" the General whispered. "When he reaches the open courtyard, I'll strike!"

The General swooped down from the roof like a black arrow. AIEEEEE!

The massive crow struck the terrified cook squarely on the head. Screaming in shock, the cook dropped the heavy silver platters, spilling the King's entire lunch across the marble floor!

In a perfectly synchronized strike, the chosen eight crows swooped down, filled their beaks with the finest royal fish and rice, and flew away before the guards could even react.

"Drop those dishes and catch that wicked crow!" the furious human King shouted from his balcony.

The royal guards quickly swarmed the courtyard and managed to catch the brave General, bringing him before the King. "Good! You've got him! Bring him here."

"O, crow!" the King thundered. "You have dared to displease me. And in this foolish venture, you've risked your very life! Why did you do it?"

The General held his head high. "For my King. He told me about his Queen's longing for the food she saw on your table. I promised to get it for her—and I have kept my promise."

The King was absolutely stunned by the bird's bravery. He turned to his ministers. "The bird was willing to sacrifice his own life for his King! Such perfect loyalty is rare! He must be rewarded."

The King turned back to the General. "Set him free!"

Then, the King issued a royal decree. "Henceforth, a portion of the food cooked for me shall be sent every single day to you and your King and Queen. And a large measure of rice shall be cooked daily for your King's entire following."

docs.title: Practise What You Preach
docs.text:
Once, the King of the Birds took his massive flock to the Himalayas in search of food.

"Now go and look for seeds and grain," the King ordered his flock. "When you find some, report to me, so we can all safely share it."

As they wandered off in different directions, one particular female bird came to a busy road where heavy wagons loaded with grain used to pass.

"Look at all that grain on the road! What a feast!" she chirped greedily. "I won't tell the King about this find! I'll keep it all to myself."

But then a thought struck her. "But what if one of the others should fly this way and see the grain?" she worried. "I know! I'll tell them about it and yet keep them away!"

She quickly flew back to the flock. "You've been away a long time! Have you had any luck?" the King asked.

"None at all!" she lied flawlessly. "In fact, I very nearly lost my life!"

The other birds were all ears.

"I happened to fly over the main highway!" she gasped dramatically. "Elephants and horses, and heavy wagons drawn by fierce, thundering bullocks go along that route."

"That sounds terrible!" the other birds chirped in fear.

"There is grain there," she continued, "But once you alight, it's difficult to soar up again before a wagon crushes you. Don't go that way. It's too dangerous."

After her terrifying warning, all the other birds were extremely careful to completely avoid the highway.

That was clever of me! the greedy bird laughed to herself. I have all this grain to myself now.

The next day, she happily flew back to the highway and began pecking at the massive piles of spilled grain. Suddenly, she heard a sound in the distance.

WHRRRRRR...

"What's that? Oh! A cart!" she noticed. She glanced down the road. "It's a long way off. There's enough time to peck a few more seeds."

What she didn't know was that it was a fast-moving, heavy express cart.

WHRRRR WHRRRR!

Before she could even spread her wings to take flight, the massive wooden wheel of the speeding cart ran directly over her, instantly crushing her flat.

That evening, when all the birds came home to roost, they found her missing. The King sent search parties in all directions to find their lost companion.

Later, a few birds returned with sad news. "We found her on the highway... a cart must have run over her. She's dead!"

The King flew to the spot at once, looking down at her crushed remains.

"What a sad fate!" the wise King sighed. "She warned you all not to go near the highway. But she could not control her own greed, and she failed to practise what she preached. Let this be a lesson to all of you!"

docs.title: The Greedy Crow
docs.text:
A gentle pigeon once made its home in a small basket hanging in the bustling kitchen of a rich merchant of Varanasi. The merchant's cook was a kind man who happily let the pigeon live there.

One day, a greedy crow flew past the window. "Mm-m! Fish! I must find my way into this kitchen and make it my home," the crow plotted, smelling the delicious food. "But how should I go about it?"

Just then, his eye fell on the pigeon peacefully resting in its basket. As the pigeon flew out in search of grass seeds, the crow quickly followed him.

After a while, the pigeon noticed him. "Why do you follow me, friend?"

"I like you," the crow lied smoothly. "I would like to feed with you and be your friend."

"But we do not eat the same food," the pigeon pointed out in confusion.

"That doesn't matter," the crow insisted. "While you peck grass seeds, I'll look for worms."

"All right. Follow me then, but don't disturb me," the gentle pigeon agreed.

I've won the first round! the crow thought gleefully.

Towards evening, as the pigeon flew back into the merchant's kitchen, the crow followed him right inside.

The kind cook looked up in surprise. "Why! Our pigeon has brought a friend! I must hang up another basket for him."

Second round won! the crow cheered as he settled into his new, comfortable basket. And so, the crow, too, began to live in the kitchen.

Then, one evening as the two birds returned home, the master of the house walked into the kitchen. "I am having a grand banquet tomorrow," he ordered the cook. "Clean and cut all this fish tonight."

The crow peered over the edge of his basket, practically drooling at the massive pile of fresh fish. Look at his mouth water! the pigeon noticed worriedly.

What luck! I've already eaten my fill today. Tomorrow I'll feast—not on worms, but on perfectly fried fish! the crow planned.

That night, the crow pretended to be in terrible agony, groaning and clutching his stomach. "Aa-a-ah! Oo-oh!"

"What's the matter with you?" the concerned pigeon asked.

"I don't know! It must be the worms I ate today! I have a terrible stomachache," the crow lied.

"Or is it the fish you want to eat tomorrow?" the pigeon asked suspiciously.

The next morning, the pigeon got ready to leave. "Come on, let's go."

"I'm not coming today," the crow groaned. "You go alone. I have a terrible stomachache."

"Nonsense! It's the fish, isn't it?" the pigeon sighed. "Take my advice. It is highly dangerous to touch men's food. Come, let's go. Up with you!"

"What! And give up what I came here for in the first place! Never!" the crow thought stubbornly. He turned to the pigeon. "I do have a stomachache. You go."

"All right, I'm going. But take care," the pigeon warned, flying out the window.
