As soon as the pigeon flew out, the cook entered and set to work. "These pieces I'll fry... And these I'll put into the curry," the cook muttered.

Ah! The best bits are to be fried! I'll settle for fried fish! the crow watched eagerly.

When the food was ready, the cook covered the steaming dishes with heavy lids. "I'll go out and rest for a while till the maid comes for the food," he yawned, walking out of the kitchen.

It is high time you went out. I cannot wait any longer, the crow thought impatiently.

As soon as the cook's back was turned, the crow swooped down from his basket. I'll take a large piece of fried fish and fly back to my basket. I can eat it there in peace without being found out.

He landed on the counter and grabbed the handle of the lid with his beak. But the lid was heavier than he expected. As he tried to lift it, the lid slipped.

CLANNG! CLANGGG! The heavy metal lid crashed loudly onto the floor.

"Oh! Oh! What have I done!" the crow panicked.

Hearing the crash, the cook immediately turned around and rushed back into the kitchen. "What was that?"

He saw the crow standing over his master's precious banquet. "You thief! Is this how you repay me for sheltering you?" the furious cook roared.

Before the startled crow could even take wing, the cook pounced on him, grabbing him firmly in his strong hands.

"I'll pluck you clean and soak you in a mixture of sour buttermilk and burning spices!" the angry cook yelled. And he did exactly that, tossing the plucked, burning bird back into its basket.

That evening, when the pigeon flew in, he saw the dying crow's horrific plight.

"Alas, my greedy friend," the pigeon sighed sadly. "If only you had listened to me! That should teach you never to be greedy again!"

docs.title: The Bulbul and the Hornbill
docs.text:
The large, fierce Hornbill was once the mighty King of the Birds. He had a massive beak and a terrifying presence. But he was a cruel tyrant, and he used to ruthlessly kill smaller birds if they made even the slightest mistake.

So one day, all the smaller birds got together and decided that they simply must have a new king to save themselves from the Hornbill's wrath. After a long discussion, their choice fell on the tiny, beautiful Bulbul.

"He has a regal appearance..." one bird suggested.

"...and he could not hurt anyone even if he wanted to," another added happily.

"But how do we break the news to the Hornbill?" a parrot asked nervously. "He won't be pleased."

The clever little Woodpecker stepped forward. "I have an idea. I'll need your help, Woodpecker. Come with me."

Sometime later, the birds approached the fierce Hornbill.

"O King, we feel that you should undergo a test and prove your worth as our ruler," they announced. "You will have to sit on a thick branch and break it with your sheer weight."

The Hornbill glared at them. "If I don't succeed, whoever does shall be deemed worthier of ruling us, I suppose?"

"Tell me which branch I should sit on," the Hornbill scoffed arrogantly.

"That's the branch," they pointed to a thick, sturdy-looking limb high up in a tree.

"If I cannot break the branch, who can?" the massive Hornbill sneered. He flew up high into the air and landed on the branch with all his incredible might.

Thud.

But the thick branch did not break. It didn't even bend.

"Remember, anyone who enters the contest must sit on an equally thick branch," the Hornbill demanded angrily.

"The Bulbul is the next contestant," the birds announced.

The Hornbill laughed. "Is the branch over there thick enough?"

"That branch is even thicker than this," they pointed out.

"If the Bulbul can break that branch, he certainly deserves to be King," the Hornbill laughed, assuming it was impossible for the tiny bird.

"Go on!" the flock cheered.

The tiny Bulbul flew up and gracefully landed on the thick branch.

CRACK!

The massive branch instantly snapped cleanly in two under the Bulbul's tiny feet!

"He... he has broken the branch!" the Hornbill gasped in utter disbelief. Humiliated and acknowledging his total defeat, the fierce Hornbill flew away forever.

And so, the gentle little Bulbul became the new King of the Birds.

What the arrogant Hornbill didn't know was that the thick branch on which the Bulbul was to sit had been secretly, almost completely bored through by the clever Woodpecker!

docs.title: Ilisa, the Gildmaster
docs.text:
Long ago, there lived an incredibly wealthy man named Ilisa. As the local Gildmaster, he had more money than he could ever possibly spend, but he was notoriously tight-fisted.

One day, while out on a walk, he realized he was late getting home. "I've stayed out longer than I should," he grumbled, hobbling along with his walking stick. "I hope my wife has not bought anything for herself in my absence. She is so extravagant."

He practically jogged, fueled by pure paranoia. "If I didn't keep a constant watch on them they would eat me out of house and home! And as for my worthless children, all they can think of is eating."

"Help a hungry man, sir," an emaciated beggar pleaded, holding out a bowl as Ilisa walked past.

"Out of my way, you wretch! You won't get a thing from me!" Ilisa yelled, raising his heavy stick and driving the poor man away. "Everyone is after my money."

As he walked through the marketplace, the sweet, irresistible smell of freshly fried jalebis hit his nose. He saw a man happily eating a plate of the golden, syrupy sweets.

"What luscious jalebis!" Ilisa gasped, his mouth instantly watering.

He stared at a sweetmeat stall, agonizing over the purchase. "But if I buy some here, I'll have to share them with my acquaintances," he reasoned, seeing a group of men sitting nearby. "Tcha! If I send my servant to buy some and bring them home, I'll have to share them with my wife and children."

Unable to control his intense craving, he hatched an elaborate plan. "Unless I eat them somewhere else," he plotted.

He rushed home, grabbed his servant, and gave him strict instructions. "Go and buy some jalebis from the sweetmeat shop. But don't bring them here! Take them to the thick bushes near the lake. I'll wait there for you, hidden from everyone."

Meanwhile, as Ilisa was making his ridiculous, elaborate plans to eat jalebis in secret, a truly unbelievable event was unfolding at the royal palace.

A man who was Ilisa's exact, perfect likeness had just presented himself before the King.

"What brings you here, Gildmaster?" the King asked.

"I have come to seek your permission... to give away some of my great wealth to the poor," the impostor announced warmly.

"What!" the King gasped in absolute shock. "You want to give away wealth?"

"Yes, Maharaj. Hoarded wealth is of no use to anyone."

"You may do so," the King nodded, completely bewildered. As the man walked out, the King whispered to his ministers. "The greatest miser in the land wanting to give away his wealth! Wonders will never cease!"

The impostor then went directly to Ilisa's house. A servant stopped him at the gate. "Master! There's a stranger in town impersonating me," the impostor ordered. "If he comes here, beat him and drive him away."

"Yes, master," the servant agreed loyally.

The impostor walked inside to Ilisa's wife. "It's a shame that Ilisa's wife should wear such dowdy clothes," he smiled gently. "Go to the market and buy whatever you want... buy something for the children too."

His wife stared at him in utter shock. "Are... are you all right?"

"I've never felt better!" the impostor laughed. "It's just that I am in a generous mood today. Tell everyone you meet that I am giving away half my wealth to the poor and needy."

He has taken leave of his senses, his wife thought worriedly. But I'd better buy something for myself before he changes his mind!

Soon, word spread, and joyous people began to stream into Ilisa's house.

"Come in, come in," the impostor greeted them warmly. "Pick up whatever you want and go."

The house turned into a frenzy as the poor and needy eagerly grabbed clothes, food, and brass pots. One clever man worked more methodically than the others. Instead of rushing into the house, he chose Ilisa's best bullock-cart, hitched up the finest pair of bullocks, loaded the cart with valuables from the storeroom, and drove away, happily singing the praises of his benefactor.

"There is no one greater than the noble Ilisa!" the man sang loudly as he drove past the lake. "May Ilisa live a thousand years!"

Hidden deep in the bushes by the lake, the real Ilisa was busy stuffing his face with jalebis. He paused, his mouth full. "Someone's praising me! What!"

He peeked through the leaves and saw the man driving the cart. "Thank you, my good man!" Ilisa called out.

"I can see that you are a good judge of men. You are right," the man smiled back.

Ilisa stepped out of the bushes and squinted at the cart. "I just have to look at a person to know..." His eyes widened in absolute horror. "MY BULLOCKS! MY CART! AND MY BELONGINGS!"

"Thief!" Ilisa screamed, lunging at the man.

"How dare you call me a thief!" the man yelled, raising his heavy wooden stick and landing a vicious blow on Ilisa's back. WHAM! "I got all these things from the noble Ilisa."

"You couldn't have. I am Ilisa!" the miser shrieked from the dirt.

"You! You are a scoundrel!" the man cursed, driving away with his newly acquired wealth.

"I'd better rush home and see what's going on there," Ilisa panicked, scrambling to his feet and sprinting back to his house.

When he reached his gates, his own guards blocked him. "Stop! What are you doing?"

"Stop them! It's the impostor the master warned us about!" a servant yelled.

Before Ilisa could explain, his own servants grabbed heavy sticks and brutally beat him. THUD! WHACK! They kicked him out into the dust.

"You can't do this to me..." Ilisa groaned, clutching his bruised head. "Hey, you, take that back to the house! It's mine!" he yelled at a beggar walking out with a bundle of brass.

"You are all going to pay dearly for this! I'll come back with the King's men," Ilisa threatened.

He rushed to the royal palace, bruised and battered. "Maharaj, I am being looted!" he cried, falling before the King. "People are carrying away my property!"

The King frowned. "But Gildmaster... you yourself came here and asked for my permission to give away your wealth!"

"Maharaj, have you ever known me to give away anything?" Ilisa pleaded desperately. "Some impostor has taken my place! I will have him brought here."

The King ordered his guards, and soon, the impersonator was brought to the palace. The King and his courtiers found themselves gazing at two men who looked absolutely identical in every single respect.

"Now who is who?" the King muttered in confusion.

"My wife could identify me, Maharaj," Ilisa suggested confidently.

His wife was immediately summoned. She looked at both men, then pointed firmly at the impostor. "That's my husband!"

"Ungrateful woman! After all I've done for you!" Ilisa shrieked in betrayal. Then he had a brilliant idea. "Now who... what... why, my barber of course! Maharaj, my barber could identify me!"

The royal barber was brought in. He carefully examined the heads of both men. "I'll have to examine their heads, Maharaj," the barber said. After a long moment, he shook his head. "This is uncanny! I cannot identify the Gildmaster, Maharaj."

"What do you mean!" Ilisa screamed. "I have a wart on my head. Have you forgotten?"

The barber pointed at both men. "You have a wart... but so does he!"

The sheer shock of the situation was too much. "Even my wife has identified him as her husband! I'll be thrown out of my own house... and I'll lose all my wealth... OOOH!" Ilisa gasped, and fainted dead away on the marble floor.

The impostor chuckled. "Throw some water on him."

When Ilisa was finally restored to his senses, the impostor smiled down at him. "I shall not frighten you any more," the impostor said softly. His disguise melted away, revealing a divine being.

"I am your father... come down from heaven," the divine being explained.

"F-father!" Ilisa gasped.

"All the wealth you possess is mine," his father declared sternly. "I was generous to the poor and needy during my life... but you have put all my wealth under lock and key and it is of no use to anyone! I will take away all my wealth if you persist in such selfish, greedy behaviour!"

Ilisa fell to his knees, tears streaming down his face. "I... I won't, father! Give me another chance!"

Satisfied that his son had finally learned his lesson, Ilisa's father summoned a magical golden chariot and returned to the celestial world.

And from that day on, Ilisa returned home a changed man. He became generous, considerate, and spent the rest of his life using his immense wealth to help the poor and needy.

docs.title: Kesiya
docs.text:
Long ago, there lived a terrible miser named Kesiya. He was the King's treasurer, a man of immense wealth, but he absolutely hated spending a single coin.

One day, as he was walking home, he saw a man eating a plate of hot, syrupy sweet pancakes called poodas.

"It's such a long time since I have eaten poodas," Kesiya thought, his mouth watering. "I could ask my wife to make some... but the cost of the ingredients!"

When Kesiya reached home, he looked so utterly depressed that his wife immediately noticed.

"You look sad. What's the matter?" she asked.

"Nothing," he sighed.

"Tell me what's wrong. Is the King angry with you for some reason?"

"No."

"Have any of the servants asked for a raise?" she guessed, knowing his nature.

"Oh, no! They're all good people," Kesiya groaned.

"What's the matter, then?"

Kesiya finally broke down. "Well, if you must know... I have a craving for poodas."

His wife smiled in relief. "Is that all? I'll make some for you right away. In fact, I'll make enough for the whole neighbourhood!"

Kesiya gasped in absolute horror. "Have you taken leave of your senses, woman! What! All right, I'll make just enough for us."

"For us?" he frowned.

"For the children, for you and for me," she explained.

"Leave the children out," Kesiya ordered strictly. "They may not care for poodas."

"Then I'll make just enough for the two of us."

Kesiya glared at her. "You don't really want to eat poodas, do you?"

"Er... well..." she stammered, realizing what he meant.

"I thought as much," Kesiya nodded in satisfaction. "Make just enough for me."

As his wife turned to go to the kitchen, Kesiya suddenly stopped her. "Wait! We must be careful. If you make the poodas in the kitchen, the sweet aroma will attract the neighbours... and then we'll have to give them some."

"I'll tell you what we'll do," he plotted. "We'll take everything we need up to the terrace on the roof, and you can make the poodas up there where no one can smell them."

So Kesiya and his exhausted wife carried all the heavy cooking utensils, the firewood, the batter, and the frying pan up the steep stairs to the terrace.

"...up to the terrace. Nobody here... good!" Kesiya grinned, locking the terrace door behind them. "Now you can get to work."

Sometime later, the sweet smell of frying poodas filled the terrace.

"Are they ready?" Kesiya asked eagerly.

"Almost," his wife replied, flipping the hot pancake.

Suddenly, a strange shadow fell across the terrace. Kesiya looked up. To his utter horror, a Buddhist monk was calmly hovering in the air above them.

"But let them cool... Oh, we have a guest," his wife noticed.

"A guest?" Kesiya snapped. He turned to the monk. "What do you want? Go away!" he yelled, waving his arms.

But the monk calmly descended and hovered just above the terrace floor.

"Do you think you can get a pooda by hovering up there?" Kesiya sneered. "I wouldn't give you one even if you were standing on this terrace."

The monk gently landed, standing perfectly still on the terrace.

"Do... do you think I'll give you a pooda just because we are standing on the same terrace?" Kesiya stammered, taking a step back. "I wouldn't give you one even if you were standing beside me."

The monk smoothly floated across the floor, appearing right beside the terrified miser.

"I won't give you one! I mean it!" Kesiya shrieked, backing away. He turned to his wife in a panic. "Give him a small one and let him go away."

His wife poured a tiny drop of batter into the hot oil. But magically, the drop instantly expanded, swelling into a massive, thick pooda that completely filled the entire pan!

"Not that one! That's too big!" Kesiya yelled. "Make a small one for him."

His wife tried again, pouring an even smaller drop. But again, it swelled into an enormous pancake. "I can't help it! That's turned out even bigger," she cried.

"The paste keeps on spreading over the whole pan. There's something very strange going on here," she warned.

"You're just a bad cook, that's all!" Kesiya scolded her. He looked at the massive pile of giant poodas. "Give him that one on top and let him go away."

His wife tried to lift the top pooda, but it was firmly stuck to the one below it. "They are all stuck together," she grunted, pulling hard.

"Pull!" Kesiya yelled, grabbing the other end of the pile.

They both pulled with all their might. Suddenly, the entire pile of poodas flew into the air, and Kesiya tumbled violently backwards, crashing onto the hard stone floor.

He sat up, rubbing his bruised head, and stared at the serene monk. "It is clear that we are not dealing with an ordinary man," Kesiya finally realized, feeling a strange sense of shame. "You can have all the poodas, respected sir."

He respectfully carried the entire tray to the monk. "Sit and eat," he offered.

"I want them not for myself..." the monk smiled gently. "...but for my Lord Buddha and his disciples. They are waiting for me."

"Buddha!" Kesiya gasped. "F-forgive me! I did not know..." He fell to his knees in deep reverence.

"Come with me if you wish to meet him," the monk offered.

The monk raised his hand, and a glowing, magical staircase appeared in the air, leading all the way from the terrace down into a distant forest. Kesiya and his wife carried the heavy tray of poodas down the miraculous stairs, till finally, they reached a clearing where Lord Buddha was teaching his monks.

Buddha accepted the offering of poodas, and they were distributed among all his hundreds of monks. Even Kesiya and his wife got their share and sat down to eat with the disciples.

"This is the first time I have shared my food with strangers," Kesiya whispered to his wife. "And strangely, I feel incredibly happy... er, what are you staring at?"

"The poodas," she pointed.

Kesiya looked at the tray. Everyone in the massive gathering had eaten their fill, but the tray was still as miraculously full as before!

"Never have I seen such a wonder! This is the greatest day of my life!" Kesiya wept in joy.

Having witnessed the true miracle of generosity, Kesiya returned home a completely changed man. The very next morning, the once-miserly treasurer woke up with a bright smile.

"Wife, tomorrow you will make poodas for the whole neighbourhood!" he announced happily.

docs.title: Nandi Vishala
docs.text:
Once upon a time, a kind Brahman received the gift of a beautiful little bull-calf. He named him Nandi Vishala. He looked after the calf extremely well, and it grew into a fine, massive, and sturdy bull.

The kind Brahman has brought me up with loving care, as if I were his own son. I must repay him for all his trouble, Nandi Vishala thought.

One day, the magical bull spoke to his master. "Master, go to a rich merchant in town. Tell him that your bull can easily draw a hundred heavily loaded carts. Make a bet on that for a thousand pieces of gold."

The Brahman could not believe his ears. "Am I dreaming or have I gone mad? I heard my bull talking!"

"You are not dreaming, master," Nandi Vishala assured him gently. "Do as I say. It will bring you good fortune."

"Are you sure I'll win such a bet?" the Brahman asked nervously.

"Of course! Or I wouldn't have suggested it."

So the Brahman went to a rich merchant in the town. "Sir, whose bull do you think is the strongest in this town?"

"There are many strong bulls in this town, but mine are the strongest," the merchant boasted proudly.

"Well, I have a bull who can easily pull a hundred heavily loaded carts," the Brahman challenged. "Can yours do better than that?"

The merchant laughed out loud. "You must be joking! I'm not! It's impossible! No bull can draw a hundred loaded carts."

"I'm willing to make a wager on that," the Brahman smiled.

"Agreed! The stake shall be a thousand pieces of gold!" the merchant laughed, absolutely certain of an easy victory.

On the appointed day, the Brahman was ready with a hundred heavy wooden carts, all loaded to the brim with sand, gravel, and heavy stones. He yoked Nandi Vishala to the very first cart.

A thousand pieces of gold will soon be mine! the Brahman thought greedily. Add to that the thousand I've saved over the past few years, and I'll have two thousand pieces of gold! With that money, I'll buy many more bulls and make many more wagers till I become the richest man in town! Everyone will have to bow to my wishes!

These greedy thoughts made the Brahman incredibly arrogant and quite unlike his usual kind self. He grabbed a heavy whip.

"Come on, you rascal! Pull! Be quick, you rascal!" the Brahman screamed, cracking the whip.

The bull was absolutely shocked by his beloved master's cruel words and terrible behaviour. Well, I'm not the rascal he calls me! I won't move! Nandi Vishala decided, planting his hooves firmly into the dirt.

No matter how much the Brahman yelled, the bull refused to pull the carts.

The merchant was jubilant. "Ha! Ha! Come on, sir! You've lost a thousand gold pieces!"

Unyoking Nandi Vishala, the heartbroken Brahman sadly walked away. He had to go and bring the gold he had kept hidden at home to pay his debt. "All my life's savings gone!" he wept.

Later, at home, the Brahman threw himself onto his bed, crying bitterly.

"Why did you call me a rascal?" Nandi Vishala asked gently, walking up to the bed. "Have I ever broken a pot or gored anyone or...?"

"No, never, my child!" the Brahman sobbed, deeply ashamed of his arrogant outburst.

Nandi Vishala immediately felt incredibly sorry for the Brahman. "All is not yet lost," the bull comforted him. "Go and make the wager again. Let the stake be two thousand pieces of gold this time. Only remember, don't ever call me a rascal again!"

The Brahman immediately went to the merchant and offered to make the exact same wager as before.

"The man seems to be a fool!" the merchant thought, laughing. "You are not content with losing a thousand pieces of gold?"

"I am so certain of winning that I'm willing to bet two thousand this time," the Brahman insisted.

"Two thousand! All right, I accept!" the merchant agreed.

When the loaded carts and the bull were ready once again, the Brahman gently patted the magnificent animal. "Now then, my fine fellow! Pull the carts along."

With a single, mighty tug, Nandi Vishala effortlessly pulled the entire train of a hundred heavily loaded carts, marching forward until the very last cart stood exactly where the first had been!

"Amazing!" the merchant gasped in pure shock.

The merchant happily handed over the gold. "You deserve every one of these gold pieces!"

The Brahman took the two thousand pieces of gold and walked home a much happier, richer, and far wiser man.

docs.title: The Servant and the Treasure
docs.text:
Once, there were two old landowners who were very close friends. One of them had a very young wife who had recently borne him a son.

"You are very lucky," his friend smiled as they sat together. "You have a son and heir to whom you can bequeath your entire fortune."

"Yes, I'm indeed lucky," the old man sighed. "But I, too, have worries. If I die, my son may never get the money. His mother is young, and she may squander it all away."

"Wouldn't the safest course be to bury your money deep in the forest?" his wise friend suggested.

"It would certainly be a wise thing to do," the landowner agreed.

So, taking his most loyal and trusted household servant, an honest man named Nanda, into his absolute confidence, the landowner went to a nearby forest and secretly buried his massive treasure at a certain spot.

"My good Nanda, when my son comes of age, show him this treasure," the old man instructed. "No one else should be told about it."

The old man died soon after. Many years later, his son, now a grown young man, met his father's wise old friend.

"My father was a rich man. But there doesn't seem to be enough money left to manage the estate," the young man explained.

"But he did leave a lot of money for you, son. Didn't you know that?" his father's friend revealed. "It's buried somewhere in the forest. Ask your servant, Nanda. He knows all about it."

The young man immediately rushed back home and went to Nanda. "Do you know where my father put his treasure?"

"Yes, master. It's buried in the forest," Nanda nodded truthfully.

"Well then, let's go and get it. I need it now," the young man said.

"I'll take you there, master."

Soon they were on their way to the forest, carrying pickaxes. Nanda was a deeply honest man. Over all these years, he could easily have taken the massive treasure for himself, but he had remained fiercely loyal to his master's dying wish.

When they finally reached the middle of the deep forest, the young man stopped. "Well, Nanda, where is the money?"

Suddenly, Nanda, who had been perfectly docile and respectful for years, turned inexplicably arrogant and highly insolent.

"What makes you think there is money buried here for you?" Nanda yelled, throwing his pickaxe to the ground.

The young man was completely taken aback by the sudden outburst. What's wrong with him? I don't understand. What shall I do? he thought in shock. I know! I'll pretend I didn't hear a word!

He turned calmly to Nanda. "All right. Let's go home then."

What a relief! the young man thought as they walked back. He's following me quietly.

A few days later, they returned to the forest. Strange! He didn't seem to hesitate, the young man observed.

But the very moment they reached the exact same spot among the trees, Nanda's insolence exploded once again. "You idiot! What do you hope to find here?"

Once again, the young man ignored Nanda's shocking insult. "Nothing! Let's go home," he said calmly, and they walked back in silence.

When we set out, I am certain that Nanda genuinely means to show me where the treasure is, the young man reasoned to himself. But, later, the moment we arrive, he begins to aggressively abuse me. What could the reason for this be? My father's wise old friend should be able to help.

So he went to the old man and described Nanda's incredibly strange, shifting behaviour.

"It's simple!" the old man laughed heartily. "You say he starts abusing you when you reach a particular spot in the forest? Then that's exactly where the treasure is buried!"

"How do you know?" the young man asked in amazement.

"Money can corrupt even an absolutely honest man," the wise old friend explained. "The moment Nanda stands precisely on the spot where the treasure is buried, the overwhelming presence of all that wealth under his feet corrupts his mind. He feels like a master, and he becomes a completely different, arrogant man."

"If that is so, why didn't he simply take it for himself?"

"Because he didn't dare! He is honest until he stands right on top of it!" the old man chuckled. He then told the young man exactly what he should do.

The next day, the young man went to the forest with Nanda again. The very moment they arrived at the spot, Nanda began to violently abuse him. "You fool! You idiot! You..."

But this time, his young master knew exactly what to do. He stepped forward, grabbed Nanda firmly by the collar, and glared at him.

"Who do you think you are talking to, you knave?" the young master roared fiercely.

Nanda instantly shrank back in sheer terror.

The young man picked up his own pickaxe. "Don't move from there, till I order you to do so!" he commanded, and began to dig exactly where Nanda was standing.

Within minutes, his pickaxe struck a solid wooden chest.

"There! I've found it!" the young man smiled. He turned to his terrified servant. "Come here! Carry this home on your head."

Without the treasure directly under his feet, Nanda's temporary madness vanished, and he obeyed meekly. The son safely took his father's vast treasure home, and the honest Nanda continued to serve him faithfully till his dying day.

The wise one's advice worked perfectly! the young man smiled happily. How grateful I am to my father's friend!

docs.title: The Hypocritical Sadhu
docs.text:
A cunning rascal of a Sadhu lived in a small, quiet forest hermitage on the outskirts of a village. By putting on a perfect act of supreme piety, he had entirely won the deep trust of a wealthy local Zamindar (landlord).

Now, the Zamindar had a large pot of gold which he desperately wanted to hide from roaming robbers. As he wondered where to put it, he thought of the holy man.

"I know what! The Sadhu is an absolute model of goodness... and dacoits would never dare attack a holy hermitage!" the Zamindar decided.

So he took his pot of gold directly to the hermitage.

"Holy sir! We Zamindars live in constant fear of robbers. So I am going to bury my gold right here in your hermitage," the Zamindar announced.

The Sadhu's eyes secretly gleamed. There's enough gold there to keep me perfectly happy for the rest of my life! he thought.

The Zamindar finished burying the pot. "I am leaving my gold buried here as I have complete faith in you. Please don't let me down, holy one."

The Sadhu closed his eyes piously. "It's not necessary to tell me that. We, who have given up the material world, never covet another's property!"

Satisfied, the Zamindar left. But a few days later, the rascal of a Sadhu secretly dug up the gold and buried it again at another hidden spot a short distance away in the forest.

Tomorrow I shall go and take my leave of the Zamindar, the Sadhu plotted.

The next day, the Sadhu appeared at the Zamindar's house. "I have decided to go away from here," the Sadhu sighed heavily.

"What's the matter? Have I displeased you in any way?" the Zamindar asked in distress.

"No, that's not the reason," the Sadhu replied. "He who has renounced the world should not live in one place for too long. It is forbidden by our vows. So I must take my leave of you."

"Well, if you must go, I cannot stop you," the sad Zamindar agreed.

The Zamindar respectfully walked a short distance with the Sadhu, then reverently bade him goodbye.

As the Sadhu walked away, a sudden, paranoid thought struck him. I must make absolutely sure he does not suspect me when he eventually finds his gold has been stolen. Our paths may cross in the future, and he might accuse me!

The Sadhu picked up a tiny piece of dry straw from the ground and stuck it carefully into his matted hair. Then, he quickly retraced his steps back to the Zamindar's house.

A little later, as the Zamindar sat talking with a visiting merchant, the Sadhu walked back in.

"Welcome, holy one! What brings you back? Have you changed your mind?" the Zamindar smiled.

"No," the Sadhu replied solemnly, pointing to his hair. "But a single straw from your roof had stuck in my hair. Since we Sadhus may not take absolutely anything which is not freely bestowed on us, I have walked all the way back to return it to you."

The visiting merchant, however, was a highly shrewd and observant man. Come to return a single straw indeed! the merchant thought suspiciously. This fellow has robbed the Zamindar of something incredibly valuable, I am absolutely certain of it!

But the naive Zamindar was completely taken in by the act. "What a sensitive man! Why, he won't even take so much as a piece of straw which does not belong to him!" the Zamindar gushed.

"Throw away the straw and go your way, holy one," the Zamindar smiled respectfully.

As soon as the Sadhu had left, the shrewd merchant turned to the Zamindar. "Have you left anything valuable in that Sadhu's care?"

"Not exactly. But I secretly buried my gold in his hermitage and asked him to keep an eye on it," the Zamindar explained.

"Well, just go and see if it is still there," the merchant advised grimly.

The Zamindar rushed to the hermitage and soon returned, pale and trembling. "It's not there! It's been stolen!"

"The thief is none other than that rascal of a Sadhu!" the merchant declared. "We can yet catch him if we're quick."

The two men sprinted after the Sadhu and quickly caught up with him on the forest path.

"Caught you, you rogue!" the merchant yelled, grabbing the Sadhu by the throat.

"Wh-what? I am a holy man who has renounced the world..." the Sadhu stammered.

The merchant pulled back his fist and landed a heavy punch squarely on the Sadhu's jaw. CRACK!

"Mercy, mercy! I'll tell you where it is!" the terrified Sadhu shrieked.

The bleeding Sadhu led them into the forest and showed them exactly where he had hidden the gold.

"So," the Zamindar glared at the bruised hypocrite, "the stolen gold didn't trouble your delicate conscience nearly as much as a single piece of straw did!"

And giving the completely humiliated Sadhu a terribly sound scolding, they took their gold and left him in the dirt.

docs.title: What's In a Name?
docs.text:
In ancient Takshashila, there once lived a brilliant Vedic scholar who had five hundred young disciples studying under him.

One of these boys was unfortunately given the name 'Lowly' when he first became a student. Despite his terrible name, he was an incredibly sweet boy.

"Lowly, can you help me with this complex shloka? I can't seem to get it right," a fellow student asked.

"Eh? Oh! It's easy. I'll help you," Lowly smiled warmly.

"Lowly, will you help me carry this heavy firewood?" another asked.

"Eh! Ah! To be sure, I will," Lowly agreed, happily lifting the bundle.

So helpful and kind was Lowly that all his fellow students absolutely liked him. But deep down, he was not happy.

Why have I been given such a terrible name? he thought miserably, holding his head in his hands. Every single time I hear it, I feel sad.

So, one day, he went to his wise teacher. "Sir, please give me a new name which sounds more respectable," he requested politely.

"All right, my son," the teacher smiled gently. "Go, travel through the land wherever your fancy takes you. Observe the world. Then come back, and I will change your name for you."

So Lowly packed a small bundle and wandered from village to village. He walked for days, till he came to the bustling outskirts of a large city. There, he saw a dead body being carried to the cremation ground by grieving pallbearers.

"What was that man called?" Lowly asked a bystander respectfully.

"His name was 'Life', sir," the man replied.

"What? Can a man named 'Life' be dead?" Lowly gasped in shock.

The man stared at him. "Why not? Whether he was called 'Life' or 'Death', he would have had to die all the same! A name only serves to mark who's who! You seem to be a fool!"

Pondering over this strange matter, Lowly entered the city. Suddenly, he heard the sharp crack of a whip. A wealthy mistress was violently beating a poor slave girl in the street.

"Mercy! Mercy, my mistress! I'll try and do better tomorrow," the slave girl sobbed on the ground.

Moved by the terrible sight, kind Lowly immediately intervened. "Wait, my good woman! Why do you whip the poor girl?"

"She is my slave," the angry mistress snapped. "I sent her out to earn money, and she has come back empty-handed!"

Lowly quickly took out one of his few coins and gave it to the woman. "Here. Keep this and spare the girl. She'll do better tomorrow."

As Lowly walked away, he spoke to a passerby who had witnessed the cruel scene. "Poor girl! She must have an absolutely accursed name to suffer such a fate!"

"She is called 'Rich'!" the passerby laughed.

"What! And with a name like that she could not even earn a day's paltry wages!" Lowly gasped in amazement.

"You seem to be a fool!" the passerby shook his head. "A name only serves to mark who's who, and not what they actually are."

More reconciled to his own name, Lowly left the city and took the long road back towards Takshashila.

"I am going to Takshashila, but I have completely lost my way," a traveller on the road told him. "Can you help me?"

"I am going there myself. You can come with me," Lowly smiled. "What is your name, friend?"

"I am called 'Guide'," the man replied.

"'Guide'? And you've lost your way?" Lowly asked, his eyes widening.

"Are you making fun of me?" the man frowned. "Whether one's name is 'Guide' or 'Misguide', one can lose one's way all the same! Everyone knows that names only serve to mark who's who."

Lowly smiled a bright, beaming smile. "Yes, I have at last come to understand that profound truth."

When he finally arrived at Takshashila, Lowly went directly to his teacher.

"Well, do you still want to change your name?" the teacher asked knowingly.

"Master, I find that death can come to a man named 'Life', that 'Rich' and 'Poor' may both be poor, and that a 'Guide' and a 'Misguide' can easily lose their way," Lowly bowed respectfully.

"I know now that a name serves only to tell who is who, and does absolutely not govern the owner's destiny. I am quite content with my own name, and do not want to change it for another."

docs.title: The Most Virtuous Student
docs.text:
In ancient Varanasi, there once lived a highly renowned and deeply respected scholar who had a beautiful grown-up daughter.

He was a master teacher and had a massive number of young students under his care. One day, a brilliant idea struck him.

"My daughter is ready for marriage," he thought to himself. "I will put my students through a secret test to find out which is the absolute most virtuous of them all."

The very next day, he gathered all his hundreds of students under the shade of a large banyan tree.

"My dear boys," the scholar sighed heavily, pretending to be in deep distress. "I cannot afford the expensive clothes and fine ornaments required for my daughter's marriage. Will you, my loyal students, help me out by stealing these things for me?"

The students gasped in shock.

"But no one should see you stealing," the teacher warned strictly. "It should be done in the absolute strictest secrecy. Only then will I accept what you bring."

From that day onwards, eager to please their beloved master, the students secretly stole gold, silks, and valuables from their own families and friends. They brought the stolen goods secretly to their teacher under the cover of night.

"I must keep what each one brings in separate lots," the teacher thought as he received the stolen goods, "so that they can be easily returned to their rightful owners later."

A few days later, the teacher was walking through the grounds when he saw one of his absolute favourite students sitting alone, looking rather dejected.

"My son, you are the only one out of hundreds who has not brought me anything!" the teacher noted.

"Yes, master," the student sighed, looking at the ground.

"Couldn't you steal even a small ring?" the teacher asked, testing him.

"No, master. It's not that."

"Then what is it?" the teacher pressed. "You know you'll not accept anything that is not taken perfectly secretly. But I find that there is no such thing as absolute secrecy in wrong-doing."

"Why do you say that?" the teacher asked, his eyes gleaming.

"Because... because, even when there is absolutely no other person in sight, I cannot hide from myself!" the young student declared firmly. "My conscience would always know I am a thief."

The teacher was absolutely overjoyed.

"I have found the perfect boy for my daughter!" he cheered, hugging the young man. "Of all my hundreds of students, he is the only one who is truly virtuous!"

Then, he immediately sent for all the other boys. He stood before the massive pile of stolen goods and pointed at it sternly.

"Stealing, even for a supposedly good cause, is deeply wrong," the teacher announced. "You have all completely failed the test of virtue. You must now return all that you have stolen to their rightful owners at once."

Then, proudly adorning his beautiful daughter with her own pure jewels, the wise scholar happily gave her in marriage to his most virtuous, honest student.

docs.title: Amrapali
docs.text:
Long ago, the powerful Licchavi nobles who ruled over ancient Vaishali took a remarkably keen interest in the maintenance of their state gardens.

One day, the best of the royal gardeners was watering the plants when he saw something strange. "What's that under the mango tree?" he wondered. "Why, it's a lady! What an extra-ordinary beauty!"

He went up to her. "Who are you, fair lady? What is your name? Where have you come from?"

"I don't have a name. I come from nowhere and have nowhere to go," the mysterious maiden replied softly.

"Are you going to live in this garden?" the gardener asked.

"Yes, if you do not mind."

"I certainly don't mind. But this garden belongs to the nobles. They should be informed," he said.

Leaving her in the garden, he rushed to the nobles. "A strange lady has appeared in the state garden! In all Vaishali there is no one as beautiful as she is!"

Intrigued, the nobles rushed out to meet her. When they reached the garden and saw her, they were absolutely mesmerised. "The gardener knew what he was talking about! She is the most enchanting lady I have ever seen!"

But almost immediately, a fierce, ugly fight broke out among the proud nobles. "Amrapali shall be mine!" one yelled, naming her after the mango tree (Amra) where she was found.

"No! You'll have to take her from me first!" another shouted, drawing his sword.

Suddenly, the maiden stepped forward. "Stop!" she commanded. "Do I have no choice in the matter? Can I not have my say?"

The nobles immediately felt ashamed. "We have been selfish, thoughtless! You choose one among us, Amrapali. The one you choose, shall marry you."

Amrapali looked at them. "If I choose one of you, I will no doubt be making him very happy. But I will be making all the others incredibly jealous and angry. So I will not marry any of you."

The elders of Vaishali, who had hurried to the garden on hearing the clash, completely supported her. "A wise decision, indeed! Amrapali cannot belong to one person. She shall belong to all of Vaishali."

"And we could have a grand house built here for her," they agreed.

As soon as the magnificent house was ready, they presented it to her. "I shall ever remain indebted to the people of Vaishali for the love they have shown me," Amrapali smiled.

To entertain the nobles of Vaishali, Amrapali, who was an excellent, natural dancer, began to give public dance performances. "Such perfect rhythm! She is matchless!" the nobles cheered. "No other kingdom can boast of such an accomplished dancer!"

But her peaceful life was soon interrupted. One evening, when the nobles were watching her perform, a messenger rushed in in a total panic.

"Master! I have bad news! Vaishali is in terrible danger!" the messenger gasped. "The Magadhan army is heading towards Vaishali!"

"What! That monster King Bimbisara again!" the nobles roared, immediately leaving for war.

As Amrapali watched the young men of Vaishali ride to the battlefield, her heart sank. "Why do men fight? What do they gain by killing one another?" she wept. "One moment they appreciate beauty and art; the next, they thirst for blood."

A few days later, while the war raged outside the city, a handsome stranger called on her. "I have come a long way to see you dance. Will you dance for me, fair one?"

Amrapali danced for him, and the stranger was absolutely captivated. "No wonder the Licchavis are proud of her. We don't have a dancer to equal her in all Magadha," he thought.

"Where do you stay in Vaishali?" Amrapali asked when the dance was over.

"I am a stranger here. I have not yet found a place to stay," he replied.

"Then you are welcome to stay at my house."

The stranger stayed with her for a week, and they fell deeply in love. But then, he sighed. "Amrapali, I must take your leave. I have to go back."

"I love you. Why don't you come away with me?" the stranger pleaded.

Amrapali laughed lightly. "You amuse me. You are a total stranger. I don't even know your name!"

The man looked deeply into her eyes. "I am Bimbisara."

"What! The dreaded King of Magadha!" Amrapali gasped, backing away in horror. "There is no room for love in the hearts of ambitious men! How can you talk of love, when this very moment because of you hundreds of men are dying on the battlefield?"

"If I give up my ambition to conquer Vaishali and cease hostilities forthwith, will you marry me?" Bimbisara pleaded desperately.

"No! I cannot!" Amrapali wept. "I belong to Vaishali! I have given my pledge."

Without another word, the utterly dejected Bimbisara turned and left. Amrapali watched him go, completely heartbroken. "Why do I feel sad to see the dreaded enemy of Vaishali go?" she sobbed.

A few days later, the nobles of Vaishali returned, cheering loudly. "That coward, Bimbisara, called off the battle and retreated!"

Why did he do it? Was it for love of me? Amrapali wondered silently.

"Amrapali, we are back. We would like to see you complete the dance you began when we left," the nobles requested.

But as Amrapali danced, her movements were entirely lifeless. Her performance was a total failure. It was evident to everyone that her heart was simply not in it.

Meanwhile, Amrapali sat brooding for weeks. "What is the matter with me? Why did I find no joy in dancing for the nobles today?"

The next morning, as she walked in her garden, she saw a young boy carrying a single, exquisitely beautiful pink lotus. "What a beautiful lotus! How fresh it is! Will you sell that lotus to me, my lad?" she asked.

"Pardon me, lady. This lotus is not for sale," the boy bowed. "Haven't you heard? Lord Buddha, the Enlightened One who knows the cause of suffering, has come to Vaishali and is camping at Kotigrama. I am taking this flower to him."

"Lord Buddha! I will go to him," Amrapali decided instantly.

She travelled to Kotigrama and sat at the very back of the massive crowd, listening to Lord Buddha with rapt attention.

"Out of desire is born grief," Lord Buddha preached gently. "Out of desire alone is born fear. Free yourself from desire and the grief and the fear will wither away."

His words fill me with peace! His voice is so soft! His words so soothing! Amrapali thought, tears of relief streaming down her face.

She began to experience a totally new kind of joy. She went to Kotigrama daily to hear him speak. His very presence stills my restless mind. How I wish he would visit my home and fill it with his being! she wished desperately. But will the Lord visit the house of an unworthy person like me?

Soon it was time for Lord Buddha to leave Vaishali. "When will I see you again, Master?" Amrapali wept as he departed.

Even after Lord Buddha had left, Amrapali continued to visit Kotigrama every single evening, sitting under the tree where he used to preach. "This is where the Master used to sit. I can still feel his presence here," she smiled peacefully.

Many months passed. And Amrapali patiently waited for the return of Lord Buddha. "My Master will come to Vaishali again. Perhaps today, perhaps tomorrow, but he will come."

One evening, she heard joyous news. "My Master is back! I can't believe my eyes!" she wept, running up to Lord Buddha and falling at his feet.

"Arise, my child," Lord Buddha smiled warmly.

"Master, will you honour me by eating at my house tomorrow?" Amrapali begged.

"I will come, my child," Lord Buddha agreed.

Amrapali sped homewards to Vaishali in her chariot to prepare for the coming of her Master. Suddenly, a few chariots carrying the nobles of Vaishali came charging from the opposite direction.

"Where are you speeding to?" Amrapali called out.

"We heard that Lord Buddha has arrived. We are off to invite him to eat with us at the palace tomorrow," the nobles replied proudly.

"But the Lord has already agreed to dine at my house tomorrow," Amrapali smiled.

The nobles were furious. "We have been forestalled! But not all is lost. Not yet. She can easily be bought off," they plotted.

They turned their chariots around. "Amrapali, if you let Lord Buddha have his first meal at Vaishali with us, we will give you all the gold you want."

"No. Even if you offer me the entire kingdom of Vaishali, I won't give up the honour of serving the Lord!" Amrapali refused firmly.

"We will go to the Lord. He cannot refuse the nobles of Vaishali," they scoffed, driving away.

Later, at Kotigrama, the nobles bowed before the Buddha. "Lord, please honour us by dining with us tomorrow."

"I am sorry. I can't," Lord Buddha replied calmly. "I have already agreed to go to Amrapali's house tomorrow."

The next day, Lord Buddha went to Amrapali's house.

"I have come, Amrapali," Lord Buddha smiled.

"Lord, you do me great honour," Amrapali wept, washing his feet.

Later, as she served the monks a lavish meal, she felt a profound sense of peace. "Lord, why do I feel such immense joy as I serve you?"

"Amrapali, you have begun to know the true joy of giving," Lord Buddha replied.

After the meal, Amrapali bowed deeply before the Enlightened One. "Lord, permit me to grant my garden and my massive house to the Sangha (the monastic order)."

"So be it, child. It could be used as a monastery," Lord Buddha accepted her noble gift.

Then, happily leaving behind her worldly life, her immense wealth, and all its heartaches, Amrapali began to lead a simple, peaceful life of profound renunciation, finding at last the deep peace of mind she had always craved.

docs.title: Upagupta
docs.text:
Long, long ago in ancient Mathura, there lived an incredibly famous dancer named Vasavadatta. She was renowned far and wide for her stunning beauty and her breathtaking art.

After every performance, the wealthy men of Mathura would praise her endlessly. "What a perfect figure! What a charming appearance! What graceful movements..."

"...And what a hard heart!" another would interrupt bitterly. "She's unique, indeed!"

"You seem bitter. Have you too been spurned by her?"

It was true. Vasavadatta was fiercely independent and entirely unimpressed by wealth or status. "I am prepared to part with all my wealth for a single smile from her," one rich admirer sighed as she rode past in her chariot.

But Vasavadatta didn't so much as glance at her admirers. "They are all madly in love with you. Couldn't you see it in their eyes?" her companion asked.

"I chose not to see," Vasavadatta replied coldly. "No! Not one of them possesses what I am looking for."

One evening, as Vasavadatta was standing on the balcony of her grand mansion, she saw a young, serene Buddhist monk pass by in the street below. She was instantly captivated by his peaceful aura.

"Quick! Run down and invite that monk in," she ordered her maid.

The maid gasped. "I can't believe it. Vasavadatta showing some interest in a man!"

The maid ran down to the street. "Vasavadatta wishes to see you, holy one. Will you come in?"

The monk stopped, his face perfectly calm. "Not now. I will see her at the proper time."

When the maid reported this, Vasavadatta was confused. "Perhaps he hesitates to visit me because he is poor and can't bring me expensive gifts. Tell him I don't want anything from him."

The maid went back out. "O monk, my friend does not crave for gifts or riches. Please visit her."

"No, I cannot. It is not yet time to visit Vasavadatta," the monk repeated firmly, and walked away.

The monk who spurned the invitation of the greatest dancer in Mathura was Upagupta, a devoted disciple of Lord Buddha.

When Vasavadatta heard his final rejection, she was absolutely stunned. "Why should the only man I choose to love, shun me?" she wept bitterly.

Heartbroken, she completely stopped giving dance performances, much to the annoyance of the wealthy people of Mathura. "Life in Mathura has lost its charm. How could she be so cruel to us!" they complained.

Meanwhile, her friend was deeply worried. She sits alone, brooding over that heartless monk. It is not good for her health and looks. I must find something to distract her.

"A famous sculptor is holding an exhibition of his works. Let us go and see it, Vasavadatta," her friend suggested. "All right, if you insist," she agreed listlessly.

At the art show, looking at the beautiful statues, Vasavadatta finally forgot her sorrow for a while. She stopped before a particularly beautiful statue. "Such exquisite workmanship! Will you sell this to me?" she asked the handsome Chief Sculptor.

"Whatever it may cost, I am prepared to buy it. Quote your price," the Sculptor smiled smoothly. "It's yours, if you agree to dance again."

Vasavadatta hesitated. "You can't go back on your word," her friend whispered. "You have agreed to pay whatever price he asks."

Vasavadatta reluctantly agreed.

The next evening, people flocked to the town hall. At the end of the breathtaking performance, Vasavadatta received thunderous applause. But instead of making her happy, the adoration only made her brood all the more. Why did that monk shun me when thousands of people long for a sight of me? she thought sadly.

In the days that followed, the Chief Sculptor began to visit the dancer at her mansion to capture her image in stone. "My art will die with me. But yours will last for centuries," she smiled as he worked.

"My talent, which brings so much happiness to you, only makes my envious rivals hate me," the Sculptor sighed.

A few days later, the Sculptor suddenly vanished without a trace. "Why doesn't he come? The work is yet to be completed. It's three days since he last came!" Vasavadatta worried.

Meanwhile, his friends and relatives were frantically searching for the Chief Sculptor. "He was last seen entering the house of Vasavadatta, three days ago," one noted. "Have his rivals done away with him?"

The next day, a horrifying discovery was made. The dead body of the missing Sculptor was found buried in the dirt not far from Vasavadatta's house.

The royal guards immediately arrested her, and she was brought before the King, charged with the brutal murder of the Sculptor.

"Do you have anything to say in your defence?" the King asked.

Utterly shocked and grief-stricken, Vasavadatta had nothing to say. She stood in completely stunned silence.

"Vasavadatta is guilty," the King decreed firmly. "Confiscate all her property and have her permanently banished from Mathura."

As Vasavadatta was violently turned out of her own house, an angry mob jeered at her and pelted her with heavy stones. "Take this, you murderess!" they screamed.

Bleeding profusely from the stones, Vasavadatta barely managed to reach the outskirts of Mathura, finally finding refuge in a dark, lonely crematorium.

Her loyal friend found her there, weeping. "Vasavadatta, I know you are innocent! They have deliberately done this to you."

"Friend, let us not blame anyone," Vasavadatta sighed weakly.

As the days passed, people coming in and out of Mathura looked upon Vasavadatta, who was now seriously ill with painful, festering wounds from the stoning, as an absolute untouchable.

"She doesn't deserve any sympathy," they sneered, spitting at her as they walked past. "Leave her to die. Why do you waste your time on her?" They would sometimes even stone her again as they passed by.

Then, one evening, came Upagupta, the serene Buddhist monk.

"Friend, cover my body," Vasavadatta whispered weakly, hearing footsteps. "Let him not see me in this terrible state."

Upagupta knelt beside her. "Vasavadatta, I have come to you."

Vasavadatta looked up at him, tears in her eyes. "O monk, when all Mathura admired me, you alone spurned me. Why have you chosen to visit me now when I am a mass of festering flesh, shunned by all?"

Upagupta smiled with infinite compassion. "At that time you did not need me, Vasavadatta. You do, now. Come, let us go to my monastery."

Upagupta gently took her with him and nursed her back to health. Though she survived, she was heavily scarred.

"I am no longer beautiful," Vasavadatta sighed sadly, looking in a mirror. "No one will look at me now."

Upagupta shook his head. "Vasavadatta, you are yet to discover a beauty far greater than the one you have lost—the true beauty of the self."

Curious to know more about this inner beauty, she began to attend the peaceful discourses of Lord Buddha.

"You can't call even this physical body your own," Lord Buddha taught the monks. "When the body is cast away it merely becomes food for others. Light the lamp within you, and only then will you truly find eternal peace."

Light the lamp within... peace... Vasavadatta thought, feeling a profound sense of clarity wash over her.

Vasavadatta walked forward and fell humbly at the feet of Lord Buddha. "Lord, admit me into your order."

"So be it, my child," Lord Buddha smiled warmly. "May you finally find peace of mind."

docs.title: Andhaka
docs.text:
One day, deep in the beautiful, tranquil forests of Mount Mandara, Lord Shiva and his wife, Goddess Parvati, were sitting peacefully together.

Parvati was in a playful mood. Creeping up silently behind her husband, she giggled and playfully covered all three of Shiva's eyes with her hands.

At once, the entire world was plunged into absolute, terrifying darkness!

Held over the blazing, fiery heat of Shiva's third eye, Parvati's hands immediately began to perspire heavily. A few drops of hot sweat flowed out and fell upon the dry ground.

Suddenly, the sweat boiled, and there rose from it a weird, misshapen being!

As Parvati removed her hands and the returning light restored the world, she stared at the creature in shock. It looked horrible, with a massive, misshapen head and completely blind eyes.

"My lord, what is this hideous thing before us?" Parvati gasped.

The omniscient Shiva smiled gently. "It is our son, dear one... born of the sweat of your hand and the heat of my eye."

He reached out to the trembling creature. "Born in darkness, he shall be called Andhaka, the blind one. As he is your son, you must care for him."

The compassionate Parvati immediately embraced the child and began to look after Andhaka with deep motherly love.

Meanwhile, far away on a desolate mountain peak, the powerful Asura King Hiranyaksha was performing incredibly severe austerities, standing on one leg in the freezing wind in order to please Lord Shiva.

Pleased by his devotion, Shiva finally appeared before him. "O Asura, what is it that you desire?"

"Great Lord, I have no son to inherit my kingdom after me," Hiranyaksha prayed. "My brother, Hiranyakashipu, has five. I beg you—grant me a son."

"Kind-hearted Shiva, listen to my prayer," the Asura wept.

"Cast off your distress, Hiranyaksha. I shall grant you one," Shiva smiled. He presented the blind, misshapen boy to the Asura King. "My son, Andhaka, is equal in might to you. Accept him as your own."

In great joy, Hiranyaksha took Andhaka to his vast kingdom, and the blind boy began to live in his grand new home.

But Hiranyaksha's power made him incredibly arrogant. He launched a brutal campaign to conquer the three worlds, striking fear into the hearts of the Devas. In absolute desperation, the Devas begged Lord Vishnu for aid.

Lord Vishnu transformed himself into Varaha, the mighty, invincible boar, and utterly destroyed the armies of Hiranyaksha, killing the Asura King. However, taking pity on the innocent, blind adopted son, Lord Vishnu blessed Andhaka and made him the new King of the Asuras.

But Andhaka's envious, ambitious cousins could not bear to see him sitting on the throne.

"Our uncle was an absolute fool to have adopted a blind fellow!" they scoffed, openly mocking him in the royal court. "What kind of King will you be—blind as you are? Anyway, what claim have you to the kingdom of one who was not your real father?"

"One of us shall be King. We have decided," they declared cruelly.

Helpless against their bullying, the blind Andhaka began to think. If only there was some way to become other than what I am. Yes... I know how!

One pitch-dark night, Andhaka secretly fled the palace and went deep into the depths of an ancient forest. In that desolate place, he began to do severe penance, standing perfectly still on one leg, with his arms raised to the sky, surviving on nothing but air.

He was soon reduced to a mere skeleton.

At last, Lord Brahma appeared before him in a blaze of light. "O Asura, cease your terrible austerities. Choose any boon you desire."

"My Lord, let me have undisputed control of the three worlds..." Andhaka begged, his hollow voice echoing. "...and let no death come to me."

Brahma shook his head. "You shall become Lord of the three worlds. But whoever is born must eventually die. You may, however, choose the specific manner of your death."

Andhaka thought carefully, trying to find an impossible condition. "Then, let the most excellent of women in the world, whatever her age, be like a mother to me," he declared cleverly. "And should I ever look upon her as anything but mother, let destruction befall me instantly."

Who would ever fall in love with their own mother figure? he thought triumphantly. I am immortal!

Brahma smiled, knowing the truth of Andhaka's real birth. "So be it."

Brahma touched Andhaka's skeletal forehead. Instantly, Andhaka gained the precious gift of vision! And at once, his frail body became full-bodied, incredibly handsome, and immensely strong.

The transformed Andhaka confidently returned to his palace. "Andhaka? Surely a blessed boon has given you both sight and this beautiful form!" his cousins gasped in shock. Awed by his newfound vigour, they offered absolutely no resistance, and he easily took his throne.

Drunk on his new power, Andhaka set out with his armies and quickly conquered the Earth and then Heaven. "Indra, you and the Devas shall pay me heavy tribute from now on," he ordered the defeated gods.

Andhaka was now the absolute master over all. But with supreme power came deep corruption. He abandoned himself to a life of complete indolence and pleasure, surrounding himself with bad companions who spurred him to greater and greater evil. He and his wicked friends began to frequent holy sites of sacrifice, brutally harassing the peaceful sages.

"Don't you know? To offer sacrifice to the Devas and not to me, is now a terrible crime!" Andhaka would laugh, destroying the holy fires.

Blinded by intense pride, he completely forgot the destiny he had chosen for himself.

Once, he happened to come to the beautiful Mandara Mountain. "What a delightful place! Friends, we shall stay here awhile," he decided.

One day, while exploring the mountain, Andhaka's ministers came upon a strange sight. "Do you see that? A hermit in our land?" one frowned.

"What a beautiful woman!" another gasped, pointing to the hermit's companion. "What are they doing here? Our King must hear about her!"

At once, the ministers rushed back to Andhaka's camp. "O Lord of Asuras, in a mountain cavern not far from here, we saw a strange hermit immersed in meditation, and with him was a truly beautiful lady. Such beauty I have never set eyes on before."

"Why didn't you find out who she is? Go, now! Bring her here to me at once," Andhaka ordered greedily.

The ministers rushed back to the cave and stood waiting quietly until the hermit finally opened his eyes.

"Look at your uncouth self. You are utterly unworthy of her," the ministers sneered at the hermit. "Our King has ordered you to hand her over to him peacefully."

"We are the ministers of Andhaka, the Lord of the three worlds," they boasted. "Our King would like to know who you are, what you are doing here, and who this beautiful lady is."

The hermit, who was none other than Lord Shiva, remained perfectly calm. "I am a hermit, observing the Pashupata vow, and engaged in penance. This beautiful young woman is my wife."

"This lady of such great charm, your wife!" the ministers laughed mockingly. "The Rakshasa is welcome to come and take her. You arrogant rogue! He will come and take her by force."

The ministers returned with Shiva's message and delivered it to Andhaka. "We advise you to put him in his place at once," they urged.

Andhaka blazed up in absolute anger, like a fire fed with ghee. "Yes, I will attack that crude hermit! Let him know exactly who Andhaka is!"

He gathered his weapons and set out for the mountain cave. As he arrogantly approached its entrance, a powerful warrior stepped in his path.

"I am Viraka—my master's guard. Who are you? What do you want here?" the warrior challenged him.

"Out of my way!" Andhaka roared, hurling his weapons.

But Viraka was no ordinary guard. He swiftly deflected the attack and unleashed a devastating shower of magical missiles. The counter-attack was so deadly that Andhaka panicked and had to run for his very life back to his camp.

Some time later at the cave, Shiva turned to Parvati. "Dear one, I see fear and sorrow in your eyes. I am going away to a quiet place to perform the most severe austerities to free you from them. Viraka will stand guard over you when I am away."

As soon as Shiva left, Andhaka, whose spies were watching the cave, immediately learned of the hermit's departure. Drawn blindly by his destiny, Andhaka gathered a massive Asura army and returned to the cave to claim the beautiful woman.

"Fell the wretch! Fell him!" Andhaka ordered his troops.

A great struggle ensued once again with Viraka. But this time, vastly outnumbered, it was Viraka who collapsed, almost completely buried under a huge pile of Asura weapons.

"Aha! Now to get hold of her!" Andhaka laughed triumphantly, stepping over the fallen guard.

Suddenly, a blinding flash of light filled the cave. A host of powerful, divine female warriors—the Matrikas—materialized out of thin air!

"Do not worry, O Parvati. We are by your side now," they announced.

Eyes flashing in pure anger, the divinities launched a devastating attack on the Asuras. They sent forth mighty streams of magical energy, mercilessly belabouring Andhaka and his men. Andhaka's army fought back desperately, but it was a completely losing battle.

Then, blazing forth like a thousand suns, Lord Shiva himself returned!

"Shiva!" the Asuras gasped in terror as he tore through their ranks.

Encouraged by her husband's return, the happy Parvati drew a sword and fought even more valiantly to prove her skill and courage to her Lord. When Andhaka's army had been utterly beaten back, Shiva embraced her. "Parvati, you are truly a brave woman."

Shiva then turned to the wounded guard. "You, too, Viraka, were no less valiant."

As Parvati and Shiva entered the safety of the cave, Andhaka slowly ventured forward again. "I see no one. Where could they have gone? They must have gone into the cave," he muttered, drawing his sword.

But a massive Asura general named Vidhasa stepped forward. "Let the evil Asura fight on if he has the might to do so!" he roared, delivering a challenge directly to the cave.

The next morning, the final battle dawned. Shiva sent off a massive army of Devas under the command of Viraka to meet the Asura threat.

The two forces had barely advanced upon one another when... LO! The Asura general Vidhasa began to grow! He expanded into a towering, terrifying giant. He opened his cavernous mouth and, like a massive vacuum, began to swallow the Devas whole! Down the Asura's throat they went, hundreds and hundreds of them.

Viraka alone managed to escape the deadly suction. He fled back to the cave and told Shiva what had happened.

Shiva calmly stepped out of the cave and began to laugh. A blinding, pure white light radiated from him, spreading over the entire Mandara region. The intense, purifying light struck the giant Asura. And sure enough, Vidhasa's mouth was forced violently open against his will!

Out came Viraka, followed by all the swallowed Devas, tumbling onto the ground. They immediately resumed the battle, wreaking absolute havoc upon the stunned Asuras.

But the Asura general wasn't finished. With a sudden lunge, he managed to swallow Shiva himself!

"Oh, no! How will my master tackle these evil creatures all alone?" Viraka panicked.

Inside the beast's dark belly, Shiva simply began repeating a powerful, magical chant that brings on the throwing up of what is swallowed. The spell worked instantly. Shiva was violently thrown up, returning to the battlefield with his resplendent form charged with fresh, divine vigour.

He rode straight up to the Asura hordes. "Shiva! You are not finished with me as yet," Andhaka yelled, charging forward with his weapons. "Beware, O wretch, of Andhaka's might!"

Shiva drew his mighty Trident, the Trishul, and unleashed a devastating attack. His Trident easily killed every single one of the charging Asura generals.

But as the Asuras' blood dripped onto the earth, a terrifying magic took hold. Suddenly, many identical Andhakas began to rise up everywhere from the Asura's dripping blood, each one screaming and attacking the Devas!

Realizing the blood magic, Shiva summoned the divine mother Goddesses to drink the falling blood before it could touch the earth. Till at last, with almost all his blood sucked dry by the Goddesses, the real, exhausted Andhaka stood completely alone before Shiva.

In one swift, impossible movement, Shiva lunged forward and pierced his Trident directly into the Asura's chest, lifting him clean off his feet and holding him aloft high in the air!

Held up on that holy, purifying Trident, Andhaka's body rapidly withered to skin and bone under the scorching heat of the sun. The Trident had pierced his heart, but thanks to Brahma's boon, Andhaka did not die.

Instead, hanging there in terrible agony, a great, miraculous change came over him. The intense purity of Shiva's weapon burned away all his wicked arrogance, leaving only deep, humble devotion.

"O moon-crested, one with the blue throat, forgive me," Andhaka wept, pressing his hands together. "O great Lord of the Universe, do not be angry with me."

Gently, Shiva lowered his weapon, lifted Andhaka off the bloody Trident, and set him safely on the ground.

"You are forgiven," Shiva smiled warmly. "O courageous Andhaka! Choose a boon."

Andhaka knelt humbly before Shiva and Parvati. "O greatest of the great, my sin of fighting you on the battlefield was born of sheer pride and delusion," he wept.

He turned to look at Parvati, finally recognizing the mother who had held him when he was a blind, misshapen infant.

"I beseech the Mother of the Universe not to be angry with me, but to look on me with compassion," he pleaded softly, remembering his boon from Brahma. "Let me see her always as Mother."

Shiva merely turned his benevolent glance upon Andhaka, and the dark curtain was finally lifted. In a flash of divine insight, Andhaka suddenly remembered the full, true story of his birth—how he was formed from Parvati's sweat and Shiva's heat, and how Parvati had lovingly held him.

"I should have been dead by now, according to the destiny I had foolishly chosen for myself," Andhaka realized in awe. "If I am still alive, it is only because of your infinite grace."

"Let me stay with your Ganas (attendants)," Andhaka begged. "I ask to be your devoted servant for all eternity."

"I grant you all this, Andhaka," Shiva blessed him.

Andhaka bowed deeply before his true father and mother, finally finding the peace and happiness he had been searching for all his life.

docs.title: Animal Tales from Arunachal Pradesh
docs.text: The folklore of Arunachal Pradesh has been handed down by word of mouth across generations. These tales mirror the joys, sorrows, triumphs, and anxieties of its diverse people, seeking to explain the deeper aspects of life and the way animals came to be.

docs.title: The Pig and the Dog
docs.text:
A man once had a pig and a dog. One day, he suddenly realized that the two animals did no work at all.

"Get up, you lazy creatures!" he yelled, kicking them out of the house. "Go and do something useful! Whichever of you does some work might find a place in my house."

The pig and the dog went to their master's field. There, the pig ploughed the field the whole day long, toiling in the mud, while the dog simply slept under a tree.

In the evening, the dog awoke, stretched, and approached the exhausted pig. "Have you finished?"

"Yes... and I am exhausted," the pig panted.

As the pig wearily set off for home, the dog jumped into the freshly ploughed field, ran up and down several times to leave his footprints everywhere, and then followed the pig home.

That night, the master asked, "What work did you do today?"

"I ploughed the field, master," the pig said proudly.

"And you?" the master asked the dog.

"Master... it was I who ploughed the field," the dog lied smoothly.

"I will have to find out the truth for myself," the man thought. He got up early the next morning and went to the field. When he returned, he was furious with the pig. "Are you satisfied with my work, master?" the pig asked.

"What work? I saw only the dog's footprints in the soil. It was he who ploughed the field," the master declared. "You liar! Go away, you lazy creature."

He turned to the dog with a smile. "Come, dog. You have done some hard work. You may come into the house with me."

Ever since that day, dogs have stayed in their masters' houses, and pigs have not been allowed inside.

docs.title: The Owl and the Tiger
docs.text:
One day, the birds and animals of the earth held a grand meeting. They chose the tiger to be the king of the jungle, and the owl to be his minister. The owl was very proud of his new position.

But the king and his minister had quite different natures. The tiger was rash and quick-tempered. One day, they had an argument which quickly turned into a violent quarrel.

The angry tiger slapped the owl so hard across the face that the owl's face was pushed in and became completely flat!

Furious, the angry bird picked up a burning log from a nearby fire and struck the tiger with it, leaving long, scorched black stripes all over the tiger's golden body.

Since that day, owls have had flat faces, and tigers have black stripes on their bodies.

docs.title: Why Crabs Have Flat Bodies
docs.text:
There once lived a crab near a river. One day, as he was looking longingly at the ripe fruit high up on a tall tree, his friend the mouse came along.

"Would you like to eat some of those fruit?" the mouse asked.

"I would love to," the crab sighed.

"I'll climb up and throw some down for you," the mouse offered. He scampered up the tree and began to pluck the fruit, tossing them down to the waiting crab.

Unfortunately, one of the falling fruits hit a nest of red ants with a loud thud.

The ants were furious and rushed out of their nest. "Who did that? It must be that boar!" they cried, seeing a wild boar passing by. "The scoundrel! Let's teach him a lesson."

They swarmed the poor boar, biting him all over. "Ooooh! Aaah!" the boar squealed, running blindly into the forest. In his panic, he crashed into a tree where a bat was sleeping.

"That boar is after me!" the terrified bat shrieked, flying away frantically. Seeking shelter, the bat flew straight into the ear of a passing elephant.

"A bat in my ear! Help! Help!" the elephant trumpeted in distress. He thrashed about wildly and ended up knocking down the small hut of an old woman.

"Come back, you brute!" the old woman yelled, chasing the elephant with a stick. "How dare you knock my hut down."

"I am sorry... but there's a bat in my ear," the elephant pleaded.

The bat flew out and said, "He knocked down the tree in which I was sleeping. I was trying to get away from a boar."

"Let's find him," the old woman declared.

When they found the boar, the old woman scolded him. "I am homeless because of you. You frightened the bat and he frightened the elephant and he knocked down my house."

"It's not my fault," the boar protested. "Some ants attacked me and I was trying to get away from them."

When the old woman questioned the ants, they replied, "We had good reason to attack him. He broke our nest."

"I didn't!" the boar snorted. "A mouse was throwing that crab some fruit and one of them hit the nest."

Finally, the old woman turned to the crab. "Is this true?"

"I... er... yes. One of the fruit did hit the nest," the crab admitted nervously.

"So you are to be blamed for the destruction of my hut," the old woman said sternly. "You'll have to give me something to make up for it."

"All right. I'll get you something from my house," the crab promised. He crept under a large stone... and stayed there, refusing to come out.

"Come out! Come out!" the old woman shouted.

Finally, the woman and the animals lost their patience. "Let's press the rock down on him," they decided. The elephant pushed his heavy weight onto the rock, crushing it down.

"Oh-oh! Help!" the crab cried out, but the old woman and the animals just walked away.

Sometime later, the crab crawled out from under the rock. But the heavy weight had crushed him, and he had become as flat as a pancake. Crabs ever since have had flat bodies.

docs.title: The Otter and the Wildcat
docs.text:
An otter and a wildcat had become good friends. One day, the otter accompanied the cat to a human village.

"I'll steal a chicken for you and one for me," the wildcat whispered. "But keep an eye out for men. Shout a warning if you see any of them coming towards me."

The cat crept into a hut and successfully caught a fowl. But the fowl squawked so loudly that the inmates of the house awoke and began to chase the cat with sticks.

The otter, who was watching from the bushes, was terrified. "After those people catch him, they'll come after me. I'd better get out of here," he thought, and ran away as fast as he could without warning his friend.

The cat narrowly escaped his pursuers and eventually caught up with the otter in the forest. "You coward!" the cat hissed.

"I... I am sorry. I am not used to this sort of thing," the otter mumbled.

The cat ignored him, greedily ate the entire fowl himself, and threw the bare, picked-clean bones to the otter. "There, that's your share."

The otter felt deeply hurt but didn't say anything.

Some days later, as they were passing by a pond, the otter said, "Let's catch some fish."

"But I can't swim," the cat replied.

"You go into the pond, catch the fish and throw them to me," the cat ordered arrogantly.

The otter dove into the pond, caught and ate several delicious fish himself, and then surfaced, tossing a handful of fish bones onto the bank. "There! That's your share."

"You rascal! I'll tear you apart!" the wildcat roared. In his blind anger, the cat forgot that he did not know how to swim. He leapt into the deep water to catch the otter, thrashed about helplessly, and was drowned.

docs.title: The Bulbul and the Hornbill
docs.text:
The mighty hornbill was once the king of the birds. But he was a cruel tyrant, known to kill smaller birds if they made even the slightest mistake.

So one day, all the birds got together in secret and decided they must have a new king. Their choice fell on the gentle bulbul. "He has a regal appearance," the heron observed, "and he could not hurt anyone even if he wanted to."
