"But how do we break the news to the hornbill? He won't be pleased," the crow pointed out.

The clever owl spoke up. "I have an idea. I'll need your help, woodpecker. Come with me."

Sometime later, the owl approached the hornbill. "O king, we feel that you should undergo a test and prove your worth to continue ruling us. You will have to sit on a thick branch and break it. If you don't succeed, whoever does shall be deemed worthier of ruling us."

The arrogant hornbill scoffed. "Tell me which branch I should sit on. If I cannot break the branch, who can?"

"That's the branch," the owl pointed to a sturdy-looking limb on a tall tree.

The hornbill flew high into the air and landed on the branch with all his massive might. But the branch did not break. He bounced and clawed, but it remained firm.

"Remember, anyone who enters the contest must sit on an equally thick branch," the hornbill declared, slightly embarrassed.

"The bulbul is the next contestant," the owl announced. "Is the branch over there thick enough?"

The hornbill inspected a nearby branch. "That branch is even thicker than this. If the bulbul can break that branch, he certainly deserves to be king. Go on!"

What the hornbill didn't know was that the thick branch the bulbul was to sit on had been secretly bored through from the inside by the woodpecker, leaving it entirely hollow.

When the tiny bulbul landed on it, there was a loud CRACK, and the thick branch snapped in two, sending the bulbul fluttering safely into the air.

"He... he has broken the branch," the hornbill muttered in shock. Acknowledging defeat, the disgraced hornbill flew away, and the bulbul became the beloved new king of the birds.

docs.title: The Porcupine and the Elephants
docs.text:
A herd of elephants used to bathe in a particular, pristine pool deep in the jungle. One day, the leader noticed the water was murky. "Why is our pool so muddy today? Somebody has been playing in the water. Who?"

He spotted a dragonfly hovering nearby. "Dragonfly, do you know who has dirtied our pool?"

"No!" the dragonfly buzzed.

"If you find the culprit, let us know," the elephant ordered.

Sometime later, the dragonfly saw a porcupine covered in wet mud. "Did you by any chance bathe in the pool the elephants use?"

"I did," the porcupine grunted.

"They are annoyed because you made it so muddy," the dragonfly warned.

"I can't help it if there's so much mud in that pool. Anyway, that pool doesn't belong to them. I'll use it whenever I want to," the prickly creature snapped.

The dragonfly flew straight back to the elephants. "I have found the animal that muddied your pool. He is not at all repentant. He says he will use this pool again. He doesn't know who he is dealing with!"

The leader of the elephant herd reached up with his trunk, plucked a single, thick hair from his head, and gave it to the dragonfly. "Show this to that stupid creature. Tell him he is picking up a quarrel with one whose hair is so thick."

The dragonfly took the heavy elephant hair back to the porcupine. "The elephants say you do not know whom you are dealing with. Is each hair of his so thick?" the dragonfly asked, struggling to carry the large strand.

The porcupine scoffed. "You call that thick? Here, take one of mine to them." He pulled out one of his sharp, solid quills.

The dragonfly carried the porcupine's quill back to the elephants. "He has sent you a hair of his own."

"WHAT!" the elephant leader gasped, staring at the thick, sharp, spear-like quill. "Is each hair of his so thick?"

"He must be a giant of a creature," a smaller elephant whimpered.

"Let's not quarrel with him, brothers. We'll find another pool tomorrow," the leader decided quickly. The terrified elephants scrambled out of the pool and ran away into the jungle, never to return.

docs.title: The Frog and the Monkey
docs.text:
Long ago, there was a Raja who had a monkey and a frog as his servants. One day, the Raja commanded, "Go to the forest and bring me some fruit. And you get me some fish from the stream."

The frog and the monkey set out to do their master's bidding. But when they reached the forest, the monkey greedily ate every fruit he picked. And when his stomach was completely full, he curled up on a tree branch and went to sleep.

He awoke only towards evening, realizing he had nothing to show for his day. "The Raja will beat me for not bringing him fruits. Now what should I do?"

Finally, he got off the tree, rolled himself thoroughly in the mud several times, and set off for home.

On the way, he met the frog. The frog had worked hard all day and had caught a large bundle of fish.

"This creature will get me into trouble," the monkey thought enviously. "Why did he have to catch so many fish, and I don't have so much as a berry to give the Raja? This won't do at all!"

The monkey ambushed the frog, snatching the bundle.

"Hey! What are you doing?" the frog cried.

The monkey tossed all the fish back into the stream.

"See what you've done! I've lost all the fish!" the frog wept.

"It doesn't matter. Why are you so afraid of the Raja? He won't remain our master much longer, you know," the monkey boasted. "I'm going to rebel against him. And when I become the new Raja, I'll make you my minister."

The frog was naive and agreed. "Oh! Now come, let's go home. Tell him you didn't catch any fish."

The Raja was furious when he saw the servants returning empty-handed. "What could I do, master?" the monkey whined. "I scoured the whole jungle for fruit-bearing trees... but I could not find any. See how dirty and dishevelled I am. Does that not show that I have been working?"

He pointed an accusing finger at the frog. "He, on the other hand, looks so clean and fresh. He must have spent the whole day playing in the water."

"You know that's not true!" the frog protested.

"You useless creature!" the Raja roared. He struck the frog on the head so hard that the top of his head became completely flat. "I sent you to catch fish, not to play in the water!"

"I did catch fish. But he threw all of them into the stream!" the frog cried, pointing at the monkey.

But the Raja wouldn't listen. "I can't stay here anymore," the frog said sadly. "There's no justice here." The frog left the palace forever and went to live in the river. And frogs, since then, have had flat heads.

The monkey was glad to get rid of the frog. A few days later, he tried to make good on his boast and rebelled against the Raja, gathering his monkey relatives to attack the palace.

"The traitor! I must act fast if I am to save myself," the Raja thought. He made a thick, black paste and covered his entire face with it. Then, he sat on his throne and waited for the monkeys to appear.

When the monkey entered, he stopped in surprise. "Where did you get that paste, master? I would like to put some on my face too."

"My servants put me in the hollow of a large tree and lit a fire around it," the Raja lied cleverly. "The smoke made my face black. It looks beautiful now."

"Please ask your servants to do the same thing for me, and my friends," the vain monkey requested.

"Gladly," the Raja smiled.

The Raja's guards rounded up the monkey and all his friends, shoved them into the hollow of a large, dry tree, and set fire to it. Only one of the monkeys escaped—a female—and her face had been permanently blackened by the thick smoke. Her descendants are the black-faced monkeys we see today.

docs.title: The Frog and the Tiger
docs.text:
A frog and a tiger were once good friends. The frog was a frequent visitor to the tiger's den, and whenever he came, the generous tiger always gave him fresh meat to eat.

One day, the frog said, "I have come to your house so often. But you have never come to mine. Please come to my house tomorrow."

"I would be delighted to come," the tiger said warmly. "But... I am a meat-eater."

"As if there's no meat in my house!" the frog boasted. "Please do come."

"Oh, all right," the tiger agreed.

The frog hopped home feeling rather worried. "What have I gone and done! From where shall I get meat for him?"

As he sat near the river, he saw a wild horse drinking water. "Aha! A horse! I'll bite off some of his flesh."

The brave little frog leaped into the air and landed squarely on the horse's back. The horse felt the sudden prick and viciously swished its long tail. THWACK! The heavy tail struck the frog, sending him flying through the air. He hit the ground with a sickening THUMP.

"Ouch! Both my legs must be broken," the frog groaned, dragging himself painfully back to his hole.

The next day, the tiger arrived. "Welcome, my friend, welcome!" the frog called out, hiding his injured legs. "Please make yourself at home while I get you something to eat."

The frog painfully hopped up to the loft of his small burrow.

After some time, the tiger heard a loud "OOOOOOH!" from above. "What is he doing?" the tiger wondered, peeking into the loft.

He was shocked to see the frog biting into his own mangled leg. "What are you doing, my friend? Why are you biting off flesh from your own leg?"

"I... uh... I had no meat to offer you..." the frog stammered in shame.

"So you wanted to give me your own flesh?" the tiger asked gently. "Do you think I would have eaten it? Why did you do such a thing?"

The frog felt deeply ashamed of his foolish pride. When the tiger eventually left, the frog dragged himself to the riverbank and jumped into the deep water. "I could never show my face to the tiger again. I shall stay in the water for the rest of my life."

And frogs to this day live mostly in the water.

docs.title: Aniruddha
docs.text:
Usha was the beautiful daughter of the noble Asura king, Bana of the thousand arms. Bana was a fierce devotee of Lord Shiva. One day, with his thousand arms, he played various musical instruments as Shiva danced the Tandava. Shiva was immensely pleased and granted him a boon, assuring him of his protection.

But after some time, Bana grew vain. He complained to Shiva, "My thousand arms are becoming a useless burden to me. Even without them, there is none equal to me, save you." Shiva, to put an end to this pride, cursed him: "O vain and foolish one, in a future encounter, one equal to you will humiliate you." But Bana welcomed the curse, looking forward to meeting a peer in fight.

A few days later, Usha and her friends were picking flowers in their favorite grove. As the day grew warm, a tired Usha drowsed under the trees and had a vivid dream. She woke up with a start, startling her friends. She had dreamt of a handsome prince who won her heart, only to disappear, leaving her in grief.

Her close companion, Chitralekha, the daughter of one of Bana's ministers, sent the others away to console her. "Do not grieve, my princess. If this prince exists, I can and shall bring him to you. But first, you will have to identify him for me from among the many portraits I shall paint for you."

Usha only knew that his skin was dark blue and he wore yellow garments. Chitralekha began to paint. She painted a number of gods, Gandharvas, and Yakshas, but Usha kept saying no. Then, among the various portraits, she painted Krishna and his son Pradyumna. Usha was abashed, for they closely resembled the prince of her dream. Finally, Chitralekha drew the portrait of Aniruddha, son of Pradyumna.

"This is he!" Usha cried, casting her eyes down in sudden shyness.

"He shall soon be here with you," promised Chitralekha.

With the help of her yogic powers, Chitralekha conveyed herself through the night sky to Dwaraka, and into the palace of Aniruddha. She found him fast asleep on his bed. Using her magic, she transported the sleeping prince, bed and all, back to the kingdom of Bana and into the presence of her dear princess.

When Aniruddha woke up, Usha explained what had happened. But Aniruddha, captivated by the lovely Usha, was not able to take his eyes off her. "I cannot dream of leaving you. I would rather stay here and delight in your company," he told her. Thus imprisoned by the tender attentions of Usha, Aniruddha married her in secret. She entertained him with song and dance, and they spent many happy days together hidden away in her chambers.

But such stolen happiness could not last forever. The palace attendants grew suspicious because the princess hardly ever stepped out of her rooms. They spied on her and discovered the Yadava prince.

They went straight to Bana. "O King, forgive us. We are afraid that your daughter has secretly married the Yadava prince, Aniruddha."

"What!" Bana thundered, "Enough! Let me see this for myself."

Bana stormed into Usha's room with his guards. "Seize him! Kill him!" he ordered.

But Aniruddha was quick. He grabbed a mace from one of the attendants and slew all of them before they could even realize what was happening. Bana, livid with rage, stepped in. Using his thousand arms, he overpowered Aniruddha and bound him from head to foot using live serpents for ropes. Aniruddha was taken away and imprisoned in the court of Bana.

Meanwhile, all of Dwaraka was upset about the disappearance of Aniruddha. Four months passed with no trace of him. Then, Sage Narada came to see Krishna with news. "Aniruddha is in the court of Bana. Bana has bound him with live serpents and imprisoned him."

Krishna immediately called a council of all his men. "Get your horses, chariots, and arms ready for battle. Aniruddha is alive, but in danger. I have just had news of him."

Krishna, Pradyumna, and Satyaki set out for Bana's kingdom with hundreds of warriors to rescue Aniruddha. When they reached the city gates, Krishna gave the command to attack, and his army surged forward, laying waste to Bana's defenses.

Bana's rage knew no bounds. He came out with an equally large army, and a great battle ensued. Shiva, who had promised Bana his protection at all times, came to his aid with his son, Kartikeya.

Krishna fought with Shiva. In no time at all, Krishna destroyed Shiva's weird hordes and unleashed the Jrimbhanastra weapon, which kept Shiva completely stupefied. Elsewhere on the battlefield, Pradyumna vanquished Kartikeya with ease.

Blind with anger, Bana rushed at Krishna. With his thousand arms, he drew five hundred bows at the same time and set two arrows in each. But Krishna was too quick for him. With his glowing discus, Krishna cut off nine hundred and ninety-six of Bana's arms, killed his charioteer and horses, and destroyed his chariot. Krishna blew his conch in triumph and was about to follow the fleeing Bana to finish him when Shiva intervened.

"Krishna, Bana is devoted to me, and I have promised him my protection. I plead with you to spare him," Shiva said.

Krishna smiled at Shiva. "It is only to humble him that I have cut off all except four of his arms. And to relieve the earth of her burden, I have destroyed his army. He still has four arms left. Besides, I grant him immortality and everlasting youth."

Shiva turned to his followers. "Bring Bana to us."

When Bana arrived, Shiva told him, "Krishna has pardoned as well as blessed you. Bring his grandson and your daughter to him."

Bana bowed and returned with Usha and Aniruddha. The whole city of Dwaraka was anxious for the return of their prince. They were overjoyed when, placing Usha and Aniruddha in the front, the victorious Krishna, Pradyumna, and Satyaki returned safely home.

docs.title: Aruni
docs.text:
One cold winter day, Aruni of Panchala, a young and devoted disciple of Sage Dhoumya, was on his way back to the hermitage carrying a heavy bundle of firewood he had collected for the sage's household. As he passed by a field that belonged to his master, he noticed something alarming.

"There's a breach in the embankment!" Aruni realized in dismay. "The master's field will dry up! If I wait here to try and stop it, I'll be delayed. And there's not a bit of firewood at the hermitage!"

Torn between his duties, Aruni rushed back to the hermitage as fast as he could.

Meanwhile, Sage Dhoumya and his disciples had assembled for the day's lessons. The sage looked around. "Where is Aruni?" he asked.

"He's gone to fetch firewood," one of the disciples replied.

Just then, Aruni came running in, panting heavily under the heavy load he carried.

"Ah! There you are!" the sage said. "Why are you late, Aruni?"

"There's a breach in the embankment," Aruni gasped. "The water is escaping..."

"Oh! Oh! Go and attend to it," the sage instructed urgently. "Quick! Leave the firewood here! Upamanyu or Veda will take it in. He's gone already!"

As Veda and Upamanyu carried the firewood in, they marveled at his dedication. "How responsible and devoted Aruni is! A disciple like him is hard to find."

Running all the way back to the field, a breathless Aruni reached the trouble spot. He laid logs across the gap and packed it tightly with wet mud. "There! That should hold the water in," he thought, feeling relieved.

But as Aruni turned to go, the rushing water broke through his makeshift dam. "Oh, no! It's given way! What shall I do? If I go back for help, the field will be drained dry!"

As he stood gazing helplessly at the widening breach, a desperate idea occurred to him. For a moment, he wavered. "If I stay here, I will miss my studies for the day. But my Guru's field! I must save the crops!" Without hesitation, Aruni laid his own body across the breach, blocking the cold water with his flesh.

Meanwhile, engaged in his daily routine, the sage forgot about Aruni and the errand he had sent him on. It was only towards evening that he realized his absence.

"The lamps are not yet lit!" Sage Dhoumya said. "It's not like Aruni to forget his duties. Where's Aruni?"

"You sent him to the field on an errand, Master," a disciple reminded him. "He has not yet returned."

"Not yet returned? I hope no mishap has befallen him. Come, let us go to the field and see."

When the anxious sage and his disciples reached the field, it was dark and quiet. "He's not here! Where could he have gone?" the sage wondered aloud.

Suddenly, a feeble voice called out from the mud and freezing water. "I am here... Master! Ar-u-u-ni!"

Tears filled his eyes as the sage bent down to lift up his freezing disciple. "He's been here all day! He stopped the gap with his own body! Bless the child! My child, are you all right?"

"Aruni! You're cold and stiff!" the other disciples cried.

"The water, Master! It's flowing out! The crops..." Aruni shivered, still worried about his duty.

"Never mind, my son," the sage said gently.

"Don't worry, Aruni. We'll take care of it," his friends assured him.

Later, when the breach had been properly attended to and all had returned to the warmth of the hermitage, Sage Dhoumya blessed the boy. "My son, your devotion and obedience to your Guru are unmatched. They shall forever bring you renown."

docs.title: Uttanka
docs.text:
When Sage Gautama worshipped Agni, the god of fire, he always chose his devoted disciple Uttanka to assist him.

"O Agni, I worship you for the sake of my Guru, who is my only god," Uttanka would pray.

"My dear Uttanka," Gautama told him one day, "I know what you are thinking. May the divine Agni come to your aid when you need him most."

The years passed. One day, Gautama called his disciples to him. "You have acquired mastery of the Vedas. You may now go and set up ashrams of your own." One by one, they came to Gautama to be blessed before departing.

Uttanka watched them leave. "Ah! Now it is my turn," he thought. "What dakshina should I offer my Guru? I'll leave it to him to decide."

But over the years, Gautama had become so dependent on Uttanka that even though hundreds of disciples came, finished their studies, and left, Uttanka had to stay on. "No, not you, Uttanka," the sage would say. "I cannot permit you to go. Not yet. I need you here."

"Will I ever set up an ashram of my own?" Uttanka wondered sadly. "But how can I go when my Guru needs me?"

So Uttanka, serving Gautama and his family, grew old without either his Guru or himself realizing it.

One day, returning with a heavy pot of water, he thought, "I am very hungry. After I've set down this water, I'll have something to eat." But as soon as he reached the hut, Gautama's wife called out to him.

"Ah! You're back, Uttanka! Please fetch me some firewood. There is none left."

"Food can wait. Her orders must be obeyed first," Uttanka told himself, turning back toward the forest.

"When will I be free to set up my own ashram and retire to a life of meditation and peace?" he wondered. Out of sheer fatigue, he threw the wood on the ground instead of putting it down gently.

As he returned to the hermitage an hour later, containing his hunger, he noticed something strange. "Ah! My back aches. It never did before. Is my devotion to my master weakening? This load feels heavier than usual."

He caught his reflection and gasped. "What's that? A lock of silver hair! It is my own! I have grown old! Decrepit!"

While Gautama patiently listened, Uttanka poured out his woes. "And I have not yet tasted worldly happiness. Though I have lovingly served you for many years, you have not yet permitted me to go."

"What's the matter, my son? Please calm down and speak out," Gautama said gently.

"Old age has overtaken me without my being aware of it," Uttanka replied.

"My son, I kept you here because of my love for you. But if you wish to go, you have my permission and my blessings," Gautama assured him. "And... if, with the strength of your penances, you become a young man, I shall give my daughter to you in marriage."

And lo! The very next moment, Uttanka regained his lost youth. True to his word, Gautama placed his daughter's hand in Uttanka's and solemnized the marriage.

After the marriage, Uttanka approached his Guru. "What guru dakshina should I give you before I go away with my wife?"

"Your excellent conduct as my disciple has been fee enough. I need no other," Gautama smiled.

"If it pleases you, you may do so," the sage added.

"Then, with your permission, may I bring something for mother?" Uttanka asked.

Uttanka went to the sage's wife. "What shall I bring for you, mother?"

"If you must, then bring me the celestial earrings worn by the wife of King Saudasa," she replied.

When Gautama heard of this later, he was alarmed. "Oh! It was not wise to demand such a fee. Saudasa has been cursed by Vasishtha and is now a cannibal. He is sure to kill Uttanka."

"Oh, I didn't know that!" his wife cried in dismay. "But, my Lord, surely he can be protected from coming to any harm by your grace?"

"And my prayers! He can and he will," Gautama promised.

Meanwhile, Uttanka trudged on till he came to a remote forest. He spotted a fierce, wild-looking man. "That must be Saudasa!"

When he saw Uttanka, Saudasa got up. "Ah! I am lucky. You have come just in time, for it's the sixth hour of the day and I'm hungry."

"Wait, O King!" Uttanka said. "I have come on an errand for my Guru. It's against dharma to injure anyone who is on such a mission."

"But this is the only hour when I am permitted to eat," Saudasa reasoned. "Besides, I am hungry. So I will not let you go."

"Then let us make a pact. After I have completed my errand, I will offer myself to you."

"All right. What is the errand?" Saudasa asked.

"I have come to beg from you the earrings worn by your wife!"

Saudasa was taken aback. "The celestial earrings! But they belong to my wife. Please ask for something else. Anything. I'll give it to you."

"It is the earrings I'll have and nothing else," Uttanka insisted. "If our pact means anything to you, if you are one who keeps his word..."

Saudasa sighed. "All right! Go to my queen and tell her that I command her to give the earrings to you."

Uttanka went to the queen with Saudasa's message.

"If it is the command of my husband, and for his good, I will obey. For no other reason would I part with these earrings," she said, handing them over.

"In what way are these earrings different?" Uttanka asked.

"These earrings free the wearer from hunger and thirst and fear of any kind," the queen explained. "They protect the one they adorn from poison, fire or any other danger. But beware—the Nagas are waiting for an opportunity to steal them. So take care of them. Hide them in the folds of your deerskin."

Uttanka returned to Saudasa. "I have got the earrings. I will give them to my Guru's wife and then return to you, as I promised."

"There is, however, one question I'd like to ask you before I go," Uttanka added. "What is it, my friend?"

"The learned say that one who does not take the advice of a friend is a fool. And one who wrongs a friend is a villain. And you, O King, have become my friend today."

"That is true," Saudasa agreed.

"Would you, a friend, advise me to return to you, a cannibal?" Uttanka asked slyly.

Saudasa knew he was trapped. "Well, as a friend I would say you'd be a fool to come back!"

"That's all I wanted to hear, my friend. Good day!" Before Saudasa could change his mind, Uttanka left him and hurried back to the hermitage.

After he had gone some distance, Uttanka felt weary. "I feel weak from hunger. I must find something to eat." As he looked around, his eyes fell on a bilva tree. "Ah! Ripe bilva fruit! But what shall I do with the earrings? If I leave them on the ground, a Naga might slink away with them. I'll put the deerskin on this branch. The earrings should be safe here."

As he climbed the bilva tree, some of the fruit fell on the deerskin. The branch broke, and the deerskin fell to the ground with a heavy thud. Uttanka looked down in horror. "My deerskin! It's fallen off the branch!"

He scrambled down. "The earrings! Ah! The celestial earrings! I must be quick." But it was too late. A snake slithered out of the grass. "It's a Naga! He's got the earrings!"

Uttanka chased the Naga, but before he could catch up with it, it had slid safely into an anthill.

A furious Uttanka began to poke at the anthill with his staff. "I'll retrieve those earrings somehow!" Burning with anger and a desire for revenge, Uttanka dug the earth with his staff for thirty-five days.

On the thirty-sixth day, an old man stood before him. "What are you doing?"

"I am digging my way to the nether regions to retrieve the earrings a Naga stole from me," Uttanka panted.

"It's a vain pursuit, my son. Give it up," the old man advised. "The region of the Nagas is thousands of yojanas away from here. You can't reach it by digging with your staff."

"If I can't recover the earrings, I shall give up my life," Uttanka declared.

The next moment, the old man transformed into a glorious figure on a chariot. "Since you are steadfast in your resolve, I shall aid you. Hold up your staff," said Lord Indra.

"There! Now your staff is charged with the force of thunder. You shall soon achieve your purpose," Indra said, and vanished.

With a few strokes of his empowered staff, Uttanka rent the earth and, following the path it yielded, soon reached the subterranean region of the Nagas.

"How shall I get through the gates? And when I do, where shall I look for the Naga who stole the earrings?" Uttanka wondered.

Suddenly, a blazing steed stood before him. To his amazement, the creature spoke to him. "Blow hard at me!"

Overcome by awe, Uttanka did not move. "Do not hesitate, my son," the horse urged. "You have often done it at your Guru's hermitage."

"At the hermitage? I don't understand. Who are you?"

"I am the fiery Agni. You have often worshipped me with your Guru. I have come to help you. Do as I say without delay."

Uttanka did as he was told and blew hard. Suddenly the steed blazed out in flames. Thick clouds of black smoke issued from its body, rose high over the wall, and poured into the palaces of the Nagas.

"Aa-ah! I am blinded!" the Nagas cried out in the darkness. "I can't see anything!"

With streaming eyes and scorched bodies, the Nagas slowly made their way through the suffocating smoke to the gates of the palace. Vasuki, their king, gave an order: "Throw open the gates!"

When they saw Uttanka standing there, the Nagas, led by Vasuki, fell at his feet and worshipped him.

"O Holy One, why have you caused this affliction to befall us?" Vasuki pleaded.

"One of you has stolen the earrings I was taking to my Guru's wife," Uttanka said sternly.

The next moment, a Naga bowed before Uttanka. "Forgive me, O Holy One. I am the culprit." He then brought out the earrings and returned them. "Take the earrings, but please rid us of this scourge."

Uttanka took the earrings and turned to Agni. "O Agni, let them know peace once more."

As soon as the smoke dispersed, Uttanka took his leave of the Nagas and safely returned to the ashram of his Guru with his prize.

docs.title: Ashoka
docs.text:
Ashoka was the son of King Bindusara, who ruled India from his capital of Pataliputra in the third century B.C. Ashoka excelled his brothers in the art of war. Therefore, when a rebellion broke out in the distant province of Taxila, he was sent to suppress it.

Weeks later, a messenger brought happy news to the capital. "Your majesty! We have happy news here. Ashoka has successfully put down the rebels."

"He shall rule there as the Viceroy," King Bindusara decided. "He will keep peace there."

However, Bindusara had a hundred sons besides Ashoka, and they were not happy to hear the news. "Ashoka has always been proud," they muttered among themselves. "The success will go to his head. It will make him more arrogant." They were unhappier still when the king recalled Ashoka from Taxila. "Ashoka back in the capital. I don't like it," one brother complained. "Especially with our old father depending on him," agreed another.

King Bindusara, observing the tension, confided in his minister. "I am worried about the future of my kingdom. Ashoka is ruthless. He may fight with his brothers for the throne."

"You have a hundred and one sons, your majesty," the minister replied reassuringly. "You have no cause for worry."

Just then, a messenger arrived with urgent news. "A rebellion—this time in Ujjaini."

"Send for Ashoka," the king ordered.

When Ashoka arrived, Bindusara told him, "The province of Ujjaini needs an able Viceroy."

"I would love to go South," Ashoka agreed eagerly.

"But the Viceroy designate... will have to crush the rebels there. You alone can do it."

"Yes, father," Ashoka accepted the mission.

Soon afterwards, Ashoka marched towards Ujjaini. A greater part of the journey was on horseback. As they rode, Ashoka marveled at the landscape. "What a lovely place!"

"This is Vidisha-nagar, sir," a soldier informed him.

A little later, they decided to rest. "Let's camp here for a while. Rest the horses," Ashoka commanded. While resting, Ashoka was charmed by the beauty of the leader of a group of passing women.

"Excuse me, lady. To which temple are you going?" he asked her.

"We are on our way to the Chaitya. Not to a temple," she replied.

"Then you are Buddhists."

"Yes!"

"Come on, Vidisha. It is getting late," her companion called out.

"Vidisha! A name as lovely as this place," Ashoka thought to himself. "And such charming eyes! Find out who her parents are. I want to meet them."

Ashoka's messenger soon returned. "Sir! She is the daughter of a reputed Shreshthi's family."

"Tell the merchant I want to meet him," Ashoka said.

Later, Ashoka met the merchant. Hesitating slightly—a rare sight for the fierce prince—Ashoka finally spoke. "I... yes. I want the hand of your daughter in marriage."

"I am honoured, sir," the merchant replied.

After a quick wedding, Ashoka proceeded towards Ujjaini along with Vidisha. Being a great warrior, in no time he routed the rebels, forcing them to lay down their arms. The people of the city gave Ashoka a grand welcome.

But later, in the palace gardens, Vidisha looked sad. "This is an occasion for rejoicing, Vidisha. But you look sad," Ashoka remarked.

"I dislike killing... even in battles... especially by you," she replied.

"That's silly. You say it, because you are a Buddhist. I am not. I am a Kshatriya king. My duty enjoins killing if need be."

"What about Bimbisara? He too was a king. He found peace in the teachings of Buddha," Vidisha argued softly.

The differences between Ashoka and Vidisha persisted. Months later, Vidisha gave birth to a son.

"A son! I am very happy," Ashoka declared. "I will name him Mahindra. He will conquer the entire world."

"I wish he conquers the hearts of men as Prince Siddhartha did," Vidisha said quietly.

Two years later, Vidisha gave birth to a daughter. "Lord! This time, let me choose the name," she asked.

"As you wish."

"I'll name her Sanghamitra."

"Oh, no! That's a Buddhist name," Ashoka frowned, but let it be.

One day, a messenger came from Pataliputra. "His majesty has not been keeping good health. Please return to Pataliputra immediately."

Ashoka had hurried consultations with Vidisha. "I have received a message from the Chief Minister. My father is ill. I have to rush to Pataliputra... and you aren't yet fit to accompany me. Will you stay at Vidisha-nagar with your parents? I'll send for you later."

"As you wish, my Lord!"

Together they traveled up to Vidisha-nagar. As Ashoka prepared to leave, he told her, "Please take care of your health. And don't preach non-violence to my Mahindra. I want him to be a King of Kings. Before I leave, is there anything you want me to do?"

"Yes. But give me your word that you will."

"I will. What is it?"

"I want you to shun violence of all kinds," she pleaded.

At Pataliputra, Ashoka was received by the Chief Minister. "Prince! Your father is no more. And your brothers are fighting amongst themselves for the throne."

"The throne! It is mine! I have earned the right to it," Ashoka declared fiercely. In his ambition, Ashoka forgot all about the promise he had made to Vidisha.

Ashoka was ruthless in killing all those who challenged his right to the throne. Sumanadevi, the wife of one of his brothers, was expecting a baby at the time. Fearing for the life of her unborn child, she sought help from a loyal princess, who showed her a secret door to escape into the woods.

Escaping from the palace, Sumanadevi wandered in the forest. An old man who was passing by took pity on her. "If you come with me to my village, I will arrange for your stay. Don't worry, daughter! My home is close by. I'll fetch my wife. You rest under this Nigrodha tree."

"No! I cannot walk any farther," Sumanadevi wept. It was under that very Nigrodha tree that a boy was born to Sumanadevi. They called him Nigrodha Kumar. Nigrodha Kumar grew up in that village and became a monk at a very tender age.

Meanwhile, Ashoka had become a powerful king. At his court, he asked his minister, "Is my kingdom peaceful? Are the borders quiet?"

"Yes, your majesty. Even Taxila and Ujjaini are quiet."

"Still we should not rest till Kalinga is conquered," Ashoka decided.

"Why, your majesty?"

"It is independent today and may raise its head any moment. War it will have to be. Commander! Prepare for the battle."

Ashoka's army invaded Kalinga. The soldiers of Kalinga fought bravely, but at last, they were defeated. "We have won. Kalinga is ours. I shall visit Kalinga," Ashoka announced.

But as he proceeded across the battlefield, he saw horrible sights. "Heaps of dead men! We have killed many," he noted grimly.

"Over 1,00,000 men to be precise, your majesty," a soldier replied.

As Ashoka moved farther, survivors cried out, "Mercy! We beg for mercy!"

"We have captured 1,50,000 men, your majesty," another commander proudly stated.

Looking around the desolate, burning land, Ashoka realized the scale of the devastation. "It looks desolate here. Lakhs have died, your majesty." Seeing the immense sufferings of the people, a wave of profound sorrow washed over him. "Oh! No! Let's return."

Back in the palace, Ashoka was tormented. "Your majesty! Aren't you well?" a minister asked.

"I have lost my peace of mind," Ashoka confessed.

To add to his grief, a messenger came from Vidisha-nagar with a letter from Vidisha Devi.

"Where is Vidisha?" he asked eagerly.

"She plans to join the Sangha of Buddha," the messenger replied softly.

Ashoka read the letter: "Arya putra, you have broken the promise. I am sending the children to you. Please look after them properly. Yours."

Ashoka drew some comfort from the presence of his children, but he continued to brood over the senseless killings in Kalinga.

One day, from his palace window, he saw a young monk passing along the road. "Who can this be? Why do I feel drawn towards him? Go! Quick! Request that monk to come here."

When the monk arrived, Ashoka asked, "Venerable one, who are you?"

"Don't you see, who I am? A Buddhist monk."

"May I know your name, please?"

"My mother called me Nigrodha Kumar."

"Nigrodha Kumar! I know, that is the name of my brother's son."

"Yes. I am your nephew. I can guess what is going on in your mind."

"You hate me. Don't you?" Ashoka asked heavily. "Because you killed my father?"

"No. I love all," the young monk replied calmly. "Hatred is destructive. It should be conquered by love."

"Is not that reason enough to hate another? Pray, where did you learn this? At such a tender age?"

"From the teachings of Lord Buddha. They bring peace to troubled minds."

"My mind has not known peace," Ashoka admitted, breaking down.

"Then seek refuge in Buddha."

"Will you accept me as your disciple?"

"Yes. Repeat after me... I take refuge in the Buddha."

"I take refuge in the Buddha. I take refuge in the Sangha. I take refuge in the Dharma," Ashoka repeated, surrendering himself to the path of peace.

A transformation came about in Ashoka. All his attention now was on bringing peace and happiness to the people. For the comfort of travelers, even in the woods, he built rest houses and dispensaries. He erected pillars and carved instructions on rocks that read, "Priyadarshi, the beloved king of god says this..."

Years passed. One day, Ashoka said, "I have given away all things dear to me. Except..."

"Except what, Mahindra?" a minister asked.

"Your children," Ashoka finished softly.

Mahindra stepped forward. "Your son wishes to join the Sangha."

"Must you, Mahindra? It is with a heavy heart - but I give you permission."

Sanghamitra also approached him. "Father, allow me to join the nunnery."

"Oh! Sanghamitra!!" Ashoka cried out, heartbroken. But he could not deny her. His wife, who had raised them, also joined the nunnery. "My husband and son have renounced the world. I take no pleasure in life. You may join the nunnery."

After a few years, Mahindra came to him. "Father, I would like to go to Lanka."

"Go, my son. Go with my blessings."

Two years later, Sanghamitra also came to bid him goodbye. "A message has been received from Lanka. They need nuns at the mission."

Ashoka wept. "Sanghamitra, I have grown old. I can bear grief no more."

"Be brave. You forget Sanghamitra is a member of the Sangha."

"I understand. I wish you success. Go, my child. Thank you, father."

Watching them leave, Ashoka thought of his beloved wife. "Oh! Vidisha, your children have left me just as you did for the sake of Dharma."

A senior monk approached him gently. "Do not grieve. By sacrificing your dear ones you have proved that Dharma is dear to you. You are indeed, Dharmashoka."

Thus Ashoka became Dharmashoka. He ruled for many years and looked after the welfare of the subjects of his vast empire as a father would look after his children. The five principles of co-existence, which he propagated, are still respected the world over. The Ashoka pillar at Sarnath reminds us even till this day of his greatness, and the 'Dharma Chakra' on the Ashoka pillar adorns the national flag of India.

docs.title: Bhujyu
docs.text:
Ships laden with riches were sailing back to Sindhu, the land of Raja Tugra. The merchants on board were quite pleased with themselves.

"We have done better on this voyage than we did ever before," one boasted. "Or can hope to do in the future."

"Patience, my friend," another advised. "Since we are taking the route through the strait, we should get home faster than usual."

"Our Raja will be pleased with the money the royal treasury will get as its share. I am impatient to land," a third added.

Meanwhile, at a hideout on an island by the strait, a group of islanders lay in wait.

"How many do you see?" the chief asked his lookout.

"Seven."

"Are our ships in position?"

"Yes. And our men are ready."

"Let them come nearer... all seven of them!" the chief smirked. "So am I. Come on. Now attack!"

The islanders ambushed the merchants. "Take everything but spare our lives," the merchants pleaded. The islanders ruthlessly transferred all the cargo to their own ships.

A few days later at the court of Raja Tugra, the merchants reported their loss. "...And as we entered the strait... we were ambushed by some islanders and robbed of all our cargo."

"These islanders have become a menace," a minister noted.

"Unless they are subdued no trade is possible with the outside world, Maharaj," a merchant pleaded.

"As your King it is my duty to make the seas safe for you. And I shall do it," Raja Tugra declared.

Later, Tugra spoke to his son, Prince Bhujyu. "Bhujyu, I am going to lead an expedition to the island."

"You? No father. As your son, that should be my privilege," Bhujyu insisted.

"But you are young, inexperienced and unacquainted with the hazards of the sea, my son."

"All the more reason why I should go, father. It would be a good beginning. How else will I gain experience?"

The Queen, Bhujyu's mother, was worried. "Oh, no, my dear child! The island chief is wicked... wily... treacherous..."

"I am the son of a king... born to destroy the wicked and protect the virtuous," Bhujyu said proudly.

"You make me happy and you make me proud, my son," the Raja smiled.

Bhujyu knelt before his mother. "Mother, send me on my first mission with your blessings."

"For a moment, I forgot my dharma. Forgive me, Bhujyu," she said, placing her hands on his head. "Live long, my son! May you be crowned with success."

The call was given, and Bhujyu's men assembled before him. "We are with you, Bhujyu!" they cheered.

Before setting sail, Bhujyu made offerings to various deities. "Let us pray to Pushan to show us the right path," he said, offering oblations to the fire. "This is for Pushan, the path-finder. And this for Indra. And this for the Ashvin twins! And the Sun-god."

The fleet set sail to the cheers of "Long live Sapta-Sindhu!"

After a voyage of three days and three nights, they spotted birds. "Birds! We must be nearing the island!"

Bhujyu blew a mighty conch, and his soldiers emitted fierce war cries. Just then, a veteran sailor of a hundred voyages came running to Bhujyu. "I fear we are in for a storm. Prince Bhujyu! A storm!"

The very next moment, a massive tempest broke out. "Help!" a sailor cried as he was thrown overboard. Even as Bhujyu tried to pull the man up, a huge wave hit the ship. Most of the ships sank and many of the men were drowned.

"Did you hear that cry?" a sailor on a surviving boat asked.

"One of our men calling out for help."

"Shall we turn the boat?"

"No! Let us get away from this accursed place." They ignored the cries for help and sailed away.

Bhujyu, however, was alive. He came up gasping for air and, with great effort, swam on. Suddenly he saw a vessel in the distance. "A ship! One of our ships! Help! Help!" he shouted.

But the men on the boat saw him and said, "Keep going."

The storm at last subsided. Bhujyu was all alone, afloat on the high seas. "No... I can't keep it up... any longer," he thought exhaustedly. "O Ashvins, come to my rescue!"

Just then, a log of wood bumped into him. "A log of wood! Praise be to your name, O Ashvins." Bhujyu slumped onto the log of wood and closed his eyes.

For some time Bhujyu drifted on, clinging to the log. When he opened his eyes, he wondered, "Where am I?" Suddenly he saw a dorsal fin cutting through the water. "A killer fish! I have neither the strength... nor the weapons..." Then he realized he still held the log. "Oh! I do! I do have a weapon!"

As the shark lunged at him, Bhujyu yelled, "Come! Come my friend!" and jammed the log hard into its open jaws. "Sink your teeth into it. Sink them hard." The shark, unable to close its mouth, thrashed away.

Overcome by thirst, Bhujyu took a mouthful of salt water, only to spit it out. "Oh, for some water! O Ashvins, please help me! Don't let me die a futile death. O Ashvins, if I must die, let it be on a battlefield... with arrows piercing my heart... not of thirst in this watery grave. Save Bhujyu who worships you. O Ashvins, you saved Antaka, Vandana, Rebha, Deerghatamas and many others."

As he lay semi-conscious, the Ashvins, the mighty twin heroes and helpers of all, appeared in a celestial boat and pulled him out of the water. When Bhujyu at last opened his eyes, he saw them. "You must be the Ashvins, the mighty heroes, helpers of all. Accept my salutations!"

Then the Ashvins gave Bhujyu some honey, milk, and soma to restore his strength. After three days and three nights, their boats safely reached the shores of the Sindhu land. As soon as they heard of the arrival of the boats, Tugra and his queen rushed there.

"Bhujyu! Oh, Bhujyu! My son!" his mother wept.

"O mother, I am safe! Alive!"

By the time the reunited family looked up to thank their saviors, the boats with the twins in them had receded into the horizon. "O Ashvins, ever-vigilant helpers of mankind," Raja Tugra prayed. "You should have waited for an offering of soma and honey!"

Later, Bhujyu approached his father. "Father, permit me to resume the campaign against the island chief."

"Are you not afraid of the sea?" Tugra asked in surprise.

"No, father. On the contrary I am more confident. With the Ashvins on my side, I have nothing to fear."

"I will not stop you, son. You have my blessings."

A few weeks later, on the island hideout, a lookout shouted, "Ships from the land of Tugra have been sighted."

"It is very kind of him to send us a fresh supply of riches so soon!" the pirate chief laughed. "Mount an attack at the usual spot."

As the ships approached, the pirates yelled, "Here they come! Attack!"

But this time the islanders were in for a surprise. "Welcome!" Bhujyu shouted, drawing his sword. Bhujyu's well-prepared men easily overpowered the islanders, recovered all the stolen riches, and returned home in triumph.

"The seas are now safe for the traders of Tugra," Raja Tugra announced proudly. "In performing your dharma, you have helped me perform mine. I am proud of you, my son."

docs.title: Atri
docs.text:
Sage Atri lived with his family on the outskirts of a dense forest.

One day, his sons came to him with alarming news. "Father, I heard the Dasyus have moved into the forest."

"How does that concern me?" Atri asked calmly, tending to a sapling.

"You must not go about in the forest anymore," his son urged.

"Why not? Surely the forest is large enough for all of us," the sage reasoned.

His sons were not convinced. "What if the Dasyus attack him?" one whispered. "I am worried. Let us follow him at a distance."

Later, Atri walked on and on till he reached the thickest part of the forest. He looked up at a giant tree. "What a tall tree! I wonder what the earth looks like from above!"

Curious, Atri began to climb the tree. He went higher and higher. At last, he reached the top. "What a magnificent view! The earth seems to expand when seen from such a height. I wonder how it would look when seen from the skies." Inspired by the vastness, he sat on a high branch and began to meditate on the idea.

Soon it was night. Atri was still lost in meditation. When he finally opened his eyes to the dawn, he marveled, "What a glorious sight!"

Atri slid down the tree, walked to a stream near by, had his bath, and prayed to the sun. Even as he opened his eyes from his prayer, he was suddenly grabbed. "Aa..." he cried out. The Dasyus had got him.

As they dragged him away, Atri cried out the names of his favourite deities: "O Ashvins... O Ashvins..."

His sons, who had been following him at a distance, heard him. "Father is in trouble!" they yelled. The boys ran as fast as their legs could carry them to help their father. But in their haste, they suddenly tripped and fell, right into a trap laid by the Dasyus.

"I knew you were anxious to join your father," the Dasyu chief mocked them.

Atri and his sons were bound and dragged to a massive tree with a hollow trunk. "You will now go on a long journey," the chief announced. "Show him the way."

Atri was thrown violently into the deep, dark hollow of the tree. "Father!" his sons cried out as they were thrown in after him.

Inside the pitch-black abyss, Atri wondered, "Is this infinity... or am I in the womb of mother earth?"

The next moment he heard noises from above and looked up. He could see his sons tumbling down. "My sons!"

"Father!" they cried as they landed beside him. "Father, they are going to set this place on fire. I heard them say so."

"What shall we do?" they asked in panic.

"Let us pray to the Ashvins!" Atri said steadfastly. "O Ashvins! Come to our rescue! Now this very moment! O Ashvins, save the boys."

Smoke began to pour down into the hollow. The boys started coughing. "Aa-a-ah!"

"You must come. Now!" Atri prayed fervently.

Their call was heard. The Ashvins, the divine twin horsemen, came riding on their celestial chariot drawn by two swift asses.

They swept through the forest, dispensing justice. Those Dasyus who dared to put up a fight fell, while the wiser ones fled in terror as the Ashvins advanced in their chariot.

The twins quickly rescued Atri and his sons from the burning tree. "We salute you, O twins," Atri and his sons bowed deeply in gratitude.

Atri's unwavering faith and courage had paid off. He lost neither his freedom nor his life. Overjoyed and relieved, they refreshed themselves at the stream. "Water! The Ashvins are here!"

docs.title: Bhujyu
docs.text:

"Shall we turn the boat?"

"No! Let us get away from this accursed place." They ignored the cries for help and sailed away.

Bhujyu, however, was alive. He came up gasping for air and, with great effort, swam on. Suddenly he saw a vessel in the distance. "A ship! One of our ships! Help! Help!" he shouted.

But the men on the boat saw him and said, "Keep going."

The storm at last subsided. Bhujyu was all alone, afloat on the high seas. "No... I can't keep it up... any longer," he thought exhaustedly. "O Ashvins, come to my rescue!"

Just then, a log of wood bumped into him. "A log of wood! Praise be to your name, O Ashvins." Bhujyu slumped onto the log of wood and closed his eyes.

For some time Bhujyu drifted on, clinging to the log. When he opened his eyes, he wondered, "Where am I?" Suddenly he saw a dorsal fin cutting through the water. "A killer fish! I have neither the strength... nor the weapons..." Then he realized he still held the log. "Oh! I do! I do have a weapon!"

As the shark lunged at him, Bhujyu yelled, "Come! Come my friend!" and jammed the log hard into its open jaws. "Sink your teeth into it. Sink them hard." The shark, unable to close its mouth, thrashed away.

Overcome by thirst, Bhujyu took a mouthful of salt water, only to spit it out. "Oh, for some water! O Ashvins, please help me! Don't let me die a futile death. O Ashvins, if I must die, let it be on a battlefield... with arrows piercing my heart... not of thirst in this watery grave. Save Bhujyu who worships you. O Ashvins, you saved Antaka, Vandana, Rebha, Deerghatamas and many others."

As he lay semi-conscious, the Ashvins, the mighty twin heroes and helpers of all, appeared in a celestial boat and pulled him out of the water. When Bhujyu at last opened his eyes, he saw them. "You must be the Ashvins, the mighty heroes, helpers of all. Accept my salutations!"

Then the Ashvins gave Bhujyu some honey, milk, and soma to restore his strength. After three days and three nights, their boats safely reached the shores of the Sindhu land. As soon as they heard of the arrival of the boats, Tugra and his queen rushed there.

"Bhujyu! Oh, Bhujyu! My son!" his mother wept.

"O mother, I am safe! Alive!"

By the time the reunited family looked up to thank their saviors, the boats with the twins in them had receded into the horizon. "O Ashvins, ever-vigilant helpers of mankind," Raja Tugra prayed. "You should have waited for an offering of soma and honey!"

Later, Bhujyu approached his father. "Father, permit me to resume the campaign against the island chief."

"Are you not afraid of the sea?" Tugra asked in surprise.

"No, father. On the contrary I am more confident. With the Ashvins on my side, I have nothing to fear."

"I will not stop you, son. You have my blessings."

A few weeks later, on the island hideout, a lookout shouted, "Ships from the land of Tugra have been sighted."

"It is very kind of him to send us a fresh supply of riches so soon!" the pirate chief laughed. "Mount an attack at the usual spot."

As the ships approached, the pirates yelled, "Here they come! Attack!"

But this time the islanders were in for a surprise. "Welcome!" Bhujyu shouted, drawing his sword. Bhujyu's well-prepared men easily overpowered the islanders, recovered all the stolen riches, and returned home in triumph.

"The seas are now safe for the traders of Tugra," Raja Tugra announced proudly. "In performing your dharma, you have helped me perform mine. I am proud of you, my son."

docs.title: Atri
docs.text:
Sage Atri lived with his family on the outskirts of a dense forest.

One day, his sons came to him with alarming news. "Father, I heard the Dasyus have moved into the forest."

"How does that concern me?" Atri asked calmly, tending to a sapling.

"You must not go about in the forest anymore," his son urged.

"Why not? Surely the forest is large enough for all of us," the sage reasoned.

His sons were not convinced. "What if the Dasyus attack him?" one whispered. "I am worried. Let us follow him at a distance."

Later, Atri walked on and on till he reached the thickest part of the forest. He looked up at a giant tree. "What a tall tree! I wonder what the earth looks like from above!"

Curious, Atri began to climb the tree. He went higher and higher. At last, he reached the top. "What a magnificent view! The earth seems to expand when seen from such a height. I wonder how it would look when seen from the skies." Inspired by the vastness, he sat on a high branch and began to meditate on the idea.

Soon it was night. Atri was still lost in meditation. When he finally opened his eyes to the dawn, he marveled, "What a glorious sight!"

Atri slid down the tree, walked to a stream near by, had his bath, and prayed to the sun. Even as he opened his eyes from his prayer, he was suddenly grabbed. "Aa..." he cried out. The Dasyus had got him.

As they dragged him away, Atri cried out the names of his favourite deities: "O Ashvins... O Ashvins..."

His sons, who had been following him at a distance, heard him. "Father is in trouble!" they yelled. The boys ran as fast as their legs could carry them to help their father. But in their haste, they suddenly tripped and fell, right into a trap laid by the Dasyus.

"I knew you were anxious to join your father," the Dasyu chief mocked them.

Atri and his sons were bound and dragged to a massive tree with a hollow trunk. "You will now go on a long journey," the chief announced. "Show him the way."

Atri was thrown violently into the deep, dark hollow of the tree. "Father!" his sons cried out as they were thrown in after him.

Inside the pitch-black abyss, Atri wondered, "Is this infinity... or am I in the womb of mother earth?"

The next moment he heard noises from above and looked up. He could see his sons tumbling down. "My sons!"

"Father!" they cried as they landed beside him. "Father, they are going to set this place on fire. I heard them say so."

"What shall we do?" they asked in panic.

"Let us pray to the Ashvins!" Atri said steadfastly. "O Ashvins! Come to our rescue! Now this very moment! O Ashvins, save the boys."

Smoke began to pour down into the hollow. The boys started coughing. "Aa-a-ah!"

"You must come. Now!" Atri prayed fervently.

Their call was heard. The Ashvins, the divine twin horsemen, came riding on their celestial chariot drawn by two swift asses.

They swept through the forest, dispensing justice. Those Dasyus who dared to put up a fight fell, while the wiser ones fled in terror as the Ashvins advanced in their chariot.

The twins quickly rescued Atri and his sons from the burning tree. "We salute you, O twins," Atri and his sons bowed deeply in gratitude.

Atri's unwavering faith and courage had paid off. He lost neither his freedom nor his life. Overjoyed and relieved, they refreshed themselves at the stream. "Water! The Ashvins are here!"

docs.title: Ayyappan
docs.text:
Emboldened by a boon from Brahma, the demon Mahishasura began harassing all on earth. The people cried out, "The evil one is here! O Devas, save us!"

The Devas held council in heaven. "This asura must be destroyed," they decided. "Because of Brahma's boon, only a woman would be able to do it. Then let us evoke one with our combined powers."

As soon as the resplendent woman appeared before them, she laughed, "Ha! Ha! Ha!"

"We will call her Chandika Devi," the Devas said.

Laughing all the while, Chandika Devi descended on earth. The whole world shook with the force of her arrival. Mahishasura, seeing her, commanded his men, "Who is that senseless woman? Go bring her to me."

But Chandika Devi vanquished his men in no time. They returned to Mahishasura. "Lord! We had to retreat."

"Take the whole army if necessary, but bring her here!" Mahishasura roared. However, his entire army was no match for Chandika Devi and was soon routed.

Mad with anger, Mahishasura approached Chandika Devi fiercely. "That woman has gone too far. I shall destroy her," he declared and attacked her. But he, too, was no match for her divine power and was soon slain. Her mission accomplished, Chandika Devi ascended to heaven.

When Mahishi, the wife of Mahishasura, learnt of her husband's death, she vowed revenge. "I shall teach the Devas a lesson. It was they who invoked the Devi." She undertook a deep penance to propitiate Lord Brahma.

When Brahma appeared before her, he asked, "Mahishi! What do you desire?"

"That I should not meet my death at the hands of either Vishnu or Shiva!" she asked. Brahma granted her the boon and vanished.

Feeling invincible, Mahishi began taunting the Devas. "To whom will you turn now? Shiva and Vishnu are powerless against me."

Shiva watched and heard all. He went to Vishnu, who had taken the form of Mohini, the beautiful enchantress.

"We must create a son who will destroy Mahishi," they decided.

"Who will rear the baby?" asked Vishnu.

"I must put an end to Mahishi's arrogance," Shiva said. "My devotees, Rajashekhara and his queen, long for a child. I will take him there!"

As soon as the radiant infant was created, Shiva said, "I will tie this gold bell around his neck before I take him to earth."

Meanwhile, on earth, King Rajashekhara was out hunting with his retinue. After a tiring day, while the party relaxed on the banks of the river Pampa, Rajashekhara wandered off alone. "How peaceful and quiet it is here," he thought.

Suddenly he heard a feeble wail. A baby's cry! Rajashekhara walked in the direction from which the sound came and saw a helpless baby lying all alone on a rock.

"Poor baby! Don't cry!" he soothed, looking around. "Not a soul in sight. To whom could it belong?"

While he debated with himself whether to take the child home or leave it, a Brahman suddenly appeared before him. "Do not hesitate, O King. Take him home."

"But who is he?"

"You will know when he is twelve years old," the Brahman replied.

"Wait! What shall I call him?"

"Call him Manikanthan, after the gold bell which hangs round his neck," the Brahman advised before disappearing.

Rajashekhara took Manikanthan home to his queen. "A child at last! How happy I am," the queen rejoiced. "He is God's own gift."

But Rajashekhara's wicked Diwan didn't think so. "The accursed child! It has upset all my plans," he muttered.

Manikanthan soon grew up into a strong young lad. He was placed under the guidance of an excellent Guru and soon mastered all there was to learn. "Manikanthan is not an ordinary child," the Guru observed.

By the time Manikanthan's studies were over, the Guru knew that he was a divine child. When Manikanthan offered him the traditional guru-dakshina, the Guru said, "From you I want compassion. Not these." The Guru brought his blind and mute son before Manikanthan. "Manikanthan, please restore his sight and speech."

Manikanthan blessed the boy, and instantly, the Guru's son saw light and spoke. "Father! I have been given a new life!"

After this first miracle, Manikanthan performed many more, and the news spread. "I have never seen such a gifted boy," people praised. "Our future is assured. He will succeed the King."

The Diwan, who overheard these comments, got really worried. "I must get rid of the boy somehow."

Then, the unforeseen happened. The queen gave birth to a son.

The Diwan was jubilant. "The throne shall yet be mine!" he plotted. A few days later, he went to the queen. "Manikanthan will soon be twelve years old. He will then be crowned heir apparent. With that, your own son will lose all chances of ever becoming the ruler."

"What can I do? I am helpless," the queen worried.

"Will you do as I tell you? For the sake of your own son?"

"I will. For my son I will do anything."

"Then pretend to have a severe headache. Leave the rest to me," the Diwan instructed. The Diwan then went to the court physician and bribed him. "Do as I tell you and you will be well rewarded."

Soon, a messenger came to the physician. "The queen is in agony. The king has asked you to examine her immediately."

After the physician had examined the queen, the anxious king asked, "What is the quickest remedy? I cannot bear to see my queen in pain."

"The only remedy for this ailment is tigress's milk," the physician lied.

The king was aghast. "Tigress's milk? But how are we to get it?" The king thought about it for a while and then sent for his Diwan. "Send as many men as possible into the forest in search of tigress's milk. The one who brings it shall have half my kingdom."

Many men went but all returned unsuccessful. "We saw a number of tigers, but..." they stammered.

Then, exactly as the wicked Diwan had foreseen, Manikanthan approached his father. "Father, let me go on this errand!"

"Manikanthan, no! You are too young. How can I let you go on such a dangerous venture?"

"For my mother's sake let me go, father."

Manikanthan persisted, and Rajashekhara finally had to yield. "All right, my son. Go if you must. But take care."

Manikanthan set out for the forest. As he wandered deeper and deeper into it, he came to the domain of Mahishi, who lived in that forest.

"Who is this creature who dares to trespass on our forest?" an asura guard demanded, running to Mahishi. "There is a boy roaming in our forests."

"How dare he, without my permission! I'll destroy him," Mahishi declared.

Mahishi went up to Manikanthan and attacked him. "Come, Mahishi. My earthly mission is about to be fulfilled," Manikanthan said calmly. A fierce battle ensued, and Manikanthan soon killed Mahishi.

As Manikanthan continued his search for a tigress, Indra, king of the Devas, appeared before him. "I shall take the form of a tigress and come with you into the city as your mount," Indra offered. The Devas who had accompanied Indra also turned themselves into tigresses. "We will follow you into the city."

As Manikanthan rode into the city on his terrifying mount, followed by an equally terrifying retinue, the citizens fled in panic. "Tigers! Help! Run!"

One of the citizens ran to Rajashekhara. "Manikanthan has returned... with a horde of tigresses."

The king rushed out to see this unusual sight and was stunned. "Manikanthan is twelve today. Today I will know who he is," he remembered.

Just then, he heard a divine voice from the heavens. "Manikanthan is none but God himself."

Rajashekhara ran forward and fell at Manikanthan's feet. "Please send the tigresses away, Manikanthan. My people are terror-stricken."

Manikanthan smiled, and the tigresses vanished.

"As soon as you left, the queen recovered. I became suspicious," the king told him. "My suspicions were confirmed when I overheard a conversation between her and my Diwan. I shall punish them for this."

"Spare them," Manikanthan said gently. "They hastened my mission of slaying Mahishi. And now I must leave this world."

"Lord, a last request," the king wept. "Show me the place in my kingdom where I may build a temple for you."

"I am pleased. Build it where this arrow I shoot falls," Manikanthan said, drawing his bow. "You may observe the path of the arrow."

The arrow flew high and fell at the summit of the Shabari hills. Manikanthan vanished, and the king began his trek to the mountain top.

"It is so high... impossible," the king thought. He decided, "Let there be just eighteen steps leading to the temple at the eastern gate."

At last, Rajashekhara reached the top of the mountain. "How will I ever achieve my task?" As he stood there, dazed, wondering where to begin, a voice guided him. "I shall send Vishwakarma to help you out."

Under the guidance of Vishwakarma, the divine architect, the magnificent temple was soon completed. "Through the Lord's grace it has risen - noble and imposing," the king said. "Will I find a befitting idol now?"

Lord Vishnu divined the king's anxiety and sent for Parashurama. "Go down to earth as a stone-carver and help Rajashekhara install the idol."

Parashurama left for Rajashekhara's palace in the garb of a sculptor. At the king's court, he announced, "I have heard of your problem. I may be able to help."

"Show me your wares before you take up the challenging task," the king requested.

Parashurama showed his flawless sculptures.

"You are no ordinary sculptor. Tell me the truth. Who are you?" the king asked in awe.

"I am Parashurama. I have already fashioned the idol," he said, assuming his own form. "Today, the day of Makara Sankranti, is auspicious. I shall install the idol straightaway."

And thus it was that the image of Ayyappa, as Manikanthan came to be popularly known, was sanctified on the hill.

Meanwhile, the wicked Diwan was afflicted by an incurable disease. "I have sinned. Will I ever be cleansed?" he suffered.

One night he had a dream of Ayyappa. "Come to my temple on the Shabari hill. But enter only after cleansing yourself in the Pampa."

The Diwan wasted no time. The next morning he set out for the river Pampa. "I have come to wash away my sins in remorse, holy Mother. Save me."

The Diwan came out of the water glowing with health. "I am cleansed. The holy mother has been merciful." Then, without pausing for breath, he ran up the eighteen steps to the temple. "Ayyappa! Ayyappa!"

When he reached the sanctum sanctorum, overcome by emotion, he fell at the feet of the idol. "Swamiye Sharanam Ayyappa!"

And to this day, as millions of pilgrims make their arduous way to the temple of Ayyappa on the Shabari hills, their faces shine with devotion as they call out, "Swamiye Sharanam Ayyappa!"

docs.title: Bahubali
docs.text:
Long ago, Nabhiraj, the father of Lord Rishabhadeva, lived in a magnificent palace in Ayodhya. He brought up Rishabhadeva with great love. When Rishabhadeva grew up and became King, he had two sons. He made his elder son, Bharat, proficient in social affairs and administration. His other son, Bahubali, learned the art of archery.

One day, it so happened that Rishabhadeva became disillusioned with his regal splendour. Seeking spiritual liberation, he renounced the world to become a muni (sage), also known as Adinatha, the first Tirthankara of the Jains. Before renouncing the world, Rishabhadeva divided his kingdom amongst his sons. Bharat was given the kingdom of Ayodhya, and Bahubali was made the king of Podanpur.

But Bharat was not satisfied. He had a burning desire to become the sovereign king of the entire world. One day, he received auspicious news from his general. "Oh Lord! A Chakra has come into being in your armoury. Take advantage of it."

"General, state clearly what you mean," Bharat asked.

"Your majesty, a Chakra is the sign of triumph. This Chakra expects from you the victory of the universe."

"We understand," Bharat proclaimed. "Tomorrow itself our army will march to gain victory over the entire earth."

Early next morning, King Bharat's huge army was arranged in columns. Led by the invincible Chakra, the army marched forward in all four directions. They advanced east across the Ganges, where petty kings surrendered and presented precious gifts. They marched south, conquering regions from Kalinga to Kerala. In the west, they crossed the Vindhya mountains and the Narmada river, vanquishing kings up to Saurashtra. Crossing the Indus, they marched to the Vijayardha mountains, bringing under control the Himavana mountain region and subjugating the non-Aryan kings of Chilat and Avarta.

After years of victory, when Bharat finally returned to Ayodhya, the Chakra suddenly stopped at the main city gate. Panicking, the people cried in unison, "What has happened?"

When this news was conveyed to Bharat, he summoned his priest. "Oh learned one, please explain why this Chakra has stopped."

"Your majesty, it seems the conquest is not complete," the priest explained.

"We have conquered all the four directions. What remains to be conquered now?" Bharat demanded.

"Pardon me, my Lord," the priest said hesitantly, "your invincible brothers have not yet offered salutation to you. Amongst them, the valiant and wise Bahubali is the chief."

"Oh, then they will be vanquished," Bharat said furiously. "Defiant Bahubali shall see the fury of our weapons. Being younger, he behaves so. But unless he surrenders, you cannot be called a sovereign king."

Furious, Bharat sent his minister with a message to his brothers: "Now only Bharat is the ruler of the entire earth. If they obey us, then they too will get some share."

When Bharat's brothers heard this, they were dismayed. They went to their father, Muni Rishabhadeva, on Mount Kailash and implored, "Father, we have bowed down only before you. In his arrogance, Bharat wants us to offer him salutation. What shall we do?"

"Strife for the kingdoms divides brothers and sons," Rishabhadeva advised. "Someday Bharat too will have to give up his kingdom. Why fight? Abandon your kingdom." Following his advice, the brothers gave up their kingdoms.

But Bahubali, the king of Podanpur, refused. When Bharat's emissary arrived demanding his subjugation, Bahubali said, "What sort of salutation to a brother who carries a sword in his hand to snatch the kingdom given by our father? Go tell him that he cannot become the sovereign king without defeating me in the battlefield."

Soon, the two massive forces stood facing each other near Podanpur. Both brothers were ready with their bows, and the generals began arranging their armies in order.

Before the war could begin, the ministers of both sides intervened. "Lord Bharat and Lord Bahubali, please wait!" one of them shouted. "It is necessary that the ministers of both sides hold talks before starting this war."

The masters agreed. The ministers assembled and reasoned, "This war is not motivated to remove any injustice. Why should the blood of others be shed just to determine which of the two brothers is a better warrior? It would be best if the armies do not combat. Only the two brothers should fight."

They proposed a peaceful duel: "Both the brothers should wage a sight-battle, a water-battle, and a wrestling match. Whoever wins in these will be declared the victor." Both kings agreed to this war of righteousness.

First started the sight-battle. Both brothers stared at each other, looking hard into each other's eyes. In the end, Bharat's eyes dampened and his lashes dropped; his neck also started aching. Bahubali, being taller, kept on staring without a blink and won.

Next, they descended into a pond for the water-battle. Bharat started splashing water on Bahubali with all his strength, but Bahubali stood quiet. Then Bahubali started showering blows on the water. The water he splashed entered Bharat's mouth, eyes, nose, and ears, whereas the water Bharat threw never reached Bahubali's face. Overwhelmed, Bharat turned his face away, and Bahubali won the water-battle too.

Finally, the wrestling match started. Both entered the wrestling arena. Their hands met in fierce grips. They clapped their thighs, clenched their fists, and showered blows on each other. Engaged in the fight, Bahubali suddenly changed his position and effortlessly lifted Bharat up with both hands.

"Shall I throw him on the ground?" Bahubali thought. "No, he is my elder brother. I shall make him sit on my shoulder." But as Bahubali brought him slowly and gently to the ground, Bharat's eyes reddened with humiliation. Deceitfully, in a fit of rage, Bharat threw his divine Chakra at Bahubali.

The spectators shouted in horror, "This is deceitful! Shame on Bharat!"

But a miracle occurred. The Chakra merely went round Bahubali and then stopped peacefully in his hands. Bahubali smiled and turned to Bharat in a harsh tone. "Bharat, you forget that this Chakra cannot harm my mountain-like body. In your anger, you also forgot that this Chakra is not effective on brothers. You wish to become a sovereign king by destroying your brothers. By deceiving me, you have tarnished our father's name."

As he spoke, Bahubali calmed down and thought. "The elder brother has behaved in such a shameful manner for the greed of a kingdom. Kingdoms are a cause of misery. Of what use is this power and glory? For a kingdom even brothers come to daggers."

He turned to Bharat and said softly, "Bharat, you did me good! Take the kingdom. I am going to perform penance. I did not behave respectfully towards my elder brother, therefore, brother, forgive me."

The sky reverberated with cheers: "Hail to the sovereign king Bahubali!"

Bahubali cast aside his royal robes and jewels. "Look after the kingdom, Bharat. I am leaving now," he said, and proceeded to become a muni.

Bahubali gave up everything. There were neither clothes on his body nor the joys of the palace. His body became emaciated as he stood on both legs performing intense penance. Creepers wound round his arms and legs, and serpents made mounts of earth near his feet. But despite his severe austerities, he did not attain perfect knowledge.

When Bharat heard of this, he approached his father, Rishabhadeva. "Father, why has Bahubali not attained omniscience? Something still disturbs him."

"He is not free from the thought that he is standing on your domain," Rishabhadeva explained.

"How can this be wiped, oh venerated one?"

"Bharat, go with your family. Offer him respect."

Bharat immediately went to Bahubali with his family and bowed deeply. "We convey our salutation to you, Muni Bahubali. The ground on which you stand is not mine, will never be mine... It belongs to everyone. Please show us the path."

On hearing this, the final trace of pique in Bahubali's mind disappeared. He attained omniscience and achieved liberation.

To commemorate the memory of Bahubali, Bharat installed a life-size statue of him in Podanpur. Time passed, and creepers and snakes made their homes nearby, hiding the statue.

Centuries later, in South India, the mother of a general named Chamundaraya expressed a desire to see Bahubali's statue. "It is very difficult to see the statue of Bahubali in Podanpur," Chamundaraya told her. "I shall erect an idol of Bahubali here on the Indra Giri."

On his bidding, artisans started hewing a huge statue of Bahubali in Shravanabelagola near Mysore in the year 983. Even today, the magnificent monolith stands as it was, its faint smile serving as a reproach to the wars waged by kings and emperors. Empires are built and destroyed, but Bahubali's spiritual empire lasts to this day.

docs.title: Bappa Rawal: The Great Rajput Hero
docs.text:
In the eighth century, as foreign invaders launched successive attacks on the western borders of India, the native Rajput rulers wasted their strength fighting among themselves for petty, selfish ends. Divided and weakened, their kingdoms faced constant peril.

It was during these turbulent times that Nagaditya, the ruler of Idur, was slain by his own Bhil subjects. His grieving queen fled into the dense wilderness with her three-year-old son, Prince Shailadhish, accompanied only by a few fiercely loyal followers and the royal priest, the Rajguru.

For years, the royal family lived in hiding, enduring hardship and constant fear. "Rajguru, I am tired of this hard life," the Queen Mother confessed one day, her eyes heavy with exhaustion.

"You will have to bear it for the sake of the young prince, Rajmata," the Rajguru replied gently.

"Will he have no respite from our enemies?" a loyal follower asked anxiously.

"He will," the Rajguru reassured them. "We plan to give him another name. Since the destiny of Mewar rests in the young prince's hands, his name shall now be Bappa Rawal."

"But he would still be easily recognized here!" the Queen Mother pointed out.

"We will take him to Parashar village in the Trikoot range, in the Abu hills," said the Rajguru. "Nobody will recognize him there."

"Must you take him away from me?" she wept, cradling her son.

"Yes, in the interests of Mewar, you must bear this separation," the Rajguru urged.

One afternoon, the Rajguru brought the young prince to Nagda, placing him in the care of a trusted Brahman family. "Bappa is hardworking and honest," the Rajguru assured the householder. "He will serve you well."

Turning to the boy, the Brahman's wife gave him his duties. "Every morning, you will have to take my cattle to the hills to graze."

"It will be done, sir," Bappa replied respectfully. That evening, as Bappa watched his protectors depart into the sunset, he swallowed his sorrow. They have gone, he thought to himself. I am all alone.

Bappa toiled faithfully every day, quickly earning high praise from the Brahman and his wife. While herding the cattle in the nearby hills, he met other local boys. "Hey! Who are you?" a boy called out one morning.

"I am Baliya, and he is Dev," another answered. "We are learning to shoot arrows. You can join us if you like."

Bappa accepted eagerly. While the cattle grazed peacefully, Bappa practiced archery until his skill eclipsed all others. "You've hit the target again, Bappa!" Baliya cheered. "He's never missed a single shot!"

Moving from archery to sword-fighting and wrestling, Bappa's strength and agility grew astonishingly. Defeated in every match, the village boys gathered around him in admiration. "Bappa, you have gotten the better of us in sword-fighting as well as in wrestling," they declared. "Henceforward, you are our leader. You command, and we obey."

However, Bappa's rapid physical development began to arouse suspicion in the Brahman's home. "Bappa is not honest anymore," the Brahman's wife complained to her husband. "Don't you see how hefty he has suddenly become?"

"He's just a growing boy," the Brahman reasoned.

"I have my doubts. The milk yield of our grey cow has decreased," she insisted.
