"You don't suspect him of drinking the milk, do you?" the Brahman asked, shaken. "No, it cannot be! Bappa is honest. My wife must be mistaken."

Overhearing their conversation, Bappa felt deeply wounded. When he stepped into the courtyard, the Brahman confronted him gently. "For several days, the grey cow's milk yield has been low, Bappa. Why?"

"I too have wondered why, master," Bappa replied, his voice choked with emotion. "The grey cow has not been giving enough milk, and I know you suspect me. You will have to let me prove my honesty to convince your wife."

Determined to uncover the truth, Bappa and his friends, Baliya and Dev, kept a vigilant watch on the grey cow as it grazed in the hills. "Could someone be milking it while we are busy wrestling or shooting?" Baliya wondered.

"Quite possible," Bappa said. "We must keep an eye on that cow."

Deep in the forest, they followed the grey cow to a secluded rocky grove. To their astonishment, the cow stopped over a natural stone formation and stood perfectly still. White milk began to flow spontaneously from its udders, trickling over a sacred Shiva Linga nestled among the rocks.

"So that's where the milk goes!" Dev gasped.

"You must inform your master," Baliya added.

Nearby, sitting atop a high rock in deep meditation, was an austere hermit. "Who's that?" Bappa whispered.

When the boys returned home and related the miraculous scene, the Brahman released a sigh of relief. "I knew Bappa was honest!" he exclaimed.

"Thank God! The mystery is solved," his wife agreed remorsefully.

Returning to the forest, Baliya cautioned, "That is Mahatma Harit. Quick! Let's run away before he sees us—he is known to have a terrible temper!"

"No," Bappa insisted. "Let's sweep away the dry leaves from his hermitage and then go."

From that day forward, the boys quietly swept and cleaned the grove every afternoon while the saint meditated. In time, Mahatma Harit opened his eyes and wondered who was tending to his hermitage with such devotion. Catching the boys one afternoon, he called out softly, "Who are you, my children? Come to me. Don't be afraid. Are you the ones who clean up the hermitage every day?"

"Yes, Mahatmaji," Bappa replied, bowing. "I am Bappa, and these are Baliya and Dev."

Pleased by their humility and devotion, Mahatma Harit smiled warmly. "I am very pleased with you. You must come to me every day, and I will instruct you in the sacred lore." As Bappa bowed, the sage mentally noted, He has the refined manners of a true prince.

During this time, the region of Nagda was ruled by a Solanki king. As autumn approached, the king instructed his prime minister, "Celebrate the Jhool Jhoolni fair with great pomp and please the Goddess. The Princess has to be married this year."

When the fair commenced, Bappa, Baliya, and Dev joined the festive crowds. The Solanki Princess also visited the jungle temple with her maidens. After offering prayers at the shrine, the maidens walked toward the swings. "Rajkumari, we forgot to bring ropes for the swings!" a maiden cried. "How will we swing?"

Noticing the young cowherds nearby, another maiden suggested, "Those boys have ropes. They might help us out." The Princess called out to Bappa and his companions.

"What can we do for you?" Bappa asked.

"We forgot to bring our ropes. Will you lend us yours?" a maiden requested.

Nudged by a spirit of mischief, Bappa whispered to his friends, "Let me tease her a little." Turning to the maidens, he smiled boldly. "Sure—but on one condition!"

"Name it," the maiden replied.

"You will have to marry me," Bappa declared, pointing playfully to the Princess.

"What! How dare you?" the maidens gasped in shock. "Aren't you ashamed of yourself? Her father is the King! You could be punished for this!"

Baliya interjected quickly, "Our friend did not suggest a real wedding! He meant a mock one—like that of dolls!"

"The young man seems to be courageous and fearless," the Princess observed quietly to her maid.

Bappa turned to leave. "Let us go, friends. Why waste our time here?"

"Wait! I accept your condition," the Princess called out unexpectedly. Standing beneath a wide banyan tree, Bappa Rawal and the Solanki Princess went through a lighthearted mock marriage ceremony, looping garlanded vines around each other.

After the ceremony, Bappa chuckled, "You will soon forget me, Rajkumari."

"Never!" the Princess replied earnestly. "I will remember this mock marriage all my life."

Months passed, and preparations were underway for the Princess's formal betrothal to a neighbouring prince. Scholarly astrologers gathered in the royal court to match their horoscopes. Suddenly, the chief astrologer gasped in alarm. "My Lord, your daughter is already married! She is not worthy of our prince."

"My daughter married without my knowledge? Impossible!" the Solanki King roared. "There must be some mistake!"

"Astrology can never be wrong, sir!" the sage insisted.

The proposed alliance was promptly cancelled. Furious, the King summoned the Princess. "Who is this person you have married?" he demanded.

"I didn't ask his name, Father," the Princess confessed. "We went through a mock wedding at the Jhool Jhoolni fair."

A few days later, sitting in her chambers, the Princess drew a detailed portrait of the young cowherd from memory. "Aha! How handsome he is!" her maid admired. "Why, your picture is a perfect replica of that boy!"

"I wish we had asked his name!" the Princess sighed.

Just then, the King entered the chamber and caught sight of the painting. "Who is this?" he asked sharply.

"The same boy I was talking about, Father," she answered quietly.

The King turned to his guards in fury. "The young man in this picture, wherever he may be, shall be captured and brought before me!"

When news of the King's manhunt reached the hills, Bappa realized he was in danger. "The King's men are looking for the boy who married the Princess in fun," he told his companions. "It is not advisable for me to stay in this place."

"We must not let them know that it was you, Bappa," Baliya said.

Taking a heavy stone, Bappa threw it into a deep canyon. "Let us take an oath of secrecy," Bappa said solemnly. "If you don't keep my identity a secret, may all the good deeds of your ancestors be buried in a launderer's well, like this stone!"

"Where will you go?" Dev asked.

"To Mahatma Harit," Bappa replied.

"We will not let you go alone, Bappa," his friends declared firmly. "The three of us will stick together."

"I am fortunate to have friends like you," Bappa said gratefully.

For months, Bappa remained in hiding in the sacred grove, immersing himself in spiritual and military scriptures under Mahatma Harit's tutelage. However, yearning to see his mother, Bappa decided to visit her. "You are taking a great risk," Baliya warned. "The King's men are still searching everywhere."

"I am no coward!" Bappa replied, setting off for his mother's dwelling.

Reaching her humble cottage, Bappa fell at her feet. "Where did you disappear, my son?" the Queen Mother wept, embracing him tightly.

"I was at the feet of Mahatma Harit, learning the scriptures," Bappa explained. Introducing Baliya and Dev, he added, "Mother, here are my inseparable friends. Now I will always live with you."

Seeing the prince grown into a formidable youth, the old Rajguru approached the Queen Mother. "The Solanki's soldiers are still looking for Bappa, and everyone has seen his portrait," the Rajguru noted. "Now is the time for us to reveal the full truth to him."

The Queen Mother sat Bappa down and disclosed his true lineage. "Son, I am the sister of Raja Mori of Chittor—a true princess! All our family's suffering was endured for your sake, Bappa. Now that you have grown up, do your duty."

Hearing the story of his ancestors, Bappa's heart swelled with pride and determination. "I promise that I will win back the territories of my forefathers and restore honor to them!" he vowed.

"May God bless you! Bappa Ki Jai!" his followers cheered.

Set on fulfilling his destiny, Bappa and his friends rode toward Chittor. Along the way, they spotted a remote hut where the renowned sage Baba Gorakhnath sat in deep meditation. "Hush! Take care not to disturb him," Bappa instructed his companions.

As they waited respectfully, Baba Gorakhnath opened his eyes and gazed intently at the youth. Sensing the divine royal aura around Bappa, the sage smiled. "Brilliant young man, a time will come when you will unite Rajasthan and lead her to victory! Take this magic sword—it will help you in your grand mission."

Accepting the blessed blade, Bappa bowed low before continuing his journey.

Upon reaching Chittor, the fifteen-year-old Bappa presented himself at the royal court of his uncle, Raja Mori. Impressed by the boy's noble bearing, Raja Mori welcomed him warmly. "Come, sit by my side," the King offered.

For several days, Raja Mori observed Bappa's keen intelligence, martial prowess, and polite conduct. Delighted with the youth, the King made a surprise declaration: "I bestow a small estate on Bappa Rawal and appoint him to my Council of Ministers!"

The unexpected elevation of a young newcomer infuriated the veteran ministers of Chittor. "This is unfair and unjust!" they murmured bitterly behind closed doors. "Our King trusts him beyond reason! He is hardly sixteen years old, completely inexperienced, and inferior to us in every way. To work under a mere boy—what a humiliation!"

Unfazed by their resentment, Bappa continued to serve with distinction. Recognizing his capability, Raja Mori approached him privately. "The commander of my forces is too old. If I appoint you in his place...?"

"I shall not disappoint you, uncle," Bappa assured him. "You honor me by calling me brave. I will stake my all to heighten your glory."

Meanwhile, news of Bappa's rising fame reached the Solanki ruler of Nagda. Investigating the youth's origins, the Solanki King realized that Bappa Rawal was indeed the brave young man from the portrait. "He is the most eligible youth for my daughter!" the King declared. He immediately dispatched a messenger to Chittor with a formal marriage proposal.

When the Solanki envoy delivered the proposal, Bappa asked, "And what of the Princess?"

"She worships your portrait, my Lord," the envoy smiled.

With Raja Mori's blessing, Bappa accepted the proposal. He traveled to Nagda, where he was united in marriage with the Solanki Princess in a grand royal ceremony. After spending a few days in Nagda, Bappa returned to Chittor with his bride.

Upon his return, Bappa addressed the disgruntled old ministers directly. "My ministers resent my appointment," he told Raja Mori. "I will give it up if you wish."

"No," Raja Mori advised him. "Use your innate abilities and win them over."

Before long, a crisis struck the realm. A massive Arab army, led by foreign invaders, advanced rapidly upon Chittor. Standing before the royal court, Bappa rallied the nobles: "A foreigner has invaded Chittor. It is our sacred duty to protect the motherland!"

Resentful and cowardly, the old councillors refused to mobilize. "We will not fight," they announced coldly.

"Your reason?" Bappa asked, controlling his anger.

"We are too old," the councillors replied sarcastically. "We return the lands and gifts bestowed upon us. Dispatch your most trusted lieutenant to the battlefield—he is young, braver, and far more experienced than we are!"

The taunt ignited Bappa's spirit. "Most certainly, I will!" he roared. "Let the aged go home and rest. Bappa will take care of the enemy, unaided!"

"Then why delay? Get to the battlefield!" Raja Mori commanded.

Bappa led his forces to the front lines, charging into battle with his blessed sword. Seeing Bappa in action, the Solanki ruler of Nagda dispatched his army to attack the invaders from the rear. Caught in a crushing double-pronged assault, the Arab forces broke rank and retreated in chaos.

As the battle ended, a soldier ran up to Bappa. "What is it, my man?" Bappa asked.

"The councillors are ashamed," the soldier reported. "They are on their way to the battlefield."

"Tell them it's too late," Bappa smiled. "The enemy has been routed!"

Ashamed of their initial reluctance, the councillors bowed before Bappa upon their arrival. "We are glad that Chittor has such a brave son in you!" they proclaimed. "We are with you!"

Rather than returning immediately to Chittor, Bappa pursued the fleeing foreign forces all the way to Ghazni. Storming the enemy stronghold, he captured Salim, the foreign ruler, and liberated the ancestral territories of his forefathers. Appointing a trustworthy representative to govern Ghazni, Bappa returned victorious to Chittor, where the citizens showered him with flowers and jubilation.

Recognizing Bappa's extraordinary leadership, the aging Raja Mori gathered his council. "Councillors, of my free will, I appoint Bappa Rawal as my successor," he announced.

At an auspicious hour, Bappa Rawal was crowned King of Chittor amid grand celebrations. Shortly after his coronation, Bappa summoned all the chieftains of the neighboring Rajput kingdoms. "Friends, if only we were united, no one from across the borders would dare to attack us," Bappa urged passionately.

Moved by his vision, the chieftains agreed unanimously. "We look to you for guidance!" they declared. "We willingly accept you as our King. Rajasthan shall be one united kingdom!"

Though most rulers submitted peacefully, Isphagul, the ruler of Bandar, resisted. Bappa led his army and defeated him swiftly. "Let this be a lesson to anyone who stands in the way of Rajput unity!" Bappa proclaimed.

Returning home, Bappa bowed before his mother. "Mother, the states of Rajasthan have merged to form one united whole."

"His promise fulfilled, Bappa returned to his mother," she wept with joy. "Bappa is their chosen king, and I am proud to be your mother."

Under King Bappa Rawal's visionary leadership, the united Rajput forces launched bold campaigns abroad, expanding their influence across Ispahan, Kandahar, Iran, Turan, and Khorasan, bringing vast territories under Indian rule.

After ruling Chittor for many prosperous years, ensuring his subjects lived in peace and contentment, Bappa Rawal handed the throne to his son. Retiring to the serene tranquility of the remote Himalayas, the legendary warrior-king lived to the ripe old age of one hundred, leaving behind a legacy of unity, courage, and honor that endured for centuries.

docs.title: Ananda Math: The Children of Mother India
docs.text:
In eighteenth-century Bengal, under the oppressive shadow of British rule and a puppet ruler, a devastating famine swept across the countryside. The land was drained of its riches, fields lay barren, and millions perished of starvation.

In the once-flourishing village of Padachihno, only three people remained in the desolate estate of the local zamindar, Mahendra—he himself, his devoted wife Kalyani, and their infant daughter. Starvation stalked their home, and money could no longer buy grain where none existed.

"Kalyani, this is the end!" Mahendra mourned, looking at his starving family. "Our money is no good; there is no food to buy anywhere."

"Can't we go to the city?" Kalyani pleaded. "Please! I can't bear to see our child suffer any longer."

"If we leave, dacoits will seize our property," Mahendra hesitated.

"If they come now, can you stop them?" Kalyani asked softly. "Please, let us leave."

Yielding to her counsel, Mahendra armed himself with a musket, and the family set out on foot. As a precaution against dishonor in those lawless times, Kalyani secretly carried a small vial of poison. After walking for miles under the scorching sun, they rested near a deserted forest hut. Leaving Kalyani and the baby inside, Mahendra ventured into the woods to search for wild fruits and water.

Scarcely had he gone when a band of famished, desperate men surrounded the hut. "Help!" Kalyani screamed as the marauders dragged her away, intending to sell her to the local garrison.

Seizing an opportunity in the jungle, Kalyani broke free and fled through the thickets, clutching her baby to her chest. But the child's frightened wail alerted her pursuers. "She is running away! Catch her!" the men shouted.

Exhausted and terrified, Kalyani ran blindly until her strength failed. "Lord Krishna! Save me!" she gasped, collapsing unconscious at the feet of a tall, white-bearded ascetic.

The saint was Satyananda, the leader of a secret monastic order dedicated to the liberation of the motherland. He lifted Kalyani and her child, carried them to safety, and dispatched his trusted disciple, Bhavananda, to search for Mahendra.

Meanwhile, Mahendra had been captured by British soldiers guarding a treasure cart. Bhavananda located the convoy and deliberately allowed himself to be captured as a harmless sanyasi. Once bound on the cart beside Mahendra, Bhavananda whispered, "There's no time for questions! Put your wrists against the iron wheel; it will cut the rope."

Before the guards noticed, another disciple, Jivananda, led a sudden ambush of yellow-robed monks. Chanting "Hari! Hari!", the monks scattered the guards and liberated the captives.

As Bhavananda guided Mahendra through the dense forest, Mahendra asked in bewilderment, "Who are you? You attacked the British to save me, yet you take no loot."

In response, Bhavananda closed his eyes and began to sing in a rich, soul-stirring voice: "Vande Mataram! Sujalam sufalam, malayaja shitalam..."

"You sing of a Mother," Mahendra observed, moved by the melody. "Who is she?"

"She is our land—our Mother India!" Bhavananda declared passionately. "We are her children, the Santaans. We have taken a sacred vow to free her from tyranny, renouncing our wealth, families, and lives."

Bhavananda led Mahendra to Ananda Math, the secret forest abbey. Deep within subterranean caverns, Satyananda showed Mahendra majestic shrines depicting the Motherland—first as she once was, glorious and rich; then as Kali, dark and stripped of all her jewels; and finally as Durga, radiant and victorious in the future.

There, Mahendra was overjoyed to find Kalyani and his daughter safe. But Kalyani, believing that family ties would hinder her husband from serving the noble cause of the Motherland, made a supreme sacrifice. That night, she drank the poison she had carried.

"Mahendra," Kalyani whispered as life ebbed away, "join the Santaans... serve the Mother..."

Grief-stricken but inspired by her sacrifice, Mahendra took the sacred oath before Guru Satyananda. Their baby daughter was entrusted to Jivananda, who placed her with his sister in a distant village to be raised in safety.

Satyananda assigned Mahendra a critical mission: "Swords and spears are ineffectual against British rifles. Convert your ancestral mansion at Padachihno into a secret fort and armory to manufacture guns and cannons."

Meanwhile, Jivananda's wife, Shanti, refused to remain behind. Disguising herself as a young male monk under the name Navinananda, she sought initiation at Ananda Math. When Satyananda tested her resolve, she effortlessly strung a massive, heavy battle bow. Impressed by her strength and spirit, the Guru accepted her into the order.

A year passed. Under Mahendra's supervision, hidden foundries at Padachihno forged seventeen heavy cannons and thousands of muskets. The ranks of the Santaans swelled to ten thousand brave warriors.

Hearing of the rebellion, the British Commander, Captain Thomas, marched a large force equipped with heavy artillery to crush the insurgents. Cannon shells rained upon the Santaan positions, creating havoc.

Seeing his comrades falter, Bhavananda mounted his horse and charged directly into the line of fire. "Let us die so that others may live! Vande Mataram!" he roared, leading two thousand warriors against the enemy guns. In a fierce melee, Bhavananda captured Captain Thomas, but British cannon fire continued to devastate the Santaan ranks on the river bridge.

Just as the battle seemed lost, cannons boomed from the forest ridge. Mahendra had arrived with the seventeen newly forged cannons of Padachihno!

"They are our cannons!" Mahendra shouted, pointing toward the enemy lines. "Santaans, charge!"

A crushing volley of cannon fire decimated the British ranks. The Santaans swept down the ridge, routing the enemy completely. In the final moments of victory, Bhavananda was struck by a musket ball. As his comrades gathered around him, he smiled weakly, whispering, "I die for my Mother... Vande Mataram!" before taking his last breath.

After the battle, Satyananda summoned Mahendra to his sanctuary. "Mahendra, your wife Kalyani did not die that night," the Guru revealed gently. "The poison was neutralized by our healing herbs, and she was brought back to life."

Shanti and Jivananda brought Kalyani and her child back to Mahendra's arms. Though the struggle for freedom was far from over and the British maintained their stronghold in Calcutta, the sacred spark of independence had been ignited. Across Bengal, the heroic sacrifice of the Santaans echoed in every heart, carried by the immortal anthem: Vande Mataram!

docs.title: Devi Choudhurani: The Bandit Queen of Bengal
docs.text:
In Durgapur, a quiet village in North Bengal, lived a poor Brahman widow and her exceptionally beautiful daughter, Prafulla. The fame of Prafulla's beauty reached Haraballabh, the wealthy but arrogant Zamindar of Bhuthnath, who selected her as a bride for his son, Brajeshwar.

Though Prafulla's mother spent all her meager savings to host a fitting wedding feast, envious neighbors spread vile rumors questioning the widow's character. Enraged by the village gossip, Haraballabh declared the marriage void of honor and ordered his son to drive Prafulla out of the house. Bound by strict filial obedience, Brajeshwar reluctantly sent Prafulla back to her mother.

For two long years, mother and daughter lived in dire poverty, spinning cotton thread to earn a pittance. Unable to watch her mother starve, Prafulla decided to seek maintenance from her in-laws. Her mother-in-law received her with sympathy, but Haraballabh remained heartless. "She cannot stay in my house!" he bellowed. "Braja, drive her out!"

Before Prafulla left, Brajeshwar met her secretly in an inner chamber. "Forgive me, Prafulla," he wept, placing his personal engraved diamond ring into her palm. "I am helpless before my father. Take this ring—sell it and live on the money until I am able to support you."

"Your name is engraved upon it," Prafulla replied softly, pressing the ring to her heart. "I will never part with it, even if I starve."

Returning to Durgapur, Prafulla arrived just in time to receive her mother's dying blessing. Left completely alone, her ordeal worsened when four thugs, hired by a lustful local zamindar, broke into her cottage at midnight and abducted her in a palanquin.

As the palanquin was carried through the dense forest, a band of forest dacoits attacked the abductors. The terrified thugs dropped the palanquin and fled for their lives. Seizing her chance, Prafulla slipped into the dark woods and stumbled upon the crumbling ruins of an ancient mansion.

Inside the ruins lay an emaciated, dying old man. Hearing her footsteps, he gasped, "Water... please bring me water..." Prafulla fetched water from a nearby stream and cradled his head as he drank.

Grateful for her kindness, the dying man whispered, "I have no heirs. Near that window, buried beneath the earth, lies an ewer filled with gold coins. Take it... it is yours." With those words, he breathed his last.

Prafulla dug up the pitcher of gold, buried the old man honorably, and set out toward the forest trail to buy provisions. There she encountered Bhavani Pathak, a legendary Robin Hood-like leader of a forest band who robbed corrupt British tax collectors and tyrannical landlords to feed the poor.

Noticing the gold coin in Prafulla's hand, Bhavani Pathak recognized her intelligence, courage, and desperate situation. "You possess beauty, wealth, and courage," he told her. "If you submit to my discipline for five years without touching this gold, I will train you to rule."

Prafulla agreed. For five years under the guidance of Bhavani Pathak and her instructress Nishi, Prafulla underwent rigorous discipline. She studied Kalidasa's literature, mastered the philosophy of the Bhagavad Gita, learned administration, and trained in unarmed combat and wrestling.

At the end of five years, transformed into a wise, fearless, and refined leader, Prafulla assumed command of the organization under the formidable title of Devi Choudhurani. Moving along Bengal's riverways in a regal golden barge, her forces struck terror into oppressive landlords and corrupt officials, redistributing wealth to the needy while strictly prohibiting unnecessary bloodshed.

Meanwhile, back at Bhuthnath, Haraballabh faced financial ruin. Dacoits had repeatedly looted his revenue collections, leaving him with tax arrears of fifty thousand rupees to the British government. Facing imminent arrest, Haraballabh sent Brajeshwar to borrow the sum from his second father-in-law, the wealthy father of Brajeshwar's second wife, Sagar.

However, Sagar's father insulted Haraballabh's reputation. Outraged, Brajeshwar severed ties with the family and stormed out. Sagar ran after her husband, begging to accompany him, but Brajeshwar rebuffed her harshly.

Observing this from the shadows, Devi Choudhurani secretly met Sagar. Recognizing Prafulla despite her regal attire, Sagar embraced her lost co-wife. Together, they devised a clever plan.

That night on the Trisrota River, Devi Choudhurani's men intercepted Brajeshwar's boat. Without firing a shot, they bound the boatmen and escorted Brajeshwar onto Devi's luxurious barge, ushering him before a veiled lady seated on a velvet divan.

"You are my slave now," the veiled lady said in a disguised voice. "Tend my feet!"

Disgusted yet polite, Brajeshwar knelt to touch her feet. The moment his hands touched her ankles, the lady threw off her veil with a triumphant laugh—it was Sagar, fulfilling her vow to make her proud husband tend her feet!

Sagar then brought Brajeshwar into an adjoining cabin where Devi Choudhurani sat. Bowing politely, Devi offered him fifty thousand rupees in gold. "Take this and pay off your father's debt," she said gently.

Brajeshwar hesitated. "I need the money, but I can only accept it as a loan to be repaid on the full moon of Baisakh."

"As you wish," Devi smiled. Slipping off a diamond ring, she pressed it into his hand. "Take this token."

Brajeshwar looked closely at the ring—it was his own engraved diamond ring! "Prafulla!" he gasped, looking up in awe. "You are Devi Choudhurani!"

Returning home, Brajeshwar handed the money to his father, explaining it was a loan from Devi Choudhurani due in Baisakh. But Haraballabh's greed overcame his honor. Repay a dacoit? he thought wickedly. Nonsense! I shall inform the British Collector and claim the bounty on her head!

When the month of Baisakh arrived, Haraballabh led British Lieutenant Brennan and five hundred soldiers to ambush Devi's barge on the river. Unaware of his father's treachery, Brajeshwar arrived on the barge to repay the loan.

Suddenly, British gunboats surrounded the barge. Realizing that Haraballabh had betrayed her, Devi acted swiftly to prevent a bloodbath. She ordered her deputy Rangaraj to raise a white flag and invite Lieutenant Brennan aboard.

When Lieutenant Brennan stepped into the cabin, Nishi and her sister, dressed in royal silk, deliberately confused the officer by claiming to be Devi Choudhurani. Moments later, Prafulla stepped forward calmly. "They are lying to protect me. I am Devi Choudhurani."

Just as Devi had anticipated from the skies, a sudden tropical gale lashed the river. Before the British soldiers could react to the tempest, Devi's master boatmen steered the heavy barge straight through the line of British gunboats, escaping into the open waters with Lieutenant Brennan and Haraballabh trapped aboard!

Inside the cabin, Nishi sternly announced to the terrified captives that they were sentenced to death. Haraballabh fell to his knees, weeping and begging for mercy.

Devi stepped in with a smile. She granted Lieutenant Brennan his freedom on the condition that he never wage war against her people again. Turning to her father-in-law, she said softly, "Go home, father. Prepare a fitting reception for Brajeshwar and his bride."

The following morning, as the storm cleared, Devi Choudhurani handed her remaining wealth to Rangaraj, instructing him to continue aiding the poor. Stripping off her royal robes, Prafulla stepped back into the world as a humble, devoted wife.

Haraballabh received Prafulla into his home with profound gratitude and respect, while Bhavani Pathak, satisfied that his noble queen had restored justice and found peace, voluntarily surrendered to the authorities.

docs.title: Kapala Kundala: The Maiden of the Wilderness
docs.text:
During the reign of Emperor Akbar, a pilgrim boat returning from Gangasagar at the mouth of the Hooghly River was engulfed by a dense, disorienting fog. The vessel lost its course and was forced to anchor along a desolate, wild stretch of coastline surrounded by dense jungles.

Anxious to cook a meal, the pilgrims disembarked. A brave young Brahman named Navakumar Sharma volunteered to venture into the dark forest to gather firewood. Armed with an axe, Navakumar cut a heavy bundle of wood, but the task took longer than expected.

Meanwhile, high tide rushed in, whipping up dangerous waves. Fearing their boat would be smashed against the rocks, the panic-stricken pilgrims set sail, abandoning Navakumar on the wild shore. Returning at dusk, Navakumar found the beach deserted. They have left me, he realized with a heavy heart. I must find shelter for the night before wild beasts attack.

Deep in the woods, he spotted a fire flickering through the trees. Approaching cautiously, he saw a terrifying, long-haired Kapalik—a Tantric ascetic of the dark arts—meditating before a fire surrounded by human skulls.

Navakumar bowed respectfully. "I am a Brahman. I have lost my way and need food and shelter."

Without a word, the Kapalik led Navakumar to his leaf hut, offered him wild fruits, and instructed sternly, "Sleep here, but do not leave the cottage until I return."

The next day, Navakumar lost his way while searching for fresh water in the wilderness. Suddenly, a breathtakingly beautiful maiden with long, flowing hair appeared on the sandy beach. She was Kapala Kundala, an innocent girl brought up by the Kapalik in total isolation from human society.

"Have you lost your way, young man?" she asked gently. "Follow me." She led Navakumar back to the cottage and vanished into the jungle before he could speak.

The following morning, the Kapalik returned and motioned to Navakumar. "I will show you the path out of the forest. Follow me."

As Navakumar walked behind the ascetic, a soft voice whispered from behind a banyan tree. It was Kapala Kundala. "Don't go with him!" she warned urgently. "He is leading you to the altar to sacrifice you to Goddess Kali!"

Hearing the whisper, the Kapalik whirled around. "Kapala Kundala!" he bellowed. As the girl fled like a frightened deer, the Kapalik seized Navakumar in a iron grip. "You cannot free yourself from me!" he snarled, dragging the struggling youth to his sacrificial altar.

Binding Navakumar tightly with heavy ropes beside the sacrificial fire, the Kapalik searched his belt for his ceremonial scimitar, only to realize he had dropped it during the struggle. As the ascetic stepped into the shadows to look for the blade, Kapala Kundala darted out of the thickets carrying the scimitar.

"Shh! Don't make a sound," she whispered, swiftly cutting Navakumar's bonds. "Follow me!"

She led Navakumar to a secluded temple in the forest and explained the situation to the elderly priest. "If she remains here, the Kapalik will slaughter her in rage," the priest told Navakumar. "You must marry her and take her with you to safety."

Grateful for her courage and touched by her purity, Navakumar readily agreed. Before the image of the Goddess, the priest solemnized their marriage.

The next day, the priest escorted the newlyweds to the nearest town, hiring a palanquin for Kapala Kundala's journey to Saptagram. Along the way, Navakumar stumbled upon a broken palanquin where a rich, veiled lady lay bound with ropes. Navakumar untied her.

"Dacoits attacked my entourage and stole my belongings," the lady explained. Navakumar escorted her to the local inn, where his wife was resting.

At the inn, the lady introduced herself as Mati Bibi and presented valuable golden ornaments to Kapala Kundala. In truth, Mati Bibi was Padmavati, Navakumar's first wife! Years earlier, when her father converted to Islam, Navakumar's orthodox family had cast her out. She had eventually risen in the Mughal court at Agra as Lutf-unnisa, a favored courtier of Prince Salim.

Arriving at Saptagram, Navakumar presented his bride to his mother and sister, who lovingly renamed her Mrinmayee. Though accustomed to the wild freedom of the forest, Mrinmayee gradually embraced her new domestic life, bound by the warmth of her husband's family.

Meanwhile, unable to forget Navakumar, Mati Bibi abandoned the court of Agra and traveled to Saptagram. Disguising her identity, she approached Navakumar, offering her vast wealth if he would accept her as his wife or even as a maid.

"That cannot be," Navakumar replied firmly. "I am already married."

When Mati Bibi reacted with a sharp, familiar gesture of anger, a long-buried memory flashed in Navakumar's mind. "Padmavati!" he gasped. "It is you!" Realizing he would not break his vows, Padmavati left in bitter frustration.

Plotting revenge, Padmavati sought out the vengeful Kapalik, who was still hunting for Kapala Kundala. Together, they hatched a sinister plot. Disguising herself as a young man, Padmavati approached Mrinmayee in secret, convincing her to meet in the forest at midnight under the pretext of gathering a rare medicinal herb for her sister-in-law.

Mati Bibi led Mrinmayee to the clearing where the Kapalik meditated. Noticing his wife sneaking out at night, a suspicious Navakumar followed her into the woods.

The Kapalik intercepted Navakumar in the shadows, pointing toward Mrinmayee standing beside the disguised Mati Bibi. "Look for yourself!" the Kapalik whispered poisonously into Navakumar's ear. "She meets a young man secretly! She has displeased the Goddess by saving you, and now she is unfaithful. You can atone by sacrificing her to Kali yourself!"

Overcome by jealousy and deceit, Navakumar confronted Mrinmayee and forced her toward the sacrificial cliff. Hurt by his lack of trust, Mrinmayee refused to defend herself. "I'd rather die than be suspected by you," she said quietly.

As they reached the edge of the sacrificial fire, the sight of the dark altar broke the spell over Navakumar's mind. He looked at the sinister Kapalik, then at his gentle wife, and realized the monstrous truth.

"Mrinmayee!" Navakumar cried out in anguish. "Tell me the truth! Tell me it was an illusion!"

"It was!" Mrinmayee explained gently. "The person I met was not a man, but a woman in disguise who warned me of the Kapalik's trap."

Navakumar fell to his knees in tears. "Forgive me, Mrinmayee! I am not worthy of you."

Humbled and reunited in deep trust, Navakumar and Mrinmayee turned their backs on the dark forest and returned home together, leaving the evil Kapalik and Padmavati behind in the shadows of their own undoing.

docs.title: Bhagat Singh: The Cheerful Young Martyr
docs.text:
On September 27, 1907, in the quiet village of Banga near Lahore, a baby boy was born into the household of Sardar Kishan Singh. The family was steeped in patriotism—Kishan Singh and his younger brother Swaran Singh were in British custody for political agitation, while another brother, Ajit Singh, was serving an exile in Mandalay. On the day of the child's birth, news arrived that Ajit Singh had been released. Rejoicing at the auspicious coincidence, his grandmother named the infant Bhagat, meaning "the fortunate one."

Growing up in an atmosphere of patriotic sacrifice, young Bhagat was acutely sensitive to his family's suffering. Watching his aunt weep for her exiled husband, the boy rushed to embrace her. "Auntie, don't weep," he promised earnestly. "When I grow up, I will drive the British out and bring Uncle home."

"The British are very strong, child," his mother, Vidyavati, cautioned. "They have guns and cannons."

"Don't worry," the boy replied with conviction. "I too will get guns."

One evening, Kishan Singh strolled through his fields with a friend and found young Bhagat digging small holes in the soil, carefully dropping pebbles into them. "What are you planting, Bhagat?" his father asked in amusement.

"Rifles!" the child answered proudly. "To free my country."

In 1916, Bhagat entered the D.A.V. High School in Lahore. He was deeply drawn to the memory of Shaheed Kartar Singh Sarabha, a revolutionary who had walked to the gallows at the age of twenty. Carrying Sarabha's photograph in his pocket, Bhagat would often tell his classmates, "He is my hero."

In 1919, when Bhagat was twelve years old, the British government passed the tyrannical Rowlatt Act, authorizing arrest and imprisonment without trial. Protests erupted nationwide, culminating in the horrific Jallianwalla Bagh massacre in Amritsar, where General Dyer ordered troops to fire upon a peaceful, unarmed crowd.

Shocked by the news, young Bhagat took a train to Amritsar. Standing amidst the bullet-ridden walls of Jallianwalla Bagh, he filled a small glass vial with the blood-soaked earth. Returning home, he placed the jar near his bed, vowing to dedicate his life to avenging the martyrs.

In the early 1920s, Bhagat joined the National College in Lahore, founded by Lala Lajpat Rai. There he befriended fellow patriots Sukhdev and Bhagwati Charan Vohra. Together, they staged nationalist plays such as Rana Pratap to awaken the illiterate masses.

When Bhagat turned sixteen, his family pressed him to marry. Unwilling to bind himself to domestic life, Bhagat left a letter for his father: "My life has been dedicated to the noblest cause, that of the freedom of the country. Therefore, there is no rest or worldly desire that can lure me now."

He fled to Kanpur, meeting the great journalist-patriot Ganesh Shankar Vidyarthi. Working at Vidyarthi's nationalist press, Pratap, Bhagat joined the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA). He threw himself into revolutionary work, distributing anti-British leaflets at public fairs and outwitting police chasers with daring agility.

Returning to Lahore when his grandmother fell ill, Bhagat founded the Naujawan Bharat Sabha in 1926 to unite youth across communal lines. In September 1928, he met Chandra Shekhar Azad in Delhi to reorganize the movement into the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA).

Events reached a climax on October 30, 1928, when the Simon Commission arrived in Lahore. A massive, peaceful demonstration led by Lala Lajpat Rai marched toward the railway station, chanting "Simon, Go Back!"

A heavy police force under Superintendent James A. Scott blocked the marchers and ordered a brutal lathi charge. Scott himself struck Lala Lajpat Rai repeatedly on the head and chest. Standing battered before the crowd, Lalaji declared in a booming voice: "I declare that every blow hurled at us today will prove a nail in the coffin of the British Empire!"

Eighteen days later, Lalaji succumbed to his injuries. Outraged by the insult to the nation, the HSRA resolved to avenge his death. On December 17, 1928, Bhagat Singh and Rajguru took position outside the Lahore Police Station. When an officer mounted a motorcycle, believing him to be Scott, Rajguru and Bhagat fired a fatal volley of bullets. Only later did they learn they had killed Assistant Superintendent J.P. Saunders.

With the entire Punjab police force on high alert, Bhagat executed a masterly escape. He shaved off his beard and hair, donned an elegant suit and felt hat, and boarded a first-class train compartment with Durga Bhabhi (wife of revolutionary Bhagwati Charan Vohra) posing as his wife, while Rajguru dressed as their servant. They passed through heavy police cordons undetected.

In early 1929, the British government sought to push the draconian Trade Disputes Bill and Public Safety Bill through the Central Legislative Assembly in Delhi. To protest these anti-labor measures, the HSRA decided to strike a dramatic blow—not to kill, but to awaken the nation.

On April 8, 1929, as the Assembly met in Delhi, Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt sat in the visitors' gallery. As the President stood up to pass the bill, Bhagat tossed two low-intensity noise bombs into an empty area on the floor. Smoke filled the chamber as leaflets rained down from the gallery, carrying the opening words: "It takes a loud voice to make the deaf hear."

Instead of fleeing, the two young men stood their ground, shouting "Inquilab Zindabad!" (Long Live the Revolution!) and voluntarily offered their wrists to the arresting officers, intending to use the court trial to spread their message nationwide.

During the trial, lawyer Asaf Ali read out Bhagat Singh's ringing statement: "We dropped the bomb to register our protest on behalf of those who had no other means left to give expression to their heart-rending agony... Our sole purpose was to make the deaf hear."

Sentenced to life imprisonment, Bhagat was transferred to Lahore Central Jail to face trial for the Saunders murder. In prison, discovering that Indian political prisoners were subjected to filthy food and brutal treatment compared to European convicts, Bhagat launched a historic hunger strike.

"Outside the jail we fight for freedom," Bhagat told his fellow inmates. "Inside the jail we must fight for our honor."

For sixty-three days, the prisoners refused food. When his close comrade Jatin Das died a martyr during the strike, public outrage forced the British authorities to concede jail reforms.

When his father filed a court petition arguing that Bhagat was innocent and absent from Lahore during Saunders' death, Bhagat wrote a stern letter to his father: "My life is not so precious as you may think it to be... You have proven weak at a critical moment. I want you to know that your conduct has pained me deeply."

On October 7, 1930, the Special Tribunal sentenced Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru to death by hanging. When a sympathetic state advocate suggested filing a mercy petition, Bhagat smiled calmly. "There is no need. It is better to die bravely than to cringe before the enemy. I am the moth of the flame of liberty."

On the evening of March 23, 1931, the jailor entered Bhagat's cell ahead of schedule. Bhagat was engrossed in a biography of Lenin. "Sardarji, we have come to lead you to the gallows," the official said quietly.

"Wait a moment," Bhagat smiled, turning a page. "Let one revolutionary meet another."

Moments later, Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru walked hand-in-hand to the gallows, singing patriotic songs that echoed through the dark corridors of Lahore Jail. Standing on the trapdoor, Bhagat called out to the British Deputy Commissioner: "Well, Mr. Magistrate, you are lucky today. You shall see how Indian revolutionaries embrace death with pleasure for the sake of their supreme ideal!"

As the hangman pulled the lever, the three young martyrs embraced death with smiles on their lips. Bhagat Singh was just twenty-three years old, but his sacrifice ignited a fire across India that ultimate brought the British Raj to its knees.

docs.title: Bhanumati: Granddaughter of Krishna
docs.text:
When Pradyumna, the valiant son of Krishna and Rukmini, returned to Dwaraka after defeating the formidable asura Vajranabha, he brought with him his new bride, Princess Prabhavati. The royal city celebrated with grand festivities, and King Ugrasena organized a sacred sacrifice at Mount Raivataka to invoke divine blessings upon the newly wedded couple.

Early the next morning, Krishna set out for Raivataka accompanied by his family, kinsmen, and retinue. While the elders gathered at the sacrificial grounds, young Princess Bhanumati—the lively granddaughter of Krishna and daughter of Bhanu—stole away into the nearby woods with her playmates.

As Bhanumati ran playfully through the dense foliage, she bumped blindly into a meditating ascetic, knocking over his holy water pot. The hermit was none other than Sage Durvasa, notorious throughout the three worlds for his fiery temper.

"You ill-mannered child!" Durvasa roared in fury. "How dare you interrupt my meditation? For this insolence, you shall be abducted by a demon—the traditional foe of your family!"

Sage Narada, who happened to be passing by, was shocked by the harshness of the curse. "O Durvasa, how can a wise soul like you curse an innocent child for a minor accident?" Narada chided gently.

Regret filled Durvasa's heart, but the words could not be recalled. "A curse once uttered cannot be revoked," Durvasa admitted sorrowfully. "However, I bless her that she shall remain unsullied through her ordeal, marry a virtuous hero, and live as a happy housewife."

Frightened by the encounter, Bhanumati and her companion hurried back to the sacrificial enclosure, resolving to keep the incident secret. Let us not breathe a word of this to anyone, Bhanumati thought. Besides, what the sage said cannot come to pass. Dwaraka, guarded by my grandfather Krishna and my uncles, is impregnable.

Years rolled by, and Bhanumati grew into a maiden of extraordinary beauty and charm. Her father, Bhanu, planned to host a grand Swayamvara for her upon returning from a pleasure cruise along the coast with Krishna and the Yadava warriors.

Meanwhile, in the demon stronghold of Shatpura, Nikumbha—the brother of the slain Vajranabha—sat brooding in hatred. His spies brought him golden news: "Lord! Krishna and the Yadava warriors have left Dwaraka on a pleasure cruise. Only the aged Ugrasena and Vasudeva remain behind with the young maidens!"

Seeing his opportunity to avenge his brother's death, Nikumbha flew to Dwaraka under cover of darkness. In the secluded royal gardens, as Bhanumati gathered flowers with her maidens, Nikumbha swooped down, seized the princess, and cast a spell of invisibility over them both.

Hearing the maidens' frantic screams, Ugrasena and Vasudeva rushed out with drawn swords, but the abductor had already vanished into thin air.

Word was rushed to Krishna's pleasure barge. Incensed by the outrage, Krishna, Arjuna—who was visiting Dwaraka—and Pradyumna immediately mounted Garuda and set off in fierce pursuit toward Shatpura.

Once far beyond Dwaraka's borders, Nikumbha rendered himself visible again. Bhanumati, recovering from her faint, glared at the demon. "Coward!" she spat. "Do you think you will escape the wrath of my father, my uncles, and my grandfather?"

"Once we reach Shatpura, it is their lives they will have to beg for!" Nikumbha sneered. But his boast turned to dread as a massive shadow covered the sky. Looking up, he saw Garuda soaring above, bearing Krishna and Arjuna, with Pradyumna following swiftly in his flying chariot.

Panic-stricken, Nikumbha held Bhanumati in front of his chest as a human shield. Seeing the demon's ploy, Krishna and Pradyumna held back their maces, but Arjuna drew his bow. With unmatched precision, Arjuna loosed a stream of arrows that pierced Nikumbha's arms and shoulders without brushing a single hair of Bhanumati.

Bhanumati fainted from terror as Nikumbha used dark sorcery to vanish once more. But Pradyumna invoked a mystic spell taught to him by his wife Mayavati, exposing the invisible demon to plain sight.

Desperate to escape, Nikumbha transformed into a monstrous yellow vulture and took flight toward Mount Gokarna, carrying Bhanumati in his sharp talons. Arjuna relentlessly rained arrows upon the bird until, mortally weakened, the vulture dropped down toward the river below.

Pradyumna swooped down in his chariot, caught the falling princess safely, and bore her away toward Dwaraka, leaving Krishna and Arjuna to finish the demon.

Cornered at the narrow entrance of a cave near Shatpura, Nikumbha unleashed his final illusion, splitting his body into thousands of identical demon forms that swarmed the sky. Unfazed by the trick, Krishna, Arjuna, and Pradyumna invoked their own divine powers, multiplying themselves into thousands of forms to engage every duplicate demon.

Using his divine vision, Krishna pierced through the illusion and identified the true Nikumbha. Raising his right hand, Krishna summoned the glowing Sudarshana Chakra and launched it through the air. The sacred discus severed the demon's head from his body, ending Nikumbha's evil reign forever.

The heroes returned to Dwaraka in triumph. However, Bhanu met them at the palace gates in tears. "The demon is slain, but my daughter was abducted! Her reputation is tarnished—who will marry her now?"

Just then, Sage Narada appeared before the royal assembly. "Do not lament, Bhanu," Narada smiled. "Durvasa's curse has run its course, but his blessing remains. Send an emissary to bring the Pandava prince Sahadeva."

When Sahadeva arrived in Dwaraka, he was wedded to Bhanumati in a grand celestial ceremony. Durvasa's ancient curse yielded its ultimate blessing, as Bhanumati departed for her new home as the cherished queen of the heroic youngest Pandava.

docs.title: Bheema and Hanuman: The Meeting of the Sons of the Wind
docs.text:
During the twelve years of exile in the forest, the five Pandava brothers and their wife Draupadi lived in the deep wilderness accompanied by their preceptor, Sage Dhoumya. When Arjuna, the third Pandava, departed for Mount Indrakeela to perform severe penance and obtain celestial weapons from the Devas, five years passed without his return. The Pandavas missed him deeply, and the forest felt empty without his presence.

One morning, the revered Sage Lomasha arrived at their encampment bearing welcome news. "Arjuna's mission has been successful!" Lomasha announced. "The Devas require his presence a little longer, but he has asked me to take you on a pilgrimage to holy shrines, across Mount Gandhamadana, to Kubera's celestial gardens. He will join you there."

Joyfully, the Pandavas and Draupadi set out with the sages. As they climbed the steep, narrow trails of Mount Gandhamadana, a violent dust storm broke out, followed by torrential rain. Bheema, the strongest of the Pandavas, protected Draupadi, holding her close beneath a shelter of trees until the tempest abated.

However, the strenuous ascent took a heavy toll on Draupadi. As the sun came out, her knees buckled and she fainted from exhaustion on the rocky path. Nakula ran forward to support her, while Yudhisthira wept in distress. "She was born a princess, yet hardship and sorrow have been her lot," Yudhisthira mourned. "Bheema, how will she cross the snow-bound peaks ahead?"

Bheema's face lit up with an idea. "Brother, do not despair! I shall summon my son, Ghatotkacha."

Closing his eyes, Bheema focused his thoughts on his demon son, born of his union with the demoness Hidimba. In a distant forest, Ghatotkacha instantly felt his father's call. Within moments, Ghatotkacha and his Rakshasa companions materialized before the Pandavas, bowing reverently.

"Father, command me!" Ghatotkacha offered.

"Carry Draupadi safely to the mountain top," Bheema instructed. "Fly low so she feels no unease."

Ghatotkacha and his strong followers lifted Draupadi and the Pandavas into the air, flying swiftly above the clouds until they alighted at the sacred hermitage of Badrikashrama on the mountain summit, where the ancient sages Nara and Narayana had meditated. There, the inhabitants received the Pandavas with high honor.

Some days later, as Bheema and Draupadi strolled through the charming woods near the hermitage, a gentle mountain breeze wafted an exquisite flower to Draupadi's feet. It was a golden Saugandhika lotus, possessing an intoxicating, celestial fragrance.

Picking up the lotus in delight, Draupadi showed it to Bheema. "Look at this exquisite flower! What a sweet fragrance it has! I will give this one to Yudhisthira, but oh Bheema, please get me some more to take back with us!"

"I would go to the ends of the earth to fulfill your wish, Draupadi," Bheema replied warmly.

Grasping his heavy golden mace and bow, Bheema marched deeper into the uncharted forests of Gandhamadana, following the direction of the scented breeze. As he strode through the jungle, a rogue wild elephant charged at him with a deafening roar. Undaunted, Bheema seized the beast by its tusks, wrestled it to the ground, and slew it with a single blow. Lions and tigers fled in terror as the prince forged ahead, clearing a path through the wilderness.

Reaching the foot of a high peak, Bheema discovered a crystal-clear lake bordering a lush banana grove. After taking a refreshing dip, Bheema raised his conch to his lips and blew a blast so powerful that the mountainside reverberated.

Deep within the banana grove sat Hanuman, the immortal monkey god of the Ramayana. Like Bheema, Hanuman was born by the grace of Vayu, the wind-god, making the two mighty heroes spiritual brothers.

Hearing the conch blast, Hanuman smiled in recognition. Only one being on earth can sound a conch so loudly—my brother Bheema! Hanuman thought. Sensing Bheema's approach, Hanuman noticed the prince's proud demeanor. Bheema has always been proud of his strength. For his own good, I must teach him humility.

Hanuman stretched himself across the narrow mountain path, lashing his long tail against the ground and letting out a roar that shook the mountain peaks.

Hearing the deafening sound, Bheema hastened into the grove, only to find an old, frail monkey lying lazily across the trail. "Impudent monkey!" Bheema bellowed. "Get out of my way or I shall thrash you!"

Hanuman looked up with sleepy eyes. "I am old and ill, and you woke me from my sleep. Being a human, you are wiser than I and ought to be more considerate. Who are you?"

"I am Bheema, son of Pandu, born to Kunti by the grace of Vayu!" Bheema declared chest out. "Now move aside!"

"I have no energy to move," Hanuman replied calmly. "If you must pass, simply leap over me."

"Leaping over a living creature is against the code of Dharma," Bheema countered. "If it were not a sin, I would leap right over you and this mountain, just as my brother Hanuman leaped over the ocean to find Rama's wife!"

"Who is this Hanuman?" the old monkey asked innocently.

"He is my handsome, strong, and intelligent brother!" Bheema boasted. "I am as strong as he! Now get up, or I will send you to the abode of Yama!"

Hanuman concealed his smile. "O mighty Bheema, have pity on an old weakling. Just push my tail aside with your hand and go across."

Smirking at the weakling monkey, Bheema reached down with his left hand to flick the tail out of the path. But to his utter shock, the tail did not move an inch.

Frowning, Bheema gripped the tail with both hands and pulled with all his might. Straining every muscle, his face turning red and sweat pouring down his forehead, Bheema heaved again and again. Yet the monkey's tail remained rooted to the earth as firmly as a mountain!

Gasping for breath, Bheema released his grip and fell to his knees, his arrogance shattered. "What is the matter with me?" he whispered in disbelief. "I, Bheema, am unable to move the tail of a mere monkey!"

Realizing he was in the presence of a divine being, Bheema folded his hands reverently. "O mighty one, forgive my harsh words! Pray tell me who you really are."

The old monkey rose, his eyes shining with affection. "I am your brother, Hanuman!"

Overjoyed, Bheema fell prostrate before him. "My brother! I am blessed beyond measure to behold you! Please, show me the colossal form in which you crossed the ocean!"

Hanuman smiled and began to expand his body. Right before Bheema's eyes, Hanuman grew taller and grander until his form towered higher than Mount Gandhamadana itself, radiating a blinding, golden light that dazzled the mountain.

"Enough, brother!" Bheema gasped, shielding his eyes. "The light emanating from your body dazzles me! I feel faint!"

Resuming his normal size, Hanuman embraced Bheema warmly. As their arms clasped, a surge of divine energy flooded Bheema's body, dispelling all fatigue.

"My strength has returned manifold!" Bheema exclaimed. "With your blessings, we are sure to conquer our enemies!"

"I shall do even more," Hanuman promised. "When you charge into battle against the Kauravas, I shall reside upon the flagstaff of Arjuna's chariot, and my battle roar will strike terror into the hearts of your foes!"

Hanuman then pointed to a hidden mountain path. "Follow that trail to the Saugandhika forest and Kubera's gardens, where the lotuses grow." With tears of affection in his eyes, the immortal monkey blessed his brother and vanished into the grove.

Invigorated, Bheema followed the path until he reached the pristine lake in the Saugandhika forest, covered in radiant pink and gold lotuses. As he bent to drink from the waters, a legion of heavy-armored Rakshasa guards, sent by Kubera, blocked his way. "Mortal, this is Lord Kubera's private garden! You cannot take these flowers without his permission!"

"This lake on the mountain belongs to all!" Bheema asserted, plunging into the water. When the guards attacked him with spears and maces, Bheema wielded his mace in a fury, slaying over a hundred Rakshasas and scattering the rest in terror.

The fleeing guards rushed to Kubera's palace. "Lord! Yudhisthira's brother has entered the lake by force!"

To their surprise, Kubera smiled. "I know why Bheema is there. Return to your posts and let him gather as many flowers as he desires."

Meanwhile, Bheema gathered armfuls of the golden Saugandhika lotuses. Filled with joy, he began to frolic in the lake, splashing the waters with such immense power that massive waves and stone-like spray whirled into the sky, shaking the mountain.

At the hermitage, Yudhisthira heard the roaring mountain tempest and grew alarmed. "Brothers, arm yourselves! A great catastrophe may be upon us!"

Only Draupadi smiled serenely. "O King, do not fear. Bheema must have found the celestial lotuses I requested."

Ghatotkacha carried Yudhisthira, Sage Dhoumya, and the Pandava brothers to the lake, where they found Bheema happily wading ashore with a mountain of glowing lotuses. Yudhisthira embraced his brother with relief and gentle reproach. "O Bheema, what have you done? Never commit such a rash act again!"

Bheema smiled warmly and placed the fragrant Saugandhika lotuses into Draupadi's hands. Moments later, Kubera's guards arrived and bowed before Yudhisthira, granting Kubera's permission for the Pandavas to dwell near the holy lake. There the Pandavas rested in peace and prosperity until Arjuna returned in triumph from the heavens.

docs.title: Bheeshma: Selflessness Personified
docs.text:
One afternoon, as King Shantanu of Hastinapura walked along the banks of the river Ganga on a hunting trip, he beheld a woman of otherworldly beauty. Enchanted by her presence, Shantanu approached her. "O divine maiden, whoever you may be, I desire to make you my queen!"

The maiden turned to him calmly. "O King, I shall willingly become your wife, but on one condition: you must never question who I am, nor criticize anything I do, no matter how strange it may seem. The moment you speak an unkind word or question my actions, I shall leave you forever."

Deeply in love, Shantanu agreed. "So be it!"

They were married and lived in happiness. But soon, the Queen began to behave in a terrifying manner. Every time a child was born to her, she carried the newborn to the river bank and drowned it in the flowing waters, whispering softly, "This is for your good."

Horrified by her actions, Shantanu suffered in silence, holding his tongue lest he lose his beloved wife. Seven sons were born, and seven times she drowned them in the river.

When the eighth child was born, Shantanu could restrain himself no longer. As she walked toward the river, he sprang forward. "Stop! Who are you, O wicked woman? How can you murder your own children?"

The Queen paused, her eyes filled with sorrow. "Alas! You have broken your promise. I am the Goddess Ganga. Our first seven children were the Vasus, cursed by a sage to take mortal birth. By drowning them immediately, I freed them from earthly suffering. But this eighth child was destined to live a long life on earth. He shall be called Gangeya."

Holding the infant, Ganga added, "Because you broke your word, I must leave you now. I shall take the child with me, raise him in the heavens, and return him to you when he comes of age." With those words, Goddess Ganga vanished into the river with the baby.

Bereft of his divine wife and son, Shantanu returned to his palace. For years he ruled Hastinapura wisely, but his heart remained heavy with longing.

Sixteen years passed. One day, while hunting along the Ganga, Shantanu noticed that the river's current had become strange and subdued. Walking along the bank, he discovered a handsome youth shooting a barrage of arrows with such astonishing skill that he had constructed a dam across the rushing river!

As Shantanu marveled at the youth's prowess, Goddess Ganga emerged from the waters. "Shantanu, this is Gangeya, the eighth son I bore you," she declared proudly. "He has mastered the Vedas under Sage Vasishta and weaponry under Parashurama. He is known as Devavrata. Take him home and proclaim him your heir."

With great joy, Shantanu brought Devavrata back to Hastinapura and crowned him Prince Regent. Devavrata's nobility, wisdom, and unmatched martial skill quickly won the hearts of the nobles and commoners alike.

Four years later, while walking along the banks of the Yamuna, King Shantanu breathed in a divine fragrance. Following the scent, he met Satyavati, the beautiful daughter of the chief of fishermen. Overcome with desire, Shantanu approached her father, Dasaraja, to ask for her hand in marriage.

The fisherman chief bowed respectfully. "O King, it is an honor. But I will give my daughter to you only on one condition: the son born unto Satyavati must inherit the throne of Hastinapura after you."

Shantanu was dismayed. How can I disinherit Devavrata, my noble and rightful heir? he thought. Unwilling to commit such an injustice, Shantanu turned away and returned to his palace, falling into a deep, silent melancholy.

Noticing his father's declining health and sorrowful demeanor, Devavrata approached him gently. "Father, you possess everything a king could desire. Why are you so sad?"

Shantanu offered vague excuses, but Devavrata questioned the King's old minister and learned the truth about Dasaraja's condition.

Without a moment's hesitation, Devavrata rode to the fisherman's cottage accompanied by the elder statesmen of the realm. "Dasaraja," Devavrata announced, "I renounce my claim to the throne of Hastinapura. I vow before all present that the son born of your daughter Satyavati shall reign as King!"

The fisherman chief bowed, but raised another concern. "O Prince, I have no doubt that you will keep your word. But what of the sons that may be born to you in the future? They may challenge my daughter's children for the kingdom."

Understanding the depth of the sacrifice required to secure his father's happiness, Devavrata raised his hand to the heavens and uttered a terrifying, solemn oath: "Chief of fishermen, from this day forth, I vow to embrace lifelong celibacy! I shall never marry, nor father any children!"

As the words echoed across the river, the heavens opened, and celestial flowers showered upon the prince. Voices of the Devas resounded from the clouds: "Bheeshma! Bheeshma!"—meaning He of the terrible oath!

Bheeshma escorted Satyavati to Hastinapura. When King Shantanu learned of his son's supreme sacrifice, tears welled in his eyes. Embracing Bheeshma, the King granted him a divine boon: Ichha-Mrityu—the power to choose the exact moment of his own death, ensuring that death could never touch him without his own consent.

Shantanu married Satyavati and fathered two sons, Chitrangada and Vichitravirya. After Shantanu's death, Chitrangada was killed in battle, leaving the young Vichitravirya to ascend the throne under Bheeshma's protective guidance.

When Vichitravirya came of age, Bheeshma rode alone to the Kingdom of Kashi, where a Swayamvara was being held for the King's three daughters—Amba, Ambika, and Ambalika. Challenging the assembled suitors, Bheeshma defeated all the rival kings in battle and carried the three princesses to Hastinapura to be brides for Vichitravirya.

As the wedding preparations began, the eldest princess, Amba, approached Bheeshma privately. "In my heart, I had already chosen King Salva as my husband, and he had accepted me," she confessed. "Knowing this, please do what is right."

Honoring her feelings, Bheeshma permitted Amba to depart with dignity to Salva's kingdom. Meanwhile, Vichitravirya was wedded to Ambika and Ambalika.

However, when Amba presented herself before King Salva, he rejected her coldly. "I cannot accept a woman who was won in battle by another man. Return to Bheeshma."

Spurn by Salva and unable to return to Hastinapura, Amba's grief turned into consuming hatred for Bheeshma. "Bheeshma is the cause of all my humiliation!" she vowed. "I shall live only to destroy him!"

Amba sought out Sage Parashurama, Bheeshma's martial guru, who vowed to aid her. Parashurama ordered Bheeshma to marry Amba. When Bheeshma respectfully refused, citing his unbreakable vow of celibacy, Parashurama challenged his former disciple to battle.

A colossal duel ensued between the two mighty warriors, lasting twenty-three days. Weapons of mass destruction shook the earth until Parashurama, unable to overcome Bheeshma's divine defense, conceded defeat.

"I have done my best, child," Parashurama told Amba. "Seek Bheeshma's protection."

"Never!" Amba declared. Retiring to the deep forest, she performed severe penance for twelve years until Lord Shiva appeared before her.

"I seek the power to slay Bheeshma!" Amba demanded.

"You shall do so in your next life," Lord Shiva granted. "You will be reborn as Shikhandin, the child of King Drupada, and bring about Bheeshma's fall."

Satisfied, Amba built a funeral pyre and leaped into the flames, her soul reborn as Shikhandin.

Years passed. Vichitravirya died childless, and through the divine intervention of Sage Vyasa, Ambika gave birth to the blind Dhritarashtra, Ambalika to Pandu, and a maidservant to Vidura. Bheeshma raised the princes with fatherly care, and later supervised the upbringing of their sons—the one hundred Kauravas and the five Pandavas.

When the great Kurukshetra War broke out, Bheeshma was appointed Commander-in-Chief of the Kaurava army. Though his heart lay with the righteous Pandavas, his oath bound him to defend the throne of Hastinapura. Before the battle began, the eldest Pandava, Yudhisthira, approached Bheeshma to seek his blessings.

"May victory be yours, my son," the grand patriarch blessed him warmly. "My body belongs to the King, but my blessings are with righteousness."

For nine days, Bheeshma fought like a lion, annihilating thousands of Pandava troops each day while ensuring that the five Pandava brothers remained unharmed. Seeing their armies decimated, the Pandavas visited Bheeshma's tent at night to ask how he could be defeated.

Bheeshma smiled gently at his grandsons. "I will never strike a woman, or anyone who was once a woman. Place Shikhandin before Arjuna in battle tomorrow."

On the tenth day of war, Arjuna rode into battle with Shikhandin standing at the front of his chariot. Seeing Shikhandin, Bheeshma lowered his bow, refusing to shoot. Seizing the moment, Arjuna loosed hundreds of arrows from behind Shikhandin.

Arrow after arrow pierced the patriarch's body until, at last, Bheeshma fell from his chariot. So closely spaced were the arrows lodged in his flesh that his body did not touch the earth—he lay suspended upon a bed of arrows (Shara-Shayya).

Both armies halted their battle and gathered in reverence around their beloved grandsire. As Bheeshma lay on the arrows, he looked up at the warriors. "My head hangs down. I need a support for my head."

Kaurava princes brought soft silk pillows, but Bheeshma turned them away. Arjuna stepped forward, drew three arrows from his quiver, and shot them into the earth beneath Bheeshma's head, creating a warrior's pillow.

"A fitting pillow for a Kshatriya!" Bheeshma smiled. "Now I am thirsty."

Arjuna shot a divine arrow deep into the earth beside Bheeshma's head. A jet of crystal-clear Ganga water sprang forth, flowing directly into Bheeshma's lips.

Invoking his boon of Ichha-Mrityu, Bheeshma chose to remain on his bed of arrows until the sun entered the auspicious northern solstice (Uttarayana). After the war concluded with the Pandavas' victory, the dying patriarch delivered his final, legendary discourses on statecraft, ethics, and duty (Rajadharma) to King Yudhisthira before peacefully releasing his soul to the heavens.

docs.title: Bikal the Terrible
docs.text:
In a quiet village bordering the dense forests of Madhya Pradesh, a wild tiger frequently prowled near human settlements at night, searching for easy prey.

One dark night, as the tiger lurked outside a wealthy merchant's home, he overheard the merchant scolding his son near an open window. "Are you not afraid of Bikal?" the father warned sternly. "With Bikal around, even the bravest of men think twice before venturing out into the night!"

In the local forest dialect, Bikal referred to any terrifying unknown danger. But the tiger, having never heard the word before, jumped to a terrifying conclusion. Who is this Bikal? the beast pondered nervously, imagining a gargantuan, multi-limbed monster far fiercer than any lion or tiger. Panic seized the predator. I had better hide somewhere for the night!

Spotting a dark cattle shed nearby, the tiger slipped inside and curled up in a corner, trembling at the thought of the monstrous Bikal.

Later that night, three thieves broke into the merchant's house and carried off two massive sacks filled with gold and valuables. Finding the load too heavy to carry on their shoulders, they searched for a transport animal.

"It's pitch-dark in that shed," one thief whispered.

"All the better for us," replied another.

Stepping inside, the thieves stumbled upon the sleeping tiger in the dark. Mistaking the striped beast for a sturdy bullock, they heaved their heavy sacks onto its back, tying them securely with thick ropes and seizing the tiger by its tail.

The tiger awoke from a nightmare of Bikal right as a thief yanked its tail. My worst fears have come true! the tiger panicked. I am in the clutches of the terrible Bikal and his henchmen! No ordinary man would dare pull my tail or load me down like a beast of burden!

Paralyzed by fear of Bikal, the mighty predator meekly allowed the three thieves to lead it by its tail out of the village. As they walked through the night, the thieves chatted merrily about buying goats, acquiring land, and getting married with their loot, while the tiger marched in silent terror under the heavy sacks.

At dawn, near the edge of a forest, the light broke. "Let's divide the loot now," one thief called out.

As they turned to unbind the sacks, the thieves finally saw their 'bullock' in the morning light. "F-friends..." one gasped, pointing with a trembling finger. "It's a... IT'S A TIGER!"

Screaming in horror, the three thieves abandoned the loot and fled through the forest as fast as their legs could carry them.

Standing alone, the tiger stared after the fleeing men. Oh, what a fool I have made of myself! the beast realized, flushed with embarrassment. They were just ordinary men! If the other forest animals hear of this, everyone will laugh at me!

Anxious to shed the embarrassing load, the tiger thrashed about, but the heavy sack wedged itself tightly between two large boulders, trapping the tiger in place.

Shortly after, a young cowherd named Shambhu walked down the forest trail. Seeing the trapped predator, Shambhu turned to run, but the tiger called out imploringly, "O Cowherd! Don't run away! I won't harm you. Somebody tied this heavy sack of money to my back, and it's stuck between these rocks. If you help me untie it, you can take all the gold inside!"

"You promise not to hurt me?" Shambhu asked cautiously.

"I promise," the tiger swore.

Shambhu unknotted the ropes, freed the tiger, and collected the sack of gold coins. Curious, Shambhu asked, "How did a tiger end up tied to a sack of loot?"

"It's all because of that Bikal!" the tiger grumbled testily. "Never utter that name again, do you hear? I hate the sound of it!"

"I-I'll never utter it again," Shambhu assured him, heaving the sack onto his shoulder and rushing home.

Overnight, Shambhu became a wealthy man. He bought cows and buffaloes, built a fine house, and married Bhagwati, the most intelligent and courageous girl in the village.

Months passed in prosperity. One evening, as Shambhu and Bhagwati sat on their porch, Bhagwati asked, "Shambhu, how did you suddenly become so rich?"

"It's a long story, Bhago," Shambhu smiled, relaxing. "It all started with this Bikal..."

Unbeknownst to them, the tiger happened to be strolling past their house at that very moment. Hearing the forbidden word echo from the porch, the tiger's eyes flashed with anger. He broke his promise! He uttered the forbidden word! I won't spare him now!

Late that night, while the couple slept soundly, the tiger crept into the house, lifted the sleeping Shambhu off his cot, and carried him away into the deep forest.

Shambhu woke up with a thud as the tiger dropped him onto the forest floor. "Ow! Where am I?" Shambhu gasped, looking up into the glaring eyes of the beast.

"You broke your promise," the tiger snarled. "So now I am going to eat you!"

Thinking quickly to save his life, Shambhu replied calmly, "Eat me by all means, but wait until morning."

"Why not now?" the tiger demanded.

"My flesh is cold and tough right now," Shambhu explained smoothly. "Under the morning sun, it will become tender and warm. To me it makes no difference whether you eat me now or later, but for you it will be a far tastier meal."

The tiger pondered. "All right, we'll wait until sunrise."

Meanwhile, back at the village, Bhagwati awoke and found her husband missing. Spotting tiger tracks near the cot, she realized what had happened. A tiger has carried him away! I must act quickly!

Grabbing a brass gong, a wooden cudgel, and a metal pot, Bhagwati marched fearlessly into the dark forest.

As the tiger waited for sunrise, a strange, terrifying racket echoed through the trees: THAK-CHHANNAN-CHHANNAN-DHAM! CHOOM-CHOOM-CHOOM!

The tiger leapt up in alarm. "What's that awful sound?"

Shambhu recognized his wife's gong and caught her distant shadow in the bushes. To play upon the tiger's fear, Shambhu shouted at the top of his voice, "YES, IT IS BIKAL! HE HAS COME TO EAT TIGERS!"

Bhagwati heard her husband's clue and immediately joined the act, calling out from behind a tree in a booming voice, "WHO'S THERE? ARE THERE ANY TIGERS AROUND FOR ME TO EAT?"

The tiger trembled violently, its knees shaking. "He's looking for tigers to eat! What should I do?"

"Quick!" Shambhu whispered. "Lie down flat and keep still! I'll tell Bikal you are just a pile of dirty clothes!"

The terrifying predator collapsed flat onto the ground, closing its eyes. Bhagwati stepped into the clearing, brandishing her cudgel. "Have you seen any tigers here that I can eat?"

"No," Shambhu replied loudly. "There are no tigers here—just this pile of dirty clothes."

"I don't believe you!" Bhagwati shouted back. "Hit that pile with a heavy rock to prove it!"

Shambhu picked up a rock. "Friend, I have to hit you gently so Bikal doesn't suspect," he whispered to the tiger.

"Do whatever Bikal says to save us!" the tiger whimpered.

Shambhu struck the tiger with the rock. From the bushes, Bhagwati yelled, "HIT IT HARDER! HARDER!"

Shambhu pelted the tiger again and again with heavy stones until the bruised beast swooned into unconsciousness.

Leaving the dazed tiger to recover from its folly, the clever couple walked back home hand-in-hand, laughing at how Bikal the Terrible had saved the day once more.

docs.title: The Tiger-Tamers
docs.text:
In a small village tucked near the forested hills of Madhya Pradesh lived two mischievous brothers. While the other villagers worked hard in the fields, the two boys spent their days playing pranks and causing trouble for everyone.

Hiding behind bushes with their bows, they would shoot arrows to shatter the clay water pots carried by village women on their heads. They chased chickens through courtyard gardens and drove herds of goats into the forest for fun.

One afternoon, after the boys drove away a farmer's goats, the aggrieved villagers lost their patience. Forming an angry posse armed with sticks and pickaxes, the men marched toward the boys. "Those rascals are becoming bolder every day!" a farmer shouted. "Let's catch them and give them a proper thrashing!"

Seeing the mob approaching, the brothers sprinted toward the woods. Outrunning the villagers, they plunged deep into the dense jungle.

Collapsing onto the grass, the younger brother panted, "Phew! Where do we go from here?"

"We can only go forward," the older brother replied. "It will take days for those villagers to cool down."

Venturing deeper into the uncharted forest, they rounded a rocky bend and froze dead in their tracks. Sitting right in the middle of the narrow path was an enormous, wild tiger.

Before the beast could spring, the younger brother unexpectedly darted forward, vaulted onto the tiger's back, and grabbed its ears, forcing its snout to the ground. Simultaneously, the older brother leaped behind and seized the tiger's tail in a tight grip!

Startled out of its wits, the tiger squirmed under their grasp. "ARRGH! What's happening?" the tiger growled in confusion. "Who are you? What do you want with me?"

"We are tiger-hunters!" the younger boy announced boldly from the beast's back. "We need a strong tiger to pull our plough."

"A tiger to pull your plough?" the predator asked, stunned. "When you plough with bullocks, you get rice and grain. What do you get when you plough with a tiger?"

"When you plough with tigers, you get gold and silver!" the boy lied smoothly. "We have already captured one tiger today, and you are the second one."

"Is that so?" the tiger asked nervously.

Pulling a small handheld mirror from his sash, the older brother held it right in front of the tiger's face. Gazing into the glass, the tiger caught sight of its own snarling reflection and mistook it for the first captured tiger!

If they've already caught such a fierce, nasty-looking fellow, the tiger thought in sheer terror, what chance do I have? I'll end up yoked to a wooden plough for the rest of my life!
