Shaking with fear, the tiger offered, "Look, friends! If it's gold and silver you are after, I can give you heaps of it! In my lifetime, I have killed and eaten several kings and princes. All their gold crowns and jewels are lying inside my cave. Come with me, and you can take it all!"

"How do we know you aren't making this up just to escape?" the older brother questioned.

"Come to my cave and see for yourselves!" the tiger pleaded.

The tiger led the brothers to the mouth of its cavern. As the boys stepped inside, the tiger seized the moment and bolted into the jungle as fast as its legs could carry it. That was a narrow escape! the beast gasped in relief as it ran. If not for my quick thinking, I would have ended up pulling a plough!

Inside the cave, the boys gasped in amazement. Scattered among the rocks were piles of solid gold necklaces, diamond-studded armlets, and royal crowns. The brothers gathered as much treasure as they could carry, wrapping it in a large cloth bundle.

Meanwhile, the escaped tiger hunted a fat sambhar deer and invited twelve fellow tigers from the surrounding forest to a grand feast outside his cave to celebrate his narrow escape.

Carrying their heavy bundle of gold, the two brothers wandered through the forest, only to realize they were lost. Suddenly, the bushes parted, and the boys spotted a pack of twelve tigers walking toward them!

"Tigers!" the younger boy gasped. "We're done for if they see us!"

"Climb that tree!" the older brother urged.

The older boy scrambled up into the high branches, but the younger brother, weighed down by the heavy bundle, was stuck halfway up the trunk when the pack of tigers arrived below, dragging the dead deer.

The thirteen tigers sat in a circle directly beneath the tree. The host tiger addressed his guests proudly: "Before we begin our feast, let me tell you of my narrow escape today. I was captured by two monstrous creatures who catch tigers to pull their ploughs. But I used my brains and escaped!"

Just then, the younger brother's arms gave out. With a loud THUMP, he lost his grip and fell from the trunk, landing right in the middle of the circle of feasting tigers!

Thinking at lightning speed, the boy scrambled to his feet, pointed at the host tiger, and roared with all his might, "YEOW! GRAB THE OLD TIGER! DON'T LET HIM GET AWAY!"

From the high branch above, the older brother immediately joined in, shouting, "GRAB HIM! IT'S THEM!"

The host tiger shrieked in terror. "MY FRIENDS, IT'S THOSE TWO MONSTERS I WAS TELLING YOU ABOUT!"

Panicked beyond measure, all thirteen tigers leaped over one another in wild chaos, scattering in every direction and fleeing for their lives through the woods.

When the dust settled, the two brothers climbed down, laughing with relief. Looking out from the high tree branches earlier, the older brother had spotted the familiar landmarks of their village trail.

Carrying their fortune in gold and silver, the two brave brothers returned home, where the impressed villagers welcomed them back as village heroes.

docs.title: Bimbisara: Model King of Magadha
docs.text:
Nearly two thousand five hundred years ago, Bhattiya, the King of Magadha, returned to his capital at Rajagriha in defeat after a grueling battle against Brahmadatta, the ambitious King of Anga. As the weary king entered the palace gates, his fifteen-year-old youngest son, Prince Bimbisara, ran to welcome him.

Seeing his father's bowed head, Bimbisara swore a silent oath: One day, I will teach that tyrant Brahmadatta a lesson he will never forget.

A few years later, King Bhattiya looked into his mirror, noticed his graying hair, and decided it was time to choose a successor. Rather than automatically appointing his eldest son, Bhattiya resolved to test the intelligence and courage of his four sons to determine who was best suited to rule.

Calling his sons, the King placed before each of them a sealed earthen pot filled with water and a tightly woven basket containing kajli, a delicate sweet dish. "You must consume the sweet and drink the water," Bhattiya instructed, "without breaking the earthen pot or opening the basket."

The three elder princes inspected the sealed containers and gave up, declaring the task impossible. But Bimbisara reflected on his tutors' lessons regarding overcoming obstacles. Taking the basket, he shook it vigorously until the brittle sweet crumbled into fine powder and trickled through the weave onto a plate. Next, he wrapped a cloth around the porous earthen pot, allowing the cloth to absorb the water, which he then squeezed into a cup and drank.

When the King and the elder princes entered Bimbisara's room, they were astonished to find both containers intact and the task completed.

That evening, Bhattiya devised a second test—this time to test their courage. He invited his four sons to a grand banquet. As the princes sat before their meal, the doors flew open, and a pack of ferocious, hungry hounds rushed into the dining hall. The three elder brothers panicked and fled. But Bimbisara remained calm, picked up a bowl of food, and held it out gently to the hounds. Tamed by his fearlessness and kindness, the dogs settled down peacefully beside him.

Delighted by his youngest son's sharp intellect and cool courage, King Bhattiya summoned his council the next day and anointed fifteen-year-old Bimbisara as the Crown Prince and King of Magadha.

When his ministers urged him to attack Anga immediately, Bimbisara displayed wisdom beyond his years. "I will make no move until Magadha is fully prepared," he declared.

To strengthen his kingdom, Bimbisara forged strategic matrimonial alliances. He married Princess Chellana, the daughter of Raja Chetaka, chief of the powerful Lichchhavi Republic of Vaishali, elevating Magadha's prestige across Northern India.

When Queen Chellana became pregnant, a royal astrologer privately warned her of a dark omen: the child she carried was destined to take his father's life. Overcome with dread for her husband's safety, Chellana secretly gave birth in isolation while Bimbisara was on a state tour and abandoned the newborn infant under a banyan tree in the wilderness.

Returning from his tour, Bimbisara spotted the abandoned, crying infant in the woods. Moved by deep paternal affection, he picked up the bleeding baby, wrapped him in his royal mantle, and brought him to the palace.

Weeping in remorse, Chellana confessed her deed. But Bimbisara embraced her gently. "Even if he is destined to take my life, he is my son. I shall raise him with all my love." He named the prince Ajatashatru ("One whose enemy is not yet born") and ordered that the child should never learn of his temporary abandonment.

Years passed, and Magadha flourished. One day, while touring the outskirts of Rajagriha, Bimbisara noticed a serene young monk sitting under a tree. It was Prince Gautama of Kapilavastu (Gautama Buddha), who had recently renounced his royal heritage.

Impressed by Gautama's radiant presence, Bimbisara offered him half the kingdom of Magadha. But Gautama smiled gently. "Such kingdoms are not for me, O King. I have walked away from one to seek the root cause of human suffering and the way to end it."

Bimbisara bowed before the Sakya prince. "When you find the path to end misery, pray return to Rajagriha and teach us."

Soon after, King Brahmadatta of Anga launched fresh raids on Magadha's borders. Bimbisara dispatched Prince Ajatashatru to lead the Magadhan army. Displaying brilliant military tactics, Ajatashatru defeated the forces of Anga and slew Brahmadatta on the battlefield. Bimbisara rewarded his son by appointing him Regent of Anga.

However, dark forces were at work. Devadatta, the ambitious and envious cousin of the Buddha, sought to build his own influence by manipulating the young prince. Traveling to Anga, Devadatta flattered Ajatashatru. "You are a brilliant conqueror," Devadatta whispered. "Why remain a mere governor? Take the throne of Magadha by force before it is too late!"

Poisoned by Devadatta's counsel, Ajatashatru sneaked into King Bimbisara's bedchamber at night with a drawn sword. But royal guards disarmed the prince.

When Bimbisara awoke and saw his son in chains, he immediately ordered his release. "If you desire the throne, my son, it is yours," Bimbisara said gently. "This drama was unnecessary."

Mistaking his father's unconditional love for weakness, Ajatashatru proclaimed himself Emperor and confined Bimbisara to a fortress cell. Devadatta continued to whisper in the young ruler's ear: "As long as the old king lives, nobles may revolt. Have him executed!"

In his prison cell, Bimbisara bore his confinement without bitterness, finding solace in the gentle memory of the Buddha's teachings.

Months later, Ajatashatru's queen gave birth to a baby boy. As Ajatashatru held his newborn son in his arms, a sudden, overwhelming surge of paternal love flooded his heart. Tears filled his eyes as he realized the depth of a father's devotion. If my love for my son is this immense, Ajatashatru thought in a flash of horror, how deeply must my father have loved me when I was born!

"Cancel my orders!" Ajatashatru shouted, rushing out to reverse the execution decree he had signed earlier.

Just then, a messenger handed him two documents: one announcing the birth of his son, and the second from the prison warden reporting that King Bimbisara had just been executed in accordance with his earlier orders.

Ajatashatru collapsed to the floor, weeping in uncontrollable agony. "Oh no! What have I done? I have murdered my own father!"

When Devadatta entered the hall to offer congratulations, Ajatashatru turned upon the monk in fury. "Wretch! Out of my sight! I shall never set eyes on you again as long as I live!"

Banishing the evil counselor, Ajatashatru ascended the throne in deep repentance, spending his reign carrying out his father Bimbisara's vision of expanding Magadha into a mighty, united empire.

docs.title: Birbal the Inimitable
docs.text: Raja Birbal, the grand vizier in the court of Mughal Emperor Akbar, was celebrated throughout India for his extraordinary wit, deep wisdom, and uncanny ability to solve complex problems. His sharp intellect turned palace rivalries into laughter and taught timeless lessons in justice.

docs.title: Pandit Gangaram
docs.text:
One day, an uneducated Brahman named Gangaram approached Birbal in distress. "Birbal Sahib, my ancestors were learned Sanskrit scholars known as Pandits. I have neither scholarship nor wealth, but I have one burning wish—I want people to address me as Pandit!"

Birbal smiled. "I can fulfill your wish in just two days, provided you follow my instructions strictly. For the next two days, whenever anyone calls you 'Pandit', you must shout at them in fury and chase them!"

Gangaram was puzzled, but agreed. The next morning, Birbal visited Gangaram's neighborhood in Bara Chowk and met a group of local boys playing in the alley. "Boys," Birbal whispered, "if you want some fun, call that man Gangaram 'Panditji' when he steps out."

As Gangaram walked out with his water pot, a boy shouted, "Panditji!"

Remembering Birbal's advice, Gangaram lunged at the boy in anger. "How dare you call me that!" he roared, chasing him down the street.

Delighted by his reaction, the rest of the neighborhood boys joined in the game, shouting "Panditji! Oh, Panditji!" as Gangaram chased them with a stick. Soon, teasing Gangaram became a popular pastime in Bara Chowk.

After two days, the boys grew tired of the game, but the title stuck. Whenever visitors asked for Gangaram's house, the neighbors proudly pointed: "Don't you know Panditji's house? Right there!"

Thus, Gangaram earned the title he had cherished all his life.

docs.title: And Curry, of Course
docs.text:
One morning in the imperial court, Emperor Akbar engaged Birbal in conversation about a wedding feast Birbal had attended the previous evening. "What did you eat at the feast, Birbal?" Akbar asked.

"Oh, Jahanpanah, I had laddus, phirni, pulao, kheer, and biryani..." Birbal began.

Before he could finish listing the menu, an urgent royal messenger interrupted with news, and the court turned to official business.

The following morning in the crowded Durbar, surrounded by ministers and courtiers, Emperor Akbar decided to test Birbal's memory. Turning to him unexpectedly across the grand hall, Akbar asked, "What else, Birbal?"

Without a second's hesitation, Birbal bowed and answered smoothly, "And curry, of course!"

Delighted by Birbal's flawless memory of their interrupted conversation from the previous day, Akbar unclasped a precious pearl necklace and presented it to him.

The envious courtiers present were utterly bewildered, assuming the Emperor was so obsessed with curry that he rewarded Birbal simply for mentioning the dish. That evening, the courtiers gathered secretly. "We must bring the finest curries to the Emperor tomorrow! Surely he will reward us too!"

The next day, a procession of servants marched into the Durbar carrying massive brass urns of steaming curries on their heads.

Akbar stared in amazement. "What is the meaning of this?"

"Jahanpanah, we brought these curries for you, knowing how much you love the dish!" a minister grinned proudly.

Understanding their foolish mistake instantly, Akbar frowned. "You fools! What Birbal said yesterday was in an entirely different context. As a punishment for your stupidity, I order you to sit right here and eat every drop of these curries!"

docs.title: The Only Rooster
docs.text:
Emperor Akbar loved to play harmless pranks on Birbal to see if he could ever outwit his clever minister. One morning, while Birbal was away on a royal errand, Akbar handed a basket of boiled eggs to his courtiers.

"I want each of you to take an egg and hide it in your robes," Akbar instructed. "When Birbal returns, I shall order everyone to dive into the palace garden pond to search for a miraculous egg. When you come up, pretend you found your egg at the bottom of the pool!"

When Birbal returned to court, Akbar announced gravely, "Birbal, last night I had a strange dream. To test the loyalty and spiritual purity of my courtiers, everyone must dive into the garden pond. A truly reliable man will find an egg at the bottom!"

One by one, the courtiers dived into the water, emerging triumphantly with their hidden eggs. "Here, Jahanpanah, I found an egg!" each proclaimed, chuckling secretly at Birbal's impending embarrassment.

Recognizing the whispering and suppressed smiles, Birbal sensed a setup. He dived deep into the pond, searched the empty bottom, and understood the trick.

Emerging from the water, Birbal shook the water from his head, flapped his arms against his sides, and crowed at the top of his lungs: "Cock-a-doodle-doo! Cock-a-doodle-doo!"

Akbar looked on in surprise. "Where is your egg, Birbal? What on earth are you crowing for?"

Birbal smiled brightly. "Surely Huzoor knows that only hens lay eggs, not roosters! You have a large brood of hens in your court, but I am the only rooster!"

The courtiers flushed with embarrassment as Emperor Akbar burst into hearty laughter, acknowledging Birbal's supreme wit once again.

docs.title: Parting of Friends
docs.text:
Emperor Akbar was deeply concerned about his sixteen-year-old son, Prince Salim. The young prince had fallen into the company of an idle courtier named Yasin, spending his days playing cards and hunting instead of learning statecraft.

"I want to separate Salim from Yasin," Akbar told his ministers, "but if I banish Yasin, Salim will resent me. If I speak ill of Yasin, Salim will defend him."

When Birbal returned from a journey, Akbar sought his help. "Give me just two days, Huzoor," Birbal assured him.

The next day in full court, Birbal walked up to Yasin, leaned over, and whispered nonsense into his ear: "Just one seed in every mango!" He then added aloud in a stern voice, "Now, do not breathe a word of this secret to anyone!"

The moment court adjourned, Prince Salim rushed to Yasin. "What secret did Birbal whisper to you?"

"Nothing, my Prince!" Yasin insisted truthfully. "He just whispered some nonsense—'just one seed in every mango'."

Salim's eyes narrowed. "Birbal wouldn't call you aside in open court just to whisper nonsense! You are hiding something from me!"

"I swear I am telling the truth!" Yasin pleaded.

"I thought you were my friend, Yasin, but you do not trust me," Salim declared in anger. "I do not want to talk to you ever again!"

Watching from behind a pillar, Birbal smiled as Prince Salim turned his back on Yasin and returned to his royal duties.

docs.title: The Phases of the Moon
docs.text:
During a secret diplomatic mission to Kabul, Birbal mingled with the local populace. Suspecting he was a foreign traveler, local guards brought him before the King of Kabul.

"Tell me truly, stranger, who are you and what do you think of my rule?" the Afghan King demanded.

Birbal bowed gracefully. "Your Majesty, you are like the full moon in all its glory. No phase of the moon can compare with your splendor."

Pleased by the compliment, the King asked, "And what of your own ruler, Emperor Akbar?"

"Oh, him?" Birbal replied smoothly. "He is like the crescent moon—thin and weak."

Delighted, the King presented Birbal with a heavy bag of gold coins.

However, news of the encounter reached Delhi before Birbal's return. An envious noble, Hussain Khan, reported to Akbar: "Jahanpanah, Birbal praised the King of Kabul as the full moon while insulting your Majesty as a mere crescent moon!"

The next morning, Akbar confronted Birbal sternly. "Birbal, on your oath, did you compare me to a crescent moon and the King of Kabul to a full moon?"

"I did, Sire," Birbal answered calmly.

"How dare you!" Akbar's voice boomed. "You are supposed to be my most loyal minister!"

"Jahanpanah, consider the nature of the moon," Birbal explained wisely. "The full moon has reached its peak and is destined to decrease in size and glory every single day. But the crescent moon is full of promise—it grows in glory day by day until it illuminates the entire world! That is why the crescent moon of the second day is venerated by all."

Akbar's stern expression dissolved into a smile. "I should have known better than to doubt you, Birbal. As usual, you win!"

docs.title: Value of Waste
docs.text:
One afternoon in court, Emperor Akbar examined a slightly chipped porcelain vase. "Take this away," Akbar ordered an attendant. "Never show me anything broken. Anything that is broken, crushed, or rotten is of no use to anyone."

"Why, Huzoor?" Birbal intervened gently. "Sometimes broken or crushed things yield the highest value."

"Prove it to me, Birbal," Akbar challenged.

Birbal smiled. "Consider sugarcane, Sire. It is broken and crushed in heavy mills, yet from its destruction comes sweet jaggery and refined sugar fit for divine offerings. Consider the cotton pod—it bursts open and is beaten to yield fine thread, which is woven into robes fit for a king. And rotten, decaying rags and old jute are crushed to produce paper, upon which the holy Scriptures and royal decrees are written!"

Akbar nodded in admiration. "Indeed that is true, Birbal. Everything in creation has its use, even that which is broken or discarded."

docs.title: The True Owner
docs.text:
An Afghan merchant presented himself at Akbar's court in tears. "Huzoor, I hired a river barge to transport my shipment of ten thousand rupees' worth of fine Persian carpets to Delhi. Now the boatman claims that he owns the carpets, and his oarsmen back his lie!"

Birbal summoned the boatman and his crew. The boatman insisted boldly, "The goods are mine! The merchant was merely a passenger." The oarsmen swore to the same story.

Adjourning the court, Birbal dressed in the simple clothes of a merchant's clerk (munim) and visited the riverbank where the boat was anchored. Accompanied by a friend, Birbal approached the boatman.

"We are looking for fine Persian carpets," Birbal's friend said. "Will you sell your stock for five thousand rupees?"

The greedy boatman, anxious to dispose of the stolen goods, agreed immediately. "Yes! I'll sell the entire lot for five thousand!"

Later that evening, Birbal visited the inn where the Afghan merchant was staying, posing as a buyer offering five thousand rupees for the carpets.

The merchant refused indignantly. "Never! I bought these carpets for ten thousand rupees in Persia, and I will sell them only at a fair profit. I will not accept a single copper less!"

Having established who truly knew the value and cost of the goods, Birbal summoned both parties to court the next morning. Calling the oarsmen aside, Birbal warned them sternly, "I know the truth. Confess now, or face execution!"

Terrified, the oarsmen blurted out, "Forgive us, Sahib! The goods belong to the merchant! Our master paid us twenty-five rupees each to lie!"

Confronted with the evidence, the boatman confessed. Birbal ordered the carpets restored to the Afghan merchant, fined the boatman five hundred rupees for the state treasury, and compelled him to pay five hundred rupees in compensation to the trader.

docs.title: Limits of Loyalty
docs.text:
Emperor Akbar once challenged Birbal: "Bring me the most loyal creature and the most ungrateful creature on earth by tomorrow morning, or face my displeasure!"

Birbal returned home thoughtfully. Noticing his concern, his daughter asked the reason. When he explained the challenge, she smiled. "Father, sleep peacefully. In the morning, take your son-in-law and our pet dog, Moti, to court."

The next morning, Birbal walked into the Durbar accompanied by his pet dog and his son-in-law.

Akbar frowned. "Birbal, why have you brought a dog into the royal assembly?"

"As per your instructions, Huzoor," Birbal bowed. "This dog represents the most loyal creature on earth. If you give a stray dog even a single morsel of food, he will remain ever grateful and follow you like a shadow till his dying day."

"True," Akbar agreed. "And the ungrateful creature?"

Birbal pointed to his son-in-law. "Here he is, Sire! He represents all sons-in-law. No matter how much wealth, affection, and respect a father-in-law bestows upon him, a son-in-law is never satisfied!"

Akbar laughed, then declared sternly, "In that case, let the dog be rewarded sumptuously, and let the son-in-law be hanged!"

Birbal stepped forward quickly. "Huzoor, if you decree hanging for ungrateful sons-in-law, every son-in-law in the realm must be hanged—including you, me, and all the nobles present, for everyone is somebody's son-in-law!"

Akbar burst into laughter. "In that case, let him go!"

docs.title: And Off It Flew
docs.text:
Emperor Akbar had a habit of listening to bedtime stories every night and insisted on hearing new tales. If a storyteller paused, Akbar would impatiently prompt, "And then? And then?"

When it was Birbal's turn to tell a story, he resolved to cure the Emperor of this wearying habit.

"Once upon a time," Birbal began, "a wealthy farmer built a massive, airtight granary to store his harvested wheat. But high up on one wall, a tiny hole remained. One morning, a sparrow flew in through the hole, picked up a grain of wheat, and off it flew."

"And then?" Akbar asked.

"Then another sparrow flew in, picked up a grain of wheat, and off it flew."

"And then?"

"Then a third sparrow flew in, took a grain, and off it flew... then a fourth sparrow flew in, took two grains, and off it flew..."

For an hour, Birbal continued describing sparrows flying in and out of the granary.

"Enough of sparrows!" Akbar interrupted peevishly. "What happened after that?"

"Jahanpanah," Birbal replied innocently, "thousands of sparrows entered that granary. Only when every single grain of wheat has been carried away can the story move on! It may take months or years."

Realizing Birbal's clever lesson, Akbar chuckled and pulled the covers up. "Forget it! I don't want to hear any more stories tonight."

docs.title: A Shared Dream
docs.text:
One morning in court, Emperor Akbar decided to poke fun at Birbal before the assembled nobles. "Birbal, I had the strangest dream last night! We were both floating in the sky like clouds when suddenly we fell down with a bang. I fell into a huge pit filled with sweet, golden honey... while you, Birbal, fell into a pit filled with foul, smelly gutter filth!"

The courtiers roared with laughter, eager to see Birbal humbled.

Birbal smiled serenely and bowed. "How extraordinary, Jahanpanah! I had the exact same dream last night!"

Akbar looked surprised. "Really?"

"Yes, Sire," Birbal continued smoothly. "But I think your Majesty woke up before the dream ended. In my dream, after we climbed out of our respective pits with great effort... you began to lick me clean, and I began to lick you clean!"

The court erupted into uncontrollable laughter as Emperor Akbar smiled sheepishly, once again acknowledging Birbal's unbeatable wit.

docs.title: The Blue Umbrella
docs.text:
In a serene, sunlit village nestled in the pine-scented hills of Garhwal, lived ten-year-old Binya. The mountains were her home, and her days were spent herding her two cows, Neelu and Gori, through the mountain pastures.

One afternoon, while searching for her cows near a clearing, Binya came upon a group of wealthy picnickers from the city. Resting under the shade of a pine tree was a lady holding the most beautiful umbrella Binya had ever seen—a bright, twirling parasol made of sky-blue silk.

Binya stood entranced, unable to take her eyes off the blue flower-like umbrella. As she drew closer, the city lady noticed the small pendant hanging around Binya's neck—a polished leopard's claw set in brass, a lucky charm given to her by her mother.

"What a lovely necklace!" the city lady remarked. "Is that a real leopard's claw?"

"Yes," her husband replied. "They wear them in the hills to ward off evil spirits."

"I must have it!" the lady declared. Turning to Binya, she offered, "Little girl, will you trade your necklace for two rupees?" Binya shook her head. "Five rupees then?" Binya shook her head again.

Frustrated, the lady looked at her blue umbrella. "What if I give you this blue umbrella in exchange for your leopard-claw necklace?"

Binya's eyes lit up with joy. Without a moment's hesitation, she unclasped her necklace, handed it to the lady, and took the brilliant blue umbrella into her hands.

From that day on, the blue umbrella was Binya's constant companion. She carried it everywhere—through the forest, down the steep hillsides, and across the stream, keeping it open even when the sun was mild just to gaze at its vibrant blue silk.

The umbrella became the wonder and envy of the entire village. The schoolmaster's wife wished she had one, the temple priest admired its bright shade, and the village children gathered around Binya just to hold it for a moment.

However, no one coveted the blue umbrella more than old Ram Bharosa, who ran a small tea and sweet shop on the Tehri road. Every day, as the Tehri bus stopped near his shop, travelers would admire the blue umbrella twirling in Binya's hand.

"Binya, that umbrella is of no use to you," Ram Bharosa called out one afternoon as she walked past his shop. "The sun isn't that hot, and silk isn't made for heavy rain. Sell it to me for five rupees."

"No," Binya replied firmly.

"I'll give you ten... twelve rupees!" Ram Bharosa persisted.

Binya shook her head with a smile and walked away, twirling her umbrella.

Unable to buy the umbrella, Ram Bharosa grew sullen and bitter. When Binya's older brother, Bijju, came to the shop, Ram Bharosa refused to give him sweets on credit. He hired a boy named Rajaram from the neighboring village to clean dishes and run errands at the shop.

Noticing his master's obsessive longing, Rajaram approached Ram Bharosa. "Babuji, why are you so sad?"

"It's all due to that girl Binya and her wretched umbrella," Ram Bharosa sighed.

"What will you give me if I get the blue umbrella for you?" Rajaram whispered.

Ram Bharosa hesitated, then whispered back, "I'll give you three rupees... but do not let anyone know!"

A few days later, while Binya was resting in a grassy meadow, Rajaram quietly crept up, snatched the blue umbrella, and ran into the forest. Waking up, Binya spotted the thief running away. "My umbrella! He's stolen my umbrella!" she shouted, sprinting after him.

Hearing her cries, Bijju joined the chase. Together, they cornered Rajaram in a shallow mountain stream. As Bijju grabbed him, Rajaram dropped the umbrella and confessed in terror, "Let me go! It was Ram Bharosa—he paid me three rupees to steal it!"

News of the theft spread like wildfire through the hills. Disgusted by the old man's dishonesty, the villagers boycotted Ram Bharosa's shop. Schoolchildren walked past without buying toffees, and no one stopped for tea. Ram Bharosa sat alone in his empty shop, watching his livelihood crumble. "Not from Ram, the umbrella thief," the children would whisper.

Kindhearted Binya watched the old man's misery with growing sorrow. She felt responsible for his ruin. An umbrella is just a pretty thing, she thought to herself. It isn't worth a man's livelihood.

One afternoon, Binya walked into Ram Bharosa's empty shop, placed her blue umbrella on the counter, and bought a single toffee. Leaving a coin, she turned and walked away, leaving the blue umbrella behind.

"Binya! Wait!" Ram Bharosa called out, running to the doorway. "You forgot your umbrella!"

"You keep it," Binya called back with a warm smile. "I don't need it anymore."

Ram Bharosa was moved to tears by her generosity. He placed the blue umbrella outside his shop for anyone in the village to borrow whenever it rained. Villagers returned to his shop, and laughter echoed once again along Tehri road.

A few weeks later, a mountain bear strayed near the shop during the night and left behind a sharp, curved bear's claw stuck in a wooden pillar. Ram Bharosa retrieved the claw, took it to a silversmith in town, and had it set in a beautiful silver locket.

The next day, as Binya walked past with her cows, Ram Bharosa called her over. "Binya, take this! You gave me the umbrella—now take this silver bear's claw necklace from me!"

Binya beamed as she put on the silver pendant. As she walked home through the golden afternoon light, her cows bells chimed, and her happy song echoed across the hills of Garhwal.

docs.title: Angry River
docs.text:
In the middle of the Great River, which began high in the snow-capped Himalayas and flowed down toward the distant sea, lay a tiny, green island. On this island stood a small mud hut sheltered by a fifty-year-old Peepul tree. Here lived twelve-year-old Sita with her grandparents.

Sita's grandfather was a gentle fisherman, and her grandmother had taught her all the household chores. Her only companion was a small rag doll named Mumta, to whom Sita whispered all her thoughts and secrets.

One monsoon morning, Sita's grandmother fell gravely ill. "I must take her to the hospital in Shahganj across the river," her grandfather told Sita anxiously. "I will have to sell our goats to pay the doctor. You will have to stay on the island alone for a few days."

Sita looked at the dark storm clouds gathering over the mountains. "I'm not afraid, Grandfather, but what if the river rises?"

"If the water reaches the island, bring the goats and hens inside," Grandfather instructed. "And if the river floods the hut, climb up into the branches of the ancient Peepul tree!"

Sita watched the small wooden boat shrink to a tiny speck on the broad river as her grandfather rowed away.

That night, torrential rain hammered down. The wind howled through the Peepul leaves, and the river roared like a enraged beast. Sita lay awake on her charpoy, clutching Mumta to her chest.

By morning, muddy brown floodwaters were tearing away at the mud banks of the island. Dead trees, drowned cattle, and debris swept past in the raging current. Realizing the danger, Sita brought the hens inside and packed their few valuables—Grandfather's hookah, grandmother's brass vessels, and their silver ornaments—into a heavy iron trunk.

Suddenly, water gushed over the threshold of the hut. The mud walls began to crumble under the force of the flood.

"The Peepul tree!" Sita cried out. Dropping everything except her rag doll Mumta, Sita scrambled out of the collapsing hut and climbed up into the sturdy lower branches of the ancient Peepul tree.

From her perch in the branches, Sita watched in sorrow as her home dissolved into the churning brown floodwaters. Soon, the entire island was submerged, and the Peepul tree stood alone against the fury of the deluge.

The raging river tore at the roots of the tree, washing away the stones and earth holding it to the riverbed. With a terrible groan of timber and a flurry of falling leaves, the fifty-year-old Peepul tree uprooted and plunged into the river with a massive splash!

Sita screamed and clung with all her strength to a thick branch as the giant tree drifted into the main current, spinning through the violent, muddy waters.

For hours, as rain lashed down, Sita's arms ached with fatigue. She watched helplessly as distant boats swept past in the storm, unable to hear her faint cries.

Just as her strength was about to fail, the drifting tree struck a submerged sandbank near a bend in the river.

"Hellooooo!" a clear voice called out through the rain.

Sita looked up to see a small wooden boat rowing toward her through the waves. At the oars was a young, dark-skinned village boy named Krishan. "Here, take my hand!" he shouted, pulling Sita out of the branches into his boat.

As the boat glided into the safety of a flooded mangrove forest, Krishan handed Sita two sweet ripe mangoes. "I haven't tasted a mango in over a year!" Sita smiled, eating eagerly.

"My name is Krishan," the boy introduced himself. "I live near the foothills. The current swept my boat past your island, and I saw you in the tree."

As dusk fell, Krishan tied the boat to a high mangrove branch. Rain pattered softly on the water. Taking out a slender bamboo flute, Krishan began to play a sweet, haunting melody that lulled the exhausted girl into a peaceful sleep.

At sunrise, the storm cleared and the sun broke through the clouds. Rowing downstream for an hour, they reached a hill village where generous folk offered them warm food and shelter.

From there, an old farmer offered Sita a ride on his slow bullock cart to Shahganj. Bidding farewell to Krishan, Sita boarded the cart, keeping his bamboo flute wrapped in her shawl.

Arriving in the busy lanes of Shahganj, Sita searched frantically among the crowds until she spotted a familiar turbaned figure—her grandfather!

"Grandfather!" Sita cried out, running into his arms.

"Sita! You are alive!" the old man wept, embracing her tightly. "I was so worried when the river flooded!"

Sita looked into his tear-filled eyes. "Is Grandmother...?"

Grandfather nodded silently. Her grandmother had passed away peacefully in the hospital.

Three days later, as the floodwaters receded, Sita and her grandfather returned to their island. The hut and the majestic Peepul tree were gone, leaving only bare rocks and river silt.

"The Peepul tree was a part of our island and our life," Grandfather sighed.

"Yes, Grandfather," Sita said softly. "It was the tree that saved me... and the boy."

Together, they set to work. Using jute bags and fresh thatch, they built a sturdy new home. In the damp soil where the Peepul tree had once stood, Sita carefully planted a fresh mango seed, watering it every day.

As weeks passed, the river cleared of mud, flowing calm, deep, and blue under the mountain skies. One bright afternoon, while her grandfather was out fishing, Sita sat on the rocks playing a sweet tune on Krishan's bamboo flute.

A soft footfall sounded behind her. Sita turned to see Krishan standing on the bank with a warm smile.

"I thought you'd never come back!" Sita laughed.

"I promised I'd teach you how to play properly," Krishan grinned, sitting beside her on the rocks.

Gazing out over the tranquil blue waters, Sita smiled. The river had taken much away, but it had also given her life, hope, and a lasting friendship.

docs.title: Buddha: The Enlightened One
docs.text:
In the foothills of the Himalayas, the Sakya clan ruled over the prosperous kingdom of Kapilavastu, governed by King Suddhodana. One night, his chief queen, Mayadevi, had a celestial dream: a magnificent white elephant with six tusks entered her womb from the heavens.

Ten months later, while traveling to her parents' home, Queen Mayadevi passed through the serene Lumbini grove. Feeling the onset of labor beneath a flowering Sala tree, she gave birth to a radiant prince on the full moon night of Vaisakha.

King Suddhodana rushed to Lumbini and brought the mother and child back to the palace. The aged hermit Asita came to inspect the infant. Looking at the baby's auspicious markings, Asita wept.

"Why do you weep, holy sage?" Suddhodana asked anxiously.

"I weep because I am old and will not live to hear his great teachings," Asita replied softly. "This boy will either become a chakravartin—a king of kings—or a supreme enlightened saint who will lead mankind out of darkness."

On the fifth day, the prince was named Siddhartha ("He who has achieved his goal"). Seven days after his birth, Queen Mayadevi passed away, and Siddhartha was lovingly raised by her sister, Prajapati.

As Siddhartha grew, King Suddhodana, fearing the prophecy of renunciation, surrounded the prince with every conceivable luxury and pleasure, shielding him from all sights of pain and decay.

Yet young Siddhartha possessed a deeply compassionate heart. One morning while walking in the royal gardens, a wounded swan fell at his feet, struck by an arrow. Siddhartha gently pulled out the arrow, bathed the bird's wound, and cradled it in his lap.

Moments later, his cousin Devadatta arrived with his bow. "That is my bird! I shot it; give it to me!"

"I will not," Siddhartha replied quietly. "You tried to take its life, while I saved it."

The dispute was brought before the council of elders, who ruled wisely: "The life of a creature belongs to the one who saves it, not to the one who seeks to destroy it."

To anchor the prince to worldly life, King Suddhodana arranged his marriage to Princess Yashodhara, daughter of the Sakyan noble Dandapani, after Siddhartha demonstrated his unmatched skills in archery and horsemanship. Years of domestic bliss followed, culminating in the birth of their son, Rahula.

However, Siddhartha's inquiring spirit could not be confined within golden walls. One day, he asked his charioteer, Channa, to drive him through the streets of Kapilavastu. On four successive journeys, Siddhartha encountered the Four Sights that altered the course of human history: an old man bent with age, a sick man groaning in agony, a dead body being carried to the cremation ground, and finally, a serene wandering ascetic with a peaceful smile.

"Does everyone grow old, fall ill, and die, Channa?" Siddhartha asked in deep anguish.

"Yes, my Lord," Channa answered. "It is the inevitable fate of all living beings."

That night, looking at his sleeping wife Yashodhara and infant son Rahula, Siddhartha made his great resolve. I must find a way to end human suffering and death, he thought.

Summoning Channa at midnight, Siddhartha mounted his horse Kanthaka and rode out of Kapilavastu into the dark forest. At the edge of the kingdom, he stripped off his royal jewels, cut his long hair with his sword, exchanged his silks for a beggar's coarse robes, and sent Channa back to the palace.

Siddhartha wandered to Rajagriha, the capital of Magadha. Seeing the majestic monk begging for alms, King Bimbisara offered him high rank and half his kingdom. But Siddhartha refused gently: "I seek not earthly kingdoms, O King, but the truth that liberates men from sorrow."

He studied under famous philosophers Alara Kalama and Uddaka Ramaputta, but found their doctrines incomplete. Wandering to the forests of Urubilva near Gaya, Siddhartha joined five ascetics in extreme mortification, reducing his daily food to a single grain of hemp.

After years of severe fasting, his body became emaciated like a skeleton. One day, while bathing in the Nairanjana river, he collapsed from weakness. Realizing that extreme self-mortification only weakened the mind without revealing truth, Siddhartha accepted a bowl of sweet rice-milk (kheer) from Sujata, a village herdsman's daughter. Disgusted by his abandonment of asceticism, the five hermits deserted him.

Restored in strength, Siddhartha walked to a grand Pipal tree (now known as the Bodhi Tree) in Bodh Gaya. Sitting cross-legged beneath its branches, he vowed: "Let my body wither, let my skin and bones dissolve, but I shall not rise from this seat until I attain Supreme Enlightenment!"

Through the night, Maras—the forces of desire, fear, and illusion—assailed his mind with terrifying storms and tempting visions. But Siddhartha remained unshaken. At dawn on the full moon of Vaisakha, total awakening dawned upon his mind. Prince Siddhartha became the Buddha—the Enlightened One.

Traveling to Deer Park in Sarnath near Banaras, Buddha sought out the five ascetics who had deserted him. Struck by his luminous aura, they fell at his feet and became his first disciples, marking the founding of the Sangha. Buddha preached his First Sermon, expounding the Four Noble Truths (Life is suffering; Suffering arises from desire; Suffering can be ended; The path to end suffering is the Eightfold Path).

Returning to Urubilva, Buddha converted the great fire-worshipper Kasshyapa after spending a night unharmed in a sacred fire hut guarded by a venomous serpent.

When King Bimbisara prepared a grand animal sacrifice in Rajagriha, Buddha walked into the royal court carrying a wounded, lame lamb in his arms. "O King," Buddha spoke softly, "the slaughter of innocent animals cannot bring spiritual merit. All living beings cherish life." Moved to tears, Bimbisara abolished animal sacrifice and gifted the Veluvana bamboo grove to the Sangha.

In Shravasti, a grief-stricken mother named Krisha Gotami brought her dead child to Buddha, begging him to restore his life. Buddha instructed her gently: "Fetch a handful of mustard seeds from a house where no child, parent, or servant has ever died." Krisha Gotami searched every home in the city, only to learn that every family had suffered bereavement. Returning to Buddha, she realized the universal law of impermanence and joined the order of nuns.

Years later, Buddha returned to Kapilavastu. His father King Suddhodana, wife Yashodhara, and son Rahula embraced his Dhamma. Young Rahula was ordained into the Sangha.

Even as his influence spread across India, Buddha faced hostility from his envious cousin Devadatta. Devadatta plotted to assassinate the Master, hurling a giant boulder down a cliff (which split in two, missing Buddha) and releasing the maddened, man-killing elephant Nalagiri in Rajagriha. But as Nalagiri charged, Buddha extended his hand in boundless loving-kindness, and the fierce elephant knelt submissively at his feet.

For forty-five years, Lord Buddha walked the dusty roads of India, teaching kings, peasants, courtesans, and robbers alike. At the age of eighty, surrounded by his weeping disciples in Kushinagar, the Master delivered his final words: "Decay is inherent in all compounded things. Strive on with diligence." He then entered Parinirvana, leaving behind a light that continues to illuminate the world.

docs.title: King Kusha: Inner Beauty Above All
docs.text:
In the ancient kingdom of Kushavati, King Okkaka and his chief queen, Sheelavati, lived in deep sorrow for they had no children to inherit the throne. Queen Sheelavati performed intense prayers until Indra, the King of the Devas, appeared before her in a vision.

"I grant you a boon of two sons, Queen Sheelavati," Indra announced. "One shall be wise and master of all arts, but physically unattractive. The other shall be exceptionally handsome, but a complete fool. Which will you have first?"

"The wise one, my Lord," Sheelavati replied without hesitation.

In due course, Sheelavati gave birth to her eldest son, whom they named Kusha. Two years later, she bore her second son, Jayampati, who grew up to be remarkably handsome.

From an early age, Prince Kusha became conscious of his dark complexion and rough features. I must excel in every art and branch of learning to make up for my looks, he thought to himself. Through relentless effort, Kusha mastered martial arts, statecraft, music, sculpture, and painting, outshining every prince of his era.

When Kusha reached sixteen, King Okkaka wished to crown him and find him a bride. But Kusha was reluctant. Would any beautiful princess marry an ugly fellow like me? he worried.

To avoid hurting his parents, Kusha devised a clever plan. Using his supreme sculpting skills, he carved a life-sized statue of a maiden out of pure gold—a figure of breathtaking, flawless beauty.

Bringing his mother to the studio, Kusha said, "Mother, if you can find me a princess as beautiful as this golden image, I shall marry her."

Queen Sheelavati dispatched royal ministers across the subcontinent with the golden statue mounted on a chariot. "Exhibit this statue in every capital," she instructed. "When you find her living likeness, ask for her hand in marriage for Prince Kusha."

After months of searching, the envoys arrived at Sagala, the capital of the Madda kingdom. They placed the golden statue near the royal bathing ghat.

Soon after, the old hunchback nurse of Princess Prabhavati walked down to the river with her maidservants. Spotting the golden figure through the trees, the nurse mistook the statue for Prabhavati herself! "Prabhavati! What are you doing here alone?" the nurse scolded, rushing forward and slapping the statue's cheek.

Pain shot through the nurse's hand as it struck solid metal. "Ouch! It's a statue!" she gasped. Looking closely, the nurse laughed, "Ha! I mistook this cheap metal figure for our princess! Why, this image is ugly compared to my darling Prabhavati!"

Overhearing the nurse's words, the delighted envoys sought an immediate audience with the King of Madda, who happily accepted the marriage proposal for his daughter Prabhavati.

Back in Kushavati, Queen Sheelavati was overjoyed. But fearing Prabhavati would reject Kusha if she saw his face before marriage, Sheelavati instituted a strict royal condition: "According to our royal house tradition, a bride is not permitted to see her husband's face until she has conceived." Princess Prabhavati accepted the condition.

The wedding and coronation were celebrated with royal pomp. Living in darkness during their nights together, Prabhavati grew deeply attached to her husband's gentle voice, noble character, and soul-stirring music on the veena, without ever having seen his face.

Longing for a glimpse of his bride, King Kusha disguised himself as a royal elephant keeper. As Sheelavati escorted Prabhavati through the palace gardens, Kusha watched her from the stables. Overcome by playful mischief, he tossed a small piece of mud at her.

Prabhavati was outraged. "How dare you, ugly fellow!" she shouted at the disguised king. "I will have my husband cut off your hands!"

Determined to see her husband's face, Prabhavati pleaded with Queen Sheelavati until the Queen arranged a procession. Handsome Prince Jayampati was dressed in royal robes to ride the lead elephant, while real King Kusha rode behind disguised as the elephant driver.

Watching from her balcony, Prabhavati admired the "handsome king", but noticed the ugly driver behind him waving at her boldly. "What an impudent elephant driver!" she complained to her nurse.

Suspicious, the old nurse ran down to the palace courtyard to inspect the royal party. To her horror, she saw the ugly driver step down first while all the ministers bowed low before him!

The nurse rushed back to the balcony. "Princess! The man sitting behind was King Kusha! Your husband is the ugly elephant keeper!"

Heartbroken and feeling betrayed, Prabhavati refused to live with an unattractive husband. Ignoring all pleas, she boarded her chariot and fled back to her parents' palace in Sagala.

Though crushed by her departure, Kusha refused to force her. "Do not stop her," Kusha told his ministers. "I will go after her alone and win her heart through true merit."

Renouncing his royal comfort, Kusha traveled to Sagala in disguise. He took up residence near the palace and spent his nights playing soul-stirring melodies on his veena beneath Prabhavati's window. Hearing the music in the dark, Prabhavati wept, recognizing her husband's touch.

Next, Kusha apprenticed himself to the royal potter. He crafted exquisite porcelain vases depicting scenes of Prabhavati and her nurse. When the potter brought the vases to the palace, Prabhavati recognized Kusha's handiwork and smashed the vase on the floor in anger: "I don't want it!"

Undeterred, Kusha became an apprentice to the royal wicker-worker, weaving a palm-leaf fan depicting Prabhavati's likeness. When the fan was presented to her, Prabhavati threw it down, realizing Kusha was constantly near her.

Finally, Kusha gained employment as an assistant cook in the royal kitchen. His culinary skills were so extraordinary that when the King of Sagala tasted a dish cooked by Kusha, he ordered that the new cook prepare all meals for the royal princesses.

Every day, Kusha personally delivered food trays to Prabhavati's chambers. Seeing him toil as a humble cook out of sheer devotion to her, Prabhavati's heart melted with secret admiration, yet her stubborn pride kept her from admitting it. For seven long months, Kusha served her faithfully without receiving a single warm word.

Seeing Kusha's endless patience and Prabhavati's stubbornness, Lord Indra decided to resolve the impasse. Indra sent royal envoys to seven powerful neighboring kings, falsely informing each ruler that King Okkaka had cast off Princess Prabhavati, and offering her hand in marriage.

All seven kings arrived at Sagala simultaneously with their armies! Discovering they had been offered the same bride, the seven monarchs were furious and delivered an ultimatum to the King of Sagala: "Give Prabhavati to all seven of us, or prepare for war!"

Terrified of total destruction, the desperate King of Sagala declared in despair, "I have no choice! I must cut Prabhavati into seven pieces and send one piece to each king!"

Hearing her father's gruesome decree, Prabhavati was terrified. She ran to her mother's quarters weeping, "Mother! If only King Kusha were here to save me!"

"He IS here, child!" her mother revealed. "He has been working in your kitchen as a cook for seven months, enduring humiliation just to be near you!"

Prabhavati ran to the royal kitchen and fell weeping at Kusha's feet. "My Lord, forgive me! I have been vain and cruel. Save me from this terrible fate!"

Kusha lifted her up gently, wiping her tears. "Will anyone dare touch my beloved wife while I am alive? Dry your tears and return to your chambers."

Stepping into the royal assembly with his golden bow, King Kusha revealed his true identity to the King of Sagala and challenged the seven invading kings. Awed by his majestic presence and martial aura, the seven kings bowed before him.

Displaying master diplomatic skill, Kusha arranged the grand weddings of Prabhavati's seven younger sisters to the seven kings, turning potential war into nationwide celebration.

Her pride completely dissolved by his valour, wisdom, and devotion, Queen Prabhavati proudly took King Kusha's hand, returning with him to Kushavati where they ruled together in peace and harmony for many prosperous years.

docs.title: Angulimala: The Bandit Who Took Refuge in Buddha
docs.text:
In the kingdom of Kosala, governed by King Prasenajit from his capital at Shravasti, a child was born to the royal priest, Garga. On the midnight of the infant's birth, a strange phenomenon occurred—every weapon in the royal armory and across the houses of Kosala blazed with a brilliant, supernatural light.

Perturbed by the omen, Garga consulted a renowned astrologer, who read the stars with a heavy heart. "I regret to inform you that your newborn son is destined to become a notorious murderer—a terror to all people."

Shaken, Garga rushed to the palace and offered to sacrifice the infant to save the realm. But King Prasenajit refused. "Lord Buddha has taught us to believe in the innate goodness of every living soul. Educate your son well under your guidance, and he will grow up to be a good citizen."

Garga named his son Ahimsaka ("The Harmless One"). True to the King's hope, Ahimsaka grew into a brilliant, gentle, and obedient youth, devoted to his studies.

At the age of fourteen, Ahimsaka traveled to the famous university city of Takshashila to study under a renowned master. Intelligent and humble, Ahimsaka quickly outshone all his peers, winning the deep affection of his guru.

His rapid success ignited bitter jealousy among the older disciples. Plotting to destroy him, the disciples staged a deception. As the guru walked past the courtyard, they loudly whispered, "Ahimsaka has become arrogant. Indeed, he thinks he is more learned than our master... and our master's wife agrees!"

Suspicions taking root in his mind, the guru walked into his house and found Ahimsaka sitting near his wife, absorbed in academic discussion. Misinterpreting the scene, the enraged guru shouted, "Ahimsaka! Get up! You have become arrogant and ungrateful! You have no place here—leave at once!"

Baffled and heartbroken, Ahimsaka tried to explain, but his guru turned him out into the street.

Ahimsaka returned to Shravasti, but when he confessed his expulsion to his father, Garga disowned him in rage. "Don't show me your accursed face! Go away!" Garga roared, slamming the door.

Shunned by society and rejected by his family for a crime he had never committed, bitterness turned Ahimsaka's heart to stone. I was innocent, yet they brand me a criminal, he thought bitterly. I shall take revenge on the society that rejected me!

Retiring to the wild Jalini forest along the trade route to Kaushambi, Ahimsaka became a bloodthirsty highwayman. He swore a grim oath to slay one thousand human victims, cutting off a finger from each victim to keep count and wearing them as a grisly garland around his neck. The people began calling him Angulimala ("He with the Garland of Fingers").

Angulimala struck terror across Kosala. Merchants, travelers, and villagers avoided the forest. He accumulated nine hundred and ninety-nine fingers in his bloody garland, needing just one final victim to complete his thousand.

Desperate citizens appealed to King Prasenajit, who dispatched a large detachment of forest guards to capture the murderer. But Angulimala ambushed the soldiers from high cliffs, hurling massive boulders and scattering the royal forces.

Hearing of the terror in Jalini forest, Lord Buddha set out alone on foot toward Angulimala's haunts. Herdsmen warned him, "Monk, do not enter that forest! The murderer Angulimala spares no one!" But Buddha walked calmly onward.

From a rocky ledge, Angulimala spotted the solitary monk walking down the trail. My final victim! he chuckled, unsheathing his curved scimitar and charging down the hill.

"Halt, monk! Halt!" Angulimala roared, sprinting after the yellow-robed figure.

Yet, to his bewilderment, no matter how fast Angulimala ran, he could not close the distance. Lord Buddha walked at a slow, peaceful pace, yet remained forever a step ahead of the gasping murderer.

Exhausted and drenched in sweat, Angulimala stopped, leaning against his sword. "Monk! Why don't you stop? Stop moving!"

Buddha turned and looked at him with eyes of infinite compassion. "I have stopped, Angulimala. I am at rest. It is you who are in perpetual, restless motion."

Angulimala blinked in confusion. "What do you mean? You were walking, yet you say you have stopped!"

"My mind is at peace because I have ceased all violence against living beings," Buddha replied gently. "You, my child, are trapped in perpetual motion because your heart is consumed by anger and fear."

The soft, divine address—"my child"—shattered the wall of hatred around Angulimala's soul. No one had called him a child or shown him love since his fall.

"Don't you know who I am?" Angulimala gasped, raising his blood-stained scimitar. "I am Angulimala! I want your fingers and your life!"

"Take them, my son, if it brings peace to your troubled mind," Buddha offered serenely.

Overwhelmed by the Master's boundless love, the scimitar slipped from Angulimala's trembling fingers. He collapsed at Buddha's feet, sobbing bitterly. "Master! Forgive me! Henceforth I shall never kill! Admit me into your fold!"

Buddha raised him up gently. "Get up, my child."

Buddha brought Angulimala to his monastery on the outskirts of Shravasti, ordaining him as a monk.

The following morning, King Prasenajit arrived at the monastery with his cavalry, preparing for a fresh expedition against the killer. "Master, I have come for your blessings to exterminate the monster Angulimala," the King announced.

"Supposing Angulimala gives up violence and lives as a peaceful monk, what would you do?" Buddha asked softly.

"I would salute him, Lord, but I cannot imagine such a miracle," Prasenajit replied.

Buddha pointed to a bald-headed monk quietly watering plants nearby. "He has become one. There he is."

The King stared in awe, saluting the transformed murderer before departing in deep reverence.

As a monk, Angulimala bore the heavy weight of his past karma with quiet fortitude. One morning while seeking alms, he heard a woman screaming in labor agony inside a cottage, her life in grave danger.

Deeply moved, Angulimala returned to Buddha. "Master, how can I ease her misery?"

Buddha instructed him: "Go to her and say: 'Sister, since I was born anew into the spiritual life, I have not intentionally taken a single life. By the truth of this declaration, may you and your child be safe!'"

Angulimala ran back and recited the solemn blessing. Instantly, the woman's agony ceased, and she gave birth to a healthy baby boy.

Days later, while walking through the markets of Shravasti, an angry mob recognized the former murderer. "It's Angulimala!" someone yelled. "Kill him before he kills us!"

Stones, sticks, and mud rained down upon the monk. Blood poured from his forehead, and his robes were torn to shreds. Yet Angulimala did not raise a hand or utter a word in defense, accepting the blows as the natural fruition of his past deeds.

Crawling back to the monastery bleeding profusely, Angulimala collapsed at Buddha's feet.

"Do you feel anger toward those who beat you, my son?" Buddha asked gently, cradling his head.

"No, Father," Angulimala whispered with a peaceful smile. "When I killed people, I did not know what I was doing. They too did not know what they were doing today. I am at peace with the world."

Reciting "Buddham Sharanam Gacchami", Angulimala breathed his last in profound serenity. Sitting beneath the Bodhi tree, Lord Buddha declared to his assembly: "Angulimala was a great soul. He conquered vice, cruelty, and anger, and has attained Nirvana."

docs.title: Amrapali: The Pride of Vaishali
docs.text:
In ancient Vaishali, the capital of the powerful Licchavi Republic, a royal gardener discovered an abandoned baby girl lying beneath the shade of a flowering mango tree. Taking the child home, he named her Amrapali ("The Mango Maiden").

As Amrapali grew into young womanhood, her extraordinary beauty became the talk of Northern India. Licchavi nobles and princes from distant lands fought bitterly for her hand in marriage, and civil war threatened to erupt over her possession.

To preserve peace, the council of elders made a radical decision. "Amrapali cannot belong to any single nobleman," the Chief Minister declared. "She shall belong to all of Vaishali as the State Courtesan."

Amrapali accepted her destiny gracefully. The state built her a magnificent palace set within a sprawling mango grove. A master of classical dance and music, Amrapali gave spellbinding performances before the assembly of nobles, becoming the ultimate pride and jewel of Vaishali.

During this era, hostilities flared between Vaishali and the kingdom of Magadha. One evening, after the Licchavi nobles rushed out from her performance to defend the borders against a Magadhan raid, a handsome stranger presented himself at Amrapali's mansion, requesting a private dance performance.

Charmed by his noble bearing, Amrapali danced for him. The stranger stayed at her mansion as her guest for a week, and a deep affection grew between them.

On the morning of his departure, the stranger took her hand. "Amrapali, come away with me to my kingdom!"

"You are a total stranger," Amrapali smiled. "I do not even know your name."

"I am Bimbisara, King of Magadha," he revealed.

Amrapali recoiled in shock. "The dreaded King of Magadha! How can you speak of love when your soldiers are killing my people on the battlefield?"

"If I order an immediate end to all hostilities and withdraw my armies from Vaishali, will you marry me?" Bimbisara asked earnestly.

"No," Amrapali replied firmly. "My heart belongs to Vaishali. I cannot betray my people."

Bimbisara gazed at her in deep admiration, called off the war, and returned to Magadha, while Amrapali remained behind, her mind troubled by the futility of human ambition and war.

Months later, news reached Vaishali that Lord Buddha was camping in the nearby grove of Kotigrama. Curiosity drawing her, Amrapali rode her chariot to the grove and sat quietly among the crowd listening to the Master's discourse.

"Out of desire is born grief; out of desire is born fear," Buddha taught in his soothing voice. "He who frees himself from desire is free from sorrow."

His words touched Amrapali's soul, filling her heart with a deep, unprecedented peace. Day after day, she visited Kotigrama to absorb his wisdom.

When the time came for Buddha to leave Vaishali, Amrapali stepped forward, fell at his feet, and requested, "Lord, will you honor me by partaking of a meal at my house tomorrow?"

"I will come, my child," Buddha accepted softly.

As Amrapali drove home joyfully, she encountered the chariots of the Licchavi nobles speeding to Kotigrama. "Amrapali, we heard Lord Buddha has arrived!" the princes called out. "We are going to invite him to dine at the palace tomorrow."

"You are too late," Amrapali smiled proudly. "The Master has already agreed to dine at my home tomorrow."

The nobles offered her bags of gold to relinquish the honor, but Amrapali refused. "Even if you offer me the entire kingdom of Vaishali, I will not give up the privilege of serving the Lord!"

The next day, Lord Buddha and his monks arrived at Amrapali's residence. After serving them a wholesome meal, Amrapali bowed low before the Master. "Lord, permit me to grant my mango grove and mansion to the Sangha to be used as a monastery."

"So be it, child," Buddha blessed her.

Stripping off her jewels and silk robes, Amrapali renounced her wealth, fame, and worldly life. She was ordained into the Sangha as a nun, spending the rest of her days in quiet meditation and finding the ultimate freedom of Nirvana.

docs.title: The Acrobat and Other Buddhist Tales
docs.text: This collection of parabolic Buddhist and Jataka tales illustrates how wisdom, detachment, and selflessness overcome human weakness, vanity, and sorrow.

docs.title: The Acrobat
docs.text:
In the ancient city of Rajagriha, crowds gathered in the square to watch a troupe of visiting acrobats. High above the ground, balanced on a bamboo pole, a beautiful young maiden performed astonishing aerial feats, flipping and twirling like a bird.

Standing in the crowd was Ugrasena, the handsome son of Rajagriha's wealthy royal treasurer. Smitten by her grace, Ugrasena went home and declared to his parents, "I want to marry the acrobat girl! If I cannot marry her, I shall starve myself to death!"

Fearing for their son's life, his parents offered a box of gold to the acrobat chief. But the proud chief refused. "I am not selling my daughter! If your son wishes to marry her, he must travel with our troupe."

To his parents' dismay, Ugrasena abandoned his luxurious life and joined the acrobats. He married the maiden, but since he possessed no acrobatic skills, he made himself useful by driving bullock carts, carrying firewood, and pitching tents.

Soon a son was born to them. As the troupe traveled from city to city, Ugrasena's wife would rock the baby in her arms, singing teasing songs: "Sleep, my son, son of a wood-fetcher... son of a cart-driver..."

Hearing her mock him, Ugrasena was deeply hurt. "Woman, are you referring to me?"

"Yes," she replied nonchalantly. "It is my acrobatic skill that makes me famous; you are just a helper."

Driven to win her respect, Ugrasena went to his father-in-law. "Teach me acrobatic feats!"

Under strict training, Ugrasena made astonishing progress. In a few months, he could turn seven somersaults in the air—outperforming even his wife!

When the troupe returned to Rajagriha, posters announced that Ugrasena would attempt a legendary feat: turning fourteen somersaults in mid-air from a thirty-foot pole! Thousands gathered in the city square, including the King and his ministers.

Climbing to the top of the high pole, Ugrasena launched himself into the air, completing the first somersault. But as he turned in mid-air, his eyes caught sight of Lord Buddha sitting serenely under a tree at the edge of the square, surrounded by peaceful monks.

Looking into Buddha's tranquil face, a profound realization struck Ugrasena: What am I doing? I am risking my life merely to gain human applause and impress a vain woman!

Sliding down the pole, Ugrasena walked past the cheering crowd straight to Lord Buddha, fell at his feet, and requested, "Venerable Master, admit me into your Sangha." Buddha blessed him, and Ugrasena found the true peace he had sought all his life.

docs.title: The Harvest
docs.text:
Once, a hard-working farmer was clearing and ploughing his field. Lord Buddha happened to walk past the field every morning, inquiring kindly about the farmer's progress.

Touched by the Master's interest, the farmer bowed. "Sir, you take such great interest in my work! When my crop is harvested, I consider you my partner and will share half the grain with you."

Buddha smiled gently and continued on his way.

Weeks passed, and the farmer's field flourished with golden wheat. "Tomorrow I shall call the reapers," the farmer thought happily as night fell.

That night, black clouds gathered, thunder split the sky, and an apocalyptic hail storm lashed the valley. By morning, the entire wheat crop lay flattened and destroyed in mud.

Devastated, the farmer returned home, threw himself onto his bed, and refused to eat or speak.

Hearing of his grief, Lord Buddha walked into his room. "Tell me why you grieve, O farmer."

"Master, last night's storm destroyed my crop," the farmer wept. "I am ruined, and I cannot keep my word to give you your share."

Buddha sat beside him and spoke softly: "You grieve not because the crop died, but because of your attachment—attachment to your crop, to your word, and to me. From attachment springs sorrow; from attachment springs fear. He who is free from attachment is free from the burden of grief."

Understanding the Master's wisdom, the farmer rose from his bed, his heart relieved of sorrow.

docs.title: The Golden Maiden
docs.text:
In Shravasti lived Kumara, the son of a wealthy merchant. When his parents pressed him to marry, Kumara carved a statue of a maiden out of solid gold, possessing matchless beauty.

"Father, Mother," Kumara declared, "if you find a maiden as beautiful as this golden image, I shall marry her; otherwise, I shall remain single."

After months of searching across many kingdoms, royal messengers located a maiden in Sagala whose beauty far surpassed the golden statue. Delighted, Kumara's parents sent rich gifts to her family to bring her to Shravasti.

Impatietly awaiting her arrival, Kumara stood on his balcony every evening. On the tenth night, he saw his father entering the courtyard with a solemn face.

"Mother, Father, why has she not arrived?" Kumara asked anxiously.

"Son," his mother wept, "she fell ill on the journey and passed away."

Kumara collapsed onto the floor in unbearable agony, weeping for his lost bride.

At that moment, Lord Buddha stepped through the doorway. "Arise, youthful one," Buddha said gently. "You do not grieve because a woman died; you grieve because the object of your intense desire was taken away. From desire springs sorrow; from desire springs fear. He that is free from desire neither sorrows nor fears."

As the Master's calm voice enveloped his heart, Kumara prostrated before Buddha and was admitted into the Sangha.

docs.title: The Perfectionist
docs.text:
In a bustling city lived a master carpenter who took immense pride in his craftsmanship.

One day, a young expectant father visited his workshop. "Sir, my wife is blessed with a child. I need a cradle."

"You have come to the right man!" the carpenter boasted. "I shall build you the most perfect cradle you have ever seen. Come back in a week."

A week later, the man returned. "Is the cradle ready?"

"A work of perfection cannot be rushed!" the carpenter replied pompously. "Give me another week."

Week after week, month after month, the father returned, but the carpenter kept polishing, carving, and refining, insisting it was not yet perfect. Soon, the child grew too old for a cradle, and the father gave up visiting the shop.

Years rolled by. The child grew up, married, and became a proud father himself!

Remembering his father's story, the young man walked into the carpenter's workshop. "Sir, the cradle my father ordered for me when I was born should be ready by now!"

The old carpenter looked up from his bench with a frown. "It isn't ready yet! And look here, young man—I take pride in my work! I will not permit you or your father to rush a true perfectionist!"

The young man stared at the old carpenter in disbelief, shook his head, and quietly walked away, realizing that perfectionism without utility is mere vanity.

docs.title: Chandragupta Maurya: The Determined Prince
docs.text:
In 321 B.C., the ancient kingdom of Magadha was governed from its capital, Pataliputra, by the nine Nanda brothers, headed by Crown Prince Nanda. The aged King Sarvartha Siddhi had retired from active rule, leaving power in the hands of his tyrannical sons.

Arrogant and ruthless, the Nandas grew deeply envious of their noble cousins, General Maurya and his one hundred sons. Among the Mauryan princes, the youngest, Chandragupta, was celebrated as the most brilliant marksman and hunter in the realm.

"The Mauryas are powerful, intelligent, and loved by the people," Crown Prince Nanda whispered to his brothers during a hunting expedition. "One day they will prove a threat to us. If we let them live, they will take the throne."

A few months later, while King Sarvartha Siddhi was away on a pilgrimage, Nanda prepared a deadly trap. He constructed a grand, secluded palace outside Pataliputra and invited General Maurya and his hundred sons to an exclusive royal banquet.

Once the guests arrived, Nanda feigned an awkward situation. "Maurya, our main hall is crowded. Please take your sons down to the private cellar dining room while our other guests are served above."

Suspecting no treachery, General Maurya and his sons descended into the underground cellar. The moment the last prince entered, Nanda's henchmen slammed the heavy iron doors shut and bolted them from the outside.

"You will never come out of this palace alive!" Nanda taunted from behind the sealed doors.

Trapped in the pitch-black cellar without food or water, the Mauryas struggled in vain to break the iron doors. As days passed, starvation took its heavy toll. One by one, the princes collapsed. Holding young Chandragupta's hand, General Maurya gasped with his dying breath: "Even if one of us survives... he must take revenge!"

"Without a doubt, Father!" Chandragupta swore through tears.

Days later, when the stench of death reached the upper halls, Nanda's guards reported that all the prisoners had perished except one youth. Unwilling to open the cellar, Nanda ordered Chandragupta transferred to the state dungeon and set fire to the palace, burning it to ashes to conceal his crime.

Meanwhile, news of the Mauryas' destruction reached King Parvatak of Parvata Desh. Wanting to confirm if any Mauryas survived before invading Magadha, Parvatak dispatched his master spy, Kamalapida, to Pataliputra.

Kamalapida brought an iron cage containing a fierce lion to the public square, issuing a royal challenge: "Set this lion free without opening or breaking the iron cage! If no one in Magadha can solve this, your king must pay tribute to Parvata Desh!"

Scholars and nobles gathered around the cage in bewilderment. The cage had no door whatsoever. How had the lion entered, and how could it be freed?

When news reached the state prison, young Chandragupta offered to accept the challenge. Brought before the court in chains, Chandragupta inspected the cage closely. He noticed that when a guard brandished a staff, the lion leapt in fury, but when a blindfolded elephant was paraded past, the lion showed no reaction at all.

Examining the guard's staff, Chandragupta realized the staff was magnetized, and the "lion" was actually a hollow iron shell filled with solid wax!

Without uttering a word, Chandragupta picked up a burning torch and set fire to the cage. As heat filled the iron enclosure, the wax lion melted away into a pool of grease, leaving behind only a bare iron skeleton.

Emperor Nanda was forced to acknowledge the youth's brilliance. Releasing Chandragupta from prison, Nanda appointed him manager of the royal guest-house, intending to keep him under constant surveillance.

One hot afternoon, as Chandragupta stood near the guest-house, he observed a bald Brahman in simple red robes walking up the path. The Brahman pricked his foot on a clump of sharp, spiky kusha grass. Instead of merely walking away, the ascetic sat down in the dust and painstakingly uprooted every single blade of sharp grass along his path.

Impressed by the man's extraordinary determination, Chandragupta approached him. "Revered one, please honor me by dining at the guest-house before you proceed."

The Brahman was Chanakya (Kautilya), the legendary scholar of Takshashila. Over dinner, Chandragupta opened his heart, recounting the murder of his father and ninety-nine brothers.

Chanakya placed a comforting hand on the prince's shoulder. "Don't worry, Chandragupta. I, Chanakya, will help you avenge your family and win the throne of Magadha."

That evening, as Chanakya finished his meal, Emperor Nanda and his brothers walked into the guest-house after a hunt. Seeing an unknown man in coarse robes occupying the seat of honor, Nanda flew into a rage. "What impudence! A beggar in rags occupying the royal seat! Get up, you scamp!"

Nanda seized Chanakya by his shoulder and shoved him off the dais. In the scuffle, Chanakya's sacred topknot of hair (shikha) came untied.

Standing erect, his eyes flashing with fire, Chanakya unbound his hair completely and swore a terrible oath before the royal assembly: "You arrogant reprobates! I shall not tie up my hair until the Nanda dynasty is completely uprooted and destroyed! If I fail, my name is not Chanakya!"

Chanakya and Chandragupta slipped out of Pataliputra that night and traveled to Parvata Desh, securing an alliance with King Parvatak to overthrow Nanda.

To neutralize Nanda's powerful external allies, Chanakya deployed master strategy. When the aggressive King of Lampaka threatened Parvatak's borders, Chanakya dispatched a spy to Kamarupa with a forged letter, inciting Kamarupa to attack Lampaka and keeping both foreign armies occupied away from Magadha.

Next, Chanakya sent envoys to the King of Kashi—a staunch ally of Nanda—soliciting his help against Nanda. Nanda's brilliant Prime Minister, Rakshasa, advised Kashi to pretend to join Chandragupta's alliance, intending to double-cross Chandragupta during the battle.

Anticipating Rakshasa's counter-trap, Chanakya set his own master trap outside Pataliputra. He arranged a fake midnight fire-sacrifice (yagna) led by his spy, Sage Masopavasi.

At midnight, expecting King Kashi to deliver a captured Chandragupta in a royal palanquin to the sacrificial fire, Nanda and his eight brothers arrived at the site.

"Deliver the prisoner!" Nanda laughed, stepping up to the palanquin.

The curtains flew open—and out leaped Chandragupta and a band of elite Mauryan commandos with drawn swords!

"We have been duped!" Nanda screamed as Masopavasi set the sacrificial canopy ablaze, surrounding the Nandas in fire.

Chandragupta charged into the fray, his spear gleaming in the firelight. Spotting Nanda, Chandragupta drove his spear through the tyrant's chest. "The murders of my father and brothers are avenged!" he declared as Nanda collapsed dead onto the burning sands.

The remaining Nanda brothers were slain in the battle, and their army surrendered.

With victory achieved, Chanakya tied up his topknot of hair in triumph. Chandragupta entered Pataliputra to the deafening cheers of the citizens and was anointed Emperor of India, founding the grand Maurya Empire.

Placing the imperial crown upon Chandragupta's head, Chanakya imparted his final counsel: "Be a just king, Chandragupta. Work unceasingly for the welfare of your people, and make India strong, prosperous, and united."

docs.title: Chandrahasa: Faith Cannot Be Vanquished
docs.text:
In the ancient kingdom of Kerala, King Medhavi and his queen prayed fervently to Lord Vishnu for an heir. Their prayers were answered, and a baby boy was born to them. But tragedy struck a month later when enemy forces besieged the fort, and King Medhavi was slain in battle.

Knowing the fort would fall, the Queen Mother entrusted her infant son to her faithful old nurse. "Take him through the secret tunnel and flee!" she wept, before performing Sati on her husband's funeral pyre.

The nurse carried the prince to the town of Kuntala, living in poverty and begging for alms to feed the child. When the boy turned five, the nurse passed away. Moved by the child's plight, the neighborhood women fed the sweet orphan boy.

One day, while playing with other children in the woods, the orphan longed for a marble. Praying to Lord Vishnu, he found a smooth, round black stone in the riverbed. Using it in their games, the boy won every match. Unknown to him, the sacred black stone was a divine Shalagrama—an emblem of Lord Vishnu's grace.

As the orphan boy grew, his gentle charm made him the leader of the village children.

One afternoon, Dushtabuddhi, the ambitious Chief Minister of Kuntala, stood on his balcony with a learned Brahman. The King of Kuntala had no sons, and Dushtabuddhi secretly plotted to seize the throne for his own son, Madana.

Pointing to the orphan playing in the street, the Brahman astrologer declared: "Mark my words, Dushtabuddhi! That boy is destined to become the King of Kuntala! Take him under your care and educate him."
