Alarmed by the prophecy, Dushtabuddhi resolved to eliminate the boy. He hired two ruthless assassins, ordering them to carry the five-year-old child into the deep forest and slay him.

In the forest, as the killers raised their axes, the boy looked up innocently and began chanting "Hari! Hari!" Touched by his purity, the assassins hesitated.

"We cannot kill this innocent child," one assassin said.

"Neither can I," the other agreed. "Look, he has six toes on his left foot. Let us cut off the extra toe to show the Minister as proof of his death!"

They cut off the extra toe, presented it to Dushtabuddhi, and left the child in the forest. Wild deer and monkeys fed the injured prince, until King Kalindaka of Chandanavati—a vassal ruler of Kuntala—came to the forest to hunt. Finding the abandoned child, the childless king adopted him as his son, naming him Chandrahasa ("One whose smile is like moonlight").

Chandrahasa was educated in scriptures, archery, and statecraft. By his eighteenth birthday, King Kalindaka formally crowned Chandrahasa as his successor.

Soon after, Dushtabuddhi visited Chandanavati. When Kalindaka proudly introduced his adopted son, mentioning that he had found him alone in the forest thirteen years ago, Dushtabuddhi realized with horror that Chandrahasa was the same street child he had ordered killed!

Determined to complete the murder, Dushtabuddhi handed Chandrahasa a sealed letter addressed to his son Madana in Kuntala. "Ride immediately to Kuntala and deliver this letter to my son. It is highly confidential."

The letter contained a secret instruction in Sanskrit: "Dear Madana, the bearer of this letter is destined to become the King of Kuntala. Therefore, without waiting for my return, give him visha (poison)."

Chandrahasa mounted his horse and rode toward Kuntala. Feeling exhausted as he approached the capital, he stopped at a royal pleasure garden and fell asleep under a tree, with the letter tucked in his sash.

It so happened that Vishaya, Dushtabuddhi's beautiful daughter, was gathering flowers in the garden with her maidens. Seeing the handsome prince sleeping beneath the banyan tree, she picked up the letter out of curiosity.

Reading her father's handwriting ("...give him visha..."), Vishaya gasped. Father must have mis-spelt my name in his haste! He obviously meant Vishaya! What a handsome man he has chosen to be my husband!

Using a plant stalk and dark kajal from her eyes, Vishaya cleverly added the letter "ya" to "visha", altering the command to read: "give him Vishaya" (marry him to Vishaya). She tucked the letter back into his sash and departed.

When Chandrahasa woke up and delivered the letter to Madana, the dutiful son read his father's command. Believing his father had found the perfect groom, Madana arranged a grand wedding that very evening, marrying Chandrahasa to Vishaya!

When Dushtabuddhi returned a few days later, he was shocked to find Chandrahasa married to his daughter! Madana showed him the letter ("...give him Vishaya...").

Realizing his daughter's intervention had foiled his plot, Dushtabuddhi seethed with rage. He immediately planned a third assassination. Sending for Chandrahasa, he instructed: "Go to the temple of Goddess Kali outside the city gates tonight at midnight to offer prayers."

Dushtabuddhi secretly instructed royal killers to hide at the Kali temple and slay whoever arrived alone at midnight.

Meanwhile, the old King of Kuntala had a vision of his impending death. He consulted his priest, who advised: "Marry your daughter to Dushtabuddhi's noble new son-in-law, Chandrahasa, and crown him King!"

Madana rode out to find Chandrahasa. Seeing Chandrahasa heading toward the Kali temple, Madana stopped him. "The King wishes to see you immediately at the palace! Take my horse and go—I will carry your offerings to the Kali temple in your place."

Chandrahasa took Madana's horse, rode to the palace, was married to the Princess of Kuntala, and crowned King of Kuntala!

Meanwhile, at the Kali temple, Dushtabuddhi's hired assassins mistook Madana for Chandrahasa and killed him in the dark.

When news reached Dushtabuddhi that Madana had gone to the temple instead of Chandrahasa, the minister rushed to the shrine, found his own son lying dead in blood, and stabbed himself in grief and guilt.

Arriving at the temple shortly after, King Chandrahasa was horrified to find both Madana and Dushtabuddhi dead. Falling before the image of Goddess Kali, Chandrahasa prayed intensely to Lord Vishnu, drawing his sword to offer his own life to restore theirs.

Suddenly, Lord Vishnu appeared in a flash of divine light. "Hold, Chandrahasa! I will not allow a pure soul like you to die!"

With a wave of his hand, Lord Vishnu restored Madana and Dushtabuddhi to life, cleansing Dushtabuddhi of all envy and hatred.

Tears of repentance flowing down his face, Dushtabuddhi embraced Chandrahasa. The noble prince returned to the capital, where he ruled over Kuntala and Chandanavati with his two wives, Vishaya and the Princess, in peace, prosperity, and righteousness.

docs.title: Chennama of Keladi: The Queen Who Defied Aurangzeb
docs.text:
In the year 1663, Somashekhara Nayaka, the ruler of the kingdom of Keladi in the Malnad region of Karnataka, attended the annual Rameshwar fair in his capital city of Bidanur. There, amid the bustling stalls, he caught sight of Chennamma, the beautiful and intelligent daughter of a local merchant.

Fascinated by her grace, the King resolved to marry her, ignoring his ministers' advice to form a political alliance with neighboring royal houses.

The royal wedding brought great joy to the citizens, but it foiled the dark ambitions of Bharama Mahuta, the powerful chief of the royal elephant force. Bharama had secretly plotted to usurp the throne, believing the King would remain unwed and without heirs.

Over the next few years, the young queen transformed herself into a formidable leader. Under royal tutors, Rani Chennamma mastered horse riding, archery, statecraft, and administration, taking a keen interest in the welfare of her subjects.

However, as years passed without an heir to the throne, King Somashekhara fell into deep melancholy. Seizing upon the King's sadness, the treacherous Bharama Mahuta offered him a slow-acting poison, claiming it was a rare potion to cure childlessness. The poison drove the King into fits of delirium, and within months, Somashekhara passed away.

The morning after the King's death, Bharama Mahuta approached Rani Chennamma, arrogantly offering to take the reigns of government. But Chennamma had already uncovered his treachery.

" Don't move!" guards shouted as Chennamma signaled.

Looking coldly at the traitor, Chennamma declared, "By poisoning the King who trusted you, you have proved yourself the blackest of traitors!" She ordered Bharama executed for high treason.

Following the King's death, Keladi descended into turmoil as rival ministers plotted against the Queen, dismissing her as an inexperienced merchant's daughter.

Before order could be restored, alarming news reached the court: a massive army from the Sultanate of Bijapur, led by General Jannopant, was marching to invade Keladi!

As panic erupted in the Durbar and senior ministers threatened to desert, Rani Chennamma stepped onto the royal dais. Unclasping her gold necklaces and bangles, she placed them into the treasury chest.

"You have just heard the news—Keladi is under attack!" Chennamma's voice echoed through the hall. "Who is to rule is not important; our first job is to repel the invader. These hands that once wore gold bangles now take up the sword to fight along with you! Will you fight?"

"We will fight!" the assembled soldiers and citizens roared back.

Realizing that Bidanur could not withstand a direct siege, Chennamma executed a brilliant strategic retreat. She ordered the capital evacuated and moved her army to the impregnable jungle fortress of Bhuvanagiri. When the Bijapur forces entered Bidanur, they found only an empty city with an empty treasury.

At Bhuvanagiri, Commander-in-Chief Thimmanna bowed before her, begging forgiveness for his earlier doubts. Chennamma embraced his loyalty and reappointed him to lead the army.

Utilizing expert hit-and-run guerrilla tactics in the dense, rain-drenched Malnad forests, Chennamma's forces decimated the Bijapur supply lines, routing the invaders and liberating Bidanur. In 1671, Rani Chennamma was formally crowned Queen of Keladi.

To secure the kingdom's future, Chennamma adopted a bright young boy, Basava Nayaka, personally training him in administration, ethics, and military command. She expanded trade with Arab, Portuguese, and Dutch merchants, bringing immense prosperity to Keladi, and successfully repelled an invasion by King Chikkadevaraya Wodeyar of Mysore.

In 1689, a historical crisis confronted the realm. Following the brutal execution of Chhatrapati Sambhaji by Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb, Sambhaji's younger brother, Rajaram, fled south toward the fortress of Jinji. Disguised as a simple monk, Rajaram entered Keladi and sought an audience with Rani Chennamma.

"Mother, I am Rajaram, son of Chhatrapati Shivaji," the prince revealed in her private chamber. "Mughal armies are hunting me. I seek safe passage through your kingdom."

Ministers warned Chennamma in panic: "Highness, granting passage to Rajaram will bring the wrath of Aurangzeb upon Keladi! We cannot stand up to the Mughal Empire!"

Rani Chennamma looked at her council with unflinching courage. "You mock me by putting your request as a question. It is the sacred tradition of Keladi rulers to protect anyone who seeks refuge. I shall face the wrath of Aurangzeb!"

She granted Rajaram shelter and arranged an armed escort to guide him safely to Jinji.

Learning of her defiance, Aurangzeb dispatched a formidable Mughal army under General Jaan Nisar Khan to crush Keladi. Chennamma sent a dignified diplomatic reply while ordering her forces to prepare for battle.

Before Chennamma's letter reached the Emperor, the Mughal army charged into the dense Malnad rainforests. As heavy monsoon rains broke out, the forests turned into soggy, impassable marshes. The Mughal troops, unaccustomed to the terrain, fell victim to dysentery, leeches, and fever, while Keladi's soldiers struck from the thickets with deadly accuracy.

Harassed day and night in the torrential rains, the Mughal army was forced to sue for peace and retreat, allowing Rajaram to reach Jinji safely!

Deeply moved by her heroism, Rajaram sent a letter of gratitude: "The rulers of larger kingdoms looked away in fear, but you stood up to the Mughals. I shall ever stand in awe of your courage."

Rani Chennamma ruled Keladi for twenty-five glorious years, noted for her justice, religious tolerance, and efficient administration. On her deathbed, she passed the crown to her adopted son, Basava Nayaka, who built the fort of Chennagiri in her honor and authored the famous Kannada encyclopedic work Shivatattvaratnakara.

docs.title: Choice of Friends: Tales from the Hitopadesha
docs.text: Adapted from Narayana's classic Sanskrit collection Hitopadesha, these timeless fables use animal characters to teach invaluable lessons on wisdom, discernment in choosing companions, and the supreme strength of true friendship.

docs.title: Choice of Friends
docs.text:
At break of dawn, on the banks of the river Godavari, a crow named Laghupatanaka perched upon a high banyan tree. Looking down, he spotted a sinister fowler approaching with a net and a bag of grain.

"Here comes the hunter, like the god of death himself!" the crow whispered, watching closely. The fowler spread his net upon the grass, scattered rice grains over it, and concealed himself inside a hollow tree.

Shortly after, a flock of wild pigeons led by their king, Chitragriva, flew overhead. Seeing the white rice grains gleaming on the grass, the young pigeons grew eager to land.

Chitragriva held them back. "Beware! How can rice grains appear in an uninhabited wilderness? I suspect a trap. Greed can lead to doom!"

However, a headstrong young pigeon scoffed, "Must we give up a feast out of vague fears? Fortune favors the bold!"

Overriding their king's wisdom, the flock landed on the grass. The moment they began pecking at the grain, the heavy net snapped tight around them, trapping the entire flock.

As the trapped birds panicked and blamed the young pigeon who had led them astray, King Chitragriva intervened sternly. "Stop carping! It is useless to cast blame in times of crisis. Listen to me: if we all beat our wings in unison at my signal, we can lift this net into the air together!"

At Chitragriva's command, the entire flock flapped their wings simultaneously. To the fowler's utter amazement, the net lifted off the ground, carrying his entire catch into the sky. The fowler ran beneath them waving his arms, but soon gave up in exhaustion and returned home empty-handed.

High above the clouds, Chitragriva guided his flock toward the western forest. "My dear friend Hiranyaka, King of the Mice, lives near the river. He has sharp teeth and will chew through our bonds."

Alighting near Hiranyaka's burrow, Chitragriva called out to his friend. Recognizing the pigeon king's voice, Hiranyaka popped his head out of his hole and gasped at the sight of the net. "My good friend! How did this happen?"

"It is the decree of fate," Chitragriva smiled. "Quick, cut our bonds!"

Hiranyaka immediately ran up to Chitragriva to gnaw his ropes first. But the noble pigeon king stopped him. "No, Hiranyaka! Cut the net around my followers first, and free me last."

"My teeth are small and my strength is limited," Hiranyaka hesitated. "What if my jaws fail before I reach you?"

"A king's duty is to protect those dependent on him, even at the cost of his own life," Chitragriva insisted.

Moved by Chitragriva's selflessness, Hiranyaka worked tirelessly until every string was severed, setting the entire flock free. Expressing deep gratitude, the pigeons took flight.

Laghupatanaka the crow, who had followed the flock from the Godavari, watched the entire rescue in awe. What nobility! What a true friend this mouse is! the crow thought. Swooping down, the crow called out, "O noble mouse, I wish to make friends with you!"

Hiranyaka retreated to his burrow doorway. "That cannot be. You are a crow and I am a mouse. You are my natural predator; we can never be friends."

"I have no desire to eat you," Laghupatanaka assured him. "I have seen your loyalty and honor. If you refuse my friendship, I shall fast unto death at your doorstep."

Touched by the crow's sincerity, Hiranyaka accepted his friendship. From that day on, the mouse and the crow shared food, stories, and deep companionship.

Months passed, and a severe drought struck the region. Finding food scarce, Laghupatanaka told his friend, "I wish to move to the southern forest where my old friend, Manthara the Tortoise, lives near a lake rich in fish and water."

"If you go, I will come with you," Hiranyaka declared. "I cannot imagine life without your company."

Placing the tiny mouse gently on his back, Laghupatanaka flew across the hills until they reached the lush southern lake. Manthara the Tortoise welcomed them warmly, sharing fresh water and fruit.

One afternoon, as the three friends conversed by the water, a panting deer named Chitranga rushed up to them, trembling with fear. "A hunter is pursuing me!" the deer gasped.

"Drink some water and rest in peace," Manthara comforted him. "The hunter will not find you in this thick forest." The four animals quickly bonded, forming an inseparable circle of friends.

A few weeks later, Chitranga failed to return to the lake at dusk. Worried, Laghupatanaka flew high over the forest canopy to search for him. Deep in the woods, he spotted the deer trapped in a heavy rope snare set by a hunter.

Laghupatanaka flew back, picked up Hiranyaka on his back, and rushed to the spot. Hiranyaka immediately set to work gnawing through the thick cords of the snare.

Meanwhile, back at the lake, slow-moving Manthara grew anxious and ploded through the forest on foot to join them. Just as Hiranyaka severed the last string and freed Chitranga, the hunter walked into the clearing!

Seeing the hunter, the swift deer bolted into the thickets, the crow flew into the trees, and the mouse darted into a hole. But poor Manthara, moving slowly across the grass, was caught by the hunter.

"Well, the deer escaped, but this fat tortoise will make a fine meal!" the hunter chuckled, binding Manthara to his bow.

Distressed by their friend's capture, the remaining three animals devised a clever rescue plan. Following Hiranyaka's instructions, Chitranga the deer ran ahead and lay down across the hunter's path, stiffening his legs and pretending to be dead. Laghupatanaka perched on the deer's body, pretending to peck at its eyes.

Coming down the path, the hunter spotted the 'dead' deer. "Aha! What luck!" he exclaimed. Dropping the bound tortoise on the grass, the hunter rushed forward with his knife to claim the deer.

The instant the hunter turned his back, Hiranyaka darted out of the grass and gnawed through the cords binding Manthara, allowing the tortoise to slip into a nearby pond.

As the hunter reached for the deer, Laghupatanaka let out a sharp caw. Instantly, Chitranga leaped to his feet and bounded away into the forest.

Returning empty-handed to the spot where he had left the tortoise, the hunter found only severed ropes. Outwitted by the united animal friends, the hunter picked up his empty bow and walked away in bewilderment.

Reunited by the lake, the four friends celebrated their freedom, proving that true unity and selfless friendship can overcome any danger.

docs.title: The Tiger and the Traveller
docs.text:
Deep in a forest, an old tiger, too weak and blind to hunt wild prey, found a discarded gold bangle near a marshy pool. I can use this shiny bangle as bait to catch a greedy human, the old predator plotted.

Holding the gold bangle in his paw, the tiger sat near the edge of the swamp. Presently, a weary traveler walked down the forest path.

"Hey, traveler!" the tiger called out in a soft, gentle voice. "Do you want this pure gold bangle? I am old and have no use for worldly riches."

The traveler stopped, eyeing the golden bangle greedily, but hesitated. "How can I trust a fierce beast like you?"

The tiger put on a pious expression. "You have every reason to suspect me. In my youth, I committed many sins and killed many animals. But a saintly hermit instructed me in Dharma, and now I lead a holy life. My claws are blunt and my teeth are gone. Come, wade across this shallow pool and take the gold!"

Foolishly allowing his greed for gold to silence his common sense, the traveler stepped into the water to reach the tiger. But after taking a few steps, he sank deep into the thick, inescapable mire of the marsh.

"Oh no! I am stuck in the mud!" the traveler cried out.

"Do not worry, my friend," the tiger chuckled, his gentle tone instantly vanishing. "I will come and help you out."

Wading slowly into the mud, the tiger pounced upon the helpless traveler and devoured him.

docs.title: The Crow, the Deer and the Jackal
docs.text:
In a verdant forest, a deer and a crow lived as devoted friends. One day, a cunning jackal named Kshudrabuddhi spotted the plump deer grazing on tender grass. What a delicious feast he would make! the jackal thought. I must win his trust to trap him.

Approaching the deer with sweet flattery, the jackal offered his friendship. The innocent deer was touched, but when he brought the jackal to meet the crow, the wise bird raised a sharp warning.

"Friend Deer," the crow cautioned, "never trust a stranger whose character you do not know! A friend chosen unwisely brings ruin."

"Do not be suspicious, Sir Crow," the jackal pleaded humbly. "I am lonely and seek only your noble company." Overriding the crow's advice, the deer accepted the jackal into their fold.

A few days later, the jackal led the deer to a farmer's crop field full of sweet, green wheat. As the deer grazed happily every morning, the farmer noticed his ruined crops and set a strong net snare along the fence.

The next morning, the deer stepped into the snare and was trapped tight. Seeing the jackal waiting in the bushes, the deer called out, "Friend Jackal! Quick, gnaw these net ropes and free me!"

The jackal chuckled to himself, gloating over his impending feast of deer meat. Aloud, he put on a hypocritical face: "Alas, my friend! Today is a sacred fast day, and this net is made of animal sinew. I cannot touch it with my teeth on a holy day. I shall return tomorrow." With that, the jackal retreated to wait for the farmer to kill the deer.

At dusk, the crow flew searching for his missing friend and found the deer trapped in the snare. "Ah, the traitorous jackal!" the crow cawed. "I warned you, but do not lose heart. I have a plan!"

The crow instructed the deer: "When the farmer approaches with his club tomorrow morning, puff up your stomach, stretch out your legs, and lie completely still as if dead. I will sit on your head and pretend to peck at your eyes. The moment I caw aloud, leap up and run!"

At dawn, the farmer arrived with a heavy wooden club. Seeing the deer lying motionless with a crow hovering over it, the farmer smiled. "The fellow is dead of fright! That saves me the trouble."

As the farmer removed the net from the deer's body to carry it away, the crow suddenly let out a sharp caw: "CR-ROA-K!"

Instantly, the deer sprang to his feet and bolted into the forest like wind.

Annoyed at being tricked, the furious farmer threw his heavy wooden club after the escaping deer. The flying club missed the deer, but struck the treacherous jackal hiding behind the bushes, killing the sly traitor on the spot.

docs.title: The Vulture, the Cat and the Birds
docs.text:
On the banks of the Ganga stood a colossal, hollow banyan tree where hundreds of birds built their nests and raised their fledglings. In the dark hollow of the tree trunk lived an old, blind vulture named Jaradgava. Out of pity for his age, the bird community shared a portion of their daily food with him, and in return, the old vulture watched over the tree.

One afternoon, while the parent birds were away searching for food, a sly cat named Dirghakarna crept toward the tree, intending to feast on the helpless fledglings.

Hearing the terrified chirping of the baby birds, the blind vulture stuck his head out of the hollow. "Who goes there?" Jaradgava croaked angrily. "Be off, or I shall strike you down!"

The cat panicked, realizing he could not fight the massive bird. Using his wits, the cat spoke in a soft, pious voice: "Revered sir, I salute your wisdom! I am a pilgrim who has renounced meat and taken vows of non-violence. I have traveled far to study sacred scriptures under your guidance."

Flattered by the cat's praises, the old vulture softened. "You are a cat, and cats eat young birds. How can I trust you?"

"I have spent years doing penance by the holy river," the cat lied smoothly. "I would never harm a living soul."

Tricked by his pious words, the vulture allowed the cat to take shelter in the tree trunk.

Every night, while the birds slept, the cat quietly slunk up the branches, snatched a few fledglings from their nests, carried them down into the vulture's hollow, and devoured them, leaving the bones scattered inside.

After several days, the bird parents noticed their fledglings vanishing. Organizing a search of the tree, they discovered a pile of bird bones scattered around the old vulture's hollow.

Assuming that the blind vulture had betrayed their trust and eaten their children, the enraged birds descended upon Jaradgava, pecking the innocent old vulture to death. Meanwhile, the real murderer, the sly cat, quietly slunk away into the forest.

Moral: Exercise extreme caution before inviting strangers into your inner circle of trust.

docs.title: Chokha Mela: The Boy Who Heard God
docs.text:
Toward the close of the thirteenth century, in the village of Mangalvedhe in Maharashtra's Solapur district, a young Mahar boy named Chokha was playing with his friends at dusk. As the twilight bell of the village temple began to chime, a strange divine resonance stirred within Chokha's soul. The ringing bells seemed to call out directly to him: "Come, Chokha, come!"

Driven by an irresistible spiritual yearning, Chokha stopped playing and ran toward the temple. His playmates called after him in alarm, "Chokha! Where are you going? You are a Mahar! You cannot enter the temple!"

Ignoring their warnings, Chokha pushed through the crowds into the temple courtyard right as the evening Arati began. Absorbed in prayer, the boy gazed wide-eyed at the glowing deity.

Suddenly, a temple priest spotted him. "Chhi! It's Chokha Mela, the son of a Mahar!" the priest bellowed. "How dare you enter this sacred place, you dirty fellow!"

"I am not dirty, sir," Chokha pleaded softly. "I bathe every day and my clothes are clean."

"Insolent wretch!" the priest shouted, seizing the boy and flinging him out onto the dusty street. "One more step inside and I'll beat the life out of you!"

Sitting by the roadside as the worshippers filed past, Chokha wept bitterly. Indignation gave way to deep reflection. Why do they call me dirty? he wondered. I scrub my body clean, yet because I am born a Mahar, they bar me from seeing the Lord.

For years, Chokha traveled from village to village, but at every temple, the doors were slammed in his face. Overcome by despair one afternoon, he sat weeping beneath a tree. A kindly trader stopped and took him home, offering him a bowl of warm gruel. But when the trader's wife questioned Chokha's caste, Chokha quietly slipped away without eating, refusing to cause domestic strife for his host.

One morning, a procession of Varkari pilgrims passed through Mangalvedhe, singing devotional hymns to Lord Vithoba of Pandharpur: "Vithala! Vithala! Vithala!"

Entranced by their ecstasy, Chokha joined the procession. After days of barefoot travel, they reached the holy town of Pandharpur on the banks of the Chandrabhaga River.

In Pandharpur, Chokha bathed in the sacred river and walked to the famous Vithoba temple. Remembering his status, he did not try to enter the main gates. Instead, he prostrated at the outer entrance, standing near the threshold every evening during Arati to catch a distant glimpse of the shrine.

A senior temple priest noticed Chokha standing at the outer gate day after day. "Lowly Mahar! Why do you stand here?" the priest mocked.

"I am grateful to stand at this gate and look toward the Lord," Chokha replied humbly.

"The sugarcane may be crooked, but its juice is sweet," Chokha added gently. "The lotus grows in mud, yet it is offered to the gods. Will the Lord reject a Mahar if his faith is pure?"

The priest's pride was stung. "How dare an illiterate Mahar lecture me on faith! If your Lord loved you, wouldn't He take you into His temple?"

The priest's taunts pierced Chokha's heart. Wandering back to the riverbank, he sat in silent agony, questioning his worthiness.

As night fell, a gentle, luminous voice echoed within his heart: "Chokha, do not let your faith be shaken by human words. The Lord is not bound by temple walls or human prejudice. Come to the gate tonight."

At midnight, Chokha walked down the quiet streets to the main gate of the Vithoba temple. As he stood before the heavy wooden doors, the iron bolts slid back automatically with a soft clank!

The heavy doors swung open. Drawn by an overwhelming spiritual pull, Chokha walked through the deserted corridors into the inner sanctum. Falling before the golden idol of Lord Vithoba, Chokha embraced the deity's feet, weeping tears of pure devotion.

At dawn, the head priest entered the sanctum to prepare the morning worship—and froze in horror. Sitting at the feet of the idol was Chokha Mela!

"You filthy Mahar!" the priest shrieked in fury. "How dare you enter the sanctum and defile the Lord!"

"How can an untouchable pollute God?" Chokha asked calmly, rising. "Can a sinner walking on mother earth destroy her sanctity? God is above all pollution."

"Quiet, madman!" the priest roared. Lunging forward, he struck Chokha hard across the face with his open palm, bruising his cheek, and dragged him out of the temple. "Get out of Pandharpur before the townspeople stone you to death!"

Without a word of anger, Chokha crossed the Chandrabhaga River to the opposite bank, built a small mud hut, and immersed himself in composing devotional Abhangs (sacred poems).

Meanwhile, back in the inner sanctum, the priest turned to perform the ritual bath of Lord Vithoba. As he looked at the golden idol, the pitcher slipped from his hands in terror.

The right cheek of the sacred idol was visibly swollen and bruised—in the exact spot where he had struck Chokha!

The priest rubbed his eyes in disbelief, but the swelling remained. Panic and deep remorse seized his heart. By striking the innocent devotee Chokha, I struck the Lord himself! the priest realized in horror. God dwells within His true devotee, above all caste and pride!

Full of repentance, the priest ran out of the temple, crossed the river, and fell at Chokha's feet outside his humble hut. "Chokha! Forgive me! I have sinned against the Lord by persecuting you! For my sake, please come back to the temple!"

Chokha raised the priest up gently. "Arise, priest. There is no anger in my heart."

Together, they recrossed the river and entered the sanctum. The moment Chokha bowed before the idol, the swelling on Lord Vithoba's cheek vanished completely.

Rani Chennamma's contemporary and fellow saint-poets hailed Chokha's divine devotion. Chokha Mela spent the remainder of his life at Pandharpur, composing immortal Abhangs that continue to resonate across Maharashtra as a timeless beacon of equality, devotion, and spiritual liberation.

docs.title: Crows and Owls and Other Stories
docs.text: Adapted from Pandit Vishnu Sharma's ancient Sanskrit masterpiece, the Panchatantra, these classic fables explore politics, war, statecraft, and human nature through the engaging medium of animal lore.

docs.title: Crows and Owls
docs.text:
Deep within a dense forest stood a magnificent banyan tree that housed a prosperous colony of crows, ruled by King Cloudy (Meghavarna).

Not far away, in a dark mountain cave, lived a colony of owls led by King Foe-Crusher (Arimardana). The owls held a deep, ancestral enmity toward the crows. Because owls possess superior night vision while crows are blind in the dark, Foe-Crusher led nightly raids on the banyan tree, slaughtering dozens of sleeping crows every night.

Distressed by the heavy losses, King Cloudy summoned his five chief ministers. "The owls attack us at night when we cannot see," the King addressed his council. "Advise me—what strategy should we adopt?"

The first minister advised, "Make peace with powerful foes."

The second minister urged, "Declare open war on the rascals!"

The third minister suggested, "Let us abandon this banyan tree and move to another forest."

The fourth minister argued, "A crocodile in its home water can defeat an elephant. Let us stay here and fortify our positions!"

Finally, King Cloudy turned to his oldest and wisest counselor, Live-Strong (Sanjeevaka). "And what do you advise, revered sir?"

"My Lord, I advise duplicity," Live-Strong replied calmly. "You must publicly stage a fierce quarrel with me. Strike me, pluck my feathers, and cast me out of the tree as a traitor. Then fly with all your subjects to Antelope Mountain. I shall remain behind, gain entry into the owl fortress, gather intelligence, and bring about their total destruction."

The next morning, as an owl spy watched from a nearby branch, the crows staged a dramatic quarrel. Live-Strong berated King Cloudy, and in response, the crow army pecked Live-Strong, stripped his feathers, and cast him down into the bushes before flying away toward Antelope Mountain.

The owl spy rushed to King Foe-Crusher. "Your Majesty, the crows have fallen out among themselves! They have beaten their senior minister and abandoned their tree!"

Foe-Crusher led his army to the banyan tree and found the battered Live-Strong lying in the grass.

"Who are you?" Foe-Crusher demanded.

"I was King Cloudy's chief minister," Live-Strong groaned feigning agony. "When I advised him to pay tribute to your Majesty, he attacked me and cast me out. I throw myself at your mercy, and I will lead you to their destruction!"

Foe-Crusher consulted his five ministers. Senior minister Red-Eye (Raktaksha) warned sharply, "Do not grant asylum to an enemy! Slay him immediately! A crow can never be trusted."

However, the other four ministers argued, "It would be unrighteous to kill one who seeks protection. He will be useful in our war against the crows."

Overriding Red-Eye's warning, Foe-Crusher granted Live-Strong asylum and ordered his guards to carry him to their cave fortress. Sensing disaster, the shrewd Red-Eye gathered his loyal followers and departed from the fortress forever.

At the cave, Live-Strong declined to sleep inside the inner chambers. "O King, I am an unworthy crow. Allow me to stay near the entrance of your cave."

Every day, as the owls slept, Live-Strong brought dry twigs and wooden sticks from the forest, stacking them neatly outside the narrow cave entrance. The owls assumed their guest was merely building a cozy nest.

After weeks of gathering wood, a massive wall of dry twigs blocked the entrance of the cave.

One morning, while the owls slept soundly inside, Live-Strong flew to Antelope Mountain and summoned King Cloudy. "My Lord, the trap is set! Bring burning torches!"

The crow army streaked through the morning sky, each crow carrying a flaming wooden branch in its beak. At Live-Strong's direction, they dropped the fire onto the dry twigs stacked outside the cave entrance.

Within moments, a roaring fire blazed, sealing the cave entrance. Dense, suffocating smoke flooded the cavern. Trapped inside the mountain furnace, King Foe-Crusher and his entire owl army perished in the flames.

Having eliminated their powerful enemies through master strategy, King Cloudy and his crows returned to their banyan tree in triumph.

docs.title: The Noble Enemy
docs.text:
Four travelers walking through a rocky mountain pass discovered a sack filled with priceless gems. Knowing the mountain paths were frequented by dacoits, each traveler swallowed a portion of the gems to conceal them safely in their stomachs.

Unbeknownst to them, a robber had been watching from behind a boulder. Aha! the robber thought. They have swallowed precious gems! I shall pretend to be a friendly traveler, join them, and when they fall asleep tonight, I will slit open their bellies and take the treasure.

The robber ran up to them with a friendly smile. "Good masters, I am alone on this dangerous road. May I travel in your company?"

"Welcome, friend! The more, the merrier," the travelers agreed.

As they walked toward the next village, they passed a mountain hut belonging to a fierce robber chief who kept a trained caged parrot. The moment the four travelers passed, the parrot began to squawk loudly: "They have gems with them! They have gems!"

The robber chief immediately dispatched his men to capture the four travelers. Searching their bags and clothes, the bandits found no gems.

"Search them again!" the chief barked. "My bird never lies!"

When a second search yielded nothing, the chief looked at the caged parrot, which continued to chant: "Gems in their bellies! Gems in their bellies!"

"The gems are in their stomachs!" the chief realized. "Take them away and slit open their bellies!"

Hearing this, the robber who had joined them panicked. If they cut open their bellies and find gems, they will cut mine open next and kill me too! But if I offer to be killed first, they will cut my stomach, find no gems, and conclude the bird was mistaken—thus saving the others!

Stepping forward boldly, the robber shouted, "Sir! You will find no gems in our stomachs. But if you insist on checking, cut open my belly first!"

The chief's men took the robber away and slit his stomach. Finding no gems inside, the chief cursed his bird. "The bird made a mistake! There are no gems!" He released the remaining three travelers with an apology.

Moral: A sensible enemy can sometimes prove to be your greatest savior.

docs.title: The Bird and the Monkeys
docs.text:
On a freezing winter night in the forest, a troop of monkeys sat shivering beneath a large banyan tree. Suddenly, one monkey spotted a glowing firefly drifting through the dark bushes.

"Look, brothers!" the monkey chattered excitedly. "A spark of fire!"

The monkeys caught the firefly, covered it with a pile of dry leaves, and began blowing on it with all their might to start a campfire.

A bird perched on a branch above watched their foolish efforts and called down gently, "Good sirs, you are making a mistake! That is a firefly, not a spark of fire. You cannot light a fire with a glow-worm."

"Mind your own business, bird!" the monkeys chattered, continuing to blow on the leaves.

Seeing them waste their energy, the bird flew down to a lower branch. "Listen to me, friends! You are wasting your breath. A firefly carries no heat."

Annoyed by her unsolicited advice, one of the monkeys grabbed the bird and struck her down onto the ground.

Moral: Do not attempt to instruct those who lack the wisdom to listen.

docs.title: The Camel Who Was Beguiled by His Companions
docs.text:
A merchant's camel collapsed from exhaustion while passing through a thick jungle. Lacking time, the merchant unloaded the burden onto other animals and left the camel behind.

In time, the camel recovered his strength, feeding on the rich forest grass. One afternoon, Madotkata the Lion, King of the forest, passed by accompanied by his court—a leopard, a jackal, and a crow.

Staring at the tall animal, the Lion asked, "What strange creature is that?"

"It is a camel, your Majesty," the crow answered.

The Lion approached the camel, heard his story, and granted him royal protection: "Fear not, my friend. Henceforth you shall live in my court under my protection."

Months passed harmoniously. But one day, the Lion engaged in a fierce combat with a wild elephant and was severely wounded. Unable to hunt, the Lion remained confined to his cave for weeks.

Deprived of prey, the leopard, jackal, and crow began to starve. Sitting outside the cave, the jackal whispered to the crow, "There is no need for us to starve. The camel's flesh could sustain us for days!"

"But the King has given him his protection," the crow reminded. "He will never kill the camel."

"Leave that to me," the cunning jackal smiled.

The jackal entered the cave and addressed the weak Lion: "Majesty, we cannot bear to see you starve. If you permit, you may kill the camel for food."

"Never!" the Lion roared in anger. "I have given him my word of protection!"

"What if the camel voluntarily offers his own body to you?" the jackal suggested smoothly. "Then you would commit no sin."

The Lion hesitated. "If he offers voluntarily, I might accept."

The jackal returned to his companions and assembled them before the Lion's cave. Stepping forward first, the crow bowed to the Lion: "Master, you are starving. Eat me and prolong your life!"

The jackal interrupted, "You are too small! The master's hunger would hardly be appeased by a bird. Eat me instead, Sire!"

The leopard pushed the jackal aside. "You are too small as well! Master, eat me!"

Watching this display of apparent devotion, the naive camel thought, All of them have offered their lives, yet the King has not harmed any of them. I too should make a noble gesture to show my loyalty!

Stepping into the center of the ring, the camel announced proudly, "Master! Eat me! I offer my body to sustain my King!"

Before the camel could realize his mistake, the Lion roared, "I accept your offer, noble camel!"

Instantly, the leopard, jackal, and crow pounced upon the unsuspecting camel, killing him on the spot.

Moral: Beware of false friends who use flattery to manipulate you to your doom.

docs.title: Dashavatar: The Ten Incarnations of Vishnu
docs.text:
According to ancient Hindu theology and the Bhagavata Purana, time moves in vast cosmic cycles called Kalpas, each comprising four Yugas: Satya, Treta, Dwapara, and Kali. Whenever righteousness declines and evil threatens to overwhelm the world, Lord Vishnu, the Preserver of the Universe, manifests on earth in divine incarnations (Avatars) to restore order, protect the good, and destroy evil.

The ten most significant incarnations are known as the Dashavatara, representing the evolution of cosmic consciousness from aquatic forms to enlightened humanity.

docs.title: 1. Matsya Avatar (The Fish)
docs.text:
At the end of a cosmic day of Brahma, as the creator felt eyelids droop with sleep before the Great Deluge (Pralaya), the sacred Vedas slipped from his mouth. An alert demon named Hayagriva caught the scriptures, intending to absorb their secret wisdom and hide them in the ocean depths.

Noticing the theft, Lord Vishnu took the form of a tiny, shimmering golden fish. In a river, he swam into the hands of the virtuous king Satyavrata (who would become Vaivasvata Manu) as he offered water to the gods.

"O King, do not throw me back into the river," the tiny fish pleaded. "Larger fish will devour me!"

Moved by compassion, Satyavrata placed the fish in his water pot (kamandalu). By next morning, the fish had grown to fill the entire pot. Satyavrata transferred it to a large pitcher, but within an hour, the fish outgrew the pitcher. He moved it to a lake, but the fish rapidly expanded to cover the entire lake! Finally, Satyavrata transported the giant fish to the ocean.

Surprised, Satyavrata bowed low before the ocean-spanning fish. "O Lord Narayana, forgive me! Why have you assumed this form?"

Lord Vishnu spoke in a divine voice: "Seven days from now, a catastrophic deluge shall submerge the three worlds. I shall send a large ship to you. Collect all varieties of seeds, herbs, and pairs of living creatures, and board the ship along with the Seven Sages (Saptarshis). Bring Vasuki, the King of Serpents, with you."

While Satyavrata prepared for the deluge, Matsya dove into the ocean depths, confronted the demon Hayagriva, slew him, and rescued the sacred Vedas to restore them to Lord Brahma upon his awakening.

When the skies darkened and tidal waves swallowed the earth, the spacious boat appeared. Satyavrata, the Saptarshis, and the creatures boarded the vessel. Lord Matsya emerged from the roaring waves, now bearing a single golden horn. Using Vasuki as a rope, Satyavrata tethered the ship to Matsya's horn. Throughout the long cosmic night of Pralaya, Matsya guided the ark through the stormy waters, imparting divine spiritual wisdom to Manu and the sages—teachings preserved today as the Matsya Purana.

docs.title: 2. Kurma Avatar (The Turtle)
docs.text:
In the sixth Manvantara, Sage Durvasa cursed Indra and the Devas to lose all their vigor and prosperity after Indra disrespected a sacred flower garland. Weakened by the curse, the Devas were defeated by the Asuras (demons) led by King Bali.

Seeking Lord Vishnu's help, the Devas were advised to form a temporary truce with the Asuras to churn the Ocean of Milk (Samudra Manthan) for Amrita, the Nectar of Immortality.

To churn the vast ocean, they uprooted Mount Mandara as a churning rod and wrapped the serpent Vasuki around it as a rope. But as the churning began, the heavy mountain sank into the soft ocean floor.

Lord Vishnu assumed the form of Kurma, a colossal turtle whose shell was as vast and hard as a continent. Diving to the ocean floor, Kurma supported Mount Mandara upon his back, serving as the steady pivot around which the mountain whirled.

When the deadly poison Halahala emerged, Lord Shiva drank it to save the universe, turning his throat blue (Neelakantha). As the churning continued, divine treasures emerged, including Goddess Lakshmi, who chose Lord Vishnu as her consort. Finally, Dhanvantari emerged with the pot of Amrita. When the Asuras snatched the pot, Vishnu assumed the form of the enchantress Mohini, outwitting the demons and distributing the Nectar to the Devas, securing their eternal victory and immortality.

docs.title: 3. Varaha Avatar (The Boar)
docs.text:
At the beginning of the new Kalpa, as Lord Brahma began the work of creation, a mighty demon named Hiranyaksha cast Mother Earth (Bhoomidevi) into the murky depths of the cosmic ocean.

Distressed, Brahma prayed to Lord Vishnu for aid. As Brahma meditated, a tiny white boar no larger than a thumb emerged from one of his nostrils. Right before the eyes of the astonished sages, the boar grew rapidly—first to the size of an elephant, then to the size of a colossal mountain, roaring with thunderous power.

Lord Vishnu as Varaha leaped high into the sky, tore through the clouds, and plunged into the fathomless cosmic ocean. Diving deep into the dark waters, Varaha located Bhoomidevi, lifted her gently upon his mighty tusks, and began ascending toward the surface.

Enraged by the rescue, Hiranyaksha confronted the boar in the ocean depths with a heavy mace. "Leave Bhoomidevi alone, you wild beast!" the demon roared.

Ignoring his taunts, Varaha placed Bhoomidevi safely upon the ocean surface, blessing her to support all life. Turning to face Hiranyaksha, Varaha engaged the demon in a fierce mace battle. As the hour of twilight approached, Varaha struck Hiranyaksha behind the ears, crushing the demon and restoring peace to the universe.

docs.title: 4. Narasimha Avatar (The Man-Lion)
docs.text:
Hiranyakashipu, the elder brother of the slain Hiranyaksha, swore vengeance against Lord Vishnu. Retiring to Mount Mandara, he performed severe penances for thousands of years until Lord Brahma granted him an extraordinary boon: he could not be killed by man or beast, indoors or outdoors, by day or by night, on land, in water, or in the air, nor by any weapon created by hands.

Armored with near-invincibility, Hiranyakashipu conquered heaven and earth, outlawing the worship of Vishnu and terrorizing all three worlds.

However, his own young son, Prahlad, grew up as an ardent devotee of Lord Vishnu. Infuriated by Prahlad's devotion, Hiranyakashipu subjected his son to terrible tortures—throwing him off cliffs, hurling him into snake pits, and feeding him poison—but Prahlad remained unharmed through Vishnu's grace.

Finally, in a rage, Hiranyakashipu dragged Prahlad into his palace hall at dusk. "Where is your Vishnu?" the tyrant roared, pointing to a stone pillar. "Is he in this pillar?"

"He is everywhere, Father," Prahlad replied calmly. "Even in this pillar."

"Then let him save you now!" Hiranyakashipu shouted, striking the pillar with his fist.

With a deafening crash that shook the heavens, the pillar split open, and Lord Vishnu emerged as Narasimha—a terrifying form with the body of a man and the head and claws of a lion!

Narasimha seized Hiranyakashipu, dragged him to the threshold of the palace doorway (neither indoors nor outdoors), laid him across his lap (neither on land nor in the sky), at the hour of twilight (neither day nor night), and tore the tyrant open with his sharp claws (using no artificial weapon). Thus, without violating Brahma's boon, Lord Vishnu destroyed the demon king and protected his beloved devotee Prahlad.

docs.title: 5. Vamana Avatar (The Dwarf Brahman)
docs.text:
Generations later, King Bali, the grandson of Prahlad, performed the sacred Vishwajit sacrifice under Guru Shukracharya, acquiring celestial weapons and conquering the three worlds. Forced out of heaven, Aditi, mother of the Devas, performed the twelve-day Payovrata vow to propitiate Lord Vishnu.

Pleased by her devotion, Lord Vishnu took birth as her son, taking the form of Vamana, a short-statured, radiant young Brahman holding a wooden umbrella and a water pot (kamandalu).

Vamana walked to the sacrificial grounds where King Bali was conducting a series of horse sacrifices. Struck by the youth's luminous aura, King Bali welcomed him warmly. "O holy youth, ask of me whatever you desire—cows, gold, land, or palatial houses!"

Vamana smiled humbly. "I seek only a small strip of land—three paces long, as measured by my stride."

"You are too young to understand your own interest," Bali laughed. "Ask for a whole kingdom!" But Vamana insisted that three paces were all he needed.

When Bali prepared to pour consecrating water to grant the gift, his guru Shukracharya realized the truth. "Stop, Bali! This dwarf is Lord Vishnu himself in disguise! Do not grant the gift!"

"I have given my word," King Bali replied honorably. "I will not prove a cheat."

As the water touched Vamana's palm, the dwarf began to expand! Growing beyond the clouds, Vamana assumed his cosmic form (Trivikrama). With his first stride, he covered the entire earth; with his second stride, he covered the heavens and upper realms.

Turning to Bali, Vamana asked, "Where shall I place my third stride?"

Recognizing the Lord, King Bali bent his head in complete surrender. "O Lord, place your third step upon my head, which is more valuable to me than all my kingdoms."

Pleased by Bali's humility and unshakeable truthfulness, Lord Vishnu placed his foot upon Bali's head, pressing him down to rule over the netherworld (Sutala), promising him the throne of Indra in the next Manvantara.

docs.title: 6. Parashurama Avatar (Warrior with the Axe)
docs.text:
In the Treta Yuga, the warrior caste (Kshatriyas) grew corrupt and tyrannical under rulers like Kartavirya Arjuna, king of the Haihayas, who misused his thousand arms to oppress the people.

To curb their arrogance, Lord Vishnu was born as Rama (popularly known as Parashurama), the youngest son of Sage Jamadagni and Renuka. From childhood, Parashurama possessed an innate mastery over weapons, favoring a mighty battle-axe (parashu) granted by Lord Shiva.

One day, while Parashurama was away, King Kartavirya Arjuna visited Jamadagni's hermitage. The sage hospitably fed the king's entire army using his divine cow, Kamadhenu. Overcome by greed, the king forcibly abducted Kamadhenu and her calf.

Returning home, an enraged Parashurama marched to Mahishmati, destroyed Kartavirya Arjuna's seventeen akshauhini armies single-handed, and severed the tyrant's thousand arms with his axe.

Later, in revenge, the sons of Kartavirya Arjuna raided the defenseless hermitage in Parashurama's absence and murdered the meditating Sage Jamadagni.

Finding his grieving mother beating her breast twenty-one times over his father's body, Parashurama swore a terrifying oath: "I shall rid the earth of corrupt Kshatriya tyrants twenty-one times!"

Gripping his battle-axe, Parashurama waged twenty-one campaigns across India, destroying oppressive rulers and restoring moral order before retiring to the Mahendra mountains to engage in penance.

docs.title: 7. Rama Avatar (The Prince of Ayodhya)
docs.text:
When the ten-headed demon king Ravana of Lanka secured boons from Brahma making him invincible against gods and demons, his reign of terror threatened the cosmos. Because Ravana had disdainfully omitted humans from his boon of invincibility, Lord Vishnu manifested as Rama, the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya.

Accompanied by his devoted wife Sita and loyal brother Lakshmana, Rama embraced fourteen years of forest exile to fulfill his father's sacred promise to Queen Kaikeyi.

In the forest, after Lakshmana punished the demoness Shoorpanakha for attempting to kill Sita, Ravana abducted Sita by trickery, carrying her to his island fortress of Lanka.

Forming a historic alliance with Sugreeva, King of the Monkeys, and the devoted Hanuman, Rama built a stone bridge (Rama Setu) across the ocean to Lanka. In a monumental war, Rama slew Ravana and his demon armies, rescued Sita, and returned to Ayodhya to be crowned King, establishing the golden era of Ramrajya—a reign defined by absolute justice and truth.

docs.title: 8. Krishna Avatar (The Divine Statesman)
docs.text:
As the Dwapara Yuga drew to a close, tyrannical kings like Kamsa of Mathura, Jarasandha of Magadha, and Shishupala oppressed Mother Earth. Lord Vishnu descended as Krishna, the eighth son of Vasudeva and Devaki, raised in the pastoral village of Gokul by Nanda and Yashoda.

After slaying Kamsa, Putana, and numerous demons in his youth, Krishna established the golden city of Dwaraka. As a master statesman, philosopher, and friend, Krishna guided the righteous Pandavas through their trials against the Kauravas.

On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, serving as Arjuna's charioteer, Krishna delivered the immortal discourse of the Bhagavad Gita, revealing his cosmic form (Vishvarupa) and instructing mankind on Duty (Dharma), Knowledge (Jnana), and Devotion (Bhakti). Following the great war, Krishna brought an end to the corrupt Yadava dynasty before returning to Vaikuntha, marking the beginning of the Kali Yuga.

docs.title: 9. Buddha Avatar (The Teacher of Compassion)
docs.text:
In the dark Age of Kali, true religion was obscured by empty rituals, animal sacrifices, and caste oppression. Lord Vishnu descended as Prince Siddhartha of Kapilavastu.

Renouncing his royal throne, wife Yashodhara, and son Rahula, Siddhartha sought the truth behind human suffering. Attaining Enlightenment under the Bodhi tree in Gaya, he became the Buddha.

Buddha preached compassion (Ahimsa), taught that desire is the root of sorrow, and abolished animal sacrifices, guiding millions across Asia toward the Middle Path and Nirvana.

docs.title: 10. Kalki Avatar (The Future Savior)
docs.text:
As the Kali Yuga reaches its absolute nadir, darkness, corruption, and injustice will consume society. Corrupt leaders will plunder the innocent, and virtue will wither away.

Lord Vishnu will manifest as Kalki, riding a magnificent white horse named Devadatta, wielding a blazing sword of righteousness. Kalki will exterminate corrupt oppressors, destroy evil across the globe, and restore moral order, inaugurating a brand new Satya Yuga of truth, peace, and divine harmony.

docs.title: Dayananda: His Strength Was His Virtue
docs.text:
On the night of Maha Shivaratri in 1839, in the town of Tankara near Morvi in Gujarat, fourteen-year-old Mool Shankar kept a strict vigil alongside his father, Karshanji Tiwari, in the local Shiva temple. As midnight approached, the tired worshippers dozed off one by one.

Alone and watchful, young Mool Shankar stared at the Shiva Linga. Suddenly, he saw a swarm of rats scurry across the sacred stone, boldly devouring the food offerings.

What is this? the boy thought in shock. If this idol cannot even protect its own offerings from mere rodents, how can it be the Lord of the Universe?

Awakening his father, Mool Shankar asked, "Father, why do we worship an idol that is helpless against rats?"

"Stop asking foolish questions!" his father snapped. "Repeat the mantra!"

Unable to quell the doubt in his heart, Mool Shankar slipped out of the temple and walked home.

A few years later, cholera struck Tankara, claiming the lives of Mool Shankar's beloved younger sister and his uncle. Watching his family weep in agony, the nineteen-year-old youth was deeply shaken by the reality of death. Is death the end of all? he pondered. I must find the immortal Truth and the means to liberate mankind from sorrow.

When his father insisted on arranging his marriage, twenty-one-year-old Mool Shankar left a quiet farewell note for his parents and escaped into the night, renouncing his family's vast wealth.

Wandering as an ascetic across India, he took the vow of lifelong celibacy (Naishthik Brahmachari) under a wandering monk who named him Shuddha Chaitanya. Later, on the banks of the sacred Narmada River, Swami Poornananda initiated him into the order of sanyasa, bestowing upon him the name Swami Dayananda Saraswati ("One who delights in compassion").

Dayananda traveled extensively through Kashmir, the Himalayas, and Kashi, studying Yoga and scriptures. Wherever he went, his heart bled for the suffering masses—oppressed by rigid caste discrimination, child marriage, superstition, and religious exploitation by corrupt priests.

At the edge of a Himalayan cliff, overcome by desolation, Dayananda contemplated leaping into the abyss. But a voice within stopped him: Dying in desolation serves no purpose. Why not use this body to lighten the misery of the world?

In Mathura, thirty-six-year-old Dayananda sought out Swami Virjananda, a blind, saintly master of Vedic Sanskrit. Virjananda looked at the books of medieval grammar Dayananda carried and said, "Throw those secondary books into the Yamuna and come back to me. The original Vedas are the only pure source of knowledge!"

For two and a half years, Dayananda served his blind Guru with unwavering devotion, fetching water, sweeping the floor, and mastering the Vedas. When his studies were completed, Dayananda offered a small bowl of cloves as his Guru Dakshina.

Swami Virjananda pushed the cloves aside gently. "Dayananda, I ask for a different dakshina. Go into the world and spread the pure light of the Vedas to a weary nation. That will be the most fitting gift to your teacher."

Fired by his Guru's command, Swami Dayananda launched a relentless campaign for social and spiritual reform. At the Haridwar Kumbh Mela in 1867, he raised the banner of Vedic revival before thousands of pilgrims.

"Idol worship, untouchability, child marriage, and the oppression of women have no sanction in the Vedas!" Swami Dayananda proclaimed in his thunderous voice. "God is one, formless, and omnipresent! All human beings—regardless of caste or gender—have equal right to education and the sacred thread of initiation!"

His revolutionary teachings outraged conservative orthodoxy. Reactionary priests threw stones, mud, and insults at him. In Anupshahar, an opponent handed him a poisoned pan. Using his advanced Yogic techniques of internal cleansing (Kriyas), Dayananda regurgitated the poison unharmed.

When the local British magistrate arrested the culprit, Dayananda requested his immediate release: "I have come to free men from bondage, not to imprison them. Forgive him."

On October 22, 1869, the famous Kashi Shastrartha (great debate) was organized in Varanasi. Twenty-seven renowned orthodox pundits lined up to challenge the lone reformer before a crowd of thousands. With unmatched erudition, Dayananda cited original Vedic texts, refuting every argument for idol worship and birth-based caste. When rowdies disrupted the assembly, the Maharaja of Kashi later visited Dayananda's camp to apologize.

On April 10, 1875, Swami Dayananda established the Arya Samaj in Bombay to unite Indians across caste barriers, promote social justice, and inspire national self-respect. In the same year, he authored his masterpiece, Satyartha Prakash ("The Light of Truth").

Traveling through Punjab, Swamiji won millions of followers. Possessing superhuman physical strength, he once bare-handedly grabbed the wooden wheel of a fast-moving horse carriage belonging to a arrogant nobleman, bringing the galloping horses to a dead halt!

In 1883, Swami Dayananda visited the princely state of Jodhpur. Observing Maharaja Jaswant Singh spending his time in debauchery with a courtesan named Nanhijan, Swamiji fearlessly admonished the King: "You are a king, a lion among men! Why do you waste your life in a dancer's den?"

Ashamed, the Maharaja reformed his ways. But the enraged courtesan, backed by British spies suspicious of Dayananda's nationalist influence, bribed the royal cook, Jagannath, to mix crushed glass into Swamiji's nightly glass of milk.

Swamiji drank the milk and fell mortally ill. When the cook broke down in tears of repentance at his bedside, Dayananda showed boundless compassion. Handing the cook a bag of gold coins, Swamiji whispered, "Run away before the Maharaja finds out, or you will be executed."

Shifted to Ajmer for treatment, Swami Dayananda's condition deteriorated. On October 30, 1883, as the sun set over the hills of Ajmer, the great reformer opened his eyes, smiled serenely, and recited his final prayer: "O Lord, May Thy Will be done! Om!" before taking his last breath.

Swami Dayananda Saraswati's legacy of fearless truth, educational reform, and national awakening laid the foundational bedrock for India's freedom struggle.

docs.title: True Friendship
docs.text:
In a thicket near a lake, lived three friends: a tortoise, a woodpecker, and an antelope.

One day, a hunter came that way. "Ah! Footprints!" he exclaimed. "I'll set the trap right here. I'll come back for him tomorrow."

At dawn, the antelope went to the lake. "It's good to be alive," he thought. "I must have a drink." Suddenly, he stepped into the snare and was caught. "Help!!" he cried out in terror.

The tortoise and the woodpecker heard his cries and rushed to his side. The woodpecker quickly came up with a plan. "I'll tell you what," he said to the tortoise, "you have strong teeth. You free him while I keep the hunter away. When he comes out of his hut, I'll give him a good pecking."

"Will you please hurry?" the antelope pleaded as the tortoise began gnawing at the tough leather trap.

"The tortoise was as slow as a tortoise could be," the woodpecker noted as he flew off towards the hunter's hut.

Meanwhile, the hunter had heard the antelope's shouts for help and rushed out. The woodpecker spied him and zoomed down, striking him right on the head. "Screeeeeeech!! Ouch!!!" the hunter yelled, dropping his sword. "Ah... ah, he's caught. I'd better get indoors."

Once inside, the hunter thought, "I've got it, I'll try to go out by the back door."

But the clever woodpecker was already waiting. "I bet he will try to get out from the back door." As the hunter opened the back door, the bird struck again. "Ouch!!!"

"Hmm, it looks safe this side," the hunter muttered, trying the front door again. But as he had expected, the woodpecker was there. Wack! With a groan, the hunter crashed back into his hut.

Leaving him there, the woodpecker flew back to his friends. "Hey friend Tortoise, you had better hurry. He'll be here any minute," he warned.

"I am trying my best," the tortoise panted. Finally, with a loud thud, the trap broke.

"Come on. Let's get out of here," the antelope urged. But the poor tortoise, as usual, was too slow.

The hunter arrived just in time to see the tortoise. "Ah, so you are the culprit. You freed my antelope," he sneered. "I'll teach you not to interfere. Since you robbed me of my dinner... I'll put you in this sack, take you home, and eat you." He shoved the tortoise into his sack.

Seeing this, the antelope knew it was his turn to save his friend. He deliberately showed himself to the hunter. "There goes my antelope," the hunter thought.

"If he sees me, he will chase me, and my friend can escape," the antelope planned.

The greedy hunter dropped his sack and ran after the antelope. "I'll lead him into my favourite old cave," the antelope decided.

He dashed into the dark cave, and the hunter followed blindly. "Huh—where did he go?" the hunter wondered, completely lost in the darkness. "Once he is in, he will not know how to get out," the antelope thought. "What a fool! He'll be trapped in the cave. He's done for."

The antelope rushed back to the spot where the tortoise had been abandoned. "Don't worry, my friend, I'll soon get you out of the bag!" Using his antlers, he ripped the sack open.

As the tortoise crawled out, the woodpecker flew down from the tree. "What happened to the hunter?" asked the tortoise.

"Ha! Ha! Ha! He is running around in circles in our old cave!" the antelope laughed, and the three friends rejoiced in their safety.

docs.title: Ruru, The Golden Deer
docs.text:
Mahadhanaka was the son of a rich merchant of Varanasi. His father, instead of sending him to school, spoilt him with all the pleasures of the world. "Enjoy yourself, my son! I can give you everything," his father would say.

"I know, father!" Mahadhanaka replied happily.

Since Mahadhanaka had never handled money before, he did not know how to use it responsibly. "Use the most expensive cloth you have, for our clothes," he commanded the tailors. "Let us have some more liquor and play another round of cards," he told his freeloading friends.

When he came of age, Mahadhanaka was married to a beautiful girl. "Your mother has cooked this for you, my lord," his wife said gently.

"Hmm... smells delicious!" he remarked, living a life of utter luxury.

Soon his parents died, and he was left alone to manage the estate. "Alas! Who will look after me now?" he wept. But his friends were only after his wealth.

"Come on Mahadhanaka, cheer up," they urged. "Let us have a game of dice and some wine to warm us up."

It didn't take long for him to squander his inheritance. One day, he opened his treasure chest and found it empty. "What! No money! If I don't do something, I'll soon have no friends either." Then an idea struck him. "Yes, that's it. I'll borrow some money and pay it back later."

He went from one money-lender to another and promised them all the same thing: "I'll return your money as soon as I become rich again."

Months passed. When Mahadhanaka showed no sign of returning the money, the lenders grew furious. "You had better pay up soon, Mahadhanaka, or else..." they threatened.

"Have patience. I will," he stammered. But he knew he would never be able to repay the money. "I would rather die than face the money-lenders," he thought in despair.

Then one day, Mahadhanaka led all his creditors to the bank of a river. "Where are you leading us?" they asked suspiciously.

"I have some treasure hidden in the river bed. I will return all that I have borrowed from you," he lied. As they reached the edge, he thought, "Now is my chance to slip away, and drown myself. That will be the end to all my sorrows." With a loud Splash!, he jumped into the raging river. "Help! Glub! Gasp! Help!" he panicked, regretting his decision as the water swallowed him.

Not very far away, on the opposite bank, lived Ruru, a magnificent golden deer. "Somebody's drowning. I must save him," thought Ruru when he heard the cries. Ruru dove into the water and swam to him. "Ho! Man. I will save your life. Hold on." He dragged the heavy man to the safety of the shore.

"How do you feel?" Ruru asked.

"Much better. Thank you," Mahadhanaka coughed, amazed by the talking golden deer.

"Before parting, promise me you will tell no one about me and where I live," Ruru requested.

"I promise, friend," Mahadhanaka swore.

Meanwhile, that very night in Varanasi, Khema, the Queen, had a vivid dream. She woke up the King in a flurry. "What is it, my queen?"

"I dreamt that a golden deer preached dharma to me," she said. "If such a deer exists, I would like to hear its discourse."

"If there is such an animal, you will," the King assured her. The King sent for his councillors the next morning. "Are there such animals as golden deer?"

"Yes, your majesty. There are," they replied.

The King announced a massive reward: a sack of gold coins placed in a gold pot on an elephant's back. A town crier was sent to tell the people of the reward. Just then, Mahadhanaka strolled by and heard the announcement. "What did you say, my friend?" he asked the crier.

"The king is offering a large reward to any man who can tell him of the whereabouts of a golden deer."

Mahadhanaka's eyes widened with greed. "Hmm, I shall soon be rich again," he schemed, completely forgetting his promise to Ruru. He hurried to the palace.

"Your majesty, this man says he knows where a golden deer may be found," a courtier presented him.

"Is that so? When can you lead us to him?" the King asked eagerly.

"At the break of dawn, your majesty."

At dawn, the royal hunting party set off. "I hope you know the way," the king warned.

"There, your majesty," Mahadhanaka pointed, "in that grove of mango and sal trees, lives the golden deer you want."

"Surround the grove," the King ordered his soldiers.

"I'll stand a little way off and see what happens," Mahadhanaka cowardishly thought, slinking away.

When Ruru heard the noise of the beaters, he realized what was happening. "That's a hunting party, I'm sure. How did they find this grove?" Instead of running, Ruru stepped out and confronted the king.

The king was astonished to hear Ruru's calm, human-like voice. "I promise I won't shoot. Come out of your hiding place," the King said, lowering his bow.

"What is it you want to know?" Ruru asked gracefully. "Your majesty, who told you about me and where I live?"

The King pointed to Mahadhanaka, who was hiding behind a tree. "He did."

Ruru sighed deeply. "I thought as much. It would have been better to save a log of wood from the river than a man like him."

"What do you mean by that?" the King asked, puzzled.

"Your majesty, I once saved this man from drowning in the river, and this is how he has repaid me."

The King's face turned red with anger. "Is that so? He shall be hanged for it!" the King declared, glaring at the trembling Mahadhanaka.

But Ruru intervened. "No, don't do that. I don't want him to die because of me."

The King was moved by the deer's compassion. "You are lucky," the King told Mahadhanaka with disgust. "But for him I would have had you hanged. Now go away."

The King then turned to Ruru with folded hands. "Ask for anything, and I'll grant it to you."

"Your majesty, from now on, let no animal or bird be harmed by anyone in your kingdom," Ruru requested.

The King immediately sent around a proclamation throughout his lands: "No living creature big or small shall henceforth be harmed. Should anyone harm them he shall be severely punished."

Ruru then went with the king to the palace, where he discoursed dharma to the queen just as she had dreamt, and then peacefully returned to his forest.

docs.title: The Hunter Outwitted
docs.text:
There once lived a wise stag in the forest near Rajagriha. One day, his sister came to him, accompanied by her young son.

"Brother, will you teach your nephew the tricks of our herd?" she asked.

"Certainly," the stag replied kindly. He turned to the young deer. "Go home now, my boy, and come back tomorrow."

"I wonder what uncle will teach me," the young deer thought excitedly the next day. For two months, the little deer went to his uncle every day to learn the ways of the forest and the secrets of survival.

One day, while wandering through the forest with his friends, disaster struck. Clack!

"Help! I'm caught! A...a...ah!" the young deer cried out in panic as he stepped right into a hunter's trap. His friends, terrified, fled back to the herd.

"Your son has been caught in a hunter's trap," they reported to his mother.

"Oh dear, what shall I do?" the mother doe cried in anguish. She hurried to her brother, the wise stag. "Brother, please help me. My son has been trapped!" she sobbed.

"Do not fear, my sister," the stag comforted her.

Meanwhile, trapped in the snare, the young deer panicked. But then he calmed down. "I must remember what uncle taught me," he thought. "I must lie stiff and pretend to be dead."

He held his breath and stiffened his limbs. He pretended so well that even the flies were fooled. "Poor dear. He's dead," they buzzed, swarming around him.

Along came the hunter, spear in hand. "I hope an animal has been trapped today," he muttered. When he saw the young deer, his eyes lit up. "Aha! It's a lucky day for me. He must have died this morning. He's stiff already."

The hunter confidently opened the trap to free the carcass. "I'll cut him up and take the flesh home. My wife will be pleased," he schemed. "On second thoughts, I'll roast some of the flesh and eat it here."

Leaving the seemingly dead deer on the ground, he turned his back and went to collect some wood to make a fire. As soon as the hunter was distracted, the young deer sprang to his feet. "Hee..hee..hee..." he chuckled silently, bounding away into the forest at lightning speed.

The hunter turned around to see his dinner escaping. "Come back! Come back!" he yelled furiously, but it was too late.

The young deer arrived safely back at the herd. "My son, you are back! How did you escape?" his mother wept with joy.

The wise stag smiled proudly at his nephew. "He was a good student!"

docs.title: Caution Pays
docs.text:
In a dense forest near Varanasi, there lived an antelope which was very fond of the sweet fruits of a particular tree.

In a village nearby lived a hunter, who had noticed the antelope's habits. Early one morning, the hunter went into the jungle. "Hmm, he is a big animal. I will get quite some money by selling his meat," the hunter schemed. He climbed the fruit tree, set up a 'machan' (a hidden platform), and waited for the antelope. "He should be here any moment."

As usual, the antelope came strolling along the forest path. "Mm...m...m... Just thinking of that fruit makes me hungry," he thought happily. Suddenly, he stopped in his tracks, his keen eyes spotting something unusual on the ground. "Hey! A man's footprints!"

"There must be a hunter nearby, waiting to kill me," he reasoned cautiously. He looked around but couldn't see anyone.

Up in the tree, the impatient hunter wondered, "Why doesn't he come near? I shall try throwing some fruit to him. Maybe that will lure him to the tree." He plucked a fruit and tossed it down.

The fruit landed near the antelope. "Oh oh, there is someone up the tree," the antelope realized. "The fruit is being thrown to me. It isn't dropping straight down as it usually does." He carefully examined the canopy above. "Yes. Now I am sure. I can see the corner of a 'machan'."

The clever antelope decided to play a trick. "I'll pretend to talk to the tree and see what happens," he thought. He looked up and spoke loudly. "Listen, tree. You have always dropped your fruit straight down on the earth. Why do you throw it at me today?"

There was complete silence from the tree.

"Hmmm. No answer. He thinks he is smart, does he?" the antelope chuckled to himself. He spoke again, louder this time. "And so tree, since you have stopped behaving like a normal tree, I too will behave differently. I shall eat my breakfast elsewhere!"

Realizing that his cover was blown and he had lost his chance, the frustrated hunter threw his javelin at the antelope with all his might. Wooosh!

The javelin embedded itself in the dirt, completely missing its target. "Hee...hee, too bad, hunter! Better luck next time," the antelope laughed, bounding away safely into the thick forest.

docs.title: The Golden Antelope
docs.text:
Long ago, there lived a king in Varanasi who had a royal gardener named Sanjaya. Every day, a wild antelope came from the forest to graze in the lush royal garden.

"Poor antelope!" Sanjaya would think as the creature bolted. "Every time he sees me, he runs away in fright."

But as time went by, the antelope became accustomed to seeing Sanjaya quietly tending the plants and stopped running away.

One day, while Sanjaya was taking a basket of fresh fruit and flowers to the palace, the king stopped him. "Have you noticed anything strange in the garden, Sanjaya?" the king asked.

"Nothing, your majesty, except that a wild antelope comes to graze there from time to time," Sanjaya reported.

The king was intrigued. "Do you think you could catch him?"

"Oh yes, your majesty," Sanjaya smiled. "If I had a little honey, I could bring him right into your palace."

The king immediately ordered some honey to be given to Sanjaya.

Armed with a pot of honey, Sanjaya returned to the garden. "I shall coat the grass where he grazes with honey, then hide myself and see what happens," he planned. He carefully spread the honey on a patch of grass and hid behind a tree.

Soon, along came the antelope. He took a bite of the grass and his eyes widened. "Hmm. This grass tastes exceptionally sweet today!" he thought, happily munching away.

From behind the tree, Sanjaya chuckled silently. "So my plan has worked. Now for the second stage. Hee hee."

The antelope became so ensnared by the taste of the sweetened grass that he refused to eat anywhere else. Knowing this, Sanjaya decided to escalate his plan. "Hmm. Now to show myself gradually," he thought.

The next day, Sanjaya stepped out into the open while the antelope was eating. At first, the antelope was startled at his appearance. "Huh?" he snorted, ready to run. But Sanjaya held out a handful of the honey-coated grass.

Soon, the antelope's lust for the sweet taste overcame his natural fear, and he was eating straight from Sanjaya's hand.

Day by day, Sanjaya lured the antelope closer and closer to the palace gates using the sweet grass. "He hasn't even noticed we are near the palace," Sanjaya thought. "Now I must lead him towards the palace."

He dropped a trail of sweetened grass leading right into the grand hall of the palace. The oblivious antelope followed the trail inside. Once the animal was fully inside, Sanjaya quickly shut the heavy wooden door behind him. Slam!

Trapped! At the sight of so many palace guards and courtiers, the antelope realized his mistake. Trembling with sheer fright, he dashed frantically to and fro, looking for an escape.

Hearing the commotion, the king came down from his chamber. Seeing the panicked, terrified animal, the king's heart filled with pity. "Antelopes are so timid that they never revisit a place for at least a week if they have seen a man there," the king observed. "Sometimes they never go back again."

He looked at the honey grass scattered on the floor. "But this antelope was carried away by the taste of the sweetened grass and completely forgot himself and his natural instincts," the king said thoughtfully. "Surely there is nothing viler in the world than the lust of taste. By falling into this trap, you have taught us all a valuable lesson about greed."

The king then ordered the doors to be opened. "Go my friend," the king said softly. "You are free to go wherever you please."

With a leap, the antelope rushed out of the palace and bounded back into the safety of the forest, never to be lured by sweet tastes again.

docs.title: The Banyan Deer
docs.text:
In two different parts of a vast forest lived two handsome golden stags. One was called the Banyan Deer, and the other was known as the Branch Deer. Each led a large herd of its own.

In the nearby city of Varanasi, there lived a King who was obsessively fond of hunting deer. His love for the sport was so great that he constantly disturbed his townsfolk. "Come on, everyone, let's go on a hunt!" he would announce, dragging the villagers away from their farms and daily chores.

Tired of the constant interruptions, the people got together and hit upon a plan. "Let us chase all the deer into the king's garden," they decided. "Then he will have all the deer he wants right in his backyard, and he will not disturb us at odd hours to go into the forest."

They gathered sticks, pots, pans, and anything that made a loud noise. The next day, they surrounded the forest and created a massive racket. Bang! Clang! Thump! Startled by the noise, they successfully drove all the deer, including the herds of the Banyan and Branch Deer, right into the king's sprawling walled garden.
