"There! Now he will not disturb us with so many deer so near him," the villagers cheered. A few townsmen then went to the King. "Sir, you distract us from work when you go hunting; so we have driven all the deer into your garden. You may shoot them whenever you wish."

The King was delighted. "I must warn my men to leave the two golden stags alone, as they are too beautiful to kill," he instructed his royal archers.

And so, the King would simply step into his garden and shoot a deer whenever he pleased. Sometimes, the head cook would accompany him. "Look, my lord! There goes a plump one," the cook would point. But if it was one of the golden stags, the King would say, "Not him. He is a golden stag."

However, the deer were being hunted in a chaotic and terrifying manner, causing injuries and panic as the herd stampeded. Seeing his brethren being slain in such large numbers and in such fear, the wise Banyan Deer decided to do something about it. He approached the Branch Deer.

"My friend, our deer cannot escape death in this walled garden, but let them not be killed needlessly in terrifying stampedes," the Banyan Deer proposed. "We will send them to the execution block by turns—one from my herd, one from yours—each day."

The Branch Deer agreed. In this way, the deer whose turn it was would willingly go to the block and peacefully wait for the royal cook to take him away. "Hmmm, this is a nice plump one today," the cook would say, simply taking the waiting deer.

One day, it was the lot of a doe from the Branch Deer's herd. She was heavily pregnant and about to become a mother. She went to her leader, the Branch Deer, and pleaded, "My lord, let me escape this time. When I have had my little one, there will be two of us to go to the block. Please spare my unborn child."

"No, no, I cannot do this. It is your turn. Go away," the Branch Deer coldly dismissed her.

Desperate, the pregnant doe went to the Banyan Deer and tearfully told him her story.

The Banyan Deer's heart melted with compassion. "Very well. You don't go this time. I will do something about it," he promised her.

That day, the Banyan Deer himself walked to the block and placed his golden head upon it.

When the cook came along, he was shocked to see the majestic leader at the block. "Oh, oh! One of the golden deer! I must turn back and tell the king."

When the King heard the news, he was completely surprised. He rushed to the spot in his royal chariot. "Why are you here?" he asked the Banyan Deer in disbelief. "I granted you immunity. You are free to live."

"Your majesty," the Banyan Deer replied calmly, "a doe who was about to become a mother came to me and asked to be spared so her fawn could live. It was her turn, but I could not let her die. So, I have come in her place. Please spare her."

The King was astounded at such profound love and self-sacrifice from an animal. He was deeply moved. "Both of you shall live. You deserve it."

"If you can spare our lives," the Banyan Deer asked respectfully, "can't you spare the lives of all the deer, your majesty?"

The King, his heart forever changed by the stag's nobility, smiled. "I can! I will!"

"And couldn't you spare the lives of all the creatures in your forest, your majesty?" the Banyan Deer pressed gently.

The King thought for a while, looking out over his vast kingdom. "All right! From this day, there shall be no hunting or killing of any kind in my kingdom!"

And so, true to the King's word, all the animals in the forest lived in peace and harmony for a long time to come, safe under the gentle wisdom of the Banyan Deer.

docs.title: Dhola And Maru
docs.text:
In the desert kingdom of Pugal, Princess Maru often woke up from the same vivid dream. "How handsome he looked! And the tenderness in his eyes..." she would sigh.

Her friends would gather around her, amused but concerned. "But Maru, you and Dhola were married when you were only one and a half and he three! And you haven't seen him since. How can you love him?"

Maru would shake her head softly. "Although I have not seen him since... my heart remembers him every moment. It is not prepared to forget him." Desperate for news of her husband, she would plead with the birds and the wind to carry her messages to the kingdom of Narwar, but to no avail.

Meanwhile, Maru's father met with a horse-trader who had journeyed far and wide. "Have your travels ever taken you to Narwar?" the King asked hopefully. "It's time Prince Dhola sent for my Maru."

The trader hesitated, his face falling. "Yes, sir. Prince Dhola has... has married again. His bride is Malwani, daughter of the powerful King of Malwa."

When Maru heard the news, she collapsed in grief. "It can't be true... it can't be true..." she wept bitterly in her mother's arms.

Her father quickly realized what had happened. Narwar had secured a political alliance with Malwa, and the powerful Princess Malwani had made sure that all messengers from Pugal were thrown into prison so Dhola would never remember his childhood bride.

"Then why don't we send some bards to Narwar to remind Dhola of Maru?" a councillor suggested.

It was an excellent idea. Maru summoned her most trusted bards. "Go, good bards, and enter Narwar by any means. Sing sad songs of love and separation to Dhola. Sing about the bride of his childhood," she instructed them, tears streaming down her face. "Tell him that I am like coal, burning for him. Tell him that he can come and remove my ashes."

The bards travelled to Narwar and found a spot in the palace gardens. They began to strum their instruments and sing. Up in the palace, Princess Malwani heard the music and panicked. "Bards? Here in the palace grounds? Shall I send them away?" she asked Dhola nervously.

"No! Their strumming is tuneful and soothing, Malwani. Let them be," Dhola replied, entranced by the melody.

The next day, as Dhola sat alone, the bards sang their true message. "There once was a child named Dhola... whose father was the King of Narwar, and at Pushkar Lake many years ago... the hand of a baby princess was given unto Prince Dhola."

Hearing this, Dhola clutched his head as the memories flooded back. "Oh God! Is it possible?" He rushed down to the bards. "Tell me about it. I must know. I am Prince Dhola!"

The bards confirmed everything. Dhola realized he had another wife waiting for him, pining away in Pugal. He returned to Malwani, distracted and deeply troubled.

"Malwani, I want to leave Narwar for a while," Dhola said carefully. "I'll go to Idar and buy some of those famous jewels for you."

"No, Dhola. I don't want any jewels. I am content with the jewels I have," she replied, sensing something was wrong.

"Or I could go to Gujarat to get some of its fine cloth for you," he tried again.

"Equally good cloth is available here, Dhola," she insisted.

Finally, Dhola sighed and told the truth. "I want to see Princess Maru of Pugal. I was married to her in my childhood."

Malwani pleaded with him not to go, but Dhola was firm. "Maru too is my wife. I must go to her. Don't stop me, Malwani."

Dhola went to the royal stables to choose his swiftest camel, but Malwani secretly followed. When Dhola was gone, she begged the camel not to take him. "I will give you good food. I will tie you in the shade of a banyan tree and rub your body with sweet-smelling oil," she offered.

"These are tempting offers, but princess, I cannot betray my master," the loyal camel refused.

Knowing she couldn't stop the camel, Malwani resorted to delaying Dhola. First, she warned him of the scorching summer winds. Then, she warned him of the muddy monsoon season. Finally, she warned him of the freezing winter. Months passed, but Dhola's resolve never wavered.

One night, while Malwani was deep asleep, Dhola silently slipped out, mounted his camel, and rode off into the desert.

When Malwani woke up and found him gone, she was devastated. Desperate, she sent her pet parrot after him with a false message. The bird caught up to Dhola and cried, "Prince, Prince—come home at once! Malwani just fell unconscious and died!"

But when Dhola showed no signs of turning back, accepting her supposed fate, the parrot confessed the truth. "Dear Prince, what I told you was not true. I know now that nothing can stop you from going to Maru. Forgive me."

Meanwhile, Umar Sumaru, a cruel chieftain who had once been rejected by Maru, heard of Dhola's journey and plotted to ambush him. When Umar Sumaru spotted Dhola in the desert, he tried to discourage him. "My poor man, you are too late. Maru of Pugal has become old and her hair has turned grey," he lied.

Dhola ignored the man's taunts, pushed his camel faster, and finally reached the gates of Pugal.

When Maru saw him, her heart soared. "Maru! It is I—Dhola!" he exclaimed, taking her into his arms.

"Now, I shall never let you go," she whispered, her years of sorrow finally at an end.

After fifteen happy days in Pugal, Dhola announced, "I am taking my Maru home."

On their journey back to Narwar, they were intercepted by Umar Sumaru, who offered them a place to rest in his camp. Unsuspecting, Dhola accepted. But that evening, while Dhola was resting, Maru overheard Umar Sumaru ordering his men to kill Dhola and capture her.

Maru quickly woke Dhola's loyal camel. She woke Dhola and they mounted the beast. "Dhola, our host was about to kill you. Your camel did everything I told him to. Let's flee from here!" she cried.

They spurred the camel into the desert night. "The sound of camel hooves! They're escaping. Follow them!" Umar Sumaru yelled. "Kill the man, but I want the lady alive. Hurry!"

But Dhola's loyal camel was incredibly swift. "Faster, my friend! Faster!" Dhola urged. They outran Umar Sumaru's men and finally reached the impenetrable gates of Narwar. "The gates of Narwar! We're safe," Dhola sighed with relief.

Inside the palace, they were greeted by Malwani. Having realized the error of her selfish ways, she welcomed Maru with open arms. "Oh, Maru! Forgive me..." Malwani cried, embracing her.

"There is nothing to forgive," Maru smiled warmly. "Let us live together happily."

Dhola looked at his two queens and smiled. "Where there is love, there can only be happiness." And so, they lived in harmony for the rest of their days.

docs.title: Dhruva
docs.text:
Dhruva was the son of Suneeti, the senior queen of King Uttanapada. Unfortunately for Dhruva and Suneeti, Uttanapada loved Suruchi, his second queen, much more and was highly partial to her.

One day, young Dhruva saw Suruchi's son, Uttama, playing near the King. As Suruchi walked up to her son, Uttama asked, "Mother, father is free. May I go and sit on his lap?"

"Certainly, my son," Suruchi replied proudly. "It is your rightful place as the future king."

When Dhruva saw Uttama sitting happily on Uttanapada's lap, he ran towards them. "I too will sit on my father's lap," Dhruva said eagerly.

Suruchi quickly blocked his path. "Certainly not!" she snapped. "You have no right to be there. Go away."

Heartbroken, Dhruva looked up at her. "Where shall I go, mother?"

"Go to Lord Narayana and seek his favour to be reborn as my son," Suruchi declared cruelly. "Only then can you enjoy the same rights as Uttama."

Stung by her harsh words, Dhruva ran crying to his own mother, Suneeti. "Why are you crying, Dhruva? Has anyone hurt you?" she asked, alarmed.

"Mother, Suruchi said I had no right to sit on my father's lap," Dhruva sobbed. "She said only Uttama had the right to sit on the king's lap."

Suneeti held back her tears. "She's right, Dhruva," she admitted softly.

"I am a kshatriya prince, am I not, mother?" Dhruva asked, his face hardening with resolve.

"Yes, my child."

"Mother, Suruchi told me to go and seek Lord Narayana's favour if I want to sit on my father's lap. Will Narayana really help me, mother?"

"Yes, my child. He never fails those who turn to him."

"Then I shall seek him out," Dhruva declared.

Suneeti hugged him. "Go, my son. He is the only one who can help us. It will not be easy to find him. But if you try hard enough you will succeed."

"I will succeed, mother. I will not return till I meet the Lord. I will become a greater king than my father or his father before him."

As Dhruva left for the forest, the great Sage Narada came to know of his resolve. Narada went to see Dhruva to test his determination. "My child, why don't you accept your fate and go back to your mother?" Narada asked.

"O Master, I am Dhruva, the son of mighty Uttanapada, a kshatriya," the boy replied firmly. "And I am determined to win the Lord's favour."

Narada was extremely pleased by his determination. "Forgive me, Master, but I will not turn back," Dhruva added. "O wise one, tell me how I can succeed in my resolve."

"If you meditate in the Madhuvan forest on the banks of the Yamuna, you will find the Lord," Narada instructed. He then taught Dhruva the sacred mantra: "Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya!" "Say that over and over again, and concentrate on that."

As Dhruva set out for Madhuvan, Narada went to the palace of King Uttanapada. The King was deeply distressed. "Alas! He has gone away alone into the forest. The beasts will devour him," the King worried.

"Fear not, O King. The Lord Himself will protect your son. He will come back bringing glory to all," Narada assured him.

Meanwhile, Dhruva reached Madhuvan. "I shall sit here and begin my meditation," he said.

In the first month, he lived only on the fruits he could find in the forest. Gradually he gave up even that, and ate only grass and leaves. In the third month, he stopped eating and lived only on water. In the fourth month, he gave up even that and lived on air. Throughout the period, he repeated the mantra over and over again. In the fifth and sixth months, he even stopped breathing.

Dhruva's immense concentration stopped the very air from flowing on Earth. There was panic in heaven. The gods rushed to Lord Narayana. "O Lord, give us life-giving air! We cannot breathe! Save us!"

"Do not despair," Lord Narayana smiled. "I shall descend to Earth and grant Dhruva the boon he seeks."

Back in Madhuvan, Dhruva felt a divine presence. "Lord, I thought I had found you but you've suddenly vanished. Have I failed in my worship?" he thought. Then, he opened his eyes.

Lord Narayana stood before him in all his glory. "The Lord has come to me!" Dhruva gasped, bowing deeply.

"I know what has brought you to Madhuvan," Lord Narayana said gently. "Your wish will be fulfilled. You shall be a great king. You shall rule for 36,000 years. Then will I give you a place in heaven where you shall stay forever as a guiding star."

When Dhruva returned to the kingdom, King Uttanapada was overjoyed. "I cannot wait to see my son. Commander, get the chariots ready. We will all go to receive him!"

Amidst great rejoicing, Dhruva was carried back to the palace. As soon as Dhruva was old enough to rule, Uttanapada crowned him king and retired to the forest. Dhruva became a legendary ruler, and to this day, he is immortalized in the night sky as the Pole Star—the Dhruva Nakshatra.

docs.title: Ashtavakra
docs.text:
Kahoda was the favourite disciple of Uddalaka, a renowned scholar in the Vedas. Uddalaka was so pleased with Kahoda's devotion and hard work that he offered him his daughter's hand in marriage. "You shall have my daughter, Sujata, for a wife," Uddalaka promised.

Kahoda and Sujata were married and lived happily in Sage Uddalaka's hermitage. A few months later, Sujata became pregnant.

"I shall spare no pains to teach him all I know," Kahoda vowed. "I must expose my child to the sacred influence of the hymns even while he is in the womb."

So Sujata sat by her husband as he recited verses from the scriptures, and the child in her womb silently absorbed what the learned father recited.

One day as Kahoda sat among his disciples, instructing them, a voice suddenly interrupted him. "That's not the way to chant that hymn, father."

Kahoda was stunned. "Who was that? Where did that voice come from?"

"It must have been the voice of our unborn child," Sujata realized.

Furious with his son for putting him to shame before his disciples, Kahoda cursed him. "Such impertinence! And from one yet unborn! O vain soul! May you be born deformed—may your body be twisted in eight places!"

The months rolled on, and the time for Sujata's confinement drew near. Knowing they would need money to provide for the child, Kahoda made a decision. "I will go to King Janaka of Mithila. He is generous to learned Brahmans. Besides, he is at the moment performing a grand yagna."

When Kahoda reached Mithila, Janaka welcomed him but gave him a warning. "A scholar named Bandhi has come to Mithila. No one can defeat him in debate. Think it over carefully. Though you will be well rewarded if you win... you will be drowned in the river if you don't."

"Let me try, O King. I accept the challenge," Kahoda insisted.

The debate began, but it did not continue for long. Kahoda was no match for the brilliant Bandhi.

"Bandhi has won again," the crowd whispered. "Poor Kahoda will be drowned like all the others."

When the tragic news reached Sujata, she broke down. "Alas! My desire for wealth has cost me my husband and my child its father."

A few days later, Sujata had a baby. Because his body was deformed in eight places due to the curse, he was named Ashtavakra. Around the same time, Uddalaka's wife was also blessed with a son, whom they named Shwetaketu.

Growing up under the tender, loving care of Uddalaka, Ashtavakra soon began to look upon his grandfather as his father. The years rolled on, and Uddalaka taught Ashtavakra and Shwetaketu everything he knew. When the boys were twelve years old, Uddalaka proudly told Ashtavakra, "You have mastered the Vedas. I am proud of you, my son."

But one day, during a minor squabble, Shwetaketu snapped at Ashtavakra, "He is not your father."

Deeply humiliated and confused, Ashtavakra ran to his mother. "Mother, where is my father? Oh! Why do you weep? Tell me!"

"Your father is dead," Sujata finally confessed, and she told him all that had happened in Mithila with Bandhi.

Ashtavakra's eyes blazed with determination. "I must defeat Bandhi and have him drowned."

He convinced Shwetaketu to go with him to Mithila. "King Janaka has not been able to complete the yagna he started twelve years ago," Ashtavakra explained.

When the boys reached the gates of Janaka's palace, the guards stopped them. "The king is performing a yagna. I have been ordered to permit only old learned Brahmans to see him," the guard said, looking at the two twelve-year-olds.

"To possess true knowledge one need not be grey-haired or bald," Ashtavakra retorted sharply. "It is wrong to judge a man's merit by his age. I may be young but I have observed the sacred vows and have mastered the Vedas."

The guards were impressed by his intelligence and took him to the King. Ashtavakra immediately issued his challenge. "O King, I have heard of Bandhi's challenge and the fate of those who failed to meet it. I have come to challenge Bandhi and to see him destroyed."

Janaka was astonished. "You speak of defeating Bandhi because you are ignorant of his skill in debate. No one has been able to defeat him." Seeing that Ashtavakra was adamant, the King tested him with a few riddles.

"What is it that does not move when born?" the King asked. "An egg," Ashtavakra replied instantly. "Which animal does not close its eyes while it is asleep?" "The fish."

Impressed by the bold, prompt answers, the King summoned Bandhi. The debate began. But in no time, Ashtavakra had him in a tight corner, and Bandhi had to admit defeat.

"Bandhi has met his match," the King announced. "Let Ashtavakra decide his fate."

"Bandhi had those whom he defeated thrown into the river. Let him meet with the same fate today. Drown him! Seize him!" Ashtavakra commanded.

Bandhi calmly stepped forward. "O King! I am the son of Varuna, the Lord of the Hydrosphere. The sages I defeated are not dead. My father was performing a yagna for which he needed sages from the Earth. The challenge was a means of sending them to him. The yagna must be over by now and they should return. Let us go to the river."

At the river, Bandhi jumped into the water. Then, as the crowd watched in amazement, one by one, the sages emerged from the water.

As Kahoda came out, he saw his son. "Ashtavakra, my son!" he cried, rushing to embrace him.

Ashtavakra fell at the feet of his father. "My blessings on you, Ashtavakra," Kahoda said, beaming with pride. "We regard Ashtavakra as the most brilliant scholar among us. Sons have often been known to excel fathers in achievements."

Then, Kahoda turned to his son. "Ashtavakra, take a quick plunge into the river and come out."

Ashtavakra did as he was told. A second later, as he emerged from the water, a miracle happened. "Mother! Father! Look! My body! It's no longer deformed!" he shouted joyfully. His twisted limbs had become straight and tall.

They all returned to Uddalaka, who rejoiced to see his grandson cured. The hermitage once again became the haven of peace it used to be.

docs.title: Jatayu
docs.text:
Many stories are told about the great vulture, Jatayu. He was the son of Aruna, the Sun God's charioteer, and he was the nephew of Garuda, the mount of Vishnu. He was a bird of majestic proportions, and when he spread his wings, it seemed as though they covered the whole sky. Jatayu lived with his older brother, the magnificent Sampati, who was the King of Birds, in a deep forest full of tall trees and wondrous creatures.

Even as hatchlings, both the brothers had been fond of flying at dizzying heights, challenging themselves and each other. "Let's see who reaches that peak first!"

As they grew older and stronger, their races became more frequent and danger-ridden. "Slow down, Jatayu!" Sampati would warn. "They may be rash and foolhardy, but how splendid they look!" Aruna, their father, would think.

One day, Jatayu challenged his brother. "I bet I can fly higher than you, brother Sampati!"

"Ha ha, we'll see," Sampati laughed.

"My sons, you must not come near us," Aruna warned them as they approached the Sun God's chariot. "It's dangerous. You must tell your sons not to fly so close to us unless they want to be scorched."

"Don't worry about us, father," Sampati assured him, but Aruna's advice went unheeded.

The two brothers rose higher and higher, their wings beating faster than even the wind. "Our forest looks like a speck of dust from up here," Jatayu marvelled.

Suddenly, Sampati realized what was happening. "I'm starting to feel a little tired... I wonder if Sampati's feeling the same... no, he seems to be all right," Jatayu thought, unaware of the imminent danger as he inched towards the sun.

"Jatayu is gaining speed! On the other hand, I feel quite tired and breathless. At this rate, I won't be able to stay ahead of him for much longer," Sampati thought.

Soon, the heat became unbearable. "It's blazing hot! Where are we? My throat is parched. Every inch of my body seems to be on fire. My brother looks exhausted too. What is happening to us?"

It was Sampati who realized what was wrong. "We are too close to Surya! It must be noon now. No wonder the sun god is shining so brightly," Sampati panicked. "I can hear the sound of Surya's horses. The chariot will be here any moment... it's too late to fly away... we'll be burnt to death!"

"If I stop the chariot, the universe will be thrown out of balance and there will be utter havoc," Surya thought, helplessly watching the birds approach.

"My sons... oh no!" Aruna cried out in horror.

"I must continue on my path. Forgive me, my children," Surya said.

Surya's chariot was dangerously close now. "Oh, how do I save him? Jatayu, no!" Sampati yelled. "There's only one way..."

With a final burst of speed, Sampati flew over Jatayu, stretching his wings wide to shield his beloved brother. The flames ate up Sampati's wings, and he hurtled to the ground in pain, having lost the power of flight. "Aaaaaargh!"

"Sampati, my brave son! What a great sacrifice you have made!" Aruna wept.

Jatayu also fell, exhausted and broken. "Sampati, where are you?"

Sampati had fallen on Mount Mahendra. "I am unable to move... and the pain... it is unbearable," he groaned to a sage who found him. "Let me tend to your burns," the kind sage Nishakara offered. Sampati remained there for the rest of his days, under the care of the sage. The two brothers never met again.

Many years later, in the kingdom of Mithila, King Dasharatha of Ayodhya was getting his son Rama married to Sita. Dasharatha met Jatayu, and they became close friends. "I must bid you farewell now, but I give you my word, I will come to your aid whenever you need me," Jatayu promised as Dasharatha left for Ayodhya.

Soon after, Dasharatha consulted his royal priests. "Why are we being punished like this? Have I offended some god?"

"Lord Shani's evil eye is upon Ayodhya," the priests explained. "You must be very careful, your majesty. If Lord Shani's eye falls on you, you will be killed."

"Then I will fly to the heavens and seek Shani out. I will beg him to put an end to our suffering," Dasharatha resolved.

Dasharatha set out in his flying chariot. Shani sensed Dasharatha approaching him. "Dasharatha is coming to meet me! I cannot let him get close to me. I promised Brahma I would not cast my eye on any living creature." Shani flew faster to evade him.

"He's evading me. But I cannot give up. My kingdom is at stake," Dasharatha thought, giving chase. "I'm getting closer."

Jatayu was watching the chase from a distance. "That's Dasharatha, the King of Ayodhya, chasing Shani! It will surely get him into trouble. Let me help him."

Shani turned around. "He's still following me! I have to stop him." Shani cast his burning gaze upon Dasharatha's chariot. "My chariot is on fire!" Dasharatha yelled as the fire spread like lightning. "Aaaah!" he screamed, falling from the sky.

"I managed to catch your father as he fell," Jatayu later recounted to Rama.

"You are safe now," Jatayu had told Dasharatha.

"Who are you, O great bird?" Dasharatha asked.

"My name is Jatayu. I am the brother of Sampati, the King of Birds, and the son of Aruna, the Sun God's charioteer. My uncle is Garuda, the mount of Vishnu."

"I have heard many stories about your courage and wisdom, Dasharatha," Jatayu said. "I could not have let you plummet to your death."

"I'm indebted to you for life," Dasharatha thanked him.

Jatayu took Dasharatha to Shani. "Shani Dev! My kingdom has been struck by a great famine. My people are starving. It is all because Shani's evil eye is upon Ayodhya," Dasharatha pleaded.

"Dasharatha, you almost lost your life pursuing Shani. Your chariot is in flames. Climb on to my back, and I will take you home," Jatayu offered.

"I have never witnessed such valour. I promise you, Ayodhya will not suffer anymore," Shani declared.

"Hail King Dasharatha! He has saved us from death!" the people of Ayodhya rejoiced when they returned.

"Thank you, noble friend," Dasharatha told Jatayu.

Years later, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita were exiled to the Panchavati forest. "I have never seen a bird of such gigantic proportions. Why, it's as big as a hillock!" Rama exclaimed when they encountered Jatayu.

"Do not be afraid. My name is Jatayu. You must be the sons of Dasharatha... you look so much like him," Jatayu greeted them. "I promised Dasharatha that I would always be there for him and his family as long as I had breath in my body. You do not have anything to fear in this forest. We are grateful to you, Jatayu."

"Our father is no more," Rama told him sorrowfully, recounting the events that led to their exile and Dasharatha's death.

"I cannot believe my dear friend is gone," Jatayu mourned.

One day, when Rama and Lakshmana were out hunting, Ravana came to their hut in disguise. "Will you give me something to eat?" he asked Sita. When she stepped out, he revealed his true form. "Ha ha! You are mine now!"

"No! Let me go!" Sita screamed as Ravana dragged her into his flying chariot.

Jatayu heard her cries. "That's Sita's voice! Help! Somebody, please save me!"

"Stop, Ravana!" Jatayu intercepted the chariot in the sky. "I promised Dasharatha I would protect his family. I have to save Sita. You are committing the greatest folly of your life."

"Out of my way, bird!" Ravana roared.

"Fool! I warned you. Let her go, Ravana. Sita is Rama's wife," Jatayu declared, attacking Ravana fiercely.

"Aaaaaargh!" Ravana screamed as Jatayu's talons tore into him.

But age was catching up with the great bird. "I am losing my strength. My limbs are failing me. Ravana is too powerful, and I have become weak with age," Jatayu realized.

"Ha ha! You foolish thing! You may be a mighty bird, but you are no match for me," Ravana sneered. Deaf to Sita's pleas, Ravana slashed at Jatayu without mercy. With a cruel swipe of his sword, he cut off Jatayu's wings. "My wing!" Jatayu cried as he fell to the earth.

Later, Rama and Lakshmana returned to find Sita missing. While searching the forest, Rama found the fallen bird. "Jatayu! What happened? Who did this to you?"

"Forgive me, Rama. I tried my best to save Sita. But he was too powerful for me... R...Ravana. He cut off my wings and talons. I'm useless without them," Jatayu gasped, his life slipping away.

"Oh Jatayu! You put your own life in danger to save Sita," Rama wept.

"You must go now... rescue Sita from that evil demon's clutches. They went south," Jatayu breathed his last.

"We were not able to perform the last rites for our father, but we will give Jatayu that honour," Rama told Lakshmana.

The two brothers built a pyre out of fragrant wood for the great bird. "I will bring the water of the seven sacred rivers," Rama said, shooting an arrow into the ground. Water sprang up from the spot, and Jatayu's soul was granted salvation.

docs.title: Nandi
docs.text:
Sage Shilada was in deep meditation. He had been praying to Lord Shiva for many years. Finally, early one morning, Shiva appeared before him. "What do you want, Shilada?" Shiva asked.

"O Shiva, I long for a child," Shilada prayed with folded hands.

"You will get what you desire," Shiva promised.

The next morning, as Shilada ploughed the land near his ashram, he found a glowing baby boy in the furrow. "A boy! He glows like the sun. I shall call you Nandi!" Shilada rejoiced, naming him 'the one who brings joy'.

Shilada brought Nandi up with great love and taught him everything he knew. "I have memorised all the shlokas, father," young Nandi would say.

"But have you understood the meaning behind each one?"

"Yes, father," Nandi replied respectfully.

"Nandi is very bright. He has unparalleled wisdom. He will surely earn great respect some day," Shilada thought proudly.

One day, two sages, Mitra and Varuna, came to visit Shilada's ashram. "Welcome, O sages," Shilada greeted them. "Nandi, you must look after our guests with great care."

Nandi served them diligently. When it was time for them to leave, the sages blessed Shilada. "May you live a long and prosperous life, Shilada."

"It has been an honour serving you," Nandi bowed.

"May you always be happy, Nandi," they said, their faces suddenly turning serious. As they were walking away, Shilada noticed their expressions. "Please stop! What is the matter, Shilada?"

"Why didn't you bless my son with a long life?" Shilada asked anxiously.

"Because... we couldn't," the sages admitted sadly. "We are sorry. Your son does not have long to live."

Shilada returned home, heartbroken. "What is wrong, father? You look sad," Nandi asked.

"Nothing, my son," Shilada tried to hide his tears, but eventually, he told Nandi what the sages had foreseen.

"Is that all? Why are you so worried?" Nandi smiled calmly. "You told me that Lord Shiva watches over us. How can you be afraid? He won't let anything happen to us. I'll pray to him."

Nandi went to the banks of the river Bhuvana and meditated on Shiva. At the end of the year, Shiva appeared before Nandi. "What is it that you want, my son?"

"I wish to be able to chant verses in your honour for a year," Nandi requested.

"So be it."

Nandi asked for the same boon year after year. His devotion to Shiva was unbreakable. After a few years, Shiva appeared again. "Open your eyes, Nandi. You do not need to do this anymore. You are already immortal. Ask for anything."

"All I wish is to be at your side for ever," Nandi prayed.

"So be it. You will be my vehicle and my closest aide," Shiva declared, transforming the boy into a magnificent, divine bull. He took Nandi to his home on Mount Kailash, and from then on, Shiva travelled on Nandi wherever he went.

Once, when the gods and the asuras churned the ocean to obtain the life-giving amrita, a deadly poison spewed out, threatening to engulf the world. "It is halahala! We are doomed!" the gods panicked.

The gods pleaded with Shiva. "Please save the world, O Lord."

"I will drink all the poison. Come, there isn't a moment to lose," Shiva said, mounting Nandi.

"Look! The poison is spreading through the world. Hurry, Nandi!" Shiva commanded. Shiva began drinking the deadly poison. To protect Shiva, Parvati put her hand on his throat and stopped the poison from flowing inside his body. But as he drank, some of the poison fell from his hands.

"If that poison falls on the ground it could kill thousands. I must do something," Nandi thought. Without hesitation, Nandi turned his head and lapped up the falling poison.

"Oh no! Nandi is drinking the poison! It will destroy him!" the gods cried out.

But to their amazement, Nandi stood unharmed. "It hasn't harmed him at all! What a wonder!"

"Nandi has devoted himself completely to me. Everything that protects me, protects him too," Shiva explained. "He is as strong as I am."

One day, Ravana, the ten-headed king of Lanka, came knocking at the gates of Shiva's abode. Nandi was guarding it as usual. "Stop! How dare you come in my way? No one stops the mighty Ravana," Ravana roared.

"Lord Shiva and Mother Parvati are not to be disturbed. You cannot enter, whoever you may be," Nandi stood his ground firmly.

"You cannot stop me. Out of my way, you insolent monkey!" Ravana insulted him.

Nandi was enraged. "You fool! How dare you insult me! Your arrogance will be your end. You will one day be defeated by monkeys," Nandi cursed him.

Ravana was shocked and eventually returned to Lanka. Nandi's curse came to pass when an army of monkeys led by the Prince of Ayodhya, Rama, defeated Ravana and his mighty army.

It is said that Nandi taught Hanuman the hymns to praise Shiva. Today, every Shiva temple has a statue of Nandi directly in front of the main sanctum sanctorum. Nandi sits there, guarding his lord through eternity.

docs.title: Shyama and Sabala
docs.text:
All the gods were in Indra's court discussing the state of the world, when suddenly a messenger rushed in. "Lord Indra, there's a matter that needs your attention. A group of asuras called the Kalakanjas were trying to get to heaven."

The Kalakanjas had devised a foolish but ambitious plan. "We will build an altar all the way up to Indra's kingdom!" their leader declared. "Bring the bricks and let each one of us lay one brick upon the other. Smell our triumph! We have found a path to heaven."

The altar grew taller and taller as each asura climbed up to lay a brick. "The gods, meanwhile, had been trying to think of a way to stop the asuras," the messenger continued.

"Their structure is getting taller too quickly. We have to do something, Lord Indra," a god warned.

"We have to fight them," another suggested.

"Is there a way to avoid war?" Indra wondered. "I need to take a closer look at what they are building."

Indra disguised himself as a commoner and went down to see. He walked up the immense stairway of bricks. "Let's go! Heaven will soon be ours!" the asuras cheered.

As Indra watched the asuras at work, he came up with a plan. He picked up a brick and placed it carefully on the altar. "I hope this works," he muttered.

Eventually, the asuras' stairway to heaven was ready. Indra watched and waited till they had climbed high enough. "The asuras began to climb," he observed.

When the structure was towering into the sky, filled with climbing asuras, Indra stealthily took his brick out from the foundation.

Instantly, the structure lost its balance and came tumbling down in a massive cloud of dust and rubble. As they fell towards the ground, the terrified asuras started turning into spiders, and landed safely on their eight legs.

However, the two asuras who were almost at the very top had the longest fall. As they plummeted, they turned into fierce hounds.

They came to be known as Shyama and Sabala, the mighty hounds representing dusk and dawn. Impressed by their fierce forms, Yama, the Lord of Death, claimed them. "You will guard the gates of Yama for all eternity," he commanded. And so, it is said, they do to this day.

docs.title: Airavata
docs.text:
Before Earth existed, there was endless water. And out of this vast expanse, a golden egg came into being.

The egg split open, and Brahma, the creator of the universe, emerged from it. From the broken eggshells, Brahma created heaven and the earth, weather and time... and creatures of all sizes and colours.

Among these magnificent creations were sixteen majestic winged elephants—eight female and eight male. Brahma declared one of them the leader of all elephants. This was the mighty Airavata, a pure white elephant with four tusks.

The elephants were sent in pairs to the eight gods. Together, they watched over the eight directions of the universe. Brahma decreed that Airavata and his mate Abhramu would guard the East with Indra.

One day, the venerable sage Dirghatapas was sitting in the shade of a large Vata (banyan) tree and meditating. "Ommmm...." he chanted.

An elephant happened to pass overhead. "I'm tired. I'll rest on that tree for a while," the winged elephant decided, landing heavily on a branch.

Crack! The branch gave way under the massive weight, and the elephant plummeted downwards. Thud! The elephant crashed to the ground right next to the meditating sage.

"How dare you disturb my prayers!" Dirghatapas shouted in anger. In a fit of rage, the sage uttered a terrible curse. "You, and all the other elephants, will lose your wings. You will never be able to fly again."

And so it came to be, that elephants lost their wings and began to live in forests on the ground. However, Airavata was the only one who was spared from the curse. The four-tusked mount of Indra retained his magnificent wings.

In time, Indra came to depend heavily on Airavata in battle. "Follow that asura, Airavata. He is getting away," Indra would command, and Airavata would soar through the clouds in pursuit. Airavata too became fiercely loyal not only to Indra, but to his family, and all of Devaloka.

Once, the gods were attacked by an immensely powerful asura named Surapadma. "Charge!" Surapadma yelled. The devas fought with all their might, but the asura army was stronger. Surapadma, having earlier received a boon from Shiva, could not be killed. The gods watched in despair as their forces were decimated.

As the battle raged on, Indra realized that Sachi, his wife, was the target. "Surapadma is after Sachi. I will take her and go away. Airavata will stay here and help you all," Indra told the gods, retreating to safety.

Without their king, the gods were confused. "There is no one to lead us. What are our orders?" they asked their guru, Brihaspati.

"Jayanta, you will lead the army now," Brihaspati told Indra's son.

"I won't let you down," Jayanta promised.

On the next day of battle, Jayanta fought bravely, causing mayhem in the enemy ranks. His heroics drew Surapadma's attention. Surapadma hurled a spear that struck Jayanta down.

Airavata, who was in the thick of the battle, stopped fighting when he saw Jayanta fall. He charged angrily at Surapadma's chariot and broke it into pieces. He then turned on Surapadma himself, but the invincible asura struck back, cutting two of Airavata's tusks. He then threw Airavata to the earth below.

Airavata fell unconscious. When he finally opened his eyes, he found himself lost and defeated on Earth. Looking at the setting sun, he decided to pray to Shiva. And so began Airavata's ardent worship.

Impressed by his devotion, and touched by his plight, Shiva appeared before Airavata. "Open your eyes, Airavata. You can ask me for anything," Shiva offered gently.

"Lord, give me back my place in heaven," Airavata pleaded.

"So be it!" Shiva declared. Not only did Shiva send Airavata back to Indraloka, but he also restored his broken tusks.

(According to another legend, Airavata was one of the divine gifts that emerged during the churning of the Ocean of Milk, and was immediately taken by Indra.)

docs.title: Gandaberunda and Sharabha
docs.text:
The evil king Hiranyakashipu had just been slain by Vishnu's fourth incarnation, the fearsome Narasimha. Hiranyakashipu had once asked Brahma for a boon to make him invincible: "I wish that no man or beast be able to slay me, that I be immortal during day and night and that no weapon be strong enough to kill me."

Vishnu's form as Narasimha was neither man nor beast. Narasimha had killed Hiranyakashipu with his claws at dusk, thus finding a way around Brahma's boon and ridding the world of the evil king's tyranny.

However, with the killing of Hiranyakashipu, Narasimha had tasted blood. He lost sight of his original purpose and continued on a fearful rampage. "Roar!" he bellowed, terrifying the three worlds.

The gods went to Shiva in panic. "We must stop Narasimha. He will destroy everything if he continues like this!" they pleaded.

"I will stop him," Shiva promised.

Shiva took the form of Sharabha, a massive, terrifying half-bird, half-lion being. He flew down to confront the rampaging avatar. "I will envelop him in my wings to calm him down. His rage will be difficult to control," Sharabha thought as he approached Narasimha.

But the act of being restrained only angered Narasimha further. "Arrrrgh!" he roared, struggling against Sharabha's grip.

And from Narasimha's body emerged a creature even bigger and more fearsome than Sharabha—the Gandaberunda, a gigantic two-headed bird of unimaginable strength.

Gandaberunda, the two-headed bird, met Sharabha in a colossal battle in the skies. "Oh no! What will happen now!" the gods watched in horror.

They fought fiercely for eighteen days. The impact of their clashes shook the heavens and the earth. The gods watched in terror as the fight destroyed everything in its wake.

At the end of the eighteenth day, Gandaberunda suddenly stopped to look around him. "What have we done! Everything around us has been reduced to ashes. This must end now," the dual minds of the bird realized.

The mighty Gandaberunda voluntarily split into two. As the creature divided, a calm and peaceful Vishnu emerged from the centre of the fearsome beast.

Seeing this, Shiva too resumed his normal form. "Let us go back, my friend," Shiva smiled.

"Yes, let's," Vishnu agreed. Peace was finally restored to the universe.

docs.title: Drona: The Master Archer and Teacher
docs.text:
Drona, the son of the great sage Bharadwaja, was named so because he was born in a 'drona', a sacred vessel. As a young boy, he proved to be exceptionally brilliant.

One day, Bharadwaja’s friend, King Prishata of Panchala, visited the ashram with his own son, Drupada. "Drona is a clever boy," the king observed. "Let him be a companion to my son, Drupada."

"As you wish, sir," the sage replied.

The two boys began their studies together. Drupada often looked at his friend in awe. "Drona, how I wish I were clever like you," he said.

"But you are stronger," Drona smiled back.

Eventually, Bharadwaja sent Drona to the great Rishi Agnivesha to master the science of arms. Drupada followed him there. "The great Rishi for his guru? Certainly! Drupada is fortunate," King Prishata remarked.

In the new ashram, their bond deepened. "Drona, let me wash our clothes," Drupada offered warmly. "You recite shlokas while I do it."

"You are kind to me, Drupada. Listen then..." Drona replied, sharing his vast knowledge.

When their studies ended and it was time to part ways, Drupada gripped Drona’s shoulders. "Drona, when I become king after my father, my palace shall be your home. I promise."

"I shall always remember you and your kind offer," Drona replied gratefully.

Years passed. Drupada ascended the throne of Panchala. Drona, however, remained in the austere ashram even after his father's death. Honoring his father's wish, he married Kripi, the pious sister of Kripa. When their son was born, a heavenly voice proclaimed, "The child shall be called Aswatthama!"

Aswatthama became Drona’s greatest joy, even distracting him from his lifelong pursuit of arms. But they lived in stark poverty. One day, hearing that the legendary warrior Parashurama was giving away all his wealth, Drona rushed to him.

"O great one, I have come for a share of the wealth," Drona panted, bowing before the fierce sage.

"You are late," Parashurama sighed. "I have nothing left but this body of mine and my weapons to offer. Quick! Tell me! Which will you have?"

Drona’s eyes gleamed. "I choose your weapons with the mantras for their use."

"They are yours," Parashurama granted, making Drona supreme in the skill of arms.

Elated, Drona hurried home. "Kripi! Aswatthama! I have returned! I have the best of Parashurama's wealth," he announced. But his joy quickly faded. He saw young Aswatthama playing with some wealthy boys.

"What are you drinking?" Aswatthama asked the boys.

"Milk!" they taunted.

"Give me some," Aswatthama asked innocently.

"The beggar's son wants milk!" the boys sneered. Cruelly, they mixed powdered rice with water and handed it to the boy. Aswatthama drank it eagerly and danced around, crying, "I too have tasted milk!"

The men nearby laughed. "Fie upon Drona! His poor son drinks that stuff and thinks it is milk!"

Drona was devastated and deeply ashamed. Gathering his family, he said, "I shall take Kripi and Aswatthama to Drupada. He will welcome us and he can share my new wealth. In Drupada's palace, Aswatthama will drink the richest of milk. Drupada loves me as his own brother."

But when Drona entered the grand court of Panchala, he was met with cold stares. "Drupada, I have come," Drona smiled. "Remember your promise to me, my friend? Now you can fulfil it."

The courtiers gasped. "No measly Brahman can be a king's friend!" a guard hissed.

King Drupada looked down at Drona with haughty disdain. "And what is this about a promise? I recall nothing. But I can give you food and shelter for a night if that is what you seek."

Cold with anger and humiliation, Drona pointed a trembling finger at the king. "I shall avenge this insult, dear Drupada, and also show you what friendship is!"

Drona left Panchala and took his family to Hastinapura, where his brother-in-law, Kripa, was the royal tutor. "Dear Kripa, for certain reasons I want you to keep my prowess in the science of arms a secret," Drona requested.

One afternoon, the young Kuru princes were playing outside when their ball tumbled into a deep, dry well. "Oh! What ill luck! The ball has fallen in," they groaned. "I can see it. But it is deep down."

Drona happened to pass by. "Shame on your princely might and your skill in arms!" he chuckled. "I will bring your ball out with mere blades of grass." Using mystical mantras, Drona shot blades of grass into the well, linking them into a chain to pull the ball out.

Astonished, the princes folded their hands. "We bow to you, sir. We have not seen anything like this. Who are you?"

"Go to Bheeshma, your grandsire, and describe what you have seen," Drona instructed. "He will know."

When Bheeshma heard the tale, he immediately recognized the master archer. "It is none but Drona. You are fortunate. Because in him you will have a teacher beyond compare."

Bheeshma personally invited Drona to the palace. "Tell me, what brings you to our city?" he asked.

"I come seeking obedient pupils who will avenge my insult," Drona declared.

"You may consider it as already done," Bheeshma promised, granting him a comfortable home and entrusting the Kuru princes to him.

"My sons, I have a certain task for you," Drona told the princes. "Promise me that you will do it when your studies are over."

While the others remained silent, young Arjuna stepped forward. "I promise!"

Arjuna's dedication quickly made him Drona's favorite. One night, a gust of wind blew out Arjuna's lamp while he was eating. Realizing he could eat in the dark purely out of habit, a thought struck him: I can make shooting in the dark a similar habit! Drona heard the twang of Arjuna's bow in the pitch dark and embraced the boy. "I shall train you till no bowman in the world is a match for you," Drona vowed.

News of Drona's unmatched academy spread far and wide. One day, a tribal boy approached him. "I am Ekalavya, the Nishada prince. I want to become your pupil."

Drona hesitated. A mere Nishada! Supposing one day he excels my royal pupils? "I am sorry," Drona refused. "I cannot accept you as my pupil."

Undeterred, the devoted Ekalavya went into the forest, built a clay statue of Drona, worshipped it, and practiced relentlessly. Months later, the Kuru princes were hunting in that very forest. Their hound began to bark at Ekalavya. Before the dog could even close its mouth, Ekalavya fired seven arrows in rapid succession, sealing its jaws seamlessly.

"Who are you? Who is your guru?" the stunned princes demanded.

"I am Ekalavya, a Nishada prince. Dronacharya is my guru," the boy answered proudly.

When Arjuna heard this, he was crushed. "How could you allow a Nishada to excel me? You promised..." he told Drona.

"Come, let us go and see the wondrous performer," Drona said, his face unreadable.

When Ekalavya saw the master, he fell at his feet. "Arise, my boy," Drona said coldly. "If you take me for your guru, give me my fee!"

"O illustrious guru, what shall I give? There is nothing I would not give my guru," Ekalavya offered with pure devotion.

"I want the thumb of your right hand!" Drona commanded.

Without hesitation, and with a smile on his face, Ekalavya severed his right thumb and placed it at Drona's feet. His archery career was ruined, and Arjuna remained unequalled.

Years later, the princes completed their training. Drona gathered them. "The time has come for me to exact my fee. Capture Drupada, the Panchala king, and give him to me."

The Kaurava princes charged first, eager to show off, but Drupada's fierce counterattack sent them fleeing in terror. Then, Arjuna and the Pandavas rode out. "Bhima, lead with your mace!" Arjuna ordered. Piercing through the defenses, Arjuna shattered Drupada's bowstring, seized the king, and dragged him before Drona.

Drona looked down at the bound king. "This was the moment I had patiently waited and worked for," Drona sneered softly. "Your kingdom now belongs to me. Do you wish to revive our friendship?"

Drupada glared in silence.

"Brave king, we brahmans are ever forgiving," Drona continued. "I ask for your friendship again, and in the name of friendship, I will give you half the kingdom. The other half I will retain. Now you need not hesitate to own me as a friend—for I too am a king."

"You are a noble soul," Drupada said through gritted teeth as he was untied. "I am grateful to you and wish to be your lifelong friend..." But internally, Drupada vowed: ...in which time I shall have my revenge.

Consumed by bitterness, Drupada sought out sages with exceptional spiritual powers. From a grand sacrificial fire, two divine children emerged: a warrior son named Dhrishtadyumna, born specifically to slay Drona, and a breathtaking daughter named Draupadi, destined to bring about the destruction of the Kauravas.

Decades later, the great Kurukshetra war tore the kingdom apart. Drona, bound by loyalty, fought for the Kauravas, while Drupada and Dhrishtadyumna allied with the Pandavas. When Bheeshma fell on the tenth day, Duryodhana declared, "Let Dronacharya command our forces!"

Drona proved invincible, cutting through the Pandava armies like a scythe. On the fourteenth day, Drupada challenged Drona directly. After a fierce, earth-shaking duel, Drona struck Drupada down, killing him.

Dhrishtadyumna was paralyzed with grief. "Take heart, brave one," Yudhishthira consoled him. "Your father's death shall be avenged. You are born to slay Drona."

But Drona could not be defeated in combat. Lord Krishna devised a ruthless plan. "Drona is invincible. We will have to resort to subtle means to defeat him. Tell him that Aswatthama is dead... Dronacharya will be disabled by grief."

"No! No! That would be a lie," Arjuna protested.

Bhima, the strongest of the brothers, had an idea. He sought out a massive war elephant named Aswatthama and crushed its skull with his mace. Running to the front lines, Bhima roared, "Aswatthama has been killed!"

Drona froze, his bow dropping slightly. "No! It can't be true. I know how skilful my son is." He turned his chariot toward Yudhishthira, knowing the eldest Pandava never told a lie. "Is this true, Yudhishthira?"

Yudhishthira bowed his head. "Yes. Aswatthama the elephant is dead," he replied, though the words 'the elephant' were swallowed by the din of the battlefield.

Drona heard only that his beloved son was gone. His spirit broke. "Aswatthama! Aswatthama!" he wept, laying down his weapons in the middle of the carnage. "For your sake I took up arms to earn a living. Now that you are dead I have no use for them." Drona closed his eyes, sat cross-legged on his chariot, and entered deep samadhi, detaching his soul from his body.

Seeing his moment, Dhrishtadyumna leaped onto the chariot and swung his sword, severing the master archer's head. But the vengeful strike meant little; unknown to the young prince, the soul of the great Dronacharya had already departed on its upward journey to the heavens.

docs.title: Durgesh Nandini
docs.text:
Late one summer afternoon, during Emperor Akbar's reign, a Rajput youth was riding from Vishnupur to Mandaran. It was Jagat Singh, the Prince of Amber and son of Raja Man Singh. He had been sent by the Emperor to stop Katlu Khan, the Pathan ruler of Orissa, who was now marching to take Bengal as well.

Suddenly, the sky darkened and a fierce storm broke out. "It's going to rain, I must hurry!" Jagat Singh thought. As lightning flashed, he spotted the Shaileshwar temple and ran inside for shelter.

Inside the temple, he was surprised to find two women—an older woman and a breathtakingly beautiful younger girl. "Please, don't be afraid! As long as I am here you are safe. I am a Rajput," he assured them. As the older woman spoke to him, Jagat Singh couldn't take his eyes off the younger one. "Is she an angel? ...A fairy... or a goddess?" he wondered.

When the storm finally abated, the women prepared to leave. "Thank you, sir. But when my master, this lady's father, asks us who brought us back, what should I tell him?" the older woman asked.

"You can tell him that the Prince of Amber, Jagat Singh, son of Raja Man Singh, was your escort," he smiled.

The younger girl looked back at him one last time, her eyes filled with newfound affection. "Farewell, my divine beauty! Will I ever see you again?" Jagat Singh whispered to himself.

The two women returned to Garh Mandaran, the home of Raja Virendra Singh. The beautiful girl was Tilottama, Durgesh Nandini, the daughter of the Raja. The older woman was Vimla.

Meanwhile, Jagat Singh reached his father's camp at Burdwan. His commander informed him that Katlu Khan had entered Bengal with 50,000 soldiers. Unfazed, Jagat Singh declared, "Maharaj! With your permission I can drive out Katlu Khan with just five thousand soldiers."

A few days later at Garh Mandaran, Raja Virendra Singh discussed the impending war with his guru. "I plan to remain neutral," the Raja stated. But he knew that remaining neutral against the mighty Mughals and Pathans was nearly impossible.

A week later, at the Shaileshwar temple, Jagat Singh secretly met Vimla. He begged her to let him see Tilottama again. "It is very difficult... unless... you come with me to the fort. Tonight!" Vimla suggested.

At midnight, Jagat Singh sneaked into the fort like a thief. Vimla led him to Tilottama's room. But their joyous reunion was cut short. Outside, Usman Khan, Katlu Khan's nephew, had arrived with his troops. Having discovered the secret door, they stormed the fort.

"Don't shout, my dear lady! I am Usman Khan, Katlu Khan's nephew," the Pathan announced, surprising Vimla on the terrace.

The Pathans quickly overpowered Jagat Singh and captured the fort. Raja Virendra Singh, Vimla, Tilottama, and Jagat Singh were all taken as prisoners to a nearby fort controlled by Katlu Khan.

In the fortress, Usman Khan approached Vimla. He slipped a ring onto her finger. "On Katlu Khan's birthday, come out here in the evening and show it to the man wearing an identical ring. He will wait for you here..."

Meanwhile, unknown to them, Jagat Singh, who had been badly wounded during the capture, was saved from the jaws of death by the tender care of Ayesha, Katlu Khan's beautiful daughter. Ayesha secretly fell deeply in love with the brave Rajput prince.

Usman Khan visited Jagat Singh's cell to deliver terrible news. "Raja Virendra has been executed. His daughter is to wed Katlu Khan. I have come with an offer from him. If you do not accept his offer, Katlu Khan has ordered me to lock you up in a cell." Katlu Khan wanted Jagat Singh to betray his father and the Mughal Emperor.

Jagat Singh refused fiercely. "I am a Rajput, Usman! I cannot ask my father to betray the Emperor."

Vimla, devastated by the news of her husband's execution, revealed a long-kept secret to Tilottama. "I am your father's secret wife, Tilottama," she wept. Seeking revenge, Vimla formulated a daring plan. She gave Tilottama a ring filled with deadly poison. "Take this ring and wait outside the zenana. A man wearing a similar ring will come to you..."

On the night of Katlu Khan's birthday festivities, Vimla dressed herself in her finest jewelry and went to Katlu Khan's quarters. Bewitched by her beauty, Katlu Khan welcomed her. "Ah, Vimla! You are enchanting," he slurred, heavy with wine.

But as he leaned in, Vimla struck. With a hidden dagger, she stabbed the Pathan leader. "I'll avenge your death, my lord!" she whispered as Katlu Khan fell.

In a panic, the guards rushed in, leaving the gates open, allowing Vimla to walk out freely.

The dying Nawab, realizing his end was near, sent for Jagat Singh. "My sons... are... young... promise me a treaty... please," Katlu Khan gasped.

"I'll request Emperor Akbar to leave Orissa to you," Jagat Singh promised the dying man.

"Yes... yes. Bengal... Akbar's... Orissa... my sons'... yes... thank..." And with those words, Katlu Khan closed his eyes forever.

In the months that followed, an agreement was reached between the Pathans and the Mughals. With the war over and the tyrant dead, Jagat Singh and Tilottama were finally united in marriage.

On their wedding day, Ayesha presented Tilottama with a casket of priceless jewellery. "They are beautiful, simply beautiful, sister. Thank you!" Tilottama beamed.

"Well, they are nothing compared to the jewel you have acquired today," Ayesha smiled bravely, hiding her own heartbreak.

After fond farewells, Ayesha left Garh Mandaran. Standing on a bridge over a gently flowing river, she took off the poison ring she had carried for so long. "I don't need it. I have found peace and happiness in the happiness of Jagat Singh and Tilottama," she smiled, and flung the ring into the water.

docs.title: Elephanta
docs.text:
Beyond Bombay harbour, across the blue waters, lies the green isle of Elephanta. A short walk uphill takes you to the magnificent cave temples, cut out of solid rock by the hand of man. Inside the main cave may be seen some of the finest sculptures of Indian art, carved about 1300 years ago.

These caves were carved patiently and laboriously by artisans during the reign of the Mauryas of the Konkan. Later, the Mauryas were defeated in a great naval battle off Elephanta by the Chalukyan King, Pulakesin II.

"Hail Emperor Pulakesin!" his soldiers cheered.

After being ruled by several Hindu dynasties, the island fell to the Sultans of Gujarat and eventually passed from them to the Portuguese. Unfortunately, the Portuguese soldiers had little respect for the ancient art and damaged many of the sculptures, using them for target practice. "Ha! Ha! I have hit the nose this time," one soldier laughed. "I have done better! I have knocked off an arm!" another boasted.

The island was later taken by the Marathas. "Hail, Emperor Sambhaji! We shall make this island an invincible fort," his men declared.

The early names of this island were Gharapuri and Sripuri. When the Portuguese first landed, they saw a massive stone elephant. "This is unique! A stone elephant to receive us!" they marvelled. "The island should be called Elephanta!"

The stone elephant was later removed by the British to the Victoria Gardens in Bombay (now Veermata Jijabai Bhonsle Udyan).

Of the seven caves at Elephanta, the main cave is the most important. It is dedicated to Lord Shiva, the great god of the Hindu Trinity.

In the main cave, the first figure at the right is that of Shiva as Nataraja, the Lord of Dancers. His dance symbolises the eternal motion of the universe. The story behind this goes back to ancient times.

Long, long ago, in a forest, there lived ten thousand rishis who by virtue of their penances had become very powerful. "We are greater even than the devas! We are invincible!" they boasted arrogantly.

At Kailasa, Shiva turned to Vishnu. "You and I must subdue these arrogant sages."

Shiva took the form of a handsome, young yogi and Vishnu took the form of his charming wife. As soon as they appeared before the rishis, the sages' wives were completely captivated by the handsome yogi. "He's sure to choose me!" one gasped. "He will not! He will choose me," another argued.

Leaving the sacrifice unfinished, the women walked towards the yogi's wife, entranced by her beauty. "You will marry me. No, she shall be mine," the sages fought over Vishnu's female form.

Suddenly, the rishis came to their senses and realised they were being deluded by some supernatural power. "May you and your magical powers be cursed!" they shouted at the yogi. "And cursed be your wife, the serpent in a woman's guise!"

Their curses, however, were of no avail against the supreme gods. So they prepared a sacrificial fire. "We will destroy them for all time," they declared, pouring ghee and chanting mantras.

Suddenly, a ferocious tiger sprang from the fire. "Our curses may have failed, but this tiger will not. Destroy him!"

Unperturbed, Shiva caught the tiger in an iron grip, flayed it, and draped its skin over his body.

The next moment, a giant snake emerged from the fire and darted at Shiva. He calmly picked it up and wore it as a garland around his neck.

Hardly had he done so when, from the fire, there emerged an evil dwarf holding a club. "Now try and overpower him!" the rishis mocked.

"You cannot! He is the evil goblin himself! He will destroy you!"

In reply, Shiva dashed the evil creature to the ground, assumed his own divine form, and began to dance upon the dwarf's back. Good had triumphed over evil! The rishis and the devas in heaven gazed in awe at the powerful dance in which all creation and the cosmic universe was set in motion.

"He has taught us a lesson in humility!" the rishis bowed. "Glory to the great Shiva!"

Another magnificent panel at Elephanta is the Lingodbhava Murti. One day, Brahma, the Creator, and Vishnu, the Preserver, were arguing about which of them was the greater. Suddenly, there appeared an endless fiery pillar, a Lingam, without beginning or end.

Vishnu, in the form of a boar, burrowed into the earth. "I will find the origin of this Lingam," he said. Brahma, in the form of a swan, flew higher and higher into the heavens. "And I shall find the end!"

After countless years, the boar returned exhausted. "I have failed. I could not find its beginning."

Neither did Brahma succeed in his mission, but he refused to admit it. He saw a Ketaki flower falling from the heavens. "You must come with me to Vishnu and say that I picked you up from Shiva's head," Brahma instructed the flower.

Brahma assumed his real form and lied to Vishnu. "I saw the end of the fiery Lingam. And I was a witness to it," the flower lied.

Suddenly, Shiva emerged from the centre of the fiery Lingam. "Brahma, you have been dishonest! From now on, none shall honour you. Nor will you have your own temple or festival," Shiva cursed him in anger.

But to Vishnu, Shiva said, "Dear Hari, you are a lover of truth! I am pleased with you. You will always be honoured as I am. You will have separate temples and festivals."

One of the most exquisite panels at Elephanta is that of the marriage of Shiva and Parvati. Parvati (Uma) had undertaken severe penances to win Shiva's love. An old Brahmana appeared before her and tried to dissuade her. "Why have you renounced the world? To win the love of Shiva? I wish to marry him."

"That mendicant?" the old man scoffed. "He has matted hair and decks himself with serpents! He is uncouth..."

"How dare you speak of my loved one so! Go away!" Parvati shouted angrily.

Suddenly, the old man changed his form. "It is you, my Lord!" Parvati gasped as Shiva revealed himself, having merely tested her devotion. And so they were married.

The next panel is of Shiva as Gangadhara, showing the descent of the river Ganga to the earth. King Sagara had sixty thousand sons who were reduced to ashes by the angry Sage Kapila. Sagara's descendant, Bhagiratha, undertook severe penances to bring the celestial river Ganga down to earth to wash their ashes and grant them salvation.

"I shall send Ganga down to earth to redeem your ancestors," Brahma finally granted. "But Shiva must break her fall, or the earth will be washed away."

As Ganga hurtled down from the heavens, proud of her mighty force, Shiva caught the turbulent river in his matted locks. "The force of Ganga's fall has been broken!" Bhagiratha rejoiced, and the holy river flowed gently onto the earth.

In the centre of the northern wall is the gigantic, iconic figure of Shiva as Mahesha (the Trimurti). It combines in himself the qualities of Creator, Preserver, and Destroyer.

Other panels depict Ardhanarishwara (Shiva and Parvati as one entity, half-male and half-female), and a scene of Ravana attempting to uproot Mount Kailasa. As the demon king lifted the mountain, Parvati trembled with fear, but Shiva merely smiled and pressed the mountain down with his toe, imprisoning the arrogant Ravana beneath it.

The last panel in the main cave shows Shiva as the Great Yogi. He sits on a lotus in deep meditation. Though the lotus grows in muddy waters, it remains pure and clean. Similarly, Shiva's face reflects the calm and spiritual strength of the supreme yogi, forever detached from worldly illusions.

docs.title: Ellora Caves
docs.text:
In the 8th Century A.D., a new empire was about to rise in Central India. Indra, the ruler of Ellichpur, a principality of the Chalukyan Empire, nursed ambitions of extending the area of his influence. "Our forefathers emigrated to Ellichpur several generations ago and carved a principality for themselves here," he thought. "But to grow I need support. I must have strong allies."

When the wedding of Chalukya King Pulakeshi's daughter, Princess Bhavanaga, was arranged, Indra saw an opportunity. "If I could win her hand, our political influence would increase tremendously," he realized. But since she was bound to choose a groom from a larger principality, Indra took a drastic step.

On the day of the wedding, Rashtrakuta Indra brazenly abducted Princess Bhavanaga! He managed to reach Ellichpur safely with his abducted bride, and there he married her.

King Pulakeshi was very angry with Indra, but their relations eventually became friendly by the time Indra and Bhavanaga's first son, Dantidurga, was born.

Dantidurga grew up in an atmosphere charged with the ambition and drive of his father. "You must train yourself to be a good soldier. Only a good soldier can be a good general," Indra taught him.

In A.D. 733, Indra died, leaving behind his brother Krishna and his young son Dantidurga. Dantidurga mourned his father deeply, but his uncle Krishna encouraged him, "Yes, uncle. I will strive hard to bring glory to the Rashtrakootas."

On the following day, Dantidurga was crowned. When he called on the Emperor Vikramaditya II, the Emperor told him, "Your late father was like a right arm to me. You have my blessings and good wishes."

In A.D. 738, Dantidurga received a message from the Emperor: "The Arabs are harassing Pulakeshi of Gujarat. Proceed forthwith to his aid."

"I will make arrangements for the campaign immediately," Dantidurga agreed. He marched to Navasari and fought a fierce battle, routing the Arab invaders. The Emperor honoured the victorious Dantidurga with the titles Prithvivallabha (Hero of the World) and Khadgavaloka (Valorous Swordsman).

The valour shown by the Rashtrakoota king strengthened the Emperor's faith in him. He relied on Dantidurga to solve ticklish issues, such as defeating the Pallava Nandivarman of Kanchi, who was causing trouble on the southern borders. After a fierce battle, Nandivarman finally acknowledged defeat.

Four years later, Emperor Vikramaditya passed away. His successor, Keertivarman, failed to win the respect of his vassals. Dantidurga used this opportunity to expand his own domain, adding the Gurjara Kingdom of Nandipura to his possessions.

To mark his victories, Dantidurga performed the Hiranyagarbha Mahayajna (a grand sacrifice) at Ujjayini.

News of Dantidurga's growing power reached Keertivarman. "Dantidurga is becoming too powerful. He must be taught a lesson," Keertivarman decided, declaring war.

The two armies met in a fierce encounter. In the end, Keertivarman's forces were pushed back, and Dantidurga rightfully claimed the title of Emperor. "Glory to Maharajadhiraja Paramabhattaraka Dantidurga!" his troops cheered.

Five years later, Dantidurga fell ill. "I have no child and heir to the throne..." he told his uncle Krishna. "I hand over the reins of the kingdom to you... to rule, to conquer and to bring glory to the Rashtrakootas..." Having settled the matter, Dantidurga passed away in peace.

Krishna was crowned Emperor, and he appointed his son Govinda as the commander of his forces. They finally vanquished Keertivarman and brought peace and prosperity to the great country.

"You seem tired, husband," Krishna's queen noticed one day. "Then let us get away from it for a few days," he agreed.

They travelled to the serene hills of Ellora. While exploring the beautiful caves, the queen suddenly made a solemn promise. "I take a vow that I shall not touch a morsel of food until I see the kalasha (topmost spire) of the temple of Lord Kailasanath."

The Emperor was taken aback. "Temples cannot be built in a day or two. It may take months, even a year... I do not know what prompted me to take the vow," he worried, "but now that I have taken it, I will have to fulfil it."

Krishna summoned the best sculptor in the land, a man named Kokkas from Pratishthana.

"I have 7000 craftsmen and artisans with me. If all of them are put on this job, it will take sixteen months," Kokkas estimated.

"Sixteen months! Can she live without food for sixteen months? Impossible!" Krishna despaired.

But Kokkas was a master of his craft. He cordoned off the site and forbade anyone from entering. On the eighth day, he led the queen to the site.

On the top of a hillock was a beautifully carved kalasha, with an orange flag fluttering in the breeze. "The vow is fulfilled, true, but it is a trick! Where is the temple?" the King asked.

"The temple is yet to be carved, sir," Kokkas explained gently. "The only way to fulfil the vow was to carve the temple working down from the top! The task is difficult but the temple should be such as befits the glory of the Rashtrakoota Empire."

Kokkas and his seven thousand artisans set about carving the massive Kailasanath temple downwards out of a single solid rock. The king came frequently to see the progress of the work, and in due course, the majestic temple took shape.

The Rashtrakoota empire and its great kings may have been lost in the lanes of history, but the magnificent Kailasanath temple bears testimony to those glorious days to this very moment.

docs.title: The King Who Stopped the River
docs.text:
Once upon a time, there was a foolish king who had a wise Diwan. One warm summer night, the king could not sleep. "BONG..." the palace gong sounded.

"It must be almost one o'clock," the king muttered, tossing and turning.

An hour later, "BONG... BONG..."

"There goes another gong... It's two o'clock," the king groaned.

At last, when he heard the sixth gong, the king sat up. "It's six o'clock! I'll have to get up now. But I feel so dull. I think I'll ride out into the countryside and get some fresh air. Tell the Diwan to get ready. We will go riding today."

So the king and his wise Diwan set out. An hour later, the Diwan asked, "Your majesty, how do you feel now?"

"Much better. But I'm thirsty. Let's stop by that river."

They stopped by a beautiful, sparkling river. "Such clear, sparkling water! Where does this river flow?" the king asked.

"It flows down to that kingdom in the East, your majesty," the Diwan replied.

The king was outraged. "Our river flowing into their country? We must stop it at once! No buts, Diwan. I want a dam built here."

The dam was built. But now, since the river could not flow down its usual course, it overflowed its banks and flooded the countryside.

"It will be worse during the monsoon," the Diwan warned.

"So what?" the king scoffed. "We've got our river all to ourselves, haven't we? How foolish can he get?"

The Diwan realized he had to do something. "Can't he see that our neighbours will soon attack us for stopping their water supply? I must get him to break down that dam... Ah! I've got it!"

That evening, the Diwan went up to the tower from which the gong was sounded and spoke to the man there. "After midnight, I want you to sound the gong every half-hour. Not every hour, as you do now. As you command, sir."

Because of the Diwan's order, it was only 3 o'clock when the sixth gong was sounded. "BONG... BONG... BONG... BONG... BONG... BONG!"

"The sixth gong! It's six o'clock. I'd better wake up the others," the man thought. He woke up the guards. "Get up! Our duty is over..."

"Is it six o'clock already? It is," they said, confused by the darkness.

"Then why is it so dark?"

"The sun should have been up by now. Something must have happened to it. What could have happened to the sun? How should I know? We'd better sound the alarm."

"Wake up! Wake up, everybody! Something has happened to the sun!"

There was panic in the palace. The commander of the army rushed to the king's chamber. "Wake up, your majesty! A terrible thing has happened!"

"Eh...?" the king yawned. "It's six o'clock, but the sun hasn't risen as yet. Hasn't risen? What do you mean the sun hasn't risen?!"

"It hasn't, your majesty. See for yourself."

The king looked out the window. "You're right. Summon the Diwan!"

When the Diwan came, the king demanded, "What's all this! Why hasn't the sun come up as yet?"

"I have been thinking about it, your majesty. There can be only one reason."

"What?"

"Somebody has caught the sun and is not letting it come over our land."

"Caught the sun? Ah, yes! That's what I thought too... But who would do such a wicked deed?"

"It must be the king of the Eastern kingdom, who else? I heard he was angry because we stopped the river from flowing into his country. And as you know, the sun passes over his kingdom before it comes over ours."

"Your majesty, we must do something about it soon or we'll be in darkness forever," the Diwan warned.

"Do you think... Do you think he would let the sun go if we let the river flow into his country again?" the king asked hopefully.

"What a brilliant idea, your majesty!" the Diwan praised. "Ah, well, my mother always said I was a very clever fellow!" the king beamed.

"Well, now... we'll have to break down that dam. I agree. Summon the commander of my army!"

"I am here, your majesty," the commander stepped forward.

"Get your men! We are going down to the river to pull down that dam as fast as we can."

The king led his men to the river, and before dawn, they broke the dam down. The river began to flow to the neighbouring country again.

"The sun should be coming up any moment now," the king said confidently.

And sure enough, as dawn naturally broke, the sun appeared over the horizon. "The sun! Look! They've let the sun go!" the men cheered.

"Your plan worked, your majesty. You have saved the country," the Diwan smiled.

"Oh, it was nothing..." the king replied modestly. The foolish king never realized how he had been outsmarted by his wise Diwan!

docs.title: Words for Words
docs.text:
A city dweller was passing through a village. He looked around at the innocent-looking villagers and smirked. "The people here are so simple. I could easily make some money here if I tried."

He approached a well-to-do villager relaxing on a swing on his porch. "Let me begin with this man. He appears to be rich... and foolish," the conman thought.

He walked up and bowed low. "Oh, what a noble face you have, sir! I can tell that you are a man of great generosity."

The villager smiled warmly. "Wife, bring ten measures of rice for my friend here!" he called out to his wife inside the house.

The conman was thrilled. He bowed even lower. "Men like you are rare, sir. You shine like a jewel on a heap of coal."

"Wife! Bring a hundred rupee note!" the villager shouted enthusiastically.

The conman's eyes sparkled with greed. He decided to turn up the flattery. "Let me recite a verse in your honour, sir. Flowers bloom only for a few hours; clouds pour only for a few days; rivers flow only for a few months; ...but your glory, sir, shines forever."

"WIFE! Bring two silk dhoties!" the villager bellowed.

The conman rubbed his hands together. "How easily I have fooled this stupid fellow!" he thought, waiting eagerly for the woman to come out with his loot.

He waited... and waited. The villager casually picked up a book and started reading on his swing.

"Er... why haven't the gifts come as yet, sir?" the conman finally asked, his patience running out.

"Gifts?" the villager looked up, surprised.

"Now, what did you do with your words?" the villager asked with a knowing twinkle in his eye.

"I... er... made you happy," the conman stammered.

"Well, I have made you happy with mine," the villager chuckled.

The conman realized that the villagers were not as simple as they looked. Embarrassed and empty-handed, he hastily left the village.

docs.title: Raman's Cat
docs.text:
The Emperor of Vijayanagara, Krishna Deva Raya, wanted to see how clever his ministers were. He sat on his throne and posed a strange challenge.

"Can any of you bring me a cat... which runs away from milk?" he asked with an amused smile.
