The ministers looked at each other in disbelief. "No cat will ever run away from milk," one declared.

"There's nothing a cat likes better," another agreed.

"What? No one?" the Emperor chuckled.

"I will bring you such a cat, your majesty," a single voice spoke up.

The man who spoke was Tenali Raman, the cleverest jester in the court. "But not immediately. Then bring it within 30 days," the Emperor challenged.

Raman went home and placed a saucer of milk before his pet cat. But this was no ordinary milk—it was boiling hot!

The cat eagerly dipped its tongue into it... and jumped back in alarm. The milk had burned its tongue! The poor cat hissed and scampered away from the saucer.

For the next thirty days, Raman fed the cat food, but every time he offered milk, he made sure it was scalding hot. The cat quickly learned to associate the sight of white milk with terrible pain.

On the thirtieth day, Raman returned to the court with his cat. "Here is the cat, your majesty," Raman presented the feline.

"Let us see if it runs away from milk," the Emperor said, ordering a servant to bring a saucer of fresh, cool milk.

As soon as the servant placed the saucer of milk in front of the cat, the creature took one terrified look at it, let out a yowl, and bolted out of the court as fast as its legs could carry it.

The Emperor and his ministers watched in sheer amazement as Raman's cat successfully ran away from the milk!

docs.title: Punyakoti
docs.text:
Hulia the tiger was weak with hunger. "He had not eaten anything for days. If I don't find some food today, I'll die of hunger," he thought miserably.

Just then, he heard the faint sound of bells. TIN-TIN

"What's that?" Hulia looked down from the hillock. "The cattle returning home after grazing!" he realized with joy.

He sprang down towards the herd. But the herd was too fast, and he missed. "What's wrong with me? I couldn't catch a silly cow!" he grumbled. Then he noticed a single cow lagging behind. "Who do I see coming this way?"

It was a gentle cow called Punyakoti. "I'd better walk faster. My child must be waiting for me," she thought.

"EH!" Hulia jumped into her path. "Yes, I've been waiting for you."

"Hulia, please listen..." Punyakoti pleaded.

"No! I won't! I am going to kill you and eat you up."

"Kill me! Eat me! But not immediately. My child is waiting for me. I'll go home, feed him and come back to you."

"WHAT! HOHOHO!" Hulia roared with laughter. "Do you take me for a fool? As if you'll come back, if I let you go!"

"Believe me, Hulia, I will. I give you my word with Mother Earth as a witness."

Hulia looked at her earnest eyes. "All right. You may go. But come back soon."

"I will, Hulia, I will," Punyakoti promised.

Punyakoti ran to her home at the foot of the hill. "Come, my child! Drink, my child. Drink as much milk as you can. For this is the last time I will be feeding you."

"Mother!" the calf cried, sensing her sorrow.

"Yes, my son. I have to go back to Hulia. He will be waiting for me." Punyakoti told him everything about her promise to Hulia.

"But mother, you don't have to go back to Hulia. He's right. We will avoid that route, Hulia can't do a thing," the other cows advised her.

"I must go. I promised him that I would return. Truth is my mother. Truth is my father. Truth is my God. Truth is everything to me."

"But mother, who will feed me when I'm hungry? Who will take care of me?" the calf wept.

Punyakoti turned to the herd. "My sisters, treat this orphan as your child. Please don't gore him with your horns if he comes in your way and please don't kick him if he is at your back. O Punyakoti!"

"Punyakoti, we will all come with you to Hulia," they offered.

"Please don't. I want you to take care of my child," she insisted.

As Punyakoti left, her calf cried, "Mother, don't go! Please don't go, mother..." But she walked steadily back up the hill.

Meanwhile, Hulia was getting impatient. "I shouldn't have let her go. She'll never come... no, there she is!" he exclaimed in surprise as Punyakoti appeared.

"She has kept her promise... even though death awaits her here. What a noble creature!" Hulia thought, deeply moved.

"Hulia, my brother, come! Here I am. Eat me," Punyakoti offered herself.

Hulia looked at her. "Eat you? Never, my noble sister. Never. Go back to your child."

"Hulia!" Punyakoti was stunned as Hulia turned back and left. And so, the truthful Punyakoti happily rejoined her child.

docs.title: How an Elephant was Weighed
docs.text:
One day, a king sent for his royal mahout. "I want to know how much my elephant weighs, and if you don't give me the answer by tomorrow morning... I'll have you beheaded."

"I'm done for! How can anyone weigh an elephant? It's impossible," the terrified mahout thought. In those days, they did not have scales that could weigh elephants.

As the mahout was wandering around in despair, he met an old sadhu. "Why do you look so glum, my friend?" the sadhu asked.

"I'm in real trouble, sir. The king has asked me to weigh his elephant," the mahout explained his impossible task.

"Is that all? Cheer up. Go and bring the animal, along with one of your friends, to the river. I'll wait for you there."

The mahout did as he was told. He brought the elephant to the riverbank.

"Now get him into that boat," the sadhu instructed.

The heavy elephant stepped into the wooden boat, causing it to sink deep into the water.

"That's it. Now mark the level to which the boat has sunk," the sadhu pointed out. The mahout drew a line on the side of the boat where the water touched it.

"Now bring the elephant out and fill the boat with sand till it sinks to the mark you have made," the sadhu continued.

After some time, the mahout and his friend filled the boat with heavy sand. "There! The boat has sunk to the mark, sir."

"Do you know why?" the sadhu asked. "It's because the sand you have put into it is equal to the weight of the elephant. Now weigh that sand on a pair of scales and... I'll know the weight of the elephant!" the mahout realized joyfully.

The next day, the mahout went to the court. "Did you find out how much my elephant weighs?" the king asked strictly.

"Yes, your majesty. It weighs 900 pounds," the mahout answered confidently.

The king was amazed by his ingenuity and richly rewarded the mahout. And the mahout never forgot the wise sadhu who had saved his life.

docs.title: The Two Pandits
docs.text:
A king invited two scholars to his palace. One of them was called Pandit Gyanraj and the other was called Pandit Vidyaraj. They were learned men, and they had something new to tell the king and his courtiers every day.

One day, the King thought, "Such great men should be given rich rewards."

He invited several hundred people to his palace. "We are here today to honour and reward two of the most learned men in our land," he announced. "Pandit Gyanraj is... er... Pandit Vidyaraj will be able to help me out." The king suddenly found that he did not know much about either man.

He pulled Pandit Vidyaraj aside. "Pandit Vidyaraj, please tell me all about your friend."

"I must be careful. If I praise Gyanraj the king might give him a better reward than the one he gives me," Vidyaraj thought. "Well... I... er... Your majesty, I do not like to say it, but Pandit Gyanraj is an... ass! He doesn't know anything. He just repeats what he hears from me."

"That's a lie! I have heard Pandit Gyanraj speak and I know he is learned," the King thought to himself, but kept quiet.

Next, he called Gyanraj over. "Pandit Gyanraj, please tell me all about your friend."

Gyanraj had the same thought. "I must be careful. If I praise Vidyaraj, the king might give him a better reward than the one he gives me. Your majesty, since you ask me, I must tell you that Pandit Vidyaraj is a... donkey! He doesn't know anything. He just repeats what he hears from me."

The King was annoyed by their jealousy. "These scholars have read many books but what is the use of all their learning? They are no better than any of us here. How can I honour such men?"

He came up with a plan. "Listen carefully and do as I say," he told his servants.

Sometime later, two servants came in carrying gifts. They placed one covered plate before each pandit.

"What is this, your majesty?" the pandits asked eagerly, lifting the covers.

"Fresh, green grass!" they exclaimed in shock.

The king smiled. "You yourselves have told me that one of you is an ass and the other a donkey. So I have given you the reward asses and donkeys would like best—the finest grass that could be found in the kingdom!"

The pandits felt very ashamed of themselves. They left the palace and no one heard of them again.

docs.title: Dodda's Dubuka
docs.text:
Dodda was invited to his friend's house for tea. His friend's mother served them a steaming hot, sweet dish.

"This is delicious! Delicious!" Dodda praised. "I'll ask my mother to make some at home. What is it called?"

"Kadubu," the mother smiled.

Dodda thanked her and set off for home. "Kadubu... Kadubu... I mustn't forget. Kadubu... Kadubu..." he repeated to himself as he walked. "I'll have to be going now... Kadubu... Kadubu..."

As he was walking through a field, still chanting, he suddenly tripped over a rock. "Ka-A-Aah! Oww... oww... oww!" he yelled as he fell.

Just then, a strange bird flew overhead and let out a loud call: "DUBUKA!"

Dodda stopped rubbing his toe and looked up. "Now what was that sweet called?" he wondered. His mind went blank, and the only word he could remember was the bird's cry. "What's that? Dubuka! Dubuka... Dubuka..."

He ran home excitedly. "Dubuka... Mother! Dubuka! I want Dubuka!"

"Enough of your jokes! What is it?" his mother asked, annoyed.

"Dubuka is Dubuka! And I want some now!" Dodda insisted stubbornly.

His mother lost her temper and gave him a smack. "Well then take this Dubuka and more Dubukas! Silly boy! Why did you make me beat you? Now your poor face looks... like a Kadubu."

"Kadubu! That's it!" Dodda's eyes widened. "That's what I want! Kadubu!"

"Then why didn't you say so? I'll make some for you right away," she said with a sigh.

She made plenty of them, and for three days, silly Dodda ate nothing but Kadubus—morning, noon, and night.

docs.title: The Heavenly Elephant
docs.text:
One day a labourer was looking for work in the fields when he saw an incredible sight—a magnificent white elephant descending from the sky. "No elephant on earth can fly! It must have come from heaven," he marvelled.

He hid behind a bush and watched. After a while, the elephant finished grazing and prepared to leave. "It's about to leave! I'll go to heaven with it!" The daring man ran out and grabbed the elephant's tail just as it lifted off the ground.

"Aha! Heaven! There should be plenty of work for me here," he thought as they soared into the clouds.

The man worked in heaven the rest of the day and spent the night there. The next morning, the elephant prepared to return to earth. "Ah, here comes the elephant! Now how do I get back to earth?" He simply grabbed the tail again and rode the elephant down.

When the man returned home, he told everyone where he had been. "It's such a wonderful place... and it's such a joy to work there! They pay one measure of rice for every hour of work! And their measure is so large! See how much rice I got for half a day's work!"

After hearing his tale, all the villagers wanted to go to heaven too. So the next morning the man took them to the field where he had seen the elephant.

"Quiet, now! Don't make a noise or he may not land here," he warned them.

As the villagers watched with bated breath, the elephant landed. "Quiet! Here he comes," the man whispered. The elephant peacefully grazed, and then as it was about to leave, the man sprinted forward and grabbed its tail.

"Now take this bag," the second villager grabbed the first man's legs. The third grabbed the second's legs, and so on. Soon, a long human chain of villagers was flying upward towards heaven.

"We'll stay up there as long as we can! We'll return with sackloads of grain!" they chattered excitedly as they dangled in the sky.

"How much did you say they pay for one hour's work?" the man at the very bottom of the chain shouted up to the first man.

"One measure of rice!" he shouted back.

"How big is the measure?"

"THIS BIG!" the first man proudly demonstrated, letting go of the elephant's tail to hold his hands wide apart.

The next moment, all of them plunged screaming down from the sky. SPLASH! They all fell into a deep river.

The heavenly elephant was so startled by the noise and the splash that he never came to that village again.

docs.title: The Man Who Loved Vadas
docs.text:
One day Khandoba, the miser, felt a strong craving. "Wife! Will you make some vadas for me?" he asked.

"Yes?" she replied. "I will, if you get me some oil and some dal."

"You'll need this tin for the oil, won't you?" she asked, handing him a container.

"No, I won't," Khandoba said, too lazy to carry it. He put on his large white turban and went to a shop in the market.

"Can you show me the best oil you have?" he asked.

"Try this one, sir. It's the best in the market," the shopkeeper offered.

"Let me smell it and see." Khandoba bent forward, as if to smell the oil in the large drum. Suddenly, his loose turban slipped and fell right into the oil!

"Oh, dear! My turban!" he cried.

"Oh, no!" the shopkeeper gasped. He quickly fished out the dripping cloth. "I'm extremely sorry, sir. Your turban..."

"Never mind. Give it to me. I'll have it washed and..."

"No, my friend. I'm in a hurry. I must leave right away. I'll come back later for the oil," Khandoba said quickly, snatching his oil-soaked turban.

Back at home, Khandoba squeezed the heavy turban into a vessel. "The vessel is full!" his wife exclaimed in surprise. "But I don't understand why you brought the oil in your turban."

"Now take this bag," she offered another container.

"What for?"

"To get the dal."

"I have my turban," he grinned mischievously.

At the market, he went to a grain merchant. "I want some good dal, sir. Can you show me the best you have?"

"Here. Have a look at the dal in these bags, sir."

"Let me... oh! My turban!" Khandoba deliberately knocked his turban into the open sack of dal. The lentils stuck to the oily cloth.

"I'll see you later," he said, picking up the dal-coated turban and rushing off.

"Here you are! All the dal you need," Khandoba presented his turban to his wife.

"Why couldn't you use a bag as everyone else does?" she sighed.

"I have my reasons. Now I am going out. I want the vadas to be ready by the time I return."

Khandoba's wife ground the dal, patted the paste into vadas, and began to fry them. Soon she had a plateful of crisp, hot vadas ready.

"M-m-m-m...! Delicious!" she tasted one. "I think I'd better taste one and see... I'll have just one more, while it's hot." Before she knew it, she couldn't stop.

Sometime later Khandoba returned. "Are the vadas ready?"

"Ye..s. Twenty of them," she replied nervously.

He looked at the single vada on the plate. "You ate nineteen vadas! How could anyone eat so many vadas!" he exploded.

"Like this!" she said, popping the last vada into her mouth.

"Hey! Wait! I...!" Khandoba lunged, but it was too late. Poor Khandoba, despite all his scheming, had to go without the vadas.

docs.title: Rama to the Rescue
docs.text:
In a village, one night, a tired man came home. "Oh-humm! What a hard day it has been! I'm feeling so sleepy..." he yawned, lying down to rest next to his wife.

Suddenly, his wife nudged him. "What's the matter? What are you looking at?" he mumbled sleepily.

"Listen! Ssssh!" she whispered, pointing towards the door.

"Somebody is trying to get in," he realized, hearing a scratching sound at the wooden door.

"I... I think he has already got in... The noise has stopped," his wife whispered, trembling with fear.

"Lie down... don't look," he whispered back. "It's a thief... he must have somehow got past Rama."

Rama was the village Kotwal (head of the police), a large, imposing man with a thick moustache, known for keeping the village safe.

"What should we do?" his wife whimpered.

"I'll tell you. Listen..." he replied in a low voice.

Meanwhile, the thief, a burly man with a mask over his eyes, had slipped into the house. He heard them whispering in the bedroom. "Voices! They're awake. I'll have to wait till they fall asleep," he thought, hiding behind the open door. He eyed a large wooden chest in the corner. "I wonder where they keep their money."

He strained his ears to hear the couple's conversation. "They're saying something. Perhaps they're talking about their money. I'd better listen closely."

The husband had devised a clever plan. He spoke up in a loud, clear voice. "If he is a boy we'll call him Rama."

"Rama? Yes... that's a good name," his wife played along loudly.

"When he's in the house, I'll call out softly to him. Rama! Rama!" the husband continued. The thief, hiding just outside the door, thought it was a bit strange.

"But what if he's in the yard?" the husband asked loudly. "Then I'll call out a little louder. RAMA, RAMA!"

The thief grinned in the darkness. "I wish they would stop this silly game and talk about their money instead," he thought impatiently.

"Or fall asleep at least!" the thief grumbled to himself.

"But my dear, what if the boy is not in the house or in the yard, but in the street?" the husband yelled.

"Oh, then I'll call out very loudly..." his wife replied at the top of her voice.

"RAMA! RAMA!" the husband screamed into the quiet night.

Down the street, the village Kotwal heard his name echoing through the air. "RAMA! RAMA!"

"Someone's calling me," Rama thought, grabbing his staff and sprinting towards the sound. He ran straight to the house from which he heard his name being called. "Oh, oh! Some thief has dug his way into this house," he noticed the hole in the wall.

Inside, the thief finally let out a sigh of relief as the couple fell silent. "Ah, at last they've stopped their chatter! Now soon they'll go to sleep and... eh!"

Suddenly, a heavy hand grabbed his shoulder. "You're under arrest," Rama boomed, lifting his staff.

"It's Rama! He heard us!" the husband whispered joyfully to his wife.

"The plan worked! We're saved," she smiled. The man and his wife, by their cleverness, had saved themselves from being robbed.

docs.title: How the Miser Outsmarted Himself
docs.text:
Once upon a time there was a miserly king. One day, a son was born to him. While everyone else rejoiced, this made him very unhappy.

"Now I'll have to give a feast to the villagers," he grumbled to his Diwan. "And they eat so much! What should I do?"

"Ah! I have an idea!" the Diwan whispered, hatching a cunning plan.

At the feast, each guest sat eagerly in front of a large plate. But they were shocked when they were served just two laddoos each.

"Is this all we are going to get?" one man frowned.

"It's shameful!" another agreed angrily.

The king stepped forward with a wide grin. "Good people, today we are going to play a wonderful game. You must be wondering why you have been served just two laddoos. Well, as soon as you finish those two, you will be served another two and when you finish those, another two and so on."

The guests looked pleased.

"Besides, for every laddoo you eat you will be paid two rupees. BUT..." the king paused dramatically. "...you must eat the laddoos without bending your arm at the elbow and without putting your mouth to your plate."

The king stepped back, looking extremely smug. "Now you may start."

The guests were perplexed. "How can we eat without bending our arms? Or without putting our mouths to our plates?" they grumbled, trying to reach the sweets with stiff arms.

"They'll have to get up without eating now! Heehee!" the king chuckled to his Diwan.

Then suddenly one man got an idea. "I know how we can do it!" he whispered to the man next to him.

"What are they whispering?" the king wondered.

"Let them whisper. There's no way they can eat without bending their elbows... or without... what are they doing!" the king's eyes widened in horror.

The clever guests had figured it out. Instead of feeding themselves, they simply picked up the laddoos with straight arms and fed them to the person sitting across from them!

"They've tricked me! Each man is feeding his neighbour!" the king cried.

The guests ate and ate... much to the dismay of their host. They fed each other plate after plate of sweets.

At the end of the feast, each guest had eaten at least fifty laddoos!

"My stomach is full and I have earned a hundred rupees," one guest burped happily.

"I have earned a hundred and two!" his friend grinned.

"Stop! Stop! Don't eat so much! You'll get a stomach-ache!" the king wept, watching his treasury drain away. "I tried to save money and ended up paying out a fortune."

docs.title: The Rat and the Cat
docs.text:
In the depths of a forest, there was a large banyan tree. In a hole, at the foot of this tree, lived a rat named Palita. Amidst the branches of the same tree there dwelt a cat called Lomasha, and Palita was in constant fear of him.

"Lomasha must still be up there. I had better stay here till he leaves," Palita would tremble in his hole. "Ah! I can see Lomasha now! Every day he devours so many of those poor birds!"

One day, a hunter came to the forest. Each day, he would spread out his net at sunset and come back at dawn the next day to check if an animal had been trapped.

Lomasha was careful to keep away from the net. But, one day, he made a mistake. "Oh, this is terrible! The more I struggle, the more firmly I am trapped!" the cat wailed, caught in the hunter's snare.

With Lomasha firmly in the net, Palita became very bold. Enjoying his new-found freedom, Palita jumped about near the very snare which held the cat prisoner. "What joy! This is the greatest day of my life! Ha! Ha! Dear Lomasha! You have no idea how happy I am!"

But his joy was short-lived. Suddenly his eye fell upon a new danger—the arrival of a terrible foe! "Oh! A mongoose!"

He looked around for a way to escape and got another shock—there was an owl on the tree! "What shall I do? If I stay here, that owl will tear me to pieces with his sharp beak... but if I try to run away the mongoose will swallow me up! On every side there is danger! Death itself is staring me in the face!"

Turning to the cat, he talked to him with friendly concern. "Dear Lomasha, I wish to help you. If you agree to do the same for me, I will release you from your present plight. Will you listen to me?"

"Do you really mean to help me? A short while ago you were rejoicing at my misfortune!" the cat sneered.

"Things are different now. I thought of you as my only enemy but I had forgotten about the mongoose and the owl. Look, there they are, waiting to pounce upon me! I thought I was lucky when my worst enemy, Lomasha, was ensnared. But, in a way, he protected me from the others by his very presence. And now he needs me and I need him."

"Friend, the answer is very simple: let me lie crouched under your body till they leave!" Palita proposed.

Of course, Lomasha agreed. Palita quickly snuggled up to the cat. The cat did not move, but lay very still. "Isn't it extraordinary? They don't even think of Palita as a rat! He is my saviour!" Lomasha thought.

"What a strange turn of events! I actually feel safe under the body of Lomasha, the cat!" Palita marvelled.

Meanwhile, the mongoose had his eyes fixed on Palita. "A rat in the arms of a cat! I've never seen anything like it in my life! Anyway, I can't wait here forever. I'll find something else to eat!" He turned and left. Pretty soon the owl, too, gave up and flew away.

After this, Palita began to cut the strings of the net with his sharp teeth. But Palita, in accordance with his own plans, worked very slowly.

"Dear friend, don't waste time. Do cut these strings quickly! It will soon be dawn and the hunter will be here!" Lomasha pleaded.

"Patience! All in good time!" Palita replied.

Palita continued to work slowly while Lomasha waited trembling with fear. When, at last, the sun rose in the east, Palita saw the hunter approaching. "See, the hunter is approaching! Now I will set you free!"

Palita cut the last strings that held Lomasha, just as the hunter reached the scene. The cat bounded away while the rat quickly leaped to his hole.

When the puzzled hunter had gone away, the cat addressed his new-found friend. "Oh, Palita, I am very grateful to you for saving my life! Let there be peace between us always! I will ask all my kinsmen and friends not to hurt you."

But the rat was wise. "You are mistaken, O Lomasha! There are times when self-interest can make a foe a friend. But sooner or later they will become enemies. It is possible you are pretending to be my friend—just to catch me the more easily! No, Lomasha, go your way—and keep your distance from the evil hunter!"

The cat shivered on hearing the word 'hunter' and made haste to run away.

docs.title: The Tiger and the Jackal
docs.text:
Deep in the jungles of the Himalayas, there was a jackal who lived the life of a recluse. This jackal was compassionate and truthful, whereas his companions were cruel and rapacious.

"What strange behaviour is this? One would think he was an ascetic practising austerities!" the other jackals sneered. "And we jackals relish flesh."

"Look, friend, the way in which you live won't do! We don't like it!" they confronted him. "All of us will give you meat. You don't have to live like an ascetic!"

"Thank you for your kind concern, but I am quite happy living on fruit," he replied gently.

"Such behaviour is perverse! Every one must remain true to his nature! Let's go and give him a bit of advice," they muttered. "Ha! Do you imagine that by imitating a higher caste you can change your position in society?"

"It's true I am low-born but I am determined that my conduct shall not be low," the righteous jackal stood his ground.

"So you believe it is up to you to decide on your conduct? Don't you realise it has all been laid down for us generations ago? We jackals have strictly followed the same rules as our fathers!"

"Friends, since we are responsible for our actions does it not follow that we have a choice over our actions? I do not wish to lead your kind of life. It is evil. It leads to discontent and temptation."

"Pah! It's no use talking to him! Let's leave him alone!" the jackals gave up.

The Tiger who was the King of the Forest had been eavesdropping. He drew near as soon as the others had left. "O righteous person! I heard every word you uttered. I respect you for such honourable sentiments! He seems to be a truly learned person of blameless conduct. Most unusual for a jackal! He is worthy of respect!"

"Please come to my court and be my Chief Minister," the Tiger offered.

"I thank you for your offer, O mighty king, but... I do not want luxury such as court life affords. I have never served another. I am quite happy where I am," the jackal politely declined.

"However, if you still wish to appoint me, I will obey—provided you treat my advice with respect and allow me to give it to you in private," the jackal added.

"I gladly agree to your conditions!" the Tiger accepted.

So the jackal went to the court and was treated by the king with great respect. But his strict rules angered the corrupt ministers. "He does not always get work because the jackal in charge wants a bribe from him!" the jackal would ensure justice. But those who were exploiting the poor animals were very angry.

"That minister is one of us. Why should he side with the common people?" the corrupt jackals grumbled. They plotted against him.

They stole the King's meat and placed it in the Chief Minister's room. "Now look for the ladies," they whispered.

"He has done an unforgivable thing. He has set the hare free and sent our good friend, the magistrate, to jail!" they complained to the king.

"Look at that fresh piece of meat laid out for the king! Take it away at once and put it in the Chief Minister's room! Leave the rest to me!" the leader of the corrupt jackals plotted.

Minutes later, the king came in. "Who has dared to touch my plate before I have eaten? SPEAK UP!" the Tiger roared.

"It was the Chief Minister. We saw him take the meat away to his rooms," the corrupt jackals lied.

"It can't be true!" the Tiger roared.

"If you will come with us, your majesty, we will show you where it is!"

There, in the Chief Minister's room, was the meat, placed by the rascally jackals!

"How can I trust anyone ever again? I thought he was honest but the fact is, he is only another jackal! I can't bear to be made a fool of like this! The Chief Minister shall be executed at once!" the Tiger commanded.

Soon after, the king's mother heard about it and hurried to speak to her son. "This cannot be true! I am certain the Chief Minister is innocent, the victim of some evil plot! I saw it with my own eyes!"

"The Chief Minister is innocent! To plot his downfall, my friends lied about him to you!" she explained. "You trusted him too much, O king! We dared not tell you the truth about him before!"

The king realized his mistake. He sent for the Chief Minister. "Good jackal, I was wrong to make so hasty a judgement against you. Please resume your duties."

"O king, first you bestowed upon me the highest honours of the land. Then you treated me as your enemy. I have wronged you. I should have judged by your actions which have always been just. Trust once lost cannot be regained. Such a scar permanently mars relationships. Please give me your permission to go away," the jackal replied sadly.

He then went away and spent the rest of his days in solitude in the forest.

docs.title: The Sage and the Dog
docs.text:
In a dense forest, inhabited only by wild animals, there once lived a good Rishi. The wild creatures loved him and came to him to be taught by him.

"That dog is the most devoted of all his disciples!" one of the animals whispered, pointing to a skinny dog sitting by the sage.

"Never once has he left the side of his guru! And would you believe it? The dog lives on nothing but fruits, roots and water!"

"How extraordinary! Are you sure?"

"I am certain! Look at him! He's so weak and thin due to fasting."

One day, it so happened that a leopard strayed into the hermitage. Hungry and thirsty, and his jaws wide open, the leopard looked like a second Yama, the Lord of Death, and he wanted to pounce upon the dog.

Trembling with fear, the dog turned to his guru. "Oh holy one! This leopard wishes to eat me! Please... find some way of saving me!"

"Don't be afraid. My son! Let thy natural form disappear and... be thou a leopard!" the Rishi commanded.

There was a blinding flash like a sun exploding. The dog was transformed into a leopard with a coat which gleamed like burnished gold! Seeing this magnificent animal of his own species, the leopard who had come to eat him fled.

Some time later, a fierce tiger came to the hermitage. GR...RR... The leopard who was really a dog, crouched in fear.

With his inner vision, the Rishi sensed the danger and quickly transformed him into a powerful tiger. Seeing this new tiger, the hungry tiger left at once.

The dog, now transformed into a regal tiger, gave up his former austere diet and began to subsist on other animals. One day, having had a large meal, he was sleeping in the yard of the hermitage.

When a rogue elephant came there, looking like a grey cloud on a stormy horizon, the tiger rushed to the Rishi for protection.

In an instant, the Rishi transformed him into a magnificent elephant!

"I must be going mad! I thought I was chasing a tiger but he appears to be one of my own species!" the rogue elephant thought, turning away.

Delighted with his new form, the Rishi's elephant wandered off to a nearby lake, enjoying the cool water. "Ha! Ha! He's gone!"

Then one day, a slayer of all animals came to the spot. It was a Sharabha, a terrible mythical animal with eight legs, and he made straight for the Rishi's elephant!

But the elephant, through the Rishi's grace, had become a Sharabha, too. Only he was bigger and more fierce than the other one! Frightened, the wild Sharabha began to slink away.

Now our Sharabha possessed the field. But, with the successive changes, he had changed in other ways too. No longer did he live on fruits and roots. He was now confirmed in the habits of a carnivorous beast.

And now, ever thirsting for fresh blood, he wished to slay the sage!

The sage divined his intention at once. "You dog! It was to me you owed your transformations as leopard, tiger, elephant! And finally, having become a Sharabha, you wish to do me injury! Be thou a dog again!"

As the sage chanted, the grand form of the Sharabha melted away, leaving behind the skinny, trembling dog.

As before, the dog rested at the feet of his guru... but he had become very cheerless. So the Rishi drove him away.

"Go away, sinful creature! No one should be placed in a position for which he is not suited! You are certainly fit only to be a dog!" the sage scolded.

docs.title: The Swan and the Crow
docs.text:
There once lived a crow who was the favourite of three boys. Every day they fed him with the remnants of their food.

"Come, lovely crow, we won't harm you! Eat! Eat!" the boys would call out.

"Doesn't he look lovely? Look at his glossy wings!" they praised.

The admiration of the children filled the crow with pride. "...And he believed himself to be the handsomest, cleverest bird in the land."

Just then he spotted a flock of swans gliding across the sky. ...And the sight irritated him!

"Strangers here! This beach is mine, I won't tolerate any ugly birds in my territory!" the crow cawed loudly.

The leader of these swans heard this as he was gliding by. "Ugly, my dear sir! Ugly! How can you say such a thing! Nobody would possibly dispute a swan's beauty! Besides we are the world's best fliers!"

"What nonsense! All you can do is flutter in the breeze like a torn ribbon..." the crow sneered. "...Whereas I...! Look! Just see what I can do as a flier!"

He turned several times in the air like an acrobat. He made zig-zag patterns in the air and... even flew upside down for a bit.

"Good Lord, what a clumsy fool!" the swan laughed.

"Those are just tricks! Any circus clown could do them! I know only one kind of flying—and I can beat you at that!" the swan challenged gently.

"Done! I take up your challenge!" the crow squawked eagerly.

The competition began at once. The swan gradually gained height and speed. Soon he was flying effortlessly across the ocean.

But the crow was so exhausted he could barely keep himself above the water. "What shall I do? There is not a tree in sight! Where can I stop to rest?" the crow panicked as the waves crashed below him.

"Help! Or I'll drown!" the crow cried out, his wings giving way.

Just then the swan returned. "What kind of flying is that, friend?" the swan asked calmly.

"Oh, don't taunt me! I am so tired! In a little while I will drown and die!" the crow gasped, his beak and wings constantly touching the water.

Taking pity on him, the swan took him on his back... and flew towards the shore. He placed the crow gently on the sand.

"Brother swan! You are my superior!" the crow admitted, his pride completely shattered. "Prosperity makes foolish ones more foolish. I am indeed only a crow. Thank you for putting me in my place!"

docs.title: Ganesha and the Moon
docs.text:
Once on his birthday, Ganesha was invited to Chandralok, the abode of the Moon, for a feast.

"Everything is splendid, but I like these modaks best," Ganesha declared, happily popping the sweet dumplings filled with coconut and jaggery into his mouth. He ate plate after plate of them.

Ganesha ate all the modaks that were kept before him. Very soon, he leaned back, rubbing his large belly. "I have eaten too much. I feel so uncomfortable. I need to get some fresh air. I'll go for a ride on my mouse."

It was a beautiful night. The moon shone so brightly, it was almost like day! Ganesha rode his mouse steadily along the path.

Suddenly, a large snake slithered across the road.

"SQUEAK!" the startled mouse cried, rearing up on its hind legs in terror.

The sudden movement threw Ganesha off balance. "Oh no! All the modaks I ate have fallen out," he cried as he hit the ground with a heavy thud, his stomach bursting open and the modaks spilling out.

"Pardon me, Ganesha. I did not mean to frighten your mouse," the snake hissed apologetically.

Up in the sky, the Moon had watched the entire incident. The sight of the elephant-headed god tumbling off his tiny steed was too much for him. The Moon burst out laughing. "HA HA HA! You look so funny! What a fall that was!"

Ganesha was very angry. But first, he put all the modaks back into his stomach. "...And used the snake to tie his stomach up," securing it like a belt.

"HO HO HO!" the Moon continued to roar with laughter. The Moon just would not stop laughing.

Furious at being mocked, Ganesha broke off a piece of his own tusk. "...And hurled it, furiously... slashing the Moon's luminous face."

"O foolish Moon! Your unblemished appearance has made you vain! I command you to vanish from the universe!" Ganesha cursed him.

Instantly, the Moon disappeared, and there was chaos in heaven and on earth.

"Oh! I cannot see anything. What has happened to the Moon? It is so dark!" the people cried.

Realizing the terrible consequences of his vanity, the Moon was sorry and came to Ganesha with the other gods.

"I have learned my lesson. I will never mock you again. Take back your curse, O Lord! The world is plunged in darkness," the Moon pleaded.

The ever-forgiving Ganesha thought for a while. Then he said, "You were wrong to laugh at my discomfort. I cannot take back my curse but I can soften it. From now on, you will not glow brightly every night."

"You will wax and wane from one bright fortnight to one dark fortnight alternately," Ganesha declared. "You will be seen as a full moon, in all your splendour, only once a month. Also, you will be invisible for one night, every month."

The Moon bowed before Ganesha... and went back to his place in the sky, destined to wax and wane forevermore.

docs.title: Ganesha and Ravana
docs.text:
Ravana, the demon king of Lanka, observed severe penance to please Shiva. He stood on one leg amidst the icy peaks of the Himalayas, chanting "Om Namah Shivaya!"

Pleased with his austerities, Shiva appeared before him. "Ask for a boon, Ravana. I will grant you anything you wish for."

"Lord, I want the Atmalinga," Ravana asked eagerly.

Shiva was dismayed. "The Atmalinga has the power to make him invincible. But I have promised to give him whatever he asks for."

Reluctantly, Shiva handed over the sacred Atmalinga. "You must walk all the way to Lanka holding the Atmalinga in your hands. It's a long way from Mount Kailash to Lanka, but I can do it," Shiva warned. "Don't put the Atmalinga down. If you do, even for a moment, it will stay rooted to that spot for ever."

Ravana was jubilant. "No one can ever destroy me! I have acquired the very power of Shiva!" And then Shiva disappeared.

Ravana started his journey back home, carefully carrying the sacred linga.

Up in the heavens, the gods watched, aghast. "Ravana is our enemy and a menace to the world! With the Atmalinga, his power will be greater than ever! He will defeat us all!"

They sought Lord Ganesha's help. "O Lord! Stop Ravana from taking the Atmalinga to Lanka. If not, the world will be in mortal danger."

Ganesha thought for a while. "Hmmm... that is not an easy task." And then, "Do not worry. I will find a way to stop Ravana." The gods were reassured.

Ganesha followed Ravana from the heavens. Ravana walked very fast. He soon reached the west coast and strode along the deserted sea shore.

"There he is! He must not see me," Ganesha thought, hiding behind a tree. "Now is my chance!"

"Ravana is a stickler for rituals. He has to perform the evening prayers at sunset every day," Ganesha knew. He invoked the power of Lord Vishnu to block out the sun with his chakra, making it suddenly dark.

Ravana was puzzled for a moment. "Strange how the sun set so suddenly! But he was unsuspecting. It is time for me to say my evening prayers."

"Ah, you creature of habit! This will cause your defeat," Ganesha chuckled.

Ravana found himself in a dilemma. "I cannot put the linga down and I cannot pray with it in my hands. I should ask someone to hold it for me. But there is no one here!" He looked around, worried.

Just then, he saw a young Brahmin boy walking on the beach. It was Ganesha in disguise. Ravana immediately held out the Atmalinga. "Here, boy, hold this for me."

The boy shook his head. "No, I can't! Just hold it till I finish my prayers. It's too heavy for me. Keep it on the ground."

"If it is too heavy for me, can I put it on the ground?" the boy asked innocently.

"NO! NO! Then I can't hold it," Ravana panicked.

"Well... all right," the boy agreed reluctantly.

Relieved, Ravana handed over the Atmalinga and walked into the sea to begin his prayers.

The wily Ganesha waited until Ravana was in the middle of his prayers. Then, he shouted loudly, "THIS IS TOO HEAVY FOR ME! I AM GOING TO PUT IT DOWN!"

Ravana was alarmed. "NO, DON'T! I'LL COME AS SOON AS I FINISH. JUST DON'T PUT IT DOWN," he yelled back from the water.

Ravana hurried through his prayers, trying to finish as soon as possible, when Ganesha shouted again. "IT'S TOO HEAVY! I'M PUTTING IT DOWN!"

Ravana finished his prayers immediately, and rushed back, but it was too late. The boy had placed the linga on the sand.

Suddenly, the sun was back in the sky. Vishnu had removed the illusion.

"I HAVE BEEN TRICKED!" Ravana roared in fury. He tried to move the linga. "IT WON'T BUDGE!"

An enraged Ravana chased the boy. "TAKE THAT, YOU RASCAL!" he yelled, hitting him thrice on his head with his fists.

Then Ganesha assumed his true form. Realising what he had done, Ravana bowed before Ganesha and accepted his defeat.

The place where Ganesha placed the Atmalinga is believed to be in Gokarna, Karnataka. There is a temple built around the Atmalinga. The temple over the Atmalinga is called the Mahabaleshwar temple. 'Mahabala' means great power and 'Ishwar' means God. Next to it is a shrine of Ganesha, where the idol has a dent on its forehead, symbolising the incident.

The great force exerted by Ravana on the linga is said to have changed its shape. The linga now resembles the ear of a cow. In fact, the name Gokarna, comes from this. 'Go' - cow and 'Karna' - ear.

docs.title: Ganesha Releases Kaveri
docs.text:
One such story begins at the wedding of Shiva and Parvati. It was an auspicious occasion. All the gods, goddesses and sages gathered at Mount Kailash, in the Himalayas.

Suddenly, the ground started to sink! The sheer weight of all the divine beings in one place was tilting the earth. "OH! AAAAAH! HELP! HELP!" the gods cried as boulders rolled down the mountains.

Shiva calmed everyone down and then summoned Sage Agastya, one of the Saptarishis. "The land, here in the north, is tilting because all of us are gathered here. If you travel south to the Podhigai hills, you will act as a counter-balance and the lands will be stable," Shiva commanded.

"O Maheshwara, I shall do as you say," Agastya bowed.

Then Shiva took a few drops of sacred water from his matted locks. "Let me pour this into your water pot. The water pot now contains the sacred river, Kaveri. Find a suitable spot in the mountains to set it down. The river will then flow out in whichever direction you point."

Bidding farewell, Agastya travelled south. On the way, he visited many temples of Shiva.

Time passed and Agastya wandered in the southern lands, looking for the perfect place to set down Kaveri. In the meantime, Indra, king of the gods, was attacked by a powerful demon named Surapadman.

"Surapadman has the upper hand. I must escape," Indra fled, riding his white elephant, Airavata.

Indra fled to the earth. He stopped at a place called Sirgazhi, where he created a magnificent garden. "I will propitiate Shiva with flowers from my garden and ask him for help against the wicked Surapadman," he planned.

But there was a terrible drought. "Alas! The flowers in my garden have wilted! How will I continue my prayers to Lord Shiva?" Indra wept.

Indra pleaded with Varuna, the rain god. "O Varuna, please blow some rain-bearing clouds here, so that my garden can bloom again."

"I cannot, Indra. Surapadman has threatened to attack me if I help you," Varuna replied helplessly.

Narada overheard them. "Poor Indra! I must help him," Narada thought. He went to Indra. "Narada told Indra about Agastya's water pot. Pray to Lord Ganesha. He will find a way to help you."

"I will do as you say, Narada," Indra agreed.

Ganesha answered Indra's prayers. "Your garden will get water, Indra," Ganesha promised. He took the form of a crow. "Agastya has the river Kaveri. I will find him and end the drought. He must be somewhere here."

Ganesha soon spotted Agastya meditating near a cliff. The crow flew down and perched himself on the water pot.

"There he is!" the crow cawed.

Agastya chased the crow away. "Shoo! Shoo!" he waved his hand.

As the crow flew off, its claws tipped the pot over! Inside the water pot, the river Kaveri misinterpreted Agastya's gesture... and flowed out in the direction he was pointing.

Suddenly, the crow took the form of a small boy. "Playing tricks on me? Come here!" an angry Agastya chased him.

Agastya caught the boy. He was about to rap him on the head with his knuckles, when the boy changed his form.

"Lord Ganesha!" Agastya gasped in shock.

"O Vighneshwara, forgive me! I almost hit you!" Agastya fell to his knees.

In repentance, Agastya began striking his forehead with his knuckles. "Stop, Agastya..." Ganesha gently held his hand. "...You are truly repentant for a deed you were about to commit unknowingly. This is a sign of true wisdom."

The drought was over. Indra's garden flourished again. The river Kaveri still flows across South India. The river Kaveri is often called the Ganga of South India. It originates in the Brahmagiri hills in Coorg, in a place called Talakaveri. Many stories are told about how the Kaveri came to flow in this region.

docs.title: Ganesha and Gana
docs.text:
The Chintamani Vinayaka temple at Theur is one of the Ashtavinayaka temples, the eight Ganesha temples near Pune. Like all the others, this temple has its own story.

King Abhishek and his beautiful queen Gunavati, were full of despair for they had no children. They went to a sage for advice. "How can we get an heir, O wise one?"

"Do not worry. Perform the penances I tell you to and you will surely have a son," the sage promised.

Just as the sage had predicted, the queen gave birth to a son. "Our son is so radiant! We shall call him Gana," the king smiled.

The young prince grew up to be a great devotee of Shiva. He prayed fervently, and Shiva appeared before him. "I am pleased with your devotion, Gana. I will grant you some boons," Shiva offered.

Shiva's boons made Gana incredibly powerful, but also arrogant and cruel. He rode through the lands, pillaging and harassing the people.

One day, Gana and his friends went hunting. "But there was no sign of a kill for hours," they rode through the forest in frustration.

After wandering for a while, they reached the ashram of Sage Kapila. "Welcome, young prince! You honour me with your presence! Please join me for a meal," the sage greeted them warmly.

"This is the Chintamani gem given to me by Lord Indra himself. It has the power to grant me whatever I desire," Sage Kapila revealed to the astonished guests. He closed his eyes and chanted a few words, and a magnificent feast appeared before them.

"What a wonderful spread! So much food!" Gana's friends marvelled.

As he ate, Gana's selfish mind had but one thought. "I must have this gem. This gem is wasted here. Let me take it to my palace where it will get the honour due to it," he told Kapila after the meal.

"Thank you, but I prefer to keep it with me," Kapila declined politely.

Gana flew into a rage. "Then I shall take it away from you!" he shouted, snatching the gem from the sage's hand.

An anguished Sage Kapila prayed to Ganesha for help. "Please get back the Chintamani, O Ganesha!"

"Do not worry. I shall restore it to you," Ganesha assured him.

Ganesha appeared in Gana's dream and threatened him. "Aaaargh!" Gana woke up in a sweat, but far from being frightened, he gathered his army for battle.

"Don't fight Ganesha, my son! Repent while you can," his father pleaded.

"Out of my way, father!" Gana yelled, pushing his father aside and thundering towards Sage Kapila's ashram.

A fierce battle ensued. Ganesha killed Gana with his axe and retrieved the gem. (Ganesha is known as Gananayaka, meaning 'one who defeated Gana').

But when he returned the gem to Kapila, the sage looked at it with sadness. "O Ganesha! This gem has brought nothing but misery. Will you keep it?"

Ganesha agreed to do so. "You have taken a great load off my mind by taking the gem!" Kapila smiled.

Sage Kapila then installed an idol of Ganesha. "This form of you, O benevolent Lord, shall be called Chintamani Vinayaka," the sage declared.

Even today devotees flock to the temple of Chintamani Vinayaka in the belief that he can help them get rid of their 'chinta' or worry.

docs.title: The Legend of Shami and Mandar
docs.text:
Sage Aurava was a learned Brahmin, whose face shone with the radiance of knowledge. To him and his wife was born a beautiful daughter named Shami.

When Shami was seven years old, her parents decided, "It is time we got Shami married."

"Yes. We shall wed her to Mandar, son of Rishi Dhaumya," Sage Aurava agreed. In those days it was the custom to get small children married.

After the marriage ceremony, Sage Aurava instructed them, "According to our custom, you will both continue to stay with your parents till you are older."

After some years, Mandar went to Sage Aurava's ashram to bring his bride home. "O sage! With your permission I will take Shami to the home of my guru, Sage Shaunak."

"You have my blessings," Aurava smiled. "You must reach your destination before it is dark, my children. Take the shorter route which goes past the ashram of Sage Bhrushundi."

Bhrushundi had earlier been a fisherman who had turned to thieving. Once, he stopped the Rishi Mugdala to rob him. "Hand over your belongings!" he demanded.

But the sage did not do as Bhrushundi demanded. Instead, a brilliant light shone from him. "Aah! What is this dazzling light that emanates from the Rishi?" Bhrushundi thought. He realised he was in the presence of a great power and asked for forgiveness.

"Rise, my son. Pray to Lord Ganesha and your past sins will be forgiven," Rishi Mugdala advised him.

Bhrushundi took the sage's advice to heart and started worshipping Ganesha with single-minded devotion. Ganesha was very pleased and bestowed a singular honour on him... an elephant trunk growing from his forehead!

On their way to Sage Shaunak's ashram, Shami and Mandar passed Bhrushundi meditating under a tree.

"Look at that, Mandar! A man with an elephant trunk!" Shami pointed.

The two burst into uncontrollable laughter. "I have never seen a funnier sight! Ha ha ha!" they giggled loudly.

Bhrushundi's eyes flew open. They blazed with anger. "Who dares laugh at me?" he demanded. In his rage, he cursed the young couple. "You will become trees! Even animals will turn away from you! No birds shall eat the leaves of the Mandar tree or rest on the thorns of the Shami tree."

"Mandar! My arms!" Shami cried as bark covered her skin.

"Shami! I can't move!" Mandar yelled as his feet rooted into the earth.

Bhrushundi stormed off, leaving the beautiful Shami and her handsome husband, Mandar, rooted to the spot as trees.

Meanwhile, Guru Shaunak waited anxiously for Shami and Mandar. "Why are they taking so long to come?" he wondered. After a few days, he went to Sage Aurava's ashram looking for them.

"But Guru Shaunak, our children left for your ashram days ago!" Aurava exclaimed.

"Something is wrong! Let us set out at once to look for them."

After days of searching, they came to Bhrushundi's ashram where they heard of the terrible fate that had befallen their children. They found the trees.

"My beloved Shami!" Aurava wept, hugging the rough bark.

"Alas! Mandar, my dearest son!" Dhaumya cried.

The two fathers decided to appeal to Ganesha for help and began practising terrible austerities. Standing on one leg in the river, they prayed fervently.

At last, Ganesha appeared before them in an imposing form. They begged Ganesha to restore their children, but Ganesha was in a dilemma.

"I cannot do that. It would displease Bhrushundi, my great devotee. But I can temper his curse."

"I cannot bring your children back to life but they will be honoured in the three worlds," Ganesha declared. "The Shami and Mandar trees will always have a special importance on earth. No worship of me or Shiva will be complete without Shami leaves being placed on the altar. Shiva will be pleased if worshipped with the flowers of the Mandar tree. The 'Panchanga' or five parts of the Shami tree, its roots, flowers, fruits, leaves and bark will have medicinal value."

Then, Ganesha vanished. Sage Aurava was inconsolable with grief. Unable to bear his sorrow he became the fire within the trunk of the Shami tree. "Aurava, my friend!" Dhaumya gasped as Aurava burst into flames and merged with the tree.

Even today, when sacrificial fires are lit, priests still rub pieces of Shami wood together and the hidden fire leaps out. To this day, people tie strips of cloth on the Shami tree as an offering or when making a vow. True to Ganesha's words, the Shami and Mandar tree remain forever auspicious.

docs.title: Ganesha
docs.text:
One day, Parvati, Shiva's wife, posted Nandi, his chief Gana (attendant), at the door to her palace.

"I am going to have my bath. Do not let anyone enter and disturb me," she instructed him firmly.

A little later, Shiva came there. Nandi was in a dilemma. "What should I do? How can I stop him from entering his own home?" he wondered. In the end, he stepped aside.

Shiva entered the palace and strode into the inner rooms.

"My Lord, here? But Nandi was told..." Parvati began in surprise.

"Nandi has disobeyed me," she thought, furious. "You may be my husband but he had no right to let you enter!"

Shiva was amused, but Parvati was deeply annoyed. She spoke to her friends about it. "You are right. None of the Ganas can really be called your own," they agreed.

"I must have a Gana of my own who will obey me alone," Parvati decided.

One of her friends had an idea. "Why don't you create a Gana who will owe first allegiance to you?"

"A good suggestion. I shall," Parvati agreed. She gathered the saffron paste from her own body and carefully sculpted a boy out of it. When she finished, she gazed in admiration at her own creation. "How handsome he is! How strong he looks!"

With her divine powers, she breathed life into him. She decked him with ornaments and blessed him. "You are my son—my very own son. I have none else to call my own," she told him affectionately.

"Command me, Mother. What should I do?" the boy asked, bowing deeply.

"Take this staff and follow me," she said. She led him to the door. "Stand here and let no one enter without my permission."

A little later, Shiva returned. "Who could that boy be? I have never seen him before," Shiva wondered as he approached the door.

To Shiva's astonishment, the youth barred his way. "Halt! No one enters without my mother's permission," the boy declared boldly.

Shiva was taken aback. "Foolish boy! Do you know who I am? Move out of my way," he demanded.

Without uttering a word, the youth struck Shiva with his staff.

Shiva was furious. "Fool! I am Shiva, Parvati's husband. How dare you forbid me to enter my own home!"

But the youth only raised his staff and hit Shiva once again.

Enraged, Shiva turned to his Ganas. "Who is that fool? What is he doing there? Throw him out and report to me."

As Shiva left, the Ganas turned upon the youth. "Get away with you or you will receive a taste of my valour," the boy threatened, swinging his club.

"You get away from here, if you value your life. You seem to forget that we are Shiva's Ganas," they retorted.

The boy stood still for a while. He was puzzled. "Shiva is my mother's lord. What should I do? Should I fight them or shouldn't I?"

Meanwhile, indoors, Parvati had heard the commotion. She sent her friend out to investigate. The friend returned and reported, "They are threatening your son. Please don't allow them to lower your prestige."

Parvati hesitated for a moment. "Shiva is after all my husband..." But why did he try to force his way in? "Let what has to happen, happen," she decided. She sent her friend out again. "Tell my son not to give in to anyone."

The friend came out. "Gentle sir, your mother says that you should not let anyone enter."

The boy was no longer in doubt. He turned to the Ganas. "I am the son of Parvati. You are the Ganas of Shiva. You must carry out his orders and I—hers."

The Ganas were doubtful. "He is Parvati's son. What should we do? Would Shiva still want us to throw him out?" They reported back to Shiva.

"Alas, Parvati! You have gone too far. You have left me with no alternative," Shiva sighed. He turned to his Ganas. "All of you are my own, including Parvati's son. So a battle may not seem proper to you. But if we don't accept the challenge, we may be misunderstood. Go! Fight and defeat him. It should be easy."

So the Ganas, fully arrayed for war, went back to the boy. The boy was amused when he saw them. "I, a mere boy carrying out my mother's orders, welcome you, the leaders of Shiva's hordes."

The Ganas rushed at him. Nandi grabbed his legs, but the boy fought back valiantly. "Ha! That's better," he grinned, kicking Nandi away. Then the boy seized an iron club and whirled it about.

"A..a..ah! My chest! He hit my knees. My head! I cannot move. My legs are broken," the Ganas cried as the boy's club rained down on them. Many of the Ganas fell. The rest fled as fast as they could.

The boy once again took his post at the door.

Meanwhile, when Brahma, Vishnu, and Indra heard the uproar, they talked the matter over with Sage Narada. "Go to Lord Shiva. He may need you," Narada advised.

They went to Shiva. "And he dares to prevent me from entering my own home," Shiva complained.

"O Brahma, go there and try to bring him under control," Shiva requested.

Accompanied by many sages, Brahma left on his mission. "I shall go disguised as a Brahman," he planned.

As they neared him, the youth suddenly jumped forward and started pulling Brahma's beard. "That should teach you a lesson!"

Brahma was taken unawares. "I have not come to fight. I have come to make peace. Listen to me!" he pleaded, but for an answer, the youth lifted his club menacingly.

"Ha! Run. I shall never fail my mother," the boy chased them away.

Meanwhile, the sages ran back to Shiva. "This matter has gone too far. The boy must be destroyed," Shiva declared. He sent for his son, Kartikeya, and Indra, the king of the devas. "Lead your Ganas and Devas to war. Vanquish the impudent upstart."

The Devas, led by Indra, and the Ganas, by Kartikeya, fell upon the boy from all directions. But the boy faced them valiantly.

Meanwhile, Parvati learned of all that had happened. She was furious. "How dare they harass my son!" Then and there, she created two Shaktis, Kali and Durga. "Go! Assist my son."

Kali stood between the boy and the enemy, opening her mouth wide, she swallowed their weapons and hurled them back. Durga took the form of lightning and destroyed the enemies' weapons before they could reach the boy. Between the two of them, they did not let a single weapon come anywhere near the boy's swinging club.

Indra and his Devas were completely routed. Even Kartikeya, who had killed the invincible Tarakasura, was helpless. They held council. "What should we do? Let us go back to Shiva."

Their praise of the boy's prowess only infuriated Shiva. "I will have to kill him with my own hands," Shiva resolved, grabbing his trident.

Shiva thundered out, followed by all of them. Unperturbed by the sight of Shiva, the boy attacked! All the chief gods, one by one they fell.

As Shiva watched him fight, he was amazed. "He is invincible. He can only be killed by cunning. I must watch for an opportunity," Shiva thought.

The same thought had struck Vishnu too. He spoke to Shiva, "I shall use my powers of delusion to fight him, if you permit me."

"You may. That is the only way," Shiva agreed.

But Kali and Durga divined their intentions. "Let us confer all our strength on the youth. Then even Vishnu's Maya will prove ineffectual," they decided.

With the added strength of the two Shaktis, the boy swung his club at Vishnu. Vishnu had to use all his energy to dodge it.

Shiva saw his plight and charged with his trident. But the boy dashed it to the ground with his club. "No wonder my Ganas were helpless," Shiva realised.

The next moment, the boy lifted his club again... but it was cut in two by Vishnu's discus.

The boy hurled the broken piece that was left in his hand... but Vishnu's mount, Garuda, caught it and protected his master.

Then, as the boy picked up his staff to hit Vishnu, Shiva came up from behind and... cut off his head!

For a moment all on the scene stood still, their gaze fixed on the valiant hero who lay dead on the ground. Then the Devas and the Ganas became jubilant. They cheered their victory.

But Shiva was troubled. "Alas! What have I done? How shall I face Parvati? He was created by her. That made him my son too."

Meanwhile, when Parvati learnt of her son's death, her grief turned to unyielding rage. "My son was killed by unfair means. For this the Devas and Ganas shall all die!"

Out of her fury, she created hundreds and thousands of Shaktis. "O Mother, what is your command?"

"Destroy all the Devas and Ganas. Devour them," she ordered.

The Shaktis immediately set about carrying out her command. Brahma and Vishnu were terrified. "Parvati must be appeased," they realised.

They propitiated Parvati and pleaded for mercy. "O great Goddess, have mercy on us. We shall accomplish whatever you ask of us. Forgive us."

"I will," Parvati replied coldly. "But my son must regain his life and must have an honourable status among you."

They went to Shiva and put Parvati's conditions before him. "It shall be done. For the sake of peace and happiness," Shiva agreed.

"Go North. Bring the head of the first creature that crosses your path. Fit that head to the boy's body and it will come to life," Shiva instructed them.

Accompanied by many sages, Brahma left on his mission. It was a single-tusked elephant that met them. They brought the head back and fitted it to the body of the boy.

The boy sat up, alive, with the head of an elephant!

Delighted, they showed him to Parvati. But she was only partially appeased. "What about his status?" she demanded.

At that moment, Indra and the other gods led Shiva to her. Shiva bowed before Parvati. "Forgive me, Parvati. Arrogance is characteristic of the male. This valiant youth shall be another son of mine."

Then Shiva placed his hand on the boy's head. "Even as a mere boy you showed great valour. You shall be Ganesha, the presiding officer of all my Ganas. You shall be worthy of worship forever. You shall also be called Vighneshwara, the queller of obstacles."

Shiva and Parvati once again began to live happily in their abode at Mount Kailash, delighted by the presence of their two sons. To this day, before any venture is undertaken, it is Ganesha who is invoked and whose blessings are sought.

docs.title: Ganga
docs.text:
Ganga, beautiful, pure, and strong-willed, was the elder daughter of Mount Himavan. She had the divine power of purifying anything that touched her.

One day, the Devas, led by Brahma, came to Mount Himavan. "Heaven needs the presence of young Ganga, sanctifier of all creatures," Brahma requested.

"If Ganga goes, the earth will suffer. But it is my duty to look to the good of all the three worlds," Himavan thought. He agreed, saying, "I am willing to send Ganga, who flows everywhere at will, purifying all in her wake, with you."

"May we take her with us now?" the Devas asked.

"Go with the Devas, Ganga, and serve them dutifully," Himavan blessed his daughter.

At about that time, life on Earth was becoming impossible for all righteous people. A mysterious group of Asuras hid in the ocean bed by day and came out to harass them at night, destroying crops and ruining sacrifices.

In heaven, the Devas were perturbed. "We must help the good people on Earth. But how can we, when we don't know who their tormentors are?"

They sought the help of Lord Vishnu. "To kill them you will have to dry up the ocean. Go to Sage Agastya. He alone can help you," Vishnu advised.

The Devas approached Sage Agastya, who readily agreed. He went to the shore, bent down, and began to drink up the entire ocean! As the waters vanished, the evil Asuras were exposed on the dry ocean bed.

"Don't spare the murderers!" the Devas cried, falling upon them and vanquishing the Asuras.

When the battle was won, they turned to Agastya. "O saviour. Now that the evil ones are dead, fill up the ocean again with the water you drank."

"I cannot," Agastya replied. "I have digested it. You will have to find some other means to fill the ocean."

The Devas went to Brahma in dismay. "A very long time must pass before the ocean will once again be full," Brahma prophesied. "King Sagara and his descendants will cause the ocean to be filled up again. He has already begun penances to obtain them."

At that moment on earth, Lord Shiva appeared before King Sagara and his queens, Keshini and Sumati. "I am pleased with your piety. You shall have many sons," Shiva blessed them. "One of your wives shall bear 60,000 proud and heroic sons who will all perish without heirs. Your race however will be continued by the one son that your other wife will bear."

Accordingly, Queen Keshini gave birth to Asamanja, a wicked prince who was eventually banished. But Asamanja had a son, Amsuman, who was kind and loved by the people.

Queen Sumati gave birth to 60,000 strong and arrogant princes.

One day, King Sagara decided to perform the Ashwamedha sacrifice (the horse sacrifice) to declare his supreme power. A sacred horse was let loose, meant to roam freely. Anyone who stopped it would have to fight Sagara's army.

Indra, afraid that Sagara's growing power might dethrone him, stole the sacrificial horse. He hid it in the nether world, right behind Sage Kapila, who was deep in meditation.

When Sagara's 60,000 sons could not find the horse on Earth, they dug a massive tunnel down to the nether world. The digging caused tremendous earthquakes.

In the nether world, they found the horse grazing peacefully near Sage Kapila. "So this sage is the culprit. Thief! Return our horse!" the arrogant princes yelled, rushing to attack the meditating sage.

Angered by their audacity, the powerful Kapila opened his eyes. Fire blazed from his gaze, and he burned all 60,000 princes to ashes by uttering but one syllable!

Meanwhile, King Sagara, worried about his missing sons, sent his grandson Amsuman to look for them. Amsuman followed the tunnel and found the horse... and the massive pile of ashes that used to be his uncles.

He saluted Sage Kapila respectfully. "I have seen them. They were so insolent that I consumed them with the fire of my penances," Kapila explained.

"But do they deserve to suffer in hell too? Is there no way for them to enter heaven?" Amsuman pleaded.

"There is. Ganga in all her purity must be brought down from heaven. If she touches these ashes, your uncles will go to heaven," Kapila told him. "You may take the sacrificial horse back with you."

Amsuman returned with the horse, and the sacrifice was completed. Later, Amsuman became king, but despite his efforts, he failed to bring Ganga down to earth in his lifetime. His son, Dilipa, also tried and failed, dying of a broken heart.

Dilipa's son, Bhagiratha, undertook severe penances to Brahma, standing on one leg in the forest for years.

At last, pleased by his single-mindedness, Brahma appeared. "I shall send Ganga down to earth to redeem your ancestors," Brahma granted. "But the earth cannot sustain the force of her fall. Only Shiva can withstand it. Perform penances to please Shiva."

For a whole year, Bhagiratha adored Shiva. Finally, Shiva appeared. "I will receive the force of Ganga as she descends to Earth," Shiva promised.

In heaven, Ganga made ready to descend. Halfway down, a proud thought occurred to her. "The mighty one is off his guard. I shall sweep him away with me to the nether world!"

But Shiva divined her intentions. As the turbulent Ganga crashed down from the heavens, Shiva caught her in his matted locks. He imprisoned her there, swirling about, unable to escape.

Bhagiratha once again prayed to Shiva. "Lord! You are the source of all kindness. Have pity on me and release Ganga."

"Your devotion merits it. For your sake I will," Shiva agreed, releasing the river.

Ganga, now purified by her contact with the Lord's head, flowed gently onto the earth. Bhagiratha led her towards the ocean.

But Bhagiratha's trials were not yet over. On the way, the irrepressible Ganga rushed forward and flooded the sacrificial fire of Sage Jahnu!

The sage was furious. "You shall be punished for this!" In one long gulp, the fuming sage drank up the entire river!

Bhagiratha and the gods pleaded with the sage. "Forgive her, holy one, and release her. Let not the endeavours of Bhagiratha be wasted."

Out of respect for Bhagiratha's penances, Jahnu relented. He released Ganga from his ear. "Henceforth, Ganga shall also be known as Jahnavi—your daughter, O holy one," the gods declared.

At last, Bhagiratha and Ganga reached the ocean bed and the nether world beyond. As Ganga's holy waters flowed over the ashes of the 60,000 sons of Sagara, their souls were redeemed and ascended to heaven.

The vast, dry ocean bed was filled with Ganga's waters. From that day onwards, the ocean came to be known as "Sagara" in honour of the king.

Brahma appeared before Bhagiratha one last time. "Since you brought Ganga to earth by your penances, she shall also be known as Bhagirathi—your daughter," Brahma blessed him.

His mission accomplished, Bhagiratha returned to his kingdom, ascended the throne, and ruled wisely for many years.

docs.title: Garuda
docs.text:
Sage Kashyap, who had married thirteen of Daksha's daughters, was the progenitor of the Devas and the Asuras, and much of the animal kingdom as well.

One day, he decided to confer a boon each on two of his wives, Vinata and Kadru. He was going to perform a yagna for more offspring.

"I would like to be the mother of a thousand mighty snakes," Kadru said.

"And you, Vinata?"

"I would like to have two sons who will surpass Kadru's thousand snakes in strength and valour," Vinata asked.

His son, Indra, King of the Devas, came with the diminutive Valkhilya rishis and other celestial beings to assist him. Indra was tasked with fetching fuel for the sacrificial fire.

As Indra flew back carrying a heavy log single-handed, he saw the tiny Valkhilya rishis struggling to carry a single twig. "Ha! Ha! Ha! Look at those puny rishis struggling with a mere twig!" Indra laughed mockingly.

The proud rishis were deeply insulted. "His pride must be humbled," they decided.

They started their own sacrificial fire, chanting powerful mantras. "By the fruit of our penances may another Indra be born—mightier than the present one! Let him be capable of going anywhere at will, of mustering any measure of strength at will, and of assuming any form at will."

Indra was terrified. "I'd better ask my father to intercede with the rishis on my behalf," he panicked.

Sage Kashyap spoke to the angry rishis. "O holy ones, the present Indra has been appointed Lord of the heavens by Brahma. Let the Indra you create be Lord of the winged creatures. Be gracious to him who bows before you."

The rishis relented. "So be it. Since this yagna is for offspring for you, accept the fruit of our actions. You shall soon be the mothers of the sons you desire," they told Vinata and Kadru.

Months later, Vinata brought forth two eggs and Kadru a thousand. They were incubated separately in warm vessels. Years rolled by.

One day, Kadru's sons burst open their shells—a thousand wriggling snakes!

For the first time in her life, Vinata felt the pangs of jealousy and impatience. "I'll break open one of them. Perhaps my sons need help," she thought.

So she broke open one of the eggs. To her horror, the lower half of the child inside was a mere lump of flesh.

"Alas! What have I done!" she cried.

The half-formed child pointed an accusing finger at her. "You shall pay for your impatience with slavery. But if you can be more patient with the other egg, my brother will deliver you from your bondage," he cursed. The child ascended to the skies to become Aruna, the charioteer of Surya, the Sun-god.

A few days later, Vinata and Kadru saw the celestial steed, Uchchaishrava, fly past.

"It is white all over. I can lay a wager on it," Vinata said confidentially.

"I think it has a black tail. I'm willing to wager my freedom on that. Let the loser become the other's slave!" Kadru challenged. They agreed to examine the horse the next day.

But Kadru had second thoughts. "Could I be wrong? Vinata was very confident. I must make the horse's tail appear black even if it isn't."

She went to her thousand snake sons. "Go, cover the tail of that horse and make it appear black. I must safeguard my freedom," she ordered.

When some of the snakes hesitated, Kadru cursed them angrily. "And if we don't, she might burn us all up in King Janamejaya's snake sacrifice!" they realized. So they slithered out and covered the white horse's tail, making it look black.

The next day, when Vinata and Kadru examined the horse, its tail was indeed black with the clinging snakes. Vinata lost the wager and was forced into slavery, bearing Kadru on her shoulders.

Meanwhile, at home, Vinata's second son broke his shell and emerged. It was a mighty eagle, Garuda! He grew rapidly and soared into the skies.

When he returned, he saw his mother serving as a slave to the snakes. "Mother, why must we do the bidding of the snakes?" he asked.

Vinata wept and told him the story of the wager and how she was tricked.

Garuda went to the Nagas. "I wish to buy our freedom. What is your price?"

"Bring us Amrit (the nectar of immortality) and you are free," they demanded.

Garuda agreed and rose to the skies. On his way to heaven, he met his father, Sage Kashyap. "Father, where can I find enough food to increase my energy?"

Kashyap pointed to a lake. "Down there you will find a huge tortoise and an elephant. They are bent on killing each other. Eat them and proceed on your mission."

Garuda swooped down, seized the elephant and tortoise in his talons, and flew to find a strong tree to perch on while he ate. He alighted on a massive banyan tree branch, but the moment he landed, the branch broke!

As the branch fell, Garuda saw tiny figures hanging upside down from it. "Why! There are some rishis hanging from it. They must not be harmed," he realized. It was the diminutive Valkhilya rishis!

Garuda caught the falling branch in his beak and flew on gently, searching for a place to put it down safely. He flew all the way to the Himalayas, where the rishis finally left the bough.

His meal over, Garuda resumed his flight to the abode of the Devas.

Meanwhile, terrible omens appeared in Indra's heaven. Lightning struck without clouds, and meteors rained down. Indra consulted his guru, Brihaspati.

"Garuda, the invincible son of Kashyap and Vinata, is on his way to rob you of the nectar of immortality. And he can do it," Brihaspati warned.

Indra and his Devas stood fully prepared to beat him back, surrounding the blazing fire that protected the Amrit.

When Garuda arrived, the skies turned dark with the dust raised by his flapping wings. The Devas were blinded. Garuda attacked with a mighty roar, tearing through the Deva army with his beak, talons, and wings.

Overcoming the onslaught, Garuda approached the pot of Amrit. It was surrounded by a wall of fierce flames. Garuda flew to a river, filled his ninety times ninety mouths with water, and extinguished the fire.

Next, he faced a revolving wheel with razor-sharp edges. Garuda reduced his size in an instant, slipped through the spokes, and reached the centre.

There, two poisonous serpents stood guard, their eyes blazing. Garuda threw up dust to blind them, snatched the pot of Amrit, and rose into the sky!

As he flew along, Lord Vishnu saw him. Vishnu was pleased. "His act of self-denial pleases me. He has the nectar of immortality in his possession but has not touched a drop of it."

Vishnu offered him boons. "I wish to be immortal and ever free from disease without drinking the Amrit. And I wish to be above you," Garuda requested.

"So be it. Now please go and perch on my flagstaff. Even so will you sit above me," Vishnu smiled.

Garuda was pleased. "O Great One, I too shall grant you a boon."

"Then be my vehicle, O mighty Garuda," Vishnu requested. And so it was.

As Garuda continued his flight, an angry Indra hurled his thunderbolt at him. The weapon struck Garuda, but it did not harm him. However, out of respect for the divine weapon, Garuda shed a single feather.

Indra was amazed by Garuda's invincibility. "O greatest of birds, I want your eternal friendship," Indra offered.

Garuda accepted. "I cannot return the nectar," he told Indra, "But I can help you retrieve it. I will place it on Kusha grass for the snakes. When they go for their ablutions before tasting it, you can take it away." Garuda also asked for the snakes to become his natural food, which Indra granted.

Garuda returned to the snakes. "Here is Amrit for you. I will place it on this Kusha grass while you go for your ablutions before tasting it," he announced. "Let my mother become free from this moment."

"Your mother is free," the snakes agreed, slithering away to purify themselves.

As planned, the moment they left, Indra swooped down and took the pot of Amrit back to heaven.

When the snakes returned and found the nectar gone, they desperately licked the Kusha grass where the pot had been placed, hoping for a few drops. The sharp grass split their tongues in two. And that is why, to this day, all snakes have forked tongues.

docs.title: True Friends
docs.text:
Long, long ago, during the reign of King Magadha, a wedding was being celebrated in Rajagriha. It was a sumptuous feast with pomp and ceremony. The bride was demure and beautiful, and the groom was Shakha Kumar, the son of the richest merchant in town.

On the same day that Shakha Kumar was born, two other boys were born in the city. One was born in a weaver's house and was named Pottik. The other, born under a Nigrodha tree, was named Nigrodha Kumar.

As children, the three boys played together. When they were about eleven years old, they set out for Takshashila for their studies. "We must go to Takshashila for studies," they agreed.
