"You two can afford the two thousand-coin fee... but my poor father is just a weaver. I cannot go with you," Pottik said sadly.

"Yes, you can," Nigrodha Kumar assured him. "I spoke to my father and he has agreed to pay for your studies too."

They studied together at Takshashila and grew into fine young men. While roaming from one place to another after their studies, they reached the outskirts of Varanasi and camped near a grove of trees for the night.

Early at dawn, Pottik woke up after hearing the cackle of a hen. "Why are you cackling so loudly over an egg?" he heard another hen ask.

"Because this one is not an ordinary egg. The one who eats the yolk of it is destined to be a king... The one who eats the white of it will become commander of the army... And the one who eats the shell will become a treasurer. Well, isn't such an egg worth crowing about?" the first hen replied.

"I must try to find out whether there is any truth in what the hen said," Pottik thought. When the hens moved away, he quietly picked up the egg.

Carefully, he separated the parts of the egg and mixed them in the breakfast porridge. "Here's your porridge, Nigrodha. With the yolk," Pottik served his friend. "And this is yours, Shakha. The white is in this one."

"But we haven't eaten any part of the egg," they protested.

"Yes, you have. I separated the parts and served them to each of you. I'll eat the one with the shell," Pottik explained, telling them what the hens had said.

"I gave the yolk to Nigrodha, the white to Shakha and I ate the shell myself," Pottik thought. Shakha was unhappy but kept silent. He pretended he was not hurt. "Who is he to decide? He should have given the yolk to me. I'll teach him a lesson one day," Shakha vowed silently.

They went into the town of Varanasi. That same day, the childless king of Varanasi had died. According to tradition, a flower-decorated chariot was sent out with no charioteer. "The new king will be the one in front of whom the chariot stops," the people explained.

The three young men rested in a park. Nigrodha Kumar slept on a stone slab. An excited mob followed the decorated chariot into the park. "Look! The horse wants to ascend those steps," the people marvelled as the chariot stopped right in front of the sleeping Nigrodha.

"Ah! What a noble face this young man has," the priest noted. "He has the signs of an Emperor."

Nigrodha Kumar was awakened and promptly installed as the King of Varanasi. He appointed Shakha Kumar as the Commander-in-Chief of his forces. "Pottik, you must stay with me as my constant companion," the new king told his weaver friend.

One day, Shakha asked to go back to Rajagriha to fetch his parents so they could live in luxury in the palace. "Will you go, Pottik?" the king asked. "Of course I will," Pottik agreed.

With a small retinue, Pottik and Shakha set off. But when they reached Rajagriha, Shakha showed his true colours. "Come with me to Varanasi," Pottik told Shakha's parents.

"No, son! Our place is here. We cannot come with you," they declined.

Pottik had spent all his money by then. "Here's Shakha's house. I'll rest here and refresh myself before going to the palace," he thought, knocking on his friend's door.

"Sir, your friend Pottik is here," a servant told Shakha.

"Had he given me the yolk, I would have become the king," Shakha thought bitterly. "Ah! Now's my chance to take revenge!"

Shakha refused to see Pottik. "Who's Pottik? I don't know anyone by that name. Beat him and throw him out. He must be a madcap."

Pottik was beaten and thrown out into the street. With a heavy heart, he returned to Varanasi. When he reached the palace, he told King Nigrodha everything.

The king summoned his Commander-in-Chief. "Really. I stopped by at his house on my way here, but he said he didn't know me and had me beaten and thrown out by his guards," Pottik repeated.

Shakha stood silently, unable to deny it.

"A favour done to a deserving noble person is like a seed sown in fertile soil. It will reap rich rewards," the king said sternly. "Indeed, any favour done to an ungrateful man is like a seed burnt by fire. Nothing ever comes out of it."

"O you wicked liar! Take him away! Kill him," the infuriated king ordered.

But Pottik intervened. "No! Please don't, O King! It is not easy to bring anyone back to life... Therefore, I don't want him killed. Please forgive him."

"If you insist. But I want him banished from my kingdom," the king declared.

Nigrodha then offered Pottik the position of Commander-in-Chief. "But I must refuse. I have always known my limitations," Pottik replied humbly.

"Why don't you accept the post of Bhandagarik (Treasurer) in my court? You will have the authority to determine the taxes payable by the various guilds. You will be in charge of the treasury."

"As you wish. I accept the post," Pottik smiled, remembering the hen's prophecy.

With his true friend Pottik by his side, Nigrodha Kumar ruled wisely and well for many years.

docs.title: Kanni, the Unlucky
docs.text:
During the reign of Brahmadatta in Varanasi, there lived a prosperous merchant called Anathapindika.

One day, a servant came to him. "Sir! A man called Kalakanni is here to see you!"

"Show him in!" Anathapindika said. "Kalakanni" was a name that meant 'unlucky'.

The man walked in hesitantly. "But, sir! He looks very poor and wretched!" the servant whispered.

"Never mind! It must be my old friend. After all, not many people have a name like his!" Anathapindika said. "Ah! It is you. How good to meet you after all these years!"

Kalakanni explained his plight. "My luck, I guess. Whoever chose my name was right. I am really unlucky. But, truly, I have been so miserable."

"Hush! Don't ever say that!" Anathapindika comforted him. "Not any more! Now you will stay with me and share my life. No buts! I will not let you go. After all how can I ever forget a friend like you? Those days we spent together at the Gurukula were the happiest of my life."

"Well, I will stay if I can be of any use," Kalakanni agreed gratefully.

Kalakanni helped Anathapindika in his work and was treated like a member of the family. "I have a big business here. I need a trustworthy friend like you to help me."

But Anathapindika's business associates were perplexed by his loyalty. "How can you trust a stranger so much? And what an inauspicious name—Kalakanni," they muttered. "Why, even uttering such a name repeatedly might bring you bad luck."

"Bah! I don't believe in such things," Anathapindika dismissed their superstitions. "He is no stranger! He is my childhood friend."

"They say that a man who walks seven steps with you becomes a friend. After a fortnight of being together, he is like a family member..." Anathapindika defended his friend. "I had spent years with Kalakanni in the Gurukula. And he is a real gem, whatever be his name."

After a few months, Anathapindika had to go on a journey. "I'll be away for a few days. We are going to our native place for a wedding."

"But who will look after your house?" a friend asked.

"Oh! Kalakanni will be there. I can trust him with my life. Here are the keys! Kalakanni! Take care."

Some thieves, knowing the master was away, plotted to rob the house. "Ah! The rich Seth Anathapindika is leaving town. Let's burgle his house tonight."

By chance, Kalakanni overheard the conversation. "Oh! How can I fight these stout rogues? The servants in the house are feeble and old. They will not be able to resist those robbers."

But Kalakanni was clever. "But what if I can fool the thieves?" he thought.

At dusk, he gathered the servants together. "Here! You blow loudly on the conchshell," he instructed one. "You must play on the drum. You have a sweet voice, you must sing. The rest of us will clap with the rhythm. Make as much noise as you can."

At night, the dacoits arrived outside the house. "Come, let's enter the empty house."

But as they crept closer, a loud cacophony of drums, conch shells, singing, and clapping erupted from inside. AAA THUMP! THUMP! CLAP CLAP

"You said it was empty! It sounds as if there's a celebration going on," one thief whispered in alarm.

"Why, there must be a real crowd inside. The seth must have returned," the leader deduced. "Let's run before anyone sees us. Leave your weapons. Let's flee."

The next morning, the servants found the abandoned weapons. "And here are footprints! Some thieves must have got in," they realised.

"Yes, we were to be robbed. But we made such a din that the thieves must have been scared off," Kalakanni smiled.

When Anathapindika returned, he was told about Kalakanni's clever trick. His business associates were finally convinced. "Your Kalakanni is really clever," they admitted.

"Of course, he is! Now do you realise why I value him so much?" Anathapindika beamed, proud of his loyal, 'unlucky' friend.

docs.title: A Monk in Need is a Friend Indeed
docs.text:
One day, King Brahmadatta of Varanasi and his son were busy discussing matters of state.

"We must send more soldiers of our army to the provinces," the king said.

"Yes, I have discussed recruiting new soldiers with the commander," his son agreed.

Just then, a servant announced an arrival. "Oh! That is the monk Tiritavachchha! I must go and receive him," the king exclaimed, rushing out. "But, father, you don't need to go. He'll be given alms like the other monks," the prince protested.

But the king rushed out to welcome the monk personally. "Come, you must sit here. On the throne?" he offered.

"How odd!" the prince thought, watching his father fuss over the monk. "Your majesty, the lunch has been served for royal guests. Shall I take your guest there?" a servant asked.

"No. He will eat with me here in my private chamber," the king insisted.

The special treatment given to Tiritavachchha did not go unnoticed. "What a fuss over an ordinary monk. Our king treats him as if he were a brave warrior who has made a great conquest," the courtiers whispered.

After lunch, the Chief Minister was summoned. "Send for the architect. I want him to build a suitable dwelling for Tiritavachchha in the compound. It should be spacious and comfortable."

Soon it reached the prince's ears. "Father, why do you give this man so much importance? He is not an expert in any art. He is neither your relative nor your friend. How come he deserves such royal treatment?"

"Do you remember, son, two months ago, I went with the army to quell a rebellion in the districts?" the king began his story.

"While leading our soldiers, my elephant ran in a frenzy and went into the forest. I was wounded and tired. Luckily we reached an ashrama. A well at last. Ah! I must have some water!"

"But there was no pulley to draw the water nor anyone around to help. I'll tie the rope around the elephant's leg and climb down the well," the king had decided.

"I climbed down the well and drank my fill of cool water. But then... it's too slippery. I can't go back," he realized, trapped at the bottom of the well.

"In the evening, the monk Tiritavachchha returned to his ashrama. 'An elephant here! Surely there must be a rider too,' the monk looked around. 'Ah! There you are. Wait! I'll help you out.' The monk took me out of the well."

"For two days the monk looked after me treating my wounds with herbs and bringing food for me from the forest. 'You have saved my life! You must come and visit me in Varanasi,' I told him."

"Had he not rescued me and looked after me, I would not have survived," the king finished his tale.

"Yes, father, now I understand that you owe your life to him," the prince nodded.

"Don't you think he deserves to be my friend?" the king asked.

"Indeed, father. We must do all we can for him," the prince smiled, finally understanding the true value of friendship.

docs.title: Lokamanya Tilak
docs.text:
On July 23, 1856, Gangadhar Pant Tilak, the headmaster of the local Marathi school in Ratnagiri, was pacing up and down the veranda of his house. His wife was about to have a baby. He had three daughters, but like other parents of his time, he longed for a son.

"It's a girl!" his mother teased him. "Poor Dada! I will tell him the truth."

"Aunty's teasing you, Dada! It's a boy!" his daughter corrected.

Whoever he looked like, the child, Bal Gangadhar Tilak, was destined to become great. Bal had his early education at home, under the strict supervision of his father.

"Dada, this sum is too simple. Give me a more difficult one," young Bal would say.

When Bal was ten years old, his father was transferred to Pune. "This is a godsend! Bal can now go to the best of schools," his father rejoiced.

Shortly after they came to Pune, Bal's mother died. It was his aunt who now looked after him.

Bal was no doubt intelligent. But he was, to his teacher's annoyance, equally stubborn. One day at school, the teacher entered and took one look at the class. The floor was littered with peanut shells.

"What's going on here? You ought to be ashamed of yourselves! All right. This time I'll let you off. Now clean up the mess, and get back to your work," the teacher ordered.

As the boys bent down to pick up the shells, the teacher's eye fell on Bal. "Hey, you! Do you think you are a prince? Come on, get to work."

"I'm sorry, sir, but I won't," Bal refused firmly. "I didn't eat in class. I didn't make the mess. So why should I take the punishment?"

"You impudent, obstinate child! I'll teach you to talk back to your teacher!" the teacher shouted, bringing down his cane. But Bal would not submit to unfair treatment.

He joined another school. But though he was an excellent student he did not do well in examinations. "Why do you always pick the most difficult questions, Bal? If you answered the easy ones, you'd get full marks!" a friend asked.

"It's not marks that I'm after. It's knowledge. So there's no point in attempting what I already know," Bal replied.

When Bal was fifteen, he was married to a ten-year-old girl named Satyabhama, as child marriage was common in those days. That same year, Bal's father died. "Bal, you must get over your grief and concentrate on your studies," his uncle advised.

And get over it he did. He was seventeen when he joined the Deccan College. On the first day as the boys assembled at the mess for lunch, a boy named Daji Abaji Khare commented on Tilak's traditional dress.

"Well, well, Tilak! How orthodox can we get! A silk dhoti—it reminds me of my father and grandfather!" Khare laughed.

"What's wrong with it? It's our traditional dress! Don't the Europeans have their formal dress for dinners?" Tilak retorted.

After the meal, Khare approached him. "You are out-spoken, aren't you, Tilak? I have much to learn from you. And I, from you, Khare." And thus began the lifelong friendship of Tilak and Khare.

"I want to improve my physique, Khare," Tilak said later. "Come to the gym with me, then. I'll make you a pehlwan (wrestler)," Khare promised.

Of all sports, swimming was Bal's favourite. One day he amazed Khare by floating on his back for hours with a loaf of bread held aloft in one hand. Tilak's diet and health no doubt improved, but when the First Year Arts results were announced, he failed.

"I've failed. Hm...m...m... I have achieved one goal—a robust physique. But... at the cost of my other goal. I'll have to strike a balance between books and sports," he realized.

He worked hard and graduated in Arts. Soon after, Tilak took up the study of Law. Around that time he struck up a friendship with Gopal Ganesh Agarkar, an M.A. student and a budding champion of social reforms.

"Without educating the people, we cannot hope to introduce social reforms," Tilak argued. "I don't see what impact social reforms imposed by an alien government can have on the people?"

"We must have political freedom first!" Tilak insisted.

The idea was soon put into shape. With the help of Vishnushastri Chiplunkar, the two friends founded a school: The New English School.

Tilak's words set Chiplunkar thinking. "I've found a way to reach the adults! Why don't we start a weekly?"

"Why one, let's have two! One in English and the other in Marathi!"

And so the two weeklies—Kesari in Marathi and The Mahratta in English—were born.

"We are lucky to catch them young. And if we don't succeed in inculcating self-respect and love for the country in them... we will succeed! If only we could reach the adults too!"

In the papers, Tilak exposed the Diwan of Kolhapur for plotting against the adopted prince. The Diwan sued the editors, and Tilak and Agarkar were jailed for sedition.

The views of the people of Pune were openly expressed in the rousing welcome given to Tilak and Agarkar on their release from jail. "Anyway, Tilak and Agarkar don't deserve to be imprisoned for publishing what they believed to be a genuine document," the people said.

Soon after their release, the friends formed the Deccan Education Society and the Fergusson College was started.

But Tilak soon found himself at odds with some of his colleagues. "Did you see the morning papers? Ranade and Gokhale, I can understand. But Tilak! I never dreamt that Tilak would have tea from a Christian kitchen!" the orthodox people complained when Tilak was tricked by a friend into attending a mission meeting.

"We must expel them from the community unless they are prepared to perform the purification ceremony," they demanded. Tilak agreed to perform the ceremony to maintain peace, but Agarkar and others refused.

"We must lead the people, not blindly follow their superstitions," Agarkar argued. The differences grew, and eventually, Tilak left the Deccan Education Society to dedicate himself entirely to the Kesari and Mahratta.

To achieve mass awakening, he set the tradition of publicly celebrating the Ganesha festival. Later he celebrated the birth anniversary of Shivaji at Raigad.

Around that time, the government raised the excise duty on Indian cloth, to help the cloth from Britain to sell better.

"For Swadeshi (indigenous) cloth, it's too expensive! Why should we buy it when imported cloth is available for the same price?" the people complained.

Tilak, however, did not sit back helpless. "If we don't buy Swadeshi cloth, who will? How will our people, our trade survive? If you love your country and your people, don't buy British cloth! Boycott it!"

Once when famine broke out in Maharashtra, farmers started leaving their villages. "I must reach them! I must instil courage into them! They must know what their rights in their homeland are!"

"The crops have failed and you have neither food nor money. Are you going to lose the roof above your head too? You have a right to live! Can't you fight for your rights even in the grip of death?" Tilak urged them to demand their rights.

Tilak did not believe in sending petitions to the government as some of his colleagues in the Indian National Congress, the Moderates, did. His methods were different.

While Tilak was busy with the struggle to secure famine relief for the farmers, the plague struck Pune. The government was at first indifferent and took no measures to arrest the spread of the dreaded disease.

Finally when it did act, it did so with a vengeance. One Charles Rand was appointed Plague Commissioner.

"I want every house in Pune disinfected. Put anyone who is ill in hospital and take the relatives to the camp outside the town—by force if necessary. That's all. You may go," Rand ordered his soldiers.

The overzealous soldiers did not respect the feelings of the citizens. They handled even babies in their cradles without any mercy.

Tilak raised his voice against the inhuman treatment. "The plague is more merciful to us than its human prototype now reigning in the city..." he wrote in Mahratta. He set up his own segregation camp for the terrified victims and a free kitchen for the poor.

Normalcy slowly returned but the wounds inflicted by the thoughtless soldiers did not heal. An angry man, Damodar Hari Chaphekar, shot at Rand.

There was a massive hunt for the unknown culprit. When Rand died of the bullet wound, the government swung into action. "Tilak has been arrested."

"The government has lost its senses," the people whispered.

Tilak was held on a charge of sedition. "Does praising Shivaji Maharaj amount to sedition? Tilak has taught us to fight for our rights. Is that sedition?"

As expected the court found Tilak guilty. The sentence was eighteen months' rigorous imprisonment. When the news reached his wife, she fell seriously ill.

Tilak too fell ill in prison. He lost 21 pounds! However, a Muslim warder tried to be of help to him. "Dadasaheb, I know you don't like food cooked with onion and garlic. Have some betel nut. It might help."

"You are kind but you are breaking prison rules. You shouldn't," Tilak replied.

Tilak spent his time in jail reading and writing. While reading the translation of the Rig Veda, a thought struck him. "Many days passed before the sun rose... How could this be true... unless... unless the Aryans had once lived in the Arctic! Yes! That's it!" It was this idea that later led him to write the book—The Arctic Home in the Vedas.

At last on September 6, 1898, Tilak was released. The temple bells of Pune rang out echoing the joy of the people.

Five years later, the plague broke out again in Pune. This time it claimed Tilak's eldest son, Vishwanath. Bearing his grief with stoicism, Tilak worked with extra zeal.

Then in July 1905, Lord Curzon announced the partition of Bengal. The partition came into force on October 16, 1905.

"Their aim is to disunite the Bengali Muslims and Hindus," Tilak warned. "But they will not succeed."

The partition only gave further impetus to the Swadeshi movement. Students made a huge bonfire of foreign clothes. Tilak started a glass factory at Talegaon and a co-operative store for the sale of Swadeshi goods in Bombay.

Since Swaraj was the goal of Tilak and his friends, like Lala Lajpatrai of Punjab and Aurobindo Ghose of Bengal, they came to be known as Nationalists.

The government tried in vain to curb the Nationalists. Lala Lajpatrai was deported to Mandalay in Burma.

When Lajpatrai was released in November 1907, the Nationalists wanted him as President of the Surat Congress. The Moderates, however, wanted Dr. Rashbehari Ghosh.

At the Congress session in Surat, Tilak was not allowed to speak before Dr. Ghosh's election. "I've a right to address the delegates. You can't stop me!" Tilak declared. The Congress split into two groups.

Around this time, revolutionaries who believed in violence became active. On April 30, 1908 the first bomb was thrown at the British residents of Muzaffarpur. The explosion echoed through the country. The government swung into action and several journalists were jailed.

A few days later, Tilak was arrested. The charge was sedition, the offence, an article he had written.

Tilak contended that while he deplored the use of bombs the only way to prevent such occurrences was to reform the administration. He made a fervent appeal to the jury.

When the jury found him guilty, Tilak proclaimed, "I maintain that I am innocent... It may be the will of Providence that the cause which I represent may prosper more by my sufferings than by my remaining free."

Tilak was sentenced to transportation for six years. In Bombay a six-day strike was observed and all the textile mills were closed. The police fired indiscriminately at the demonstrators.

Tilak was deported to Mandalay. Tilak spent five years and eight months in Mandalay. His sole companions were books and birds.

Tilak's prolonged detention totally unnerved his wife and she died when he was in Mandalay. "I'm sorry. It must be a great loss to you," the jailor comforted him.

"One of us had to face the other's death. I am glad it is I. I only wish I could have been with her when the end came," Tilak replied stoically.

He began to write a treatise on the Gita—The Gita Rahasya.

On June 17, 1914 at 2:00 A.M. there was a knock at Gaikwad Wada, Tilak's residence. The whole household woke up. Tilak was back!

The news spread. Friends came running to his house. "Lala Lajpatrai, Bipin Chandra Pal and Aurobindo have gone into self-imposed exile. The press is gagged. National schools are closed," they told him.

"Somebody has to do the work I am doing. If no one does it, the British will rule as they like," Tilak declared.

Tilak took up the cause of Home Rule, the people's right to form their own government. He toured all over India and spoke to the people. "Home Rule is the only remedy. Insist on your rights. India is your own home, isn't it? Then why not manage it yourselves?"

Tilak by then had come to be revered as 'Lokamanya' (Respected by the people) by people all over India. When he went to Lucknow to attend the Congress session, following the unification of the Moderates and Nationalists, he was taken out in procession.

At the Lucknow Congress, he made his famous declaration: "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it."

On August 1, 1920, exactly eight months after his return to India from a visit to England to present India's case for Home Rule, Tilak died. But the cause for which he fought was taken up by millions of his countrymen.

"Britishers, Quit India!"

And Tilak's lifelong struggle at last bore fruit on August 15, 1947, when India became independent.

docs.title: Hakka and Bukka
docs.text:
In the year 1310, Malik Kafur, leading the Delhi Sultan's army, besieged the Warangal fort.

Inside the fort, King Pratapa Rudra of Warangal discussed the situation with his ministers. "Our army's morale is still high, isn't it?"

"Yes, your majesty. But our supplies are low. And Malik Kafur is pressing hard," his minister replied.

"Attack is the best form of defence. Especially when we have little choice," Pratapa Rudra decided.

The two armies clashed head-on. The battle was an ill-fated one for Warangal. As an act of surrender, Pratapa Rudra was forced into sending a gold image of himself with a gold chain around the neck to Malik Kafur.

Malik Kafur returned to Delhi. "I must hasten to Delhi to inform the Sultan that I have opened the road for the conquest of the South," he declared.

Two years later when Sultan Khilji died, a new Sultan, Ghiyas-ud-din Tughluq, took power. "Now is the time for us to throw off the alien yoke. Stop our tribute to Delhi," Pratapa Rudra decided.

In the consequent battle with the new Sultan, the unlucky king was again defeated. As the victors rode away with their rich booty... the common people groaned in frustration.

"The greedy men from Delhi will return again to ransack our cities. We must resist. Our king is brave but he is old. The young men of this land must come to his aid," the people cried.

Meanwhile Hakka and Bukka, sons of the chieftain, Sangama, were already on their way to the palace at Warangal.

"Hakka, what is it that attracts you to Pratapa Rudra?" Bukka asked.

"He is a born leader. Though twice defeated, he is sure to rise again to fight the Sultan," Hakka replied confidently.

At Warangal, Pratapa Rudra welcomed them. "You say you are from the land of Virupaksha (Hampi). What brings you here?"

"Love of our land. We will drive the Sultan's men away," Hakka promised.

Once again Pratapa Rudra refused to pay tribute. "Let him come. This time we are fully prepared," the king declared.

The Sultan's army attacked, led by the prince himself. In the battle that followed, Hakka and Bukka fought valiantly. The Sultan's army was repulsed.

Pratapa Rudra was jubilant. "Let's celebrate our great victory."

"Not yet, your majesty. Danger still lurks," Hakka warned.

That night, Hakka proposed a plan. "True or not, we must spread the rumour in the enemy camp to suit our own ends. A good idea. It should demoralise their army."

They spread the rumour that Sultan Ghiyas-ud-din was dead. Most of the military chiefs in the enemy's camp believed the rumour. "The Sultan is dead. Let's return to Delhi," they panicked. "Our presence is needed there. We must start at once."

In the dead of night, without informing their commander, most of the chiefs left with their men for Delhi. On the way, they were ambushed by Hakka and his men. Caught between the two armies of Hakka and Bukka, the enemy was butchered.

But the victory was short-lived. The Sultan's army marched again. "Your majesty, the Sultan's army is again on the march. Any time now we will be attacked," the spies warned.

As Pratapa Rudra sallied forth majestically, Hakka was worried. "The king has penetrated too deep into the enemy lines, Bukka. Rush to his side, Hakka. I'll take care of this end."

Struck by an arrow, Pratapa Rudra's horse reeled. "The king! Kill him!" the enemy shouted. Though unhorsed, the king held the enemy soldiers at bay. But the king was outnumbered. A vicious blow from the enemy sent his sword flying.

"Don't harm him! The Sultan wants him alive," the enemy commander ordered. The king was captured, and the soldiers of Warangal ran helter-skelter.

Later, as Pratapa Rudra was being taken to Delhi, Hakka and Bukka followed unnoticed. But what they found out made their hearts sink.

"Pratapa Rudra has killed himself," the soldiers said. "The Sultan will not pardon our leaders for this."

"Alas, we have lost our only hope," Hakka grieved.

The two brothers went on foot to the Hoysala capital. They called on the Hoysala king, Veera Ballala. "Tughluq's army took us by storm; we were defeated," Veera Ballala told them. "But I could appoint you as governor for the northern part of our empire."

"However, the post carries with it only authority; no army, no territory," the king added.

"I'll build an army and liberate the lost territory for you, your majesty," Hakka promised.

As Hakka and Bukka left for Kampili, the king warned them, "You were beaten at Warangal. You were beaten again at Kampili. Yet you talk of victory. What keeps you going, Hakka?"

"What else, except love for my motherland, your majesty?"

When the two brothers were about to enter Kampili, a shepherd stopped them. "My lords, the Sultan's governor has announced a reward for your heads. Don't enter Kampili."

"We will have to lie low for the time being," Hakka decided.

Thus Hakka and Bukka disguised themselves as shepherds and hid from the governor of Kampili. One day, the two brothers took the sheep out to graze deep into the forest.

"Look, brother! A holy man in meditation. Let's seek his blessings," Bukka said.

It was Sage Vidyaranya. When the sage opened his eyes, the brothers fell at his feet. "I have been waiting for you. The desolate state of my native land makes me very sad. I need men of vision and valour," Vidyaranya told them.

"I think I can help you there. Come with me," the sage offered. He led them to a secret cave. "We can train thousands of soldiers in this spacious hide-out!"

Vidyaranya's discourses started attracting large crowds. "And the Lord said: Action, then, O Arjuna, is thy duty..." Some officials eyed these gatherings with growing suspicion, but the governor, Muhammad Khan, dismissed them. "That is a pious crowd. Leave it alone."

After the discourse, the apparently harmless and pious men were trained to be soldiers by Hakka and Bukka in the secret cave.

One day, Hakka and Bukka rode out with their men. Under cover of a dark night, they attacked Kampili. Hakka climbed the wall of the fort, opened the gate, and Bukka rushed in. In the battle that followed, the governor fled. The people of Kampili gave a rousing welcome to Hakka and Bukka.

"We are free once again!" the people cheered.

In the hour of triumph, Vidyaranya had a word of caution. "This is only the beginning. Your goal lies far ahead."

One day Hakka and Bukka crossed the river Tungabhadra while out on a hunt with their dogs. As they reached the opposite bank, their dogs gave chase to a hare. Then all of a sudden, it came to a halt and faced the dogs.

"What, a hare turning back to give fight to dogs! Strange!" Hakka marvelled.

When this incident was reported to Vidyaranya, the sage understood its significance. "It was the aura of that place which made the hare bold. Build a fort here and found a kingdom."

Soon a city came up at that exact spot. "You are the source of strength and inspiration to us. We will call the new city Vidyanagara," Hakka told the sage.

"Don't name it after a mere mortal. This city will herald victory for the country. It is a city of victory. Call it Vijayanagara," Vidyaranya advised.

Assuming the name Harihara Raya, Hakka became the first king of Vijayanagara. The small kingdom eventually rose to be a mighty empire that flourished for over three hundred years.

docs.title: Hanuman to the Rescue
docs.text:
When Rama, the exiled prince of Ayodhya, attacked Lanka, Ravana, the king of Lanka, himself rode out one day to the battlefield. In the course of the day's battle, he hurled his spear at Rama's brother, Lakshmana.

Even as Lakshmana fell, Rama came forward and drove away the mighty Rakshasa.

Rama knelt by his fallen brother in grief. "Oh, my noble brother... where will I find another like you? Lakshmana! Lakshmana!"

"Save him, Sushena. You are a great physician," Rama pleaded.

"Life has not left him as yet. I could easily revive him with the herb called Vishalya Karani," Sushena replied. "But I must get it before daybreak."

"Where is this herb to be found?"

"It grows on either side of the river which flows down one of the peaks of Mount Gandhamadana. But it would take eighteen years to go there and come back!"

"I could do it in much less than eighteen hours," Hanuman volunteered.

"Go, Hanuman. Look for a golden creeper with blue flowers," Sushena instructed. "Cast aside your anxieties, Lord Rama. Lakshmana will be saved."

Then Hanuman, the son of the wind, made himself larger and larger, and with one great leap rose high up into the sky.

As he flew along, supported by the wind, Ravana saw him from Lanka. "Where does Hanuman go at this hour? Ah! The Vishalya Karani! The only herb that can save Lakshmana!"

Ravana immediately summoned his uncle, Kalanemi. Kalanemi was a fearful monster with four heads, eight eyes and eight arms. He was also a magician.

"Yes, uncle Kalanemi, I need your help. Go to Mount Gandhamadana and destroy Hanuman with your magic. If Lakshmana does not inhale the fumes of the herb before daybreak, he will die and then Rama too will die of grief," Ravana ordered.

Kalanemi was reluctant. "Oh what use would that be to a dead man? For I shall surely meet my death at his hands if I go after him. No, Ravana! I cannot go to Gandhamadana. The son of the wind is cunning. He'll dash out my brains."

"Go at once!" Ravana roared.

"What a predicament. I am dead if I go and dead if I don't," Kalanemi whimpered, "Y-yes."

Kalanemi used his magic to transport himself to Mount Gandhamadana. There he transformed himself into a hermit, created a beautiful hermitage out of thin air, and sat down outside it.

When Hanuman arrived, he greeted the hermit. "I am in great haste, sir. I have no time for food and drink. I am looking for a herb called Vishalya Karani."

"Go and bathe in that river, my child and then you can refresh yourself on fruits," the disguised Kalanemi suggested smoothly. "If one fails to persuade his guest to eat, he is sure to be doomed."

"All right. I'll do as you say," Hanuman agreed.

"The crocodile will tear him to pieces... and I shall go to Ravana and claim the reward," Kalanemi thought wickedly as Hanuman waded into the river.

The crocodile saw him from afar, moved forward silently, and lunged forward. But Hanuman saw it approaching. Caught in its jaws, Hanuman wrestled with the beast, pulled it out of the water, and flung it to the shore. Then he pounced on it and tore open its breast.

As he stepped away from its carcass, a beautiful maiden appeared from the body. "I am a celestial dancer. Once when I was dancing, my foot accidentally touched the person of Indra. He was so enraged that he turned me into a crocodile... and decreed that I should live in this river till I was liberated by you."

"Beware of the false hermit, O son of the wind!" she warned him before vanishing.

Hanuman rushed back to the false hermit. "You're back!" Kalanemi stammered.

"I'll do what the crocodile couldn't. Throw aside your fruit, false one... and prepare for your death," Hanuman growled.

The earth shook as the combatants wrestled with one another. Hanuman flung his adversary to the ground, coiled his tail round him, and hurled him into the sky. The Rakshasa shot through the heavens like a comet... and landed right back in Lanka, at Ravana's court!

Ravana was furious. "It's Kalanemi! He has failed in his mission." Ravana then commanded the Sun God to rise before the appointed hour.

Some time later, Hanuman, who was searching frantically for the herb, was startled to see a rosy glow on the horizon. "What! Is the sun ready to rise? I must stop him at once!"

The Sun God's charioteer was startled to see a huge monkey dropping down from the sky and barring the road to the east. Hanuman darted forward, caught hold of the chariot, and whirled it round and round. "I shall burn him and fling him down to earth," the Sun God warned.

"Listen, O Deva, I am the son of the wind. I have come to get a herb which could save Lakshmana's life," Hanuman pleaded. "Do not rise before Lakshmana is restored to health, I beg you."

"My sympathies are with Rama, O son of the wind. But how can I save myself from Ravana's wrath if I disobey him?" the Sun God asked.

"I'll tell you how. Come closer..." Hanuman said. Then, Hanuman caught him in a warm embrace, and before he knew what was happening, bundled him under his arm!

Hanuman rushed back to Gandhamadana. "Not much time left. Sushena said it's a golden creeper with blue flowers... but I can't see any such plant here."

As he wandered, he came across some Gandharvas. They teased him and pulled his hair. Hanuman was enraged. He made himself as huge as a hill and struck back at his tormentors. Seven huge lion-faced Rakshasas joined the fight, but Hanuman coiled his tail around them and flung them down.

Pandemonium broke out among the Gandharvas until Hanuman suddenly remembered his mission. "I can't waste time fighting these people. Rama and the others must be waiting anxiously for my return."

"Well, if I can't find the herb... I'll take the whole mountain with me," Hanuman decided.

He dug his hands under the mountain, uprooted it, and lifted it above his head. With a mighty leap, he soared into the sky, carrying the entire mountain.

Rama and everyone else were amazed to see Hanuman descending with a huge mountain. Later, when he had lowered his load, Sushena himself went up with him and gathered the plants he wanted.

The physician made a paste out of the herbs and held it to Lakshmana's nose. As the fumes entered his nostrils, Lakshmana stirred.

"Lakshmana is safe. Victory to Rama!" the army cheered.

"I am indebted to you, O son of the wind. Now please take the mountain back," Rama said gently.

"I'll do that at once," Hanuman replied, picking up Mount Gandhamadana and setting off across the sky to restore it to its original place. He even healed the Gandharvas he had wounded during their battle.

Then Hanuman sped back to Lanka. "What is that shining under your arm, son of the wind?" Rama asked when he returned.

"Oh, that is the sun. Ravana had ordered him to rise before the appointed hour. So I had to hold him prisoner," Hanuman smiled.

"How extraordinary! Let him go at once," Rama marvelled.

Hanuman lifted his arm and let the Sun go. They all paid homage to the Sun God, and he departed to rise from his usual place in the east.

"You have saved Lakshmana's life, Hanuman. I am ever indebted to you," Rama said, embracing the great monkey hero.

docs.title: Harsha-The Great Ruler of Thaneshwar
docs.text:
About 1,400 years ago, Thaneshwar, a powerful kingdom in Northern India, was ruled by Prabhakar Vardhan. During this period the Huns had started harassing the people of Thaneshwar. So the king sent his able sons, Rajyavardhan and Harsha, to punish them.

"Har Har Mahadev!" the princes cried as they led their army into battle. After a bitter fight, the Huns retreated.

"Victory to Prince Rajyavardhan! Victory to Prince Harsha!" the soldiers cheered.

"Halt! It is getting dark. Let us stop following them," Rajyavardhan ordered. The Huns did not attack again.

"There has been no fighting for several days. I think I'll go on a hunt," Harsha decided.

While hunting far away from the camp, Harsha saw a messenger riding furiously towards him. "Prince Harsha, your father is very ill. He wants to see you and Prince Rajya," the messenger gasped.

"I'll go to him. You inform my brother," Harsha instructed.

Both princes rushed to Thaneshwar, but they were too late. Their father had passed away.

A few days later, Harsha comforted his brother. "Prince Rajya, you will now have to sit on the throne."

"Oh, no! Let Harsha be the king," Rajyavardhan protested in his grief.

"Certainly not. Being the elder brother, it is your right and duty to rule. Prince Harsha is right, sir," the ministers agreed.

"My brothers are young and inexperienced. Please guide them," their sister Rajyashri urged the ministers. And so, Rajyavardhan was crowned. "Long live King Rajyavardhan!"

Rajyashri, their sister, was married to Grahavarman, the king of Kannauj. Meanwhile, Devgupta, the greedy king of Malwa, was busy plotting with King Shashank of Gaud.

"The time is ripe. We can now grab the kingdoms of Thaneshwar and Kannauj," Devgupta schemed.

"But they are very powerful, sir," a minister warned.

"I know. But if we kill Grahavarman, the inexperienced Rajyavardhan will not be able to defend both kingdoms," Devgupta smiled wickedly. "I have a plan. During the spring festival, we will disguise ourselves as simple peasants and mingle with the commoners of Kannauj. When Grahavarman comes to celebrate the festival with his people, we will kill him."

According to their plan, Devgupta and his soldiers, dressed in ordinary clothes, mingled with the people of Kannauj. As King Grahavarman was entering a temple, Devgupta struck. "Die! Grahavarman, die!"

After killing Grahavarman, Devgupta forcibly carried Rajyashri with him and threw her into a prison in Malwa. "Help! Help!" she cried in vain.

The news of Devgupta's treachery reached Rajyavardhan. "Devgupta has invited his death," Rajyavardhan swore.

"Maharaj, allow me to attack Malwa," Harsha pleaded.

"No, Harsha! Being the elder brother and the king, it is my duty to do so," Rajyavardhan insisted. He marched towards Malwa.

Meanwhile in Malwa, Devgupta mocked his captive. "Say whatever you like. But I shall soon be the king of Kannauj as well as Thaneshwar."

Devgupta had allied with King Shashank. "When the battle begins, attack Rajyavardhan from the rear. Once he is dead, we've as good as won," Devgupta told Shashank.

In the ensuing battle, Rajyavardhan fought fiercely and finally killed Devgupta. "Victory to Maharaj Rajyavardhan!"

Shashank was very upset over the death of Devgupta, but he pretended to be an ally. "I am grateful to you for your help, King Shashank. You shall always be my most trusted friend," Rajyavardhan said, unaware of the betrayal to come. Shashank then treacherously murdered Rajyavardhan.

Because of the battle, everything was disorganized in Devgupta's prison. The single soldier left to guard the prison possessed a noble heart. He opened a secret passage for Rajyashri. "This secret passage will carry you to the jungle."

Thus Rajyashri escaped from Devgupta's prison. Devgupta's soldiers were patrolling the jungle, but luckily she was not spotted by them.

Harsha, meanwhile, was devastated by the news of his brother's death. "I pledge that I will uproot the cause of these sufferings," he vowed, taking the throne.

He searched the Vindhya forests for his sister. Disturbed over her fate and believing there was nobody to help her, Rajyashri decided to end her life. Just as she was about to step into a fire, Harsha arrived. "Stop! Sister stop!"

"Brother Harsha!" she wept, collapsing into his arms. "Your bad days are now over, dear sister."

Very soon Harsha conquered the various small kingdoms to bring lasting peace in Northern India. "Now that all the kingdoms have come under one flag, there would be no more battles between them. Poor peasants will live in peace at last," he declared.

Unaware of Harsha's peace plans, the great South Indian king, Pulakeshin, was worried about the rising strength of Harsha. "Harsha has won kingdoms as far as Kathiawar. He may attack us one day. We must attack before he does," Pulakeshin decided.

As Harsha was returning home after conquering Kathiawar, Pulakeshin attacked him. The fighting continued for several days. Losses were heavy on both sides.

Harsha's tired soldiers had to retreat. He was very sad. "All those men were killed for nothing. I have had enough of this blood-shed. There shall be no more wars," he resolved. "In future, I shall use my strength to protect the weak and the poor."

To fulfil his dream, Harsha gave financial help to the poor. He appointed physicians to treat the sick free of cost. He helped students to pursue their studies. People prospered, and followers of different faiths lived in peace and harmony.

During this period, Hiuen Tsang, the famous Chinese pilgrim and scholar, came to India. After spending some time in Eastern India, he met Harsha.

"Welcome, scholar Hiuen Tsang. I was looking forward to meeting you," Harsha greeted him warmly.

"Sir, I have come here to learn more about the Buddhist religion," Hiuen Tsang said. He stayed in India for a few years.

During Hiuen Tsang's stay, Harsha decided to hold a seminar on religions. Eminent scholars of Hinduism, Jainism and Buddhism came to participate. The seminar continued for several days.

"Acharya Hiuen Tsang, you are the most noble scholar I have ever known," Harsha praised him.

But some of the greedy participants did not like that a foreigner should be praised by their king. "If Hiuen Tsang makes a good impression on the king, we may not get those rich rewards, we expect," they muttered. "Yes, he must be finished before he does more harm."

But this conspiracy was reported to Harsha immediately. "I warn the conspirators that anybody who tries to harm Acharya Hiuen Tsang shall be punished. Proclaim this to all," he declared from his throne. As a result of this stern warning, nobody tried to harm Hiuen Tsang.

From the next day, Harsha started presenting gifts to the scholars and priests of different religions. Then came the turn of poor peasants. Soon the gifts brought for donation were exhausted, but still many people remained without gifts.

"Bring all the remaining wealth from the royal treasury," Harsha ordered. The royal wealth was also given away, but even then some people were left out.

In the end only one man was left out. "I am sorry that nothing is left with me except the clothes I am wearing. Please accept them," Harsha said, offering his ornaments and royal garments.

"Sister Rajyashri, can you lend me a cloth so that I can donate my dress?" Harsha asked his sister.

"Yes, brother," she smiled, handing him a simple cloth.

In this manner, every five years, Harsha gave away all his wealth to the scholars, the priests and the poor. "Long live Maha-raj Harsha!" the people cheered.

After completing his studies, Hiuen Tsang went back to China. He carried with him memoirs of his visit which became world famous as testimonies of our great ancient heritage.

"I have known many kings but there is none like Harsha," Hiuen Tsang wrote. "I have seen many countries but there is none like Bharat. Truly Bharat is a great country and her greatness lies in her people and in her rulers like Harsha."

docs.title: Hemu
docs.text:
Hemu was born in a small town called Rewari, in modern Haryana. As his father was a grocer, he was brought up to be a shopkeeper.

"Welcome, Sethji. What can I do for you?" young Hemu would ask the customers.

But Hemu had ambitions beyond the grocery store. "Father has no head for business. I'll have to take matters into my own hands or die a miserable grocer," he thought.

One day, he told his father, "I'm sorry, father. Our stock is finished. We did, but a stranger came here yesterday and bought up all of it."

"You should have kept some in stock! I'll buy my supplies elsewhere in future!" the angry customer yelled.

"Hemu! You don't plan to sell supplies to the army!" his father gasped, realizing his son's plan.

"I do, father. It's the only way of attracting the Sultan's attention," Hemu replied confidently. "I don't plan to remain a grocer for the rest of my life."

Hemu patiently waited for his customer to come. "Hemu, I hear the Sultan has sent reinforcements to Ferozepore to crush the rebels," a friend told him. "That's good news! Ferozepore isn't far away."

Then one day, the customer he had been waiting for arrived. It was an army officer. "Welcome, Faujdar Sahib. May I have the honour of serving the Sultan and your noble self?" Hemu offered.

"Do you have enough grain to supply the Sultan's army?" the officer asked.

"Enough and more, sir. I've been saving it for you ever since I heard the army had entered our district."

A few days later, Hemu set out for Agra, the capital of Sultan Islam Shah. "Take care, my son. Life at court is full of intrigue. Don't trust anyone," his father warned him.

Sultan Islam Shah, son of the famous Afghan, Sher Shah, was an able ruler. When Hemu reached Agra, the Sultan was planning a campaign against the rebel governor of the Punjab.

"My father drove Humayun out of India. He could come back if rebels weaken the unity of my empire. This rebel must be crushed," the Sultan declared.

"It should. But with the famine there, our success will depend on our supplies," a general noted.

"Your majesty, I know an enterprising young man called Hemu who could get us all the grain we'd need!" the officer suggested.

When Hemu was conducted to the Sultan, he was asked, "Is it true that you can get food for an army on the march, in a famine-stricken region?"

"Certainly, your majesty. Our loyal farmers would be honoured if they could be suppliers to your majesty's troops," Hemu replied.

"Very well. I hereby appoint you Superintendent of Bazars," the Sultan declared.

Hemu undertook a whirlwind tour of the kingdom. "Remember, this grain is for the government troops. So quote me a fair price," he told the farmers. Hemu proved to be a resourceful man, and the Sultan took him with the army to the Punjab, paying him generously.

"The grocer, Hemu, no doubt feeds the army. But he fleeces the Sultan," the jealous nobles murmured at the Sultan's tent. "Instigate the tribesmen there against him."

A few days later, the tribesmen of Kashmir captured Hemu. "The tribesmen of Kashmir have killed the rebel and his men. We may safely return now," the nobles lied to the Sultan.

But Hemu survived. So Islam Shah stayed in the Punjab. Sometime later, a message arrived from Mirza Kamran, Humayun's brother. "He seeks asylum in your kingdom," the messenger announced.

"It's strange that a Mughal should seek asylum in our camp," the Sultan mused.

"An enemy's foe is a friend, your majesty. We could turn it to our advantage," Hemu suggested. "Your majesty, Mirza Kamran is fleeing from Humayun. By helping Kamran you can upset Humayun's plans."

The Sultan was intrigued by Hemu's remark. "You are shrewd, Hemu. You shall lead my secret service."

Having strengthened his frontier, Islam Shah came to Delhi, where he fell ill. Now seriously ill, Islam Shah went to his favourite resting-place—Gwalior.

"The Sultan is on his death-bed and the prince is but a minor. The courtiers have no unity. Humayun is only waiting for a chance to capture Delhi," Hemu observed astutely.

Hemu was proved right. On the death of Islam Shah, the young Sultan was murdered by his own uncle who later ascended the throne assuming the title of Sultan Muhammad Adil Shah.

"I have decided to take up the reins of the empire founded by Sher Shah, to provide efficient administration and to win greater glory," Adil Shah announced. He appointed Hemu as his minister.

"I know you will be loyal. Besides, you, a humble grocer, can never be a threat to me," the Sultan reasoned.

But the Afghan nobles resented Hemu's growing influence. "This grocer is no statesman. His advice to the Sultan, to seize the jagirs from the powerful nobles for redistribution, is dangerous," they grumbled.

Rebellions broke out. Junaid Khan of Ajmer rebelled. "Your majesty, entrust me with a small force. I'll put Junaid Khan in his place," Hemu volunteered.

"Bravo, Hemu! But you are no soldier," the Sultan laughed.

"My common sense and intelligence can lead the soldiers to victory. Give me a chance and I'll prove it."

At Ajmer, Hemu used defensive tactics that made Daulat Khan, Junaid's general, believe that victory was within his grasp. Then, with a mighty roar, Hemu attacked. Daulat Khan was slain.

When Junaid Khan learnt about the defeat of his men, he was furious. "Prepare to march at once!" he ordered.

At Hemu's camp, Hemu noted, "They have more men than I do and my men lie tired and wounded. But I must win! My future depends on it."

And at night, when Junaid Khan's forces were asleep, he attacked. The enemy was taken by surprise, and a triumphant Hemu returned to the court at Gwalior.

Meanwhile, at court, Taj Khan Kermani, another noble, left Gwalior with his men in defiance. Adil Shah sent forces in pursuit. The two armies met at Chhibramau, 50 miles from Agra, but Taj Khan fled to Bengal.

Soon, Ibrahim Khan also rebelled in Agra and Delhi, proclaiming himself Sultan. Adil Shah sent envoys, but Ibrahim refused. Hemu marched against him, defeated Ibrahim's forces, and Taj Khan fled to Bengal.

At about the same time, the governors of Bengal and Lahore declared their independence. The empire began to fall apart.

A year later, Humayun died. "Your majesty, Humayun is dead. He fell from a staircase while hurrying to say his evening prayers," a messenger reported to Hemu.

"Akbar, his successor, is only a child. Your majesty, now is our chance to wipe out the Mughals from the land," Hemu urged Adil Shah. "You are right, Hemu! You shall lead the expedition."

When Hemu reached Agra, he found the fort deserted by the Mughals. The cowards were too scared to face him and had taken to their heels.

At the Mughal court in Delhi, Tardi Beg, the governor, held consultations with his generals. "Hemu is on his way with a huge army. He is out to crush us," Tardi Beg panicked. "We cannot defend Delhi. We must retreat to the Punjab and join Prince Akbar."

The Mughals were fully prepared when Hemu attacked, but Hemu was in the thick of the battle, encouraging his men. His charge routed the Mughals. They had to flee, leaving a large number dead, and all the Mughal wealth fell into Hemu's hands.

Hemu made a triumphant entry into the palace in Delhi. On ascending the throne, Hemu assumed a new name—Vikramaditya.

"I shall proclaim myself King and found a new dynasty!" he decided, as the Afghans were fighting among themselves and the Mughals were without a leader.

Hemu's first concern was to defend his new-found kingdom against the Mughal army despatched by Akbar. "The veteran soldier, Bairam Khan, is leading the Mughal soldiers. This is going to be a decisive battle. It's victory or death."

The two armies met on the historic plains of Panipat, near Delhi.

Just as the armies were about to attack, the leading elephant in Hemu's army was struck by lightning. "A bad omen. We shall be destroyed!" his men cried.

"No power can defy our will. Victory goes to those who fight for it. Take courage and fight!" Hemu rallied them.

The spirited charge of Hemu and his men scattered the Mughal forces. Bairam Khan was uneasy. "Only a miracle can save us now. Or is this the end of the Mughal rule in India?"

Just then a chance arrow... pierced Hemu's eye. He swooned.

"The King has been hit! He's dead!" his soldiers panicked. Believing their leader to be dead, Hemu's forces scattered in panic. That which was certain victory turned into defeat.

Hemu's elephant slowly wandered into the shelter of the nearby jungle, carrying the unconscious and unfortunate hero who, but for this stroke of fate, might have become the Emperor of India and changed the course of history.

docs.title: Heroes of Hampi
docs.text:
In the early fourteenth century, the armies of the Delhi Sultanate attacked the Deccan hoping to expand their territories. Over the next twenty years, pitched battles were fought, as the kingdoms of the south defended their lands.

Once, in a break between battles, two brothers, the warriors Hakka and Bukka, went hunting on the banks of the river Tungabhadra.

"We need a new strategy, Bukka. Our army is being beaten back every single time. All we can do for now is to try and hold the fortress of Anegundi, and worry about feeding the army," Hakka sighed.

As they rode, their hunting dogs chased a rabbit. "The rabbit is tiring! We've almost got it now," Bukka cried.

Suddenly, the rabbit stopped, turned around, and snarled fiercely at the dogs. GRRR! The dogs yelped in terror and backed away.

"The rabbit is snarling! What could make a rabbit act so brave?" Hakka marvelled. "I've never seen a rabbit act like that!"

They met an ascetic sitting nearby. "It is the land here—a land of bravery. This is where you should set up your kingdom of victory. The rabbit is a sign," the ascetic told them. "The battles are not over. Now you should build a kingdom. What better place than here, in this land of heroes?"

"But we are just local chieftains, Sangama's sons. How could we hope to build an entire kingdom?" Bukka asked.

"I don't think he knows what he's saying. Maybe we should leave," Hakka whispered.

"No! Send your dogs back and hear me out. Let me tell you about this place. Then you can decide if I'm right or wrong," the ascetic said. "Below you flows the river Pampa, or Tungabhadra. She was deeply in love with Shiva. First, Shiva married Sati..."

The ascetic began his story. Daksha held a great yagna. He invited all the gods and goddesses, but Shiva and Sati were left out. Hurt, Sati decided to attend the sacrifice anyway, but she was humiliated. Sati invoked the god of fire and burst into flames.

Filled with sorrow and anger, Shiva tore off a strand of hair and threw it on the ground. From the hair emerged a terrible god, fearsome in his rage, and filled with a thirst for destruction—Veerabhadra.

Veerabhadra led his army down from Mount Kailasha, bent on destruction. They destroyed the yagna, smashed the altar, and crushed the offerings. Then, Veerabhadra beheaded Daksha. The other gods tried to stop him, but no one could withstand his fury.

The devas went to Vishnu. "How can we stop Veerabhadra? He will destroy the world!"

Vishnu worshipped Veerabhadra with offerings of lotuses, hoping to pacify him. He did this 999 times. "The thousandth time... I'm one lotus short! I can't stop now!" Vishnu plucked out his own lotus-shaped eye and offered it to Veerabhadra.

Veerabhadra was touched, and his anger vanished. "Your devotion is unmatched. I will call off my army and stop destroying the world. I will also restore your eye. Take this Sudarshan Chakra. No being in the world can stand against it," Veerabhadra blessed him.

"The place where this happened is now called Chakratirtha. It is not very far from here, on the banks of the river Pampa," the ascetic explained.

"Well, Sati was reborn as the beautiful river Pampa. In fact the word Hampi is a corruption of her name, Pampa. This time her father was Brahma," the ascetic continued. Pampa did penance until Shiva appeared before her and agreed to marry her.

"So the ascetic began another story. 'Kishkindha, the kingdom of monkeys, was ruled by the powerful Vali, with help from his brother Sugriva.'"

One night, a demon called Mayavi appeared at the gates of Kishkindha and challenged Vali to a fight. Vali rushed forward, and Mayavi fled into a cave.

"Sugriva, I'm going in after him. You stay here. If you see blood coming out, then it means I'm dead. Just block the entrance and go home," Vali ordered.

Sugriva waited an entire year. Finally, blood flowed out. Hearing Mayavi's roar of victory, Sugriva thought his brother was dead. He blocked the mouth of the cave and went home sadly. Shortly after, a reluctant Sugriva was crowned king. Hanuman was his minister.

But it was Mayavi's blood. Vali returned, furious that Sugriva had blocked the cave. "Traitor! Murderer! Fratricide!" Vali roared, driving Sugriva out.

"We should go to Rishyamukha," Hanuman advised the fleeing Sugriva. "Rishyamukha is the hill on which Vali once threw a demon's corpse. It fell near the dwelling of a rishi who was furious with Vali. 'If you ever step near my ashram again, you will die!' the rishi had cursed Vali." So Sugriva and Hanuman lived safely in a cave on Rishyamukha hill.

A few days later, two strangers came by. It was Rama and Lakshmana, searching for Sita, who had been kidnapped by Ravana. Sugriva showed them Sita's ornaments, which she had dropped as a trail while being abducted. Rama helped Sugriva defeat Vali and regain his kingdom, and in return, Sugriva and Hanuman helped Rama rescue Sita.

After the war in Lanka, Hanuman went back to his penance in Hampi and concentrated on Veerabhadra. When Veerabhadra appeared, Hanuman asked, "I want a deity and a temple of my own. A place for me to pray and derive strength from."

"I shall erect an idol on this very spot for you," Veerabhadra granted. An idol appeared, but it started growing taller and taller. Hanuman started building a temple around the idol, but it kept growing. Finally, the idol stopped growing.

"The idol is still here in the Uddana Veerabhadra temple," the ascetic finished his story. "This is a place steeped in valour. It is a sacred place. This is a good place for a fortified city. The river will act as a barrier against invaders, in addition to supplying a city with water. Now do you still think I am a madman?"

Hakka and Bukka were spellbound. "You are very wise, sir. Won't you be our advisor in setting up this kingdom?"

"I will. But now I must go and complete my prayers for today," the ascetic said, and disappeared amongst the trees.

Hakka and Bukka rode home filled with determination. They stopped at a small Hanuman temple. "Do you by any chance know a sage who lives in the forest?" they asked some locals.

"That's Vidyaranya! Haven't you heard of him? He is a very learned man," the people told them.

With the sage's advice, Hakka and Bukka built a real Vidyanagara, a kingdom of learning, which could also be Vijayanagara, a kingdom of victory.

With Vidyaranya as their advisor, Hakka and Bukka did set up the vast kingdom of Vijayanagara. Its capital was a sprawling city, home to painters, sculptors, dancers, musicians and writers. Hakka became its first king, Harihara Raya I. Though the kingdom of Vijayanagara is long gone, the remains of its magnificent palaces and ornate temples still stand testimony to a glorious epoch in the history of India.

docs.title: Choice of Friends
docs.text:
One day at break of dawn, a crow perched on a tree near the river Godavari saw a fowler approaching. "O Lord! Here comes Yama (the God of Death) in person. I wonder what he is up to," the crow thought.

The fowler fixed his net, scattered some rice on the ground, and hid himself in the hollow of a tree.

A little later, the King of the Pigeons and his flock happened to fly past. "Look! Do you see those grains?" the pigeons cried. "O King, let us feast on them."

"No! I suspect a trap. How did those grains come here?" the wise King asked. "Beware! Greed can lead us to doom as it did the greedy traveller."

"And he told them the story of the Tiger and the Traveller."

*One day a tiger, too old to hunt, was walking by a marshy pool when he saw a gold bangle. "I may as well pick it up. It could be of some use," he thought. Just then a traveller passed by the opposite bank. The tiger spotted him. "Men are tasty and men love gold," he grinned.

"Hey! You there! Do you want this gold bangle? I have no use for it," the tiger called out.

The traveller was tempted but cautious. "A gold bangle! It's tempting. But no. I must not risk my life for it. How can I trust a fierce beast like you?"

"I have every reason to suspect me. I have been wicked in my time. But now? On the advice of a sanyasi I am changed. So come across and take this," the tiger lied smoothly.

The traveller's love of gold overcame his natural fear. He waded across the pool but got stuck in the mire! "Oh! Oh! It's a mire! I'm stuck! Help!"

"Ho! Ho! So you are stuck in the mud, I see. Never mind. Wait there. I'll come and help you out," the tiger smirked, wading menacingly towards the trapped man. He pounced on him. "Alas! What a fool I've been. I let greed overcome my reason," the traveller realized before his end.*

At the end of the story, the majority of the pigeons still wanted the grains. "Fool! Fool! Must we give up a feast for vague fears?" they argued.

The King could not stop them. "Fools! This could be your last meal. I cannot desert my subjects. I will go along with them," he sighed, and they all flew down.

Hardly had they started pecking at the grain when the fowler's net fell on them! "Alas! We're trapped. Our king was right. If only we had heeded his words!" they lamented, turning on the pigeon who had urged them.

"Don't waste time carping. You were ready to share the feast. Now share the blame too," the King scolded. "Get ready! When I give the signal, all of you take off at the same time."

"Flap! Flap!" The pigeons beat their wings and lifted the net into the air, flying away just as the fowler ran out to catch them. The fowler chased them, but soon they flew out of sight. "There goes my net. I may as well return home," he grumbled.

"How shall we get rid of this wretched net, O King?" the pigeons asked.

"A friend of mine, the King of the Mice, lives in the forest near by. We will go to him. He will cut our bonds," the King replied.

They landed near the mouse's retreat. The mouse heard the commotion and was alarmed, hiding inside. "Friend mouse, aren't you going to welcome us?" the King called out.

"Why it's my good old friend!" the mouse rushed out. Seeing them trapped, he ran forward to gnaw at the strings. "No, my friend. First free my followers," the King insisted.

"But I am small and my teeth tiny. Suppose my strength fails me before I free you," the mouse argued.

"It does not matter. My duty is to protect my subjects, at the cost of my life if need be," the King declared. Touched by his nobility, the mouse freed the flock and then the King. Thanking the mouse, the pigeons flew away.

The crow, having followed the pigeons and seen everything, flew down. "O staunch friend, let me too be your friend," he asked the mouse.

"How can that be? I am your natural food. The next thing I know, you will be eating me. We can never be friends," the mouse laughed. And the mouse told him the story of the Deer, the Jackal, and the Crow.

*Long, long ago there lived a deer and a crow who were good friends. One day a jackal saw the deer and thought, "What a delicious meal he would make." He approached the deer, pretending to be a lonely friend in need. The deer took pity on him.

When the crow saw them together, he warned the deer, "Don't you know that you must think twice before making utter strangers your friends?" And he told the deer the story of the Vulture, the Cat, and the Birds.

(A blind old vulture lived in a hollow tree. A cat came to eat the fledglings while the parent birds were away. The vulture stopped him, but the cat pretended to be a pious, vegetarian scholar. The vulture trusted him and let him live in the hollow. Day after day, the cat ate the fledglings. When the birds returned and found their young missing, they investigated. The cat slipped away, leaving the bones in the hollow. The birds, finding the bones, assumed the vulture was the culprit and pecked him to death. "And that is why one should always be wary of strangers," the crow concluded.)

But the jackal flattered the deer, and they became friends anyway. One day, the jackal showed the deer a field full of sweet grain. As the jackal had hoped, the farmer set a snare and caught the deer.

"Quick! Dear friend, you are just in time. Gnaw at this net and free me," the deer begged the jackal.

"I'm sorry, my friend. This net is made of sinews. Since I am fasting today, I cannot bite them. I'll come and free you tomorrow," the jackal lied, hiding nearby to wait for the deer to be killed so he could eat him.

The crow, searching for his friend, found him trapped. "Ah! The traitor! The sly knave! Well, I warned you," the crow sighed. He devised a plan. "Lie on your back, puff your stomach out, stiffen your legs and be very still. I will peck at your eyes. When the farmer comes, he'll think you're dead and remove the net. Then when I croak, spring to your feet and run!"

The farmer arrived, thought the deer was dead, and removed the net. The crow croaked, and the deer sprang up and ran! Annoyed, the farmer flung his heavy club at the fleeing deer, but it missed and hit the hiding jackal instead, killing him on the spot!*

"But I am not like that jackal," the crow insisted to the mouse. "I swear I will not eat you."

The mouse was touched by the crow's devotion. "Come my friend, let me entertain you with the best of my hoard of food." They became the best of friends.

One day, the crow said, "O friend mouse, food for me is getting scarce in this forest. I would like to move away... to the forest where my old friend tortoise lives."

"If you insist on going, I too will go with you. I can't imagine life without you," the mouse agreed.

So the crow carried the mouse on his back, and they flew to the lake where the tortoise lived. The tortoise was overjoyed, and the three friends lived happily together.

One day, a deer came panting up to them. "What's the matter?" they asked.

"A hunter. He's after me," the deer gasped. The friends quickly hid. The hunter arrived, couldn't find the deer, and left. The deer then joined their group of friends.

Sometime later, the deer did not return from grazing. The crow searched from the sky and found him trapped in a hunter's snare. He flew back and told the mouse, who rushed to gnaw the strings and free the deer.

But the slow tortoise had followed them out of worry! "Why did you come? If the hunter returns..." the deer cried.

And the hunter did return! "I will wade across and take the net," he grumbled. The deer bounded away, the crow flew up, and the mouse scrambled into a hole. But the tortoise was caught. The hunter tied him to his bow and walked away.

"Alas! Our friend has been caught!" the crow cried.

"Don't despair! Quick, we still have a chance to rescue him," the mouse said, quickly forming a plan. "Deer, go and lie across the hunter's path, as if dead. Quick! Friend crow will hover over you and peck at your body. When he sees you, he will put friend tortoise down. And I will gnaw at the binding strings."

The deer and crow took their positions. The hunter saw the 'dead' deer. "A deer! It's been a good day for me," he cheered. He put the tortoise down and drew his knife, walking towards the deer.

The mouse quickly gnawed the tortoise's bonds. The tortoise slipped silently into the water. As the hunter got close, the deer sprang up and ran away! The hunter turned back, only to find the tortoise gone too.

"I've lost both," the hunter sighed, returning home empty-handed.

The four friends were reunited and lived happily ever after.

docs.title: In Search of Sita
docs.text:
In their search for Sita, Ram and Lakshman reached the shores of Lake Pampa. "Look, Lakshman! That must be the Rishyamuk mountain," Ram observed. "Yes! We will find Sugreev, the monkey chief, there."

From his hidden abode upon the hill, Sugreev saw the two brothers approaching. "Look at those two men coming in this direction. They look very purposeful and strong. I'll find out who they are!" Sugreev summoned his minister, Hanuman. "Go disguised as a Brahmin student and make discreet enquiry."

Hanuman disguised himself and approached the visitors respectfully. "Who are you two heroes, one dark and the other fair? Why are you roaming in the forest in the guise of Kshatriyas?"

When Hanuman heard that they were Ram and Lakshman, searching for Sita, he recognized Ram and fell to his feet. "Forgive me, I am dull and ignorant not to have recognised you."

Ram lifted Hanuman to his feet and clasped him in a warm embrace. Hanuman, thrilled, resumed his original monkey form. "My lord, on the summit of this hill lives Sugreev. He can help in the search for Sita by going in different directions."

Hanuman carried the two brothers on his massive back up the hill to Sugreev. Ram and Sugreev embraced each other warmly, making a firm alliance of friendship. "I saw Sita being carried away, weeping copiously..." Sugreev recalled, and he showed Ram the ornaments Sita had dropped.

"She cried out for you and then when she saw us, threw a garment down," Sugreev added. Ram pressed the cloth to his bosom, lost in thought. "My dear one! My sweet Sita. I miss you so!"

Sugreev then shared his own tale of woe—how his brother Vali had misunderstood him, usurped his throne, and taken his wife. Ram pledged to help Sugreev.

Ram slew Vali, and Sugreev was crowned king of Kishkindha. However, as the rainy season began, the search for Sita had to be postponed. Ram and Lakshman set up home on the Pravarshan hills, while Sugreev enjoyed his reclaimed kingdom.

Months passed. The rains ended, and autumn arrived. Ram grew worried and impatient. "I hope she is alive! Now look at Sugreev! He has got his kingdom and treasury, comfort and family, he has quite forgotten me," Ram lamented. "How dare he do so? With this bow that caused Vali's death, I will kill his foolish brother too," Lakshman said furiously.

Lakshman stormed into Kishkindha. Seeing him, a terrified Sugreev apologised profusely. "Pleasure and enjoyment are so addictive that I forgot my promise. Forgive me if you can," he begged. At one word of command from Sugreev, a massive army of monkeys was formed and sent in all directions to find Sita.

Hanuman, Angad, Nal, and Jambavan headed south. After days of searching without food or water, they reached the southern ocean. Exhausted and losing hope, they sat on the beach, steep in sorrow. "If we go back without news, Sugreev will kill us. It's death in any case!" Angad sighed.

Their loud comments attracted the attention of Sampati, an old, flightless vulture. "I'll eat them all up! I haven't had a good meal for so many days," the vulture thought. But when Angad mentioned Jatayu's noble sacrifice for Ram, Sampati stopped.

"What happened to Jatayu?" Sampati asked, revealing he was Jatayu's elder brother. Upon hearing the tale, Sampati wanted to help. "I have the long range vision of a vulture! I can see that. On the Trikut mountain is Lanka, the kingdom of Ravan. There, in a grove of Ashok trees, is Sita steeped in sorrow."

As he spoke, Sampati's burnt wings miraculously sprouted and grew back, a blessing for helping Ram's cause.

Now the monkeys faced the ocean. "I am old now and not as strong as I was. In my younger days, I could do the impossible!" Jambavan lamented. Angad was unsure if he could make it back.

"Why are you silent, Hanuman?" Jambavan asked. "You are brave, strong and swift like your father, the Wind God. You are a veritable storehouse of intelligence, discretion and knowledge."

Encouraged by their praise, Hanuman began to grow in size until he was as tall as a mountain. "I can cross this salty sea easily! It's child's play for me," he roared, and leapt across the ocean.

On his flight, he met the Mainak mountain and the serpent mother Surasa, who tested him. Hanuman used his wit to pass them and flew on, finally reaching Lanka.

Hanuman assumed a tiny form, as small as a mosquito, and entered Lanka at night. The demoness Lankini guarded the gate, but with a mighty blow, Hanuman toppled her over.

Exploring the city, Hanuman saw a house with symbols of Ram painted on the wall and a Tulsi plant in the courtyard. It was the house of Vibheeshan, Ravan's brother, who was a devotee of Ram.

Vibheeshan told Hanuman about Sita. "She sits forlorn under a big tree in the lovely Ashok Vatika, surrounded by demonesses."

Hanuman found the Ashok grove and hid in the branches of the tree above Sita. Soon, Ravan arrived, trying to entice Sita. "O fair-faced one! All these queens, including Mandodari, will become your handmaidens if you consent to marry me!" Ravan cajoled and threatened.

Sita plucked a blade of grass and held it before her. "Can the light of a firefly make a lotus bloom? Have you no fear of the arrows of my Lord Ram?" she retorted.

Furious, Ravan gave her a month's time to submit or face death. After Ravan left, Hanuman dropped Ram's signet ring into Sita's lap.

Sita recognized the ring at once. Hanuman appeared before her and narrated the story of Ram. To reassure her, he showed his mammoth form, then shrank back to a tiny monkey.

Hanuman conveyed Ram's message and took a jewel from Sita to show Ram as proof of his visit.

Before leaving, Hanuman decided to teach the demons a lesson. He ravaged the royal orchards and fought off Ravan's guards. Ravan sent his son Akshay, whom Hanuman killed. Then came Meghnad, who captured Hanuman using the divine Brahmastra.

In Ravan's court, Hanuman boldly advised Ravan to beg Ram's forgiveness. Enraged, Ravan ordered Hanuman's tail to be set on fire.

Wrapping his tail in oil-soaked rags, the demons set it ablaze. But Hanuman shrank his body, slipped out of his bonds, and sprang from place to place, setting the entire city of Lanka on fire! Only Vibheeshan's house was left untouched.

After burning the city, Hanuman extinguished his tail in the sea, bid farewell to Sita, and leapt back across the ocean to his friends.

With a mighty roar, Hanuman returned. "He looks radiant! He must have found Sita," the monkeys cheered.

Hanuman presented the Chudamani (jewel) to Ram. Ram clasped it to his heart, deeply moved. Hanuman recounted all he had seen in Lanka and urged an immediate attack.

"Glory to Ram! The Lord of Kosala!" the monkey army cheered. At the head of the enormous horde, Ram, strong and brave, led his army towards the sea for the ultimate victory of good over evil.

docs.title: Indra and Shachi
docs.text:
Indra was the king of the Devas. He lived with his queen, Shachi in his celestial city, Amaravati.

"Lord! We are so happy that I fear it might not last," Shachi told her husband one day.

"Nothing untoward can happen to us, my dear," Indra reassured her.

But there was cause for them to fear. At that very moment a son was born to Tvashta, Indra's enemy. The son, Trishiras, was born with three heads. "You shall recite the Vedas with one head... consume the divine Sura with the other and... watch the world with the third," Tvashta told his son. "My son, you shall become King of the Gods."

Trishiras grew up fast. He became strong-willed and powerful. "I shall perform severe penances and destroy Indra, my father's enemy," he vowed.

Indra heard of Trishiras and of his intense penance to destroy him. He sent for the Apsaras, the celestial maidens. "You must distract Trishiras and break his penance," Indra commanded.
