"We will tempt him with these fruits and flowers. I will dance. Your voice is sweet and enchanting. You must sing. We will lead him away from meditation into a life of pleasure," the Apsaras planned.

Their charms were heavenly. But Trishiras was unmoved. The Apsaras returned to Indra, defeated. "We have failed. Trishiras is steadfast..."

Shachi was worried. "Be careful! I fear for your safety," she told Indra.

"At this rate he will usurp my throne. I must kill him," Indra decided.

Indra threw his thunderbolt at Trishiras while he was praying. Trishiras fell dead, but his eyes remained unshut and staring.

"Those eyes haunt me. I must get them closed. They drain me of all my strength," Indra shuddered.

At that moment a woodcutter happened to pass by. "Please cut his heads off with your axe," Indra asked him.

"His neck is too thick. My axe will become blunt," the woodcutter replied.

"My thunderbolt shall give it an edge, fine and strong," Indra promised. The woodcutter then cut Trishiras' heads off.

"Now I will have to atone for my sin. I have murdered a Brahman," Indra realized. He retreated to do penance for a long time. Finally, a divine voice spoke to him: "Arise, Indra. You have atoned for your sin by your penance."

"Now I am free. I shall return to Amaravati," Indra rejoiced.

Meanwhile Tvashta heard of Trishiras' death. He was deeply grieved and very angry. "I shall avenge my son's death. From the power of my prayers... shall rise an Asura," he vowed.

Tvashta performed a sacrifice and created a terrifying Asura. "You are Vritra. Go forth and kill Indra."

When Indra heard of Vritra, he prepared to meet the challenge. "I knew no good would come of killing Trishiras," Shachi worried.

There was a terrible battle in which Vritra caught Indra between his teeth! "He has caught Indra. Quick! Hand me the Jrimbhaka Astra," a Deva shouted.

The Jrimbhaka Astra (a weapon that causes yawning) found its mark. "I cannot control these yawns. That astra does it," Vritra groaned, opening his mouth wide to yawn.

"This is the moment for me," Indra thought, making himself tiny and escaping from Vritra's mouth.

He and his subjects went to Vishnu for advice. "Lord, help us," they pleaded.

"Make peace with Vritra. Then watch for your chance to kill him. I will help you," Vishnu advised.

Indra made peace with Vritra, but Vritra was wary. "Let us be friends! I admire you!" Indra said.

"All right. But on my conditions. You must not kill me with any weapon—wet or dry, of wood or of stone... by day or by night!" Vritra laid out his terms.

"I agree," Indra promised.

One evening they were on the sea-shore. "I must kill him soon—but without using a weapon," Indra thought. "Foam cannot be considered a weapon. Vishnu has promised to help me. This twilight is neither night nor day. Foam is neither wet nor dry. Vishnu, help me!"

Vishnu entered the foam, and when Indra threw it at Vritra, the Asura was killed immediately by the power of Vishnu within it.

But as Indra had betrayed Vritra, and had previously murdered Trishiras, he was deeply ashamed to face the world. "I have killed Vritra. But I cannot show my face to anyone. I shall hide where none can find me," he wept.

Indra ran away. All was darkness and chaos, on earth and in heaven. "There is no light anywhere." "Oh! Someone is beating me in the dark." "How dare you enter my house?" the gods and mortals stumbled in the dark.

The Devas met in a council. "Without a king the heavens are in chaos. We cannot allow this anarchy to go on. Indra has deserted us. We must find another king," they decided.

"Let us go to earth and invite King Nahusha to be our king. King Nahusha is a good and pious man," they agreed.

The Devas went to King Nahusha's court. "Our king has run away. Please be our king," they requested.

"I am a simple mortal. How can I be your king?" Nahusha hesitated.

"We will invest you with our powers and strength. There is no one else. You must agree!"

"Well. If you insist," Nahusha accepted. The Devas made Nahusha their king.

In Amaravati, Nahusha enjoyed all the celestial pleasures. "Isn't this Indra's famous elephant? May I ride on it?" he asked. "I am a lucky man to own this."

"This is your palace and garden. You may give your orders. We are here to obey them," the gods told him.

"You are all so kind. I do not deserve all this," Nahusha said at first. But power soon corrupted his mind.

"I am the Lord of the three worlds. The only thing I lack is a queen," Nahusha thought. "Go to Shachi. Tell her that I am the new king. She must now be my queen."

When Shachi heard the message, she was shocked. "I will never be his queen. Better death," she wept, running to Brihaspati's house for protection.

Nahusha was furious. "He is protecting my queen! I will have to punish Brihaspati!" The Devas tried to calm him down. "She is in grief. She will come round. You must be patient."

So, prompted by Brihaspati, Shachi went with the Devas to Nahusha. "First let us search for my lord. If he cannot be found I shall become your queen," she proposed.

"That is reasonable. I agree," Nahusha conceded.

The Devas then went to Vishnu for advice on how to find Indra. Agni, the Fire-God, led the search. They found Indra hiding inside the stem of a lotus in a lake! He had made himself tiny to hide.

"Indra! The Devas need you! You must come back!" they called.

"I have done terrible things. I cannot come back," Indra refused.

Shachi prayed to Upashruti, a Goddess of the night. "Oh mighty goddess! Help me!" Upashruti appeared and led Shachi into the dark night, over oceans and mountains, directly to the lake where Indra was hiding.

Upashruti slit the lotus stem, and inside cowered a tiny Indra. "Lord! You do not know my plight," Shachi wept, explaining Nahusha's demands.

"You must save me, or Nahusha will make me his queen tomorrow," she pleaded.

Indra told her what she should do. "Go quickly and do as I have told you."

The next morning Shachi went to Nahusha. "Indra has run away. I am ready to be your queen," she announced.

"I expected this," Nahusha smiled greedily.

"But I have a special request to make. You should come to me in a palanquin borne by the seven sages," Shachi demanded.

"That is easy. I am their king. I will come this evening," Nahusha agreed arrogantly. "Tell the seven sages to be ready to carry my palanquin. I shall put on my finest clothes and jewels."

Nahusha set out in the palanquin borne by the seven venerable sages.

Meanwhile Shachi went to Brihaspati's house. "I have done my part," she said.

"Then I shall send for Indra," Brihaspati replied. He invoked Agni in a sacrifice. "Agni! You must find Indra and bring him here, at once." Agni called the Devas, and they brought Indra back.

At that moment Nahusha was on his way to Shachi's palace. "Lazy fellows! How slow they are!" he yelled at the sages carrying his palanquin. "It is all the fault of Agastya. Because he is short he cannot keep pace with the others."

"I shall goad him on," Nahusha thought, and he kicked Sage Agastya! "Sarpa! Sarpa! (Move on! Move on!)" he shouted.

When Nahusha kicked him, Agastya became angry. "How dare you! Sarpo Bhava! (Become a snake!) My curse will turn you into a snake," Agastya proclaimed.

Instantly, Nahusha was transformed into a snake and fell from the heavens.

Just then Agastya came hurrying up to the Devas. "We are free at last. Nahusha is now a mere snake," he announced.

"Only because of Shachi's loyalty to me!" Indra said gratefully.

The Devas and sages welcomed Indra back to his throne, and he was reunited with his devoted wife, Shachi.

docs.title: Indra and Vritra
docs.text:
Indra, King of the Devas, ruled heaven. His guru, Brihaspati, performed sacrifices which gave the Devas divine grace... and invincible victory in their battles with their cousins, the Asuras.

"The Asuras won't trouble us again," Indra boasted.

One day Brihaspati came to see Indra. Engrossed in the dance at his court, Indra did not see his guru and remained rudely seated. "His triumphs have made him arrogant!" Brihaspati realized. "I shall not stay where I'm no longer respected."

As he stomped out in a rage, Indra suddenly noticed him. "It's my guru! When did he come? Alas! I've unwittingly offended him!" To appease the guru, Indra rushed to his house. But it was one of Brihaspati's disciples who received him.

"Where is my guru?" Indra asked.

"My lord, he has left your realm for good," the disciple replied.

"My negligence has cost me my guru. We are lost without him," Indra despaired.

When the news reached them, the Asuras were jubilant. "Now is the time to attack the Devas!" they cheered. "On to heaven!"

The Asuras invaded heaven. This time the Devas could not drive them back. Defeated in the battle, Indra sought Brahma's advice. "What you lack is the spiritual guidance of a guru. Go and seek the help of Vishwarupa, the son of Sage Twashta," Brahma advised.

Indra called on the three-headed Vishwarupa. "O son of Twashta, we beg you to become our guru and pray for our prosperity."

"When the very Devas whom I seek to propitiate ask me to be their guru, would I refuse?" Vishwarupa agreed, though his mother was related to the Asuras.

Indra took Vishwarupa to heaven, where the three-headed guru performed sacrifices. Reinforced by the spiritual power of Vishwarupa, the Devas invaded the kingdom of the Asuras and inflicted a crushing defeat on them.

Indra called on Vishwarupa. "Thanks to your spiritual leadership, we have defeated the Asuras."

"I am glad. And yet... the Asuras are my mother's relatives. What should I do?" Vishwarupa thought to himself. "I know. Henceforth I shall strive for the good of the Asuras too."

Meanwhile, Indra conferred with the other Devas. "The battle with the Asuras proved indecisive. What could the secret of their renewed strength be?"

"I shall perform a sacrifice for your success... and for the success of the Asuras too," Vishwarupa mumbled during a ritual.

All the Devas assembled to watch the sacred ritual. "May the offering of these grains make the Devas stronger," Vishwarupa chanted. Indra, who was seated near Vishwarupa, suddenly became suspicious. "The guru seems to be uttering a prayer in a low tone. For whom is that prayer? Why hasn't he poured all of it?"

"And may this offering fill the Asuras with strength," Vishwarupa whispered silently.

"So that is the secret of their strength! Traitor!" Indra realized. He drew out his sword and cut off Vishwarupa's heads.

As the guru fell, one of his heads took the form of a chataka bird, another of a sparrow, and the third of a pheasant. All three soared high in the air.

"It was rash. Yet I had to kill him. I shudder to think of what the future holds for me and my subjects," Indra sighed.

When he divined the fate that had befallen his son, Sage Twashta was ominously disturbed. "I feel uneasy. Has my son come to harm? I shall beget another son to avenge the killing of Vishwarupa," he vowed.

Twashta performed a sacrifice and created a massive, terrifying Asura from the fire. "You are Vritra. Go forth and kill Indra."

When Indra heard of Vritra, he prepared to meet the challenge. Vritra stood up, bridging earth and heaven. The Devas rained a shower of arrows and spears on him, but the great Asura opened his huge mouth and swallowed them. The Devas fled from heaven.

"Come back, O Devas. Won't you give me battle?" Vritra mocked.

Seething with anger, Indra hurled his trident at Vritra. Vritra caught it with his left hand and struck Indra's mount, Airavata, with it.

Indra hurled his vajra with all his might, but Vritra aimed his terrible trident at Indra and shattered it. Enraged, Vritra fell upon Indra and struck him with a massive bludgeon.

"I've lost my weapon. How can I continue the fight?" Indra despaired.

"I lose an arm and I still fight. You miss a mere weapon and would flee? Our combat is like a game of dice. The outcome is not known to us. Fight, Indra, fight!" Vritra roared.

Picking up his broken vajra, Indra smiled at Vritra. "O Vritra, it is a pleasure to fight with one as valorous as you. Here comes my vajra!"

As Indra's vajra struck, it severed one of Vritra's arms. But the mighty Asura opened his mouth, drew a deep breath, and swallowed Indra whole!

"Indra is finished!" the Devas cried. "Vritra roared with anger."

To his surprise, Indra found himself being drawn towards the Asura. Along with Airavata, he fell into the mouth of the Asura, went down his gullet...

Inside, he cut open Vritra's abdomen with his vajra... and came out.

"Indra is back! But the evil Asura is still alive!" the Devas cheered.

Indra hurled his vajra with all his might at Vritra... and this time the vajra found its mark, beheading the great demon.

"I salute you, Vritra. You died a glorious death," Indra said, paying his respects to a valiant foe.

docs.title: The Jackal and the Rats
docs.text:
One day, while roaming in the forest in search of food, a jackal suddenly spied a troop of rats. Their king was a huge bandicoot.

"I could attack them. But then I'd catch only one and the rest would run away," the jackal thought. "If I'm clever, however, these rats could provide me with food for many days."

So he followed them to their hole. When the last of them had gone into the hole, the jackal stood outside on one leg, his mouth open and his face turned towards the sun.

A little later, when the rats came out again, they were amazed. "Why are you standing on one leg?" they asked him.

"If I stood on all four, the earth would not be able to bear my weight," the jackal lied smoothly.

"Why do you keep your mouth open?"

"To take in air, my only food."

"And why is your face turned upwards?"

"To worship the sun."

The rats were deeply impressed. "How lucky we are to have a saint in our midst! We shall worship him every morning and evening," they decided.

"It has worked! He really thinks I am a saint!" the jackal chuckled inwardly.

The next morning, the rats came out. "Ah! Here they come. I must pose again for them." Their worship over, the rats trooped away.

...While the jackal swiftly swooped upon the last of them and ate him up!

This went on for many days. Then the bandicoot noticed something. "For all his living on air, how plump this saint has become! And my subjects are fewer! Could the jackal...? I'll find out."

So that evening as the rats were ready to set out, the bandicoot instructed them, "Today all of you go ahead. I'll come out last."

"If my guess is correct, he'll pounce on me. I must be ready," the bandicoot thought.

The next moment the jackal sprang at him... but missed! "So this is your game! You rascal!" the bandicoot squeaked.

The bandicoot dug his teeth into the jackal's throat and killed him. Back came all the other rats and they had a grand feast on their false saint.

docs.title: The Jackal and the Lion
docs.text:
A hungry jackal once suddenly came across a lion who was on his way home.

"What do you want?" the lion roared.

"I cannot hope to escape. It would be wiser to play humble," the jackal thought quickly. "My lord, please let me be your humble servant," he pleaded.

"All right. Follow me," the lion agreed.

"What luck! I'll never have to go hungry again," the jackal rejoiced.

When they reached the lion's den, the lion commanded, "If you do as I tell you, you will be well fed."

"Your word is my command, my lord."

"You will go to the top of the mountain each day and see if there are any animals roaming in the valley below. And if I see one, my lord? You will come and tell me about it. Then you will say: 'Shine forth in all your might, my lord.'"

He sped back to the lion. "I've seen an elephant. Shine forth in all your might, my lord," the jackal reported and fell at his feet.

The lion killed the elephant and ate his fill. Then, after he had eaten his fill, "You may take what's left." So the next day the jackal went to the mountain top.

As the days went by, the jackal grew fatter and fatter. But, alas! He grew less and less humble. One day he thought, "Why should I live on leftover food? I, too, am a four-footed creature! Why work for the lion when I could kill elephants and buffaloes for myself?"

He appealed to the lion. "My lord, I have lived for too long on what you kill. I would like to eat an elephant I have killed myself."

The lion was silent for a while. "What a foolish idea! He'll be killed himself!" he thought.

"O jackal, only lions can kill elephants. Give up this silly idea. And be happy to eat what I kill."

"Please, my lord. Don't deny me this chance. I'll wait here, while you go to the mountain-top. When you see an elephant, come to me and say, 'Shine forth in all your might, jackal,' and I'm sure to kill it."

At last the lion gave in. "All right. I'll do it."

A little later, the lion came back. "I have just spied an elephant coming this way. Shine forth in all your might, jackal."

The jackal nimby bounded away on the trail of the elephant. "I'll catch him by the throat and kill him."

He sprang at the elephant... but missed him! The puzzled elephant just walked over him. And that was the end of the foolhardy jackal.

docs.title: The Clever Jackal
docs.text:
A group of rogues were once having a grand party. Towards midnight, one of them complained, "Can I have some more meat?"

"You can have more wine if you like, but there's no meat left," another replied.

"What! No meat! But I must have some!" the drunk rogue insisted. "I'll go to the charnel-grove, kill a prowling jackal, and bring you its meat."

Club in hand, the braggart swaggered off. When he reached the grove, he thought, "When a jackal comes near, I'll kill him with my club." He lay down, pretending to be a corpse.

A little later, a pack of jackals came by. "Look, there's a corpse. Come on!"

"Wait! Let me make sure we're safe," an older, wiser jackal cautioned them.

"The smell of a living man! Just as I thought! He is only pretending to be dead," the clever jackal realized.

"Just then he noticed the club. 'He is probably waiting to kill one of us.' Wait here. I'll take care of the rascal," he told the pack.

He crept up to the man... caught the club with his teeth... and gave it a slight tug.

"It must be a bandicoot! I'd better tighten my grip," the man thought.

The next moment the jackal let go of the club with a jerk! The startled rogue jumped to his feet, flung his club at the jackal... and missed!

"I dare not face my friends after my vain boast. I'd better go home and sleep," the rogue muttered, walking away in shame.

The next moment, to his surprise, the jackal stood before him. "Ho! Brahman, you couldn't have mastered the spell better than I!" the jackal mocked, and off he ran. The rogue chased him, but the jackal was too fast.

docs.title: The Jackal and the Magic Spell
docs.text:
In a secluded spot in a forest, the family priest of Brahmadatta, King of Varanasi, was once repeating a secret spell. A jackal lying near by pricked up his ears. "If I listen carefully I, too, can master that."

A little later the Brahman got up. "There! I've mastered it."

"And off he ran," the jackal thought, having learned the spell.

The jackal escaped deep into the forest. "I'll first get married and then, using the spell, I'll bring all the four-footed creatures of the forest under my sway."

He soon found himself a she-jackal. "If you become my wife you shall be queen of all the animals of the forest." She agreed.

Later he uttered the spell and all the animals began to flock towards him. They seated the jackal and his wife on a lion which stood on two elephants.

"You are our master, O mighty one! You are our king!" they cheered. They conferred a title on the jackal and bowed to him. "Hail Sarvadata, chosen king of the animals!"

All this went to the jackal's head. "My subjects, we shall capture the city of Varanasi! We shall camp here and send a message to the king."

When the king received the message, his family priest was with him. "'Surrender your kingdom or die fighting for it,' he says. He has struck terror everywhere. His camp covers an area of thirty-six miles! Who is this animal?" the king wondered.

"He is Sarvadata, the jackal-king. Leave him to me. I'll find a way of defeating him," the priest assured him. "All right. May you be successful."

"I must first find out what he intends to do." The priest went out to the city walls. "O Sarvadata, how do you plan to take this city?"

"I will make the lions roar and create panic and chaos among the people. Then I will march into the city," the jackal boasted.

"Oh! So that's it!" the priest chuckled. "Impossible! These noble creatures will never obey a common jackal."

"Don't brag. Get him to roar—if you can," the priest taunted.

"That's what you think! The lions will all obey me. Even this lion on whose back I sit will roar. Obey your king! Roar with all your might!" the jackal commanded.

GRR-RR! GRR-R-R!

The lion roared and roared. Terrified, the elephants shook off the lion. The jackals crashed to the ground.

Seeing the elephants run amuck, all the animals broke into a stampede and ran helter skelter. In the stampede, the jackals were trampled to death.

That was the end of King Sarvadata who had dared to dream of conquering Varanasi.

docs.title: The Jackal and the Otters
docs.text:
A jackal's wife once wanted to eat some fresh Rohita fish. Promising to bring it for her, the jackal went to the river.

"I've promised to bring her the fish. But how am I going to do it?" he wondered.

Just then he saw two otters dragging along a huge Rohita fish. "This fish should last us a long time," they said.

The jackal drew nearer. "Yes, but how shall we divide it?" one otter asked.

"If I divide it, I'll have to give him the larger share," the other otter thought.

"You divide it."

"No, you divide it."

"Here's my chance! They're sure to ask me to divide it for them," the jackal thought.

"What's the matter, friends?" he asked smoothly.

"This fish was caught by both of us. We cannot decide how to divide it. Will you do it for us?"

"Just as I expected! Certainly! You can leave it to me. I've settled many cases before and settled them fairly," the jackal boasted.

The jackal then cut off the head and the tail of the fish. "You take the head... and you the tail. Now let's see how he'll divide the rest."

"And before their very eyes he ran off with the best portion of the fish!"

"Fools! This could be your last meal," the jackal mocked as he ran.

"There goes our delicious fish!" one otter cried.

"If only we had trusted each other instead of inviting an outsider to interfere!" the other otter lamented.

Meanwhile, the jackal returned home to his wife, bringing the best part of the fish, proving himself a very clever thief indeed.

docs.title: The Jackal and the She-Goat
docs.text:
Long ago, in a cave on the slopes of the Himalayas, there lived a herd of wild goats. One day, as a jackal and his mate were prowling about for food, they saw the goats grazing.

"Come! Let us kill one of them," the she-jackal suggested eagerly.

"Wait! If we are clever, we'll have food enough for many months," the jackal replied.

They waited till the goats began to wander apart as they grazed. "Let's follow that one till he is far away from the others."

A few hours later... "There! I've killed him. Now help me drag him to our cave," the jackal told his mate.

Many months passed and, one by one, the goats were eaten by the jackals. "The only one left was a wise she-goat," the she-jackal observed. "I dare not go out. The jackals are about again!" the she-goat thought, hiding in her cave.

"That she-goat seems to be wise to us. She does not come out at all," the she-jackal said in frustration.

"The jackal had an idea. You go alone every day and try to win her confidence. When she begins to trust you I will lie outside our cave and pretend to be dead, and you... bzz... bzz..." he whispered his plan to her.

"There's no harm in speaking to her from inside. She may be innocent," the she-goat thought when the she-jackal approached.

Eager to carry out the plan, the she-jackal hastened to the goat's cave. "O wise goat, do you live here all alone? It's the wife of the jackal! Please don't be afraid. I've come to make friends with you. Please come out."

"No! I don't trust you. Go away. You killed all my relatives," the she-goat retorted from inside.

"It was my husband, not me. If you don't trust me, you needn't come out. But, please, don't refuse to talk to me."

"I am very unhappy. No one is willing to be my friend, because of my husband's evil ways," the she-jackal lied.

The kind-hearted goat felt sorry for the she-jackal. "Please don't say that. I'll be your friend."

As each day passed the she-goat's trust in the jackal increased, till one day... "Tomorrow we shall carry out the next part of our plan," the she-jackal thought.

The next day, she cried, "Oh, I am left all alone! My husband is dead. Please come and help me bury him."

"She can't be lying. He must really be dead," the she-goat thought. "Don't weep, my friend. I'll come with you."

As they were about to set out, however, the she-goat suddenly became doubtful. "Dead or alive, he's cruel and I'm afraid to come out," she said.

"And I had thought you were my friend! How unfortunate I am that I must bury my husband all by myself!" the she-jackal wept loudly.

"No! No! I cannot come. I'm afraid of him," the she-goat insisted. "But what harm can he do to you now that he is dead?"

"Friend, you walk ahead and show me the way. I'll follow," the she-goat suggested carefully.

A little later, they neared the jackal's cave. "Ah, footsteps! Here they come," the 'dead' jackal thought.

He forgot that he was supposed to play dead, and opened his eyes to look at the plump goat.

"He's alive!" the she-goat noticed, and ran for her life.

"The wicked wretch wants to kill me. They are both traitors!" she realized as she ran.

When she had gone, the she-jackal scolded her mate. "Humph! I thought you had won her confidence! I had! But you, my lord, had to be a fool and spoil it all."

The jackal was so crestfallen that his mate felt sorry for him. "Don't look so unhappy! I'll bring her again. This time, be on your guard."

"Ah! My friend, you have performed a miracle! As you came near him, my husband came to life again. He wants to meet you and thank you," the she-jackal returned to the goat's cave.

The ruse worked. But the she-goat had a plan of her own. "Dear friend, I've changed my mind. You'd better not come. Your cave might be burgled while you are away."

"No! Please don't bother. Some other time, perhaps," the she-jackal said nervously.

"But I want to come and... bring my friends to celebrate his miraculous recovery. I've invited an escort of two thousand dogs!" the she-goat announced cheerfully.

"Two thousand dogs! I've had enough of this goat," the terrified she-jackal muttered. "Then she ran for her life... till she reached her mate."

"Quick! We must run. Or else we'll make a meal for two thousand dogs!" she yelled.

"Two thousand dogs!" the jackal yelped in panic.

The jackal and his mate took to their heels, and they were not seen or heard of ever again.

docs.title: Jagannatha of Puri
docs.text:
Long long ago, King Indradyumna of the Sun Dynasty ruled over Utkal (ancient Orissa). He had conquered many lands and won great fame, but still he was not happy.

"I have everything a king could wish for! But I yearn to do what no man has done. Something truly wonderful, truly great! Something which will make people remember me for ever," he pondered. "Yes, I know! I will build a temple—a monument to God. It shall be the most beautiful temple on earth! But my beautiful temple must have an equally beautiful image. Where will I get such an image?"

His question was answered that night in a dream. "O King, behold this hill! It is named Nilachala. On this hill is a cave. Inside the cave you will find the most beautiful image ever seen of Nilamadhav—the blue-coloured Krishna," a voice proclaimed.

"I've found him! Nilamadhav—the God who is blue in colour! He shall be the one to grace my temple," the king rejoiced upon waking. "But... where is Nilachala Hill? Where do I look for it?"

The very next day he spoke to his trusted men about the dream. "Find the hill and the image and report to me. I shall then go and bring Nilamadhav to Utkal myself."

The officers set out in different directions. Among them was a young man named Vidyapati. "Something tells me that I should ride into the jungles... that I am bound to find the hill there."

He met people, but they had never heard of Nilachala. Undiscouraged, he searched deep into the jungle, eventually losing his way. Faint with hunger and thirst, he staggered up to a tree and collapsed.

He was found by a cowherd and a beautiful maiden, Lalita, daughter of the Savara chieftain, Vishvavasu. "Do not be afraid, beautiful maiden. I am Vidyapati from Utkal. I lost my way in the jungle," he introduced himself.

"Come with me, I'll take you to my father," Lalita offered. Vidyapati stayed with the Savaras, and soon, he and Lalita fell in love and were married.

However, Vidyapati noticed that Vishvavasu left every dawn and returned at dusk. He asked Lalita about it. "Sssh! Don't speak so loudly. It's a closely-guarded secret. My father goes deep into the jungle to a cave on a hill. Yes, the cave of the great Lord Nilamadhav, on Nilachala Hill."

"Nilachala! Nilamadhav!" Vidyapati realized. He begged Lalita to ask her father to take him there. Vishvavasu reluctantly agreed, but insisted Vidyapati be blindfolded.

That night, Vidyapati secretly took a handful of mustard seeds from the kitchen. The next day, as he was led blindfolded, he dropped the seeds along the path. "Pour rain, pour hard. Water my mustard seeds so they will sprout and flower!" he prayed.

When they reached the cave and the blindfold was removed, Vidyapati saw the magnificent image. "Beautiful! He is beautiful..."

A few days later, after the rains, Vidyapati followed the trail of yellow mustard flowers back to the cave to confirm the route. Then, he took leave of Lalita and Vishvavasu and returned to King Indradyumna.

"My lord, I have found Nilachala and Nilamadhav," he announced joyfully. The King, his Queen Gundicha Devi, and his army set out immediately.

But as they approached, a furious sandstorm arose, turning the landscape into a desert. "Where is the hill? My cave? My Nilamadhav? The trail of mustard plants ends abruptly here. And beyond them nothing but sand!" Vidyapati cried in despair. Nilamadhav had hidden himself from the eyes of men.

Vidyapati ordered his soldiers to drag Vishvavasu out. "It's my father-in-law, Vishvavasu! Let me go, you knaves!" the chief shouted. Vidyapati accused him of hiding the image.

But a divine voice spoke: "O Indradyumna, you still keep my beloved devotee Vishvavasu in chains! Free him at once! Only if the two of you together lift me up shall I consent to be moved."

The King freed Vishvavasu, embracing him. Later, Indradyumna had another dream. "O King, your tears have moved me. But I cannot appear again as Nilamadhav, in which form I am lost to the world for ever. Instead, I shall appear in the form of a Daaru (log) floating on the sea."

The next morning, they found the Daaru on the seashore. It bore the Shankha, Chakra, Gada, and Padma—the symbols of Vishnu. They harnessed elephants and men to pull it, but it wouldn't budge until Indradyumna and Vishvavasu, the two rival devotees brought together in love, lifted it together.

The Daaru was brought ashore, but no craftsman could carve it. Their fine axe-blades broke upon striking the wood.

Then, an old man walked in. "I am willing to carve the Daaru, but... I will sculpt it as I like. Besides, I must be left undisturbed for fifteen days in a sealed room. On no account should you break open the door." It was the divine architect Vishvakarma in disguise.

The King agreed. But after ten days, Queen Gundicha became restless. "My lord, why is there no sound from within? Could something have happened to the old man? Shouldn't we open the door and see?"

Despite the King's reluctance, she insisted, and the door was broken open. "The old man has vanished! Oh, no! What have I done! We are left with three incomplete images," the King lamented.

Just then, Sage Narada appeared. "O Indradyumna, I come from Vaikuntha. I've been sent to tell you that you should not be disappointed with these images. It is Lord Vishnu's will that the Daaru should be carved thus. I shall start work on the temple to enshrine these wooden images."

The magnificent temple was built by the seashore, with a huge enclosure for cows to be given to the poor. The cows' hooves created a crater which filled with underground springs, becoming the Indradyumna Tank.

"Magnificent! Only Lord Brahma is worthy of consecrating it. Let us both go and invite him to earth," the King suggested to Narada.

They went to Brahmaloka, but Lord Brahma was deep in meditation. They had to wait for hours. "We have been here a whole day! How much longer will we have to wait?" the King asked.

When Brahma opened his eyes, he agreed to come. But on earth, many summers had come and gone. One day of Brahma was equal to thousands of years on earth! A different king, Gala Madhav, was now ruling over Utkal.

Gala Madhav had discovered the temple buried under the sand and installed his own deity in it. When Indradyumna and Narada descended, they were shocked by the changes.

They found the temple. "This man is the king who originally built this temple. He reigned here a long, long ago. You must give back his temple to him," Narada told Gala Madhav.

"Are you mad too? A king from long ago? Then how is he still alive today? Where is the proof?" Gala Madhav demanded.

"If you are willing to believe an old tortoise then I will give you proof!" Narada said. A huge tortoise rose from the Indradyumna Tank. "My name is Akupar. I remember you, Indradyumna. I have lived for centuries in this tank... Yes, it was you who built this temple."

Lord Brahma appeared and Gala Madhav sought forgiveness. The three wooden images were recovered from where Vishvakarma left them, placed on the Mahavedi, and Brahma performed the Pranapratishtha pooja.

"My lord, let no one claim this temple as his after I die. Henceforth, this temple shall belong to all mankind," Indradyumna wished.

"Noble King, your fame will live on! As you have given over this temple to the people of the world, it shall be known as the shrine of 'JAGANNATHA'—Lord of the Universe," Brahma proclaimed.

Indradyumna is remembered in Orissa to this day, as are Gundicha Devi, Vidyapati, Lalita, and Vishvavasu, especially during the magnificent car festival of Jagannatha—the Ratha Yatra.

docs.title: Jallianwalla Bagh
docs.text:
In the year 1919, there was unrest in Punjab. In Amritsar, the British rule was considered both oppressive and inefficient by the people. "Prices are soaring, what can I do? The government extorts high taxes!" people complained.

To make matters worse, the government passed the Rowlatt Bill. "If the government wants to, it could have you arrested and sent to jail, merely on suspicion," the people realized with shock. "The Rowlatt Act gives the government that power."

People in Amritsar, as in other parts of India, looked to Gandhiji for guidance. "Gandhiji says the Rowlatt Act is a symbol of national humiliation!" Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlew, a prominent Gandhian, explained. "Satyagraha is a process of self-purification. Ours is a sacred fight... to this end Gandhi wants that all the people of India should observe a day of hartal on March 30."

March 30, 1919, was observed as a day of prayer and total abstention from work. No shops were opened. The streets were deserted. In the evening, a meeting was held at Jallianwalla Bagh, attended by over 30,000 persons. The meeting was a total success, and there was no violence.

Soon after, there was news that the date of the hartal had actually been postponed by Gandhiji to April 6. The April 6 hartal was as successful as the previous one.

Mr. Irving, the Deputy Commissioner of Amritsar, was a worried man. "These men, Kitchlew and Satyapal, seem to have a tremendous influence over the people. They just call for a hartal and the people obey," he thought. He called a conference of senior officials. "If we deport Kitchlew and Satyapal, I expect trouble," he warned. They decided to deport the leaders secretly.

On April 10, Dr. Kitchlew and Dr. Satyapal were called to the Deputy Commissioner's bungalow on the pretext of a discussion. Soon after, they were placed in motor cars and driven away under a British escort.

In spite of all precautions, the news spread within an hour. "Our leaders have been arrested! Close your shop! Stop all business!" Excited groups of people gathered in the streets to protest. Soon the crowds snowballed to 50,000 ordinary, unarmed citizens.

"We must have our leaders back or we must be prepared to die! We will talk to the Deputy Commissioner! Where are our leaders?" the crowd demanded.

The crowd walked towards the civil lines, until they reached the carriage-bridge. "Look! There are mounted troops on the carriage-bridge!"

"Keep moving! Don't be afraid," the leaders urged.

When they had nearly reached the other end of the bridge, the mounted soldiers, in desperation, turned round and fired at the crowd. Three or four died and many were wounded.

The people were provoked. They picked up stones stacked on the roadside and threw them at the picket. "We must have our two leaders back... or we are ready to die!"

The police fired again, bringing the crowd to a standstill. The sight of the dead and wounded being carried through the bazaars roused the crowds to anger and revenge. Tension began to mount. "Yes, he's dead! Killed by the Sahibs! We will kill the Sahibs!"

Disorganised and leaderless, the crowd had now become a mob. They attacked the railway goods yard, the telegraph office, and set banks on fire, killing the British managers. In their blind fury, they even attacked an English woman, Miss Sherwood, knocking her from her bicycle and beating her, though she was later saved by local shopkeepers.

The town had become unsafe for Europeans. Women and children were taken to the Govindgarh fort under armed escort.

At 9:00 P.M. on April 11, Brigadier-General Dyer arrived in Amritsar and took charge. "The whole city is hostile to the British. We are besieged. Civil authority has come to an end," the officials told him.

On April 13, Dyer issued a proclamation in Urdu and Punjabi. "Any person found in the streets after 8 P.M. is liable to be shot down. Any gathering of more than four men will be treated as unlawful and dispersed by force of arms."

Immediately after this, two men went round the city banging an empty tin. "A meeting will be held at Jallianwalla Bagh at 4:30 P.M. Messages from Kitchlewji and Satyapalji will be read out! All are welcome," they announced.

Dyer was informed. "So they still persist in their folly. Well, we shall see who has the last laugh!" he declared.

Jallianwalla Bagh was a large enclosed area in the heart of the town, surrounded by houses, with only three or four narrow openings. That day happened to be Baisakhi, the New Year's Day, particularly auspicious to the Sikhs. The town was full of holidaying peasants from neighbouring villages who had come to the Golden Temple and the Bagh to relax, unaware of the ban.

Soon the crowd swelled into a big gathering of at least 25,000 people, including teenagers and children. The meeting began, and speakers addressed the gathering peacefully.

Suddenly, a plane flew overhead. People panicked, but were reassured by a political worker. "Sit down! Sit down! Don't panic! The plane is doing its work and we will continue ours."

Meanwhile, Dyer had assembled a striking force of ninety men. At 4:15 P.M., it was sent to Jallianwalla Bagh. Dyer and his troops advanced up the narrow passageway.

Seconds later, General Dyer gave the order. "FIRE!"

"There was a flash... but the people just could not believe their eyes. 'Ho! Ho! Blank shots! Those were blank shots!'"

But the grim reality of the situation was realised as a number of people in the crowd fell, some dead and some wounded. Blindly some people began to run.

"But where could they go? The soldiers with their guns were at the entrance." While they were running helter-skelter, the soldiers were reloading and firing. They fired low, aiming at maximum killing.

On Dyer's order, the guns were directed straight at the narrow gaps through which people were trying to escape. People continued to fall, but still the shooting did not stop.

When the shooting stopped ten minutes later, there were heaps of dead bodies everywhere. At least one thousand people were killed and three times that number wounded. The wounded cried out for water.

"But the soldiers just marched away." Even in a war, wounded enemy soldiers are given first aid, but at Jallianwalla Bagh, unarmed citizens were abandoned to die in misery. Dyer instituted a strict curfew at 8:00 P.M., preventing anyone from helping the wounded.

"I'll tell you what. For a start it should not deport the people who stand for non-violence and who could act as the conscience of the people," prominent citizens told the Deputy Commissioner later, appalled at the massacre.

The British tried to suppress the Jallianwalla Bagh episode through censorship, but the news slowly trickled through India. There was a general outcry. The British government was forced to set up a committee of enquiry, called the Hunter Committee.

"I could have dispersed them for some time. But then they would all have come back and laughed at me and I considered I would be making myself a fool," Dyer callously told the committee.

The Hunter Committee criticized Dyer for opening fire without warning and continuing to fire after the crowd had begun to disperse. He was forced to resign. Yet, many British officials and citizens approved of his actions, viewing him as the man who "saved the Punjab."

But as far as Indians were concerned, the Jallianwalla Bagh episode was the beginning of the end of the British Raj. It turned millions of loyal supporters into nationalists, strengthening the resolve for freedom.

docs.title: Jasma of the Odes
docs.text:
Jasma was the daughter of Nayak Dalo Dhand, chief of the nomadic Ode tribe. When Jasma was born, her mother said, "She reminds me of a jasmine, fair and pretty as she is. Then let us call her Jasma."

Unfortunately for Jasma, her mother died when she was just a toddler. "Poor motherless Jasma. I must marry again and bring a mother for you," Dalo decided.

Dalo married a girl called Dali. But Dali soon became jealous of her stepdaughter. "Jasma—Jasma. I'm sick of that name. I wonder why she didn't die with her mother," she thought. From the day she set eyes on her, Dali disliked Jasma and often ill-treated her.

As Jasma grew up, Dali's dislike turned into jealous hatred, for Jasma was easily the most beautiful girl in the Ode tribe. She scolded her all day. "Lazy girl. Go to the well and fetch some water if you have nothing else to do."

"I didn't say I wouldn't, mother," Jasma replied dutifully.

That night Dali spoke to Dalo. "We must arrange Jasma's marriage soon. She has come of age. What about my cousin, Bhalo Bhand's son, Rupa?"

"B... but Rupa is a cripple!" Dalo protested. But Dali did not give up and forced Dalo to agree.

When they went to Bhalo Bhand, Dalo was honest. "Cousin, you know that all I have is for Jasma. I have one head of cattle. Please accept it and look after my little flower."

"Cattle or no cattle, Jasma shall become my daughter-in-law!" Bhalo declared warmly.

On the day of the wedding, Jasma went through the ceremony bravely. But that night when she saw Rupa's crippled form and face clearly for the first time, she broke down. "He's ugly too! Alas! What have I done to deserve this?"

"Jasma, forgive me. I know how you must feel. I will try to make you as happy as I can. Please don't weep," Rupa comforted her, showing his heart of gold.

Jasma was moved by his words. Ashamed of herself, she fell at his feet. "I am sorry, Rupa. I should ask your forgiveness. I will be a loyal wife to you and will look after all your needs."

A few months later, Jivaji Barot, a court poet of Gujarat, happened to pass by the Ode camp. "What a beautiful girl! I have never seen anyone like her," he marvelled.

He hurried back to Patan, the capital of King Siddharaj Jaisingh. "Your majesty, I have seen the most beautiful girl in creation—Jasma, the daughter of the chief of the Ode tribe," Jivaji reported, singing verses praising her beauty.

"A mere tribal girl! You call her a queen! If it is an exaggeration, Jivaji, I'll behead you!" the King warned.

"If you see her, your majesty, you will call her a goddess. But tribal customs will not permit Jasma to come to the palace," Jivaji explained.

Meanwhile, a terrible famine hit Gujarat. The Odes were badly hit and starving. Jivaji advised the King, "The Odes are badly in need of work. If your majesty can provide it, the Odes will come to Patan."

The King agreed and announced the construction of the Sahasra-Linga lake. The Odes accepted the work and camped on the outskirts of the capital.

The next day, as the King rode to the site, he saw Jasma carrying a load. "My God! What a beautiful creature! She must be a goddess," he gasped.

He rode up to her. "O beautiful one, what is your name?"

"I am Jasma of the Ode tribe, your majesty," she replied simply.

"Delicate one, you remind me of the jasmine flower. Say you will become mine and I will take you to my palace! I will make you the Queen of Gujarat," the King offered.

"I'd rather live in my hut. Ha! Ha! Ha! What would I do with a kingdom?" Jasma laughed, spurning him.

Her laughter maddened the King. "If you will not come willingly, I'll take you away by force."

"Don't you dare touch me. I am a married woman," Jasma retorted fearlessly.

The King sent his minister with a bag of gold to buy her from Dalo and Rupa. "A bag of gold... for you, provided you leave Jasma behind for the King," the minister offered.

"We work with earth and stone. What would we do with gold? We will not sell our honour. Leave us!" Dalo and Rupa rejected the offer angrily.

The spurned King decided to take her by force. He sent a palanquin to fetch her. Jasma filled the palanquin with rocks and sent it back. "I'll spare the tribe but I'll kill her husband and make her mine," the furious King decided, ordering his guards to kill Rupa.

The guards attacked the Ode camp. The Odes fought back bravely, but Rupa was killed. Then the King ordered his army to capture the rest of the tribe.

The Odes fled in the dead of night, but the royal army pursued them. The Odes fought to the finish. The King's army was initially routed, but the commander changed strategy and surrounded them. Dalo was killed.

When Jasma heard of the deaths, she refused to run away. "I am an Ode. An Ode never runs away! I will join my husband," she declared.

She prepared a funeral pyre and committed Sati. As the flames enveloped her, she cursed the King: "Alas! I am doomed! My kingdom is doomed. The great King Siddharaj Jaisingh will die a leper for lusting after a chaste wife."

As the flames consumed her, Laldev, Indra's messenger, appeared. "I am Laldev... I have come to take you to heaven."

"I will not go with you leaving all those who died for me behind," Jasma replied firmly.

Struck by her loyalty, Laldev granted her wish. "Then I shall bring them back to life." One by one, the dead Odes breathed again. Rupa and Dalo were revived.

"You may stay, Jasma. The doors of heaven are ever open to you. Come when you are ready," Laldev smiled, returning to heaven.

Many, many years later, Jasma died. After her death, songs were composed on her and temples were built in her memory. "Jasma, you were famous for your beauty, but we remember you for your virtues," her people sang.

docs.title: The Greedy Forester
docs.text:
The Bodhisattva assumed many forms through many life cycles. And so it was that when Brahmadatta reigned in Varanasi, he was born an elephant in a forest in the Himalayas.

In a grove of deodar trees, the proud mother stood, her newborn by her side. "Have you seen the new baby? He's white all over and shines like molten silver. He looks like a full moon leaning on a dark cloud," the other elephants marvelled.

The elephant grew up in the forest, with other little elephants. As he grew older, he noticed that most of his companions were selfish, greedy, and even cruel. The white elephant decided to leave the herd. "I don't belong here. I'd rather live alone."

And so he dwelt apart, giving help and good counsel when needed. One day he saw a little monkey weeping. "Why are you crying?" "I'm small and weak. My friends tease me." The elephant comforted him. Many an evening found him advising an eager group of animals, young and old. Soon he came to be known as 'Good King Elephant'.

One day, a forester from Varanasi lost his way in that forest. "I have never felt more frightened in my life. Every path seems to lead me farther away from home," he cried, exhausted and terrified. As the light grew fainter, he saw a shadow. "An elephant! I'm as good as dead! Heaven help me! I'll be crushed to pulp!"

But every time he moved, the elephant followed. Every time he stopped, the elephant too stopped. "Why, he seems harmless! He's only following me, not chasing me as I thought," the forester realized. "I'll turn round and stop. Let's see what he does."

To his surprise, the animal spoke to him. "I heard you scream. Is there anything I can do for you?"

"What a kind, gentle voice! And yet, so deep and powerful!" the forester thought. "I've lost my way. I want to go to Varanasi," he replied.

"Come home with me. I'll guide you to the city," the elephant offered. The elephant took the forester to his cave and gave him food and rest. In the evening they walked to a clearing. "Let us watch the sun go down. It fills my heart with peace," the elephant said. "I envy you, your peace of mind. We men are restless creatures," the forester replied.

After a few days, the forester said, "I'd love to stay on, but I have work to do in Varanasi."

"I'll take you to the edge of the forest and set you on your way to the city," the elephant agreed. The forester rode on the elephant's back. As they went, the forester thought greedily, "I must remember this clump of trees... and this hill and this winding path. I may need to come back some day."

At the edge of the forest, he dismounted. "There lies the road to Varanasi. Thank you, my friend."

A few days later, the forester happened to visit the ivory bazaar at Varanasi. "What beautiful things you create!" he told the craftsmen.

"They could be even more beautiful, but good tusks are hard to come by. It's the best. But it's rare and costs much more than the tusk of a dead one," the craftsmen explained, referring to the ivory of a living elephant.

Greed got the better of the forester. He went back to the forest and traced his way to the elephant. "What is the matter? You look unhappy," the elephant asked.

"I am up to my neck in debt. A piece of your tusk can save me," the forester lied.

"I will gladly give you both my tusks. But you will have to cut them off. What you hold are no ordinary tusks. They are the source of all my wisdom."

"I came prepared. I knew you would not fail me," the forester said, bringing out a saw and eagerly cutting off the tusks.

Back in Varanasi, the forester received a big sum of money for the tusks. But a month later, his greed flared again. "After I buy these silks and jewels, there'll be no money left. I should have cut closer to the flesh. What a waste of good ivory."

That night he could not sleep. "If I cut any more, the elephant will suffer. But I must not be sentimental. I must get hold of those precious stumps of tusks."

So back he went to the elephant. Greed had hardened his heart. "Your tusks did help to clear my old debts. But I need more money in order to live," he pleaded.

"You may have what is left of my tusks," the noble elephant agreed. He crouched down to give the tusks. The forester carried out his cruel plan, pinning the elephant's trunk down with his foot and sawing them off. The elephant was left torn and trembling, but not a word of reproach escaped his lips.

Suddenly, the forester felt the ground heave under his feet. The earth split open and a fire raged. The forester fell in, realizing he was being punished for his greed, but it was too late.

As the flames consumed him, a voice was heard: "An ungrateful man is never satisfied—not even if he is given the whole world."

As for the wise elephant, he lived the rest of his life in the peace and quiet of the Himalayas.

docs.title: The Brave Quail
docs.text:
In a forest near Varanasi, there once lived some quails. The shady grove in which they nested was also the favourite grazing ground of a herd of elephants. A wise and just elephant, the Bodhisattva, was the leader of that herd.

One day, one of the quails laid some eggs. Soon the fledglings were hatched. One day, the elephant herd came marching towards the grove.

"Look! The elephants are making for our grove! What shall I do? I can only fall at their feet and beg for protection," the mother quail cried.

As the leader came close, the mother quail wasted no time. She was at his feet, her head lowered in salute. "O mighty elephant, my little ones are in danger. If your herd enters this grove, they will be trampled to death."

"Do not fear. Your fledglings will not be harmed," the leader assured her. The elephant stood over the nest while his herd grazed, protecting the babies. When they had had their fill, he warned the quail. "There is a rogue-elephant, a wild and dangerous animal, who might soon be coming this way. You must appeal to him for mercy and hope for the best."

Soon after the elephants had gone, the rogue-elephant appeared. He looked fierce. The mother quail begged him just as she had the leader. "O powerful one, I beg of you, spare my young ones!"

"How dare you come in my way? I will soon show you how strong I am!" the arrogant rogue roared. He lashed at the nest, crushing it, and stamped the babies to death.

"There! That is the end of your silly brood," he scoffed, and walked away.

As the quail wept over the remains of her dead children, she made a vow. "I will soon show you how strong I am!"

Grief had made her bold and set her thinking hard. She went to a crow and told him her sad tale. "Depend on me. Such wickedness should not go unpunished," the crow promised. "You must spot this rogue-elephant and peck out both his eyes."

Having got the crow on her side, the quail went to an ant. "I heard about your babies. I am deeply grieved," the ant said.

"My little friend, I need your help. My friend the crow will peck out his eyes. After that you must lay your eggs in the empty sockets. The little ones will begin to bite."

"A good idea! When they hatch, my little ones will begin to bite," the ant agreed.

The quail then went to the frog. "Dear friend, leave everything and come out! I am trying to get my friends to help me punish him."

"You can count on me," the frog replied.

"When the ants hatch, the rogue-elephant will be badly stung. He will run blindly, looking for water to ease his pain. This is what I want you to do... The croak of a frog! There must be water near by. I'll follow the sound," she explained her plan.

A little later, the crow darted at the elephant... plucked his eyes out, and flew away. Then, the ant laid her eggs in his blind eyes.

"A-A-AH! My eyes are on fire. Water! I need water!" the blinded, agonized elephant cried.

Just then the frog croaked from a steep precipice close by. "CROAK!"

As the elephant got to the edge of the precipice, the frog leapt onto a narrow ledge below and croaked with all his might.

The elephant followed the sound and went hurtling down the cliff to his death!

When the crow, the ant, the frog and the quail met later, the quail looked down at the dead elephant. "I hope the story of the elephant will be a good example to all strong creatures who harm the weak and the helpless."

docs.title: The Royal Elephant
docs.text:
When King Brahmadatta reigned in Varanasi, there lived, not far from the city, a colony of carpenters. Every day they would sail down the river and enter a dense forest to fell trees for timber. Thus they moved from forest to village and back again. Work was plentiful.

One day, as they sat down to their midday meal, they saw an elephant limping towards them. "He is limping and seems to be in great pain," one carpenter noticed.

The elephant came up to the carpenters and held out a foot. "I stepped on something—I think it's a splinter," he seemed to indicate.

"You are right. It has entered quite deep into the flesh," a carpenter said. They removed the splinter, bathed and dressed the wound, and nursed the foot back to health.

"You have been very kind to me. I would like to come here every day and help you," the elephant seemed to say.

The elephant served them well for a number of years. One day he brought a young one with him. "Look! Our friend has brought a young one with him today. What a beautiful white animal!"

"I am no longer as active as I used to be. I have brought my son to take my place," the old elephant conveyed. He then walked away, leaving the elephant torn between his old life and his new friends, but the young white elephant stayed.

The young elephant worked hard. He helped to load and unload timber. He fetched and carried tools. And transported logs. More than that, he brought joy and laughter into the carpenters' lives, playing with their children and swinging them on his trunk.

One day, quite by accident, the rain washed some of his droppings into the river. Later, downstream, where the royal elephants bathed, the trainers noticed something. "Our elephants are refusing to get into the water."

"The water contains the droppings of a noble animal. He must live upstream somewhere," the trainer guessed. "Let us report the matter to the King."

When the trainer reported the matter to the King, the King himself set out with a band of trusted men. "That must be the animal we are looking for. How nobly he bears himself!" the King exclaimed when they found him.

"Do you think he wants us to work in the palace?" the carpenters wondered.

"If he did, he would send for us, not come here himself," the chief carpenter reasoned.

"We would like this elephant for our royal stables. Pay them a hundred thousand pieces of gold for his tail, the same for his trunk and for each of his four feet," the King offered.

"You must not think me greedy, your majesty. They need clothes. And the children too must be provided for," the elephant seemed to convey.

"We admire your loyalty. We shall more than satisfy you," the King promised.

Upon receiving this assurance, the elephant agreed to go with the King. It was a sad leave-taking. "Who will play with us?" the children cried.

The city wore a festive air. The elephant, regally caparisoned, was led to his gaily decorated stable. The nobles and ministers looked on in admiration. "He is the most majestic animal I have ever seen."

The King made over half his kingdom to the elephant. "You are a brother to me and a companion for life," the King told him. The elephant became deeply attached to the King and followed him everywhere. "My Lord, he refuses to touch his food till you have first fed him a morsel," the keepers noted.

A few months later, the Queen was expecting a baby. "The elephant has brought us great good fortune. My power as a monarch is now unchallenged. I feel our child is destined for great things," the King rejoiced.

But their joy was short-lived. The King fell seriously ill. As he lay dying, he thought, "My elephant will be broken-hearted. Our child will never know a father!"

"We must keep the sad news from the elephant," the Queen and ministers agreed. "I wonder how long it will be before he finds out!"

Meanwhile, the King of Kosala was marching towards Varanasi. "The King is dead, and confusion reigns. We should ask them to suspend fighting for a week," the ministers decided. The King of Kosala acceded to their request.

According to the astrologers, the Queen would give birth to her child within a week. If it was a son, they would fight. If not, they would surrender. "Long live the new-born King!" they cheered when a son was born. "Fight for the King! Fight to the last man!"

They fought bravely, but the lack of leadership told on them. "We have lost heavily. What do you expect? Our leader lies in a cradle," the generals despaired.

"I have an idea. Let the royal elephant take charge," the Prime Minister suggested.

They went to the Queen who sent for the elephant. While he gazed at her son, she told him, "His father is dead. If the King meant anything to you, he is now your charge."

"The King dead! Why was I not told? How can I live without my master?" the elephant grieved.

"As I do, for his child and for the kingdom that is his by right... and which is at this very moment in grave danger," the Queen pleaded.

The elephant knelt to receive the Queen's blessing. "I shall talk no more of death. Your son shall rule," he vowed.

"We now have something to live for," the Queen smiled.

As the keeper of the royal stables got him ready for the battle... the King's men felt their hopes rise. "Get the troops out! Let no man dawdle!"

And then the elephant charged forward, the skies resounding with his mighty trumpeting. "Put courage in your hearts and strength in your arms! Follow our leader. He is divinely inspired!" the generals shouted.

The Kosala armies were routed, the battle won. The elephant stood before the Queen, his proud head bowed before his infant master. "My grief is great for your dead father. But I live for you and for the great destiny that awaits you, my prince," the elephant seemed to say.

And the Bodhisattva (for the child was none other than he) grew up to inherit a kingdom which he ruled wisely and well.

docs.title: The Magic Chant
docs.text:
A pandit and his pupil were once passing through a forest. Suddenly, robbers jumped out! "Halt! Who goes there?"

They bound the pandit to a tree. "We have no money. Please let us go," the pandit pleaded.

"Ha! Ha! You cannot deceive us with humble talk and simple clothes! If you are carrying no money, your pupil can bring it from your house," the robber chief laughed. "Run fast and bring a thousand gold coins. Only then will your guru be freed!"

"Master, I'll come back as soon as possible," the pupil whispered to the pandit. "So please do not tell these thugs that you know the Vaidarbha Mantra! Now hurry up, boy, and do what you've been told!"

The Vaidarbha Mantra is a chant known to very few. If uttered when the stars are in a favourable position, it produces a shower of gems.

That night, as the pandit shivered in the cold, he looked up at the sky. "Oh! The planets are moving into the correct position. Should I tell the robbers about the Vaidarbha Mantra and so be free again?"

Remembering his pupil's advice, he hesitated. But finally, he reasoned, "After all, what is the use of knowledge if one cannot utilise it in times of crisis? Hey, chief! Listen! I have something important to say!"

"If I start a shower of gems, will you let me go?"

"A shower of gems? Are you joking?" the chief laughed.

"No, I'm serious. I can do it by means of a magic chant." The chief agreed.

"I can't do it without having a ritual bath and changing into clean silk clothes," the pandit said.

So the pandit was set free, bathed, and sat reciting the chant. The very next moment... "Gems! Those are precious gems falling down!" the robbers cried in amazement. They collected the gems and allowed the pandit to go.

But, the next moment, another gang of robbers surrounded them. "Stay where you are! You are surrounded! Tie them all up!" the chief of the second gang ordered.

"Wait! First tell me why you are doing this!" the first chief pleaded.

"What a stupid question! For your gems, of course," the second chief replied.

"He's invaluable! He knows a magic chant that will bring down a shower of jewels. That's how we came by ours!" the first chief revealed, pointing to the pandit.

The second gang let the first gang go and turned to the pandit. "My friend, let's have a big shower of gems!"

"You shall have it—but only after one year. I... I'm... sorry. The chant is effective only at a specific time. The auspicious moment will not occur again for another year," the pandit stammered in fear.

"Liar! Scoundrel! We won't let you get away with it," the furious second chief roared, killing the pandit. "Come on, let's go after those rascals we set free! They've tricked us, but they won't go far with those gems!"

A fierce fight ensued between the two gangs, and the first gang was vanquished. But the second gang then fought amongst themselves over the treasure. "Realising it was useless to fight, they pretended to be friends. They hid the treasure in the hollow of a tree."

Only two robbers were left alive. "I am feeling hungry. Let's go and eat," one said. "One of us should stay here to guard the treasure. You take the head..."

One robber went to buy food. The other hid behind a rock. "I'll hide behind this rock and surprise him when he comes back. I will share neither food nor jewels with him. I'll get rid of him!"

Meanwhile, the robber who bought food thought, "I'm starving. I'll eat first and then take the rest for my friend. Then he set off again, sprinkling poison on the remaining food."

When he arrived, the waiting robber sprang out and killed him. "And now, for some food. Ah! I am as rich as a king today!"

But it was all in vain. For, having eaten the poisoned food, he, too, fell dead.

When, two days later, the pupil returned with the money, he found the place littered with corpses. He realized what had happened. "My guru used the chant for the benefit of undeserving people! If only he had heeded my advice!"

docs.title: The Drummer
docs.text:
A drummer and his son once went to Varanasi to play at a festival. At the end of the festival, they were rewarded handsomely.

"Here is some money for both of you. Your son is a very good drummer," the organizers said, giving them money, jewels, and clothes.

As they walked back through the forest, the son was overjoyed. "Father, I enjoyed the festival very much."

"You gave a good performance, son," the father smiled.

The next moment, the son started beating the drum loudly. "What are you doing?"

"Beating the drum—what else? I feel on top of the world today, father!"

"Don't make such a din! You will attract robbers! This forest is full of them!" the father warned. "I'll play a martial beat to frighten them off, father!" the son replied, playing a rhythmic march.

Some thieves hiding in the jungle heard the loud drumming. "It appears a royal party is hunting in the forest," they thought, running away.

After a while, the father said, "Enough, son! Don't do it again! That should only be played once!"

But the boy went on. "Stop! Why don't you listen to me?" his father pleaded.

"Oh, father, you worry too much! I'll scare the thieves good and proper!" the son laughed, continuing to drum.

The thieves, hearing the continuous drumming, grew suspicious. "What! The same drum-beats thrice! That is no royal party. It is just somebody who has made a lot of money and is feeling happy! Let's attack him!"

The thieves quickly spotted the drummer and his son. "Stop! Don't move! Give us all you have!" they threatened. They stripped them of their money, clothes, and jewels, and ran off with the loot.

"I told you to stop, didn't I? But you wouldn't listen. In your pride, you foolishly invited your own doom by playing at the wrong time!" the father scolded.

"I have learnt my lesson now, father!" the poor boy sighed.

docs.title: The Sadhu and the Ram
docs.text:
One day, there was great excitement in Varanasi. People had gathered to watch a ram-fight. A sadhu happened to pass by and pushed his way in to watch.

"What is going on?" he asked.

"A ram-fight! Oh, what fine beasts they are!" someone replied.

The sadhu saw a ram retreating with his head bowed low. "He is stepping back! He is bowing before me!" the sadhu thought proudly. "O, noble beast! Only you, in this whole crowd of people, know a great soul when you see one!"

A man in the crowd called out in warning. "Be careful! He is not bowing before you...! He is preparing to attack! Please step back!"

"No, I won't! The poor animal wants me to bless him!" the arrogant sadhu refused.

A moment later... "A-A-H!" The ram charged forward with immense force and butted the sadhu, knocking him flat on the ground.

As the people rushed to help him up, the sadhu groaned in pain, realizing his folly. "I have invited this agony on myself! I was foolish and vain! Now I have paid the price!"

docs.title: The Day the Earth Broke Into Two
docs.text:
One day a rabbit was relaxing under a bilva tree. When a strange thought struck him. "I wonder what will happen if the earth breaks into two?"

Just then, a bilva fruit fell from the tree with a loud "THUD", hitting a dry palm leaf.

"Wh... what is that?" the terrified rabbit gasped. "Is the earth really going to break? I must run away to a safe place!"

He hopped up and ran as fast as his legs could carry him. The second rabbit saw him and followed. "What's the matter? Where are you going?"

"Don't ask me, brother. Just run for your life! Oh! The earth is about to break!" the first rabbit cried.

Very soon, a horde of frightened rabbits were running. Then they met a deer. "What has happened? Why the panic? I'll ask them." Learning that the earth was going to break, the deer also joined the rabbits.

Pigs, tigers, elephants, and other animals joined the stampede. Very soon a panic-stricken troupe was fleeing through the forest.

Then a lion came by. "What's going on here?"

"Oh, don't you know? The earth is breaking!" an animal cried.

"Hmm.... But that is impossible!" the wise lion thought. He climbed to the top of a hill and roared. "GRR-GRR-GRR!"

The animals froze in their tracks. "Why are you running? What is the matter?" the lion demanded.

"The earth is breaking, O mighty one! We must go away from here!"

"Who saw this happen?" the lion asked. The elephant blamed the tiger, the tiger blamed the panther, until finally, the rabbit was brought to the lion.

"Did you really see the earth breaking, little one?"

"Yes, my lord. I saw it with my own eyes. I was just lying down there wondering what I would do if the earth were to break, when suddenly... it happened! I heard a deafening noise and fled."

"A palm grove... a bilva tree... Hmm.... I am sure he has made a mistake," the lion deduced. "I'll go with the rabbit and find out what really happened. Wait here till I come back."

The lion took the trembling rabbit on his back and ran to the palm grove. "Show me the place where you heard the noise. Don't be afraid. I'm here to protect you."

"That's the bilva tree where I heard the thundering noise!" the rabbit pointed.

The lion looked closely at the palm leaves underneath the bilva tree. "Ah! Just as I thought! The sound of the fruit falling on dry leaves frightened the rabbit and he thought that the earth was breaking."

Returning to the waiting animals, the lion told them what he had discovered. "Friends, you did not check if this was true. Blindly relying on a false rumour, you would have paid a heavy price today."

docs.title: The Foolish Tree
docs.text:
Long ago, two trees lived in peace and friendship in a forest which was full of wild beasts. Lions and tigers roamed freely in the forest and preyed on other animals.

After they had had their fill, they left the carcasses behind... causing a foul stench all round. The forest was reeking with the smell.

One day, one of the trees complained. "Friend, these animals are such a nuisance! I feel suffocated by the smell. Yes, it is becoming difficult to live here."

"I can't bear it any longer! I'll drive these animals away," the tree declared angrily.

"Oh, no! Please don't! The wild animals protect us, and keep men away! But just imagine, how pure and pleasant the air will be!" the other tree warned him gently.

"I'll do it! I'll drive them away!"

One night, shortly afterwards, a strong breeze blew through the forest. "Ah! A strong breeze is blowing this way. Here is my chance!" the foolish tree thought.

The tree shook itself vigorously, making a loud noise. SHR-SHR-SHR!

This awakened the animals sleeping beneath it. "What's happening? Look at that tree! How it is swaying!" the animals gasped.

"The tree is possessed! Let's run away!" the tigers cried.

"Yes, all of us had better leave! Hurry!"

The next morning, the foolish tree was delighted. "At last, I've got rid of those animals! Already, the air smells fresher!"

Many days passed. Quiet and peace reigned in the forest. Then, one afternoon, a cowherd passed that way. "Sh...o...o...o... come back, you naughty one! The lion will eat you up!" he shouted to a straying calf.

Following the calf, the boy ventured into the forest. "I hope a tiger doesn't pounce on me!" But as he looked around, he noticed something. "But there seem to be no tigers here! And no animal footprints anywhere around!"

He went home and told his family about his discovery. The next day, the villagers arrived. "It looks as if no animals have lived here for ages! This land could be very useful to us."

As the men examined the trees, the wise tree whispered to the foolish one, "You see what they are planning? I had told you to let the wild beasts alone."

"Don't be silly. I can frighten those men away, as I did the beasts!" the foolish tree boasted.

The tree shook itself violently, making a loud sound. "What is that awful sound, father?" a young boy asked.

"It's nothing, son! Just the breeze, rustling through the leaves!" the father smiled.

The tree tried again, shaking even harder. "Hey! Watch out! Oh! It's nothing to worry about! Just a few twigs have fallen down! We'll cut down these trees. The wood will come in useful."

Soon the forest was full of people from the village nearby, hacking down the trees. "Just a few more strokes, brother! Yes, then the tree will fall down."

"They're pointing to me! What shall I do?" the foolish tree cried in despair.

That night, it pleaded with its friend. "Help, friend! Help me, please! You heard them, didn't you? They are coming for me tomorrow!"

"It is too late! Now you must bear the consequences of your action!" the wise tree sighed sadly.

And that was the end of the foolish tree.

docs.title: The Lost Gram
docs.text:
Once a king and his army had encamped outside a city. A monkey watched them closely from a tree. "I have been feeling hungry all day but now it appears I'll have a feast!"

A little later, the soldiers brought out big pots. "Let's roast some gram for the horses."

"Ha! Just as I expected. How lucky I am!" the monkey rejoiced.

"We'll feed the horses when the gram gets cold," the soldiers said, walking away.

"This is my chance!" As the soldiers turned to leave, the monkey swooped down from the tree... and ate a handful of gram. "This is delicious!"

But though his hunger was satisfied, his greed was not. "I must take away as much as I can!"

He stuffed his mouth with gram... and grabbed some more with both front paws. "I'll have enough for many more days!" he thought, climbing back up the tree.

But as he climbed, one grain rolled out and... fell on the ground.

"Oh... Oh, my gram! It's gone!" the monkey gasped.

Without thinking, he threw away all the gram he had in his paws and... sprang down to look for the one fallen grain.

"Where could it have gone? It must be somewhere around here!" he searched frantically in the dirt.

"I can't find it anywhere! And, alas, I have lost the rest of the gram too!" he cried in despair.

Thus, the foolish monkey, in being niggardly about one grain of gram, lost all he had.

docs.title: True Friends
docs.text:
Long, long ago, during the reign of King Magadha, a wedding was being celebrated in Rajagriha.

"What a sumptuous feast! And such pomp and ceremony!" the guests exclaimed. "And why not? After all, the groom's father is the richest merchant in town."

The bride was demure and beautiful. At first, she was treated with tenderness. "No! Don't do any work. Your lovely hands will be spoilt," her mother-in-law would say.

But a few months later... "Don't sit there dreaming. Can't you lend a hand in the housework?" the mother-in-law scolded. "Lovely, my foot! Two whole years she has been married to my son and still they have no child..."

The worried daughter-in-law confided in her maid. "I will not get any respect in this house unless I am with child."

"Why don't you pretend to be so?" the maid suggested. "With the advice and help of the maid, the daughter-in-law pretended to be an expectant mother and once again she began to be fussed over."

In due course, she said, "I think it's time to go to my parents' house for the delivery."

She set out on her journey. "There's a caravan ahead of us! Let's try to go with them," she told her maid.

They accompanied the caravan which camped in the evening near a grove of trees. Next morning, the caravan had moved on! And as the lady and her maid woke up, "What's this? A new-born baby!"

"Oh! The caravan people must have abandoned it!" the maid guessed. "Are you thinking what I'm thinking? Yes! Let's pretend it is your baby."

They hurried back with the baby boy. "An heir at last! What a lovely boy! We must celebrate his birth with great pomp," the family rejoiced.

At the naming ceremony, he was named Nigrodha Kumar.

Just then, two more boys were born in the city on the same day. In the merchants' house, the boy was named Shakha Kumar. In the weaver's house, there was a new-born who was named Pottik.

"Let them be brought here! They will be good company for my son," the merchant declared.

The three boys played together all day. When they were about eleven years old, they went to Takshashila for studies. "You two can afford the two thousand-coin fee... but my poor father is just a weaver. I cannot go with you," Pottik said sadly.

"I spoke to my father and he has agreed to pay for your studies too," Nigrodha offered generously.

While roaming from one place to another after their studies, they reached the outskirts of Varanasi. They spent the night under some trees. Early at dawn, Pottik woke up hearing the cackle of a hen.

"Why are you cackling so loudly over an egg?" the hen's mate asked.

"Because this one is not an ordinary egg. The one who eats the yolk of it is destined to be a king... the one who eats the white of it will become commander of the army... and the one who eats the shell will become a treasurer," the hen explained.

When the hens moved away, Pottik quietly picked up the egg. "I must try to find out whether there is any truth in what the hen said."

Carefully he separated the parts of the egg and mixed them in the breakfast porridge. "Here's your porridge, Nigrodha, with the yolk. And this is yours, Shakha. The white is in this one. I'll eat the one with the shell," Pottik thought to himself.
