When they had finished eating, Pottik repeated what he had heard. "Now if what the hen said is true, you will become a king soon, Nigrodha. Shakha will become the commander of the army and I the treasurer."

Shakha was unhappy but kept silent. "Who is he to decide? He should have given the yolk to me. I'll teach him a lesson one day," Shakha fumed inwardly.

They were warmly welcomed at the first house they visited in Varanasi. "It's been seven days since our childless king died. Now who will rule here?" the host asked.

"Don't you know the tradition? A flower-decorated chariot will be sent out today. It will have no charioteer. But the new king will be the one in front of whom the chariot stops."

The three young men set out. Nigrodha slept on a stone-slab in a park while the others slept on the steps outside.

An excited mob followed the decorated chariot. "The horse has stopped! At the entrance of the park!"

"Ah! What a noble face this young man has," the royal priest said, looking at Nigrodha. "His feet have the signs of an emperor."

The hubbub awoke Nigrodha. "Oh, sire! You are our chosen king. Accept this crown and the kingdom," the priest said.

Nigrodha Kumar was promptly installed as the king. "I name Shakha Kumar as my Commander-in-Chief. Pottik, you must stay with me as my constant companion," the new king declared.

One day, Pottik said, "I miss my father and mother. I will go and fetch them here."

With a small retinue, Pottik set off for Rajagriha. He brought his parents back. On his way to Varanasi, Pottik stopped at Shakha's house. "Tell your master that his friend Pottik is here."

But Shakha was arrogant. "Who's Pottik? I don't know anyone by that name. Beat him and throw him out. He must be a madcap."

The guards threw Pottik out. "What an ungrateful wretch. I'm sure my friend Nigrodha will not behave thus," Pottik thought.

Pottik reached the palace late in the evening. "O King! Your friend Pottik desires to see you," a guard announced.

"Bring him in right away," Nigrodha smiled. "Come, you must be tired after your journey."

Nigrodha welcomed him warmly, gave him clothes and ornaments, and made him the royal treasurer. "You will have the authority to determine the taxes payable by the members of the various guilds. You will be in charge of the treasury."

Shakha's silence infuriated the king. "O you wicked liar! Take him away! Kill him," King Nigrodha roared when he learned what Shakha had done.

"No! Please don't, O King! It is not easy to bring anyone back to life... therefore, I don't want him killed. Please forgive him," Pottik intervened.

"If you insist. But I want him banished from my kingdom," the king declared.

"A favour done to a deserving noble person is like a seed sown in fertile soil. It will reap rich rewards. Indeed, any favour done to an ungrateful man is like a seed burnt by fire. Nothing ever comes out of it," the king concluded.

With Pottik by his side, Nigrodha Kumar ruled wisely and well for many years.

docs.title: The Hidden Treasure
docs.text:
One day, long long ago, a woman came walking dejectedly down the road that led away from the city of Mithila. Her steps were slow and her feet dragged. She wore the garb of a maid but seemed to be of noble birth.

"I cannot walk a step farther. What shall I do? Perhaps that carter will..." she thought.

"How far are you going, sir? Can you take me to Kalachampa?" she asked.

"You are very kind, sir! I... I don't know how to thank you," she said as he offered her a ride.

As the cart jogged along, the exhausted woman fell asleep. When she woke up it was evening.

"Which city is this?" she asked.

"Why, it's the city of Kalachampa! We have arrived."

After alighting from the cart, she walked into the city. "I came here because this was the only city I'd heard of. Where shall I go now?"

A little later, a teacher came by with his pupils. "Who are you? You seem to be in distress," he asked.

"I... I am... I am the Queen of Mithila. My husband was killed in battle. I had to flee the city for the sake of my unborn child."

"Please don't hesitate. Tell me the truth. I may be able to help you. You may stay at my house. My wife will look after you."

The Brahmana and his wife looked after her with love and care. After a few days, a son was born to her. "What shall we call him, brother?" she asked.

"We'll call him Mahajanaka," the Brahmana replied.

The Queen and her son stayed on in the house. When Mahajanaka grew a little older, he made friends with the boys in the neighbourhood. But they often quarrelled, and Mahajanaka, who was the strongest, would beat up the others.

"He has no father!" the boys mocked him.

Mahajanaka ran to his mother. "Oh, mother! Who is my father?"

"How can I, son? Our enemies shouldn't know that we are alive! Oh, what shall I do?" she thought, but eventually she told him the truth. "You are the son of King Aritthajanaka of Mithila! A few days before you were born, your father had to do battle with his enemy, Poljanaka. We lost, and our king is no more!"

Years passed. Mahajanaka grew up to be a handsome and learned man. "Mother, do you have any money with you?"

"I have a few gold coins and ornaments. They are all yours."

"With my half I shall buy some merchandise, carry on trade overseas and earn the money I'll need to raise an army. I wish to go to Mithila to claim my throne."

Soon the day of departure dawned. "Mother, bless me! And don't worry. May God be with you, my son."

Meanwhile, in Mithila, Poljanaka was alive but seriously ill and he had no son. "I have given the matter deep thought and made my decision. The man my daughter chooses for a husband, shall be the king," he told his ministers.

"Your majesty, Princess Shivali will choose a good man, but how can we be sure that he will make a good king?"

"I have thought about that too. Her suitor will have to perform three tasks to prove his intelligence, strength and wisdom," the king replied. "First, he must point out the head of the Princess' square bed. Then he must string a bow which would ordinarily require the strength of a thousand men. Lastly, with the help of sixteen clues, he will have to find the sixteen pots of gold I've hidden in and around the palace."

A few days later the King died.

The ministers tried to find a husband for Shivali. The General tried, but failed to please her. The Treasurer tried, and failed. All the others who tried to woo her also failed.

Meanwhile, on board Mahajanaka's ship, a storm broke. Wave after mighty wave lashed at the ship. "I must remain calm! I must not panic. I have a mission to accomplish. I must not let death rob me of the chance to regain the throne of Mithila," Mahajanaka resolved, holding on to the mast.

As the ship sank under the onslaught of the waves, Mahajanaka tightened his grip on the mast. A few days later, Mahajanaka was washed ashore.

Later, in a garden outside the eastern gate of the city of Mithila, he overheard two men talking. "...and so Mithila is still without a king!"

"I am in Mithila! And Mithila is without a king! But I have neither weapons nor money. I must move cautiously," he thought, falling asleep on a stone slab.

At the palace, the ministers consulted the royal priest. "Send out the sacred chariot. It will lead us to the right man."

The chariot, accompanied by the royal priest and ministers, rolled past the houses of the general and the treasurer, and out of the eastern gate. It came to a halt near the spot where Mahajanaka lay sleeping.

"He's a total stranger," the ministers observed. "Let the drums be beaten. If he's a coward, he will flee. If he is brave, he won't be frightened."

When the din awoke him, there was no sign of panic in Mahajanaka's eyes. He went back to sleep calmly.

The priest came forward. "Rise, O noble one. The kingdom awaits you!"

When Mahajanaka arrived at the palace, Princess Shivali tested him. "The Princess is waiting for you in her chamber," a maid informed him. He deliberately took his time.

When he finally entered, Shivali thought, "How serene and confident his manner! How majestic! He is the man for me!"

"May I relax here for a while?" he asked, sitting on her square bed. "Give it to me," he said, holding out a pin.

Shivali handed it to him, and he placed it on the bed. "That—the place where Shivali would naturally preserve her pins when she removes them—is the head of the bed," the ministers marvelled. "It is. He is right."

Next, he was taken to the armoury. There, Mahajanaka lifted the great bow with ease and strung it. "He's strung it! It's incredible!"

"The last test. You have to find the sixteen pots of gold hidden in and around the palace. Are there any clues?" he asked.

"Yes. ...where the sun rises; and the second where the sun sets. The third..."

That night Mahajanaka thought hard. "...sun... sun... That's it! The sun also symbolizes light—the light of knowledge and truth. Now I know!"

The next morning, he asked, "Did your king honour ascetics and scholars? Where did he receive them when they came?"

The ministers took him outside the palace. "This is where the king received ascetics and scholars."

"Have this place dug up and you will find the first pot."

The place was dug up, and a huge pot of gold was found! He deciphered the remaining clues one after the other, and all the sixteen pots were found.

"You have proved yourself on every score, my lord. You must now marry the Princess, and make us happy," the ministers bowed.

"First, I want my mother and my uncle to be brought here from Kalachampa."

After a few weeks, the Brahmana arrived with Mahajanaka's mother. Mahajanaka and Shivali were married.

When Mahajanaka was crowned King of Mithila, he made an announcement: "I declare that the gold found in the pots will be distributed among the needy."

The declaration was received with great joy. "Long live King Mahajanaka!!"

docs.title: Kalakanni, The Unlucky
docs.text:
During the reign of Brahmadatta in Varanasi, there lived a prosperous merchant called Anathapindika.

One day, a servant announced, "Sir! A man called Kalakanni is here to see you!"

"But, sir! He looks very poor and wretched!" the servant added.

"Never mind! It must be my old friend. After all, not many people have a name like his! Show him in!"

Kalakanni walked in hesitantly. "Ah! It is you. How good to meet you after all these years!" Anathapindika welcomed him warmly.

"But why do you look so down and out?"

"My luck, I guess. Whoever chose my name was right. I am really unlucky. Truly, I have been so miserable," Kalakanni sighed.

"Hush! Don't ever say that! Not any more! Now you will stay with me and share my life. Those days we spent together at the gurukula were the happiest of my life."

"I have a big business here. I need a trustworthy friend like you to help me." And Kalakanni helped Anathapindika in his work.

Anathapindika's business associates were perplexed. "How can you trust a stranger so much?"

"He is no stranger! He is my childhood friend."

"But you have not seen him for years! And what an inauspicious name—Kalakanni (Unlucky). Why, even uttering such a name repeatedly might bring you bad luck."

"Bah! I don't believe in such things," Anathapindika dismissed their fears. "They say that a man who walks seven steps with you becomes a friend. After a fortnight of being together, he is like a family member..."

"I don't know how you can stand an ugly man all day. And he is a real gem, whatever be his name," Anathapindika defended his friend.

After a few months, Anathapindika told Kalakanni, "I'll be away for a few days. We are going to our native place for a wedding."

"But who will look after your house?"

"Oh! Kalakanni will be there. You mean you will trust him with all your valuable belongings? I can trust him with my life."

"Here are the keys! Kalakanni! Take care."

"Don't worry master! I will guard your house day and night," Kalakanni promised.

Some robbers saw Anathapindika leave. "Ah! The rich Seth Anathapindika is leaving town. Let's burgle his house tonight. The servants in the house are feeble and old. They will not be able to resist us."

By chance Kalakanni overheard the conversation. "Oh! How can I fight these stout rogues? But what if I can fool the thieves?"

At dusk, he gathered the servants together. "Here! You blow loudly on the conch shell. You must play on the drum. You have a sweet voice, you must sing. The rest of us will clap with the rhythm. Make as much noise as you can."

At night, when the dacoits arrived, they heard the loud din. "AAA THUMP! THUMP! CLAP CLAP."

"It sounds as if there's a celebration going on. Why, there must be a real crowd inside. The Seth must have returned. Let's run before anyone sees us," the robbers panicked and fled.

The next morning, the servants found footprints outside. "Thieves must have got in... but were scared off by our din."

When Anathapindika returned, he was told about Kalakanni's clever trick. "Of course, he is! Now do you realise why I value him so much?" Anathapindika smiled proudly at his friends.

docs.title: A Monk In Need Is A Friend Indeed
docs.text:
One day King Brahmadatta of Varanasi and his son were busy discussing matters of state. "We must send more soldiers of our army to the provinces," the king said.

Just then... "Oh! That is the monk Tiritavachchha! I must go and receive him," the king exclaimed, rushing out.

"But, father, you don't need to go. Given alms like the other monks," his son suggested.

But the king rushed out to welcome the monk. "Come, you must sit here. On the throne? How odd!" the prince thought.

"Your majesty, the lunch has been served for royal guests. Shall I take your guest there?" the minister asked.

"No. He will eat with me here in my private chamber," the king insisted.

After lunch, the chief minister was summoned. "Send for the architect. I want him to build a suitable dwelling for Tiritavachchha in the compound. It should be spacious and comfortable."

The special treatment given to Tiritavachchha did not go unnoticed. "What a fuss over an ordinary monk. Our king treats him as if he were a brave warrior who has made a great conquest," the courtiers whispered.

Soon it reached the prince's ears. "Father, why do you give this man so much importance? He is not an expert in any art. He is neither your relative nor your friend. How come he deserves such royal treatment?"

"Do you remember, son, two months ago, I went with the army to quell a rebellion in the districts?" the king asked.

"While leading our soldiers, my elephant ran in a frenzy and went into the forest. I was wounded and tired. Luckily we reached an ashrama. A well at last. Ah! I must have some water!"

"But there was no pulley to draw the water nor anyone around to help. I'll tie the rope around the elephant's leg and climb down the well."

"I climbed down the well and drank my fill of cool water. But then... it's too slippery. I can't go back," the king explained.

"In the evening, the monk Tiritavachchha returned to his ashrama. 'An elephant here! Surely there must be a rider too,' he thought."

"The monk looked around... and found me. He took me out of the well. 'You look exhausted. Eat some fruit and rest,' he told me."

"For two days the monk looked after me, treating my wounds with herbs and bringing food for me from the forest. 'You have saved my life! You must come and visit me in Varanasi,' I told him."

The king looked at his son. "Had he not rescued me and looked after me, I would not have survived."

"Yes, father. Now I understand that you owe your life to him. Indeed, father. We must do all we can for him," the prince realized. "Don't you think he deserves to be my friend?"

The prince smiled, finally understanding the true value of friendship and gratitude.

docs.title: Jayadeva
docs.text:
Jayadeva was born in Kendubilva, a small village on the eastern coast of India, nearly eight hundred years ago. His father was a poor Brahman named Bhojadeva, and his mother was a pious woman named Bhama Devi.

Humming the name of Lord Hari, Bhama Devi would churn curds. "Hari bol... Hari bol..."

"What's that you said?" she asked her young son.

"Hari bol..."

"Very good, my son. Always sing of Hari," she smiled.

When Jayadeva was six years old, he began to study at the village school. As the years passed by, Bhojadeva found it increasingly difficult to make ends meet and had to borrow money from a neighbor, Niranjan Babu.

"Don't worry. Jayadeva will soon be able to help you," his wife comforted him. But Jayadeva was lost in the world of the Bhagawat Purana, especially the exploits of Krishna.

"Father, permit me to go and spend some time in the forest. I want to be away from books and from people. I want to be alone to meditate," Jayadeva asked one day. With his parents' blessings, he went to the forest, where he sang songs in praise of Radha and Krishna in a state of ecstasy.

A few weeks later, Jayadeva returned to his village. Niranjan Babu met him. "Jayadeva, where have you been? In your absence both your parents have died. Your father had taken a loan from me... which he failed to repay. So I have occupied his house instead."

"Bhaja Radhe Govindam!" Jayadeva chanted peacefully.

Suddenly, smoke billowed from the house. "Smoke! A house in the village is on fire!" Niranjan Babu cried. It was Jayadeva's house.

Shocked, Niranjan Babu stood rooted to the ground, but Jayadeva ran towards the burning house. "Jayadeva! Come back!"

"Look! The fire has gone out! Nothing has been burnt! How strange!" Niranjan Babu marvelled. "I see the hand of God in this. Forgive me, Jayadeva, and accept me as your disciple!"

Jayadeva did not stay for long. He left the village and set out for Puri to worship Lord Jagannatha. Walking for many miles in the scorching summer heat, overcome by thirst, he fell down unconscious.

A cowherd, carrying water and curds, gently placed Jayadeva's head on his lap. "Here, have some curds. You will feel better."

"You are a good, kind child. What are you doing here in the forest?" Jayadeva asked as he revived.

"I am looking after my cattle. I am a cowherd," the boy replied. Then he pointed to the temple of Puri. "There's the temple! Lord Jagannatha!"

Such was his eagerness that Jayadeva broke into a run. Later, he realized, "So it was you, Lord Krishna! It was you who revived me with cool curds. And I didn't recognize you!"

Later, when he returned to the temple, a Brahman named Sudeva Sharma spoke to him. "I want you to marry my daughter, Padmavati."

"But that is not possible! My life is dedicated to the Lord!" Jayadeva protested.

"Lord Jagannatha appeared to me in a dream and asked me to arrange this marriage," the Brahman explained.

"I will leave my daughter with you. You must decide whether or not you will carry out the Lord's will."

Accepting it as divine will, Jayadeva married Padmavati. They returned to Kendubilva, where he built a hut, installed an image of Krishna, and began to write his famous Sanskrit poem, the Gita-Govinda, about Radha's immortal love for Krishna.

One day, he wrote: "O Radha, place your tender feet upon my head as a diadem divine..." He hesitated, thinking it disrespectful to portray God humbled by a woman. "No! Would God let himself be humbled in this way by a woman?" He crossed out the lines and went to the river for a bath.

While he was away, Lord Krishna took Jayadeva's form and came to the hut. He asked Padmavati for food, ate his meal, and then completed the verse in the manuscript: "Place those tender petals, your delicate feet, upon my head as a diadem divine." Then he vanished.

When the real Jayadeva returned and saw the completed verse, he was bewildered. Padmavati explained that he had just eaten and written it. "Now I see the light, Padma. The Lord loves Radha so much that he is prepared to become her slave," Jayadeva smiled, realizing the Lord had visited them.

When Jayadeva completed the Gita-Govinda, it won immediate recognition. However, the King of Utkal had also composed songs about Lord Krishna, which he called "Gita-Govinda". The King wanted his version to be more popular, but the people preferred Jayadeva's.

To settle the matter, the King placed both versions before the image at the Jagannatha temple. The door was shut. After some time, it was opened. Jayadeva's work was on top of the King's!

"I accept your verdict, Lord Jagannatha!" the King bowed. The Lord then instructed the King in a dream to include verses from his work in each chapter of Jayadeva's.

After some time, Jayadeva left for a pilgrimage. The King of Jeypore, a generous man, hosted him and gave him a large offering of gold coins. As Jayadeva passed through a forest, three men joined him.

The three men were dacoits. When night fell, they cut off Jayadeva's hands, threw him into a dry well, and ran away with his gold coins.

Lakshmana Sena, the King of Gaud, happened to be passing by and heard singing from the well. His men rescued Jayadeva. Impressed by his saintliness despite his suffering, the King appointed Jayadeva as his spiritual guide.

Following Jayadeva's advice, the King built a rest-house for travellers. One day, the three dacoits came there, disguised as sadhus. Jayadeva recognized them but welcomed them warmly.

"Accept these gold coins. My guards will see you safely to our borders," Jayadeva offered.

On the way, the guards asked the 'sadhus' why the saint was so kind to them. The dacoits lied, "He was to be hanged. But we came to his aid. So only his hands were cut off!"

Suddenly, there was a loud rumbling noise and the dacoits fell into a pit that opened beneath their feet, swallowed by the earth for their terrible lie.

The guards reported this to the King. Jayadeva then disclosed the truth about the dacoits. "Though they treated you so badly, you were willing to forgive them?" the King marvelled. "You are indeed a saint!"

The next moment, as Jayadeva prayed, his hands were miraculously restored.

Some time later, Padmavati joined her husband at the palace. The Queen befriended her, but one day, to test Padmavati's devotion, the Queen falsely told her that Jayadeva was dead.

"My lord!" Padmavati gasped, and fell dead from the shock, for her love was so powerful she died the moment she believed her husband had passed away.

When Jayadeva arrived and saw the tragedy, he began to recite from his Gita-Govinda. A few moments later, Padmavati sat up, alive again, and joined her husband in singing the praises of Krishna.

Jayadeva and Padmavati lived long. When they were quite old, they went to Brindavan, where they spent the last days of their lives with other devotees, singing of the glory of Lord Krishna.

docs.title: Jayadratha
docs.text:
In the capital of Sindhu, Queen Duhshala was anxiously waiting for the return of her husband, Jayadratha, from Hastinapura.

"I hope all is well, my lord," she greeted him.

"All is well, Duhshala. In fact things couldn't be better... The Kauravas, your brothers, have found a supremely clever way of humbling your cousins, the proud Pandavas! They invited the Pandavas to play a game of dice... The losers would surrender all rights to their kingdom and go into exile for thirteen years," Jayadratha explained.

"The Pandavas fell for that? Didn't they know my uncle, Shakuni, would help my brothers to win?"

"Naturally, Shakuni won the game for Duryodhana with his tricks. Ha! Ha! Ha!" Jayadratha laughed.

Meanwhile, the Pandavas—Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva—with their wife, Draupadi, had reached the Kamyaka forest.

One day, while the Pandavas had gone away as usual in search of game, leaving Draupadi at home, Jayadratha happened to pass by. He saw Draupadi and was captivated.

"I am Draupadi, the wife of the valiant Pandavas. My husbands have gone out hunting. They will be pleased to have your King as their guest," she offered graciously.

But Jayadratha had evil intentions. "Why should you suffer with the Pandavas? Forsake them. Be my Queen and live in comfort," he proposed.

"To lay hands on me is to invite the wrath of Arjuna and Bhima upon yourself. It is like kicking a sleeping lion," Draupadi warned fiercely.

Disregarding her anger, Jayadratha carried her to his chariot and drove away.

When the Pandavas returned and discovered Draupadi missing, they found chariot tracks and immediately set out in pursuit. In the battle that followed, the Pandavas scattered Jayadratha's army.

"Jayadratha fled for his life, leaving Draupadi behind."

Bhima and Arjuna pursued the scoundrel. Before leaving, Yudhishthira had told them, "For the sake of our cousin, Duhshala, do not slay Jayadratha, though he is so wicked!"

Bhima caught Jayadratha, pulled him by the hair, and kicked him hard. "You coward! You dare to carry away a lady by force yet you run away from a man!"

"Bhima, don't kill him. Remember what Yudhishthira told us," Arjuna cautioned.

Instead of killing him, Bhima shaved Jayadratha's head, leaving five tufts of hair, to humiliate him. "If you wish to live you must publicly declare that you are the slave of the Pandavas," Bhima ordered.

Jayadratha was forced to agree and was brought before Yudhishthira. "Let him go! He is our slave and you have shaved his head. That is enough for me," Yudhishthira pardoned him.

Jayadratha left, burning with shame and anger. "I will have my revenge..."

He went to the banks of the Ganga and performed severe penances, imploring the protection of Lord Shiva. When Shiva finally appeared, Jayadratha asked, "Lord, I want to be able to defeat all five of the Pandavas in battle!"

"That is not possible. No one can defeat the Pandavas. However, you will be able to check the advance of the Pandavas once. But even so you won't be able to stop Arjuna," Lord Shiva granted him.

Years later, the great war of Kurukshetra broke out between the Kauravas and the Pandavas. On the thirteenth day of the battle, Drona was the Kaurava commander. He formed the impenetrable Chakravyuha formation.

The Kauravas lured Arjuna away from the main battlefield using the Samshaptakas, a tribe of warriors sworn to fight to the death. While Arjuna was busy fighting them, the young Abhimanyu, Arjuna's son, penetrated the Chakravyuha.

However, Jayadratha used his boon to hold back the other four Pandavas, preventing them from following and assisting Abhimanyu. Trapped inside, Abhimanyu fought valiantly but died a hero's death against overwhelming odds.

When Arjuna returned and heard of his son's death, his grief turned to fury. "Arjuna, we could not follow Abhimanyu into the Chakravyuha. Jayadratha held us back," Yudhishthira explained.

Arjuna took a terrible vow. "Tomorrow, before sunset, I will kill Jayadratha on the battle-field—and if I fail I will enter fire."

The next day, Drona arranged a massive formation to protect Jayadratha, placing him twelve miles behind the front lines.

Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, launched a fierce attack, releasing volleys of arrows and breaking through the Kaurava ranks. He defeated many warriors, including Shrutayudha, whose mace recoiled and killed him because he threw it at the unarmed Krishna.

As they fought their way deep into the enemy lines, Arjuna's horses grew tired. Arjuna created a pool of water with an arrow and built a protective wall of arrows around them. Krishna unharnessed and refreshed the horses.

As evening approached, the Kauravas grew jubilant, thinking Arjuna would fail his vow. The sun was getting low.

"Look! Krishna and Arjuna are here again!" but the Kauravas kept a careful watch on Jayadratha.

Suddenly, it became dark. "Krishna! The sun has set!" Arjuna cried.

"No, Arjuna. This is an illusion. You have nothing to fear," Krishna assured him.

Believing the sun had set and he was safe, Jayadratha came out to look. "Darkness at last! I am safe!" he cheered.

"At that moment the darkness was dispelled and the sun was visible again. 'The sun has not set! It was only an illusion wrought by Krishna!'" the Kauravas realized in horror.

"Then Arjuna struck." With a powerful arrow, he severed Jayadratha's head, fulfilling his vow just before the sun truly set.

Victorious, Krishna and Arjuna blew their conches to announce their victory, avenging the death of brave Abhimanyu.

docs.title: Jnaneshwar
docs.text:
One morning, towards the end of the thirteenth century, SiddhOPant Kulkarni, the chief officer of Alandi, saw a young boy deep in meditation on the bank of the river Indrayani.

"I am Vitthal, the son of Govind Pant and Nirabai of Apegaon. I left home on a pilgrimage seven years ago," the boy explained when he opened his eyes.

SiddhOPant took young Vitthal home. That night, Lord Panduranga appeared to SiddhOPant in a dream. "Give Rukmini to Vitthal. Their children will be great spiritual teachers."

The marriage of Vitthal and Rukmini was celebrated with great pomp. For some years, Vitthal led a happy life. But then tragedy struck; both his parents died in quick succession. SiddhOPant took them to Alandi to help Vitthal overcome his grief.

However, Vitthal felt restless. "I have no peace of mind. Permit me to take up Sanyasa," he pleaded with Rukmini repeatedly. But she would not agree.

One day, Vitthal went out to wash his hands... and did not return. He had gone to Kashi. There, he met Swami Ramananda. "I have neither parents nor wife. Please initiate me into the path of Sanyasa," he lied. Ramananda initiated him, calling him Chaitanya Swami.

Months went by. One day, Swami Ramananda visited Alandi. Rukmini went to make obeisance to him. "Arise, daughter. May you be blessed with many children who will guide mankind," he blessed her.

"Why child! What's the matter?" he asked when she wept.

"My husband has deserted me. Vitthal of Alandi? Your husband? But..." Ramananda remembered his first meeting with Vitthal Pant and realized he had been deceived.

When Ramananda returned to his hermitage at Kashi, he confronted Chaitanya Swami. "My words cannot prove false. Rest assured, daughter. Your husband will come back to you," he had promised Rukmini. He ordered Vitthal, "Go back to Alandi and lead the life of a householder."

Vitthal obeyed, but the orthodox Brahmans of Alandi took a different view. "Once having renounced the world, a man cannot return to his family. Vitthal and his wife must be excommunicated."

Vitthal and Rukmini had to leave the village. They lived in a small hut near the river bank. Four children were born to them: Nivritti, Jnanadev, Sopan, and Mukta.

From a very young age, little Jnanadev took a keen interest in the Bhagavad Gita. But Vitthal was worried. "My children are so bright and religious. Yet I cannot perform their thread ceremonies so long as we remain excommunicated."

He approached the Brahmans. "Please take us back into the fold."

"We don't have the authority to do that. Please go to Paithan and obtain the sanction of the learned scholars there," they replied.

The family went to Tryambak. There, a tiger attacked them. Nivritti was separated and lost his way. He found a cave and entered it, meeting a Sadhu, Gahininath. The holy one initiated him into the Natha cult. Nivritti then found his way back to his family and, in a secluded spot, initiated his brother Jnanadev.

Soon after, they all went to Paithan, where Vitthal pleaded with the Brahman scholars. "If he is Jnanadev (God of Knowledge), where is the need for him to learn?" a scholar mocked.

Just then, a village water-man prodded his buffalo past them. "Faster Jnana, faster."

"What is the difference between your Jnanadev and that buffalo, Jnana?" a Brahman asked.

"There is no difference at all. As the assembly of Brahmans burst into laughter, Jnanadev's soft voice was heard. All are equal."

"Wait! You say there is no difference between us and that buffalo. Can that buffalo recite the Vedas?"

Jnanadev walked up to the buffalo, placed his hand on its back, and asked it to recite. To the utter amazement of the gathering, the buffalo recited the Vedas in a deep, resonant voice.

The Brahmans were overwhelmed with awe. "We are grateful to you. But we propose to live as ascetics without the sacred thread. Then allow us to give you a letter of commendation at least."

As the party proceeded to Pandharpur, the buffalo followed them. On their way, they stopped at Nevase. A funeral procession passed by. A woman fell at Jnanadev's feet. "May you enjoy long life with your husband and children," he blessed her.

"Sir, my husband is dead. I am on my way to become a Sati," she cried.

"He who made me utter those blessings would surely breathe life into your husband," Jnanadev said. He told the pall-bearers to place the body on the ground. "Rise, O Satchidananda!"

The dead man came back to life! "As the Gita tells you, the soul is immortal... Jnanoba, explain the Gita in Marathi. Let the multitude benefit," Nivritti commanded.

Jnanadev began to explain the Bhagavad-Gita in chaste and simple Marathi, later known as the 'Jnaneshwari'.

The only one who was blind to his greatness was a Brahman named Visoba. When Mukta went to buy an earthenware pan to make sweet pancakes, Visoba broke every pan in the shop to prevent her.

Mukta ran home weeping. "Why do you cry? Fry the pancakes on my back," Jnanadev offered. The pancakes began sizzling. "NO! IMPOSSIBLE! IT IS COOKED! HE IS A GREAT SOUL!" Visoba, who was secretly watching, realized. He became Jnanadev's disciple.

One day, Yogi Changdev, riding on a tiger, came to meet them. Jnanadev, his brothers and sister were sitting on a wall. "Look! It's Yogi Changdev on a tiger! He's coming to meet us! Let's welcome him. Wait! You don't have to get down. Move wall, move forward! Towards the sage!" Jnanadev commanded.

The lifeless wall moved! Changdev was wonderstruck, jumped off the tiger, and sought Jnanadev's teachings.

A few months later, the siblings travelled to Kashi. Mudgalacharya was performing a sacrifice and an elephant was to choose the most worthy man to be honoured. The elephant chose Jnanadev.

The Brahmans grumbled. Namdev, a great saint accompanying them, intervened. "Let's go to the temple then. The one from whose hand Lord Vishweshwara will eat the purodasha is the worthiest."

At the temple, Jnanadev prayed and offered the purodasha. The next moment, it disappeared! The Lord had accepted it. Jnanadev was taken in procession to the sacrificial site and offered the seat of honour.

On his return to Alandi, at the tender age of twenty-one, Jnanadev attained the state of blissful consciousness and withdrew from the world, leaving behind a legacy as the founder of the Bhakti movement in Maharashtra.

docs.title: Kacha and Devayani
docs.text:
In the days of yore, the Devas and the Asuras were ever struggling for the lordship of the three worlds. Both had wise men to guide them. Brihaspati, the son of Sage Angiras, lived in the abode of Indra, King of the Devas. And Shukracharya, the favourite pupil of Sage Angiras, lived in the capital city of the Asura King, Vrishaparva.

But of the two, Shukracharya alone knew the craft of Sanjivani, the secret of reviving the dead. As long as Shukracharya was with them, the Asuras knew their numbers would never decrease. Those who fell in battle were simply brought back to life to continue the war on the Devas.

The valiant Devas, tired of the unequal struggle, went to the brilliant, handsome youth, Kacha, the son of Brihaspati. "You must go to Shukracharya and learn the craft of Sanjivani. Only then can we vanquish our sworn enemies," they told him.

The obedient and dutiful Kacha immediately set out to meet Shukracharya. "I am Kacha, the son of Brihaspati. I wish to become your disciple. I shall obey you and serve you loyally," he humbly presented himself.

"I accept you as my pupil, O worthy Kacha," Shukracharya gave him a warm welcome. He also introduced him to his daughter, Devayani, whom he loved dearly. "Come Devayani, my child. Meet the worthy Kacha."

All learning in those days was handed down by word of mouth. The pupil lived with his guru's family as one of his household. Kacha helped Devayani with her daily chores and tenderly fulfilled her slightest wish. Within a few days of his arrival, Kacha won his way into Devayani's heart.

As the years passed, the Asuras became suspicious of Kacha. "It is strange! Why should the son of the learned Brihaspati come to our city and choose Shukracharya for his guru? I sense treachery. The only knowledge Shukracharya has, that Kacha's father has not, is the secret craft of Sanjivani," they deduced.

The Asuras plotted to kill Kacha secretly, knowing their guru would not let his pupil be slain. One day, while Kacha was in the forest tending the cattle, the Asuras fell upon him. They slew him, cut him into pieces, and fed his flesh to their dogs.

That evening, the cattle returned home without Kacha. "It is late! The cattle have not returned and neither has he. If he is dead, I do not want to live any more," Devayani wept.

"Do not weep, my child. Will not your father use his secret for his own daughter's happiness?" Shukracharya comforted her. He closed his eyes, used his Sanjivani knowledge, and lo! Kacha stood before Devayani.

"The Asuras killed me! I do not know how I came back to life but here I am," Kacha explained.

Kacha continued living in the home of his guru. One day, Devayani asked him to gather some rare flowers from deep in the forest. The Asuras were waiting for just such an opportunity. They waylaid him and slew him again. "This time they carried his body to a lonely spot... We shall pound his carcass into paste and dissolve it in the waters of the ocean."

When Kacha didn't return, Devayani pleaded with her father again. Once again, Shukracharya used his secret knowledge to recall Kacha from the dead.

The Asuras were at their wit's end. "How can we kill Kacha if Shukracharya keeps bringing him back to life?" At last, they had an idea. They killed Kacha for the third time, cremated his body, collected the ashes, and mixed them into a goblet of wine.

The greying Asura then took the goblet of wine to Shukracharya as a gift. Shukracharya, who had a weakness for wine, emptied the goblet in one gulp.

When Devayani waited in vain for Kacha once more, she begged her father to revive him. As Shukracharya thought of a way out, he realized the truth. "The Asuras killed me, but I do not know how I happen to be inside your stomach," Kacha's voice echoed from within Shukracharya!

Shukracharya was in a dilemma. "Kacha can come out only by killing me," he told Devayani.

"But father, I will not live if either of you dies," she cried.

Shukracharya realized the real purpose of Kacha's visit. "I now see why you came and truly you have succeeded. There is only one way by which both of us can survive to ensure Devayani's happiness. I will have to teach you the craft of Sanjivani," he decided.

And so Kacha learned the craft from inside Shukracharya's stomach. Then, he emerged from Shukracharya's mangled body. He immediately used the Sanjivani craft to bring his guru back to life. Shukracharya was pleased with his pupil's wisdom but cursed himself for drinking wine. "Henceforth wine shall be forbidden for those engaged in the pursuit of wisdom."

With his studies complete, Kacha prepared to leave. Devayani confessed her love. "Now that you are free to marry, will you not take me for your wife?"

Kacha held her hand tenderly but refused. "Peerless one! I was reborn in your father's stomach. I am therefore your brother. I can't marry you. I must return."

The broken-hearted Devayani cried out in grief and anger. "You made use of one who was sinless in her devotion to you. I therefore lay a curse on you. You will never be able to use the craft of Sanjivani."

"Dearest Devayani, it is wrong of you to curse me. For this, no rishi's son will ever marry you. But I may still teach the craft of Sanjivani to others," Kacha replied.

Then Kacha departed for the abode of Indra, King of the Devas, leaving Devayani behind. With the passage of time, Devayani completely forgot about the existence of Kacha and lived happily with her father and playmates.

docs.title: Kalidasa
docs.text:
Kalidasa, one of the greatest poets the world has ever known, lost his parents when he was barely five years old. He was brought up by a kind-hearted but unlettered cowherd. Kalidasa grew into a handsome youth, but he was an uneducated rustic. He spent his free time at the village temple dedicated to Kali, earning him the name 'Kalidasa'.

Now it so happened that the King of Varanasi had a beautiful and talented daughter, Princess Vasanti. She was very proud of her knowledge. The King was worried about finding a husband equal to her in accomplishment. He suggested Vararuchi, a great scholar.

But Vasanti challenged Vararuchi to a mute debate. "I will ask questions only through gestures and you must answer them," she decreed.

On the appointed day, Vasanti raised one of her fingers. "What, in the name of God, does that mean!" Vararuchi wondered. Thinking she meant to poke out one of his eyes, he raised two fingers in response, meaning he would poke out both of hers!

Vasanti, however, interpreted his gesture philosophically. "He understands me! His answer is correct. What he says is that, besides God, the individual self is also a true and eternal being. That makes it two, and not one."

As her second question, Vasanti held up her five fingers. Vararuchi, thinking she wanted to slap him, showed a fist, meaning he would box her. Vasanti again misinterpreted it. "Ah, the perfect answer! Though the physical elements are five, they become one in the human body."

Princess Vasanti acknowledged defeat and was married to Vararuchi. But after the ceremony, Vararuchi fumbled over a simple Sanskrit word for 'camel'. Vasanti was shocked. "Aren't you a scholar?"

"No. I am only a cowherd," he confessed.

"Get out! Get out of my sight!" Vasanti screamed, throwing him out.

Vararuchi felt cheated and humiliated. Seeking revenge, he wandered into the forest where he saw a strange sight—a man sitting on a branch and cutting the very branch he was seated on! It was the brash Kalidasa. As expected, Kalidasa fell from the tree.

"I could use this boor to pay her back with a trick of my own!" Vararuchi plotted. He and his friends dressed Kalidasa as a pandit and took him to the palace, instructing him not to speak.

They presented him to Vasanti for a mute debate. When Vasanti raised one finger, Kalidasa thought she wanted to poke out his eye, so he raised two fingers. The same misunderstanding occurred, and Vasanti accepted defeat.

But when Vasanti discovered she had married an idiot, she was furious. "Go away! Only a great scholar can be a fit husband for me—and you are only a country bumpkin!" she raged, driving him out.

Kalidasa was deeply humiliated by Vasanti's words. "How can I become a scholar? Ah, there's the temple of Mother Kali. I'll ask her to grant me knowledge," he resolved.

Inside the temple, he meditated and stayed without food or drink for many days. Finally, in desperation, he drew a sword to sacrifice himself at Kali's feet.

"Don't do it, son! I am pleased with you. I grant your wish. You shall be endowed with genius," Mother Kali appeared and blessed him.

Kalidasa returned home glowing with happiness and spoke to Vasanti in chaste Sanskrit. "Asti kashchit vagvisheshah? (Is there any special speech?)" she asked, surprised.

"Yes. By the grace of Mother Kali, I have learnt Sanskrit," he proudly replied. He then told her he would begin three great works with each of the words she had just uttered ("Asti", "Kashchit", "Vag"), honoring her as his guru who set him on the path of knowledge.

Vasanti was enraged that he considered her his guru and not his wife. "May you meet your end at the hands of a woman!" she cursed him.

After this, Kalidasa led the life of a wanderer. He wrote beautiful poetry in Sanskrit, including the famous Meghaduta. From time to time, he stopped at various courts. His favourite was the court of King Bhoja at Dhara.

One day, King Bhoja challenged his scholars: "Can anyone compose a verse ending with 'Taatam, tatam, tam, tatatam, tatam, tah'?" All the scholars were perplexed by the seemingly meaningless syllables.

But Kalidasa, a ready wit, composed a poem describing a golden bowl of sandal-paste falling down the stairs, making that exact sound. King Bhoja awarded him the prize and embraced him as a friend.

Kalidasa lived at the court and wrote magnificent plays, including Malavika-Agnimitra and the incredibly successful Shakuntala. He also wrote epic poems like Kumarasambhava and Raghuvamsha.

However, King Bhoja and Kalidasa eventually had a misunderstanding. Bhoja wanted a poem in his praise, but Kalidasa refused. "You are a great and noble king, but the praise of mortals is not the aim of poetry." Kalidasa left Dhara.

King Bhoja missed his friend. He devised a plan to find him. He announced a reward of a lakh of gold coins for anyone who composed a 'new' poem, but instructed his scholars to falsely claim they had heard every submitted poem before, thereby rejecting them all.

Kalidasa, hearing of this trick, helped a poor villager by composing a poem for him to present to the King. The poem stated: "Ninety-one crores of gold coins did your father once borrow from me. Isn't it time now, O King Bhoja, to return the money?"

The King's scholars were trapped. If they claimed they had heard it before, the King would have to pay the massive debt! The King gladly accepted it as a new poem, paid the reward, and recognized Kalidasa's wit. He went to Kalidasa's village, apologized, and they were reunited.

Some time later, after another quarrel, Kalidasa set sail for Lanka. Avoiding the court of King Kumaradasa so Bhoja wouldn't find him, Kalidasa lived in the house of a dancer.

King Kumaradasa offered a reward to anyone who could complete a specific verse. The dancer brought the verse to Kalidasa, and he easily completed it.

But the dancer was greedy. "The King will surely give an award for this verse. Why should I let Kalidasa have the money?" she thought. She stabbed Kalidasa in the back, killing him, fulfilling Vasanti's curse.

The dancer took the poem to King Kumaradasa, claiming she wrote it. But the King recognized the genius. "It is rather like Kalidasa's work. Did you really write those lines?" he demanded. He interrogated her, threatening her with the dungeon, and she confessed.

Heartbroken, Kumaradasa arranged a grand funeral for the poet. As he witnessed the flames enveloping Kalidasa's body, the King was so overcome by grief that he leaped into the funeral pyre and was consumed in the blaze alongside his great friend.

docs.title: Kannagi
docs.text:
Handsome Kovalan and quiet, charming Kannagi, of a merchant clan in the Chola kingdom, were married with pomp and ceremony. They lived happily in the city of Poompuhar.

"I will make that house a heaven for you," Kovalan promised.

Three years later, during the spring festival, Kovalan saw Madhavi, a beautiful dancer, perform. "What an excellent dancer! Perfect! Her technique is perfect!"

He was captivated. "She is just another dancer. I was foolish to have lost my heart to her... to have squandered all my money on her," Kovalan later realized, but only after he had lost all his wealth. He had even given Madhavi an emerald necklace that was a state tribute.

While Kovalan stayed with Madhavi, poor Kannagi was always in tears. "He does not deserve such a good wife. The cad has taken away all her jewels and money," her friends sympathised.

"Send him away even if he does. You are too good for him," they advised.

"No. Don't say that. My husband is my god," Kannagi replied faithfully.

One day, utterly disillusioned after misunderstanding a song she sang, Kovalan walked away from Madhavi forever. He returned to Kannagi.

"I will never neglect you again," he promised. "But I'll need some capital to start my business again."

"My lord, I still have my anklets—the last of my jewels. We can sell one of them," Kannagi offered.

"It will fetch a better price in the markets of Madurai. Let us go there," Kovalan decided.

They set out on foot for Madurai, the capital of the Pandya kingdom. It was a long and arduous journey. When they arrived, Kovalan left Kannagi in the care of a kind couple and went to the market to sell the anklet.

When he reached the street of the goldsmiths, he asked the Queen's goldsmith, "Sir, do you know anyone who would like to buy this anklet?"

"It's beautiful," the goldsmith said. But he thought, "It's exactly like the Queen's anklet! The one my wife has been yearning for... I stole the Queen's anklet and gave it to my wife. If I can fool the King, I'll be safe."

The goldsmith went to the King. "I suspect him of stealing the Queen's anklet. I am going to the King," he told Kovalan to wait.

"I brought back the anklet because it was not safe in my house. A thief tried to steal it but I caught him and retrieved it," the goldsmith lied to the King.

The King, distracted by the Queen's pouting over the lost anklet, blundered. "Good work. Let the thief be taken away and beheaded," he ordered his guards, without even a trial.

The guards found Kovalan. Despite his noble appearance, they carried out the King's orders. "He was beheaded. The King's orders. He stole the Queen's anklet."

When Kannagi heard the news, she fainted. "My husband, a thief? Is the King mad?" she cried when she revived.

At the market place, Kannagi sat down near the body. "Look! The severed head has joined the body!" the people gasped.

Kovalan spoke to her. "Kannagi, why do you look so sad? You'll join me soon." Then he died in her arms.

"Your King is a murderer. This anklet will prove Kovalan's innocence," she declared, charging towards the palace. The crowds followed her.

"Go and tell your King that a woman seeks justice. Her husband has been killed unjustly," she demanded at the gates.

She stood before the King and Queen. "My innocent husband tried to sell an anklet. Someone called him a thief and you, O unjust King, beheaded him without even a trial!"

"So you are the thief's wife. He stole this, my Queen's gold anklet which has pearls inside," the King said.

"My gold anklet has diamonds inside. Here is the second of the pair!" Kannagi challenged. She took the anklet from the King and broke it. The diamonds lay scattered on the floor.

"What have I done! I have killed an innocent man. I can atone only by my own death," the King realized his grave injustice. Overcome by his sense of guilt, the King fell dead. The Queen soon followed him.

But Kannagi's anger was not appeased. She reached the palace gates and cursed the city: "If I am the chaste wife of an innocent man, let this cursed city burn to ashes in the flames of my wrath. Let the old, the disabled and the children be spared."

She tore off her left breast and flung it at the city. Instantly, Madurai was engulfed in flames.

Later, the Goddess of Madurai came down to her. "Listen to me, O Kannagi. The King was not at fault. It is your husband's sin in a previous birth that has brought about this tragedy. Kovalan was one of the Pandya King's soldiers. He brought about the death of an innocent man... whose wife cursed him: 'If I am the pure wife of an innocent man, you and your wife shall know the pain that is now ours.'"

Kannagi understood. "Bless us, O Goddess. Release us from the throes of this curse."

"You will go to heaven with your husband. He will come down and take you. Madurai will be rebuilt," the Goddess promised.

As promised, Kovalan came down in a winged chariot. "Kannagi, we are now free of all earthly pains and pleasures," he said, taking her to heaven.

Years later, temples were built to honour the divinity of Kannagi. Even to this day, people worship her as the epitome of chastity and devotion.

docs.title: Kannappa
docs.text:
It was the custom of the hunters of Uduppur, in ancient Tamil Nadu, to begin the day with prayers to Lord Murugan. Their chief, Nagan, invoked the blessings of the Lord for his tribe.

Nagan and his wife Tattai were childless. "Tattai, have faith in Lord Murugan. You too will be a mother one day," Nagan consoled her. They arranged a festival to propitiate Lord Murugan, praying for a son.

Their prayers were answered. A son was born to them. "He is strong-limbed. We'll name him Tinnan," Nagan declared. The boy later came to be known as Kannappa.

Tinnan grew under the loving care of his parents into a youth of extraordinary strength. He practised hard and became an expert archer, learning how to wield the spear and the sword.

When Tinnan was about sixteen years old, some villagers approached his father. "Nagan, wild animals are destroying our crops and cattle. Please come to our help."

"I am getting old. I will send my son, Tinnan," Nagan replied.

Tinnan and his companions accompanied the fear-stricken villagers. In the forest, Tinnan valiantly fought and killed a tiger that attacked him. Later, they caught a boar in a snare. The boar broke loose, but Tinnan pursued and killed it at the foot of the Kalahasti hill.

"I am thirsty, Nanan. I'll go with you to fetch some water," Tinnan said to his companion while another, Kadan, lit a fire to roast the boar.

As the two climbed the hill, Tinnan felt a strange sensation. "Nanan, something is happening to me. Why does my heart feel so light and joyful?"

Suddenly he broke into a run towards a temple. When Tinnan entered the temple, he was overwhelmed by devotion upon seeing the Shiva Linga. "Lord!" he cried, shedding tears of joy. "Lord, I have found you. Lord, how lonely you must be! From now on, I'll remain with you."

Nanan told him that a priest used to come and worship there. Tinnan decided to bring food for the Lord. "You two may eat. I have to take food for my lord," he told his companions.

Tinnan carefully chose the tastiest piece of roasted meat. "Hmm. This is delicious. My lord will like it."

He also wanted to take flowers and water. "How can I carry both these things at the same time? The flowers will get squashed! Of course! It is simple!" he thought.

With the meat in his hands, he filled his mouth with water from the stream, and gathered flowers in his hair.

When he reached the shrine, he gave the deity a ritual bath with the water from his mouth, decorated the Linga with the flowers from his head, and placed the meat before the Lord. "Lord, I have brought you the tastiest piece of meat. Please accept it."

That night, Tinnan stood guard outside the temple to protect the Lord from wild animals, while his companions returned to the village to inform Nagan of the change in Tinnan. Nagan came to fetch him, but Tinnan refused to leave. "Father, I cannot leave. This place is my home now."

The next morning, while Tinnan was out hunting, the temple priest arrived. He was shocked to see the offerings of meat. "Lord of all Lords, who has defiled your shrine? He must be punished!"

The priest cleaned the shrine, bathed in the lake, brought water in a clean vessel, gathered flowers, and completed his formal devotions.

But as soon as he left, Tinnan returned with his offering of roasted meat, water in his mouth, and flowers in his hair. He removed the priest's flowers and performed his own worship.

This happened every day. Tinnan and the priest alternately offered worship. The priest grumbled about the defilement, while Tinnan worshipped with pure, innocent love.

Meanwhile, at Kailas, Lord Shiva told Parvati to "wait and see" who was the true devotee—the holy Brahman or the uncouth hunter.

On the morning of the sixth day, when the priest came to the shrine, he saw that one of the Linga's eyes was bleeding! "Wh... what! The eye is bleeding! This is something unheard of! Some disaster is likely to happen! I'd better go," the frightened priest fled.

A few minutes later, Tinnan arrived. "My Lord, your eye is bleeding! Who has hurt you? You must be in pain!"

He ran to get herbs and applied a paste to the affected eye. "There, this will make you well again."

But the bleeding didn't stop. "What shall I do to relieve your suffering, Lord?" Tinnan wondered. Then he had an idea. "I know what to do. I will give you my eye."

With an arrow, Tinnan gouged out one of his own eyes and replaced the Lord's bleeding eye with it. "Lord, you are cured! I am so happy."

But Tinnan's joy was short-lived. "Oh! The left eye has begun to bleed!"

"I know what to do now. I will give you my other eye, Lord," Tinnan decided without hesitation.

He raised the arrow to his left eye, but paused. "But, Lord, when I take out my other eye, how will I know where to place it?"

He found a way out. He placed his foot on the left eye of the Lord to mark the place, and got ready to take out his other eye.

At that moment, Lord Shiva appeared and stopped him. "Tinnan, stop! Many have prayed to me for sight. But only you have offered me your eyes."

The next moment, Tinnan's sight was fully restored. He stood with folded hands before his beloved master.

"Peerless Kannappa, come with me to Kailas, where you shall ever be by my side," Lord Shiva blessed him.

"Lord, I am truly blessed," Kannappa, the Eye-Saint, replied with devotion.

docs.title: Kanwal and Kehar
docs.text:
Mahmud Shah, the Sultan of Gujarat, was being entertained by a group of dancers from Marwar. Among them, a dancer in a red dress caught his eye. "She is so agile and graceful. I must speak to her after the dance is over," he thought.

Later, he approached her. "Your beauty bewitches me. Will you consent to be mine?"

"No, I cannot! I cannot!" the dancer replied, rushing back to her mother, Jawahar.

"How dare you refuse an offer from the King of Gujarat? Have you gone mad?" her mother scolded.

"You know, mother, I'm betrothed to Kehar," she replied firmly. She was Kanwal, the daughter of the famous dancer Jawahar.

When Jawahar conveyed this to Mahmud Shah, he was furious. "Kehar! He's just a petty chieftain under Mahmud Shah's command," the Sultan mocked.

"My Kehar is a lion among men. Mahmud Shah is just a jackal," Kanwal retorted proudly.

"You called me a jackal! You'll regret this. I'll throw your lion in a cage, bound in chains," Mahmud Shah threatened, placing Kanwal under the watch of his guard, Falooda Khan.

The next day, Mahmud Shah challenged his men to capture Kehar. Jalal, a chieftain, accepted the challenge. He devised a plan with Arabkhan, an ace swimmer.

At a big mela on the banks of the river Sabarmati, Jalal invited Kehar for water games. Arabkhan tried to drown Kehar by dragging him under the water. But Kehar overpowered him, giving him a severe beating before dragging him out of the water. Jalal had to pretend it was all a game, but Kehar suspected treachery.

The next morning, Kehar asked Mahmud Shah for permission to return to his jagir. "Stay a little longer, Kehar! Go after Holi, next week," the Sultan insisted, secretly hatching another plan.

On Holi day, Kehar arrived with his friends Khetji and Sangji at Mahmud Shah's palace. They were led into an enclosed courtyard and asked to remove their weapons. Suddenly, Mahmud Shah's men attacked. Kehar was outnumbered, bound in iron chains, and thrown into a cage. His friends managed to escape.

Mahmud Shah placed the cage in Kanwal's courtyard. "Come and have a look at your lion! Is he worth pining over? Ask Kanwal to be mine, Kehar, and I will free you," he taunted.

"You cheat! I will kill you just as Bheema killed Jarasandha," Kehar roared.

Refusing food and drink, Kehar languished in the cage. Kanwal brought him delicacies, but he ignored her. Desperate, Kanwal sent a letter to his friends, Khetji and Sangji.

They sent a reply: "O brave Rajput! ... How will you fulfil your vow of killing Mahmud if you starve? Rouse yourself, recall all your fighting skills and escape!"

Kanwal read the letter to Kehar. "How right they are. Let's eat the food you've brought, Kanwal," he finally agreed.

They planned an escape for the next new moon night. Kanwal's maid, Tuna, drugged the wine of the guard, Falooda Khan. When he collapsed, Kanwal gave Kehar a file and a dagger. Kehar cut his chains, broke the lock, and emerged from the cage. He killed the awakening Falooda Khan and escaped over the fort walls using the terrace.

Kehar reached his home and met his friends. They decided it was too dangerous to stay in Gujarat. "Let's leave at once for Mewar across the border," Sangji suggested.

They reached Bethun, a border town in Mewar, and sought refuge with the village chief, Gango, a Bhil. "I place both myself and the Bhils of my sixty villages at your service," Gango offered generously.

With Sangji, Khetji, and Gango's men, Kehar began attacking royal posts across the border in Gujarat. He looted the rich and distributed the wealth among the poor.

Mahmud Shah, enraged, sent Jalal to capture Kehar. When Jalal arrived in Baria, Kehar devised a plan. He accepted a meal invitation from Bhoonga, a cunning man who informed Jalal's soldiers.

When the soldiers arrived at Bhoonga's house, Kehar charged out the backdoor, mounted Jalal's own horse, beheaded Jalal in a swift stroke, and sped away.

Mahmud Shah proclaimed a huge reward for capturing Kehar. Meanwhile, Kanwal hatched a plan. She sent a message to Kehar through her maidservant: "Moonghda's marriage party will be leaving from Ajmer for Ahmedabad. Join it and come here."

Kehar disguised himself as a Sadhu, accompanied by four armed men, and joined the marriage procession, safely entering Ahmedabad.

Kanwal then changed her strategy with Mahmud Shah. "Sir, I am tired of waiting for Kehar... I am willing to marry you," she told him. "But I have a few conditions. I wish to be married on the same day as Moonghda... Secondly, I wish to be led to the altar from the Great Dome, amid noisy fireworks... And thirdly, my mother should be permitted to come right up to the Great Dome in a palanquin."

Mahmud Shah eagerly agreed to all conditions. At night, fully armed, Kehar got into the palanquin at Kanwal's mother's house and was carried into the women's quarters at the Great Dome.

As the deafening fireworks and blinding celebrations began, Mahmud Shah walked into the bride's quarters, expecting Kanwal.

"Mahmud!" a voice called out. It was Kehar.

"Here we are, face to face at last. Let's see now who's the lion and who's the jackal," Kehar challenged.

They unsheathed their swords and fought bravely. Since they were well matched, they discarded their weapons and fought barehanded. Finally, Kehar overpowered Mahmud Shah and killed him. The noise of the fireworks completely masked the sounds of their struggle.

"Kanwal, you are mine now! Let's go," Kehar said.

Both got into the palanquin, which was brought back to Kanwal's mother's place. From there, the pair mounted horses kept ready and rode off into the night towards Bethun, free at last.

docs.title: Kapala Kundala
docs.text:
During the reign of Akbar the Great, a lone boat was returning from a voyage of pilgrimage to Gangasagar. At the mouth of the River Hooghly, a dense fog descended, and the boat lost its way.

"We are lost! If we strike against a rock, it will be worse if we are washed ashore. Then we'll be at the mercy of wild beasts in the jungles," the passengers panicked.

When the fog cleared, they disembarked on the shore. "We are tired and hungry. Let us eat first and then rest," one said. "But how shall we cook? We have no firewood."

Navakumar, a brave young man, volunteered. "I will try. But will someone come with me?" When no one dared, Navakumar went into the forest alone.

While he was gathering wood, the tide came in. The passengers on the boat realized they would be stranded. "We should wait for a little while longer... Can come back for Navakumar later, when the storm has abated," they decided, abandoning him.

The stormy waters drove them far off the coast. Navakumar returned to find the boat gone. "No sign of them! They have been swept away by the sea!" Or, perhaps, they've deserted me!

Looking for shelter, Navakumar saw a fire flickering in the distance. He found a Kapalik (a votary of the Goddess Kali who offers human sacrifice) meditating before the fire.

"I am a Brahman. I need food and shelter," Navakumar asked.

The Kapalik took him to his cottage. "You can eat this fruit and sleep here. But don't leave the cottage till I return." Navakumar slept till the next evening.

When he awoke, the Kapalik had gone again. Navakumar wandered into the forest and lost his way. Suddenly, he met a beautiful maiden. "I am Kapala Kundala. Come with me!" she said, leading him to a cottage and then disappearing.

The next day, Navakumar encountered her again. "Don't go with him! He is going to sacrifice you to Goddess Kali," she whispered urgently, and fled like a frightened deer.

The Kapalik caught Navakumar. "You cannot free yourself from my grip," the strong Kapalik dragged him to the place of sacrifice and bound him firmly.

"Don't try to resist... But where is my scimitar? I must have dropped it while he was struggling with me," the Kapalik muttered, going to look for it.

When he was gone, Kapala Kundala suddenly appeared with the scimitar. "Sh-shh! Don't make a noise! He might hear us. I'll free you in no time," she whispered, cutting his bonds.

She led Navakumar to a temple in the forest and told the priest the whole story. "Is there anything I can do to save her from the Kapalik?" Navakumar asked.

"Yes. You can marry her, and take her with you. She is a Brahman. She was brought up by the Kapalik," the priest explained. "I would be happy to do so."

So Navakumar and Kapala Kundala became husband and wife. The next day, the priest escorted them to the nearest village. "Here is some money for your expenses on the way. Hire a palanquin for Kapala Kundala."

After the priest left, a palanquin was hired. Navakumar walked behind, but gradually lost pace. By nightfall, he was worried about dacoits.

He stumbled upon a broken palanquin. "Kapala Kundala! Is it you?" Looking inside, he saw a lady whose hands and feet were bound. "Oh! It's not Kapala!"

He untied her. "My servants had just left me and gone ahead to make preparations at the village inn... when suddenly dacoits attacked. They took away all my belongings, while my palanquin-bearers fled."

"How shall I reach the inn now?" she asked.

"You can come with me. I am going to the inn to join my wife. I hope she is safe," Navakumar offered.

They reached the inn. "Ah! My wife has arrived already. Thank God she is safe."

Navakumar introduced the lady. "This is my wife, Kapala Kundala. I am Navakumar Sharma of Saptagram."

"My name is Mati Bibi. I have come to thank you," the lady said. She offered valuable ornaments to Kapala Kundala as tokens of gratitude. "Please accept these ornaments... I still have some left in the luggage which my men had already taken to the inn."

When Mati Bibi returned to her room, her maid questioned her. "Why did you give such valuable jewellery to a stranger?"

"He is not a stranger. He is my husband," Mati Bibi revealed. "Yes. I was married to him. But when my father became a convert to Islam, his father decided to spurn me."

Mati Bibi, whose real name was Shyama, was now the rival of the famous Mehr-unnisa for the Prince's heart in the Mughal court, known as Lutf-unnisa.

A few days later, Navakumar arrived at Saptagram with his bride, receiving a joyous welcome. His family renamed Kapala Kundala as 'Mrinmayee'.

At first, Kapala Kundala was not happy. "One has to work all the time here. How I miss my care-free life in the forest!" she confided in her sister-in-law.

In the meantime, Mati Bibi had returned to Agra. But her thoughts were still with Navakumar. When Emperor Akbar died and Prince Salim was to be crowned, she decided to leave Agra. "Let him marry Mehr-unnisa. I am leaving Agra right now! I will go to Saptagram," she declared.

She summoned Navakumar. "Take me as your wife," she pleaded.

"That cannot be. A poor Brahman cannot take a rich woman of the court as his maid," he refused.

"You can't escape me. When I want something, I get it," she threatened angrily. Her gesture revived a childhood memory in Navakumar. "Just because I am your wife you cannot order me around like this!" she had said then. "Padmavati! My God!" he realized.

Disguised as a man, Mati Bibi went to the forest near Navakumar's house to wait for an opportunity. There, she saw the Kapalik! He had survived a fall from a cliff but lost an arm.

"I am interested in Kapala Kundala too. What have you against her?" she asked the Kapalik.

"I brought her up, but she deceived me. She ran away and married Navakumar. I want her to die."

"I also desire her death. I will tell you how to bring about her death. Listen," Mati Bibi plotted.

Mati Bibi, disguised as a man, approached Mrinmayee at night. "The herb must be plucked by a woman exactly at midnight. And she must be alone," she told Mrinmayee, luring her into the forest.

Navakumar, suspecting his wife was meeting a lover, followed her into the forest. He met the Kapalik. "She comes here to meet this young man!" the Kapalik fueled his jealousy. "You displeased the Goddess by running away. Now you can atone by sacrificing your wife to Kali."

Blinded by jealousy, Navakumar agreed. "I'll do it. Yes, she is unfaithful to me."

He confronted Mrinmayee. "You have been unfaithful to me. I am taking you to the Kapalik."

"That's not true. However, I don't wish to defend myself... I'd rather die than be suspected by you."

As they neared the place of sacrifice, Navakumar suddenly came to his senses. "That is the man who wanted to kill me! What have I done! I have killed an innocent woman. I can atone only by my own death."

Mrinmayee stopped him. "And this is the woman who saved me! How can I kill her at his suggestion?" Navakumar realized.

"Mrinmayee! Why don't you show me the light? Why don't you tell me that what I saw was an illusion?"

"It was! You thought I was meeting a man, but it was a woman in disguise!" Mrinmayee explained.

"I believe you, Mrinmayee. Forgive me. I am not worthy of you," Navakumar apologized deeply. "The forest makes us take leave of our senses. Let's go away from here quickly."

It had all started in the forest and was about to end in the forest. We do not know what happened afterwards to the evil Kapalik and Mati Bibi. They were left in the dark while Navakumar and Kapala Kundala came back to the light.

docs.title: Karttikeya
docs.text:
In ancient times, the Devas and the Asuras were forever at war, and often it was the Asuras who were defeated. After one crushing defeat, an Asura king named Akhirsen went to his daughter, Maya, a sorceress.

"The Devas are victorious again. O the shame of it!" he lamented.

"Don't worry, father. I will not rest till I teach the Devas a lesson!" Maya vowed. Taking the form of a beautiful woman, she went to the forest where the great Sage Kashyapa sat deep in meditation. "I will marry this sage and bring forth children more powerful than the Devas," she plotted.

Her magic changed the place into a pretty garden where she danced and sang melodiously till the sage looked up. "Please let me serve you as your wife, O holy one," she requested. The virtuous sage, ignorant of her designs, accepted her.

In due course, a son was born to them. "You, my mighty Surapadman, shall be the lord of the three worlds," Maya declared. Later, she gave birth to two more sons, Simhamukha and Taraka.

When the boys came of age, Kashyapa told them, "I am going away, my sons. Take care of your mother, lead a virtuous life, and be devoted to the Lord."

"Now is the time to act. Propitiate Lord Shiva and obtain boons which will make you invincible," Maya instructed them.

The three left home and performed severe penances to win the grace of Shiva. But when their efforts did not bear fruit, Surapadman, the eldest, jumped into the sacrificial fire.

He was saved from the flames by Shiva himself. "Why are you sacrificing yourself?" Shiva asked.

"Lord, I want to be the master of the universe. I want to have a body that will not perish," Surapadman prayed.

"No one can have an immortal body. But you will be invincible and rule the universe for a long time to come. You shall not be defeated by any power except mine," Shiva granted the boon.

Intoxicated with triumph, the three brothers returned home and then began ravaging kingdom after kingdom. They eventually attacked Devaloka, routed the Devas, and took them captive.

"We'll make them our slaves. Indra will be our fisherman, and Vayu our sweeper," Surapadman decreed from his throne, mocking the captive gods. He also imprisoned Indra's son, Jayanta. Surapadman then had the magnificent city of Mahendrapuri built by Vishwakarma to serve as his capital.

The desperate Devas approached Lord Brahma. "Shiva had promised to help us. That was long ago. Let's go to Kailas once more and pray to him," Brahma advised.

At Kailas, Shiva listened to their pleas. "Be patient! Your miseries will soon end because their days are numbered," he assured them.

Then Shiva assumed a majestic form with six faces. From his faces, six divine sparks shot forth, dazzling the Devas with their splendour. "The child born out of these sparks will slay the Asuras," Shiva proclaimed.

"Vayu, you and Agni carry these sparks to Ganga. She'll carry them to the Sharavana (forest of reeds) on the Udaya mountain," Shiva instructed.

Ganga brought the sparks to the Sharavana, where they turned into six beautiful babies resting on lotus flowers. Six celestial nymphs, the Krittikas, happened to pass by and cuddled the infants.

Shiva and Parvati soon arrived. "My children!" Parvati cried. As she stepped closer and embraced them, the six babies merged into one child with six heads and twelve arms.

"Peace! Peace! As Parvati's son he will be named Skanda and as the son of the Goddess of the forest, Sharavana; as the Krittikas' son, Karttikeya; as Ganga's Kumara; as Agni's son, Mahasena; and as mine, Guha," Shiva declared.

Soon, nine divine beings emerged from the lake. "Those are your Ganas—Veerabahu and his companions," Shiva told Karttikeya.

Shiva took Karttikeya to Mount Kailasa. When the time came to subdue the Asuras, Shiva gave him a divine weapon. "You shall lead the Devas to victory. Crush the Asuras and liberate the Devas. Here is the matchless Vel, your spear. May success be yours!"

Armed with the spear, Karttikeya set out. His first obstacle was the Asura Krauncha, who took the form of a massive mountain to block his path. Without a word, Karttikeya hurled his spear, shattering the mountain and destroying Krauncha.

When the news reached Taraka, he mocked, "Krauncha destroyed? By a slip of a boy? Let me go and size him up."

Taraka confronted Karttikeya but found his weapons useless against the divine spear. Karttikeya hurled his spear, and Tarakasura was slain.

Next, Karttikeya sent Veerabahu as his envoy to Surapadman, asking him to release Jayanta and stop persecuting the Devas. Surapadman haughtily refused and threatened Veerabahu, who fought his way out and returned with the message of war.

Karttikeya's army marched to Mahendrapuri. On the first day, Surapadman's son, Banukopan, caused great damage to the Deva army but was eventually slain by Veerabahu.

The next day, Simhamukha led the attack. He used a magical missile that wound itself like a rope around Veerabahu and the Deva army, hurling them far away. Karttikeya countered the move and then faced Simhamukha.

After a fierce battle, Karttikeya hurled the 'Indrayudha' (thunderbolt) at Simhamukha. As he fell, Simhamukha's ego shattered. "Now I see you in all your glory. O Lord, bless me."

"You shall serve Goddess Kali as her vehicle," the merciful Karttikeya blessed him.

The next day, Surapadman himself led the Asuras. He used sorcery to create an illusion of darkness and revived his dead army. Karttikeya used a special astra to destroy the revived Asuras and dispel the darkness.

"I'll change my form and attack him," Surapadman decided. He assumed the form of a huge bird and attacked. Karttikeya, using Indra in the form of a peacock as his mount, fought back in the sky.

Surapadman escaped and took the form of a massive tree. "Learn, O blind one! Darkness cannot hide before light!" Karttikeya hurled his spear, cutting the tree in two.

Surapadman emerged in his natural form and charged. Quick as lightning, Karttikeya hurled his spear, fatally striking the Asura king.

As he fell, a great change came over Surapadman. "Lord, pardon me. I repent for my evil acts. Lord, I seek refuge in you," he gasped.

Karttikeya looked at the fallen Asura with compassion. "Surapadman, repentance washes away all one's sins. You shall serve me as my vehicle and also as my emblem," he promised.

Thus, Surapadman assumed two forms—one of a peacock to serve as a vehicle to the Lord, and another of a cock to adorn Karttikeya's flag post. The Devas rejoiced and bowed to Karttikeya in reverence.

docs.title: Kesari the Flying Thief
docs.text:
Kesari, the incorrigible thief, had been banished from the city with the warning that if he returned and was caught, he would be hanged.

"I dare not go back to the city. At least not for a few days! If they catch me..." he thought, hiding in a forest outside the city.

Suddenly, he saw a strange sight. A man was floating down from the sky! "He's coming down! But how did he get up there?" Kesari watched in amazement.

The man landed, removed his sandals, and went to bathe in a nearby stream. "It's the sandals! He's removing them! He can't touch earth without removing them!" Kesari realized.

As the man bathed, Kesari crept out and snatched the magic sandals. Putting them on, he instantly rose into the air.

"Hey! Come back! Those are my sandals!" the man shouted.

"Were, my friend, were!" Kesari mocked, flying away. "Who says thieving is bad? The gods themselves approve. If they didn't, would they have sent me this gift to make my job easier?"

Kesari flew back to the city. He found a deserted temple dedicated to Goddess Kali. "A deserted temple! It would make a fine hiding-place for my booty!" he decided. "O Goddess, make me prosperous! Give me wealth!" he prayed before setting out.

That night, Kesari stole precious gems from a jeweller's shop. The next morning, the jeweller complained to the King. The King summoned the Kotwal (chief of police).

The Kotwal explained, "I saw a man prowling in the jeweller's shop. I ran out and tried to chase him... But he hovered about for a while, just to tease me... and then rose high into the sky... and vanished. I'd have to have wings for that, Maharaj!"

"A flying thief may be hard to trace and catch. But as king it is my duty to bring him to book. I myself will hunt him down," the King resolved.

The King dressed as a common soldier and, with a small party of men, searched the forest. "Let us each search a different part of the forest. We'll be able to work faster that way," the King suggested.

While resting, the King smelled a familiar fragrance. "Mm-m-m! The fragrance of musk and... sandalwood? It's coming from that direction. There must be a temple somewhere there."

He found the deserted temple and saw Kesari worshipping the Goddess with the stolen gems. "Ah! I was right. There's somebody worshipping the Goddess!"

When Kesari left to fetch more offerings, the King hid inside the dark temple. When Kesari returned and offered the food to the deity, the King stepped out and captured him. "Don't try to struggle! Let me go!" Kesari cried.
