The King was about to arrest him when he noticed Kesari's calm and peaceful expression while he had been meditating earlier. "I have never seen such a peaceful expression before," the King marvelled. "He has no wealth. No wonder he has peace."

The King's men arrived. "Ah! There's the scoundrel pretending to be a monk! Ha!" they shouted.

"No! This is not the Kesari I set out to catch. Forgive me, O enlightened one," the King said, falling at Kesari's feet. "What force brought over the change I do not know... but I do know that I am in the presence of a man who has found peace."

"I salute you, noble one," the King bowed. Kesari the thief had, indeed, transformed into Kesari the enlightened one, realizing that true peace comes not from stolen wealth, but from inner devotion.

docs.title: Vasudeva
docs.text:
Vasudeva came to meet his friend in the city of Shripura to ask him for a loan. "I desperately need some money," he pleaded.

"I have none to spare. But... our King gives two gold coins to the first Brahmana he meets in the morning," his friend advised.

"Two gold coins! Why, that's precisely what I need!" Vasudeva rejoiced.

"I'll spend the night here and go to the palace a little before dawn," he decided. That night, Vasudeva couldn't sleep. "What if I don't wake up in time? What if another Brahmana gets there before I do and receives the money? The best thing would be not to sleep at all!"

He went to the palace right away in the middle of the night and waited outside. "Then I'm sure to be the first."

Suddenly, guards seized him. "Don't try to struggle! Let me go!" he cried.

"I haven't done anything. I only wanted to see the King!"

"What are you doing? You did, did you? Don't worry you'll see him. In good time," the guards sneered, tying him up and throwing him in a dungeon.

The next morning he was brought before the King. "This is the dangerous spy we caught last night, Maharaj."

"Please let me go, Maharaj. I am not a spy," Vasudeva begged.

"Then what were you doing outside the palace at midnight?" the King asked.

"I was making sure no one got there before I did."

"For what?"

"To receive the two gold coins you give to the first Brahmana you see in the day!"

The King was moved. "Poor man! All that for just two gold coins! My good man, why two gold coins? I shall give you anything you want. Anything I want, Maharaj! Then may I have..."

"Maharaj, I need some time to think about what I want," Vasudeva requested.

"Take your time, my good man. Come back to me when you've decided."

Vasudeva went to a quiet spot in the forest to think. "Twenty coins will take care of all my needs. But two hundred would bring me many comforts. Shall I make it two hundred coins... or perhaps two thousand? The King can surely afford more. I'll make it a lakh. But a crore will get me every possible thing I want on earth. And two would... two would..."

He realized that his greed was growing exponentially. "And the more you have the more you want. There are no limits to one's wants. There is only one solution..."

When Vasudeva returned to the palace, the King asked, "Well, my good man? Have you decided what you want?"

"Yes, Maharaj. Nothing."

"Nothing?" the King was surprised.

"Yes. I wanted money because I was unhappy without it. But I have realised that with it I would be even unhappier," Vasudeva explained.

"In fact, happiest is he who does not want anything," he concluded.

The King smiled. "And I bless you for giving me an opportunity to learn this truth."

docs.title: Jinadatta
docs.text:
Jinadatta, a rich but generous merchant of Pottanapura, had fallen on bad days. His business was failing.

"Why don't you go to my father and ask him for a loan?" his wife, Purna, suggested.

"No! No, Purna. I can't take money from your father," Jinadatta refused, his pride hurt.

"Why not? It's only a loan. You will repay it when business improves. He won't refuse. I know my father," she insisted.

"What if he refuses?" Jinadatta worried, but finally agreed. "If you are so confident... well... all right... I'll go."

"You must go today! I'll get some food ready for the journey," Purna smiled, going into the kitchen. She came out with a small packet. "Here you are! Just enough food to last you till you reach father's house."

Jinadatta set out. Towards noon he sat under a tree by a tank and opened his lunch packet. "Roasted gram powder! Good Purna does pamper me! She's probably given me all that was left in the house," he thought affectionately.

Just then, a monk approached. "A monk! How lean and feeble he looks. He must have been fasting."

"Holy sir, please honour me by accepting a portion of my meal," Jinadatta offered. The monk accepted, and the two shared the meagre meal.

When they finished, the monk went his way and Jinadatta continued his journey.

When he reached his father-in-law's house, the reception was lukewarm. "Why it's Jinadatta! Come in, come in," his father-in-law said, eyeing his clothes. "But you seem to have fallen on bad days. These shabby clothes... How thin you have become! Have you been ill?"

"Not ill, but... I have had a spate of bad luck in business. We have lost everything but the house. I have come to you for a loan, to start afresh," Jinadatta explained.

"Oh! I see! But... Well, Jinadatta. I've just invested all my spare money. I am so-o-o sorry but... I hate to refuse you but... at the moment... I will not be able to help you, I'm afraid."

Shocked by the blunt refusal, Jinadatta walked away in a daze. "He won't refuse, I know my father," Purna had said. "Poor Purna! If I tell her the truth it will break her heart. She was so sure that her father would help us."

He sat down by a river, feeling utterly defeated. He noticed a bundle his father-in-law had given him as a parting gift. "What's that gleaming on the ground?"

He opened it. "Pebbles! Oh! They are just pebbles," he sighed. "This should save her from the first shock. Later, when she finds out that this bundle contains only pebbles... I can always say that there must have been a mistake somewhere."

When Jinadatta reached home, Purna greeted him joyfully. "Oh! What a big bundle! I can see that father has been quite generous."

"Purna, I'm going out for a while after I've washed and changed," Jinadatta said quickly, trying to avoid the moment of truth.

When Jinadatta, who had gone out to pacify his creditors, returned, he found a feast waiting for him. "Wh-what's all this?" he asked in astonishment.

"Your favourite dishes!" Purna smiled.

"You know we already owe the grocer a lot of money. Why did you borrow..."

"Borrow? Why should I borrow?" Purna laughed. "One precious stone was enough to pay..."

"Which... what precious stone?" Jinadatta stammered.

"From the bundle father gave us."

"Where is the bundle?" Jinadatta asked, rushing into the room. "But... but... I don't believe it!"

The bundle was filled with gleaming, priceless gems! "Your father didn't give me anything!" he tried to explain, but Purna wouldn't believe it.

Yes, Jinadatta's bad days were certainly over. Now whether it was his generosity to the monk or his kind heart or Purna's faith in her father's goodness that turned mere pebbles into precious stones, is not known. But Jinadatta never again sat to a meal without first offering some of it to a mendicant.

docs.title: Kochunni
docs.text:
Kochunni lived with his parents at Kayamkulam, a little-known town in central Kerala. His parents were poor, and his father was a petty thief. Young Kochunni went hungry most of the time when his father couldn't steal anything.

"Why should I stay here and starve? I'll run away and try to earn an honest living," Kochunni decided one day.

Tired and hungry, Kochunni came to a town called Evoor. An old Brahman named Annavi saw him. "That old Brahman looks a kind sort. I'll ask him for some food."

Annavi was indeed kind-hearted. He fed Kochunni a bowl of steaming rice gruel. "I'll try to persuade a friend of mine to hire you," Annavi promised, and got Kochunni a job in a shop.

Kochunni worked hard in the shop during the day and at night taught himself to read and write. Within a few years, he was keeping accounts and managing the shop single-handed.

One day, an unusual teacher came to Kayamkulam—an expert in the art of warfare, hypnotism, and mesmerism. Kochunni's master hired the teacher. Kochunni also wanted to learn.

"No, I cannot. How do I know you will not misuse what you learn? After all, your father was a thief," the teacher initially refused. But when he found out Kochunni was secretly watching and learning anyway, he agreed to accept him as a pupil.

One day, a customer came to the shop for molasses. Kochunni remembered he had sent the last of it to his master's house. Wanting to please the customer, he ran to his master's house, found it locked, squeezed through a gap in the wall, and brought the molasses.

The following day, his master confronted him. "Sir, Kochunni is not to be trusted. We saw him break into your house..." people had reported.

"Still, we feel that you should dismiss him. He might rob you. After all, his father was a thief," they insisted.

The master dismissed Kochunni. Hounded by society for the sins of his father, Kochunni had but one choice. "Well, if that's the way they want it, I shall be a thief—but an honest one. I'll steal from the rich and give to the poor."

He soon gathered a small band of loyal followers, making Mammad and Kochupillai his lieutenants. Soon the name of Kochunni came to be dreaded by the rich and loved by the poor.

He loved making a fool of pompous men. Once, he robbed a rich money-lender named Nair who boasted that no one could break into his house. Kochunni not only broke in but also took all of Nair's wealth, leaving him bewildered.

In contrast, he rewarded those who were kind. Once, disguised as a poor man, he was fed by a kind Brahman. Later, he rewarded the Brahman handsomely with stolen wealth. He also retrieved a precious ruby ring from an arrogant aristocrat and returned it to the poor old man it belonged to.

However, tragedy struck Kochunni's personal life. His mother-in-law was an ill-tempered woman who constantly nagged him and his wife. One day, during a heated argument, she insulted him and his friend Krishna Pillai. In a fit of rage, Kochunni struck her with a stick. To his horror, she fell dead.

"I have killed her! I'll surrender myself to the police," he told his grieving wife. He allowed himself to be arrested, but on the way, he managed to overpower the police and escape.

The Tahsildar, determined to catch him, cleverly played on the greed of Kochunni's lieutenants, Mammad and Kochupillai. "I will make you rich beyond your wildest dreams... if you catch him," he bribed them.

The treacherous lieutenants threw a party and got Kochunni drunk on a special brew. When he was fast asleep, they tied him to a cot and called the police.

Kochunni woke up and, though outnumbered and tied, put up a gallant fight. "Traitors! All of you! Take this—and this!" he yelled. But he was finally overpowered and carried out.

Kochunni was kept in a solitary cell, under a heavy guard. "Alas! Too late I realise the truth. Justice is one thing, law another. My aims were noble and just but law does not take these things into consideration. My methods were unlawful and for that I pay; for that I repent," he reflected bitterly.

Three months later, even before his trial was going on, Kochunni died. His spirit roamed free once more. It was almost as though he had willed himself to die. The memory of this gallant outlaw lingers on amidst the graceful palms and sparkling lagoons he loved so well.

docs.title: Konark
docs.text:
King Narasimha Deva I was an ambitious ruler, a conqueror, as well as a generous king. There was only one thing he lacked—an heir.

"O Surya, if my devotion has pleased you, I pray to you, grant me an heir," he prayed to the Sun God.

Soon after, his prayer was answered. "My Lord, you have been blessed with a son. He is as radiant as the sun," the Queen announced.

"We will name him Bhanudeva," the King decided. He summoned his ministers. "To commemorate my victories in battle, and as a mark of gratitude to the Sun God for giving me an heir, I will build a temple... right here, at Konark, where it will greet the first rays of the fiery sun as they fall on the earth." ('Konark' means 'Corner of the Sun').

Under the orders of the chief minister, Sibai Santra, workers started throwing stones into the water to lay the foundation in a gorge. But the strong current carried the stones away.

Tired and frustrated, Sibai stopped at a cottage. An old woman gave him some hot khiri (a thin gruel). Sibai was so hungry that he plunged his fingers right into the middle of the bowl, burning them.

"Ha ha! Why, you're just like that foolish Sibai Santra! Throwing stones in the middle of the water instead of starting from the sides," the old woman laughed.

Realisation dawned on Sibai. He started filling up the sides of the gorge first, and the foundation was successfully laid.

The King appointed Bisu Maharana as the chief architect. "I have brought 1200 of the best craftsmen in the kingdom... The King wants to build a great temple... You know I can't disobey the King's orders. I'll be back as soon as the temple is completed," Bisu told his pregnant wife before leaving.

Bisu started to plan and build the temple in the form of Surya's vehicle—a chariot drawn by seven golden horses, with twenty-four wheels representing the hours of the day.

Meanwhile, in Bisu's home, his little son, Dharmapada, was growing up. While other children played, Dharmapada spent all his time reading his father's old manuscripts on architecture. When Dharmapada turned twelve, he set out for Konark to meet his father.

On his way, he rested near the temple complex and ate some berries. He shared them with a man passing by. "These berries are delicious! Bisu, you must taste these berries," the man said to his companion.

"My son!" "Father!" Dharmapada and Bisu were joyously reunited.

But Bisu and the craftsmen were in deep distress. "What do we do now? If we don't fix the crown stone by Magha Sukla Saptami, the King will have us all beheaded," Bisu lamented. They had tried many times, but the crown stone refused to hold up due to complex magnetic calculations.

"May be I can help. I can do it. I can fix the crown stone," Dharmapada offered. Bisu reluctantly gave the boy a chance.

Dharmapada set to work, and his calculations worked! He successfully placed the stone, saving the day.

At first, there was great relief and happiness among the craftsmen. But then, a dark thought arose. "What will happen when the King finds out that it was a 12-year-old boy who fixed the crown stone and not us? We will be beheaded!" they worried. "Bisu, you must send him away... The lives of those 1200 craftsmen are more important than mine," some argued cruelly.

Dharmapada overheard this. Unnoticed by his father, he climbed right to the top of the temple. Bisu looked everywhere and finally saw him. "Dharmapada! Son! Where are you going?"

"This should save her from the first shock," Dharmapada thought of his mother. He stood at the very top of the temple... and jumped into the river below, sacrificing his own life to save the 1200 craftsmen from the King's potential wrath.

"I have built a temple but lost a son," Bisu wept bitterly.

The grand temple of Konark stood for many years, a testament to the skill of the artisans and the sacrifice of a young boy. Till, gradually, it fell prey to vandals and natural disasters. But what remains is still breathtaking, and the stories around this ancient monument will continue to be told for ages to come.

docs.title: Krishna: An Ally of the Pandavas
docs.text:
Sage Shukadeva narrated the exploits of Krishna and Balarama to Pareekshit.

"Once while Balarama was journeying on Mount Raivataka, he met an ape called Dvivida. Dvivida, who was said to possess the strength of ten thousand elephants, was enraged by the slaying of his friend, Narakasura. 'I must avenge the death of my dear friend. This is where that Krishna, the slayer of my friend, lives. I will ravage this whole area,' the ape roared."

Dvivida created havoc, throwing huge boulders on houses and churning the seawater until coastal villages were submerged. He even harassed women on the Raivataka hill. Balarama confronted him. Dvivida uprooted trees and rocks to attack, but Balarama parried the blows and crushed him barehanded, hitting him a fatal blow on the collar bone.

Sometime later, Prince Duryodhana arranged a Swayamvara for his daughter, Lakshmana. Samba, the son of Krishna and Jambavati, attended and boldly carried the bride away.

Karna, Duryodhana, and other Kaurava warriors gave chase. "At last the Kaurava warriors overpowered Samba... Samba was tied up and taken captive to Hastinapura."

Sage Narada conveyed this news to the Yadavas in Dwaraka. The Yadavas prepared for war, but Balarama intervened. "Let us avoid any fights in the family. I myself will go to Hastinapura and persuade them to free Samba."

Balarama went to Hastinapura and sent a message through Uddhava. The Kauravas welcomed Balarama, but when he delivered King Ugrasena's request to release Samba, they turned arrogant. "Ha! You Yadavas really think too much of yourself... Just because you are related to us by marriage, it does not make you our equal in status."

Enraged by the insult, Balarama struck his powerful plough into the ground. "Today I will render the earth free from these vain and foolish Kauravas. I will drag their city into the Ganga!"

The earth began to shake, and the city walls trembled. Panic-stricken, the Kauravas rushed out, begging for mercy. "They hurriedly released Samba. Here is your Samba and Lakshmana, his wife."

Balarama returned to Dwaraka with the newly-weds, where they were joyously received.

One day, Narada visited Dwaraka to see how Krishna managed his thousands of wives. He was astounded to find Krishna present in every palace simultaneously—playing chess in one, performing a yajna in another, and in serious discussion in yet another!

While Krishna was in his council hall, Sudharma Sabha, a messenger arrived. "O Lord, you are the saviour of all. I have come to seek your help... He was the messenger sent on behalf of the thousands of kings held captive by Jarasandha in Girivraja."

Just then, Narada arrived and conveyed Yudhishthira's wish to perform the Rajasooya Yajna. Krishna advised Yudhishthira, "First you must subdue all the kings of the land... We should attack Jarasandha first."

Krishna, Bhima, and Arjuna went to Girivraja disguised as Brahmins. They demanded a duel from Jarasandha. "We are not Brahmins in search of alms. We are Kshatriyas keen on fighting," Krishna revealed.

Jarasandha laughed and chose to fight Bhima. "Like two mighty elephants fighting, Bhima and Jarasandha were pitched against one another." They wrestled barehanded for twenty-seven days.

On the twenty-eighth day, Bhima confided, "O Krishna, I cannot defeat Jarasandha."

To give Bhima a hint, Krishna took up a twig and tore it into two halves, throwing them in opposite directions. Understanding the hint (which referred to how Jarasandha was born from two halves joined together), Bhima felled Jarasandha and tore his body into two!

Jarasandha's son, Sahadeva, was installed as king, and the imprisoned kings were finally freed. They joyously accepted Yudhishthira's supremacy.

During the Rajasooya Yajna in Indraprastha, Yudhishthira offered the first worship to Krishna, guided by Sahadeva's advice.

Shishupala, the Prince of Chedi, could not bear this. "How can you people be misled by a young fool like Sahadeva? With so many learned venerable sages present here, how can you select a cowherd like Krishna for the first worship?" he shouted insultingly.

When Shishupala lunged at him, Krishna calmly released his Chakra and cut off his head.

The Rajasooya Yajna was successfully completed. However, Duryodhana burned with envy seeing the wealth and glory of the Pandavas. Wandering in the magical palace built by Maya, he was confused by illusions, falling into pools he thought were floors and bumping into doors he thought were open.

"Many people including Bhima and the queens burst into laughter at Duryodhana's plight." Humiliated and mocked, Duryodhana's heart was filled with bitterness and a thirst for revenge.

docs.title: Krishna: The Lord of Dwaraka
docs.text:
After killing Kamsa, Krishna and Balarama went to meet their true parents, Vasudeva and Devaki. To spare them from awe, Krishna used his Maya to make them forget his divinity for a while. "Overwhelmed by Krishna's words, Vasudeva clasped Krishna in a fond embrace."

Krishna then comforted his grandfather Ugrasena and restored him to the throne. "You are now the King of the Yadavas," Krishna declared. Kamsa's cruel reign had caused many subjects to flee, but now they welcomed them back and settled them in their own homes.

After a joyful reunion with Nanda, his foster father, the Upanayana (thread ceremony) of Balarama and Krishna was performed. The boys then set out for the ashrama of Sage Sandipani in Avanti for their formal education.

"Within sixty-four days, they mastered all the sixty-four arts and crafts." For their gurudakshina, Sage Sandipani asked them to restore his son, who had drowned in Prabhasa.

Krishna went to the ocean and learned that a mighty Asura named Panchajana, who dwelt in a conch shell, had taken the boy away. Krishna dived into the sea, killed the Asura, and took the shell, which came to be known as Panchajanya. He then went to the abode of Yama, the Lord of Death, and brought the boy back.

With the blessing of their guru, the two boys returned to Mathura.

Knowing how much his foster parents and the Gopikas missed him, Krishna sent his friend Uddhava to Vrindavana with a message. "Tell them I am well... and eagerly awaiting my return," Krishna said.

Uddhava found Nanda, Yashoda, and the Gopikas deeply engrossed in their love for Krishna. He delivered Krishna's message: "I am the eternal soul... Be assured." Though moved by the message, they still longed for his physical presence. After staying for many months, Uddhava returned to Mathura and described their devotion to Krishna.

Krishna then fulfilled a promise to visit Sairandhri, the woman whose hunch he had cured. Later, he visited Akrura and sent him to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas. Akrura returned with worrying news: "Dhritarashtra favours the Kauravas and the Pandavas are not treated fairly... They even tried to poison them."

Meanwhile, Kamsa's two queens, Asti and Prapti, had returned to their father, Jarasandha, the King of Magadha. When they told him how Kamsa was killed, Jarasandha was enraged. "I will punish him," he roared.

Jarasandha attacked Mathura seventeen times with a huge army. Seventeen times, his army was destroyed by Krishna and Balarama. But each time, Krishna let Jarasandha go to collect another army, slowly lightening the burden of evil from the earth.

"On the eighteenth occasion, just before Jarasandha reached Mathura, the Asura Kalayavana, heard of the might of Krishna and Balarama." Kalayavana, whom even intrepid warriors feared, attacked Mathura with an army of 23 Akshauhinis.

Faced with two formidable enemies, Krishna decided to build an invincible fortress city in the ocean. "The city was named Dwaraka." With his divine power, Krishna transported all the Yadavas safely to the new city overnight.

Leaving the rest of his subjects safe, Krishna came out of the main gate of Mathura, unarmed. Kalayavana saw him and gave chase. Krishna led him on a long run, always staying just out of reach, until they came upon a hillside cave.

Krishna ducked into the cave. Kalayavana followed. In the dim light, Kalayavana saw a sleeping form covered with Krishna's yellow garment. Thinking it was Krishna, he gave it a swift kick.

The sleeping man slowly woke up. When he opened his eyes fully and looked at Kalayavana, the mighty Asura caught fire and was reduced to a heap of ashes!

The sleeping man was King Muchukunda. He had helped the Devas in a long battle against the Asuras. Weary, he had asked for a boon to sleep. The Devas granted it, adding: "If any fool disturbs your sleep, he will be reduced to ashes the instant you look at him."

Krishna appeared before King Muchukunda in his resplendent form. Muchukunda was overwhelmed with devotion and bowed respectfully at Lord Krishna's feet.

docs.title: Krishna: The Enchanter
docs.text:
Pareekshit asked Sage Shukadeva, "What happened to Mathura... while Krishna led Kalayavana to his death?"

"Balarama and his army... decimated the entire army of Kalayavana," Shukadeva explained. They took all the treasure to Dwaraka.

Then Jarasandha attacked Mathura for the eighteenth time. With their people safely in Dwaraka, Krishna and Balarama pretended to be afraid and fled to the Pravarshana hill. Jarasandha surrounded the hill and set it on fire. But Krishna and Balarama leaped to safety and returned to Dwaraka, leaving Jarasandha convinced he had destroyed his enemies.

In due time, Balarama married Revati.

Krishna had set his heart on Rukmini, the lovely daughter of the King of Vidarbha. However, her brother, Rukmi, hated Krishna and arranged for her to marry Shishupala, the Prince of Chedi.

Heartbroken, Rukmini sent a trusted old Brahman to Krishna with a message. "I have chosen you for my husband... Come to Vidarbha the day before the wedding and take me away forcibly," she pleaded.

Krishna immediately set out for Kundinapura, the capital of Vidarbha. On the day before the wedding, as Rukmini emerged from the temple of Goddess Parvati, she saw Krishna waiting. "And lifting her up, placed her on his chariot," speeding away with her.

Rukmi chased after them, vowing to kill Krishna. "But Krishna destroyed all of them with ease." Krishna was about to kill Rukmi, but Rukmini pleaded for her brother's life. Krishna spared him, but tied him up and shaved patches of his hair to humiliate him.

Balarama intervened. "Disfiguring a relative is as bad as killing him," he chided Krishna, and released Rukmi. Disgraced, Rukmi could not return to his capital and built a new city called Bhojkat instead.

Krishna and Rukmini were formally married in Dwaraka with great pomp.

"Pradyumna was very good-looking and was in fact an incarnation of Kamadeva, the God of Love." When Pradyumna was just ten days old, a demon named Shambarasura kidnapped him and threw him into the sea, knowing the child was destined to be his foe.

A huge fish swallowed the baby. The fish was caught and presented to Shambarasura. The cook, Mayavati, found the infant alive inside the fish! Narada appeared and revealed the baby's identity to Mayavati, who was actually an incarnation of Rati, Kamadeva's wife.

Mayavati raised Pradyumna in secret. When he grew up, she told him his true identity and taught him the magical arts needed to defeat Shambarasura.

Pradyumna challenged the demon. "Shambarasura rose rapidly in the air and began to rain weapons down... With the help of Maha-Maya, Pradyumna could counter all the wicked moves of the Asura. Finally he took up a sharp sword... and cut off Shambarasura's head."

Mayavati took Pradyumna back to Dwaraka, where he was joyfully reunited with his parents, Krishna and Rukmini.

Later, Satrajit, a devotee of Surya (the Sun God), received the legendary Syamantaka gem. The gem produced gold every day and protected against disease and famine. Krishna suggested Satrajit give it to King Ugrasena, but Satrajit refused.

One day, Satrajit's brother Prasena went hunting wearing the gem. He was killed by a lion, which in turn was killed by Jambavan, the bear-king. Jambavan took the gem for his child.

In Dwaraka, rumors spread that Krishna had murdered Prasena for the gem. "I must clear my name," Krishna resolved.

Krishna followed Prasena's trail, found the dead lion, and traced the tracks to Jambavan's cave. He fought Jambavan continuously for 28 days. Finally, Jambavan realized he was fighting his Lord. "I bear you no grudge, Jambavan," Krishna said.

Jambavan offered Krishna the gem and his daughter, Jambavati, in marriage.

Krishna returned to Dwaraka with Jambavati and the gem, clearing his name. He gave the gem back to Satrajit. Embarrassed, Satrajit offered his daughter Satyabhama to Krishna in marriage, along with the gem. Krishna accepted Satyabhama but let Satrajit keep the gem.

However, Shatadhanva, another Yadava who had hoped to marry Satyabhama, was angry. In the dead of night, he killed Satrajit and stole the gem.

Satyabhama went to Krishna, who was in Hastinapura. Krishna and Balarama returned to pursue Shatadhanva. Frightened, Shatadhanva left the gem with Akrura and fled.

Krishna chased Shatadhanva, eventually running him down and killing him. "But when they searched him for the gem—Oh! He doesn't have the gem. We need not have killed him," Krishna realized.

Akrura, fearing for his life, had fled the city. Krishna sent for him. "Uncle, I know that Shatadhanva left the gem with you," Krishna said.

Akrura produced the gem. Krishna showed it to the people of Dwaraka to finally clear his name of all doubts, and then graciously returned the gem to Akrura to keep.

docs.title: Krishna: Victory over Kamsa
docs.text:
On the auspicious day of Shivaratri, Krishna, Nanda, and other cowherds visited Ambikavana, a forest near the river Saraswati. Abstaining from any food to observe the fast, they went to sleep on the river bank.

Suddenly, a huge python pounced on the sleeping Nanda. "Help, Krishna! Help! Hurry! The python is swallowing me," Nanda cried. The cowherds woke up in alarm and beat the python with sticks and burning logs, but in vain.

Then Krishna came and gave a gentle kick to the python. With the mere touch of his foot, the python vanished. In its place stood a youth in all his radiance. "I am a Vidyadhara named Sudarshana... Proud of my wealth and good looks, I laughed at seeing their ugly faces. They cursed me to become a python," he explained. Bowing to Krishna, the youth disappeared, freed from his curse.

One moonlit night, Krishna and Balarama were roaming in the forest accompanied by Gopas and Gopikas, filling the air with melodious music. Just then, Shankhachooda, a devotee of Kubera, appeared and ran off with the Gopikas.

"Oh Rama! Oh Krishna!" they cried. Krishna and Balarama chased him. Shankhachooda ran, but Krishna pursued him and with a single stroke of his hand, knocked Shankhachooda's head off. Krishna took the shining gem from the demon's head and presented it to Balarama.

One day, a huge bull confronted Krishna. "It was the demon Arishtasura in the form of a bull. As he stamped the ground with his enormous hooves the very earth trembled." Krishna stood calmly. The enraged creature leapt towards him. Krishna caught hold of his horns and drove the Asura back forcefully. With a deft movement, Krishna pulled out its sharp horns and killed the demon.

Meanwhile, Sage Narada visited King Kamsa in Mathura. "Krishna, who lives in Vraja, is the son of Devaki... The two boys in the care of his friend Nanda... All the Asuras sent by you to kill them have been killed themselves," Narada revealed.

Kamsa trembled with rage. He immediately plotted to kill Krishna and Balarama. He summoned his wrestlers, Chanura and Mushtika, and the keeper of his mighty elephant, Kuvalayapeeda. He planned a grand bow sacrifice and a wrestling match, inviting the boys. Kamsa sent Akrura, a Yadava kinsman, to fetch them from Vrindavana.

While Akrura was on his way, a demon named Keshi assumed the form of a mighty horse and galloped into Vrindavana, terrorizing the people. "Keshi rushed at Krishna with his enormous mouth wide open as if he would devour the entire sky."

Krishna effortlessly thrust his left arm into the open mouth of the demon. "At the touch of Krishna's hand Keshi's teeth began to fall. Then Krishna's arm began to swell inside the mouth of the demon... till it choked the demon's throat." Keshi collapsed and died.

A few days later, while Krishna was playing hide-and-seek with his friends, the demon Vyomasura appeared, disguised as a cowherd. He joined the game as one of the thieves and started kidnapping the boys, hiding them in a cave.

"As soon as Vyomasura tried to take away another 'sheep', Krishna pounced on him, like a lion attacking a wolf." Krishna caught him roughly, pinned him to the ground, and killed him. Then Krishna freed all the trapped boys from the cave.

Akrura arrived in Vrindavana and delivered Kamsa's message. Early next morning, Krishna, Balarama, and the cowherds left for Mathura, leaving the Gopikas heartbroken. On the way, while bathing in the Yamuna, Akrura had a vision of Lord Vishnu resting on the serpent Shesha, realizing Krishna's divine nature.

In Mathura, Krishna and Balarama explored the city. They encountered a rude washerman of Kamsa. When the washerman insulted them, Krishna slapped him, killing him instantly, and distributed the fine clothes among his friends.

They then met a tailor who fitted the garments, and Sudama, a flowerman who worshipped them and presented them with garlands.

Walking further, they met Trivakra, a hunchbacked woman carrying special sandalwood paste for Kamsa. Touched by Krishna's gentle smile, she offered him the paste. "Krishna placed his two feet on the toes of the hunchback. He raised his hands and with his fingers, he pushed up her chin with a slight jerk." Her hump vanished, and she stood erect, a beautiful woman.

Finally, they reached the site of the bow sacrifice. Ignoring the guards, "Krishna reached for the mighty bow. With his left hand he deftly lifted the bow, strung it, and in a matter of minutes, split the bow into two." They vanquished the guards and returned to their camp.

The next morning, Kamsa inaugurated the festival of wrestling matches. As Krishna and Balarama arrived at the arena gates, the mahout provoked the elephant Kuvalayapeeda to attack them.

"For long, Krishna teased and taunted the elephant. Then with one hand he pulled at the trunk and floored him. Krishna then pulled out the tusks, and with them, he finished off both, the elephant and its keeper."

Carrying the tusks, the brothers entered the arena. The wrestling began. Krishna fought Chanura, while Balarama fought Mushtika. "Unperturbed, Krishna caught hold of his arms and flung him on the ground after turning him around several times in the air." Chanura was dead. Balarama defeated Mushtika, and they also killed the other wrestlers who stepped forward.

Seeing this, Kamsa panicked and ordered his men to drive the brothers away and kill Vasudeva, Ugrasena, and Nanda.

But before anyone could act, "Krishna leapt up to his high seat... Catching Kamsa by his hair Krishna threw him down. Krishna then jumped on Kamsa, killing him that very instant."

Kamsa's eight younger brothers rushed to take revenge, but Balarama killed them all.

Thereafter, Krishna and Balarama hurried to the prison and freed Vasudeva and Devaki, bowing before their true parents.

docs.title: Krishna: The Childhood of the Eighth Avatar of Vishnu
docs.text:
Krishna is the most endearing character in Indian mythology. He is at once the common cowherd engaging the milk-maids in playful banter and the supreme intellectual engaged in the exposition of the Gita philosophy.

Vasudeva, a nobleman, had married Princess Devaki of Mathura. As he was taking his bride home, Devaki's cruel cousin, Prince Kamsa, was driving the chariot. Suddenly, a heavenly voice thundered, "Kamsa, you shall soon die. The eighth child born to Devaki will slay you!"

Enraged, Kamsa drew his sword to kill Devaki on the spot. "If she lives to have that eighth child! I'll slay her before then!" he roared.

"Kamsa! Don't!" cried Vasudeva, jumping in front of the blade. "Your sister has done you no harm. As for the children who are yet to be born, I shall give each one to you, the moment it is born. I promise."

Kamsa lowered his sword with a sneer. "I'll spare Devaki, but I'll make sure that you keep your word." He imprisoned the newlyweds in a heavily guarded palace.

True to his cruel word, Kamsa visited them every time a baby was born. "Devaki, give me the baby," he demanded.

"No!" Devaki wept as Kamsa seized the infants, dashing them to the ground.

"I am helpless, Devaki!" Vasudeva mourned as they lost six of their children. The seventh child was miraculously carried away to Rohini, Vasudeva's second wife in Gokul, and Kamsa was told the baby was still-born.

"Good," Kamsa laughed. "The next will be the eighth one—the slayer of mighty Kamsa. Ha! Have Vasudeva and Devaki put in chains."

Then came the eighth child. On a dark, heavily raining midnight in the month of Shravan, a beautiful baby boy was born. "What a beautiful baby he is!" Devaki whispered. "And he knows he must not cry!"

"How I long to hold my son... but these chains..." Vasudeva sighed. Suddenly, with a sharp clank, the chains fell off his wrists. "Look! The chains have fallen off. And the doors have opened! It's a miracle!"

"Devaki! Quick! Give the child to me," Vasudeva said urgently. "I'll take him to our friend, Nanda, in Gokul."

Vasudeva placed the baby in a basket and stepped out into the storm. The palace guards had fallen into a deep, magical slumber. He reached the banks of the raging river Yamuna, which was in spate. However, as Vasudeva approached, the waters parted, allowing him to cross safely. When he reached Nanda's house, he found that Nanda's wife Yashoda had just given birth to a baby girl. Vasudeva swiftly swapped the babies, leaving his son and taking the girl back to the Mathura prison.

The moment Vasudeva re-entered the cell, the doors slammed shut, and the heavy chains wrapped around him once more.

"Did you hear that? It's the wail of a new-born baby!" a guard yelled, waking up. "Come! Let's report this to the king!"

Kamsa rushed into the cell. "The eighth child! I will be there in a moment."

"Spare the baby, Kamsa," Vasudeva pleaded. "What harm can a girl do you?"

Ignoring him, Kamsa seized the baby girl by her legs. Just as he was about to dash her to the ground, she escaped his grip, flew into the sky, and her voice echoed down: "The one who shall destroy you still lives!"

In Gokul, Nanda and Yashoda rejoiced at the birth of their beautiful boy, whom they named Krishna.

Desperate to eliminate the threat, Kamsa sent the wicked demoness Putana to slaughter every newborn in the region. Smearing her breasts with poison, she went to Gokul disguised as a beautiful woman.

"What a sweet baby! May I hold it?" she asked Yashoda.

Flattered, Yashoda handed the baby over. Putana fed him her poisoned milk, but the divine infant not only drank the milk—he sucked the life right out of the demoness. Putana collapsed dead in her true, hideous form, leaving the villagers stunned and baby Krishna perfectly safe.

As the years went by, Krishna grew into an adorable but incredibly mischievous boy. He and his older brother Balarama played on the banks of the Yamuna with the other cowherd boys (gwalas). Krishna loved fresh butter and constantly raided the neighbors' kitchens.

"Krishna has eaten all the butter in my house," a frustrated gopika complained, dragging the boy to his mother.

"My Krishna did that? I don't believe it," Yashoda defended him. "Why, I gave him a whole pot of butter only a short while ago."

Another day, the gopikas tried hanging their butter pots out of reach from the ceiling. "What shall we do?" the boys wondered.

"I have an idea," Krishna grinned. They formed a human pyramid, and Krishna effortlessly climbed up to drink the milk.

"Yashoda! Krishna was drinking the milk straight from my cow!" another woman shouted.

Krishna turned to his mother with innocent eyes. "But mother, the cow gave it to me."

"And the butter on your face? Who gave that to you?" Yashoda asked sternly.

"The gwala boys smeared it on my face. All out of spite," Krishna lied smoothly.

"Krishna, you are becoming naughtier day by day. I will have to punish you!" Yashoda tied him to a heavy mortar. "Let's see you move now."

"Balarama will untie me. I'll go to him," Krishna thought. He began to crawl, dragging the heavy mortar behind him. The mortar caught between two massive trees. Krishna pulled with all his might, and with a tremendous crack, uprooted both mighty trees.

"He uprooted those two mighty trees! This boy is a marvel!" the villagers gasped. Frightened by these strange happenings, the people of Gokul decided to pack up and move across the forest to Vrindavan.

In Vrindavan, Krishna herded the cattle and played his flute. His melodious music enchanted the entire village. "His music fills me with a strange yearning," the gopikas would sigh.

One day, a massive, angry bull charged through the fields. "It's Hastin! Run, Krishna!" the boys screamed.

"I won't," Krishna smiled. "I'll tame him." As the bull snorted fiercely and charged, Krishna nimbly leaped onto its back. "Quiet, my friend. I won't hurt you," he said gently, calming the beast.

Another time, Krishna found his friends crying. "My cows are dead. They drank water from Kaliya's pool," one wept. Kaliya was a massive, venomous multi-headed serpent who had poisoned a section of the river.

"Our cows will not be safe as long as Kaliya lives," Krishna said, marching toward the river.

"Krishna! Come back! Kaliya will kill you!" his friends shrieked, but Krishna dove straight into the venomous waters. Kaliya wrapped his massive coils around the boy, trying to crush him. Krishna effortlessly slipped free, subdued the serpent, and leaped onto its many hoods, dancing triumphantly on the monster's heads. Defeated, Kaliya begged for mercy, and Krishna ordered him to take his family and leave the pool forever.

As time passed, Krishna began to question the villagers' traditions. Seeing them prepare a grand offering out of fear, he asked, "We worship Indra because we are afraid of him. We ought to worship Mount Govardhana, whom we love!"

Persuaded by the young boy, the villagers made their offerings to the mountain instead. This infuriated Indra, the god of rain, who unleashed a terrifying, apocalyptic thunderstorm upon Vrindavan.

"Indra is angry. We shouldn't have worshipped Govardhana," the villagers panicked.

"Come my people, follow me," Krishna called out. He walked to the base of the mountain. "Look! It's rising!"

With a casual push of his little finger, Krishna lifted the entire Mount Govardhana into the air like an umbrella. "Bring your cattle with you and take shelter. It is dry here." The villagers huddled in awe under the floating mountain for seven days and nights, until Indra's fury was spent and the humbled god retreated.

News of these miracles finally reached Kamsa in Mathura. "You fool! Why have you let him live so long? Didn't I order you to have him killed?" he raged at his commander, Pradyota.

"I have tried everything. Even the mad bull, Arishta, and the wild horse, Keshi," Pradyota trembled.

"Have it announced that in a fortnight I will perform the bow sacrifice... and invite all, including Krishna," Kamsa schemed. "You must see to it that he does not return alive."

He summoned the wise Akrura to fetch the boys from Vrindavan. Nanda, Krishna's foster father, was deeply suspicious. "No, Akrura. I don't trust Kamsa. I will not send my son."

Akrura was forced to reveal the truth. "Nanda! Krishna is the son of Prince Vasudeva."

When Yashoda heard this, she was heartbroken. "Krishna, did you hear that? Akrura says that Devaki is your mother. I am your mother, aren't I?"

Krishna wiped her tears. "Please don't be upset, mother. I shall always look upon you as my mother. But... I seek the permission of my elders to go to Mathura!" Reluctantly, the village elders agreed, knowing the Yadava chiefs would protect him.

As Krishna and Balarama arrived in Mathura, a massive crowd followed them to the sacrificial hall. At the gates, Kamsa's commander stood guard over a colossal, sacred bow.

"Sir, I am Krishna. May I inspect the bow?" Krishna asked politely.

"Certainly! Do step in," Pradyota grinned, thinking, So this is the boy I have to kill.

Krishna lifted the massive weapon effortlessly. "Is this the bow? It doesn't look very heavy." He pulled the string back until, with a deafening crack, the sacred bow snapped in two.

"Victory to Krishna! He is the saviour!" the jubilant crowd roared.

When Pradyota delivered the news, Kamsa was terrified. The next morning, as the boys approached the wrestling arena, Kamsa unleashed a gigantic, maddened elephant upon them. "Watch out, Krishna! The elephant...!" the crowd screamed. The beast seized Krishna in its trunk, but the boy wrestled free, lifted the monstrous animal by its own trunk, hurled it into the air, and killed it.

Inside the arena, Kamsa announced, "Krishna, Kamsa has heard much about your strength and valour. He has invited you to wrestle... with me!"

"It's an honour, Chanura. I am ready," Krishna replied to the giant wrestler.

Kamsa pitted Balarama against another brute, Mushtika. The crowd was outraged at the unfair match, but Balarama quickly overpowered Mushtika, killing him.

Meanwhile, the massive Chanura rushed madly at Krishna, trying to crush him in his mighty arms. But Krishna nimbly slipped away. As Chanura charged again, Krishna dashed him to the ground with a sickening thud. "Chanura is dead! Victory to Krishna!" the arena erupted.

Furious and panicked, Kamsa ordered his soldiers to kill the boys. But Krishna leapt over the guards, vaulted into the royal balcony, and seized Kamsa by the hair. Taking the tyrant's own sword, Krishna threw him to the ground and killed him.

The crowd was stunned into silence as Krishna stood over the fallen king and lifted the crown from Kamsa's head.

He then marched down into the palace dungeons and broke the chains of his true parents, Vasudeva and Devaki. Finally, he sought out Kamsa's imprisoned father, Ugrasena.

"My lord! I have brought you what rightfully belongs to you," Krishna said, handing him the crown.

This was only the beginning. Krishna lived long to triumph over all evil, and his great deeds are remembered to this day.

docs.title: Kumbhakarna
docs.text:
Kumbhakarna was the younger brother of Ravana, the ten-headed Rakshasa King of Lanka. He was strong, but sadly, a big bully who loved to frighten those weaker than him.

"Ravana, do you know what I've done today? I walked into the Dandaka forest and frightened the sages there! Ha! Ha! Ha! You should have seen them run!" Kumbhakarna boasted.

But his actions angered Indra, King of the Devas. Indra attacked Kumbhakarna with his army of Devas. "Come, Indra. I was waiting for you," Kumbhakarna sneered.

Indra hurled his powerful mace, but Kumbhakarna caught it and swallowed it! Then he leapt forward, caught hold of Indra's elephant, Airavata, by its tusks, and flung it far away. Indra's army fled in terror.

"Not yet. We're strong and powerful. But we must pray to Lord Brahma and get more strength and more power," Ravana decided. The three brothers, Ravana, Kumbhakarna, and Vibhishana, went to a quiet place to perform severe penance.

When Indra heard this, he was worried. He went to Saraswati, the Goddess of Speech, for help. "O Saraswati, only you can save us now."

Lord Brahma was pleased with the penances and appeared. He granted Ravana the boon to be Lord of the Three Worlds, and Vibhishana a boon of righteousness.

But before Kumbhakarna could speak, Saraswati cast her spell on his tongue. "I want to sleep all the time, my Lord!" he blurted out instead of asking for invincibility.

"May you sleep for twenty-four hours a day!" Brahma granted instantly.

"My Lord, my brother didn't know what he was asking for. Please take back the boon," Ravana pleaded in horror.

"I cannot take back a boon I have granted. But... all right. He will remain awake for a whole day, once every six months. But if anyone wakes him up on any other day, his life will be in danger," Brahma amended.

Kumbhakarna was taken to his palace, where he immediately fell fast asleep.

Years later, ignoring Vibhishana's advice, Ravana carried away Sita, the wife of Rama. Vibhishana left Lanka and joined Rama, who marched on Lanka with a huge army of monkeys.

Ravana first sent the terrible Rakshasa, Bhasmalochana, who could reduce anyone to ashes with a mere look. But Rama quickly created millions of mirrors. When Bhasmalochana opened his eyes, he saw hundreds of his own fiery eyes reflected back at him, and he was burnt to ashes by his own power!

Then Ravana himself went to battle. He was struck unconscious by Rama's arrows. When he recovered, Rama shot off his crowns. Deeply humiliated, Ravana fled back to his palace.

"What should I do? When I, the King, have to flee, what hope is there for my subjects? Go wake Kumbhakarna up! Ask him to see me," Ravana ordered, completely forgetting Brahma's warning.

The servants went to Kumbhakarna's palace. As the giant breathed in, the servants were sucked into his nostrils! When he breathed out, they were blown away.

They tried making a huge racket with drums and conches, pouring ice-cold sandalwood paste on him, beating him with huge logs, and even making elephants walk over him. "It's useless. He hasn't even stopped snoring!"

Finally, someone brought steaming hot food. "Mm-m-m! Delicious!" Kumbhakarna woke up, his mouth watering. After devouring mountains of food, he went to Ravana.

"It's not Indra but men and monkeys who are about to attack Lanka," Ravana explained.

"I've no doubt about it. You are mighty and fearless. I'm sure you'll succeed," Ravana encouraged him, despite Kumbhakarna's initial rebuke about abducting Sita.

Kumbhakarna marched alone to the battlefield. The earth shook under him, and the monkey-warriors trembled with fear. "We're no match for this giant."

He created havoc, crushing monkeys underfoot. Neela, a mighty monkey, hurled a huge sal tree at him, but Kumbhakarna broke it into bits. Sugreeva, the monkey-king, challenged him. He snatched Kumbhakarna's iron rod and broke it, but Kumbhakarna picked Sugreeva up like a toy and marched off towards Ravana's palace.

"I'll make a gift of you to Ravana," Kumbhakarna laughed. But suddenly, Sugreeva tore off Kumbhakarna's ear and leaped back to his army.

Filled with shame and bleeding, Kumbhakarna returned to the battlefield in a rage. "Call Rama, of whom I've heard so much. Let me see him fight."

Rama faced him. "Here I am. Use all your strength. After I've killed you and Ravana, I'll make Vibhishana King of Lanka, take Sita, and return with her to Ayodhya," Rama challenged.

Rama shot a dart, which Kumbhakarna brushed off. Then Rama fixed a special arrow to his bow. "The arrow found its mark." With a final, agonizing cry, Kumbhakarna fell dead.

When the news reached Ravana, he was devastated. "Alas! Why did I wake up my brother in spite of the warning?" he wept. "I will avenge his death. I'll slay Rama."

But in the final battle with Rama, it was Ravana who was slain. Rama made Vibhishana the Lord of Lanka, rescued Sita, and returned triumphantly to Ayodhya.

docs.title: The Lord of Lanka
docs.text:
Sumali was the King of the Rakshasas. From the nether-world, where he lived, he once came to visit the world of men, along with his beautiful daughter, Kaikesi.

He travelled from one place to another looking for the worthiest of men for his daughter to marry. At last, he saw a magnificent chariot descending from the sky in front of the hermitage of Vishrava. It was Kubera, Vishrava's son, the rich and mighty King of Lanka.

"Daughter, approach Vishrava and win him over. If he marries you, you will have sons as mighty as Kubera," Sumali advised.

Kaikesi went to the hermitage of Vishrava while he was busy offering his evening prayers. "I understand. But you have come to me at the wrong hour," the sage warned her. "The children born to us will be wicked Rakshasas..."

Vishrava married Kaikesi, and within a few years, Ravana (the ten-headed one), Kumbhakarna, Shoorpanakha, and Vibheeshana were born.

Years rolled by. Ravana and Kumbhakarna grew up arrogant and ambitious. Jealous of Kubera's wealth and power, Ravana made a vow: "Mother, here and now I make this vow. I will excel Kubera."

Ravana immediately set out for Gokarna along with his brothers, Kumbhakarna and Vibheeshana. They began performing severe penances. During the penance, Ravana did not touch food. One by one he sacrificed his heads in the holy fire.

When he was about to sacrifice his tenth head, Lord Brahma appeared before him. "I am pleased with you. Ask for a boon."

"O Lord, grant me immortality," Ravana asked.

"Immortality is not for you. Ask for something else," Brahma replied.

"Then let me not die at the hands of gods, demons, Rakshasas, serpents and spirits. I am not afraid of mortals," Ravana requested, arrogantly dismissing the threat of human beings.

"So be it. And I grant you more. Your heads will be restored to you. You will also have the power to change your form at will," Brahma granted. Brahma also granted boons to Vibheeshana (righteousness) and Kumbhakarna (a long sleep).

When they returned home, Ravana sent a messenger to Kubera's court: "Go and tell Kubera that he must vacate Lanka, immediately. I wish to occupy it."

Kubera consulted his father, Vishrava, who advised him to yield. Kubera vacated Lanka, and Ravana became the king. As the days passed, Ravana became increasingly arrogant, harassing the gods, sages, and Gandharvas.

When Kubera sent a messenger advising Ravana to mend his ways, Ravana was furious. "Impudent man! You will pay for this," he yelled, slaying the messenger. "Now I must teach Kubera the lesson of his life."

Ravana attacked Kubera's kingdom. A great battle took place. Because of the boon, Ravana was invincible. He defeated Kubera and seized 'Pushpaka', the flying chariot of Kubera.

As Ravana flew over Mount Kailas in the Pushpaka chariot, it came to a dead halt. Nandi, Shiva's attendant, came down. "Ravana! This is Lord Shiva's abode. Do not disturb him," he warned.

"Whose orders are these? Shiva's? I'll uproot this very mountain on which he rests," Ravana roared. Assuming a huge form, Ravana tried to lift Kailas.

The mountain shook, terrifying even Parvati. But Lord Shiva simply pressed the mountain with his toe, crushing Ravana's hands beneath it.

"Ahh! Help!" Ravana cried out in pain. Advised by his minister, Ravana began singing hymns in praise of Shiva for many years. Pleased with his devotion, Lord Shiva permitted Ravana to remove his hands and gave him a sword.

Ravana resumed conquering the world. In the Himalayas, he saw a beautiful maiden, Vedavati, meditating to win Lord Vishnu as her husband. Ravana advanced towards her and seized her by the hair.

Enraged by his touch, Vedavati created a fire and jumped into it. "I will be born again and be the cause of your death," she cursed him.

Vedavati took birth in a lotus, which Ravana found. But remembering the curse, he threw the baby into the river. She was eventually found in a furrow by King Janaka and named Sita. Sita grew up and married Rama, the Prince of Ayodhya, who broke Shiva's bow to win her hand.

Meanwhile, Ravana's arrogance continued. He seized Rambha, the betrothed of Nalkoober (Kubera's son). Nalkoober laid a terrible curse on him: "If he ever marries a woman against her will, his heads will break into seven pieces."

Years later, undaunted by his past misdeeds, Ravana disguised himself as a monk and visited the Dandaka forest where Sita was living in exile with Rama. While Rama was away hunting, Ravana kidnapped Sita and brought her to his Ashoka garden in Lanka.

"I love you. Why do you waste your time on Rama? Marry me," he pleaded, but fear of Nalkoober's curse kept him from forcing her. Sita steadfastly refused him.

Rama, with a huge army of monkeys and bears, constructed a bridge across the ocean and invaded Lanka. Ravana's brothers and courtiers advised him to return Sita, but he was angry. He banished Vibheeshana, who then joined Rama's side.

The war began. One by one, the warriors of Ravana fell, including the mighty Kumbhakarna.

Desperate, Ravana used magic to deceive Sita, showing her an illusion of Rama's severed head and bow. But her grief turned to relief when she learned it was a trick and Rama was very much alive.

Finally, Ravana himself entered the battlefield. The two foes met in a fierce duel. Rama used a crescent-shaped weapon to sever Ravana's heads, but they kept regenerating. At last, Rama used a powerful divine weapon, and the arrow found its mark in Ravana's chest. The Lord of Lanka fell dead.

As Ravana lay defeated, Vibheeshana wept. "Don't grieve, Vibheeshana! He has died like a brave warrior," Rama consoled him.

Lanka now had a new, righteous lord. Rama crowned Vibheeshana King of Lanka, and after rescuing Sita, returned triumphantly to Ayodhya.

docs.title: The Churning of the Ocean
docs.text:
Long ago, while roaming the earth, the short-tempered sage Durvasa met a celestial nymph carrying a garland of heavenly flowers. Heady with their intoxicating fragrance, the sage requested the garland, and the nymph gladly presented it to him.

As Durvasa walked on, he met Indra, King of the Devas, riding upon his grand four-tusked white elephant, Airavata. Desiring to honor the King of Heaven, Durvasa presented the sweet-smelling garland to Indra.

Indra took the garland casually and draped it over Airavata's forehead. Intoxicated by the perfume, the elephant plucked the garland with its trunk, threw it onto the dirt, and trampled it underfoot.

Seeing the sacred garland of Sri—the Goddess of Fortune—disrespected and trampled, Durvasa flew into a terrible rage. "O arrogant Indra!" Durvasa roared. "Just as you cast this divine garland to the ground, so shall your power, prosperity, and glory crumble! All the Devas shall lose their vigor!"

Instantly, the curse took effect. Plants withered, cool streams dried up, and the Devas felt their strength drain away. Taking advantage of their weakness, the Asuras (demons), led by King Bali, attacked the heavens. Powerless to resist, the Devas were routed and fled to Mount Meru to seek Lord Brahma's aid.

Brahma meditated and led the weakened gods to Vaikuntha, the abode of Lord Vishnu. The Devas bowed before Vishnu's radiant light, pleading, "O All-Knowing Lord, help us! We are powerless against the Asuras."

Lord Vishnu looked upon them with compassion. "Listen carefully. For the moment, you must make peace with your enemies, the Asuras. Join hands with them to churn the Ocean of Milk to extract Amrita, the Nectar of Immortality. Once you drink the Nectar, you will become immortal and fear nothing."

"But Lord," Indra asked, "will not the Asuras take the Nectar for themselves?"

"Leave that to me," Vishnu assured them. "Make peace with them now."

Indra traveled to King Bali's court in the netherworld. Surprised by the unarmed gods, Bali listened as Indra proposed a joint venture to churn the Ocean of Milk and share the Nectar of Immortality. Recognizing the immense value of the prize, Bali and the Asura leaders—Shambara, Pauloma, and Kalakeya—agreed to a temporary truce.

Together, the Devas and Asuras gathered on the shores of the Ocean of Milk, casting sacred medicinal herbs into the waters. To serve as a churning rod, they uprooted the colossal Mount Mandara, which extended eleven thousand yojanas above the earth.

However, as the combined armies carried the massive mountain toward the sea, they collapsed under its crushing weight. Seeing many gods and demons crushed beneath the peak, Lord Vishnu arrived on Garuda. With a single healing glance, he revived the injured warriors, effortlessly lifted Mount Mandara with one hand, placed it on Garuda's back, and transported it to the ocean.

To act as the churning rope, they invited Vasuki, King of the Serpents, promising him a share of the Nectar. Vasuki agreed and coiled his long body around Mount Mandara.

As they prepared to churn, Indra and the Devas walked toward Vasuki's head. But the Asuras grew suspicious and bellowed, "We are mighty warriors! We will not hold the tail of a snake! We demand to hold the head!"

Indra hid a smile and yielded politely. "As you wish, friends. We shall hold the tail."

With the Asuras holding Vasuki's head and the Devas holding his tail, the great churning began. But as Mount Mandara whirled, there was no support beneath its immense weight, and the mountain began to sink into the ocean bed.

Seeing their distress, Lord Vishnu assumed his second avatar, Kurma—a gigantic turtle as vast as an island. Diving to the ocean floor, Lord Vishnu supported the base of Mount Mandara upon his impenetrable shell, enabling the churning to resume with furious speed.

As the mountain whirled, Vasuki's thousand mouths exhaled clouds of poisonous fire and smoke, engulfing the Asuras near his head. Lord Vishnu sent down cool, refreshing rain clouds over the Devas at the tail to keep them energized.

Suddenly, a thick, terrifying cloud of black poison—Halahala—emerged from the ocean depths, threatening to incinerate all life in the universe. Panicked, the Devas and Asuras cried out to Lord Shiva on Mount Kailash.

Hearing their prayers, Lord Shiva gathered the deadly poison into the palm of his hand and swallowed it. To prevent the poison from harming his body, his consort Parvati pressed his throat, holding the poison there. The lethal venom turned Shiva's neck bright blue, earning him the eternal title Neelakantha ("The Blue-Throated Lord").

With the poison cleared, the churning resumed, and the ocean began to yield its sacred treasures: 1. Surabhi (Kamadhenu), the divine wish-fulfilling cow, given to the sages. 2. Uchchhaishrava, the flying white horse, taken by Bali. 3. Airavata, the majestic four-tusked white elephant, taken by Indra. 4. The Apsaras, celestial maidens who danced for the gods. 5. Parijata, the celestial flower tree. 6. Varuni, the goddess of wine. 7. Shree (Goddess Lakshmi), the Goddess of Fortune, emerging radiant from the golden waves. As holy rivers poured water over her from golden jars, she looked upon all the gods and chose Lord Vishnu as her eternal consort, placing the Vaijayanti garland around his neck.

Finally, Dhanvantari, the divine physician, emerged from the churning waters holding a golden vessel containing the precious Amrita.

At the sight of the Nectar, the Asuras roared, pushed Dhanvantari aside, and snatched the golden pot by force, fleeing to the shore.

As the Asuras began fighting among themselves over who should drink first, Lord Vishnu assumed the form of Mohini—a maiden of bewitching, matchless beauty.

Mesmerized by her enchanting smile, the Asuras forgot their quarrel and ran to her. "O beautiful lady!" Bali pleaded. "Please take this pot and distribute the Nectar equally among us!"

Mohini smiled playfully. "I am only a woman, and women are fickle. How can you trust me?"

"We trust you completely!" the Asuras declared. "Promise to abide by your decision!"

Mohini seated the Devas and Asuras in two parallel rows. She began serving the Nectar to the row of Devas first, flashing captivating glances at the Asuras to keep them spellbound.

Noticing that the Nectar was being given only to the gods, a clever Asura named Rahu disguised himself as a Deva and slipped into the row between Surya (the Sun God) and Soma (the Moon God).

As Rahu raised the cup and took a sip, Surya and Soma recognized him and alerted Lord Vishnu. Instantly, Vishnu launched his Sudarshana Chakra, severing Rahu's head just as the Nectar passed his throat! The immortal severed head flew into the sky, where it remains as a demon node, periodically attempting to swallow the Sun and Moon, causing eclipses.

Realizing they had been duped, the Asuras drew their weapons and attacked. But the Devas, invigorated by the Nectar of Immortality, fought with invincible strength. Lord Vishnu joined the battle with his Sudarshana Chakra, routing the demon host and driving them deep into the netherworld.

With peace restored to the universe, the victorious Devas placed Mount Mandara back in its original sanctuary and returned to the heavens in joy and glory.
